Poilievre accuses Trudeau of coming home empty-handed after Trump, PM hail 'productive' Florida meeting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievre-accuses-trudeau-coming-home-empty/docview/3134520628/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+accuses+Trudeau+of+coming+home+empty-handed+after+Trump%2C+PM+hail+%27productive%27+Florida+meeting&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. president-elect Donald Trump hail their surprise meeting in Florida on Friday night as "productive," federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says Trudeau came home empty-handed for Canada.
Poilievre made the comment Sunday in response to a reporter's question about the Trudeau-Trump three-hour dinner at Trump's Mar-a-Lago Club in Palm Beach, Fla. The pair talked cross-border issues including Trump's threat to slap massive 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian products being imported into the U.S.
"While I'm a critic of Mr. Trudeau's, I did feel badly that he went in with such a position of weakness," the Conservative leader said.
"Normally when a prime minister goes to the United States to meet a president they're looking to make gains. What gains did we hear from Mr. Trudeau? None."
Trump posted on social media that he had "a very productive meeting" with Trudeau and "made it very clear that the United States will no longer sit idly by as our Citizens become victims to the scourge of this Drug Epidemic."
Trump, who is announcing appointments and other moves to prepare for his Jan. 20 return to the White House, added that "Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has made a commitment to work with us to end this terrible devastation of U.S. Families."
Trudeau, whom sources told the Star initiated the visit that included Canada's Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc and advisers, as well Trump's interior secretary and commerce secretary nominees, other advisers and their wives, also called the meeting "productive" and shared a photo of him and Trump together, both smiling.
"I look forward to the work we can do together, again," Trudeau posted. A spokesperson told the Star the pair "shared a productive wide-ranging discussion over dinner last night, centred on collaboration and strengthening our relationship."
"As Canada's closest friend and ally, the United States is our key partner, and we are committed to working together in the interests of Canadians and Americans."
None of that was enough for Poilievre who suggested the prime minister should have flown home with substantial commitments or exemptions from the president-elect, who has not backed off his threat of import surcharges that would have a devastating impact on Canada's energy, auto and manufacturing export.
He contrasted the dinner talk with former prime minister Stephen Harper's cross-border negotiations on so-called "Buy American" protectionist policies and the 2012 Softwood Lumber Agreement, adding that Trudeau is "a weak prime minister who's lost control of our borders, lost control of immigration, lost control of crime and drugs, and lost control of our economy."
Poilievre had previously said Trudeau doesn't have the "brains or backbone" to stand up to Trump.
When the Conservative leader was asked Sunday if he has reached out to members of Trump's transition team, he replied: "I'm not the Prime Minister."
During Sunday's press conference, Poilievre shared a number of demands for the Liberal government bolster the U.S.-Canada border - one the key asks in Trump's tariff threat - including measures to increase patrols and technology to crack down on illegal drug trafficking, as well as the tightening of visa rules and working with provincial law enforcement. He also said Canada should also cap the number of asylum seekers as it faces a significant influx in refugee claims.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford had a more positive response to the meeting, posting on X that he was glad Trudeau went to Trump to learn more about the president-elect's concerns.
"Now we need the federal government to show us how they'll honour their commitment to all premiers to make our border more secure," said Ford.
He added that Canada's premiers are ready to meet at any time to review Trudeau's promised plan to avoid "disastrous tariffs."
A source told the Star's Tonda MacCharles that Trudeau, Trump and their teams discussed trade, border security, fentanyl, defence matters including NATO, Ukraine, and icebreakers (Canada, the U.S. and Finland recently agreed to a trilateral pact to rebuild their icebreaking fleets).
Other topics on the table included China, energy issues and pipelines, including those that feed Canadian oil and gas into the U.S. like Keystone XL, Line 5, as well as the Trans-Mountain project and Canada's liquid natural gas export capacity.
The leaders also discussed next year's G7 meeting, which Canada will chair in Kananaskis, Alta. It will be held seven years after Trump left the 2018 G7 at Charlevoix, Que. and called out Trudeau for making "false statements" in his opposition to U.S. steel and aluminum tariffs, the Star previously reported.
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre holds a news conference in a hotel ballroom in Ottawa on Sunday.

Subject: Presidents; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 2 of 500

Did Bonnie Crombie get 'sold a bill of goods'? Here's why the Liberal leader is struggling to find her footing and take down Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/did-bonnie-crombie-get-sold-bill-goods-heres-why/docview/3134520624/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Did+Bonnie+Crombie+get+%27sold+a+bill+of+goods%27%3F+Here%27s+why+the+Liberal+leader+is+struggling+to+find+her+footing+and+take+down+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's 29 kilometres from Mississauga city hall to Queen's Park. But the journey, like the traffic, is complicated.
Bonnie Crombie admits she didn't realize that at the time.
Now, much sooner than expected, the former Mississauga mayor who won the leadership of Ontario's struggling Liberals a year ago this weekend is girding for an early election against Premier Doug Ford.
"When I got here, I thought 'I'm experienced, I'm a polished leader, you know? Seventy-eight per cent of Mississauga voted for me,'" says Crombie, a target of Progressive Conservative attack ads.
"I thought it would be easier. It's been tough," adds Crombie, who commands a small caucus of nine MPPs. They hold third place in the 124-seat legislature, where she has yet to run for a seat.
"There's a lot of work to do, and I'm up for it."
Several Liberal insiders contacted by the Star agreed the party's comeback bid faces challenges. And the latest polling from Abacus Data suggests Crombie is "treading water" with voters, says chief executive David Coletto.
In the first poll after she became leader, conducted in late January, the Liberals were at 27 per cent support, four percentage points higher than this month's Abacus survey.
"Her leadership alone hasn't had an effect of closing the gap with Ford," Coletto says, noting the unpopularity of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his federal Liberals is a drag on the Ontario party.
Hoping to break out, the provincial Liberals recently unveiled their first campaign ad punching back at the premier, with Crombie telling voters they deserve "better."
Ford's Tories are running well ahead of opposition parties even with an ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, 2.5 million Ontarians lacking a family doctor, long waits in overcrowded hospitals, controversial bike lane closures and homeless encampments.
"Doug Ford has had six years to fix it," Crombie says.
If Ford calls an early election, it could provide Crombie and the Liberals the opening they are looking for, says Liberal strategist Dan Moulton.
That's because sending Ontarians to the polls early - instead of waiting for the next scheduled vote in 2026 - is a risk for Ford, Moulton says.
"It's a gamble. It's not a great time to be an incumbent government anywhere in the world ... it may not be the walk in the park that he had in 2022, post-pandemic. It's a different environment now. Voters are angry," he adds.
"There's opportunity there for Bonnie and the Liberals."
The latest Abacus poll for the Star two weeks ago suggests 45 per cent of voters believe it's time for a change in government, but that alone is not enough to flip the balance of power at Queen's Park.
Ford's Conservatives enjoy 43 per cent support, the Liberals 23 per cent, the official opposition NDP under Marit Stiles are at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens are at five per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 people from Nov. 15 to 19 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size it would be plus or minus 3.1 per cent, 19 times out of 20.
"I have no illusions about where we stand in the polls," says Crombie, who will host her first leader's fundraising dinner Tuesday night at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.
Liberals do not underestimate the premier by any means.
"This guy is really good. Things seem to break his way," says one party stalwart, speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations.
"Bonnie's got an urgent challenge. She needs to create buzz so she can recruit 25 winning candidates. Right now, that's really difficult."
That's a reference to the number of seats some Liberals believe Crombie will need to gain in an election as early as next spring to leapfrog the New Democrats into second place in the legislature.
So far, recruitment efforts have yielded several notable candidates in ridings the Liberals hope to take back. For example: Vince Gasparro, formerly a senior aide to Toronto mayor John Tory and Prime Minister Paul Martin; hospital executive Lee Fairclough who came close to winning Etobicoke-Lakeshore in 2022; and former deputy Toronto police chief Peter Yuen in Scarborough-Agincourt.
The Liberals saw their support improve dramatically in a September byelection to replace former Ford cabinet minister Todd Smith in Bay of Quinte. They took one third of the ballots to 39 per cent for the victorious Tory, down from 49 per cent under Smith.
But the Liberal party is far from the political powerhouse it was for almost 15 years in government until a crushing 2018 defeat and a disappointing 2022 loss that confirmed there would be no easy rebound.
While the Liberals received more votes than the New Democrats in the last election, those ballots did not translate into seats because NDP support is concentrated in certain ridings, which helped them win 31.
"This party is like a start-up. There is no infrastructure. There is no money," says another Liberal veteran, maintaining the "draft Crombie" movement that lured her from a comfortable perch running Ontario's third-largest city underplayed the challenge.
Instead, Crombie is running Ontario's third party.
"I think Bonnie got sold a bill of goods. It's not an easy job. If they're in third place one more time, I don't know what the future is," adds the veteran Liberal.
Crombie is in the "challenging" position of needing to take seats from the NDP as she moves the Liberals to the centre with promises of an income-tax cut to entice voters who have shifted to the Conservatives, says Coletto.
"She has to build a very difficult coalition," adds the pollster, who maintains it will be tough for either the Liberals or NDP to break out because they are splitting the anti-Ford vote.
Speaking of the NDP, "there's seats there that, if you ignore them, you'll be the next one not to fill the minivan," warns Flavio Volpe, a senior Liberal staffer at Queen's Park during the McGuinty years who is now president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association.
Liberals have been dubbed the "minivan" caucus because of their nine MPPs, three fewer than needed for official party status in the legislature which would give them millions in taxpayer funding for staff and operations.
Looking back on her first 12 months as leader and ahead to the biggest fight of her political career, Crombie is candid.
"I was really comfortable right at the beginning. I had a lot of confidence. And then I realized, 'maybe I don't know what I don't know," says the one-time Mississauga-Streetsville Liberal MP from 2008 to 2011.
After almost a decade wearing the mayor's chain of office and projecting authority, Crombie has concluded that persona does not translate to the new job, something her advisers also acknowledge.
"This is a much different ballpark ... there's a lot to learn," adds Crombie, sitting in the Liberal caucus boardroom with its unglamorous view of a back parking lot at Queen's Park.
"They're telling me to be more my authentic self, be more conversational ... I just need to be me."
With an eye to an early election, Crombie is trying to spend more time at the legislature fielding questions from reporters to build her comfort level and profile.
There has been surprise both inside and outside the party that she has not been a more adroit political performer, but some question whether that's a fair expectation because the leap to the higher stakes of provincial politics is an adjustment.
"I think it surprised a lot of Liberals who thought they were getting something different," says the party veteran who acknowledges Crombie has appeal. "She's still got that X factor."
Indeed, Crombie was the strongest performer among her leadership rivals: Liberal MPs Nate Erskine-Smith and Yasir Naqvi and Grit MPP Ted Hsu.
But, unlike the mayors of Toronto, Ottawa or Hamilton, the mayor of Mississauga - despite the city's size - does not regularly face a phalanx of newspaper, TV, radio and digital reporters.
"When she's in a scrum at Queen's Park and questions are flying at her it's slightly out of her comfort zone. She's getting better at it," says one adviser, who points out "there's not a big press gallery at Mississauga city hall."
That's why some at Queen's Park are surprised the Liberals haven't urged Crombie to be at the legislature more often.
Since her leadership victory last Dec. 2, she has been spending long days on the road travelling the province to meet voters, speak to community groups, find candidates, raise campaign cash and resuscitate riding associations.
Earlier this month, Crombie began ramping up efforts to reach voters, sending a major signal she's returning the party - which drifted left under former premier Kathleen Wynne - back toward the centre.
To that end, Crombie has promised a 22 per cent cut to middle-class income taxes- similar to what Ford pledged in 2018 but hasn't delivered. It's also intended to distance her from Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
In last May's Milton byelection, voters repeatedly told Tory canvassers at the doorstep they were looking forward to defeating the federal Liberals. The provincial Grits privately acknowledge that Trudeau's lack of popularity hurt them.
That's one reason Crombie invited Trudeau critics like former federal health minister Jane Philpott and former British Columbia premier Christy Clark to address hundreds of delegates at the party's London policy convention in September.
Looking ahead, her campaign branding emphasizes "Team Bonnie" over the Liberal name and is signalling the election promises to come will be aimed at attracting centrist voters.
"We're focusing on the basics, because when you get the basics right, everything else will fall into place," says Crombie.
She is keenly aware Democrats lost the U.S. presidential election because "they didn't pay enough attention to affordability ... to meeting people's needs."
"We have nothing to lose. We're going to be very bold," Crombie adds in the conference room that is just a floor below and a short walk from the premier's corner office at the legislature.
"I feel I am the right person to do this, but it's a big hill to climb."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombiewon the leadershipof Ontario's struggling Liberals a year ago and today admits it's been tougher than she thought it would be.

Real story of rehabilitation: Ontario is backing rehab instead of safe drug injection sites. Those using the system say both are needed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/real-story-rehabilitation/docview/3134456154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Real+story+of+rehabilitation%3A+Ontario+is+backing+rehab+instead+of+safe+drug+injection+sites.+Those+using+the+system+say+both+are+needed&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One sunny afternoon, just weeks after Ontario announced its controversial plan to shift gears on its approach to the toxic drugs crisis that has claimed more than 10,000 lives in four years, a cluster of men and women laughed and chatted outside a window-lined complex on the edge of an East York forest.
Some residents of the Bellwood residential addiction treatment centre appeared barely out of their teens, while others could be grandparents. A few women walked on wooded trails while two men steered wheelchairs on paved paths.
Inside, it felt like the first week of school. Recovering drug users were nervous, moody and excited, wondering what activities to sign up for and how to fit in. A 50-year-old woman named Stacey bounded in the front door and embraced the first staff member she found.
"I want everyone to know what I learned," she told the staff member as she held her in her arms.
Rehabilitation saved Stacey's life. Now four months sober, the retired business manager is determined never to return to the addiction she once suffered.
"This place has been life-changing for me," she said, trembling with emotion. "I am so grateful that I could come here."
But later that afternoon, as clouds dimmed the Ontario sky, Stacey said something else. As she celebrated the wonder of having earned a new life, she worried about others who had not found redemption. Some had not put in the work needed to succeed. Others had put in the work but were still in trouble.
Stacey's mixed message is the real story of residential rehabilitation.
The Ontario government recently pledged $378 million in new funding for addiction therapy programs like the ones at Bellwood, which is operated by Edgewood Health Network (EHN) Canada. This investment in "new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment Hubs, will support community safety, along with the recovery of those struggling with opioid addiction," Health Minister and Deputy Premier Sylvia Jones said in a press release last month. It's the kind of boost that could let more people like Stacey finally reclaim their lives.
But all that new money is coming with a catch. At the same time as the government is pumping new cash into rehab, it is slashing funding from another pillar of drug recovery: harm reduction.
In August, the Ford government announced that it was shutting down 10 of the province's 23 safe consumption sites, including all that sit within 200 metres of a school or child care centre, and prohibiting all new sites and safe supply initiatives. The government says the move was driven by concern for public safety.
"It's the worst thing that could ever happen to a community to have one of these safe injection sites in their neighbourhood," Premier Doug Ford said in August.
"(G)iving someone, an addict, a place to do their injections, we haven't seen it get better."
But advocates and those who work in the recovery community fear a focus only on rehab will mean abandoning more people with addictions to hopelessness and death.
"People are dying every day because they don't have access to life-saving services," said Dr. Christina Basedow, an addictions expert and operations manager at EHN Canada.
"It is very detrimental to the people suffering to pit (harm reduction) against rehab."
The downside of a strategy focused just on rehabilitation is not just about those who try rehab and fail to break their addictions. It begins with those who, for many reasons, struggle even to try.
Inside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, one of the harm reduction facilities the Ontario government has slated to close, Jen, 47, sits at a designated safe consumption station surrounded by all her possessions in small plastic bags.
Jen, whose dishevelled hair and puffy eyes betray sleepless nights living on the street, has weaned herself off heroin. But she continues to use other hard drugs.
As she mixed her crystal meth powder with sterile water and selected a syringe, the former musician raved about the site's services and staff.
"They have saved my life countless f-king times ... seen me at my absolute worst."
A heavy drug user since age 18, Jen said that she doesn't want to get sober. She chooses to use drugs to escape from her troubled past and cope with daily life. The thought of what will happen when South Riverdale closes terrifies her.
"We aren't going to have a place to go," she said, her voice rising in anger.
"I will wander in the streets and laneways. I will shoot up in public. I will die."
People familiar with addiction say harm reduction is not just for those who don't want rehab. Even people who complete therapy need its support.
"Every addict has their journey," said Tony Lehal, a Bellwood manager who was once a rehab patient himself. "There are many paths to recovery."
According to the Canadian Drug Rehab Directory, around about 75 per cent of adults will overcome their addictions. But most people need multiple attempts - an average of five - to recover fully.
The path of a man named Aaron reflects these statistics. From seventh grade, the 25-year-old trans man turned to drugs to mitigate feelings of emptiness, identity confusion and abandonment by his father. Over the years, Aaron smoked pot and partied with ecstasy, cocaine and ketamine. He used uppers to get him through the day, then drank himself to sleep.
Aaron was 19 when a drunk driver killed his mother. His substance abuse spiralled. Overwhelmed with grief, he harboured a tormenting suspicion that his actions had somehow led to the tragedy.
"She was my everything," he said, his chiselled jaw trembling slightly. "And I had been lying, stealing and cheating, not treating her well."
Aaron finally got help when his girlfriend gave him an ultimatum at age 21. He checked into Bellwood.
But his years of trauma and addiction took almost as long to unwind. He needed four residential stays to understand that he suffered from post-traumatic stress, learn that his addictions were killing him and master techniques to stay sober.
Today, Aaron lives at New Beginnings, a recovery home providing round-the-clock care. Group meetings, a case worker, a sponsor, a therapist and a mentor support him. He feels content and secure and is working as a yoga instructor.
But Aaron still has moments of crisis and knows how easy it is to relapse. He is infuriated by plans to close harm reduction sites, like Moss Park, that help people with an addiction who do relapse.
"Recovery is not a straight line," he said. "It's not linear."
People healing from addiction need places where they are safe and supported, Aaron said. "Without it, they risk failure and death."
The dimly lit entrance of Moss Park Consumption Treatment Services, another safe consumption site slated to close, feels a world away from the gleaming Toronto skyscrapers that flicker behind its graffiti-marked façade.
Inside, sitting in a crowded room pulsing with life, Chris Halls, 53, drew a picture of his cherished childhood dog on a community mural as he recounted 10 different rehabilitation programs that he has tried, including Bellwood, without success.
Today, Chris is a regular fentanyl user and has no plans to stop using drugs. He is dependent on the Moss Park centre to keep him safe.
"It would be a travesty to close this place, like closing homes for elderly adults," he said.
"Without it, people will die."
Last year, Moss Park staff saved 517 people from overdose deaths and removed more than 497 inappropriately discarded needles from the community, according to researcher Seff Pinch. Harm reduction centres serve people, like Chris, who choose drugs but they also save the lives of people who must wait for rehabilitation.
Moss Park made 55 referrals for in-patient detox over the last year, but only two clients received beds. Toronto's three million residents can access only 29 publicly funded, medically supervised detox beds, according to Pinch's research.
At privately run centres like Bellwood, detox and rehabilitation are connected. But in Toronto's public facilities, detox and rehab are separate programs, Pinch said.
Drug users must complete detox before applying for residential rehabilitation. Even after getting through detox, they often face weeks or months of waiting for admission to rehab.
There were 257 publicly funded drug treatment beds in Toronto in 2023, according to research by the Institute for Mental Health Policy Research at CAMH. Ontario's plans do not state how many new rehab spaces are planned for the new centres.
While drug users wait to get clean, safe consumption sites can help keep them alive.
"People in recovery feel vulnerable and have lower tolerance to the drugs they once used,"said Gabriella Skubincan of the South Riverdale Community Health Centre.
"Their risk of relapse and overdose is very high."
Over the past three months, as the government detailed plans for its new rehab centres, there were more than 800 overdose deaths in Ontario, according to the Office of the Chief Coroner. The province faces a stark dilemma: Although a focus on rehabilitation can rescue some individuals from the depths of dependency, advocates say that it could leave others in peril.
Ontario's drug users navigate tangled paths, each journey mirroring the complexity of addiction.
Today, Stacey celebrates sobriety at home with her husband while Aaron seeks solace in therapy.
Jen confronts a fresh crisis, her tent stolen, and she turns to meth for relief.
Chris is burdened by uncertainty and fears for his future.
"I don't know why I am an addict, but I can't change it. I just am."
CREDIT: Katharine Lake Berz Special to the Star

Subject: Drugs; Rehabilitation; Addictions; Harm reduction

Location: Canada

Document 4 of 500

Let's talk honestly about police budgets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lets-talk-honestly-about-police-budgets/docview/3134456150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Let%27s+talk+honestly+about+police+budgets&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An extraordinary thing happened the other week.
Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish stood up to an ever-increasing police budget, perhaps the highest-profile Canadian politician to do so in recent memory. By standing up she actually stood down, quitting the Peel Region police board over a 21 per cent budget hike of $131.7 million.
Parrish said she couldn't wear two hats: board membership where she is required to pass the budget, and also representing the people of Mississauga who are facing other big problems, like food security and housing crises. Ultimately, she admitted her resignation wouldn't keep it from being passed, but she demonstrated that the police budget doesn't exist in a vacuum.
If money goes to the police, it doesn't go to somewhere else. Other problems, some of which are certainly related to the need for police, may go unattended or get less attention than they need. Other services may shrink. The problem is we never seem to be able to talk honestly about all this.
Despite often taking up the biggest portion of a municipality's total budget, attempts to halt or slow down the exponential growth of police budgets are met with fierce, even bullying, opposition from police, sometimes from management and union alike. It's unlike how any other city department behaves, even critical ones that save lives or provide life-giving services and safety.
Parrish's Toronto counterpart, Mayor Olivia Chow, faced this last winter during the run-up to the annual city budget debate when the chief of police as well as the Toronto Police Association (the union) mounted a forceful public relations campaign that implied people should be scared if the budget was not increased and that Toronto could become very dangerous.
It was bullying rather than open dialogue, with shades of previous unseemly campaigns like Operation True Blue in 2000 where the union tried to raise money to unseat politicians critical of police.
Ultimately, the campaign was a success and city council passed the 2023 budget giving police the $12.6 million they requested, a figure the police board approved earlier. In some ways Chow and council's capitulation to the police is understandable: considering their behaviour last year and years ago, who needs or wants a fight with the cops if they're going to fight like that?
It's in the public interest and the duty of politicians to insist on a good faith and open discussion, though. Good faith is a term I use a lot in this column, as it's missing from so much public discourse these days. Like many Canadian government departments or divisions, police operations are largely opaque. We're to trust they're operating as efficiently and properly as possible. It was only a few years ago that Toronto police started releasing budgets with some clearer line items so the public could get a better idea of where our money is going.
We're also to trust reviews that often happen internally, without public scrutiny. The recent tribunal hearings around Supt. Stacy Clarke's conduct was a rare high-profile window into one. In August, my colleague Edward Keenan asked, in exasperation about this and other cases, "Just what does a cop have to do to get fired in this town?"
It's interesting that this pushback on the police budget is happening in Mississauga. The 21 per cent hike is magnitudes larger than Toronto's last year, but the dynamics are different, too.
Mississauga is served by Peel Regional Police, which covers municipalities like Brampton, too, so the politics are dispersed among local and regional councils. On Thursday, Peel regional council - including Parrish - passed a motion directing Peel police to "review and reconsider" its budget, with an eye to reducing it.
Parrish is also a politician who has, let's just say, followed the beat of her own drum. Unique in political positions and temperament, for better or worse (and Mississauga voters decided she was for the better last June). Perhaps it takes an iconoclast like Parrish, who doesn't seem bound by conventional political instincts and propriety, to create room for real conversations about police budgets, oversight and transparency.
To be clear, what Parrish has done is not radical, not part of any "defund the police" move - just insisting on a real conversation.
Contrast this with her mayoral predecessor, Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, who announced last week that she would "fund the police," seemingly rejecting any notion of defunding.
Precisely worded, along with making the announcement itself in a speech to the Police Association of Ontario, which represents associations across the province, there also doesn't seem much room for open discussion either.
That's a shame because premiers have incredible power over cities, and can drive police reform and shape budget considerations. If quitting the police board doesn't change anything, another question to ask might be, "Just what does it take to have an honest conversation about police budgets around here?"

Subject: Police reform; Mayors; Councils; Budgets; Politics; Transparency; Resignations; Police; Budgeting

Parkside camera vandalized again: 'Somebody's on a mission,' says resident after photo radar pole was cut down for a second time

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parkside-camera-vandalized-again/docview/3134456010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parkside+camera+vandalized+again%3A+%27Somebody%27s+on+a+mission%2C%27+says+resident+after+photo+radar+pole+was+cut+down+for+a+second+time&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Who is vandalizing the photo radar camera meant to slow drivers on Parkside Drive?
That's what nearby residents in the High Park area were asking Saturday after they awoke to see the city's automated speed enforcement device lying sideways on the ground - its pole sheered at the base - one day after being reinstalled following the same vandalism in mid-November.
Robert Nowacki, who lives across the street, said a previous on-ground incarnation of the photo radar box was repeatedly vandalized, including somebody using spray paint to block the lens and scrawl the word "tax" on its side.
"Now they've done it again - you can't win, somebody's on a mission. A tax isn't how I see it. I would rather see drivers go a little slower here," he said, gesturing at cars whizzing past on Parkside Drive.
The street, which runs along the east side of High Park, connects Bloor Street West to Lake Shore Boulevard and the Gardiner Expressway, and, in the past decade, has seen seven collisions with people either killed or seriously injured. Another 1,480 crashes with vehicle-only damage have occurred on Parkside Drive since 2014.
Nowacki said there is no obvious suspect. "I wish I had set up a camcorder in my window," he said, adding: "If it keeps happening, maybe that's what I'll do."
The first photo radar device on Parkside, south of Algonquin Avenue, was installed in April 2022. It was part of a city program that in July 2020 started snapping photos of speeding vehicles and mailing tickets to the owners in a bid to slow motorists in school and safety zones.
The Parkside photo radar became the busiest in the city, nabbing more motorists than the other 74 devices.
While no longer the busiest camera, a total of 63,633 tickets had been issued from photos snapped at that location by September, according to the latest city data.
Faraz Gholizadeh, co-chair of the community group Safe Parkside, said it appeared that once again somebody used a powered cutting tool to fell the camera, which had been reinstalled only hours earlier by the company contracted to supply and maintain them.
"My guess is maybe they're one of the people that got caught by the speed camera and they're unhappy that they are contributing to the $6.8 million it generated, but I'm just guessing at this point," he said.
Although it might be possible to vandal-proof the camera, that would not necessarily help its goal: to slow drivers and make them less of a danger to community members including kids, Gholizadeh said.
"People are still speeding day and night on what is essentially a highway running through a neighbourhood," he said, adding that physical changes to the street design are needed.
"The community, Toronto residents, they voted for our councillors, for our mayor with that (Vision Zero) goal in mind," he said. "We want to be a sustainable city, we want to be a safe city and we're just not seeing that action to the degree that we've been calling for. It's past time the city got into gear and started making the changes that we've been asking them to make for many years now."
City council voted to explore additional safety measures on Parkside in 2021, after a chain-reaction crash killed two seniors. City staff later released a two-kilometre redesign plan that would remove one of two southbound traffic lanes and replace it with a two-way cycle track on the west side from Bloor to Lake Shore.
The fate of that plan is in doubt, however, after Premier Doug Ford blamed bike lanes for worsening traffic congestion and his government passed a law last week giving them a veto over any proposed municipal bike lanes that affect vehicular traffic.
In an email, city staff said Toronto "condemns all acts of theft and vandalism," adding that the camera supplier is assessing the latest damage and planning repairs.
The area's city councillor, Gord Perks, urged the camera vandal to stop, adding, "There is very good evidence that speeds have been reduced as a result of cameras and other interventions we've made."
Mayor Olivia Chow, in an email, said "acts of vandalism that jeopardize the safety of Torontonians are entirely inappropriate, particularly on a dangerous section of road like Parkside Dr. That's why Councillor Perks has worked closely with the community to spearhead safety improvements on Parkside Drive, which we passed at our most recent council meeting."
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Subject: Vandalism; Cameras; Community; Automobile drivers; Traffic

Document 6 of 500

'We have nothing to lose': A year after winning Liberal leadership, Crombie struggling to find her footing as election looms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-have-nothing-lose/docview/3134456008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+have+nothing+to+lose%27%3A+A+year+after+winning+Liberal+leadership%2C+Crombie+struggling+to+find+her+footing+as+election+looms&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's 29 kilometres from Mississauga city hall to Queen's Park. But the journey, like the traffic, is complicated.
Bonnie Crombie admits she didn't realize that at the time.
Now, much sooner than expected, the former Mississauga mayor who won the leadership of Ontario's struggling Liberals a year ago this weekend is girding for an early election against Premier Doug Ford.
"When I got here, I thought, 'I'm experienced, I'm a polished leader, you know? Seventy-eight per cent of Mississauga voted for me,' " says Crombie, a target of Progressive Conservative attack ads.
"I thought it would be easier. It's been tough," adds Crombie, who commands a small caucus of nine MPPs. They hold third place in the 124-seat legislature, where she has yet to run for a seat.
"There's a lot of work to do, and I'm up for it."
Several Liberal insiders contacted by the Star agreed the party's comeback bid faces challenges. And the latest polling from Abacus Data suggests Crombie is "treading water" with voters, says chief executive David Coletto.
In the first poll after she became leader, conducted in late January, the Liberals were at 27 per cent support, four percentage points higher than this month's Abacus survey.
"Her leadership alone hasn't had an effect of closing the gap with Ford," Coletto says, noting the unpopularity of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his federal Liberals is a drag on the Ontario party.
Hoping to break out, the provincial Liberals recently unveiled their first campaign ad punching back at the premier, with Crombie telling voters they deserve "better."
Ford ahead despite scandal
Ford's Tories are running well ahead of opposition parties even with an ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, 2.5 million Ontarians lacking a family doctor, long waits in overcrowded hospitals, controversial bike lane closures and homeless encampments.
"Doug Ford has had six years to fix it," Crombie says.
If Ford calls an early election, it could provide Crombie and the Liberals the opening they are looking for, says Liberal strategist Dan Moulton.
That's because sending Ontarians to the polls early - instead of waiting for the next scheduled vote in 2026 - is a risk for Ford, Moulton says.
"It's a gamble. It's not a great time to be an incumbent government anywhere in the world ... it may not be the walk in the park that he had in 2022, post-pandemic. It's a different environment now. Voters are angry," he adds.
"There's opportunity there for Bonnie and the Liberals."
The latest Abacus poll for the Star two weeks ago suggests 45 per cent of voters believe it's time for a change in government, but that alone is not enough to flip the balance of power at Queen's Park.
Ford's Conservatives enjoy 43 per cent support, the Liberals 23 per cent, the official opposition NDP under Marit Stiles are at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens are at five per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 people from Nov. 15 to 19 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. Opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, but for a random sample of this size it would be plus or minus 3.1 per cent, 19 times out of 20.
"I have no illusions about where we stand in the polls," says Crombie, who will host her first leader's fundraising dinner Tuesday night at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.
Liberals do not underestimate the premier by any means.
"This guy is really good. Things seem to break his way," says one party stalwart, speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations.
"Bonnie's got an urgent challenge. She needs to create buzz so she can recruit 25 winning candidates. Right now, that's really difficult."
That's a reference to the number of seats some Liberals believe Crombie will need to gain in an election as early as next spring to leapfrog the New Democrats into second place in the legislature.
So far, recruitment efforts have yielded several notable candidates in ridings the Liberals hope to take back. For example: Vince Gasparro, formerly a senior aide to Toronto mayor John Tory and prime minister Paul Martin; hospital executive Lee Fairclough, who came close to winning Etobicoke-Lakeshore in 2022; and former deputy Toronto police chief Peter Yuen in Scarborough-Agincourt.
The Liberals saw their support improve dramatically in a September byelection to replace former Ford cabinet minister Todd Smith in Bay of Quinte. They took one third of the ballots to 39 per cent for the victorious Tory, down from 49 per cent under Smith.
But the Liberal party is far from the political powerhouse it was for almost 15 years in government until a crushing 2018 defeat and a disappointing 2022 loss that confirmed there would be no easy rebound.
While the Liberals received more votes than the New Democrats in the last election, those ballots did not translate into seats because NDP support is concentrated in certain ridings, which helped them win 31.
"This party is like a start-up. There is no infrastructure. There is no money," says another Liberal veteran, maintaining the "draft Crombie" movement that lured her from a comfortable perch running Ontario's third-largest city underplayed the challenge.
Instead, Crombie is running Ontario's third party.
"I think Bonnie got sold a bill of goods. It's not an easy job. If they're in third place one more time, I don't know what the future is," adds the veteran Liberal.
Crombie is in the "challenging" position of needing to take seats from the NDP as she moves the Liberals to the centre with promises of an income-tax cut to entice voters who have shifted to the Conservatives, says Coletto.
"She has to build a very difficult coalition," adds the pollster, who maintains it will be tough for either the Liberals or NDP to break out because they are splitting the anti-Ford vote.
Speaking of the NDP, "there's seats there that, if you ignore them, you'll be the next one not to fill the minivan," warns Flavio Volpe, a senior Liberal staffer at Queen's Park during the McGuinty years who is now president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association.
Liberals have been dubbed the "minivan" caucus because of their nine MPPs, three fewer than needed for official party status in the legislature that would give them millions in taxpayer funding for staff and operations.
Looking back on her first 12 months as leader and ahead to the biggest fight of her political career, Crombie is candid.
"I was really comfortable right at the beginning. I had a lot of confidence. And then I realized, maybe I don't know what I don't know," says the one-time Mississauga-Streetsville Liberal MP from 2008 to 2011.
After almost a decade wearing the mayor's chain of office and projecting authority, Crombie has concluded that persona does not translate to the new job, something her advisers also acknowledge.
"This is a much different ballpark ... there's a lot to learn," adds Crombie, sitting in the Liberal caucus boardroom with its unglamorous view of a back parking lot at Queen's Park.
"They're telling me to be more my authentic self, be more conversational ... I just need to be me."
There has been surprise both inside and outside the party that she has not been a more adroit political performer, but some question whether that's a fair expectation because the leap to the higher stakes of provincial politics is an adjustment.
"I think it surprised a lot of Liberals who thought they were getting something different," says the party veteran who acknowledges Crombie has appeal. "She's still got that X factor."
Indeed, Crombie was the strongest performer among her leadership rivals: Liberal MPs Nate Erskine-Smith and Yasir Naqvi and Grit MPP Ted Hsu.
But, unlike the mayors of Toronto, Ottawa or Hamilton, the mayor of Mississauga - despite the city's size - does not regularly face a phalanx of newspaper, TV, radio and digital reporters.
"When she's in a scrum at Queen's Park and questions are flying at her it's slightly out of her comfort zone. She's getting better at it," says one adviser, who points out "there's not a big press gallery at Mississauga city hall."
That's why some at Queen's Park are surprised the Liberals haven't urged Crombie to be at the legislature more often.
Since her leadership victory last Dec. 2, she has been spending long days on the road travelling the province to meet voters, speak to community groups, find candidates, raise campaign cash and resuscitate riding associations.
Earlier this month, Crombie began ramping up efforts to reach voters, sending a signal she's returning the party - which drifted left under former premier Kathleen Wynne - back toward the centre.
Promised tax cut
To that end, Crombie has promised a 22 per cent cut to middle-class income taxes- similar to what Ford pledged in 2018 but hasn't delivered. It's also intended to distance her from Trudeau's Liberals.
In last May's Milton byelection, voters told Tory canvassers at the doorstep they were looking forward to defeating the federal Liberals. The provincial Grits privately acknowledge that Trudeau's lack of popularity hurt them.
That's one reason Crombie invited Trudeau critics like former federal health minister Jane Philpott and former British Columbia premier Christy Clark to address the party's London policy convention in September.
Looking ahead, her campaign branding emphasizes "Team Bonnie" over the Liberal name and is signalling the election promises to come will be aimed at attracting centrist voters.
"We're focusing on the basics, because when you get the basics right, everything else will fall into place," says Crombie.
She is keenly aware Democrats lost the U.S. presidential election because "they didn't pay enough attention to affordability ... to meeting people's needs."
"We have nothing to lose. We're going to be very bold," Crombie adds in the conference room that is just a floor below and a short walk from the premier's corner office at the legislature.
"I feel I am the right person to do this, but it's a big hill to climb."
CAPTION: "I thought it would be easier. It's been tough," says Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, whose party sits well behind Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the polls. "There's a lot of work to do, and I'm up for it."Crombie celebrates after winning the leadership last December.Richard Lautens Toronto StarR.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

"We want to be a sustainable city, we want to be a safe city and we're just not seeing that action to the degree that we've been calling for. It's past time the city got into gear and started making the changes that we've been asking them to make for many years now."

The fate of that plan is in doubt, however, after Premier Doug Ford blamed bike lanes for worsening traffic congestion and his government passed a law this past week giving them a veto over any proposed municipal bike lanes that affect vehicular traffic.

Document 8 of 500

Ontario is backing rehab instead of safe drug injection sites. Those who know the system say both are needed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-is-backing-rehab-instead-safe-drug/docview/3134327172/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+is+backing+rehab+instead+of+safe+drug+injection+sites.+Those+who+know+the+system+say+both+are+needed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One sunny afternoon, just weeks after Ontario announced its controversial plan to shift gears on its approach to the toxic drugs crisis that has claimed more than 10,000 lives in four years, a cluster of men and women laughed and chatted outside a window-lined complex on the edge of an East York forest.
Some residents of the Bellwood residential addiction treatment centre appeared barely out of their teens, while others could be grandparents. A few women walked on wooded trails while two men steered wheelchairs on paved paths.
Inside, it felt like the first week of school. Recovering drug users were nervous, moody and excited, wondering what activities to sign up for and how to fit in. A 50-year-old woman named Stacey bounded in the front door and embraced the first staff member she found.
"I want everyone to know what I learned," she told the staff member as she held her in her arms.
Rehabilitation saved Stacey's life. Now four months sober, the retired business manager is determined never to return to the addiction she once suffered.
"This place has been life-changing for me," she said, trembling with emotion. "I am so grateful that I could come here."
But later that afternoon, as clouds dimmed the Ontario sky, Stacey said something else. As she celebrated the wonder of having earned a new life, she worried about others who had not found redemption. Some had not put in the work needed to succeed. Others had put in the work but were still in trouble.
Stacey's mixed message is the real story of residential rehabilitation.
The Ontario government recently pledged $378 million in new funding for addiction therapy programs like the ones at Bellwood, which is operated by Edgewood Health Network (EHN) Canada. This investment in "new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment Hubs, will support community safety, along with the recovery of those struggling with opioid addiction," Health Minister and Deputy Premier Sylvia Jones said in a press release this month. It's the kind of boost that could let more people like Stacey finally reclaim their lives.
But all that new money is coming with a catch. At the same time as the government is pumping new cash into rehab, it is slashing funding from another pillar of drug recovery: harm reduction.
In August, the Ford government announced that it was shutting down 10 of the province's 23 safe consumption sites, including all that sit within 200 metres of a school or childcare centre, and prohibiting all new sites and safe supply initiatives. The government says the move was driven by concern for public safety.
"It's the worst thing that could ever happen to a community to have one of these safe injection sites in their neighbourhood," Premier Doug Ford said in August.
"(G)iving someone, an addict, a place to do their injections, we haven't seen it get better."
But advocates and those who work in the recovery community fear a focus only on rehab will mean abandoning more people with addictions to hopelessness and death.
"People are dying every day because they don't have access to life-saving services," said Dr. Christina Basedow, an addictions expert and operations manager at EHN Canada.
"It is very detrimental to the people suffering to pit (harm reduction) against rehab."
The downside of a strategy focused just on rehabilitation is not just about those who try rehab and fail to break their addictions. It begins with those who, for many reasons, struggle even to try.
Inside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, one of the harm reduction facilities the Ontario government has slated to close, Jen, 47, sits at a designated safe consumption station surrounded by all her possessions in small plastic bags.
Jen, whose dishevelled hair and puffy eyes betray sleepless nights living on the street, has weaned herself off heroin. But she continues to use other hard drugs.
As she mixed her crystal meth powder with sterile water and selected a syringe, the former musician raved about the site's services and staff.
"They have saved my life countless f--king times ... seen me at my absolute worst."
A heavy drug user since age 18, Jen said that she doesn't want to get sober. She chooses to use drugs to escape from her troubled past and cope with daily life. The thought of what will happen when South Riverdale closes terrifies her.
"We aren't going to have a place to go," she said, her voice rising in anger.
"I will wander in the streets and laneways. I will shoot up in public. I will die."
People familiar with addiction say harm reduction is not just for those who don't want rehab. Even people who complete therapy need its support.
"Every addict has their journey," said Tony Lehal, a Bellwood manager who was once a rehab patient himself. "There are many paths to recovery."
According to the Canadian Drug Rehab Directory, around about 75 per cent of adults will overcome their addictions. But most people need multiple attempts - an average of five - to recover fully.
The path of a man named Aaron reflects these statistics. From seventh grade, the 25-year-old trans man turned to drugs to mitigate feelings of emptiness, identity confusion and abandonment by his father. Over the years, Aaron smoked pot and partied with ecstasy, cocaine and ketamine. He used uppers to get him through the day, then drank himself to sleep.
Aaron was 19 when a drunk driver killed his mother. His substance abuse spiralled. Overwhelmed with grief, he harboured a tormenting suspicion that his actions had somehow led to the tragedy.
"She was my everything," he said, his chiselled jaw trembling slightly. "And I had been lying, stealing and cheating, not treating her well."
Aaron finally got help when his girlfriend gave him an ultimatum at age 21. He checked into Bellwood.
But his years of trauma and addiction took almost as long to unwind. He needed four residential stays to understand that he suffered from post-traumatic stress, learn that his addictions were killing him and master techniques to stay sober.
Today, Aaron lives at New Beginnings, a recovery home providing round-the-clock care. Group meetings, a case worker, a sponsor, a therapist and a mentor support him. He feels content and secure and is working as a yoga instructor.
But Aaron still has moments of crisis and knows how easy it is to relapse. He is infuriated by plans to close harm reduction sites, like Moss Park, that help people with an addiction who do relapse.
"Recovery is not a straight line," he said. "It's not linear."
People healing from addiction need places where they are safe and supported, Aaron said. "Without it, they risk failure and death."
The dimly lit entrance of Moss Park Consumption Treatment Services, another safe consumption site slated to close, feels a world away from the gleaming Toronto skyscrapers that flicker behind its graffiti-marked façade.
Inside, sitting in a crowded room pulsing with life, Chris Halls, 53, drew a picture of his cherished childhood dog on a community mural as he recounted 10 different rehabilitation programs that he has tried, including Bellwood, without success.
Today, Chris is a regular fentanyl user and has no plans to stop using drugs. He is dependent on the Moss Park centre to keep him safe.
"It would be a travesty to close this place, like closing homes for elderly adults," he said.
"Without it, people will die."
Last year, Moss Park staff saved 517 people from overdose deaths and removed more than 497 inappropriately discarded needles from the community, according to researcher Seff Pinch. Harm reduction centres serve people, like Chris, who choose drugs but they also save the lives of people who must wait for rehabilitation.
Moss Park made 55 referrals for in-patient detox over the last year, but only two clients received beds. Toronto's three million residents can access only 29 publicly funded, medically supervised detox beds, according to Pinch's research.
At privately run centres like Bellwood, detox and rehabilitation are connected. But in Toronto's public facilities, detox and rehab are separate programs, Pinch said.
Drug users must complete detox before applying for residential rehabilitation. Even after getting through detox, they often face weeks or months of waiting for admission to rehab.
There were 257 publicly funded drug treatment beds in Toronto in 2023, according to research by the Institute for Mental Health Policy Research at CAMH. Ontario's plans do not state how many new rehab spaces are planned for the new centres.
While drug users wait to get clean, safe consumption sites can help keep them alive.
"People in recovery feel vulnerable and have lower tolerance to the drugs they once used,"said Gabriella Skubincan of the South Riverdale Community Health Centre.
"Their risk of relapse and overdose is very high."
Over the past three months, as the government detailed plans for its new rehab centres, there were more than 800 overdose deaths in Ontario, according to the Office of the Chief Coroner. The province faces a stark dilemma: Although a focus on rehabilitation can rescue some individuals from the depths of dependency, advocates say that it could leave others in peril.
Ontario's drug users navigate tangled paths, each journey mirroring the complexity of addiction.
Today, Stacey celebrates sobriety at home with her husband while Aaron seeks solace in therapy.
Jen confronts a fresh crisis, her tent stolen, and she turns to meth for relief.
Chris is burdened by uncertainty and fears for his future.
"I don't know why I am an addict, but I can't change it. I just am."
CAPTION: A 50-year-old woman named Stacey enters the Bellwood residential addiction treatment centre in Toronto.Aaron's years of trauma and addiction took almost as long to unwind. Aaron finally got help and checked into Bellwood residential addiction treatment centre after a girlfriend's ultimatum.Chris Halls prepares to shoot up at Moss Park Consumption Treatment Services.

Subject: Drugs; Rehabilitation; Addictions; Harm reduction

Document 9 of 500

Double-digit hikes? Fear-mongering? Resignations? Just what does it take to have an honest conversation about police budgets?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/double-digit-hikes-fear-mongering-resignations/docview/3134327162/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Double-digit+hikes%3F+Fear-mongering%3F+Resignations%3F+Just+what+does+it+take+to+have+an+honest+conversation+about+police+budgets%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An extraordinary thing happened last week.
Mississauga mayor Carolyn Parrish stood up to an ever-increasing police budget, perhaps the highest-profile Canadian politician to do so in recent memory. By standing up she actually stood down, quitting the Peel Region police board over a 21 per cent budget hike of $131.7 million.
Parrish said she couldn't wear two hats: board membership where she is required to pass the budget, and also representing the people of Mississauga who are facing other big problems, like food security and housing crises. Ultimately, she admitted her resignation wouldn't keep it from being passed, but she demonstrated that the police budget doesn't exist in a vacuum.
If money goes to the police, it doesn't go to somewhere else. Other problems, some of which are certainly related to the need for police, may go unattended or get less attention than they need. Other services may shrink. The problem is we never seem to be able to talk honestly about all this.
Despite often taking up the biggest portion of a municipality's total budget, attempts to halt or slow down the exponential growth of police budgets are met with fierce, even bullying, opposition from police, sometimes from management and union alike. It's unlike how any other city department behaves, even critical ones that save lives or provide life-giving services and safety.
Parrish's Toronto counterpart, Mayor Olivia Chow, faced this last winter during the run up to the annual city budget debate when the chief of police as well as the Toronto Police Association (the union) mounted a forceful public relations campaign that implied people should be scared if the budget was not increased and that Toronto could become very dangerous.
It was bullying rather than open dialogue, with shades of previous unseemly campaigns like Operation True Blue in 2000 where the union tried to raise money to unseat politicians critical of police.
Ultimately, the campaign was a success and city council passed the 2023 budget giving police the $12.6 million they requested, a figure the police board approved earlier. In some ways Chow and council's capitulation to the police is understandable: considering their behaviour last year and years ago, who needs or wants a fight with the cops if they're going to fight like that?
It's in the public interest and the duty of politicians to insist on a good faith and open discussion though. Good faith is a term I use a lot in this column as it's missing from so much public discourse these days. Like many Canadian government departments or divisions, police operations are largely opaque. We're to trust they're operating as efficiently and properly as possible. It was only a few years ago that Toronto police started releasing budgets with some clearer line items so the public could get a better idea of where our money is going.
We're also to trust reviews that often happen internally, without public scrutiny. The recent tribunal hearings around Supt. Stacy Clarke's conduct was a rare high-profile window into one. In August, my colleague Edward Keenan asked, in exasperation about this and other cases, "Just what does a cop have to do to get fired in this town?"
It's interesting that this push back on the police budget is happening in Mississauga. The 21 per cent hike is magnitudes larger than Toronto's last year, but the dynamics are different too. Mississauga is served by Peel Regional Police, which covers municipalities like Brampton too, so the politics are dispersed among local and regional councils. On Thursday, Peel Region council - including Parrish - passed a motion directing Peel police to "review and reconsider" its budget, with an eye to reducing it.
Parrish is also a politician who has, let's just say, followed the beat of her own drum. Unique in political positions and temperament, for better or worse (and Mississauga voters decided she was for the better last June). Perhaps it takes an iconoclast like Parrish, who doesn't seem bound by conventional political instincts and propriety, to create room for real conversations about police budgets, oversight and transparency.
To be clear, what Parrish has done is not radical, not part of any "defund the police" move - just insisting on a real conversation. Contrast this with her mayoral predecessor, Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, who announced last week that she would "fund the police," seemingly rejecting any notion of defunding. Precisely worded, along with making the announcement itself in a speech to the Police Association of Ontario, which represents associations across the province, there also doesn't seem much room for open discussion either.
That's a shame because premiers have incredible power over cities, and can drive police reform and shape budget considerations. If quitting the police board doesn't change anything, another question to ask might be, "Just what does it take to have an honest conversation about police budgets around here?"
CAPTION: Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish quit the Peel Region police board over a 21 per cent budget hike of $131.7 million.

Subject: Police reform; Mayors; Councils; Budgets; Politics

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Title: Double-digit hikes? Fear-mongering? Resignations? Just what does it take to have an honest conversation about police budgets?

Document 10 of 500

What this piece of Toronto history tells us about Doug Ford's battle with bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-this-piece-toronto-history-tells-us-about/docview/3134327155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+this+piece+of+Toronto+history+tells+us+about+Doug+Ford%27s+battle+with+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When talking about bike lanes, the prominent urban planner Brent Toderian is fond of saying that it's hard to justify the decision to build a bridge by looking at the number of people swimming across a river.
Thinking this over in Toronto's current situation got me considering the maxim a little too literally - about a Toronto decision to build an actual bridge, and what that might teach us. But I'll come back to that in a minute.
First, a couple of things I think about the Great Bike Lane War of 2024, in which Premier Doug Ford is preparing to rip out a bunch of protected cycle tracks across Toronto:
1) The section of the Bloor bike lanes that extend west of South Kingsway into Etobicoke is one where there is a good-faith debate to be had about whether their use justifies the disruption they cause. Car traffic there was an absolute clownshow during their installation, and remains noticeably worse than before. If you travel that stretch regularly, you hardly ever see any bikes in those lanes.
2) The opposite is true of the rest of the Bloor lanes, where from High Park through to downtown, cars mostly move just fine (by Toronto standards) and the bike lanes are often lined up with riders four or six bikes deep, slowing each other down. Meanwhile, on University Avenue, car traffic is as heavy as it always was, but it isn't even that bad - during the day, cars are moving along at or close to the speed limit between traffic lights, while the pinch points seem to be caused by Ontario Line subway construction. Those bike lanes are full of people using the road as a great feeder route into downtown. Removing lanes in these places is not about traffic congestion - it's a politically calculated act of antagonism.
Now, back to the Etobicoke section: acknowledging the common-sense logic of those who resent them, I still wonder if we've had enough time to see if those bike lanes would justify their own existence. I have seen areas with that kind of suburban character where cycling has become popular when it's a safe option - especially for local trips to the store, high school students getting to school, families travelling to the park for sports - for neighbourhood stuff.
And also for getting downtown: in Paris, which has ambitiously installed cycling infrastructure over the past decade, more commuters coming from the suburban outskirts of the city into the downtown now travel by bicycle than by car, according to a study by L'Institut Paris Région. This is a massive transformation in travel patterns from five years earlier.
This is the planning ahead that building out a transportation network involves. You might say you build a bridge for the people who might cross the river there in the future, not the people who currently swim across.
Which got me thinking about the 1913-18 construction of the actual Bloor Viaduct across the Don River, one of the most iconic pieces of infrastructure in Toronto.
When it was proposed, it was controversial (defeated in three referendums before finally being approved), because it was expensive and because it would be a "bridge to nowhere," since the area east of the Don River was so sparsely populated. As was the area west of the river: a report outlined in the Star in 1910 suggested that Bloor Street East would likely never be "a great business street."
There's a Toronto archives photo of that bridge at rush hour in 1919, one year after it opened, that shows it backed up with bumper-to-bumper traffic. Which may offer a lesson that some things about Toronto traffic never change, and may also be a lesson about the concept of "induced demand," which says the amount of car traffic will expand to fill all available road space. But it also shows that "bridge to nowhere" was quickly part of making the area "somewhere."
That bridge connecting the east end of Toronto to the west was essential to the growth of the city. It spurred massive residential and commercial development. Under the road was a platform designed to carry subway cars - on a line that would not open for another 50 years.
The city builders of the time had the foresight to build for the city Toronto would become.
I don't think some protected cycle paths stretching into Etobicoke represent that same level of city-building ambition. But I do think it's at least possible, as part of a network and given time for people to consider them as an option, they could eventually help spur an equally difficult-to-imagine transformation in the city.
And I do wish we had someone in charge who could even comprehend the concept of building for the city we'll be in the future, instead of unbuilding in attempt to get back to the emptier streets of the past.
CAPTION: In this photo from 1919, cars are stuck in a traffic jam on the Danforth Viaduct - only a year after a structure once dismissed as a "bridge to nowhere" first opened.

Subject: Automobiles; Traffic; Bridges; Bicycling

Location: Don River

Document 11 of 500

The greatest political deception of our time is unfolding: Billionaires recasted as populist revolutionaries.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/greatest-political-deception-our-time-is/docview/3134270509/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+greatest+political+deception+of+our+time+is+unfolding%3A+Billionaires+recasted+as+populist+revolutionaries.&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's just one thing I'd hope to bring back from the 'decade of greed', Nov. 23
Charlie Angus' analysis of the 1980s "greed is good" neo-con ideology provides essential context for understanding how figures like Donald Trump and Elon Musk have masterfully positioned themselves as populist champions of the working class while advancing policies that primarily benefit the ultra-wealthy. Clearly the greatest political deception of our time is unfolding before us: billionaires recasting themselves as populist revolutionaries.
Current history reveals a perverse inversion of traditional class warfare. Instead of the underprivileged rising against wealth and power, today's so-called "revolutionaries" are billionaires, cleverly portraying teachers, social workers, and minimum-wage earners as the "elite." This would be laughable if it weren't so effective - and so painful.
Conservative strategist David Frum, who made his name as a countercultural critic of the progressive equalitarian 1960s human rights revolution, once revealed the neo conservative playbook states that equality should never be the goal of conservatives. Actually, this philosophy of inequality, rooted in Milton Friedman's trickle-down free market economics, has devastated American manufacturing while enriching global corporations. Now, the very Republican ideologues who gutted the middle-class campaign as saviours, promising to fix the problems they created. The script has flipped - the wealthiest among us now pose as freedom fighters battling an imagined "elite" of teachers, social workers, and minimum-wage earners.
Consequently, what's so upsetting to those paying attention is how the "have-mores" have convinced the "have-nots" that the enemy isn't billionaires hoarding wealth but anyone advocating for a fairer system. This so-called populist revolution elevates reality TV celebrities, social media influencers, and billionaire demagogues to positions of power, sidelining educated and credentialed experts from making critical life-and-death decisions. It's the MAGA playbook in action, where ignorance is strength, and the result is deeper social injustice. But that's the system people voted for, swayed by voices like Jordan Peterson's, who frame social justice warriors as woke and the greatest threat to society. Imagine that: social justice, rebranded as a menace to society. Not being greedy is a vice.
As Canadians approach crucial elections, we must learn from the American experience. When Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre position themselves as voices of the common people, we must ask whose interests they truly serve. The stakes are too high to fall for this neo-con scam once again.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
CAPTION: Toronto Star illustration using photos from Tony Bock, Ken Faught and dreamstime.

Subject: Social justice; Social workers; Populism; Influencer marketing

Business indexing term: Subject: Influencer marketing

Title: The greatest political deception of our time is unfolding: Billionaires recasted as populist revolutionaries.

Title: news quiz

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: OP.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 30, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper
