Document 13 of 500

PM meets with Trump in Florida: Idea came from Trudeau's request amid tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-meets-with-trump-florida/docview/3134268334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+meets+with+Trump+in+Florida%3A+Idea+came+from+Trudeau%27s+request+amid+tariff+threat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and a handful of his top advisers flew to Florida to dine with president-elect Donald Trump and members of his team Friday night, but it was unclear if the surprise summit would lead Trump to drop his threat of tariffs on Canadian exports.
Trudeau, accompanied by Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc, and chief of staff Katie Telford, had dinner at Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort in West Palm Beach, according to a Canadian source, on a weekend in which many Americans celebrate Thanksgiving.
The face-to-face meeting came at Trudeau's suggestion, said the Canadian source with knowledge of their communications.
It is the first meeting between the president-elect and a foreign leader, and comes just under two months before Trump is to be inaugurated as U.S. president. It also comes just days after Trump threatened to impose a 25-per-cent tariff on America's three biggest trading partners, Canada, Mexico and China, with an additional 10 per cent slapped on China (possibly on top of an earlier vow to impose 60 per cent tariffs on China).
At the dinner table Friday night were key Trump advisers North Dakota Gov. Doug Burgum, nominated for Interior Secretary who will lead Trump's effort to open federal lands to more oil and gas drilling, and Commerce Secretary nominee Howard Lutnick, a staunch enthusiast of tariffs, and Trump's nominee for national security adviser Mike Waltz. Other guests included Pennsylvania senator-elect Dave McCormick and his wife, Dina Powell, a former Trump deputy national security adviser.
Canada's ambassador to Washington Kirsten Hillman and Trudeau's deputy chief of staff Brian Clow were not at the Mar-a-Lago dinner but had travelled to Florida to accompany the Canadian delegation.
On Monday night after Trump's threat, Trudeau called the president-elect, and officials reported they had a constructive conversation, but with no resolution of Canada's concerns.
Earlier Friday, without tipping his hand, Trudeau said in Prince Edward Island that what is "important to understand is that Donald Trump, when he makes statements like that, he plans on carrying them out. There's no question about it.
"Our responsibility is to point out that in this way, he would actually not just be harming Canadians, who work so well with the United States, he would actually be raising prices for American citizens as well, and hurting American industry and businesses."
Trudeau added, "ultimately, it is through lots of constructive, real conversations with President Trump that I'm going to have that will keep us moving forward on the right track for all Canadians."
The Friday evening Mar-a-Lago high-stakes meeting occurred out of the public eye.
Canadian media were not aware of Trudeau's surprise trip until flight trackers alerted the departure of the prime minister's plane headed south to Florida.
That is in stark contrast to Trump and Trudeau's first meeting at the White House in February, 2017 which was a major bilateral summit shortly after Trump took power for his first term, at which border security was also front and centre.
The presence of LeBlanc, the minister responsible for border security, is aimed at countering Trump's concerns laid out in his online statement that "thousands of people are pouring through Mexico and Canada, bringing Crime and Drugs at levels never seen before."
LeBlanc said this week that the Trudeau government is prepared to beef up border security, with more money for technology, drones and more Mounties and border guards on the 49th parallel. But LeBlanc is also a hail-fellow-well-met political actor who is trusted by Trudeau, and gets along well with his ideological opposites, like Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
Trump's Monday night tariff threat immediately sent the value of the Canadian dollar and the Mexican peso into a fall, and saw economists predict it could trigger a recession in this country.
The relationship between the two leaders has been front and centre since Trump's re-election, and since his statement posted on Truth Social prompted an emergency first ministers' meeting.
"I'm surprised and impressed," said Flavio Volpe, head of Canada's Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, that the Canadian leader was meeting with the president-elect.
He said the kind of personal facetime with the U.S. Republican president heading into office is invaluable for Trudeau, and for Canada in the months ahead.
"The PM is demonstrating the humility that's required (by) going to the source, also the prime minister is demonstrating to the contrary of all the predictions, that Donald Trump wouldn't want to work with him is untrue."
Volpe, whose industry is threatened by Trump's threat of 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian and Mexican products, said "nothing final" may come of the meeting between the two, however he said the federal government may get a sense of what the next steps could be.
"The threat came through social media which doesn't have a basis in law. Maybe what we get out of this is what legislation he (Trump) will consult and what he's talking about."
And likewise, said Flavio, with LeBlanc - Canada's minister responsible for border security - on board, Trump will get a good look at what Canada is doing at the border, which will allow Trudeau to argue to ease the threat.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had dinner at U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's Mar-a-Lagoresortin West Palm Beach Friday.Saul Loeb AFP via Getty Images file photo

CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 14 of 500

Canada's weak leaders meekly bow to mad king

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-weak-leaders-meekly-bow-mad-king/docview/3134268297/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+weak+leaders+meekly+bow+to+mad+king&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump is a walking reality distortion field, and almost immune to any reality outside of his own, which he has created and cultivated and tries to extend beyond himself. Historian Timothy Snyder, who has studied totalitarianism, has talked about this, and Trump is shockingly successful at it. It's one of the most dangerous things in the world.
And now Canada is living next door to America's Nero again, and it will be worse this time. Trump isn't even president yet and he's already got us scrambling with a half-formed burp. Trump fundamentally doesn't understand tariffs, but loves his fantasy version of them, so he threatened Canada and Mexico with a 25 per cent tariff on all goods entering the United States, "until such time as drugs, in particular fentanyl, and all illegal aliens stop this invasion of our country! Both Mexico and Canada have the absolute right and power to easily solve this long simmering problem."
In real world terms, Trump threatened to impose tariffs that would damage and perhaps crash several major U.S. industries - say, the auto industry - and damage and perhaps crash Canada's and Mexico's economies, until such time as Canada and Mexico solve part or all of America's problems with immigration and drugs. Or are seen to be doing that, in his upside-down-sideways view. Whichever.
Now, the business community and smart diplomacy might rein Trump in. Trump is surrounded by crazier, more venal weirdos this time, but maybe reality can break through.
Canada's reaction, though, showed a country ripe for the picking, smelling of panic and surrender. There are facts, of course. The two borders are incredibly different; barely any fentanyl is caught coming from Canada to the U.S., though it's rising slightly. The same is true of irregular crossings, on a border that spans nearly 9,000 kilometres.
Still, many Canadian politicians didn't just accept Trump's bark-at-the-waiter framing, but hopped to attention, ready to serve. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, predictably, put out a statement asking Trump to exclude oil from tariffs, and pledged to send extra patrols to the Alberta border, which is, uh, not a hotspot. One supposes she is already familiar with the crossing at Coutts.
Quebec Premier François Legault, who has long seen immigration as a winning issue for him, called for action on the border. Ontario's Doug Ford, who was sometimes helpful on federal-provincial calls during COVID - the premier would push the recalcitrant ones with the message of, we're all on the same damn team - went ahead and met with Canada Border Services Agency and the U.S.'s Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) to find out "what needs to happen to secure our border."
And Pierre Poilievre, who is very likely to be our next prime minister, looked like a one-gear car. His initial statement was both shameless (the prime minister must put partisanship aside) and laughable (... and commit to the Conservative Party of Canada's list of policies, from axing the carbon tax to securing the border to banning illegal drugs). Poilievre more or less said Trump was putting America first, and he would put Canada first. And on Thursday he said, in vaguely Trumpian prose, "because the prime minister has vacated the border and turned it open to anyone who wants to come in, the premiers are now taking responsibility for our border."
It doesn't help that the federal Liberals - who announced unspecified money towards border services after a call with the premiers - are exhausted and dead in the polls, and that frees up the provinces to play to their own agendas.
But take a step back here, and what are we doing? Trump's demands aren't based in any reality but his, but even by that standard they aren't real demands. He still hasn't communicated any actual demands to the federal government beyond the statement. He hasn't asked for any measurable action at all.
And even on that basis, Ford met with the DEA, and Smith begged for relief and sent out patrols, and Poilievre accepted the framing of an open border, and nobody seems to worry for a second that they're trying to please a mad king.
Snyder's first rule of tyrants is simple: do not obey in advance, and too many seem ready to forget that rule, in a vulnerable country. I called Gerry Butts, who was Justin Trudeau's principal secretary and adviser until 2019, through the first Trump negotiations.
He's worried Canada isn't building a more resilient, low-carbon economy, and hasn't built domestic economic resilience elsewhere, and faces a different political landscape now. We are vulnerable as a country - we always are - and need unity more than ever.
"As someone who works with global investors all the time now, our main pitch is invest in Canada because we have open, free, reliable access to the largest market in the world," said Butts, who is currently vice-chair of the Eurasia group. "It's a very dangerous time, right? We could get picked apart."
We could. Even if Trump backs down this time, he is a clear and present danger, and Canada will need strength and a united calm to deal with him.
But in a divided country, plenty are showing Trump just where to pry.
CAPTION: Too many Canadian politicians didn't just accept Donald Trump's bark-at-the- waiter framing, but hopped to attention, ready to serve, Bruce Arthur writes.Joe RaedleTribune News Service
CREDIT: Bruce Arthur Toronto Star OPINION

Document 15 of 500

The greatest political deception of our time is unfolding: There's just one thing I'd hope to bring back from the 'decade of greed,' Nov. 23 How the 'have-mores' have convinced the 'have-nots' that the enemy is those advocating for a fairer system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greatest-political-deception-our-time-is/docview/3134268254/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+greatest+political+deception+of+our+time+is+unfolding%3A+There%27s+just+one+thing+I%27d+hope+to+bring+back+from+the+%27decade+of+greed%2C%27+Nov.+23+How+the+%27have-mores%27+have+convinced+the+%27have-nots%27+that+the+enemy+is+those+advocating+for+a+fairer+system&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's just one thing I'd hope to bring back from the 'decade of greed,' Nov. 23
Charlie Angus's analysis of the 1980s "greed is good" neo-con ideology provides essential context for understanding how figures like Donald Trump and Elon Musk have masterfully positioned themselves as populist champions of the working class while advancing policies that primarily benefit the ultra-wealthy. Clearly the greatest political deception of our time is unfolding before us: billionaires recasting themselves as populist revolutionaries.
Current history reveals a perverse inversion of traditional class warfare. Instead of the underprivileged rising against wealth and power, today's so-called "revolutionaries" are billionaires, cleverly portraying teachers, social workers and minimum-wage earners as the "elite." This would be laughable if it weren't so effective - and so painful.
Conservative strategist David Frum, who made his name as a countercultural critic of the progressive equalitarian 1960s human rights revolution, once revealed the neo-conservative playbook states that equality should never be the goal of conservatives. Actually, this philosophy of inequality, rooted in Milton Friedman's trickle-down free market economics, has devastated American manufacturing while enriching global corporations. Now, the very Republican ideologues who gutted the middle-class campaign as saviours, promising to fix the problems they created. The script has flipped - the wealthiest among us now pose as freedom fighters battling an imagined "elite" of teachers, social workers, and minimum-wage earners.
Consequently, what's so upsetting to those paying attention is how the "have-mores" have convinced the "have-nots" that the enemy isn't billionaires hoarding wealth but anyone advocating for a fairer system. This so-called populist revolution elevates reality-TV celebrities, social media influencers and billionaire demagogues to positions of power, sidelining educated and credentialed experts from making critical life-and-death decisions. It's the MAGA playbook in action, where ignorance is strength, and the result is deeper social injustice. But that's the system people voted for, swayed by voices like Jordan B. Peterson, who frame social justice warriors as woke and the greatest threat to society. Imagine that: social justice, rebranded as a menace to society. Not being greedy is a vice.
As Canadians approach crucial elections, we must learn from the American experience. When Ontario Premier Doug Ford and federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre position themselves as voices of the common people, we must ask whose interests they truly serve. The stakes are too high to fall for this neo-con scam again.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto

Document 16 of 500

TTC set to replace aging subway fleet: Ottawa commits $758 million to help transit agency buy new cars for Line 2

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ttc-set-replace-aging-subway-fleet/docview/3134268246/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+set+to+replace+aging+subway+fleet%3A+Ottawa+commits+%24758+million+to+help+transit+agency+buy+new+cars+for+Line+2&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The TTC is getting a new fleet of subway trains.
At the transit agency's Greenwood Yard on Friday, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland announced the federal government will commit $758 million toward the $2.3-billion purchase of new cars for Line 2. The TTC says the 55 new trains are urgently needed because Line 2's current fleet will start reaching the end of its 30-year service life in 2026.
"Our government knows how important Toronto is, we know how important public transit in Toronto is, and we are really glad to be making this further investment today," said Freeland, who is Liberal MP for University-Rosedale.
"We know that Toronto is growing and we need to have public transit that grows with the needs of our growing city."
Ottawa's contribution, which will come from the federal Liberal government's new Canada Public Transit Fund, matches the amounts Toronto and the Ontario government pledged toward the Line 2 replacement as part of the so-called new deal that Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow announced one year ago. The Ontario PC government's share was contingent on its federal counterpart putting up a matching third.
"Twelve months later, we're here today," said Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, who joined Freeland and Chow at the press conference. He said the provincial government was "thrilled to see that day arrive, to celebrate these new TTC cars and what it means for transit within the city of Toronto."
The TTC had said it needed full funding for the new trains by March 2025, and expects the first of them to start arriving by 2030. The agency has budgeted $163 million for a repair program to keep the current vehicles running until the replacements can be put in service.
Line 2, the TTC's main east-west subway, opened in 1966, and is responsible for more than 400,000 daily boardings. While the TTC stresses that the 26-kilometre line is safe, its trains and signalling system are in need of modernization.
The risks of keeping trains running past the end of their service life was underscored last year when the Scarborough RT derailed, sending five people to hospital and forcing the permanent shutdown of the line earlier than planned. In June, then TTC CEO Rick Leary warned there could be similar problems on Line 2 if its trains weren't replaced.
Chow told reporters that riders have noticed Line 2 has become less reliable lately due to track and signal problems, but the new cars will enable the transit agency to install a modern automatic train control system that will allow more frequent service.
The mayor called the funding announcement a "generational, once in a lifetime investment to keep the TTC moving," and said it was evidence of what the three levels of government can accomplish when they work together.
Both Freeland and Sarkaria said the new trains would be built in Thunder Bay, the site of an Alstom rail plant that employs about 500 people.
However, interim TTC CEO Greg Percy clarified that the agency has yet to launch the procurement process for the vehicles. He said it's "ready to hit send" on a notice that will go out on Monday, and while the TTC would welcome Alstom vying for the contract, the process will "be open more broadly as well."
The Thunder Bay factory, which was owned by Bombardier until Alstom took over the company's rail business in 2021, has filled previous orders for TTC subways and streetcars. In 2022 it lost out on a major contract for new trains for the province's Ontario Line project.
The Canada Public Transit Fund will provide communities across the country with $3 billion in transportation funding a year. According to Friday's announcement, Toronto is set to receive almost $1.2 billion in baseline financial support from the fund over the next decade.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

The whole package will cost $6.28 billion, which is, coincidentally, enough to cut heating bills and carbon emissions over the long term for 300,000 Canadian households per year by providing them with free heat pumps, according to the Institute for Research on Public Policy.

But hey, what else would we do with all that borrowed money? Spend it on a 401 tunnel? Why stop at one?

Document 18 of 500

Trudeau government's 'Team Canada' approach to Donald Trump is already on shaky ground. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-team-canada-approach-donald/docview/3134130137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+%27Team+Canada%27+approach+to+Donald+Trump+is+already+on+shaky+ground.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It's Show-Don't-Tell time.
The Trudeau government's strategy in the face of Donald Trump's latest 25 per cent tariff threat is fourfold: prove Canada is a reliable neighbour bent on securing borders against illegal migration and drugs.
Keep up the diplomatic charm-offensive. Keep the list of politically-sensitive American products that could be strategically hit with Canadian counter-tariffs in the back pocket. And hope an all-hands-on-deck approach takes hold.
It could work. Or not.
There is a potential Canadian tariff list in the works, for use "if needed," Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland told premiers Wednesday night, according to sources with knowledge of the discussions. But it's not one the Trudeau government is prepared to release or talk about publicly.
In an interview with the Star, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said while such a list is useful to have, "my preference and I think, strategically and purposefully, is it needs to be a diplomatic resolution."
More important at this time, he said, is "communicating to Americans the impact that this will have to them, to their families, to their tables, to their vehicle, to their electricity bills is incredibly powerful and truthful and important. And I think that the citizens of the United States are not going to tolerate a 25 per cent increase in, for example, their gas bills or electricity bills or their grocery bills. And some of these measures will cause just that."
If the Trudeau government is planning any kind of advertising campaign to do that, nobody's saying. But the Ford government is. Ontario's ads launch Monday.
You should expect, however, that Ottawa will try to get media to cover how Mounties and border guards flex their muscle along the Canada-U.S. border, touting, along with ministers, drug busts, or arrests of illegal smuggling operations, or new investments to "visibly and demonstrably" show - as Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said - that the RCMP and CBSA work "daily" with American and local partners to keep the border secure.
The Trudeau government has agreed to beef up resources, and provide RCMP and the border agency with "new technologies, drones, helicopters, additional human resources necessary in the case of surge requirements," he said.
"This is a binational challenge," LeBlanc said. "None of this is a one-way street."
The target audience: worried Canadians and Americans, premiers and lawmakers south of the border.
The goal is to show Canada is not Mexico. And not China.
Then there is that diplomatic charm offensive that has been underway for months. There is a hurry-up-and-wait feel to it right now.
Canadian Ambassador Kirsten Hillman and her Washington team have criss-crossed the U.S., met with decision-makers and influencers, including confidantes of Trump, as have a couple of cabinet ministers. However, many of Trump's cabinet picks, those key to the Canada-U.S. relationship, remain to be confirmed. Protocol niceties that need to be followed while President Joe Biden is still in the Oval Office limit some of the kinds of meetings and discussions that can be had. For now, cabinet ministers in Ottawa are seeking informal meetings with those nominees, and talking to other influencers and decision-makers, according to two other federal sources.
Here, too, the premiers have a role to play.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew told CBC Thursday that the premiers are united in their desire to secure the Canada-U.S. relationship and are stepping up.
Furey, who is also chair of the eastern premiers group including Quebec, said he's written to New England governors to request a meeting to press Canada's case, and has raised with the governor of Massachusetts what a 25 per cent tariff would mean to Boston residents' electricity rates.
Fundamentally, Furey said, "I don't think a trade war helps the economy of either country. More importantly, it hurts the citizens of both countries."
As for that "Team Canada" approach, it's already on shaky ground.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith questioned whether Trudeau's the right person to lead Canada's defence, claiming in an interview with CBC there's too much "personal animus" between Trump and Trudeau. In the first ministers' meeting, she downloaded a litany of grievances about Trudeau's approach, two sources said.
Premier Doug Ford, whose office initially issued a statement critical of the federal government as "slow to act and stuck on the backfoot" was more upbeat in person, saying he thought "it was a real constructive conversation."
"We all voiced our opinion to the prime minister how important it is to listen to the threat from president-elect Trump," Ford told reporters. "We have to take our borders seriously and really tighten up the borders and he agreed. He knows how serious it is.
"We look forward to continuing to work with the prime minister and the federal government on this," he added.
Ford stressed he's "confident ... we'll be able to move forward on this in a rapid fashion" in terms of sending Trump a message that Canada is making border security a priority.
"The most important thing is that it's Team Canada, we're all sticking together. We put political stripes aside and are doing well as Team Canada," he said.
But one senior provincial official privy to the talks said the fact that Trudeau told the premiers he "would come back with a plan," suggested that as of Wednesday night they didn't have one.
"They finally read the room and communicated about addressing the border but I'd judge them by their actions: how many officers/drones/planes? And by when? We don't know. When will we know?" said the insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the closed-door meeting.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre flatly dismissed Trudeau's appeal to set aside partisanship to present a united front. Poilievre slammed Trudeau as "weak" and without the "brains or backbone" he presumably has to stand up to Trump. Poilievre demands an immediate election to replace Trudeau.
Alberta's Smith and Quebec's François Legault had earlier taken Trump's side, with Smith calling the president-elect's arguable premise - that "thousands of people are pouring through Mexico and Canada, bringing Crime and Drugs at levels never seen before" - "valid," and Legault calling it "legitimate."
Before the meeting, Legault said he doesn't trust Ottawa not to horse-trade away Quebec's aerospace, aluminum, forestry and agricultural products in favour of Alberta's oil and Ontario's auto-sector and wants a Quebec seat at any table.
Behind closed doors, Trudeau and Freeland again urged them not to amplify Trump's claims, with Freeland saying, "let's not negotiate against ourselves," according to a federal source on the call.
Furey said Thursday he felt they all share that goal. "While we have domestic differences of opinions with the federal government often, and almost all jurisdictions do, I do believe that there was, there can be, and should be a united front in approaching the United States, respecting the regional sensitivities."
He said Trudeau and Freeland promised more information-sharing and transparency on what they have in store.
At the same time, Furey said, the Liberal government faces a reality when dealing with the unpredictable Donald Trump, that was obvious with his Monday night bombshell social media threat against America's three biggest trading partners. It "came out of left field" and was "an example of creating chaos."
"Sure, they have a strategy, but what's the strategy for chaos?" Furey asked.
"You can be strategic. You can plan. You can stay up all night. You can have all the best people. But when you have an actor that is willing to be and wanting to be chaotic at times, it's difficult to plan."
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, seen with Public Safety Dominic LeBlanc on Wednesday, has told premiers that there is a potential Canadian tariff list in the works "if needed,"according to sources.

Document 19 of 500

Justin Trudeau must tell Donald Trump he will defend Canada's interests and retaliate if needed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-must-tell-donald-trump-he-will/docview/3133976562/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+must+tell+Donald+Trump+he+will+defend+Canada%27s+interests+and+retaliate+if+needed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Few Canadians were particularly nostalgic for the return of the flame thrower to the south, but here we are. Donald Trump is again sowing chaos for his neighbours and a Canadian government is once again consumed with heading off huge damage from the president-elect.
A threatened 25 per cent tariff on Canadian and Mexican exports to the United States, as Trump proposed in a social media message, would strike a huge blow to the Canadian economy, costing thousands of jobs and likely pushing us into recession. More than three-quarters of all Canadian exports, worth about $600 billion, moved to our southern neighbours last year. The threat alone is already having an impact.
The move would be devastating to Canada but American consumers and many businesses would also pay a heavy price; it would drive up the costs of importers in that country, costs which would then be passed on to American consumers who would face another uptick in inflation after voting for a president they expected to bring day-to-day costs down.
Trump has tied the tariff threat to border security and the flow of fentanyl across his southern and northern borders but to somehow cast Mexico and Canada as equal actors in these spheres is ludicrous.
According to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), border patrol officers had 23,721 "encounters" with people illegally crossing the Canadian border last year. It's true that the number of such encounters at the Canadian border has risen sharply in recent years. But at the Mexican border, the comparable number was 1.5 million.
Tightening the border would be in Canada's interests and the federal government has already moved to reimpose visa requirements on Mexican nationals entering this country. But when Trump's border czar Tom Homan says the northern border is a "huge national security issue," he's overstating the case by far.
Meanwhile, the amount of fentanyl seized at the Canadian border last year was reported at 19.5 kilos, 0.2 per cent of the 9,933 kilos seized at the Mexican border. The Canadian number should be zero, but we are just not a significant part of that problem, certainly not to the extent that the Americans are a problem with illegal guns flowing north to Canada, to cite one example.
It is dangerous to shrug and say the president-elect is bluffing, that this is a shot across the bow in a bid to soften us up for demands that we reach NATO spending goals quicker or prepare for a 2026 renewal of CUSMA, the tripartite Canada, U.S., Mexico trade deal. Sure, Trump will hear from U.S. business interests and states about the risks of such a move. And he may not like the impact his threats have on the markets. But we must prepare for the fact that Trump, in a second term surrounded by loyalists instead of those who would restrain his more radical proposals, might actually choose to do this.
So, how does Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government react?
First, the prime minister has properly exuded calm, admonishing those on the other side of the aisle who appear to be "freaking out." He can properly tell the incoming U.S. administration that we are working on the border and will continue to do so because that is in our interest. He can tell Trump, as his government has been saying, that we recognize the need to ramp up defence spending in a volatile world, because it is the price of membership in NATO. That is not conceding to a bully; it is part of responsible negotiating. At the same time, he shouldn't be quick to throw Mexico under the bus (we need all the friends we can get in this environment) or to dismiss legal avenues that may prove our best recourse.
He is right to forge a united Team Canada approach as he did in 2017-18 in the first time of Trump and that team must continue to work the edges, bringing our case to Republicans in the Senate and the U.S. House, reminding U.S. governors, mayors, business and union leaders of the price of such action on both sides of the border. He must continue to make it clear that we will defend Canadian interests and, if necessary, retaliate.
But there are significant differences from the first time Trudeau wrestled with Trump. The U.S. president-elect is now surrounded by loyalists, with few critical thinkers anywhere to be found. And Trudeau then was in his first, majority, term, able to cobble together that united front. This time, a minority Trudeau government, hanging on to power after nine years, is heading into an election year and Pierre Poilievre seems prepared to undermine the Canadian case by blaming Trudeau for every Trump accusation, real or imagined. Trudeau is also dealing with a more restive group of first ministers. Ontario's Doug Ford, himself gearing up for re-election, somehow felt that Trump was a family member who "stabbed him right in the heart," and Alberta's Danielle Smith seems prepared to march to Trump's drum.
We can leave the outrage to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum. Neither outrage nor concession is a way to deal with a bully. The Liberal government should instead make a robust case for our national interests. We can keep reminding Republicans that this will cost American consumers who rely on Canadian imports for their essential needs. If they remain unconvinced, they should brace themselves for sticker shock at the gas pumps if Trump slaps a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian oil and gas.
It's a good case. Ottawa should make it with confidence.
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump arrives before the launch of the sixth test flight of the SpaceX Starship rocket in Boca Chica, Texas on Nov. 19, 2024.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Donald+Trump%27s+25+per+cent+tariff+threat+raises+fears+of+recession+in+Canada%2C+while+Trudeau+government+won%27t+rule+out+retaliation%2C+Nov.+16+Trudeau+government+facing+pressure+to+change+%24250+cheque+plan%2C+Nov.+26+Pierre+Poilievre+is+trying+to+own+Justin+Trudeau+for+respecting+international+law%2C+Nov.+28+The+left+is+exposing+its+moral+inconsistencies%2C+McQueen%2C+Nov.+27+Consumer+debt+hits+%242.5+trillion+in+second+quarter%2C+with+credit+card+debt+at+its+highest+level+in+17+years%2C+Nov.+27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump only cares about appearing to win
Donald Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat raises fears of recession in Canada, while Trudeau government won't rule out retaliation, Nov. 16
I am quite surprised by recommendations that we retaliate against Donald Trump's 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods. This foolishness being touted by such incompetent thinking as we hear from Premier Doug Ford and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre shows that they truly do not understand Trump. He is all about the show. He only needs to look like he won, as we saw with all the tinkering around NAFTA, including renaming it to his preferred designation (USMCA, United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement). Very little was changed, but he declared victory. So, let's all tone down the foolish rhetoric and devise a plan to make Trump look good while inflicting only minimal discomfort and upset to Canada and Canadians. We'll all come out better in the end. Meanwhile, I plan to avoid, wherever possible, any spending on American goods or services.
Tom Doris, Toronto
Protect border and increase defence spending
Canada should hire thousands of people, put them in uniform, equip them and deploy them along the border with the United States. This would address president-elect Donald Trump's concerns not only about illegal immigrants and drugs getting into his country, but also increase our defence spending closer to two per cent of GDP. Two birds, one stone.
John Collins, Hastings, Ont.

Expand rebate, but lower the income ceiling
Trudeau government facing pressure to change $250 cheque plan, Nov. 26
It's hard to understand why the government would choose to send cash to those earning as much as $150,000 while not helping the many with much lower incomes. I recommend including seniors, the disabled, the unemployed in the handout - but cutting it off at an income of $75,000.
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
Most humans have an aversion to injustice
Pierre Poilievre is trying to own Justin Trudeau for respecting international law, Nov. 28
Thanks for Linda McQuaig's column setting the facts straight on the authority of the International Criminal Court. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is predictable in his response to any distracting stimulus from the complex real world, like a kitten reflexively pouncing on flickering sunlight. Yes, evidence suggests there is an aversion to injustice that may be innate in humans - how come Poilievre has no such instinct?
John Meyers, Goderich, Ont.
Even on the right one can find inconsistencies
The left is exposing its moral inconsistencies, McQueen, Nov. 27
I agree that there are considerable inconsistencies on the left, especially as they relate to the Israel-Palestine conflict. One reason may be that it is much easier to boycott goods made in Israel than products from China. However, even on the right, if one looks hard enough, you can find inconsistencies.
The inconsistencies may be most obvious between economic conservatives and social conservatives. Economic conservatives can be pro-choice on abortion and for liberal rules for medical assistance in dying. Their social conservative brethren likely have opposite views on those issues.
Even what one considers inconsistency is debatable. Pope Francis likely believes it is inconsistent to be opposed to abortion but in favour of capital punishment. Not everyone would agree with him.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
Regarding Mark McQueen's use of the word "woke," defined in the Merriam Webster dictionary as "aware of and actively attentive to important societal facts and issues, especially of racial and social injustice." He could have made his point more convincingly had he not resorted to disparaging those of us who care about issues of social and racial justice.
Teresa Ganna Porter, Newmarket
The song Mark McQueen questions in his column, "Is This Peace?" by Japanese-Kuwaiti YouTuber Maryam Shehab, was included in a school ceremony to underscore peace as a universal value. Its lyrics broadly promote harmony, fitting well with Remembrance Day themes. While it is true the song has appeared in videos highlighting the dire situation in Gaza, labelling it a "Palestinian resistance" song is ridiculous. If the school had played an Arabic rendition of "Baby Shark," would that, too, be deemed a "resistance song?"
Anthony Issa, Canadians for Justice and Peace in the Middle East, Toronto
Ocean of consumer debt is a boon to banks
Consumer debt hits $2.5 trillion in second quarter, with credit card debt at its highest level in 17 years, Nov. 27
The article blames "high living costs and rising unemployment" for record consumer debt, but the banks should also be recognized for their reprehensible (or ingenious?) plan to float the entire consumer economy on an ocean of debt, from which they profit endlessly.
Prices at some retailers could be higher for everyone because retailers have to pay a credit card processing fee, whether a credit card is used or not. So, people who pay for their purchases in cash still can end up paying a sticker price that includes the merchant's credit card fees. The cost of the various "loyalty" programs can also be included in the sticker price, whether you use those programs or not. There is no escape.
Noel Cooper, Brechin, Ont.

Document 21 of 500

Ottawa abandons vow to fix HIV law: Government had pledged to end 'over-criminalization' of those who don't disclose status of virus

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-abandons-vow-fix-hiv-law/docview/3133958205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+abandons+vow+to+fix+HIV+law%3A+Government+had+pledged+to+end+%27over-criminalization%27+of+those+who+don%27t+disclose+status+of+virus&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just ahead of World AIDS Day, the federal government is reneging on a promise to help stop prosecuting people who don't disclose their HIV status to sexual partners when there's no possibility of transmitting the virus.
Canadian law has long required individuals living with HIV to disclose their status to a sexual partner if there's a realistic possibility of transmission - under threat of criminal prosecution. But modern medications now let individuals manage their HIV to the point where the virus can't be transmitted, regardless of condom use.
In 2022, then-justice minister David Lametti pledged to "put an end, a positive end" to the "over-criminalization" of HIV nondisclosure. "Regrettably," those plans have come crashing down, a spokesperson for Lametti's successor, Arif Virani, confirmed to the Star this week, blaming the Conservatives' filibustering in the House of Commons for making it difficult to move forward.
"Instead of demonstrating bravery and finally ending criminalization, our political leaders have shut down progressive reforms," said Alexander McClelland, chair of the steering committee of the Canadian Coalition to Reform HIV Criminalization, speaking Thursday on Parliament Hill with other advocates and people living with HIV.
"They are not going to change this context which puts the over 65,000 people living with HIV in this country at risk, who are currently made to live their lives in the context of fear and uncertainty and violence."
Certainty for people living
with HIV
Under current law, failure to disclose HIV status in cases where there's a realistic possibility of transmission can result in a criminal charge - usually aggravated sexual assault - which can lead to a maximum term of imprisonment for life, as well as being included on the sex offenders registry and near-certain deportation for non-citizens.
But what a "realistic possibility of transmission" means can vary across the country. In some provinces, disclosure is not required if the individual both has a suppressed viral load thanks to medication and uses a condom. In others, like Ontario, Crown attorneys have been directed since 2017 not to prosecute if the viral load was suppressed, regardless of condom use - a recognition that the science is clear that an undetectable viral load alone means a person cannot transmit the virus.
Advocates say people don't disclose their status for a variety of reasons, as the stigma surrounding HIV can open people up to the risk of violence. They have long called on Ottawa for Criminal Code reforms that would bring certainty for people living with HIV across the country - changes that would put an end to the use of sexual assault laws to prosecute people for not disclosing, and would limit the use of the criminal law to the rare cases of intentional and actual transmission of the virus.
In the meantime, Canada continues to hold the "heinous designation of being a global hot spot" for HIV nondisclosure criminalization, having prosecuted 225 people since 1989, McClelland said.
One such case is that of Jennifer Murphy, an Ontario woman who was convicted of aggravated sexual assault in 2013 because no condom was used in an otherwise consensual sexual encounter, even though Murphy's viral load was undetectable, and therefore untransmissible. She was sentenced to three years in prison and designated a sex offender. Her conviction was overturned by the Court of Appeal in 2022.
"I was made out to be a monster," she told Star after she won her appeal. "I want the public to know that people with a suppressed viral load pose no threat to anyone."
In 2022, Lametti told the Star in an interview ahead of public consultations on the issue that he was going to get reform done. This built on the acknowledgment made by his predecessor, Jody Wilson-Raybould, in 2016 that there was an "over-criminalization of HIV nondisclosure" in Canada.
"No one should underestimate my good faith on this," Lametti told the Star.
Conservative filibustering on other issues this fall in the House of Commons has made it difficult to move forward with that reform, Virani's spokesperson said this week.
"This has forced us to make difficult decisions about what can be advanced this session," spokesperson Chantalle Aubertin said. "We deeply appreciate the patience and resilience of advocates and want them to know that their efforts continue to inspire the work ahead."
Advocates feel 'ghosted'
by Ottawa
Advocates are questioning why the Liberals didn't move faster, noting that the government's own public consultations - which showed a majority of respondents favoured removing nondisclosure from the reach of sexual assault laws - ended in early 2023.
In interviews with the Star, they spoke of meeting regularly with Lametti and his staff, feeling optimistic that change was on the horizon. But the government has "ghosted" them since Virani took over in July 2023, said André Capretti, a policy analyst with the HIV Legal Network.
In early November this year, Capretti said the call came in from Virani's office that there was no path forward, blaming the Conservatives and a "lack of consensus" between the government and advocates on law reform.
Capretti acknowledged "some distance between our position and their position, but what we would say is that we made many, many attempts to close that gap, find some kind of middle ground, find a compromise, and we were rebuffed."
He continued: "It's a bit rich of them to blame it on a lack of consensus when they made no attempt to reach a consensus."
The co-executive director of the HIV Legal Network, Janet Butler-McPhee, added that they always knew "this would be a compromise, negotiated position, but there's nothing constructive about a lack of response and a lack of dialogue that allows any of that compromise to happen."
McLelland said the coalition and people living with HIV are feeling let down.
"We were really hopeful under the previous minister; they engaged genuinely and honestly with us in a transparent way, and this response has been really disrespectful and disappointing," he said."And talking to people who have experienced criminalization first-hand, they're also just really, deeply hurt."
CAPTION: A spokesperson for Justice Minister Arif Virani said Conservative filibustering in the House of Commons has made it difficult to move forward on reforming the laws surrounding HIV disclosure.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Document 22 of 500

Trudeau must be prepared to retaliate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-must-be-prepared-retaliate/docview/3133958185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+must+be+prepared+to+retaliate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Few Canadians were particularly nostalgic for the return of the flame thrower to the south, but here we are. Donald Trump is again sowing chaos for his neighbours and a Canadian government is once again consumed with heading off huge damage from the president-elect.
A threatened 25 per cent tariff on Canadian and Mexican exports to the United States, as Trump proposed in a social media message, would strike a huge blow to the Canadian economy, costing thousands of jobs and likely pushing us into recession. More than three-quarters of all Canadian exports, worth about $600 billion, moved to our southern neighbours last year. The threat alone is already having an impact.
The move would be devastating to Canada, but American consumers and many businesses would also pay a heavy price. It would drive up the costs for importers in that country, costs that would then be passed on to American consumers, who would face another uptick in inflation after voting for a president they expected to bring day-to-day costs down.
Trump has tied the tariff threat to border security and the flow of fentanyl across his southern and northern borders, but to somehow cast Mexico and Canada as equal actors in these spheres is ludicrous.
According to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), border patrol officers had 23,721 "encounters" with people illegally crossing the Canadian border last year. It's true that the number of such encounters at the Canadian border has risen sharply in recent years. But at the Mexican border, the comparable number was 1.5 million.
Tightening the border would be in Canada's interests and the federal government has already moved to reimpose visa requirements on Mexican nationals entering this country. But when Trump's border czar Tom Homan says the northern border is a "huge national security issue," he's overstating the case by far.
Meanwhile, the amount of fentanyl seized at the Canadian border last year was reported at 19.5 kilos, 0.2 per cent of the 9,933 kilos seized at the Mexican border. The Canadian number should be zero, but we are just not a significant part of that problem, certainly not to the extent that the Americans are a problem with illegal guns flowing north to Canada, to cite one example.
It is dangerous to shrug and say the president-elect is bluffing, that this is a shot across the bow in a bid to soften us up for demands that we reach NATO spending goals quicker or prepare for a 2026 renewal of CUSMA, the tripartite Canada, U.S., Mexico trade deal.
Sure, Trump will hear from U.S. business interests and states about the risks of such a move. And he may not like the impact his threats have on the markets. But we must prepare for the fact that Trump, in a second term surrounded by loyalists instead of those who would restrain his more radical proposals, might actually choose to do this.
So, how does Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government react? First, the prime minister has properly exuded calm, admonishing those on the other side of the aisle who appear to be "freaking out." He can properly tell the incoming U.S. administration that we are working on the border and will continue to do so because that is in our interest.
He can tell Trump, as his government has been saying, that we recognize the need to ramp up defence spending in a volatile world, because it is the price of membership in NATO. That is not conceding to a bully; it is part of responsible negotiating.
At the same time, he shouldn't be quick to throw Mexico under the bus (we need all the friends we can get in this environment) or to dismiss legal avenues that may prove our best recourse.
He is right to forge a united Team Canada approach, as he did in 2017-18 in the first time of Trump, and that team must continue to work the edges, bringing our case to Republicans in the Senate and the U.S. House, reminding U.S. governors, mayors, business and union leaders of the price of such action on both sides of the border. He must continue to make it clear that we will defend Canadian interests and, if necessary, retaliate.
But there are significant differences from the first time Trudeau wrestled with Trump. The U.S. president-elect is now surrounded by loyalists, with few critical thinkers anywhere to be found. And Trudeau then was in his first, majority, term, able to cobble together that united front. This time, a minority Trudeau government, hanging on to power after nine years, is heading into an election year and Pierre Poilievre seems prepared to undermine the Canadian case by blaming Trudeau for every Trump accusation, real or imagined.
Trudeau is also dealing with a more restive group of first ministers. Ontario's Doug Ford, himself gearing up for re-election, somehow felt that Trump was a family member who "stabbed him right in the heart," and Alberta's Danielle Smith seems prepared to march to Trump's drum.
We can leave the outrage to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum. Neither outrage nor concession is a way to deal with a bully. The Liberal government should instead make a robust case for our national interests.
We can keep reminding Republicans that this will cost American consumers who rely on Canadian imports for their essential needs. If they remain unconvinced, they should brace themselves for sticker shock at the gas pumps if Trump slaps a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian oil and gas.
It's a good case. Ottawa should make it with confidence.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Presidents; Costs; Border patrol; Border security; Tariffs; Canada-US relations; Economic impact

Document 23 of 500

Eglinton LRT set to open in new year: Software flaw that posed a safety risk has been identified

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/eglinton-lrt-set-open-new-year/docview/3133958173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Eglinton+LRT+set+to+open+in+new+year%3A+Software+flaw+that+posed+a+safety+risk+has+been+identified&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The head of Metrolinx has offered new details on the progress of the long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT, but stopped short of providing a firm opening date for the beleaguered project.
Phil Verster, CEO of the provincial transit agency, said Thursday he expects the project to be completed "in the new year" after contractors recently identified a software design flaw in the line's signalling system. The flaw posed a safety risk "that's unacceptable for operations," Verster said, adding that a software fix will be implemented by the end of the year.
"There's no compromise on safety or on the quality of our operations," he said at a Thursday board meeting. Verster also said that testing on the line is roughly 95 to 96 per cent complete. In September, the agency conducted a key test of emergency response systems. It involved some 40 actors and simulated a train coming to a sudden stop and throwing passengers forward.
Early in the new year, Metrolinx is preparing for a "revenue service demonstration," Verster said. The process involves testing whether trains can operate at optimal service levels before the line opens.
When reached by the Star, a spokesperson for Metrolinx said: "We need to have confidence that key milestones are being met, and once we have an opening date, we will share that with the public."
Verster's comments come after the multibillion-dollar Eglinton LRT project has missed multiple deadlines and has run significantly over budget. The Metrolinx CEO previously said his agency will give the public three months' advance notice before the Eglinton LRT begins service.
Premier Doug Ford had said in October that the line, which began construction in 2011, may not open until the new year. The project was initially set to be completed by 2020.
At Thursday's board meeting, Verster also provided an update on the Finch West LRT. He said Metrolinx has made "significant progress" on the line and major construction has finished at all stations. When it opens, the 10-kilometre light rail transit line will feature 18 stops, connecting Finch West Station to Humber College. Metrolinx has also yet to provide a firm opening date for the Finch West LRT, which officials previously said was supposed to open this year.
CREDIT: Joshua Chong Culture Reporter

Document 24 of 500

Will another 'new deal' save Toronto?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-another-new-deal-save-toronto/docview/3133958167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+another+%27new+deal%27+save+Toronto%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A week ago, the Ontario NDP announced that former Toronto mayor David Miller would be coming up with a "new deal" for municipalities to implement if that party wins an election.
Those of us who recall Miller as Toronto's mayor early in the millennium may remember that he fought for - and won - a "new deal for cities" (in the form of a share of the gas tax) from the federal government in 2005, and a "new deal for Toronto" (in the form of the City of Toronto Act that, among other things, allowed the city to implement the land transfer tax) from the provincial government in 2006.
This week, Toronto city manager Paul Johnson gave a talk to the U of T's Institute on Municipal Finance and Governance that amounted to a victory lap celebrating the "new deal for Toronto" agreed to by Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow in 2023 (which uploaded the Gardiner and Don Valley Expressway onto the province's books, and gave the city hundreds of millions of dollars for shelters, housing and transit), and outlining what the next phase of that might look like.
See, if you hang around this place long enough, you can watch the new deal everyone demanded and then celebrated become old, as everyone starts demanding a new new deal, and then just as that one is being celebrated we're all on the lookout for the new new new deal that'll be coming up next.
It can feel like the revolving-door trend cycle of seasonal fashion or Top 40 pop music. Like Grandpa Simpson said, "I used to be with 'it,' but then they changed what 'it' was. Now what I'm with isn't 'it' anymore, and what's 'it' seems weird and scary."
Except that in the case of new deals for Toronto, they don't really change what "it" is all that much. Mostly, "it" is money.
Now, the calls for a new deal often involve asking for more autonomy and authority for cities, too. That's top of mind for some right now, with Ford bigfooting Toronto city council yet again to rip out bike lanes. NDP Leader Marit Stiles, in appointing Miller, specifically called out Ford's "meddling" as part of the problem. Miller's own 2006 new deal was one of the rare occasions where municipal autonomy was actually addressed, giving the mayor and council specific powers, while delivering not dollars but the new authority to raise their own.
But usually it's mostly about money. You could add other celebrated deals over the years to the list, uploading transit construction projects or sharing revenue, and the bottom line of all of them is, well, the bottom line.
Johnson's speech on Tuesday generally had a celebratory, pep-talk vibe to it. In sitcom terms, his demeanour is like that of Ted Lasso, in contrast to city managers of earlier eras such has Joe Pennachetti, who generally came off like Lou Grant on a particularly exasperating day.
But at one point Johnson hinted at the reason why this new deal leads immediately to talk of the next one. The highway uploads freed $1.9 billion that went immediately to address the city's infrastructure backlog. On the bright side, as Johnson said, "The City of Toronto doesn't have too many $1.9-billion days." On the other side, that cash went toward addressing a funding deficit that stood at $26 billion.
Only 14 new deals away from clearing the overdue infrastructure backlog! Whoo-hoo!
Or maybe one deal, if it was the right one. When he was mayor, Miller spent a lot of time campaigning for a share of the sales taxes - "one cent of the GST," meaning essentially a one per cent sales tax (or a 1/13th share of today's existing HST) given directly to cities. This has kind of remained the Holy Grail of potential "new deal" funding tools for many city watchers.
Because it would: a) produce a truckload of money by municipal standards; b) would be ongoing and predictable; and c) would grow when the economy and inflation grow and expenses go up.
Johnson claims he's "agnostic" on that or any other particular revenue source - specifically because he doesn't want the city to fixate on one way to skin the cat if that way is a complete non-starter for the premier or prime minister sitting on the other side of the negotiating table. But his explanation of the city's situation made it pretty clear that what's needed is something that shares those essential characteristics.
If Miller antes up with something like that in the NDP platform, will Ford and Bonnie Crombie need to match those table stakes in drafting their own new deal proposals? A new deal to end all new deals? As a provincial election issue?
Fat chance, maybe. But if you've been around long enough watching the let's-make-a-new-deal cycle, perhaps any kind of chance is worth hoping on.
CAPTION: The Ontario NDP tapped former Toronto mayor David Miller to come up with a "new deal" for municipalities if they win the next election. He already negotiated one "new deal" back in 2005, Edward Keenan writes.Jennifer Gauthier
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Document 25 of 500

Poilievre can learn from Ford on how to handle tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-can-learn-ford-on-how-handle-tariff/docview/3133958138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+can+learn+from+Ford+on+how+to+handle+tariff+threat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Pierre Poilievre are very different kinds of conservative leaders. Their response to Donald Trump's tariffs threat to Canada this week is laying bare those differences in a stark way.
Ford is doing a lot of things right; Poilievre, perhaps not so much.
In the wake of Wednesday's emergency meeting of Canada's first ministers, Ford told reporters on Thursday that he was focused on taking a "positive" tone. Poilievre, wrapping himself in Canada-is-broken rhetoric, has been sounding relentlessly negative.
For the federal Conservative leader, a potential economic disaster for Canada is another chance to cast Justin Trudeau as a bad prime minister.
"With the threats coming from the United States, with our border and chaos, our economy collapsing and everything broken, we need real, responsible leadership from a strong, smart prime minister who has the brains and backbone to put Canada first," Poilievre told reporters on Thursday.
Dispositionally, Ford and Poilievre are different types of populists. Ford is more of a happy warrior, who likes to be liked. The premier has frequently shown himself willing to admit when he makes a mistake and reverse himself.
Poilievre, on the other hand, is more of an angry populist, who tends to double down when he goes too far, whether that's in name calling or personal attacks on his detractors.
The two leaders also have very different interests at risk. Ford heads up a government that has huge stakes in any tariff war with the U.S. He is very aware that any attack on Trudeau and Canada will hurt Ontario in a big way.
That's why the province is rolling out a multimillion-dollar advertising blitz in the United States next Monday, to directly tell Americans how much their economy relies on trade with Canada's most populous province.
Poilievre has obviously decided he doesn't benefit from helping Trudeau, to the point of agreeing with Trump's allegations that Canada is doing a bad job with the border and illicit drug traffic.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and Quebec's François Legault have gone out on that limb too, with Smith going so far as to say that someone other than Trudeau should be dealing with this second coming of Trump. She also said in a social media post this week that Trump had expressed "valid" concerns in the tariff threat that landed as a bombshell Monday night.
This highlights another difference with Ford. Back during Canada's first tumultuous round with Trump from 2017 to 2021, neither Smith nor Poilievre were in any consequential positions. They have yet to see close up what kind of disruption Trump can wreak on Canadian governments and the economy.
Ford, though, got an immersion course along with Trudeau and learned then, as he did during COVID, that there are a lot of good reasons for Ontario and the federal government to work together. The premier and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland forged a friendship then that continues to this day, and may well see them chatting regularly again, in what they called "therapy" sessions.
As my colleagues at Queen's Park have chronicled, Ford has undergone a total transformation on his views about Trump. When he first came to office in 2018, he was probably the most pro-Trump premier in the country.
That all soured when COVID hit and Trump threatened to withhold exports of personal protective equipment from the U.S. to Canada.
As Ford told Queen's Park columnist Martin Regg Cohn at a democracy forum in 2021: "It was a real slap in the face when President Trump cut us off to the PPEs." He also told a student at the forum, who suggested Ford was Canada's version of Trump: "Boy, that was a real slap calling me Donald Trump. I'm anything but Donald Trump."
Ford has been striking that same tone this week, making clear that he's taking very personally the ways in which Trump is treating its northern neighbour.
"It's like a family member stabbing you right in the heart," Ford told reporters on Tuesday night.
"What I found unfair about the comments is to compare us to Mexico ... I can tell you, Canada is no Mexico. I found his comments unfair. I found him insulting."
Of course, when dealing with Trump, things can always get personal. It bears noticing that Poilievre keeps talking about Trudeau as "weak" - more than a dozen times in the Commons on Wednesday - a word that Trump once fired at the prime minister during a 2018 tantrum.
It's not clear how Ford or Poilievre are going to use their own personal networks to deal with the incoming Trump presidency. Poilievre was asked on Thursday whether he might try to go to Mar-a-Lago to meet Trump.
"I have not been invited to that, so ..." Poilievre replied.
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Tory leader calls GST cut a 'tax trick': Liberal measure would drop charge on numerous items

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-leader-calls-gst-cut-tax-trick/docview/3133958130/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+leader+calls+GST+cut+a+%27tax+trick%27%3A+Liberal+measure+would+drop+charge+on+numerous+items&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals' GST holiday bill is one step closer to becoming law after it was passed by a majority of the House of Commons late Thursday.
The Liberals and NDP worked together to curtail the usual debate on the legislation, passing it a day after it was first introduced.
The Conservatives and Bloc Québécois voted against the bill, which will now go to the Senate.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has called the measure inflationary and a "tax trick."
"This isn't a tax cut. This is an inflationary two-month temporary tax trick that will drive up the cost of living," he said.
A vote on the GST measure was expected to pass Thursday evening. The proposal would remove the tax from all groceries, restaurant meals, children's clothes, toys and diapers, as well as beer and wine, for a two-month period, beginning Dec. 14. The GST holiday was expected to pass due to support from the NDP's Jagmeet Singh. Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet has criticized the proposal as a vote-buying measure.
The Liberals have also proposed sending $250 cheques to anyone who worked in 2023 and made less than $150,000, but have not yet introduced legislation on that measure. The New Democrats and Bloc - joined by some members of the Liberal caucus - want to see eligibility expanded to more Canadians.
Poilievre said Thursday that Trudeau's GST measure is about the prime minister's unpopularity.
At Queen's Park, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy emphasized Ontario would match the HST cut so the provincial portion of the sales tax will also come off for two months.
With files from Robert Benzie and The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie and The Canadian Press

Title: Tory leader calls GST cut a 'tax trick':   Liberal measure would drop charge on numerous items

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

PCs won't back NDP bill for stat holiday: Sept. 30 date would recognize abuse at residential schools

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-wont-back-ndp-bill-stat-holiday/docview/3133958122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+won%27t+back+NDP+bill+for+stat+holiday%3A+Sept.+30+date+would+recognize+abuse+at+residential+schools&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government will not support a New Democrat's bill to make the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation a statutory holiday in the province, the Indigenous affairs minister said Thursday.
But Greg Rickford left some wiggle room to declare the day a holiday in the future. "We are not prepared to support a holiday for now," he said in an interview.
"I don't think until we get clear consensus from those Indigenous-led groups that the commemorative and memorializing activities should move forward, until we've heard from them and we haven't."
Ontario's only First Nation representative at Queen's Park, New Democrat Sol Mamakwa, tabled a bill this month for his proposed Day of Reflection on Indian Residential Schools on Sept. 30, which has been colloquially known as Orange Shirt Day.
The bill had its second reading and debate on Thursday evening.
The day is a federal statutory holiday and several other provinces and territories have also made it one. Numerous unions across Ontario have successfully negotiated the day as a holiday in their collective agreements.
The day recognizes the abuse suffered by Inuit, First Nations and Métis people at more than 100 state- and church-run residential schools across the country.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press
