Document 28 of 500

PM, premiers have a game plan for Trump: But their 'Team Canada' approach against threat of U.S. tariffs may already be on shaky ground

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-premiers-have-game-plan-trump/docview/3133957846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM%2C+premiers+have+a+game+plan+for+Trump%3A+But+their+%27Team+Canada%27+approach+against+threat+of+U.S.+tariffs+may+already+be+on+shaky+ground&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA It's Show-Don't-Tell time.
The Trudeau government's strategy in the face of Donald Trump's latest 25 per cent tariff threat is fourfold: prove Canada is a reliable neighbour bent on securing borders against illegal migration and drugs.
Keep up the diplomatic charm-offensive. Keep the list of politically sensitive American products that could be strategically hit with Canadian counter-tariffs in the back pocket. And hope an all-hands-on-deck approach takes hold.
It could work. Or not.
There is a potential Canadian tariff list in the works, for use "if needed," Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland told premiers Wednesday night, according to sources with knowledge of the discussions. But it's not one the Trudeau government is prepared to release or talk about publicly.
In an interview with the Star, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said while such a list is useful to have, "my preference and I think, strategically and purposefully, is it needs to be a diplomatic resolution."
More important at this time, he said, is "communicating to Americans the impact that this will have to them, to their families, to their tables, to their vehicle, to their electricity bills is incredibly powerful and truthful and important. And I think that the citizens of the United States are not going to tolerate a 25 per cent increase in, for example, their gas bills or electricity bills or their grocery bills. And some of these measures will cause just that."
If the Trudeau government is planning any kind of advertising campaign to do that, nobody's saying. But the Ford government is. Ontario's ads launch Monday.
You should expect, however, that Ottawa will try to get media to cover how Mounties and border guards flex their muscle along the Canada-U.S. border, touting, along with ministers, drug busts or arrests of illegal smuggling operations, or new investments to "visibly and demonstrably" show - as Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said - that the RCMP and CBSA work "daily" with American and local partners to keep the border secure.
The Trudeau government has agreed to beef up resources and provide RCMP and the border agency with "new technologies, drones, helicopters, additional human resources necessary in the case of surge requirements," he said.
Then there is that diplomatic charm offensive that has been underway for months. There is a hurry-up-and-wait feel to it right now.
Canadian Ambassador Kirsten Hillman and her Washington team have criss-crossed the U.S., met with decision-makers and influencers, including confidantes of Trump, as have a couple of cabinet ministers. However, many of Trump's cabinet picks, those key to the Canada-U.S. relationship, remain to be confirmed. Protocol niceties that need to be followed while President Joe Biden is still in the Oval Office limit some of the kinds of meetings and discussions that can be had.
Here, too, the premiers have a role to play.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew told CBC Thursday that the premiers are united in their desire to secure the Canada-U.S. relationship and are stepping up.
Furey, who is also chair of the eastern premiers group including Quebec, said he's written to New England governors to request a meeting to press Canada's case, and has raised with the governor of Massachusetts what a 25 per cent tariff would mean to Boston residents' electricity rates.
Fundamentally, Furey said, "I don't think a trade war helps the economy of either country. More importantly, it hurts the citizens of both countries."
As for that "Team Canada" approach, it's already on shaky ground.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith questioned whether Trudeau's the right person to lead Canada's defence, claiming in an interview with CBC there's too much "personal animus" between Trump and Trudeau. In the first ministers' meeting, she downloaded a litany of grievances about Trudeau's approach, two sources said.
Premier Doug Ford, whose office initially issued a statement critical of the federal government as "slow to act and stuck on the backfoot" was more upbeat in person, saying he thought "it was a real constructive conversation."
"We all voiced our opinion to the prime minister how important it is to listen to the threat from president-elect Trump," Ford told reporters. "We have to take our borders seriously and really tighten up the borders and he agreed. He knows how serious it is."
But one senior provincial official privy to the talks said the fact that Trudeau told the premiers he "would come back with a plan," suggested that as of Wednesday night they didn't have one.
Before the meeting, Quebec's François Legault said he doesn't trust Ottawa not to horse-trade away Quebec's aerospace, aluminum, forestry and agricultural products in favour of Alberta's oil and Ontario's auto-sector and wants a Quebec seat at any table.
Behind closed doors, Trudeau and Freeland again urged them not to amplify Trump's claims, with Freeland saying, "let's not negotiate against ourselves," according to a federal source on the call.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 29 of 500

Ottawa abandons promise to fix 'over-criminalization' of people living with HIV

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-abandons-promise-fix-over-criminalization/docview/3134130649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+abandons+promise+to+fix+%27over-criminalization%27+of+people+living+with+HIV&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just ahead of World AIDS Day, the federal government is reneging on a promise to help stop prosecuting people who don't disclose their HIV status to sexual partners when there's no possibility of transmitting the virus.Canadian law has long required individuals living with HIV to disclose their status to a sexual partner if there's a realistic possibility of transmission - under threat of criminal prosecution. But modern medications now let individuals manage their HIV to the point where the virus can't be transmitted, regardless of condom use.In 2022, former Justice Minister David Lametti pledged to "put an end, a positive end" to the "over-criminalization" of HIV non-disclosure. "Regrettably," those plans have come crashing down, a spokesperson for Lametti's successor, Arif Virani, confirmed to the Star this week, blaming the Conservatives' filibustering in the House of Commons for making it difficult to move forward. "Instead of demonstrating bravery and finally ending criminalization, our political leaders have shut down progressive reforms," said Alexander McClelland, chair of the steering committee of the Canadian Coalition to Reform HIV Criminalization, speaking Thursday on Parliament Hill with other advocates and people living with HIV."They are not going to change this context which puts the over 65,000 people living with HIV in this country at risk, who are currently made to live their lives in the context of fear and uncertainty and violence."Certainty for people living with HIVUnder current law, failure to disclose HIV status in cases where there's a realistic possibility of transmission can result in a criminal charge - usually aggravated sexual assault - which can lead to a maximum term of imprisonment for life, as well as being included on the sex offenders registry and near-certain deportation for non-citizens. But what a "realistic possibility of transmission" means can vary across the country. In some provinces, disclosure is not required if the individual both has a suppressed viral load thanks to medication and uses a condom. In others, like Ontario, Crown attorneys have been directed since 2017 not to prosecute if the viral load was suppressed, regardless of condom use - a recognition that the science is clear that an undetectable viral load alone means a person cannot transmit the virus. Advocates say people don't disclose their status for a variety of reasons, as the stigma surrounding HIV can open people up to the risk of violence. They have long called on Ottawa for Criminal Code reforms that would bring certainty for people living with HIV across the country - changes that would put an end to the use of sexual assault laws to prosecute people for not disclosing, and would limit the use of the criminal law to the rare cases of intentional and actual transmission of the virus.In the meantime, Canada continues to hold the "heinous designation of being a global hot spot" for HIV non-disclosure criminalization, having prosecuted 225 people since 1989, McClelland said. One such case is that of Jennifer Murphy, an Ontario woman who was convicted of aggravated sexual assault in 2013 because no condom was used in an otherwise consensual sexual encounter, even though Murphy's viral load was undetectable, and therefore untransmissible. She was sentenced to three years in prison and designated a sex offender. Her conviction was overturned by the Court of Appeal in 2022. "I was made out to be a monster," she told Star after she won her appeal. "I want the public to know that people with a suppressed viral load pose no threat to anyone."In 2022, Lametti told the Star in an interview ahead of public consultations on the issue that he was going to get reform done. This built on the acknowledgment made by his predecessor, Jody Wilson-Raybould, in 2016 that there was an "over-criminalization of HIV non-disclosure" in Canada."No one should underestimate my good faith on this," Lametti told the Star.Conservative filibustering on other issues this fall in the House of Commons has made it difficult to move forward with that reform, Virani's spokesperson said this week."This has forced us to make difficult decisions about what can be advanced this session," spokesperson Chantalle Aubertin said. "We deeply appreciate the patience and resilience of advocates and want them to know that their efforts continue to inspire the work ahead."Advocates feel 'ghosted' by OttawaAdvocates are questioning why the Liberals didn't move faster, noting that the government's own public consultations - which showed a majority of respondents favoured removing non-disclosure from the reach of sexual assault laws - ended in early 2023.In interviews with the Star, they spoke of meeting regularly with Lametti and his staff, feeling optimistic that change was on the horizon. But the government has "ghosted" them since Virani took over in July 2023, said André Capretti, a policy analyst with the HIV Legal Network.In early November this year, Capretti said the call came in from Virani's office that there was no path forward, blaming the Conservatives and a "lack of consensus" between the government and advocates on law reform. Capretti acknowledged "some distance between our position and their position, but what we would say is that we made many, many attempts to close that gap, find some kind of middle ground, find a compromise, and we were rebuffed."He continued: "It's a bit rich of them to blame it on a lack of consensus when they made no attempt to reach a consensus."The co-executive director of the HIV Legal Network, Janet Butler-McPhee, added that they always knew "this would be a compromise, negotiated position, but there's nothing constructive about a lack of response and a lack of dialogue that allows any of that compromise to happen." McLelland said the coalition and people living with HIV are feeling let down."We were really hopeful under the previous minister; they engaged genuinely and honestly with us in a transparent way, and this response has been really disrespectful and disappointing," he said. "And talking to people who have experienced criminalization first-hand, they're also just really, deeply hurt."
CAPTION: Minister of Justice and Attorney General of Canada Arif Virani, seen here during Question Period on Nov. 18.

Subject: Condoms; Human immunodeficiency virus--HIV; Criminalization; Disclosure; Reforms; Sex crimes; Criminal law

Business indexing term: Subject: Disclosure

Location: Canada

Document 30 of 500

Metrolinx provides update on long-delayed Eglinton LRT after finding software flaw

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/metrolinx-provides-update-on-long-delayed/docview/3134130185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Metrolinx+provides+update+on+long-delayed+Eglinton+LRT+after+finding+software+flaw&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The head of Metrolinx has offered new details on the progress of the long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT, but stopped short of providing a firm opening date for the beleaguered project.
Phil Verster, CEO of the provincial transit agency, said Thursday he expects the project to be completed "in the new year" after contractors recently identified a software design flaw in the line's signalling system.
The flaw posed a safety risk "that's unacceptable for operations," Verster said, adding that a software fix will be implemented by the end of the year.
"There's no compromise on safety or on the quality of our operations," he said at a Thursday board meeting.
Verster also said that testing on the line is roughly 95 to 96 per cent complete. In September, the agency conducted a key test of emergency response systems. It involved some 40 actors and simulated a train coming to a sudden stop and throwing passengers forward.
Early in the new year, Metrolinx is preparing for a "revenue service demonstration," Verster said. The process involves testing whether trains can operate at optimal service levels before the line opens.
When reached by the Star, a spokesperson for Metrolinx said: "We need to have confidence that key milestones are being met, and once we have an opening date, we will share that with the public."
Verster's comments come after the multibillion-dollar Eglinton LRT project has missed multiple deadlines and has run significantly over budget. The Metrolinx CEO previously said his agency will give the public three months' advance notice before the Eglinton LRT begins service.
Premier Doug Ford had said in October that the line, which began construction in 2011, may not open until the new year. The project was initially set to be completed by 2020.
At Thursday's board meeting, Verster also provided an update on the Finch West LRT. He said Metrolinx has made "significant progress" on the line and major construction has finished at all stations. When it opens, the 10-kilometre light rail transit line will feature 18 stops, connecting Finch West Station to Humber College.
Metrolinx has also yet to provide a firm opening date for the Finch West LRT, which officials previously said was supposed to open this year.
CAPTION: Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster provided an update Thursday on the Eglinton LRT.

Subject: Light rail transportation; New year

Document 31 of 500

When it comes to Donald Trump's tariff threat, Doug Ford is getting it right. Pierre Poilievre? Not so much

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-comes-donald-trumps-tariff-threat-doug-ford/docview/3134130151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+it+comes+to+Donald+Trump%27s+tariff+threat%2C+Doug+Ford+is+getting+it+right.+Pierre+Poilievre%3F+Not+so+much&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Pierre Poilievre are very different kinds of conservative leaders. Their response to Donald Trump's tariffs threat to Canada this week is laying bare those differences in a stark way.
Ford is doing a lot of things right; Poilievre, perhaps not so much.
In the wake of Wednesday's emergency meeting of Canada's first ministers, Ford told reporters on Thursday that he was focused on taking a "positive" tone. Poilievre, wrapping himself in Canada-is-broken rhetoric, has been sounding relentlessly negative.
For the federal Conservative leader, a potential economic disaster for Canada is another chance to cast Justin Trudeau as a bad prime minister.
"With the threats coming from the United States, with our border and chaos, our economy collapsing and everything broken, we need real, responsible leadership from a strong, smart prime minister who has the brains and backbone to put Canada first," Poilievre told reporters on Thursday.
Dispositionally, Ford and Poilievre are different types of populists. Ford is more of a happy warrior, who likes to be liked. The premier has frequently shown himself willing to admit when he makes a mistake and reverse himself.
Poilievre, on the other hand, is more of an angry populist, who tends to double down when he goes too far, whether that's in name calling or personal attacks on his detractors.
The two leaders also have very different interests at risk. Ford heads up a government that has huge stakes in any tariff war with the U.S. He is very aware that any attack on Trudeau and Canada will hurt Ontario in a big way.
That's why the province is rolling out a multimillion-dollar advertising blitz in the United States next Monday, to directly tell Americans how much their economy relies on trade with Canada's most populous province.
Poilievre has obviously decided he doesn't benefit from helping Trudeau, to the point of agreeing with Trump's allegations that Canada is doing a bad job with the border and illicit drug traffic.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and Quebec's François Legault have gone out on that limb too, with Smith going so far as to say that someone other than Trudeau should be dealing with this second coming of Trump. She also said in a social media post this week that Trump had expressed "valid" concerns in the tariff threat that landed as a bombshell Monday night.
This highlights another difference with Ford. Back during Canada's first tumultuous round with Trump from 2017 to 2021, neither Smith nor Poilievre were in any consequential positions. They have yet to see close up what kind of disruption Trump can wreak on Canadian governments and the economy.
Ford, though, got an immersion course along with Trudeau and learned then, as he did during COVID, that there are a lot of good reasons for Ontario and the federal government to work together. The premier and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland forged a friendship then that continues to this day, and may well see them chatting regularly again, in what they called "therapy" sessions.
As my colleagues at Queen's Park have chronicled, Ford has undergone a total transformation on his views about Trump. When he first came to office in 2018, he was probably the most pro-Trump premier in the country.
That all soured when COVID hit and Trump threatened to withhold exports of personal protective equipment from the U.S. to Canada.
As Ford told Queen's Park columnist Martin Regg Cohn at a democracy forum in 2021: "It was a real slap in the face when President Trump cut us off to the PPEs." He also told a student at the forum, who suggested Ford was Canada's version of Trump: "Boy, that was a real slap calling me Donald Trump. I'm anything but Donald Trump."
Ford has been striking that same tone this week, making clear that he's taking very personally the ways in which Trump is treating its northern neighbour.
"It's like a family member stabbing you right in the heart," Ford told reporters on Tuesday night.
"What I found unfair about the comments is to compare us to Mexico ... I can tell you, Canada is no Mexico. I found his comments unfair. I found him insulting."
Of course, when dealing with Trump, things can always get personal. It bears noticing that Poilievre keeps talking about Trudeau as "weak" - more than a dozen times in the Commons on Wednesday - a word that Trump once fired at the prime minister during a 2018 tantrum.
It's not clear how Ford or Poilievre are going to use their own personal networks to deal with the incoming Trump presidency. Poilievre was asked on Thursday whether he might try to go to Mar-a-Lago to meet Trump.
"I have not been invited to that, so ..." Poilievre replied.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford, left, and federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Tariffs

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Title: When it comes to Donald Trump's tariff threat, Doug Ford is getting it right. Pierre Poilievre? Not so much

Document 32 of 500

'This is intimidating': Ontario defence lawyer under fire for letter asking alleged victim to refute spousal abuse allegations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-intimidating-ontario-defence-lawyer-under/docview/3133723585/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+intimidating%27%3A+Ontario+defence+lawyer+under+fire+for+letter+asking+alleged+victim+to+refute+spousal+abuse+allegations&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a move experts describe as an intimidation tactic, an Ontario defence lawyer sent the complainant in an ongoing intimate-partner violence case a letter asking her to help get the charges dropped against her spouse - the man who allegedly choked her and assaulted her with a bat. In the letter obtained by the Star, Thornhill-based lawyer Fadi Matthew Kazandji tells the complainant it's "our understanding" that the alleged incidents leading to the charges - assault with a weapon, assault by choking, unlawful confinement and death threats - are the result of "certain misunderstandings" between her and her partner, who live in Halton Region.This could affect his client's "future employment and life," Kazandji writes. Should it not have been her intention to involve the police or to pursue the case further, Kazandji tells the complainant he'd be pleased to discuss preparing an affidavit - a sworn legal document - outlining that she wants the charges dropped."This affidavit would accurately detail the events in question and convey your desire for the prosecution not to proceed," Kazandji writes."There will be no criminal charges against you for rectifying the issue and providing truthful information at this stage."Kazandji did not return the Star's multiple requests for comment.While defence lawyers in intimate-partner violence cases can communicate with any potential witness, including the complainant, victimologists argue the letter crosses the line by pushing the woman toward dropping the charges while offering inappropriate legal advice - after all, a defence lawyer is in no position to speak for police about whether a complainant will face consequences for saying an alleged incident was just a big misunderstanding. "This is intimidating," said Jo-Anne Wemmers, a professor in the School of Criminology at Université de Montréal. "It's a form of psychological violence, and in the literature it's often referred to as legal abuse - how offenders can use the law to continue to intimidate and abuse."She also took issue with the emphasis on the consequences the charges will have on the accused."What about the consequences that it's had on her?" Wemmers said. "I find it a very selfish and self-serving approach."She said it points to the need for complainants to be able to access free, reliable legal information "to reduce their anxiety and help make the right decision for them, but unfortunately that's not always available."Victims advocate Jeff Bradley concurred the letter is intimidating, and questioned Kazandji giving legal advice to someone he doesn't represent. "It seems totally inappropriate; why would somebody's defence lawyer then be giving the survivor guidance?" said Bradley, a PhD candidate in legal studies at Carleton University."Intimate-partner violence is an epidemic, and we already know the system doesn't lead to justice for most survivors. And so if this becomes a common practice, you wouldn't even want to try going through the legal system."Just this week, the Ford government rejected an NDP motion on the International Day for the Elimination of Violence Against Women that would have declared that intimate-partner violence is indeed an epidemic, after 58 women died from it in Ontario last year. An NDP bill which could help increase funding to tackle the problem remains before a legislative committee for study. Speaking generally, the president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association told the Star that while defence lawyers in IPV cases can contact the complainant prior to a trial, they "must recognize the many dynamics that are almost invariably present in such cases, and they must conduct themselves in a very careful and professional manner."A lawyer may not, in most cases, purport to give legal advice to the complainant. Ensuring that the complainant has access to independent legal counsel of their own is a common and wise approach," Boris Bytensky said."We should not automatically rush to condemn or criticize defence lawyers who reach out to complainants and other family members in such cases. This contact, if handled properly, is often critically important to ensuring that your client receives a robust defence, and that justice is ultimately done."
CAPTION: The case is being heard at the Milton courthouse.

Subject: Domestic violence; Defense; Attorneys; Assaults

These are the best new wines in the LCBO Vintages section - and they won't last long

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/these-are-best-new-wines-lcbo-vintages-section/docview/3133723582/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=These+are+the+best+new+wines+in+the+LCBO+Vintages+section+-+and+they+won%27t+last+long&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: High-scoring, inexpensive wines fly off shelves. But so do top-rated, more premium bottles. They quietly appear in the LCBO's Vintages section, often without any fanfare, and are gone in a flash. So you need to be in the know and act fast. This column is your cheat sheet of best new releases in Vintages now. Every bottle offers outstanding value.
2020 Westcott Vineyards Estate Pinot Noir, VQA Niagara Escarpment, Ontario (Vintages 427500, $31.95)
Score: 95
This classically styled Pinot Noir nods toward Burgundy with its translucent, earth-red hue and bright ruby inflections. Each swirl coaxes a delicate, savoury perfume into the bowl of the glass that's serene and calming. The fragrance suggests oven-fresh cranberry cobbler, cool clay, blood orange and roasted mushroom. Then a sheer flood of bright and shadowy fruit slides in like rippling silk, anchored by umami-rich undertones. This top-tier Pinot Noir tastes more expensive than it is. Build a dinner party around it with roasted pork and mushrooms.
2022 Chalk Hill Chardonnay, Sonoma Coast California (Vintages 420943, $26.95)
Score: 94
Gleaming pale and luminous in the glass, this captivating Chardonnay sweeps in with sophistication and immediacy. Cool and crisp, saturated yet poised, it is the vinous equivalent of a ballet dancer. White orchard fruit as well as raw nut and mineral aromatics lead to a lifted, linear-tasting expression. Chiselled flavours suggest sliced pear and lemon zest, pistachio and crushed sea shell. That briny minerality leaves the palate seasoned. Enjoy this vivid, expressive white on its own, with soft cheeses or with poached seafood dunked in beurre blanc.
2022 Tenuta San Guido Guidalberto, IGT Toscana, Italy (Vintages 72439, $81.95)
Score: 96
Guidalberto has pedigree you can taste. It is the second wine of Tenuta San Guido Sassicaia, the iconic Super Tuscan that fetches more than $300 per bottle. Each glass starts with a dynamic interplay of dark berries and forest floor, cherries and charcuterie - a beautiful scent. That invitation leads to an entry that tastes fresh, exciting and fruit-driven at first and then shifts toward something more delicate and intricate. Each sip slips from berries to earth to slate to graphite to cool violet and on and on. Serve with fine wine lovers.
2023 Greywacke Sauvignon Blanc, Marlborough, New Zealand (Vintages 164228, $28.95)
Score: 95
This dignified expression of Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc shines pale, almost water-white with an arresting aromatic intensity. Then it beams in with remarkable salinity, tightly wound fruit extract and a certain stately restraint. Rather than unbridled fruit expression, the flavour is majestic, pure and framed with firm structure. Star-bright acidity drenches the palate as flavours call to mind green mango, lime, kiwi fruit and, again, lemongrass with a salted stone finish and pleasant little grip. Bring on fish tacos, guacamole and chips and a few bottles of this. It's too good not to share.
2021 Wynns Coonawarra Estate Black Label Cabernet Sauvignon, Australia (Vintages 84996, $44.95)
Score: 93
This big, inky-purple-black red starts with gentle scents of dusty cocoa, blackcurrant and a whiff of warm cedar. Then it thunders in with rich jam-jar intensity. Roasted coffee and salted dark chocolate thread the poached plum and muddled blackberry middle before slowly tapering to let finer brush strokes of toasted almond, pencil shavings and spice cake show through. This is a plush, lush, cashmere crush of Cabernet with enough juiciness to keep it drinkable. Serve it with strip loin or grilled Portobello steak and potatoes for a big night in.
2021 Gérard Bertrand Château L'Hospitalet Grand Vin AOP La Clape, Sud de France (Vintages 30362, $41.95)
Score: 95
This Syrah-Grenache-Mourvèdre blend delivers immediate pleasure but becomes more characterful with each sip. The fragrance is soft and allusive, pointing toward midsummer berries, roasted chestnuts and wild rose. Then, that brooding, meditative bouquet moves to more the same on the palate but with an energetic vibrance that lifts the wine as well as a plush, velvety mouth feel that lends hedonism. As each sip recedes, the fruit turns brawny with charcoal and peppercorn, roasted hazelnut and parched clay emerging on the long finish. Pour it by the fire with charcuterie.
CAPTION: 2020 Westcott Vineyards Estate Pinot Noir.2022 Chalk Hill Chardonnay.2022 Tenuta San Guido Guidalberto.2023 Greywacke Sauvignon Blanc.2021 Wynns Coonawarra Estate Black Label Cabernet Sauvignon.2021 Gérard Bertrand Château L'Hospitalet Grand Vin AOP La Clape.Our wine critic's picks for the best new Vintages selections at the LCBO.

Trump should focus on reducing America's demand for illicit drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-should-focus-on-reducing-americas-demand/docview/3133628044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+should+focus+on+reducing+America%27s+demand+for+illicit+drugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's 25% tariff threat raises fears of recession in Canada, while Trudeau government won't rule out retaliation, Nov. 16
Canada is not the cause of the U. S. A's problems with drugs and illegal aliens, yet Donald Trump feels compelled to throw Canada under the bus. Trump should know that particularly as respects fentanyl it is a case of supply and demand and perhaps he should work hard on reducing the Americans' demand for this drug. This is headline grabbing nonsense and Canada should continue to have meaningful discussions with state and business leaders behind the scenes. Hopefully these people can deliver a dose of reality to the Trump administration.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
How Ottawa should handle Trump's tariff threat
Canada should put a 100 per cent tariff on orange juice. We do not produce any orange juice. We get it all from the U.S. If Canadians want to keep drinking it they will have to pay up. Or they can switch to apple juice which we produce ourselves. Tropicana will run to Washington complaining they have lost a market the size of California due to tariffs.
John Vipond, Oakville
Trumps 25 per cent tariff is a negotiating tool
The media has chosen to take a 25 per cent tariff as a done deal and are catastrophizing the consequences to drive viewer and reader traffic. Everyone needs to take a deep breath and relax. It is a threat only and U.S. President-elect Donald Trump has a history of making threats to convince other countries to take action on a variety of issues and it has worked. Trump understands the negative economic consequences for the U.S. of the threatened tariff if implemented. It is a negotiating tool the same as a strike or lockout is in labour relations. He has made the threat now to give the countries to be affected sufficient opportunity before he takes office to take the actions he has indicated would remove the threat. Smart, not unhinged.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
Apply Trump's logic to our illegal gun problem
Countries have been fighting drugs for a very long time. The Opium War was in the 1840s. No one has figured out how to solve the drug problem, including Donald Trump in his first term in office. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should tell Donald Trump he is putting a 25 per cent tariff on U.S. products until he stops the thousands of guns entering Canada through the United States bringing higher levels of violent crime. Last time I checked, countries are responsible for securing their own borders. This is not something you outsource to another country.
Michael Yaffe, Toronto
Canada needs to review trading relationships
A quick fix to bring Donald Trump's bullying tactics to heel would be an equivalent export tax on all energy resources and raw materials exported to the United States. Canada is America'slargest energy source andnumber two supplier of nonenergy mineral resources after China. Canada should not cringe in fear of Trump's tactics of bullying and misinformation or cave in to his demands. We need to use whatever tools we have available to work out a reasonable fair-trade deal that benefits both partners. Without that, it is no longer a trade deal but exploitation and no partnership. Trump will try a workaround to existing agreements and we must be prepared to act in kind.
We also need to review our trading relationships. Expanding our trading relationships with other partners would make us far less susceptible to Trump's tactics. We must also expand our military capacity. National service and our own military supply chain and sources could create thousands of new jobs across Canada in manufacturing. If we love our country, is it not worth defending it economically and militarily?
Robert Holden, Keswick, ON
A lack of respect for post-secondary educationSheridan College announces major program cuts and layoffs amid international student cap, Nov. 26
On Tuesday at 2:52 p.m., a schoolwide email informed Sheridan College faculty and students of the suspension of 40 academic programs, reducing the student body by around 30 per cent. This includes my own program, Creative Writing & Publishing, a program uniquely designed to combine both aspects of the publishing industry into a singular, comprehensive degree. This news comes amidst already rocky relations between the college board and teachers' union; faculty received news of program closures at the same time as the student body. For students this is hard enough, but to notify faculty of imminent mass layoffs in such a manner shows deep disrespect from the board. At its core, the decision is rooted in low levels of funding and a lack of respect for post-secondary education. This must be the topic of discussions going forward; without both, post-secondary education in Ontario will continue to suffer.
Quin Braden, Mississauga
What price is Ford willing to pay to get re-elected?
Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Nov. 26
Ontario proposes fast-tracking drug-consumption-site bill, skipping public hearings, Nov. 26
On Wednesday, the Star reported that Premier Doug Ford is fast tracking legislation that will rip up bike lanes on busy streets, essentially legalizing brazen vandalism. Later that day it was announced that the legislation to shut down safe injection sites will be pushed through without public consultations. Neither of these initiatives is supported by evidence. Both will lead to increased fatalities. We must ask ourselves how many lost Ontario lives the Premier is prepared to accept as collateral damage in his bid for re-election. Fifty? One hundred? One thousand? More?
Patrick McDonald, Toronto
Ford is failing the people of Ontario
There is something maliciously pigheaded about a government that passes legislation to make the public roads less safe for its citizens, but then also absolves itself of the responsibility for doing so. Even more so, when government action on important matters, like fixing the health-care crisis, improving our education system, working to provide more housing, and addressing the homelessness problem, is ignored. Throughout his time in office, Premier Doug Ford has wasted his mandate on the petty - closing the Ontario Science Centre, which could have been fully repaired with the petty change from his cancelling the Beer Store contract a few months early - and the inconsequential - the invisible licence plates he championed, ripping up a public park to install a private spa, and sending every Ontarian a small cheque of our own money just before he calls an election. We have to do better for our province than this failure of a politician.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
CAPTION: Canadian and American flags fly near the Ambassador Bridge at the Canada-USA border crossing in Windsor, Ont.

Document 35 of 500

Is another 'new deal' going to save Toronto? Don't let the Ted Lasso talk fool you

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-another-new-deal-going-save-toronto-dont-let/docview/3133627872/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+another+%27new+deal%27+going+to+save+Toronto%3F+Don%27t+let+the+Ted+Lasso+talk+fool+you&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A week ago, the Ontario NDP announced that former Toronto mayor David Miller would be coming up with a "new deal" for municipalities to implement if that party wins an election.
Those of us who recall Miller as Toronto's mayor early in the millennium may remember that he fought for - and won - a "new deal for cities" (in the form of a share of the gas tax) from the federal government in 2005, and a "new deal for Toronto" (in the form of the City of Toronto Act that, among other things, allowed the city to implement the land transfer tax) from the provincial government in 2006.
This week, Toronto city manager Paul Johnson gave a talk to the U of T's Institute on Municipal Finance and Governance that amounted to a victory lap celebrating the "new deal for Toronto" agreed to by Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow in 2023 (which uploaded the Gardiner and Don Valley Expressway onto the province's books, and gave the city hundreds of millions of dollars for shelters, housing and transit), and outlining what the next phase of that might look like.
See, if you hang around this place long enough, you can watch the new deal everyone demanded and then celebrated become old, as everyone starts demanding a new new deal, and then just as that one is being celebrated we're all on the lookout for the new new new deal that'll be coming up next.
It can feel like the revolving-door trend cycle of seasonal fashion or Top 40 pop music. Like Grandpa Simpson said, "I used to be with 'it,' but then they changed what 'it' was. Now what I'm with isn't 'it' anymore, and what's 'it' seems weird and scary."
Except that in the case of new deals for Toronto, they don't really change what "it" is all that much. Mostly, "it" is money.
Now, the calls for a new deal often involve asking for more autonomy and authority for cities, too. That's top of mind for some right now, with Ford bigfooting Toronto city council yet again to rip out bike lanes. NDP leader Marit Stiles, in appointing Miller, specifically called out Ford's "meddling" as part of the problem. Miller's own 2006 new deal was one of the rare occasions where municipal autonomy was actually addressed, giving the mayor and council specific powers, while delivering not dollars but the new authority to raise their own.
But usually it's mostly about money. You could add other celebrated deals over the years to the list, uploading transit construction projects or sharing revenue, and the bottom line of all of them is, well, the bottom line.
Johnson's speech on Tuesday generally had a celebratory, pep-talk vibe to it. In sitcom terms, his demeanour is like that of Ted Lasso, in contrast to city managers of earlier eras such has Joe Pennachetti, who generally came off like Lou Grant on a particularly exasperating day.
But at one point Johnson hinted at the reason why this new deal leads immediately to talk of the next one. The highway uploads freed $1.9 billion that went immediately to address the city's infrastructure backlog. On the bright side, as Johnson said, "The City of Toronto doesn't have too many $1.9 billion days." On the other side, that cash went toward addressing a funding deficit that stood at $26 billion.
Only 14 new deals away from clearing the overdue infrastructure backlog! Whoo-hoo!
Or maybe one deal, if it was the right one. When he was mayor, Miller spent a lot of time campaigning for a share of the sales taxes - "one cent of the GST," meaning essentially a one-per-cent sales tax (or a 1/13th share of today's existing HST) given directly to cities. This has kind of remained the Holy Grail of potential "new deal" funding tools for many city watchers.
Because it would: a) produce a truckload of money by municipal standards; b) would be ongoing and predictable; and c) would grow when the economy and inflation grow and expenses go up.
Johnson claims he's "agnostic" on that or any other particular revenue source - specifically because he doesn't want the city to fixate on one way to skin the cat if that way is a complete non-starter for the premier or prime minister sitting on the other side of the negotiating table. But his explanation of the city's situation made it pretty clear that what's needed is something that shares those essential characteristics.
If Miller antes up with something like that in the NDP platform, will Ford and Bonnie Crombie need to match those table stakes in drafting their own new deal proposals? A new deal to end all new deals? As a provincial election issue?
Fat chance, maybe. But if you've been around long enough watching the let's-make-a-new-deal cycle, perhaps any kind of chance is worth hoping on.
CAPTION: The provincial NDP tapped former Toronto mayor David Miller to come up with a "new deal" for municipalities if they win the next election. Miller already has negotiating a few "new deals" on his resume.

Subject: Provincial elections; Local elections; Cities

Title: Is another 'new deal' going to save Toronto? Don't let the Ted Lasso talk fool you

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 28, 2024

Document 36 of 500

Bicycle commutes triple Ford's stat: Study reveals more Torontonians cycle than premier has argued in his battle against bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bicycle-commutes-triple-fords-stat/docview/3133627228/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bicycle+commutes+triple+Ford%27s+stat%3A+Study+reveals+more+Torontonians+cycle+than+premier+has+argued+in+his+battle+against+bike+lanes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unreleased $6.2-million transportation study obtained by the Star reveals more Torontonians cycle than Premier Doug Ford has claimed in his crusade to restrict bike lanes.
The Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning, triple the "one per cent" Ford often cites.
That number jumps to 3.6 per cent of those who reported using their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips were made.
Although that's lower than some cycling advocates have argued in opposing Ford's removal of bike lanes on Yonge Street, Bloor Street and University Avenue, bicycle use has been steadily climbing since the 2006 survey.
Testifying last week at the legislative committee examining the bill, Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, said the study should be made public to better inform the debate around bike lanes.
Asked at committee by Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon (Beaches-East York) if it would "be helpful if the government released the Transportation Tomorrow Survey," the high-ranking municipal bureaucrat nodded.
"Yes. We have access to it as a partner in it and we believe that it should be available for public consumption and planning," said Hayward, who spoke out against the province's legislation and questioned the use of "very old data" by the government.
"The Transportation Tomorrow Survey 2022 is available and it demonstrates numbers that are very different from that," she said, adding in some parts of the city "closer to 10" per cent cycle.
"In wards that are served by the bike lanes in question, the numbers are staggering and higher."
As first reported by the Star, city officials used the data in the provincial study in a confidential staff report to council this month.
Under the new law, which passed Monday, municipalities must seek provincial approval for any bike lanes that remove vehicular lanes. Intended to reduce traffic gridlock, critics have warned it will make cycling less safe because existing Toronto infrastructure will also be removed.
The exhaustive survey - co-ordinated by the Ministry of Transportation in partnership with 24 municipalities and transit agencies, including the City of Toronto, the TTC and Metrolinx - is normally conducted every five years and involves thousands of interviews.
Done in fall 2022 and spring 2023, it was supposed to be made public last month, but has remained under wraps as Ford's Progressive Conservatives pushed through controversial measures designed to ease congestion.
"No decision is sought for the release, given it is regular ministry business to release the results once the survey is complete," says the 38-page "preliminary findings draft" PowerPoint deck obtained by the Star.
It found "an increase in walking and cycling trips was observed, especially in Toronto" since the last survey that was done in 2016. (The scheduled 2021 survey was delayed a year by the COVID-19 pandemic.)
Asked about mode of transportation in the "AM Peak," 3.1 per cent of people in Toronto aged 11 and up commuted by bicycle, while 13 per cent walked.
About 46 per cent of Torontonians drove their own cars or trucks in the morning, with another 11 per cent being passengers in private vehicles and 25 per cent took public transit.
The numbers change slightly when surveyed about "all day" modes of transportation: 3.6 per cent rode bikes, 50 per cent drove, 12 per cent were passengers, 22 per cent took transit, and 10 per cent walked.
For the rest of the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, 66 per cent drove their own vehicles in the morning, with another 13 per cent passengers, eight per cent walked, six per cent took transit and one per cent cycled.
Asked about "all day" transportation use, 70 per cent drove, 16 per cent were passengers, five per cent walked, five per cent took transit and 0.9 per cent used bicycles.
Toronto had by far the most daily cycling trips of any municipality in the survey with 164,806, followed by Waterloo with 20,995, 18,822 in Hamilton, 17,047 in Peel, 14,568 in York, and 11,959 in Halton.
That compares to 52,171 daily users in Toronto of paid ride share services like Uber. In Peel, there were 14,426 daily ride share users with York at 6,066, Halton at 4,221, and Durham at 3,595.
"One per cent of the population cannot take up 50 per cent of the roads," the premier said Monday in Burlington.
But the survey found cycling is continuing to grow in popularity - one per cent of Torontonians did their morning commute by bike in 2006, 1.6 per cent in 2011, 2.5 per cent in 2016 and 3.1 per cent in 2022.
Similarly, 1.1 per cent of Torontonians reported "all day" riding trips in the 2006 study, 1.9 per cent in 2011, 2.7 per cent in 2016 and 3.6 per cent in 2022.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Ford government mulls three new power plants: Province to release new energy plan early next year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-mulls-three-new-power-plants/docview/3133627224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+mulls+three+new+power+plants%3A+Province+to+release+new+energy+plan+early+next+year&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a push to meet forecasts for soaring electricity demand in the next 25 years, Ontario is testing support for new power plants - possibly nuclear - in three locations.
Ontario Power Generation is being tasked with beginning discussions with municipal, community and Indigenous leaders for sites it owns in Port Hope, just east of the Darlington nuclear plant, Nanticoke on the north shore of Lake Erie near Port Dover, and along the St. Clair River south of Sarnia.
"With demand continuing to grow, it is clear we must build for the future and plan ahead," Energy Minister Stephen Lecce said Wednesday. New plants are needed to meet demand from 2035 onwards and the government will consider "all types of new energy generation," he added.
Calling more nuclear power "the only way we can reduce (carbon) emissions" while increasing the electricity supply, Lecce hinted at the need for new power plants last month when the province's Independent Electricity System Operator issued a forecast showing the need for power will soar 75 per cent by 2050.
That's the equivalent of adding four-and-a-half cities the size of Toronto to the grid.
The community consultations come as Premier Doug Ford's government prepares to release a new provincial energy plan early next year. Lecce has said it will include a "significant expansion" of energy efficiency programs and that nuclear generation will be the "overarching focus."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner called for public hearings on the new energy plan and urged the government to emphasize renewables like solar, wind and storage as costs fall for those sources of electricity.
The forecast of soaring demand was up from a previous forecast of a 60 per cent increase in demand by 2050. That increase is fuelled by a continuing surge in energy-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence, population growth, increasing electrification of cars, public transit, home heating, electric vehicle battery plants and steelmaking as it transitions to electric arc furnaces from coal.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 38 of 500

Lawyer's letter to alleged victim 'intimidating': Defence asked for help to get spousal abuse charges dropped

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lawyers-letter-alleged-victim-intimidating/docview/3133627192/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lawyer%27s+letter+to+alleged+victim+%27intimidating%27%3A+Defence+asked+for+help+to+get+spousal+abuse+charges+dropped&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a move experts describe as an intimidation tactic, an Ontario defence lawyer sent the complainant in an ongoing intimate-partner violence case a letter asking her to help get the charges dropped against her spouse - the man who allegedly choked her and assaulted her with a bat.
In the letter obtained by the Star, Thornhill-based lawyer Fadi Matthew Kazandji tells the complainant it's "our understanding" that the alleged incidents leading to the charges - assault with a weapon, assault by choking, unlawful confinement and death threats - are the result of "certain misunderstandings" between her and her partner, who live in Halton Region.
This could affect his client's "future employment and life," Kazandji writes. Should it not have been her intention to involve the police or to pursue the case further, Kazandji tells the complainant he'd be pleased to discuss preparing an affidavit - a sworn legal document - outlining that she wants the charges dropped.
"This affidavit would accurately detail the events in question and convey your desire for the prosecution not to proceed," Kazandji writes.
"There will be no criminal charges against you for rectifying the issue and providing truthful information at this stage."
Kazandji did not return the Star's multiple requests for comment.
While defence lawyers in intimate-partner violence cases can communicate with any potential witness, including the complainant, victimologists argue the letter crosses the line by pushing the woman toward dropping the charges while offering inappropriate legal advice - after all, a defence lawyer is in no position to speak for police about whether a complainant will face consequences for saying an alleged incident was just a big misunderstanding.
"This is intimidating," said Jo-Anne Wemmers, a professor in the School of Criminology at Université de Montréal.
"It's a form of psychological violence, and in the literature it's often referred to as legal abuse - how offenders can use the law to continue to intimidate and abuse."
She also took issue with the emphasis on the consequences the charges will have on the accused.
"What about the consequences that it's had on her?" Wemmers said. "I find it a very selfish and self-serving approach."
She said it points to the need for complainants to be able to access free, reliable legal information "to reduce their anxiety and help make the right decision for them, but unfortunately that's not always available."
Victims advocate Jeff Bradley concurred the letter is intimidating, and questioned Kazandji giving legal advice to someone he doesn't represent.
"It seems totally inappropriate; why would somebody's defence lawyer then be giving the survivor guidance?" said Bradley, a PhD candidate in legal studies at Carleton University.
"Intimate-partner violence is an epidemic, and we already know the system doesn't lead to justice for most survivors. And so if this becomes a common practice, you wouldn't even want to try going through the legal system."
Just this week, the Ford government rejected an NDP motion on the International Day for the Elimination of Violence Against Women that would have declared that intimate-partner violence is indeed an epidemic, after 58 women died from it in Ontario last year. An NDP bill that could help increase funding to tackle the problem remains before a legislative committee for study.
Speaking generally, the president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association told the Star that while defence lawyers in IPV cases can contact the complainant prior to a trial, they "must recognize the many dynamics that are almost invariably present in such cases, and they must conduct themselves in a very careful and professional manner.
"A lawyer may not, in most cases, purport to give legal advice to the complainant. Ensuring that the complainant has access to independent legal counsel of their own is a common and wise approach," Boris Bytensky said.
"We should not automatically rush to condemn or criticize defence lawyers who reach out to complainants and other family members in such cases. This contact, if handled properly, is often critically important to ensuring that your client receives a robust defence, and that justice is ultimately done."
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Document 39 of 500

Ottawa vows to tighten border: Trudeau meets with premiers to forge a battle plan against Trump's threat of imposing tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-vows-tighten-border/docview/3133627060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+vows+to+tighten+border%3A+Trudeau+meets+with+premiers+to+forge+a+battle+plan+against+Trump%27s+threat+of+imposing+tariffs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is promising to boost border security to appease Donald Trump in the wake of the incoming U.S. president's threat to slap a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian goods and services.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau convened an emergency meeting with the premiers Wednesday to forge a battle plan after Trump's trade broadside two days earlier.
Trudeau told them that no one should assume the new president might change his mind or is bluffing, so his ultimatum must be taken seriously.
Trump announced Monday he would unilaterally impose the 25 per cent levies on all products from Canada and Mexico on Jan. 20 unless America's northern and southern neighbours stem the tide of illegal immigrants and fentanyl.
Speaking to reporters after Wednesday's first ministers' meeting, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said "we can make additional investments to reassure Canadians that all of the measures necessary are in place and will continue to be in place" at the border.
That means more funding for the RCMP and the Canada Border Services Agency, although LeBlanc had no details on that.
But he warned "we'll continue to tighten the screws" to curb illegal border crossings between both countries.
Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland appealed for a "strong, smart and united" front among the premiers.
"Now is really a moment for us not to squabble amongst ourselves," said Freeland.
"One of the premiers - who I will not name, I'll allow him to speak for himself - said with a little bit of a chuckle, 'I can't believe I just agreed with Prime Minister Trudeau about so many things,' " she said.
A source privy to the talks, and who spoke to the Star confidentially to discuss the meeting, confirmed that it was Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford told his colleagues "we have to tighten up our border on both sides" and implored Ottawa for more CBSA and RCMP funding.
"We don't need a plan a month down the road, we need a plan now," he told reporters shortly before the 5 p.m. confab, in which he also appealed for unity so "we all sing from the same song sheet."
Ford also said Canada needs to go on the offence in a trade war.
"We're going to make sure that we do retaliate - that's the only option if they start putting 25 per cent tariffs," he said, reiterating the unfairness of Trump equating Canada with Mexico when it comes to illegal migrants and drug smuggling.
Afterward, he issued a statement saying that "the federal government has been slow to react and is stuck on its backfoot," although sources said he was more collegial and less critical behind closed doors.
Quebec Premier François Legault said Trump's concerns are "legitimate," but suggested he would not support Canada retaliating with reciprocal tariffs, saying "the last thing we want is a trade war."
But many other Canadian business leaders and premiers have adopted Ford's harder line in reaction to Trump's gambit, and Trudeau's government is prepared to impose retaliatory levies on U.S. products.
A source said Freeland told the premiers that Ottawa is drafting a list of American products that could be subjected to dollar-for-dollar tariffs in retaliation for Trump's aggressive moves - as they did during his first term in 2018.
In his announcement on Monday, Trump, who previously vowed to impose 60 per cent tariffs on China, also pledged an additional 10 per cent.
His social media posts reverberated around the world, with markets and leaders taking notice. The U.S. dollar rose in relation to the Canadian dollar and Mexican peso.
For all the Liberals' advance preparation, the tweets landed as a bombshell.
Trudeau's immediate response was to call Trump and explain Canada is not Mexico, is not the same source of illegal immigration or fentanyl and wishes to work together to address issues at the border.
That chat, described as friendly and non-confrontational, did not result in Trump dialing down his rhetoric.
Trudeau has also appealed to opposition parties in the House of Commons to back a "Team Canada" approach.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rejected the prime minister's ability to speak for Canada, saying Trudeau is too "weak" to face the "unprecedented threat" of Trump's tariffs, and that "foreign leaders believe they can walk all over him."
"Our economy is weakened, our borders are weakened, our military is weakened and most of all, our prime minister is weakened," said Poilievre, calling for an election public opinion polls suggest he would win.
Quebec's Legault signalled he, too, does not trust Canada to represent his province's interests, and publicly demanded that a Quebec representative be part of any talks with the Trump administration, saying Ottawa should not "offer compromises" that would protect Alberta's oil and Ontario's auto sector, but imperil Quebec's aerospace, aluminum, forestry and agricultural products.
In reality, Trump does not view tariffs as a solely economic negotiation.
His nominee for Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent, wrote in a column for Fox News less than two weeks ago saying that Trump has effectively used tariffs in the past as "a negotiating tool with our trading partners" and for achieving foreign policy objectives.
"Whether it is getting allies to spend more on their own defence, opening foreign markets to U.S. exports, securing co-operation on ending illegal immigration and interdicting fentanyl trafficking, or deterring military aggression, tariffs can play a central role," he wrote.
"Our size gives us market power and the ability to dictate terms - other countries need us more than we need them. We have but to use that power."
With Files From Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With Files From Rob Ferguson

Document 40 of 500

Can Doug Ford really stop cyclists from suing over bike lane removal? Here's what the legal experts say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/can-doug-ford-really-stop-cyclists-suing-over/docview/3133627044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Doug+Ford+really+stop+cyclists+from+suing+over+bike+lane+removal%3F+Here%27s+what+the+legal+experts+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cyclists might want to sue the province for removing bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University, but even if they could, they're likely facing an uphill battle.
The Ford government's Bill 212, which was pushed through Queen's Park on Monday, forces municipalities to get provincial approval for any new bike lanes that remove a traffic lane, while also taking special aim at Toronto's bike lanes on arterial streets. A late addendum to the legislation also protects the province against any lawsuits from people or their families from suing if someone is killed or injured on a road where a bike lane was removed, a move that caused outcry from advocates and opposition party leaders.
Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria have blamed the bike lanes on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue for Toronto's infamous congestion problems, though city data paints a different picture.
"It doesn't make sense to rip up some of our busiest roads in the entire city to accommodate a very few percentage of people that use their bikes," said Sarkaria, who has not yet determined if sections of the existing lanes will be retained or when dismantling would begin.
Can the Ford government stop cyclists' lawsuits?
The short answer? Yes. "The government can legislate whatever it wants," said James Sayce, a partner with Koskie Minsky LLP.
It's a move that the Ford government has tried before. In 2019, the government tried to modify the Crown Liability and Proceedings Act to shield itself from negligence lawsuits. That section of the bill was shot down by the Ontario Court of Appeal in 2021, in a case that Sayce argued. Similarly, a class-action suit before the courts around the province's handling of COVID-19 in long-term-care homes was certified despite the liability protections the government has in the Crown Liability and Proceedings Act.
"There is a history of this government trying to immunize itself from lawsuits," said Sayce. "And so, either successfully or unsuccessfully, it appears to be interested in limiting its own liability."
There's also a fine line in what people can sue the government for, and legislation is careful to note what lawsuits are allowed, Erik Knutsen, a law professor with Queen's University, explained.
"They only took away the right to say, 'Don't try and overturn this decision on the back end of a negligence lawsuit about a bike accident, because that's not going to work,'" said Knutsen.
The legislation still allows for lawsuits if a contractor injures someone in the process of removing the bike lanes and still leaves room for a lawsuit if, for example, the road is damaged or changed after the bike lanes are removed, which then causes an injury.
But still, Knutsen is skeptical that any lawsuit that blames the removal of bike lanes for an injury or death would go through, because the plaintiff would have to demonstrate that the removal of the bike lanes caused their injury, instead of the driver's actions.
"If you look at basic negligence law ... it's a pretty hard case to say that, 'But for that the are the removal of bike lanes, I would not have been injured,'" said Knutsen. "I don't know how plaintiffs would do that."
And, even if a class-action or personal injury suit were to go through Ontario's court system, it could take years, or even decades, for a case to get to trial.
"There are larger systemic issues where the courts are overburdened and they're underfunded," said Sayce. "So, part of the calculus is no one's going to bother (with a bike lane lawsuit), right? People will stay away, because it becomes so difficult to litigate these cases."
CAPTION: The Ford government has preemptively banned lawsuits against it for its controversial bike lane legislation. Is that legal?

Subject: Legislation; Negligence; Class action lawsuits; Bicycling

Title: Can Doug Ford really stop cyclists from suing over bike lane removal? Here's what the legal experts say

Document 41 of 500

Letters to the editor: Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat raises fears of recession in Canada, Nov. 16 Sheridan College announces major program cuts and layoffs amid international student cap, Nov. 26 Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Nov. 26, and Ontario proposes fast-tracking drug-consumption-site bill, Nov. 26

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3133626815/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor%3A+Trump%27s+25+per+cent+tariff+threat+raises+fears+of+recession+in+Canada%2C+Nov.+16+Sheridan+College+announces+major+program+cuts+and+layoffs+amid+international+student+cap%2C+Nov.+26+Doug+Ford%27s+government+pushes+through+law+restricting+bike+lanes%2C+Nov.+26%2C+and+Ontario+proposes+fast-tracking+drug-consumption-site+bill%2C+Nov.+26&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Apply Trump's logic to our illegal gun problem
Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat raises fears of recession in Canada, Nov. 16
Countries have been fighting drugs for a very long time. The Opium War was in the 1840s. No one has figured out how to solve the drug problem, including Donald Trump in his first term in office. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should tell Trump he is putting a 25 per cent tariff on U.S. products until he stops the thousands of guns entering Canada through the United States, bringing higher levels of violent crime. Last time I checked, countries are responsible for securing their own borders. This is not something you outsource to another country.
Michael Yaffe, Toronto
Trump should focus on reducing demand for drugs
Canada is not the cause of America's problems with drugs and illegal aliens, yet Donald Trump feels compelled to throw Canada under the bus. Trump should should work on reducing Americans' demand for fentanyl. This is headline-grabbing nonsense and Canada should continue to have meaningful discussions with state and business leaders behind the scenes. Hopefully these people can deliver a dose of reality to the Trump administration.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
If Trump hands you a lemon,
drink apple juice
Canada should put a 100 per cent tariff on orange juice. We do not produce any orange juice. We get it all from the U.S. If Canadians want to keep drinking it, they will have to pay up. Or they can switch to apple juice, which we produce ourselves. Tropicana will run to Washington complaining they have lost a market the size of California due to tariffs.
John Vipond, Oakville
Trump's tariff is a negotiating tool
Everyone needs to take a deep breath and relax. U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has a history of making threats to convince other countries to take action on a variety of issues, and it has worked. Trump understands the negative economic consequences for the U.S. of the threatened tariff if implemented. It is a negotiating tool. He has made the threat now to give the countries affected sufficient opportunity before he takes office to take the actions he has indicated would remove the threat. Smart, not unhinged.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
Canada needs to review trading relationships
A quick fix to bring Donald Trump's bullying tactics to heel would be an equivalent export tax on all energy resources and raw materials exported to the United States. Canada is America'slargest energy source andsecond-biggest supplier of non-energy mineral resources after China. Canada should not cringe in fear of Trump's tactics of bullying and misinformation or cave in to his demands. We need to use whatever tools we have available to work out a reasonable fair-trade deal that benefits both partners. Without that, it is no longer a trade deal but exploitation and no partnership. Trump will try a workaround to existing agreements and we must be prepared to act in kind.
We also need to review our trading relationships. Expanding our trading relationships with other partners would make us far less susceptible to Trump's tactics. We must also expand our military capacity. National service and our own military supply chain and sources could create thousands of new jobs across Canada in manufacturing. If we love our country, is it not worth defending it economically and militarily?
Robert Holden,Keswick
Seneca shows lack of respect for students, faculty
Sheridan College announces major program cuts and layoffs amid international student cap, Nov. 26
On Tuesday at 2:52 p.m., a school-wide email informed Sheridan College faculty and students of the suspension of 40 academic programs, reducing the student body by about 30 per cent. This includes my own program, Creative Writing & Publishing, a program uniquely designed to combine both aspects of the publishing industry into a singular, comprehensive degree. This news comes amid already rocky relations between the college board and teachers' union; faculty received news of program closures at the same time as the student body. For students, this is hard enough, but to notify faculty of imminent mass layoffs in such a manner shows deep disrespect from the board. At its core, the decision is rooted in low levels of funding and a lack of respect for post-secondary education. This must be the topic of discussions going forward; without both, post-secondary education in Ontario will continue to suffer.
Quin Braden, Mississauga
What price will Ford pay to get re-elected?
Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Nov. 26, and Ontario proposes fast-tracking drug-consumption-site bill, Nov. 26
On Wednesday, the Star reported that Premier Doug Ford is fast tracking legislation that will rip up bike lanes on busy streets, essentially legalizing brazen vandalism. Later that day it was announced that the legislation to shut down safe injection sites will be pushed through without public consultations. Neither of these initiatives is supported by evidence. Both will lead to increased fatalities. We must ask ourselves how many lost Ontario lives the premier is prepared to accept as collateral damage in his bid for re-election. Fifty? One hundred? One thousand? More?
Patrick McDonald, Toronto
Ford is failing the people of Ontario
There is something maliciously pigheaded about a government that passes legislation to make the public roads less safe for its citizens, but then also absolves itself of the responsibility for doing so. Even more so, when government action on important matters, like fixing the health-care crisis, improving our education system, working to provide more housing, and addressing the homelessness problem, is ignored.
Throughout his time in office, Premier Doug Ford has wasted his mandate on the petty and the inconsequential. We have to do better for our province than this failure of a politician.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
