Document 89 of 500

Think there's a 'war on the car'? Here's the truth behind the rhetoric in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/think-theres-war-on-car-heres-truth-behind/docview/3132134626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Think+there%27s+a+%27war+on+the+car%27%3F+Here%27s+the+truth+behind+the+rhetoric+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For decades, some have bleated on and on about a "war on the car."
Any tiny improvement in road safety for pedestrians, cyclists or even drivers themselves has been met with complaints. Even the act of asking (begging, really) for things that can save life or limb is greeted with alarm from some as if it were part of this "war."
So what if we declare an actual "war," then?
Clearly one side of this debate is itching to do so by using, even metaphorically, the word "war" to describe the excruciatingly slow and incremental addition of safe infrastructure in Toronto. The Ford government's Bill 212 to remove some of those relatively modest improvements is - to lean into the ridiculous rhetoric - an act of war. So is the surprise amendment banning lawsuits against the province if cyclists are killed or injured where lanes are removed. That's a clear indication they know what they're doing could grievously harm people.
Consider how the safe infrastructure fight is waged instead. Bike lanes on Bloor took decades of advocacy, but because a small group of business people with Progressive Conservative connections called "Balance on Bloor" have the premier's ear, as reported by The Trillium, they might be removed quickly. That such a move is politically popular with the majority of Ontarians, even if some live outside of or far from Toronto, gives Ford the cover he needs to make it.
The handful of high-profile lanes notwithstanding, most streets in Toronto have zero bike infrastructure. The ironic shamelessness of including "balance" in the name of the advocacy group demonstrates a swaggering disrespect to Ontarians and disregard of good faith.
Good faith is important here. Bike lane implementation required studies, engineering reports and often extensive public consultation. Meanwhile, opponents of safer streets have been treated with kid gloves. Toronto police admitted to giving up on traffic enforcement, gaslighting residents by saying they were doing so. A city walk today is rife with red-light running, rolling stops and other aggressive behaviour.
Businesses leery or even against lanes were also treated the same, presented with studies showing businesses can thrive with lanes and cyclists are good customers. Business groups like Bloor Annex BIA have even spoken up and made that case themselves.
The response to that good faith has been "Balance on Bloor" and Bill 212.
As reported by the Star, the Ford government used outdated cycling ridership figures while new, higher, numbers have not been released publicly. Trillium reported that the draft of a briefing document prepared for Ford's cabinet contained research that demonstrated removing bike lanes could actually worsen congestion, but it hasn't been discussed publicly either. Why not be transparent? Why the bad faith?
All this obfuscation and scapegoating hides the truth from drivers: the main enemy in any perceived war is other drivers. Only fewer cars on the road will solve the issue of congestion.
In this rhetorical war, cyclists are often characterized as soft, weak or even "woke," but there is nothing tougher than a cyclist in Toronto. They ride through heat, cold, rain and, yes, even the handful of days Toronto gets snow. Adults and children ride on fraught streets bereft of safe infrastructure. Rarely can a route from A to B be completed entirely using safe infrastructure, though until Bill 212 we were heading in the right direction.
Toronto cyclists and pedestrians contend with some drivers who use their vehicles as weapons. Drivers who creep toward them when using a crosswalk, as if trying to get them out of the way faster. When almost nailed by a driver's actions, pedestrians and cyclists frequently get a little happy wave of "sorry!" like it's no big deal. That's a microcosm of the problem in this debate: it's life or death, but one side doesn't see that.
But before heading into battle, it's worth asking if anyone would actually win such a war. If the cyclists stop shopping at the businesses fighting safety measures such as protected bike lanes, would that satisfy anyone? If bike lanes disappear, and every cyclist takes up an entire lane of traffic instead (calling it the "Doug Ford Express") who would come out ahead? It may be legally allowed under the Highway Traffic Act, and likely to happen when the separated lanes disappear, but it would also be extremely risky - to the person on two wheels and the one driving the car.
If declaring a "war on the car" seems extreme, it's simply a reflection of the rules of engagement and rhetoric established by Ford and those opposed to safer streets. If they accuse cyclists and pedestrians of waging war, a war they should get.
CAPTION: Two cyclists make their way along the bike lane next to backed-up cars along east-bound Bloor Street W in Etobicoke in this October image.

Document 90 of 500

Women are still not worthy of ordination in the Catholic Church

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/women-are-still-not-worthy-ordination-catholic/docview/3132134611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Women+are+still+not+worthy+of+ordination+in+the+Catholic+Church&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Women are leading the way in the Catholic Church, Nov. 19
In her praise of the Catholic Church's recognition of a few women, letter writer Anne MacCarthy completely skirts the reality that women are still not considered worthy of ordination. She could have included Catherine of Siena, a medieval mystic and pious laywoman, who engaged in papal and Italian politics through extensive letter writing and advocacy, before dying of anorexia nervosa. But even that bold saint knew her place. Retired journalist Janice Kennedy nailed it. That women are the inferior is an unswerving belief, solidly entrenched after two millennia, in the Catholic Church. As a boy, thoroughly immersed in Catholicism, I never questioned why my chums and I could serve as altar boys but girls of our age could not. There is a glimmer of hope now. Since 1994, girls can serve at Mass. But why not the priesthood? When considering the confessional, many women would prefer mumbling about very personal experiences to someone of their own gender. To the Catholic Church, tradition is as sacred as slivers from the True Cross. To be ordained, and to do God's work, it is essential that one has the proper genitals. Catholic women should realize that, like Martha in the New Testament, your continued service to the Church will be seen as an act of service and devotion. It's always been that way.
Garry Burke, Oro-Medonte, ON
Taxpayers deserve to know the costs of Ford's projects up front
Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18
The Ontario government, which prides itself on strong fiscal management, does a poor job of providing cost estimates for new infrastructure. It won't make public the price of ripping out Toronto's safe streets nor the cost of paving the Greenbelt to build Highway 413. Why all the secrecy? If Ontario has nothing to hide, it should come clean and tell us what taxpayers will shell out for these destructive projects. When we buy a loaf of bread for a few dollars we're told the price before we get to the cash register. Don't we deserve the same information before we spend millions or billions?
Most Canadian cities could use a population boost
There's a growing crisis of young people fleeing the GTA, Nov. 16
One of the problems with Canada is the demographic trend of the huge influx of population growth concentrated in the GTHA. With a yearly increase of 75,000 and almost 1/5th of the population of Canada, isn't it about time that we start encouraging young people to move elsewhere? Most Canadian cities could use an increase in population to stimulate their economies and maybe we could slow down the paving of our farmlands and forests.
Jerry Penttila, Brampton
Releasing names of persons not found guilty of wrongdoing serves no public good
Canada must confront history of harbouring Nazis, Nov. 18
The Commission of Inquiry on War Criminals, headed by Justice Jules Deschênes, dismissed claims about how "thousands" of "Nazi war criminals" were hiding in Canada as "grossly exaggerated." He ordered the Commission's subject files be kept confidential to protect the privacy of hundreds of innocent Canadians and their descendants. Releasing the names of persons not found guilty of wrongdoing during the Second World War serves no public good. Bernie Farber, a former CEO of the Canadian Jewish Congress, seems to be forgetting how Irwin Cotler, who represented the CJC before the Deschênes Commission, publicly welcomed its findings. That's a fact. I know because I was there. So why is Farber regurgitating this canard yet again?
Lubomyr Luciuk, Kingston
And I can't believe it takes 13-plus years to finish the Eglinton LRT
Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18
Transport minister Prabmeet Sarkharia can't believe the exorbitant cost to rip out the bike lanes. To him it makes no sense. But neither does a 13-going-on-14-year wait for the Eglinton Crosstown LRT with no end in sight seem reasonable. Yet here we are. Why not give Toronto taxpayers, the losers in this transit debacle -- coincidently entirely under the control of the provincial government -- an opening date and an apology for the multiyear delay and we'll provide them with a cost-benefit analysis on the bike path removal. Sound like a fair trade-off?
Richard Kadziewicz, Scarborough
The province doesn't pay for anything, we taxpayers foot the bills
In the squabble about Toronto's bike lanes, Premier Doug Ford's wish to remove them, and the cost of doing so, the minister of transportation noted that the province would be footing the bill. "The province" doesn't pay for anything. Whatever costs Ford is racking up, whether it's sending cheques to his rich friends, cutting up contracts (The Beer Store), or ripping out bike lanes, it is taxpayers who are paying for his whims. Those tax dollars could fund better health care, education, affordable housing and a better quality of life for the people of Ontario.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
CAPTION: Ralph Fiennes as Cardinal Lawrence in 'Conclave.'

Document 91 of 500

The birth of 'President's Choice' and other fascinating tales for the business reader on your holiday gift list

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/birth-presidents-choice-other-fascinating-tales/docview/3132134485/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+birth+of+%27President%27s+Choice%27+and+other+fascinating+tales+for+the+business+reader+on+your+holiday+gift+list&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Christmas approaches, it's time to think of enlightening books for the businesspeople on your gift-giving list.
The timeless books highlighted here, with their lessons on tempering bold risks with caution and staying close to the customer, have become more relevant since their initial publication.
Fly by Wire: The Geese, the Glide, The Miracle on the Hudson.
William Langewiesche (2009; Farrar, Straus and Giroux).
This is a rip-roaring story with insights on human ingenuity, fatal arrogance, and corporate cupidity on each of its succinct 193 pages.
Its two heroes are Chesley "Sully" Sullenberger, who safely landed his crippled Airbus A320 on the Hudson River in 2009, and Bernard Ziegler, the engineering czar at Airbus SE who made Sully's miracle possible.
Ziegler convinced Airbus to adopt his invention of "fly-by wire," a revolutionary automatic flight-control system that has helped the world's more than 300,000 airline pilots save thousands of lives.
Airbus introduced fly-by-wire in 1983; it has since been adopted by Boeing and other aircraft makers. Ziegler, a former French Air Force fighter pilot who saw action in the Algerian war, knew that wise pilots acknowledge their fallibility.
But others, stricken by hubris, recommit to a mistake rather than admit error and correct it.
In telling the author his motive for making a plane as goof-proof as possible, Ziegler cites a widely applicable adage: "To err is human, but to persist is diabolical."
The Edible Man: Dave Nichol, President's Choice and the Making of Popular Taste
Anne Kingston. (1994, Macfarlane, Walter & Ross).
This award-winning book tells the story of marketer Dave Nichol's role in building Loblaw Cos. from a sleepy Ontario supermarket chain into one of the world's biggest grocery retailers.
That transformation, starting in the early 1980s, was largely accomplished with the triumph of Nichol's private label "President's Choice" goods over pricier and sometimes inferior national brands, available exclusively at Loblaws and its sister chains No Frills, Fortinos, Provigo and others.
Nichol constantly refreshed the line of PC products with new and often improbable items like Memories of Szechwan Peanut Sauce and gourmet Italian dog food, obliging Loblaw patrons to visit frequently for the next talked-about PC products.
These were showcased in Nichol's chatty and widely read "Insider's Report" flyer. Kingston also opens windows to the often-secretive worlds of new-product development and the strategies by which food purveyors try to change consumer tastes.
Nichol's Loblaws was a precursor to personality driven enterprises led by Elon Musk and his ilk, where the chief product is the ethos created by the marketer.
Father Son & Co.: My Life at IBM and Beyond
Thomas J. Watson Jr. and Peter Petre. (1990; Bantam Books)
This acclaimed memoir tells two important stories, of IBM's bet-the-company investment in the mainframe computers that would become ubiquitous at public and private institutions worldwide in the mid-20th century; and the difficult relationship between the author and his larger-than-life namesake father, founder of the modern IBM, whose punch-card tabulating machines had about 90 per cent of the U.S. market.
Yet it was Watson Jr., CEO from 1956 to 1971, whose IBM would succeed where others failed in embracing a disruptive technology at the expense of a lucrative existing business destined for obsolesce.
Watson Jr. succeeded with a "family" of progressively more powerful yet compatible mainframes, dubbed System/360, by which customers could easily trade up as their needs grew.
The book offers a winsome model of visionary action that is disciplined by careful risk calculation. It also tells of the fights between father and son that the author describes as "savage, primal, and unstoppable."
The story is relevant in a contemporary Canadian economy where family feuds over succession have jeopardized some of the country's largest enterprises.
Accidental Empires: How the Boys of Silicon Valley Make Their Millions, Battle Foreign Competition, and Still Can't Get a Date
Robert X. Cringely. (1992; HarperBusiness)
Meet the young men, some almost frightfully immature, who helped launch the Information Age in which we now live.
This comprehensive history might also be the most entertaining saga of how computing devices became everyday appliances.
The revolution started with the desktop PCs that scruffy tech entrepreneurs like Bill Gates and Steve Jobs kept tweaking until they became more powerful than the room-sized mainframe computers that had ruled computing since the 1960s.
The book is an immersive experience in which the reader is a first-hand witness to the fierce debates among inventors over their competing operating systems and hardware designs.
At times the geeks collaborated, at others they sabotaged each other, all the while outfoxing IBM and the other tech giants of their day.
There's a lesson here on the speed and daring of innovation in a wide-open field.
Eventually the boys' fledgling enterprises became today's Microsoft, Apple and Oracle, bureaucratic near-monopolies with limited innovative prowess, just like the earlier giants they supplanted.
Happy holidays, and happy reading!
CAPTION: fly_by_wire.JPGThe_edible_man.JPGFather_Son.JPGAccidental_empires.JPGFrom an insider's story of family succession to the Silicon Valley man-boys who launched the Information Age, writes David Olive, here are four classics that need to be on the bookshelf of every business reader.

Subject: Books; Pilots

Location: Silicon Valley-California; United States--US

Company / organization: Name: Airbus SAS; NAICS: 336411, 336412, 336413

Document 92 of 500

Crombie fires back at Ford with her first campaign ad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-fires-back-at-ford-with-her-first/docview/3132035774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+fires+back+at+Ford+with+her+first+campaign+ad&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is fighting back.
The Liberal leader finally launched her first campaign commercial Friday after enduring millions of dollars in attack ads from Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
Taking aim at Ontario's chronic doctor shortage at a time when some 2.5 million people don't have a family physician, Crombie's advertisement says people deserve "better."
"Every day I hear that you can't find a doctor," she intones over footage of a diverse group of Ontario families going about their daily lives.
"Your kid's school is overcrowded and you can't afford the essentials. You feel stuck," says Crombie before a veiled reference to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now being investigated by the RCMP.
"And every day, you hear about another one of Doug Ford's friends getting richer while you fall behind. Enough is enough," she says.
"Your government should be working for you. As your premier, I'll make sure we're getting the basics right: your health care, your kids' education, a home and a life you can actually afford, and a strong economy that brings opportunity back to Ontario so you can get ahead."
The one-minute spot will initially be online only, but the party has plans to air it on more costly broadcast television in the weeks ahead.
Crombie's salvo comes after an 11-month blitz of Tory ads attacking her, which polls suggest have been effective for the governing party.
It's believed the Tories have spent about $10 million of party donors' money on their ads targeting the rookie Grit leader.
Mindful that polls show Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals are not popular, Crombie's commercial emphasizes her personal brand more than her party's name.
"I'm Bonnie Crombie and I'm here for Ontario," it concludes.
The ad comes as Ford is considering a snap spring election, one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Negative campaigning; Political advertising

Premier Ford don t hide costs from Ontario taxpayers Ford ...[Derived Headline]: Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18 Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18 There's a growing crisis of young people fleeing the GTA, Nov. 16 Canada must confront history of harbouring Nazis, Nov. 18 Women are leading the way in the Catholic Church, Nov. 19

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Ford+don+t+hide+costs+from+Ontario+taxpayers+Ford+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Ford+government+disputes+Toronto%27s+%2448-million+bike+lane+removal+cost%3A+%27It+can%27t+cost+double%2C+Nov.+18+Ford+government+disputes+Toronto%27s+%2448-million+bike+lane+removal+cost%3A+%27It+can%27t+cost+double%2C+Nov.+18+There%27s+a+growing+crisis+of+young+people+fleeing+the+GTA%2C+Nov.+16+Canada+must+confront+history+of+harbouring+Nazis%2C+Nov.+18+Women+are+leading+the+way+in+the+Catholic+Church%2C+Nov.+19&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Ford, don't hide costs from Ontario taxpayers
Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18
The Ontario government, which prides itself on strong fiscal management, does a poor job of providing cost estimates for new infrastructure. It won't make public the price of ripping out Toronto's safe streets nor the cost of paving the Greenbelt to build Highway 413. Why all the secrecy? If Ontario has nothing to hide, it should come clean and tell us what taxpayers will shell out for these destructive projects. When we buy a loaf of bread for a few dollars we're told the price before we get to the cash register. Don't we deserve the same information before we spend millions or billions?
Gideon Forman, Toronto
We the taxpayers foot the bills, not the province
In the squabble about Toronto's bike lanes, Premier Doug Ford's wish to remove them, and the cost of doing so, the minister of transportation noted that the province would be footing the bill. "The province" doesn't pay for anything. Whatever costs Ford is racking up, whether it's sending cheques to his rich friends, cutting up contracts (the Beer Store), or ripping out bike lanes, it is taxpayers who are paying for his whims. Those tax dollars could fund better health care, education, affordable housing and a better quality of life for the people of Ontario.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
13-year wait for Eglinton LRT continues
Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18
Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkharia can't believe the exorbitant cost to rip out the bike lanes. To him it makes no sense. But neither does a 13-going-on-14-year wait for the Eglinton Crosstown LRT with no opening date seem reasonable. Yet here we are. Why not give Toronto taxpayers, the losers in this transit debacle - coincidently entirely under the control of the provincial government - an opening date and an apology for the multi-year delay and we'll provide them with a cost-benefit analysis on the bike path removal. Sound like a fair tradeoff?
Richard Kadziewicz, Scarborough
Most cities could use more people
There's a growing crisis of young people fleeing the GTA, Nov. 16
One of the problems with Canada is the demographic trend of the huge influx of population growth concentrated in the GTHA. With a yearly increase of 75,000 and almost one-fifth of the population of Canada, isn't it about time that we start encouraging young people to move elsewhere? Most Canadian cities could use an increase in population to stimulate their economies, and maybe we could slow down the paving of our farmlands and forests.
Jerry Penttila, Brampton
Releasing names does no public good
Canada must confront history of harbouring Nazis, Nov. 18
The Commission of Inquiry on War Criminals, headed by Justice Jules Deschênes, dismissed claims about how "thousands" of "Nazi war criminals" were hiding in Canada as "grossly exaggerated." He ordered the Commission's subject files be kept confidential to protect the privacy of hundreds of innocent Canadians and their descendants. Releasing the names of persons not found guilty of wrongdoing during the Second World War serves no public good. Bernie Farber, a former CEO of the Canadian Jewish Congress, seems to be forgetting how Irwin Cotler, who represented the CJC before the Deschênes Commission, publicly welcomed its findings. That's a fact. I know because I was there. So why is Farber regurgitating this canard yet again?
Lubomyr Luciuk, Kingston, Ont.
Catholic Church still believes that women are inferior
Women are leading the way in the Catholic Church, Nov. 19
In her praise of the Catholic Church's recognition of a few women, letter writer Anne MacCarthy, completely skirts the reality that women are still not considered worthy of ordination. She could have included Catherine of Siena, a medieval mystic and pious laywoman, who engaged in papal and Italian politics through extensive letter writing and advocacy, before dying of anorexia nervosa. But even that bold saint knew her place. Retired journalist Janice Kennedy nailed it. That women are the inferior is an unswerving belief, solidly entrenched after two millennia, in the Catholic Church. As a boy, thoroughly immersed in Catholicism, I never questioned why my chums and I could serve as altar boys but girls of our age could not. There is a glimmer of hope now. Since 1994, girls can serve at Mass. But why not the priesthood? When considering the confessional, many women would prefer mumbling about very personal experiences to someone of their own gender. To the Catholic Church, tradition is as sacred as slivers from the True Cross. To be ordained, and to do God's work, it is essential that one has the proper genitals. Catholic women should realize that, like Martha in the New Testament, your continued service to the Church will be seen as an act of service and devotion.
Garry Burke, Oro-Medonte, Ont.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Catholic churches; Public good; Cost estimates

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost estimates

Location: Canada

Document 94 of 500

The first minister of misdirection: Why Doug Ford is trying so hard to distract you

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/first-minister-misdirection/docview/3132034433/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+first+minister+of+misdirection%3A+Why+Doug+Ford+is+trying+so+hard+to+distract+you&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Theories abound for why Doug Ford is charging toward an early election like a tween toward Taylor Swift tickets. Or anyone toward Taylor Swift tickets, really.
The Ontario premier wants to ensure he can seek a new mandate while the unpopular Justin Trudeau remains in Ottawa. Or he wants to avoid doing so once Pierre Poilievre has replaced him. Maybe he wants to be judged before the Greenbelt land-swap is fully investigated. Or he wants to run as a guy who can stand up to the potential trade fight of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump. (That's a new one.)
But what if it's simpler than all that? What if Ford just wants to avoid the same fate that has recently befallen acting governments the world over? What if he just wants to avoid the Curse of Incumbency?
Because it is definitely a thing. Incumbent parties have either been turfed or suffered steep losses in elections across the world this year: Britain, France, Japan, South Africa, India and on and on. Most recently, the United States, despite a number of economic indicators that have been pointing in the right direction for months, voted to throw itself into the arms of an unpredictable and previously ousted opposition candidate.
Post-pandemic economic frustration appears to be at the root of angry voter sentiment across the world. In that light, the Ford government's recent policy pronouncements start to make a little sense, even if only from an electoral standpoint. Because in isolation, they are harder to defend.
The push to bring beer, wine and cocktails into everything from gas stations to big-box stores? A perfectly reasonable idea, but one that does not begin to justify the hundreds of millions of dollars that Ford is spending to do it 18 months ahead of schedule. The planned $200 cheques for every Ontarian? A justifiable expense in times of prosperity, but a baffling one when the government is already running a deficit - and all the more absurd when even the wealthiest residents will get the same handout as the poorest. As for the premier's war on bike lanes, it seems much the same as his proposed tunnel under Highway 401: a plan that lacks evidence, or even basic forethought, but does signal to a certain constituency that Doug Ford is Against Gridlock. And For Cars, Especially Large Ones.
If there is a Single Unifying Theory to all of this stuff, it is that they are distractions. Many voters no-doubt welcome the opportunity to avoid separate trips to the LCBO or Beer Store to buy a six pack, but it's not like the previous, somewhat-liberalized alcohol-retail system was a crisis that needed urgent action. Those $200 Good Vibes cheques won't help anyone navigate the troubled health-care system or deal with the ongoing housing shortage. And removing a handful of bike lanes in downtown Toronto will not make a noticeable impact on driving times in the city (otherwise it would already be much faster to travel by vehicle on roads that do not have dedicated bike lanes, which it very obviously isn't.)
But what all those policies will do is attract attention, and give people something to talk about, even if none of it has anything to do with Ontario's more intractable problems. It's government by shiny bauble. Concerned about soaring rents, or a tight labour market or your inability to find a family doctor? Doug Ford does not have a presentable solution.
But do you like BEER? Do you like FREE MONEY? Do you wish your long commute would magically become SHORTER? Well, the PC government has some of that stuff! Or, at least, it will promise you some of that stuff. The truth is Toronto's gridlock will never be solved by tearing up some bike lanes or spending $30 billion on a tunnel. The cost of living crisis won't be fixed with giveaway cheques. We're never getting back the millions Ford spent to buy us all the right to buy a tall can in an Esso a year early. He might as well be promising two cars in every garage and a unicorn in every pot.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based
in suburban Toronto and a regular
contributor to the Toronto Star.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has nothing to offer on the most pressing issues of the day, Scott Stinson writes. All he has is government by shiny bauble.Michelle Mengsu ChangToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor;

Subject: Beer; Elections

Location: United States--US

People: Swift, Taylor

Title: The first minister of misdirection:   Why Doug Ford is trying so hard to distract you

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: OP.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 23, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: Commentary, Opinions

'A unicorn in every pot': Why Doug Ford is so desperate to distract you

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/unicorn-every-pot-why-doug-ford-is-so-desperate/docview/3132033940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+unicorn+in+every+pot%27%3A+Why+Doug+Ford+is+so+desperate+to+distract+you&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 96 of 500

Chow urges Ford to keep cyclists safe: Mayor speaks about her own experience of being injured while riding her bike on Bloor Street

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-urges-ford-keep-cyclists-safe/docview/3132033921/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+urges+Ford+to+keep+cyclists+safe%3A+Mayor+speaks+about+her+own+experience+of+being+injured+while+riding+her+bike+on+Bloor+Street&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford should focus on trying to keep cyclists safe - not on protecting his government from lawsuits over ripped-out bike lanes, Mayor Olivia Chow said Friday.
Chow was reacting to the Ford government's latest move on cycling in Toronto - a surprise amendment to protect the government from lawsuits if cyclists are injured or killed on streets that used to have bike lanes.
The clause is part of Ford's proposed law to rip out city-installed bike lanes on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue, and to have a veto over the installation of future bike lanes that require removal of a vehicle lane, as a way to combat traffic gridlock.
Chow, an avid cyclist who does not own a car, declined to wade into whether removing cyclists' right to sue is legally sound, but said the premier and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria should instead worry about improving safety in a year that has so far seen a record six cyclists killed by motorists on Toronto streets.
"I've been doored (hit by an opening vehicle door) on Bloor Street without a bike lane. Now I ride on Bloor Street with a bike lane and I feel very secure ... Safety is always first," Chow told reporters after an unrelated co-op housing announcement on Queen Street East. She added that many drivers feel safer with cyclists in their own lanes and not weaving in and out of traffic lanes.
She noted the late August death of Navjot Kaur, a 26-year-old woman struck by a Jetta driver from behind while riding with her husband on Burnhamthorpe Road in Etobicoke. The 28-year-old driver faces charges.
"I keep thinking, had that road had a bike lane, what would have happened? Her husband wouldn't be grieving," said Chow, adding there will be more similar cases as cycling, including with Bike Share Toronto, is becoming a more popular option.
"Let's continue to look at the data, continue to talk to the provincial government - let's hope we find a middle ground."
While Ford dodged repeated reporters' questions Friday on the indemnification provisions in the legislation, the premier insisted he has "a phenomenal relationship with Mayor Chow."
"She's such a really good person, a nice person. We may differ sometimes on policies, but we get along very, very well, and we'll sit down and I know that she agrees that the Bloor West ... bike lanes are coming out," he said in Hamilton.
"And don't be shocked if she's standing beside me as we're taking them out."
Chow has not publicly said she agrees with Ford that any section of the Bloor lanes should be removed, and in a statement Friday her spokesperson reiterated that "the mayor and city council do not support ... the province removing municipal infrastructure that keeps people safe on our roads."
However, Chow said last week that critics of the western stretch of the Bloor cycle tracks in Etobicoke "have a point," and the city is exploring compromise measures, such as removing a centre median, that could restore a lane of car traffic while preserving the bikeways.
Sarkaria, a Brampton MPP, told reporters Thursday that removing the right to sue is a way to protect cyclists: "The safer thing for a cyclist to do would be to make a decision to go on streets that are safer. That's what we believe is the right way to do it, is on our secondary streets where there is much less traffic volume and ultimately safer for cyclists and everyone else."
An expert on civic infrastructure told the Star last month that secondary streets won't connect many commuting cyclists to their final destination downtown.
"Eventually people are going to need to be on those (main) streets and if (bike lanes) are too inconvenient or too circuitous, they're just not going to be used," said Matti Siemiatycki, a U of T professor of geography and planning.
Dave Shellnutt, a lawyer who specializes in representing fellow cyclists, said the provision to strip cyclists of the right to sue the province is an attempt by the Ford government to evade responsibility for serious injuries and deaths they know their bike lane removals will cause.
"This is the government saying they know as a result of their actions people will be injured or killed, the government will be responsible, but they want to take away any recourse to hold them accountable for their actions," said Shellnutt, adding he'll examine the legislation to see if it can be challenged in court.
"As a legal professional, a person who commutes in the city and as a dad questioning letting a kid bike once the bike lane is removed, all options are on the table and we'll be looking at everything."
With files from Robert Benzie and Ben Spurr
CAPTION: Proposed legislation by the Doug Ford government would remove the right to sue if a cyclist is injured riding on a road where bike lanes had been removed.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter With files from Robert Benzie and Ben Spurr

Document 97 of 500

Un probleme: As the TDSB expands French Immersion programs to meet growing demand, it's also struggling with a shortage of teachers that's leaving some kids behind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/un-probleme/docview/3132033526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Un+probleme%3A+As+the+TDSB+expands+French+Immersion+programs+to+meet+growing+demand%2C+it%27s+also+struggling+with+a+shortage+of+teachers+that%27s+leaving+some+kids+behind&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jennifer Lynch was hopeful this school year would usher in stability for her son, but by mid-September, the teacher in his Grade 5/6 French Immersion class was suddenly gone for good.
And so it began in Room 341 at Withrow Avenue Junior Public School: a revolving door of supply teachers, most of them English-speaking. And when they weren't available, lunchroom monitors stepped in, which largely amounted to babysitting.
There was little structure, little discipline, little learning, and lots of time outdoors.
"It's frustrating," says Lynch. "Every day these kids were walking into the classroom and having no idea who was going to teach them."
It's a scene that has played out elsewhere as the Toronto District School Board struggles to staff its growing French Immersion programs, against the backdrop of a nationwide teacher shortage, which is especially acute in some areas, including French.
The board is working to improve the recruitment and retention of qualified teachers, but faces significant challenges keeping up with the demand for French Immersion. It's monitoring the impact of opening eight new French Immersion programs in the last three years, so no new sites will open in 2025-26. The issue of supply and demand came up at a recent committee meeting where staff presented enrolment figures.
"It's great that we have robust enrolment, I'm just worried about too much enrolment if we don't have the teachers," said Trustee Shelley Laskin, adding French Immersion classes in her west-end ward were without a permanent teacher.
"I don't want to be offering the program if we can't fully staff all the classrooms," she said at the Oct. 23 meeting, where senior staff shared her concerns. "I think that's completely unfair."
Laskin suggested staff report back on how the TDSB will fill positions next year before confirming registrations for the 2025-26 academic year. The deadline to apply for Early French Immersion, with entry in junior kindergarten, is Nov. 29.
"I just want to try to avoid the situation where parents have enrolled in good faith, or are continuing in the program, and start the school year and there isn't a qualified teacher teaching their child."
Around the start of November, the TDSB had 127 vacancies for teachers in its French programs - a mix of contract and long-term occasional positions. (It needed 103 elementary teachers, and 24 secondary teachers.)
The board says it has French-qualified teachers in other areas, but can't move them because of collective agreements.
Lynch's older daughters have had great experiences with French Immersion, but not her son. Last year was "a disaster" because the teacher had difficulty managing the class, was frequently absent, and kids missed chunks of curriculum, especially in math.
About a dozen of those kids are in Room 341. This year was off to a good start with a new teacher. But days later, she was gone due to unavoidable personal circumstances.
Last month, on curriculum night, there was no teacher. Instead, the vice-principal fielded questions from upset parents, who grew disheartened when they visited another Grade 5/6 French Immersion class down the hall with a permanent teacher.
"It almost wanted to make you cry," says Lynch. "The difference was astonishing. They had been taught certain units and we looked at their workbooks ... (Our kids) didn't even have workbooks. There's no doubt that the kids in Room 341 have been let down."
Laura Sikstrom, whose son is also in Grade 6, says for weeks, no assignment, worksheet, test or library book went home. There was no class trip, no community walk, no chatter about upcoming projects.
"My son describes it as feeling abandoned," says Sikstrom, who worries about the impact on learning and "his sense of being nurtured at school."
School "is supposed to be a place where kids feel safe, welcome and engaged, and it has not been that place," she says.
For weeks, parents wrote to school officials and politicians. Some contemplated moving their kids into the English stream, if it meant having a permanent teacher. Some paid for private tutoring, while others couldn't afford it.
"This is exacerbating inequality in the classroom," wrote Kristin Olson to local MPP Peter Tabuns, NDP representative for Toronto-Danforth. "While the entire class is behind on the curriculum, the kids whose parents cannot afford extra tutoring support are being left even further behind."
"We should never have found ourselves in this position to begin with," says Olson, whose daughter is in the class.
In late October, Tabuns spoke in the legislature about how the kids in Room 341 were preparing to meet their 20th supply teacher.
"Students are demoralized and the parents are completely frustrated," he said. "This is a not a new problem or an isolated problem."
Ontario has three French as a Second Language (FSL) programs. Core French is mandatory from grades 4 to 9, with kids learning French. Boards can offer Extended French and French Immersion, which are optional and include additional subjects taught in French. (French Immersion is more intensive, with more subjects in French.)
"Meeting labour market needs for FSL instruction has become an increasing challenge for all English language public school boards," according to the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, which since 2017 has led a committee to come up with solutions.
It says boards are using different strategies to increase recruitment, retention and professional support, but lasting change will take time.
In an interview, Education Minister Jill Dunlop said the government launched a $12.5-million French-language teacher recruitment and retention strategy in 2021 and last year, when she was post-secondary minister, 110 French-language teacher education spots were added at the University of Ottawa and Université de l'Ontario français in Toronto.
"We do recognize it's an issue," she told the Star's Kristin Rushowy. "It's not just an Ontario issue, but a Canada-wide issue, as well as the teacher shortage, too."
Dunlop has met stakeholder groups and said shortening the two-year teacher training window is among the suggestions she's heard. (To deal with a teacher surplus, the then-Liberal government in 2015 extended teachers college from one year to two and cut enrolment.)
"We are looking at every opportunity in front of us."
Dunlop also said the province is working with the Ontario College of Teachers to "accelerate the certification of internationally trained teachers coming to Ontario, and then also accelerate the training for students who are already in the teaching program to be able to enter classrooms."
(The college offers transitional certificates so those in teacher education programs can get teaching experience while finishing their studies.)
While Dunlop would like to see French qualified teachers in immersion and not end up in English classrooms, in the end, "it is a board decision."
Some boards have barriers to accessing French Immersion, such as admission lotteries, enrolment caps or lack of busing. But the TDSB guarantees admission, with Early French Immersion starting in junior kindergarten and Middle French Immersion starting in Grade 4. (Extended French is being phased out.)
According to a TDSB report, Early French Immersion was introduced in a few Toronto schools in the 1970s. At the time of amalgamation in 1998 it was offered in 41 sites and remained that way until 2009. That's when a new policy was implemented that guaranteed admission, although not to a specific school so kids may need to get bussed. There are now 79 Early French Immersion sites, and 40 Middle French Immersion sites.
The number of students, from kindergarten to Grade 12, in French Immersion and Extended French peaked in 2019-2020 nearing 30,000, about double what it was in 2000. The pandemic saw a decline and in 2023-2024 total enrolment was just above 26,000. However, enrolment in elementary French Immersion in the past two years was the highest ever, nearing 20,000.
Committed to inclusivity and accessibility, the TDSB opened eight new French Immersion sites in the past three years, including one this year at Bannockburn Public School.
"By prioritizing historically underserved areas, TDSB has reduced commute times by creating more geographically local access points for families, and addressed accommodation pressures in densely populated areas," said TDSB spokesperson Zoya McGroarty. "The introduction of a French Immersion program in more schools closer to home directly benefits families, which is crucial in enhancing community involvement and ensuring that all students belong. However, long-term growth in the program has implications, such as staff shortages."
At the committee meeting, TDSB staff said they're turning to "out of the box ideas" for solutions.
They prioritize applicants for French positions and secure them before they're hired by another board, interview candidates year-round, and hire non-certified individuals through French assessments.
They go to career fairs, faculty of education fairs and francophone community events to drum up interest. And they've created a video for prospective hires on how great it is to work at the TDSB and live in Toronto.
Staff said they've been proactive about hiring French-qualified teachers, but that the collective agreements allow contract teachers, even if they've been hired for a specific assignment, to transfer to another position of their choosing. For instance, last spring the TDSB hired 24 teachers for French positions in elementary schools, but three took vacant jobs in other areas.
Trustee Dan MacLean was puzzled the TDSB has qualified French teachers, but "we don't seem to have the ability to require our staff to be able to fill our needs."
"I'm all for teachers being able to select what assignments they would prefer to do, and not do, as long as the system is able to meet its requirements about the programming that we're offering," he said at the meeting.
Helen Victoros, president of Elementary Teachers of Toronto, a local of the Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario (ETFO), says trouble staffing French programs has nothing to do with the collective agreement.
The underlying reasons include increasing program demand, a deterioration of working conditions, and long-standing concerns among educators in these programs, like the lack of appropriate French-language resources, tools, and supports, which creates "exponential workload and thus retention issues."
She said French teachers are often left without professional development opportunities, don't receive curriculum resources and spend a lot of their own time and money seeking out resources and supports.
Similarly, Marisa Gallippi, president of ETFO-Toronto Occasional Teachers' Local, says the province needs to increase funding to address this.
"French Immersion programs are especially impacted, with a concerning trend of newly-hired teachers leaving the profession before reaching the five-year mark," she says. "Many report a 'sink or swim' approach in schools due to a lack of resources and proper support."
She says boards and administrators need to work with French teachers to better understand their challenges and find solutions. For instance, teachers are often expected to create their own resources from scratch because much of what's available isn't current or at the appropriate difficulty level.
When no French-speaking teacher is available - last year, some French Immersion classes had no permanent teacher for much of the year - the TDSB provides students with exposure to French through a virtual library of educational programs and a handbook that's used by English-speaking substitutes.
In early November, the families of Room 341 were relieved to hear a long-term occasional teacher would stay on for the year.
Last week, rather than parent-teacher interviews, there was another curriculum night in the class. This time, kids had workbooks and there was a teacher, who spoke of needing to corral kids' behaviour, introduce expectations, and address learning gaps.
"We didn't get a straight answer on how extensive" the gaps are, says Sikstrom. "I have a lot of sympathy for the teacher because he has come, two months in, into this disaster and he doesn't have a lot of time to get it back on track."
It's a sentiment shared by Olson, who hopes the teacher stays on and the class gets additional resources to get kids up to grade level.
Looking ahead, she says, "I'm cautiously optimistic."
CREDIT: Isabel Teotonio Toronto Star

Subject: Public schools; Parents & parenting; Teaching; Tutoring; Students; Classrooms; Kindergarten; Curricula; Immersion programs; Teacher education; School boards; Families & family life; Committees; French as a second language; French language; Language teachers; Learning

Document 98 of 500

Four classics for the business reader on your gift list

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/four-classics-business-reader-on-your-gift-list/docview/3132033432/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+classics+for+the+business+reader+on+your+gift+list&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Christmas approaches, it's time to think of enlightening books for the businesspeople on your gift-giving list.
The timeless books highlighted here, with their lessons on tempering bold risks with caution and staying close to the customer, have become more relevant since their initial publication.

This is a rip-roaring story with insights on human ingenuity, fatal arrogance and corporate cupidity on each of its succinct 193 pages.
Its two heroes are Chesley "Sully" Sullenberger, who safely landed his crippled Airbus A320 on the Hudson River in 2009, and Bernard Ziegler, the engineering czar at Airbus SE who made Sully's miracle possible.
Ziegler convinced Airbus to adopt his invention of "flyby wire," a revolutionary automatic flight-control system that has helped the world's more than 300,000 airline pilots save thousands of lives.
Airbus introduced fly-by-wire in 1983; it has since been adopted by Boeing and other aircraft makers. Ziegler, a former French Air Force fighter pilot who saw action in the Algerian war, knew that wise pilots acknowledge their fallibility.
But others, stricken by hubris, recommit to a mistake rather than admit error and correct it.
In telling the author his motive for making a plane as goof-proof as possible, Ziegler cites a widely applicable adage: "To err is human, but to persist is diabolical."

This award-winning book tells the story of marketer Dave Nichol's role in building Loblaw Cos. from a sleepy Ontario supermarket chain into one of the world's biggest grocery retailers.
That transformation, starting in the early 1980s, was largely accomplished with the triumph of Nichol's private label "President's Choice" goods over pricier and sometimes inferior national brands, available exclusively at Loblaws and its sister chains No Frills, Fortinos, Provigo and others.
Nichol constantly refreshed the line of PC products with new and often improbable items like Memories of Szechwan Peanut Sauce and gourmet Italian dog food, obliging Loblaw patrons to visit frequently for the next talked-about PC products.
These were showcased in Nichol's chatty and widely read "Insider's Report" flyer. Kingston also opens windows to the often-secretive worlds of new-product development and the strategies by which food purveyors try to change consumer tastes.
Nichol's Loblaws was a precursor to personality driven enterprises led by Elon Musk and his ilk, where the chief product is the ethos created by the marketer.

This acclaimed memoir tells two important stories, of IBM's bet-the-company investment in the mainframe computers that would become ubiquitous at public and private institutions worldwide in the mid-20th century; and the difficult relationship between the author and his larger-than-life namesake father, founder of the modern IBM, whose punch-card tabulating machines had about 90 per cent of the U.S. market.
Yet it was Watson Jr., chief executive from 1956 to 1971, whose IBM would succeed where others failed in embracing a disruptive technology at the expense of a lucrative existing business destined for obsolesce.
Watson Jr. succeeded with a "family" of progressively more powerful yet compatible mainframes, dubbed System/360, by which customers could easily trade up as their needs grew.
The book offers a winsome model of visionary action that is disciplined by careful risk calculation. It also tells of the fights between father and son that the author describes as "savage, primal and unstoppable."
The story is relevant in a contemporary Canadian economy where family feuds over succession have jeopardized some of the country's largest enterprises.

Meet the young men, some almost frightfully immature, who helped launch the Information Age in which we now live.
This comprehensive history might also be the most entertaining saga of how computing devices became everyday appliances.
The revolution started with the desktop PCs that scruffy tech entrepreneurs like Bill Gates and Steve Jobs kept tweaking until they became more powerful than the room-sized mainframe computers that had ruled computing since the 1960s.
The book is an immersive experience in which the reader is a first-hand witness to the fierce debates among inventors over their competing operating systems and hardware designs.
At times the geeks collaborated, at others they sabotaged each other, all the while outfoxing IBM and the other tech giants of their day.
There's a lesson here on the speed and daring of innovation in a wide-open field.
Eventually the boys' fledgling enterprises became today's Microsoft, Apple and Oracle, bureaucratic near-monopolies with limited innovative prowess, just like the earlier giants they supplanted.
Happy holidays, and happy reading!
CAPTION: Fly by Wire: The Geese, the Glide, The Miracle on the HudsonWilliam Langewiesche (2009; Farrar, Straus and Giroux)The Edible Man: Dave Nichol, President's Choice and the Making of Popular TasteAnne Kingston (1994, Macfarlane, Walter & Ross)Father, Son & Co.: My Life at IBM and BeyondThomas J. Watson Jr. and Peter Petre (1990; Bantam Books)Accidental Empires: How the Boys of Silicon Valley Make Their Millions, Battle Foreign Competition, and Still Can't Get a DateRobert X. Cringely (1992; HarperBusiness)
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Canada's immigration minister weighs a crackdown + a Liberal minister steps away to clear his name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-immigration-minister-weighs-crackdown/docview/3131838082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+immigration+minister+weighs+a+crackdown+%2B+a+Liberal+minister+steps+away+to+clear+his+name&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, Nov. 22 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on an alleged mafia boss' ties to Toronto real estate developers, Doug Ford's ban on cyclists' lawsuits and a free cheque and tax cut from the federal government.
DON'T MISSThis alleged mafia boss has ties to Toronto real estate developersThousands of wiretapped calls detailed how Angelo Figliomeni developed connections with GTA developers. He's intervened in disputes on their behalf and stockpiled his own massive property portfolio. Here's who he's connected with.
Who is Angelo Figliomeni? The suspected leader of a powerful Toronto-based faction of Italy's 'Ndrangheta mafia, Figliomeni was arrested in 2019 for his involvement in alleged laundering of more than $70 million. The case against Figliomeni fell apart after the charges against him and his associates were stayed.Doug Ford is banning cyclists from suing his governmentIf you're injured or killed biking on streets that used to have bike lanes, the Ford government can't be sued thanks to a new amendment to its controversial bike lane legislation. It's part of a broader warning the government's transportation minister had for cyclists: stay off of Bloor, Yonge and University. Here's more on the amendment and its implications.

Document 100 of 500

Bonnie Crombie launches first campaign ad, blames Doug Ford for doctors' shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-launches-first-campaign-ad-blames/docview/3131838076/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+launches+first+campaign+ad%2C+blames+Doug+Ford+for+doctors%27+shortage&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is fighting back.
The Liberal leader finally launched her first campaign commercial Friday after enduring millions of dollars in attack ads from Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
Taking aim at Ontario's chronic doctor shortage at a time when some 2.5 million people don't have a family physician, Crombie's advertisement says people deserve "better."
"Every day I hear that you that you can't find a doctor," she intones over footage of a diverse group of Ontario families going about their daily lives.
"Your kids' school is overcrowded and you can't afford the essentials. You feel stuck," says Crombie before a veiled reference to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now being investigated by the RCMP.
"And every day, you hear about another one of Doug Ford's friends getting richer while you fall behind. Enough is enough," she says.
"Your government should be working for you. As your premier, I'll make sure we're getting the basics right: your health care, your kids' education, a home and a life you can actually afford, and a strong economy that brings opportunity back to Ontario so you can get ahead."
The one-minute spot will be initially be online only, but the party has plans to air it on more costly broadcast television in the weeks ahead.
Crombie's salvo comes after an 11-month blitz of Tory ads attacking her, which polls suggest have been effective for the governing party.
It's believed the Tories have spent about $10 million of party donors' money on their ads targeting the rookie Grit leader, who won the leadership last Dec. 2.
Mindful that polls show Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals are not popular, Crombie's commercial emphasizes her personal brand more than her party's name.
"I'm Bonnie Crombie and I'm here for Ontario," it concludes.
The ad comes as Ford is considering a snap spring election, one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: The people of Ontario are struggling while Doug Ford's friends keep getting richer. As Premier, I'll focus on getting the basics right: better healthcare, quality education for your kids, and a strong economy - because your government should work for you.Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Premier Doug Ford.

Subject: Leadership; Negative campaigning; Political advertising

Document 101 of 500

Province, feds sign school food deal: $108M from Ottawa will let Ontario feed 160,000 more kids

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-feds-sign-school-food-deal/docview/3131759510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province%2C+feds+sign+school+food+deal%3A+%24108M+from+Ottawa+will+let+Ontario+feed+160%2C000+more+kids&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is set to get more than $108 million from the federal government to expand existing school food programs and start feeding an extra 160,000 kids per day before the end of the school year, the Star has learned.
The new funding is slated to be unveiled Friday, when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is expected to announce that Ontario is the third province to sign on to its emerging National School Food Program, after Newfoundland and Manitoba inked deals this fall.
Federal Families, Children and Social Development Minister Jenna Sudds told the Star ahead of the announcement that the funding - $108.5 million over the next three years - will go to Ontario's existing Student Nutrition Program and First Nations Student Nutrition Program, which provide meals through more than 13 agencies to schools across the province. That means "close to a million Ontario kids" will have access to school food before summer break, representing almost 75 per cent of the province's schools, she said.
Sudds added the goal is ultimately to have a program available to any student who wants food, when asked about how Toronto's municipal government is looking into delivering a "universal" school food program for Canada's biggest city.
According to the Daily Bread Food Bank, more than 100,000 children across 220 schools in the city "do not have access to good food," while more than one in 10 people in the city rely on food banks to eat.
"Obviously we have ambitions of seeing an inclusive program with no barriers, that any child that needs access can access," Sudds said. "Having said that, it's going to take a bit of time as we work with partners on the ground, and the capacity to be able to reach that.
"This is the incremental work that we are spearheading, along with our partners, to strive to get to that universality."
The funding comes from a national program the Trudeau government first announced in April ahead of this year's federal budget, though it had been building a broader policy since at least 2022 to guide such a program to ensure any food provided is nutritional, among other things.
In the budget, the Liberals earmarked $1 billion over the next five years, promising the money would feed an additional 400,000 students across Canada.
Newfoundland and Labrador was the first province to sign on, when it inked a deal in September to get $9.1 million over three years from the federal government to provide meals to another 4,100 kids. Manitoba then signed on in October, and is set to receive $17.2 million over the next three years to expand the province's existing food programs to another 19,080 kids.
Michael Parsa, Ontario's minister of children, community and social services, said in a statement to the Star that the province has a "long and proud history of supporting both student nutrition programs, and we are pleased to welcome the government of Canada as a new partner in this important work. With this partnership, we can help give more children and youth the healthy start they need to learn, grow, and succeed."
Ontario Education Minister Jill Dunlop noted studies have shown that "students struggle to fully engage in learning when they're hungry. It's simple: when children eat well, they can concentrate on learning and developing important skills, like reading, writing and STEM disciplines." She called the agreement "a historic investment in the future of our children and youth (that) will support not only their academic success, but also their overall well-being."
Last year, the province spent almost $40 million on its student nutrition program, which provides free snacks, breakfasts and lunches to kids in thousands of schools across Ontario.
"I think it's so helpful for kids to have a little meal in them," Ontario Premier Doug Ford told reporters last month as negotiations with Ottawa were ongoing. "It makes them think better during the day."
But at the annual summer meeting of Canada's premiers in July, Ford and other leaders had stressed that they didn't want federal programs like school nutrition to overlap with ones that already exist in their provinces, calling that a duplication and a waste of taxpayer dollars. Ford argued it is best for federal funds to support provincial programs already in place.
And if not, "you know what happens?" Ford said, "the premiers get pissed. It's as simple as that. They just jump in there and think they know better than what we know, (what) we deal with on a daily basis."
Sudds said the deals ensure that new programs are in line with the federal policy on nutrition for school food, and the federal money is provided on condition that the provinces don't cut back on their own funding to feed schoolchildren.
In Ontario's case, Sudds said the new money could be used to provide breakfast, snacks or lunches, depending on what independent agencies decide is best.
"We don't profess to have that on-the-ground knowledge, but these lead agencies who have been doing the work over a number of years - many of whom are volunteers - certainly have that knowledge and the passion and the energy ... to be able to make those informed decisions about how best to support these kids," she said.
Sudds also said the $108.5 million for Ontario was allocated according to the number of students in the province, along with a "top-up" for students in northern and remote areas. After three years, the program will be reassessed with a planned new tranche of funding from the money Ottawa has budgeted.
With a federal election guaranteed within the next year, and a Liberal minority government that could fall sooner to a confidence vote, Sudds alleged the oppositions Conservatives - who are riding high in the polls - would cut the school food program.
Though Poilievre has never explicitly said he would do so, he has repeatedly attacked the program over how it hasn't yet started feeding kids. At times he has claimed "there is no food program," and at others alleged it is only fuelling the size of the government bureaucracy.
"(Trudeau's) multimillion-dollar school food program has not served a single ham sandwich or a single little bowl of Kraft Dinner, not one meal to one child. It exists to feed bureaucracy in Ottawa, not kids in schools," Poilievre said Sept. 25 in the House of Commons.
Sudds said it's clear the program "is at jeopardy" if the Conservatives take power.
"It's a really scary thought to me," Sudds said.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Students; Meals; Food programs; Schools; Nutrition; Food security; Federal funding; Partnerships

Document 102 of 500

Justin Trudeau's shameless giveaway plan is incoherent, unnecessary and frankly embarrassing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-shameless-giveaway-plan-is/docview/3131759248/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+shameless+giveaway+plan+is+incoherent%2C+unnecessary+and+frankly+embarrassing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Quick, someone tell Doug Ford: Now's your chance to secure federal billions for a tunnel under Highway 401.
I kid, I kid. But it is hard not to escape the extremely Fordian air of the emergency policies announced on Thursday afternoon by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. A $250 one-time vote-buying cheque? Liberals were mocking the Ontario Premier's plan for $200 handouts, right up until they decided to steal the idea. Instead of its euphemistic title of Working Canadians Rebate, they might as well have called it Our Last Shred of Dignity.
It's true, at least, that the federal Liberal plan will at least be means-tested, the cheques only going to those that earn less than $150,000, but that still includes an awful lot of voters who are not, by any standard, desperately in need of a $250 handout. If you are making six figures and cannot make ends meet, I think we can go ahead and call that a budgeting problem, not a revenue problem.
There's even less need for such largesse given the complementary plank to JustinBucks, a temporary, two-month pause on GST/HST on a raft of items: children's clothing, snacks, restaurant meals, pre-made foods and, wait for it: beer. BEER. Technically, beer, wine, cider, and pre-mixed cocktails below 7% alcohol by volume, but you truly could not come up with a more Doug Ford-esque policy than cheaper beer, unless Trudeau announced a nationwide ban on road tolls and protected bike lanes. (Note: Not a suggestion.)
Pity the Liberals and their supporters who must now trot out and try to sell these ideas as anything more than the shameless adventures in politicking that they are, the product of a government and a leader that have been mired in lousy polling numbers so deeply and for so long that they are now casting about for any sort of rope to help them climb out.
This is the same party that has for literal years now insisted that there was nothing to be done about the rising prices of consumer goods because inflation was a global problem, due to forces beyond their control and exacerbated by the necessary spending during the pandemic. And they had a point, as any economist would be happy to explain. But now, never mind: here's a bunch of cost-slashing measures because inflation sucks and we hate it and always have.
The best evidence that this policy was thrown together on the fly, and in no small part because the federal NDP just proposed eliminating the GST on a host of items themselves, is that it isn't particularly coherent. There is a distinct whiff of Christmas savings about the whole idea. The Liberal list of GST-exempt items includes party supplies, common gift items like toys, books and video-game consoles(!), and even Christmas trees. But the two-month exemption does not begin until Dec. 14. Anyone throwing a holiday party or doing their Christmas shopping before the middle of next month is out of luck.
Only the pointy end of the Christmas season, in other words, will be covered by the Christmas-oriented tax relief. (As someone who routinely leaves Christmas shopping until the heady days of Dec. 22 or so, I am fine with this.) There's an obvious reason for the delay, as retailers and restaurants (and the government) need some time to adjust to the new rules, but Canadians have now been told that they must wait for more than three weeks to do their holiday and party shopping if they want to take advantage of the Liberals' kindness. Hold all beer purchases until Dec. 14. (I will not do this.)
The Liberals, of course, also need the support of another party in the minority Parliament to get the changes approved, and the NDP has already signalled it will oblige. Ah how things have changed since the heady days of ... September. Remember those many months ago leader Jagmeet Singh furrowed his brow angrily (on video!) and insisted he was done with propping up Trudeau? It turns out he was not that done after all. All it took this time was a billion-dollar handout to voters.
Rank populism: not just for Doug Ford, anymore.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives to make an announcement regarding a two month suspension of GST on selected goods, at Vince's Market, a grocery store in Sharon, Ontario, on Thursday November 21, 2024.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson

Subject: Shopping; Beer; Voters; Prime ministers; Christmas

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Justin Trudeau's shameless giveaway plan is incoherent, unnecessary and frankly embarrassing

160,000 Ontario kids will get a chance to eat for free under new school food program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/160-000-ontario-kids-will-get-chance-eat-free/docview/3131758940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=160%2C000+Ontario+kids+will+get+a+chance+to+eat+for+free+under+new+school+food+program&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
