Document 104 of 500

No, Pierre Poilievre, Justin Trudeau isn't forcing us to eat bugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-pierre-poilievre-justin-trudeau-isnt-forcing/docview/3131758923/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No%2C+Pierre+Poilievre%2C+Justin+Trudeau+isn%27t+forcing+us+to+eat+bugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Even after trying the delicious cricket Bolognese, I was skeptical of the cricket hot dog. Rightly so: It sure tasted like whatever a hot dog is supposed to taste like, but it had the consistency of ground bugs.
I was sampling the insect fare while visiting the test kitchen of One Hop Kitchen, a Toronto-based start-up trying to figure out how to make crickets an affordable, sustainable, less-weird food staple. "We don't want it to be that people are eating bugs because they have to," Eli Cadesky, company co-founder told me. "That's dystopian."
That dystopia is here, if you listen to Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre.
"Whereas Justin Trudeau is attempting to impose a global woke agenda on Canadians, whereas Justin Trudeau used $9 million of Canadian taxpayer dollars to bet big on an edible bug factory," begins a petition launched by the party this month. It asks supporters to sign their name to declare "no international body will have the right to impose its policies on our country."
The Conservatives invited their militants to stand up and declare: "We WON'T Eat Bugs."
Let's state the obvious: Watching the leader of the official opposition - the likely future prime minister, and a former Minister of the Crown - debase himself with bug-brained conspiracy theories designed to continue ratcheting up the paranoia of a small subset of the population is embarrassing. Sad, even.
But this is, unfortunately, a core part of the Conservative party strategy these days. Poilievre and his team now see this wing of their party, one taken by conspiracy theories, as useful agents of chaos to be exploited. They see a right-wing online media ecosystem, slobberingly happy to engage in any cooked-up fantasy if it makes them money, as a partner in this mission. And they welcome the negative coverage from the mainstream press - myself included - as a useful foil in their quest to delegitimize journalism.
At the core of all this is a trait that has rarely been central to Canadian conservatism: Nihilism. Poilievre's movement has decided that exploiting grievance and paranoia, no matter how irrational and destructive, is worth it if it means winning.
But let's go back to where this insect obsession comes from.
Humans have been eating bugs since we've been human. Today, mealworms are a staple of some East Asian cuisines, whilst lobsters are really just sea bugs. Insects are cheap and efficient protein - in recent years, there has been optimism that insects could reduce our CO2 emissions and fight hunger.
This was, generally, uncontroversial. Even if most people aren't keen to nosh on ant antipasti, processed insects showed promise as pet food, livestock feed, and for those with a rugged kind of try-anything attitude. Even Tucker Carlson ate a cicada snack on-air in 2021.
The trouble came when the World Economic Forum, the elite club for world leaders and internationalists, began touting the possible benefits of a bug-based diet. As anti-vaccine activism grew in 2021, and the Forum became a particular target for uber-online conspiracy theorists, a refusal to eat bugs became a rallying cry.
"They," proclaimed prolific conspiracy theorist Alex Jones in 2021, wanted to "feed the insects chemical and bioweapons-type stuff" in order to achieve the ultimate goal: To "control your mind."
This claim bounced around various anti-vaccine groups, and became a focal point of Poilievre's beloved Freedom Convoy in 2022.
When the party launched a leadership race to replace Erin O'Toole, candidate Leslyn Lewis first took up the mantle of the bug-eating refuseniks, telling fellow conspiracy theorist Jordan B. Peterson in an interview: "They've been programming us to want to eat bugs."
Lewis, of course, lost that race: She earned less than 10 per cent of the vote on the first ballot. But she is now infrastructure critic in Poilievre's caucus, and the leader often echoes her out-to-lunch pronouncements.
"Dr. Lewis was right," Poilievre proclaimed on Twitter. "Weird, woke Liberals pushed us to eat bugs."
Singing Alex Jones karaoke might seem like a bizarre move from the Conservative leader. But, as senior Conservatives have admitted to me multiple times over the years, Poilievre sees his path to victory in the next general election by siphoning support from People's Party Leader Maxime Bernier, who has been all too happy to regurgitate the bug-eating trope. And there is, apparently, nothing Poilievre won't do to win the fealty of that grumpy and paranoid three per cent of the electorate.
Poilievre making a mountain out of an anthill is just the most recent example of this plan to win power. He has also vowed to ban "digital ID," playing into baseless fears of Ottawa trying to impose a Chinese-style social credit system; echoed the "great reset" theory, a lazy repackaging of Illuminati hokum; tipped his hat to anti-vaxxers by trying to forbid vaccine mandates; and has said he'll preclude his ministers from going to Davos for the World Economic Forum, if elected. He has spent years harnessing the intense distrust of a small minority, issuing warnings of "global elites" trying to orchestrate a "power grab" here in Canada.
It would be one thing if this were merely an unhinged fantasy, but real people do bear the brunt of these attacks.
The $9 million for an "edible bug factory" he howled at is quite real; the factory is run by the Aspire Food Group. With federal grants and loans, they moved from Texas to London, Ontario to build an advanced manufacturing facility to turn bug protein into protein shakes, candles, animal feed, and a host of other products. This is actually an entrepreneurial success story, and now they've become the target of a Conservative-led smear campaign.
The Conservative party still has serious concerns and reasonable policy objectives. Its caucus still holds thoughtful and serious people. And I don't doubt that the architects of this insect-obsessed communications strategy see it as little more than an act, a useful technique to extract money and energy from people who harbour genuine fear of an international conspiracy to ban meat and mandate bugs. But that nihilism, that obsessive belief that these manipulations can be justified in pursuit of power, that offensively dim view they take of voters - it's depressing.
Nobody can fault Poilievre for tilting hard at a deeply unpopular government - and there's certainly a lot to go after - but we are in the midst of a trust crisis. People no longer trust government, the media, our institutions, and at the extreme end, people have stopped trusting their fellow citizens. Populists, at their best, recognize that lack of trust and convey a plan to fix the institutions and restore what's been lost. Populists like Poilievre, however, seem intent on intensifying that distrust until the anger becomes an end in itself, feeding a perpetual culture war that prioritizes fighting over governing.
Poilievre should be careful about the damage he's doing to the system that he hopes to run. The Conservatives will, sooner or later, govern again. Will they bother trying to convince the masses, those afraid of our supposed dystopia, to trust again? Or will they continue feeding this anxiety, blaming all their problems on some unseen dark forces, like President-elect Donald Trump does?
I, for one, hope Poilievre stops feeding these paranoid delusions and tries eating some bugs for a change.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks with reporters in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Nov. 21, 2024 in Ottawa.

Subject: Paranoia; Conservatism; Vaccines; Dystopias; Economic summit conferences; Political leadership; Political parties; Insects; Trust; Conspiracy; Negative campaigning; Populism; Nihilism; Elections; Proteins

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic summit conferences

Document 105 of 500

Justin Trudeau handing out cheques and implementing a tax holiday isn't enough. We need to abolish the GST

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-handing-out-cheques-implementing/docview/3131758717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+handing+out+cheques+and+implementing+a+tax+holiday+isn%27t+enough.+We+need+to+abolish+the+GST&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seems as though the US election has shaken things up among Canadian policymakers. After Democrats dropped the conversation on grocery price gouging by the end of the summer, Donald Trump continued to call out high food prices and ultimately won the election. Now a race to push forward affordability policy measures has started ahead of the forthcoming Canadian federal election.
First out of the gate was Jagmeet Singh, who last week committed to remove the GST from everyday essentials such as groceries, internet, phone bills, diapers, and children's clothing.
Now, the Trudeau government is bringing in a winter "GST holiday" on a selection of goods including pre-made food, children's clothing, and beer. It is a temporary measure that runs from Dec. 14 until Feb. 15, and purports to give Canadians a break during the holiday season. The government is also sending out cheques of $250 to any working Canadians who made less than $150,000 in 2023.
It sounds like a good start. But a temporary reprieve isn't a long-term solution to the affordability crisis - and could worsen things if corporate power is left unchecked.
Removing the regressive GST implemented by Brian Mulroney in 1989 would be a major relief measure for the working-class. Making this measure permanent would help to insulate Canadians from further economic shocks as the world's economic volatility increases. With U.S. Tariffs, global conflict, and climate disasters looming, a permanent GST cut is but one measure to help stop inflation before it starts.
To make sure that corporations cannot swoop in and take advantage of the price vacuum left behind by the GST, the NDP also proposed excess windfall profit taxes. Windfall profit taxes have become an inflation fighting policy tool around the world, as profit-induced inflation pushes up prices on everyday essentials. This is not some left field policy tool used to attack corporations. Even Conservative UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak introduced a windfall profit tax in 2022 on oil and gas companies.
At his policy announcement, Jagmeet Singh also mentioned the consideration of price controls, a policy tool recently popularized by the Broadbent Institute's 2024 Ellen Meiksins Wood Lecturer, Isabella Weber.
The data backs up the case that corporate profiteering is a major culprit behind the price jumps at the grocery store. Her proposals were put to the test in Germany, shortly after the start of the War in Ukraine, as the German government implemented a gas price brake to assuage fears of household heating becoming unaffordable. The price brake worked, and Germans did not have to bear the full economic brunt of volatile gas prices.
We can also do more regarding corporate concentration among grocery retailers, who only got a slap on the wrist for the bread price-fixing scandal just a couple of years ago. Weber has also suggested building strategic food reserves, similar to the US strategic petroleum reserves built after the 1973 energy crisis, to help prevent food shortages and price gouging. Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson is looking to build city-run grocery stores in underserved communities where food insecurity is high and opening shops may not be profitable. When Canadian governments already subsidize food banks at the "tipping point," we need to seriously consider the alternatives to ensure people can afford the necessities of life.
There is more that can be done. Canada once thought monitoring and regulating the price of essentials like groceries was a good idea too. The Food Price Review Board was an initiative born out of the David Lewis-led NDP, working with the Pierre Trudeau minority Parliament, amid the global oil shock of the 1970s. Instead of leaving it to the markets to decide, the Board proposed using the mechanisms of government to ensure food security amid global price volatility. The Trudeau government of the time ultimately abandoned the Food Price Review Board by 1975. It is an idea worth revisiting.
There is no time to spare when Canadians are struggling. Any measures that fall short of delivering for the working-class will lead us towards the global trend of far-right electoral successes, where their affordability measure is to attack the outsiders without, and not the insiders with plenty.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives to make an announcement regarding a two month suspension of GST on selected goods, at Vince's Market, a grocery store in Sharon, Ontario, on Thursday November 21, 2024.
CREDIT: Clement Nocos, Contributor

Subject: Review boards; Gasoline prices; Canadians; Working class; Price gouging; Food prices; Prime ministers; Wage & price controls; Windfall profits; Grocery stores

Document 106 of 500

Eligible businesses to get slice of WSIB's surplus: Premier says some refunds could be as much as $70,000

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/eligible-businesses-get-slice-wsibs-surplus/docview/3131758655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Eligible+businesses+to+get+slice+of+WSIB%27s+surplus%3A+Premier+says+some+refunds+could+be+as+much+as+%2470%2C000&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Businesses will get a financial boost - as well as a break on fees - after the Workplace Safety and Insurance Board posted a $2.5-billion surplus for its 2024 fiscal year.
"This includes delivering $2 billion in surplus funds back to Ontario businesses through the WSIB, which is a direct result of the agency's new approach to strong financial management," Premier Doug Ford announced Thursday during his speech at the Ontario Chamber of Commerce's annual economic summit.
"For a small business, like a bakery with fewer than five employees, this could mean a refund of up to $2,300," Ford said. "For a small construction company with 50 employees, the refund could climb to $46,000. And for a growing company with 100 employees, the refund could reach $70,000. That's money these businesses can reinvest back into their workers and into our economy, creating more jobs and opportunity."
The premier also announced that the average premium rate for businesses will drop to $1.25 from $1.30 per $100 of insurable payroll, saving them a total of $150 million a year, "lowering premium rates to the lowest level in half a century, all without reducing workers' benefits."
Since taking office in 2018, the government has reduced these rates by more than 50 per cent in total - saving businesses a total of $18.6 billion.
While businesses welcomed the move, New Democrat MPP Lise Vaugeois (Thunder Bay-Superior North), her party's WSIB critic, said the announcement does nothing for workers and called it "disgusting."
"Injured workers have been pushed into poverty over many, many years by this government" and previous ones, she said. "Are they putting any money into workers' pockets? Zero."
The latest news, she added, also does nothing to address "deeming," which can drastically reduce a worker's WSIB payments.
Deeming is when the WSIB determines an injured worker is able to do a job and then cuts their compensation based on those potential wages - even if they have not found employment.
"They just take it off - it's entirely arbitrary," Vaugeois said. "They deemed that that is a job you could have, so you are just being lazy by not getting it. It doesn't matter if your back is broken."
Among the other measures Ford announced included $400 million for workers' mental health, as well as injury prevention, care and recovery, and a $1,000 bonus for employers who have WSIB-approved health and safety action plans in place. The mental-health care is provided in partnership with hospitals including those in the Greater Toronto Area, Hamilton, Barrie and Ottawa.
The one-time rebate is available to employers who have had a clean record since 2020, and the government estimates more than 280,000 could be eligible. The funds are to be sent out next February.
In 2022, the province provided similar rebates totalling $1.5 billion.
"I'm proud that our strong financial position is allowing us to help businesses and contribute to the economic prosperity of Ontario while also investing $2.5 billion every year to help people recover and return to work by covering health-care costs and income replacement," said Jeff Lang, CEO of the WSIB, in a written statement.
The WSIB provides support and services to injured workers, as well as replacement income as they recover.
"The Ontario government is doing the right thing, putting more surplus funds back in the hands of businesses where they belong," said Julie Kwiecinski, the Canadian Federation of Independent Business's director of provincial affairs in Ontario.
The Ford government is also doing away with the $150 fee for apprentices taking their first qualification exam.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 107 of 500

Province to ban lawsuits after bike lanes removed: Government would be shielded from injury claims

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-ban-lawsuits-after-bike-lanes-removed/docview/3131758343/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+ban+lawsuits+after+bike+lanes+removed%3A+Government+would+be+shielded+from+injury+claims&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria has a message for cyclists: for your own safety, stay off Bloor, Yonge and University when bike lanes are removed.
While Sarkaria's proposed legislation, Bill 212, doesn't make it illegal to ride on those roads, a surprise amendment Thursday protects the government from lawsuits if cyclists are injured or killed on streets that used to have bike lanes.
Critics called that indemnification provision a troubling development that suggests Premier Doug Ford's government knows its controversial changes will make roads more hazardous for riders.
"The safer thing for a cyclist to do would be to make a decision to go on streets that are safer," Sarkaria said after the Progressive Conservatives used their majority to pass the amendment in a legislative committee hearing.
"That's what we believe is the right way to do it, is on our secondary streets where there is much less traffic volume and (where it is) ultimately safer for cyclists and everyone else."
New Democrat and Liberal MPPs on the committee condemned the change to the bill, which Sarkaria confirmed would give Ford's government power to remove all bike lanes on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue should it so decide.
"These new amendments turn a bad anti-bike bill into a terrible anti-bike bill," said Jessica Bell (University-Rosedale), who often cycles to and from the legislature.
The amendment states that "claims arising from collisions between motor vehicles, cyclists or pedestrians ... are not claims of negligence."
Blocking lawsuits is "above-the-law stuff," said New Democrat MPP Jennifer French (Oshawa). "No one can bring anything against the Crown."
Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon (Beaches-East York) said cyclists have the right to be safe on city streets.
The government maintains bike lanes on primary routes are contributing to gridlock and last month introduced the legislation that allows the province to override municipal decisions on where these lanes should go.
Municipalities will be required to get provincial approval for bike lanes if they take out a lane of traffic for cars and trucks, which critics fear could effectively be a ban on new lanes.
Opponents of the legislation repeatedly warned fewer bike lanes will lead to more deaths and injuries involving cyclists. Six riders have been killed in Toronto this year.
"It feels a lot safer as a car driver to know cyclists can zip past me in their lane and not come in front of my car," said French.
Ford said this week it's "a bunch of hogwash" that the City of Toronto estimates it will cost $48 million to remove 22 kilometres of bike lanes that cost $27 million to install.
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," said the premier.
Brian Rosborough, executive director of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, said gridlock in Toronto is "complex" and can't be solved by simply getting rid of bike lanes. The legislation, for example, does not take aim at curb-lane dining under the CaféTO program in warmer months, or at lanes closed for construction of condominiums and other large buildings.
With an early election looming in the spring, Ford's Tories believe bike lanes will be a useful wedge issue against the NDP and Liberals that will help the PCs hold some of their dozen Toronto seats.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 108 of 500

Trump nominates envoy to Ottawa: Veteran politician played key role in president-elect's win in Michigan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-nominates-envoy-ottawa/docview/3131758119/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+nominates+envoy+to+Ottawa%3A+Veteran+politician+played+key+role+in+president-elect%27s+win+in+Michigan&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President-elect Donald Trump has tapped a veteran politician from Michigan - Ontario's largest single trading partner - to be the next U.S. ambassador to Canada.
Pending congressional approval, Dutch-born Pete Hoekstra, 71, will succeed David Cohen, President Joe Biden's envoy to Ottawa, after Trump is sworn in on Jan. 20.
"Pete is well-respected in the Great State of Michigan - A state we won sizably," Trump wrote on social media.
The president-elect used the announcement to tout his own track record in taming Canada and Mexico, saying he "overhauled the disastrous NAFTA agreement, the worst Trade Deal in the History of the United States, which was switched to the USMCA (Mexico/Canada), which no one thought could be done."
Signalling more trade tumult may loom, Trump said in his next term in the Oval Office, Hoekstra will "help me once again put AMERICA FIRST. He did an outstanding job as United States Ambassador to the Netherlands during our first four years, and I am confident that he will continue to represent our Country well in this new role. Thank you, Pete!"
Hoekstra is no stranger to longtime Canadian embassy personnel in Washington. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1993 to 2011 and was chair of the House intelligence committee. As chair of the Michigan Republican Party, he was instrumental in Trump winning that key state over Democratic Vice-President Kamala Harris on Nov. 5. The Detroit Free Press credited Hoekstra with professionalizing a state party that was riven by factional infighting.
David Wilkins, a former Republican governor of South Carolina who served as U.S. ambassador to Canada under President George W. Bush, said Trump has chosen someone with elected office experience who has a "close relationship with the president and his team."
"Folks in Canada ought to be very pleased with this appointment because you've got an experienced ambassador, you've a former public official and you've got someone who obviously has a close relationship with the president, and those are all very positive factors."
Hoekstra's role will be to serve as "the president's voice in Canada, as the president's spokesperson in Canada, basically," Wilkins said. "And so you advocate for the present administration, number one, and two, you also listen and you report to the president or the president's team what's important to Canada."
In a congratulatory social media message to Hoekstra, Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to Washington, noted "Canada and the United States share the strongest bilateral relationship in the world, and I look forward to working with you to make it even stronger."
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly - who was in Washington on Thursday to meet Republican and Democratic senators along with trade stakeholders - cited Trump's comments in noting Hoekstra's appointment, and emphasized Canada's "goal is to make sure that we continue to abide by this important trade deal."
Joly said the U.S. senators she met are "clear" that Canada is determined to protect its market access on "national security grounds" and against "any form of back door that China could have."
However, Wilkins said fears that the Trump administration will put the Canada-U.S. trading relationship at risk are overblown.
"I do not think our trade relationship is under threat. I think it's strong. It will continue to be strong. We're Canada's biggest customer and will remain the biggest customer. There are always issues in any trading relationship that need to be worked on. There are always tweaks," Wilkins said, "but the foundation of our relationship is very strong and will continue to be strong in my opinion."
At Queen's Park, there was palpable relief Trump had selected an ambassador from a border state that did more than $80 billion in two-way trade with Ontario in 2022 - eight per cent of the total trade between the Canada and the U.S.
Premier Doug Ford has worked closely with Michigan's Democratic Gov. Gretchen Whitmer to strengthen those ties, since the auto industry is hugely important to the economies of both the province and the state.
"There is no partnership more important to the economic success of Ontario than our close ties with the United States," Ford said Thursday at the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
"As we head into the review of the USMCA, our government is doing everything we can to strengthen and grow our historic partnership. We're continuing to build relationships with our U.S. counterparts at all levels of government and all political stripes, driving home the message that we need to reject protectionism and promote a 'Buy Can-Am' approach," said the premier.
His comments came one day after he announced the Council of the Federation of provincial and territorial leaders is urging Ottawa to do a separate trade deal with the U.S., excluding Mexico for the transshipment of Chinese-made electric vehicles to evade American and Canadian tariffs.
Bruce Heyman, who was Democratic president Barack Obama's ambassador to Canada, said Hoekstra is an "experienced ambassador ... (who) obviously knows Trump well and has his trust (and is) experienced with Congress."
Hoekstra, he said, is "clearly an insider and this is who Canada will need to get (the) straight scoop and real lay of the land and work with D.C." and "comes from a border state that will have direct knowledge and understanding of Canada (and Trump's) early naming shows the importance of the relationship."
That means it "should be good news for the Canada-U.S. relationship as they will have someone to work with to navigate the changes that are coming."
Hoekstra is co-author of the 2015 book "Architects of Disaster: The Destruction of Libya," and has been a hardliner against terrorism.
An examination of his social media history shows he is also a vocal critic of Chinese trade practices - and of Beijing stealing American technology - and a strong proponent of Israel.
While his tweets demonstrate few concerns with Canada, he has publicly questioned Biden's electric vehicle subsidy strategy, which Ottawa and Queen's Park have matched.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Tonda MacCharles Queen's Park Bureau

Full text: New ambassador doesn't share Canadian values
Trump nominates former congressman Pete Hoekstra as ambassador to Canada,
Nov. 20
U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's choice for ambassador to Canada, Pete Hoekstra, will be in a country opposed to many of his values, as demonstrated by his past voting record. This is a guy who opposes women having reproductive freedom, voted against increasing children's health care, opposed gay marriage and adoption rights and was against paid parental leave for government staff. He received a top "A" rating from the NRA because of his opposition to tightening gun ownership legislation. He bought into the myth of Iraq having weapons of mass destruction and propagated the lie that led to so much death and destruction with a false claim that hundreds of chemical weapons had been found in Iraq, even though it was known and reported that they were actually outdated armaments left over from prior hostilities. I hope he is smarter now, but it sounds like he'll clash with what I most fervently hope are solid Canadian traditions and policies.
Barry Kent, Markham
Of course, Donald Trump gives a glowing recommendation to his proposed ambassador to Canada. But Canadians should be aware of the controversies that dogged Pete Hoekstra's time as ambassador to the Netherlands. Virulent anti-Muslim lies - in 2015 he said Muslim youths had created so-called "no-go" zones in the Netherlands and were burning politicians - a dishonest attempt to deny he ever made those lies, a doubly dishonest attempt to deny he denied making them. Not to mention his close relationship with the far right Dutch party, Forum for Democracy. The Dutch media did an excellent job of holding him accountable at the time. Will ours? We shall see.
John Gavin, Toronto
Will Canada's leadership stand up to Trump?
Donald Trump cares little about maintaining good relationships with any country or international organization that opposes his agenda. To be sure, he and his choice of ambassador to Canada, Pete Hoekstra, will demand that Canada bow to his trade demands. Our political leadership should remember that their role is to put Canada first, and in response to such demands, advise Trump, for example, that unless he withdraws his trade demands, we will close the Welland Canal to U.S. traffic, cease the export of Quebec Hydro, cease the shipment of oil and gas, cease the shipping of vital Canadian mined minerals, and enforce our 200-mile limit on the east and west coasts. Canadian water is another commodity that gives Canadian leadership "push back." The coming months will tell all Canadians if our political leadership can find enough courage to stand up to an elected bully.
Gord Wilson, Port Rowan, Ont.
No sign Harris lost because
of her gender
America refuses to elect a woman. Canada has no reason to gloat, Opinion, Nov. 20
Gillian Steward's account of Kamala Harris's election loss reproduces the sort of blame-avoidant explanation that the Democrats have specialized in for years. There's no evidence that Harris lost because of her gender. Following the debate, she was, in fact, ahead in the polls but squandered this lead by placing her bets mostly on the abortion question, and by trying to court anti-Trump Republicans with a business-friendly platform. As exit polls clearly showed, the economy - not gender (or immigration, for that matter) - was the overriding concern of working-class voters. Undoubtedly, voters would have been better off under Harris than under the rancid kleptocrat who was elected. But Harris did little to convince them of it.
Scott Staring, Toronto
Kudos to CPKC for hydrogen stations
CPKC's hydrogen fuelling stations for locomotives now up and running, railway says, Nov. 20
Congratulations to Canadian Pacific Kansas City railway for using hydrogen power on locomotives running between Edmonton and Calgary, and kudos to them for doing it in Canada's hotbed of the fossil fuel industry. This article should have appeared on the front page of the paper. At the same time, why wouldn't Metrolinx engage in the same type of pilot on GO lines here in Ontario? This opportunity is best exploited right now on limited-distance routes like our commuter system. How do we convince Via Rail to press the feds for the same opportunity on the Montreal-Toronto-Ottawa corridor? Sounds like a great way to attack fossil fuel emissions and start approaching our targets for their reduction.
Tom Doris, Toronto
What is the chance of my car being stolen?
Here are Canada's most stolen vehicles - and there's a reason why EVs aren't making the list, Nov. 19
Interestingly, this article tells us that SUVs dominate the top 10 most stolen vehicles. However, the statistics are based on the number of cars stolen by model. What people most want to know is the chance that their car has of being stolen. The data in this article doesn't answer that crucial question. One would need to know not only how many vehicles of each model were stolen, but also how many of each vehicle are on the road.
For example, the Honda Civic is the only conventional car that was on the top 10 list. Since it is among the most popular models sold in Canada during the past decade, this is hardly surprising. There is a great number of Civics on the roads. Civic owners may not need to worry too much, as it is likely that the chance of an individual Civic being stolen is quite low.
Eric Mintz, Toronto
Train civilians
to direct traffic
at intersections
I was walking near Front and Jarvis streets intersection on Wednesday. It's a very busy intersection and became a logjam as a result of a large tractor-trailer and several cars entering the intersection on a red light. They blocked the entire intersection for two and a half full signal cycles until traffic flowed again, leaving a lot of angry motorists, pedestrians and cyclists. We entrust our children to school crossing guards who are trained and cost a fraction of the amount paid to police officers who would otherwise perform the same function. Surely placing trained civilian intersection guards to control traffic at the worst of these busy intersections would also make sense.
Michael Bines, Toronto

Document 110 of 500

Ontario businesses will get $2 billion back after Workplace Safety and Insurance Board posts massive surplus

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-businesses-will-get-2-billion-back-after/docview/3131918147/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+businesses+will+get+%242+billion+back+after+Workplace+Safety+and+Insurance+Board+posts+massive+surplus&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Businesses will get a financial boost - as well as a break on fees - after the Workplace Safety and Insurance Board posted a $2.5-billion surplus for its 2024 fiscal year.
"This includes delivering $2 billion in surplus funds back to Ontario businesses through the WSIB, which is a direct result of the agency's new approach to strong financial management," Ontario Premier Doug Ford announced Thursday during his speech at the Ontario Chamber of Commerce's annual economic summit.
"For a small business, like a bakery with fewer than five employees, this could mean a refund of up to $2,300," Ford said. "For a small construction company with 50 employees, the refund could climb to $46,000. And for a growing company with 100 employees, the refund could reach $70,000. That's money these businesses can reinvest back into their workers and into our economy, creating more jobs and opportunity."
The premier also announced that the average premium rate for businesses will drop to $1.25 from $1.30 per $100 of insurable payroll, saving them a total of $150 million a year, "lowering premium rates to the lowest level in half a century, all without reducing workers' benefits."
Since taking office in 2018, the government has reduced these rates by more than 50 per cent in total - saving businesses a total of $18.6 billion.
While businesses welcomed the move, New Democrat MPP Lise Vaugeois (Thunder Bay-Superior North), her party's WSIB critic, said the announcement does nothing for workers and called it "disgusting."
"Injured workers have been pushed into poverty over many, many years by this government" and previous ones, she said. "Are they putting any money into workers' pockets? Zero."
The latest news, she added, also does nothing to address "deeming," which can drastically reduce a worker's WSIB payments.
Deeming is when the WSIB determines an injured worker is able to do a job and then cuts their compensation based on those potential wages - even if they have not found employment.
"They just take it off - it's entirely arbitrary," Vaugeois said. "They deemed that that is a job you could have, so you are just being lazy by not getting it. It doesn't matter if your back is broken."
Among the other measures Ford announced included $400 million for workers' mental health, as well as injury prevention, care and recovery, and a $1,000 bonus for employers who have WSIB-approved health and safety action plans in place.
The mental-health care is provided in partnership with hospitals including those in Greater Toronto, Hamilton, Barrie and Ottawa.
The one-time rebate is available to employers who have had a clean record since 2020, and the government estimates more than 280,000 could be eligible. The funds are to be sent out next February.
In 2022, the province provided similar rebates totalling $1.5 billion.
"I'm proud that our strong financial position is allowing us to help businesses and contribute to the economic prosperity of Ontario while also investing $2.5 billion every year to help people recover and return to work by covering health-care costs and income replacement," Jeff Lang, CEO of the WSIB, said in a written statement.
The WSIB provides support and services to injured workers, as well as replacement income as they recover.
The Canadian Federation of Independent Business said "the Ontario government is doing the right thing, putting more surplus funds back in the hands of businesses where they belong," said Julie Kwiecinski, the CFIB's director of provincial affairs in Ontario.
The Ford government is also doing away with the $150 fee for apprentices taking their first qualification exam.
CAPTION: The WSIB provides support and services to injured workers, as well as replacement income as they recover.

Document 111 of 500

Ford government to ban lawsuits against province if cyclists are hurt or killed on streets where bike lanes were removed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-ban-lawsuits-against-province-if/docview/3131918057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+ban+lawsuits+against+province+if+cyclists+are+hurt+or+killed+on+streets+where+bike+lanes+were+removed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria has a message for cyclists: for your own safety, stay off Bloor, Yonge and University when bike lanes are removed.
While Sarkaria's proposed legislation, Bill 212, doesn't make it illegal to ride on those roads, a surprise amendment Thursday protects the government from lawsuits if cyclists are injured or killed on streets that used to have bike lanes.
Critics called that indemnification provision a troubling development that suggests Premier Doug Ford's government know its controversial changes will make roads more hazardous for riders.
"The safer thing for a cyclist to do would be to make a decision to go on streets that are safer," Sarkaria said after the Progressive Conservatives used their majority to pass the amendment in a legislative committee hearing.
"That's what we believe is the right way to do it, is on our secondary streets where there is much less traffic volume and ultimately safer for cyclists and everyone else."
New Democrat and Liberal MPPs on the committee condemned the change to the bill, which Sarkaria confirmed would give Ford's government power to remove all bike lanes on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue should it so decide.
"These new amendments turn a bad anti-bike bill into a terrible anti-bike bill," said Jessica Bell (University-Rosedale), who often cycles to and from the legislature.
The amendment states "claims arising from collisions between motor vehicles, cyclists or pedestrians ... are not claims of negligence."
Blocking lawsuits is "above-the-law stuff," said New Democrat MPP Jennifer French (Oshawa). "No one can bring anything against the Crown."
Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon (Beaches-East York) said cyclists have the right to be safe on city streets.
The government maintains bike lanes on primary routes are contributing to gridlock and last month introduced the legislation that allows the province to override municipal decisions on where bike lanes should go.
Municipalities will be required to get provincial approval for bike lanes if they take out a lane of traffic for cars and trucks, which critics fear could effectively be a ban on new lanes.
Opponents of the legislation repeatedly warned fewer bike lanes will lead to more deaths and injuries involving cyclists. Six riders have been killed in Toronto this year.
"It feels a lot safer as a car driver to know cyclists can zip past me in their lane and not come in front of my car," said French.
Ford said this week it's "a bunch of hogwash" that the City of Toronto's estimates it will cost $48 million to remove 22 km of bike lanes that cost $27 million to install.
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," said the premier.
Brian Rosborough, executive director of the Association of Municipalities, said gridlock in Toronto is "complex" and can't be solved by simply getting rid of bike lanes.
The legislation, for example, does not take aim at curb lane dining under the CaféTO program in warmer months or at lanes closed for construction of condominiums and other large buildings.
With an early election looming in the spring, Ford's Tories believe bike lanes will be a useful wedge issue against the NDP and Liberals that will help the PCs hold some of their dozen Toronto seats.
CAPTION: Hundreds of cyclists bike eastward along Bloor Street to show support for bike lanes on Nov. 9, 2024.

Subject: Legislation; Litigation

Document 112 of 500

Who is Pete Hoekstra, Donald Trump's pick for U.S. ambassador to Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-is-pete-hoekstra-donald-trumps-pick-u-s/docview/3131474399/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+is+Pete+Hoekstra%2C+Donald+Trump%27s+pick+for+U.S.+ambassador+to+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President-elect Donald Trump has tapped a veteran politician from Michigan - Ontario's largest single trading partner - to be the next U.S. ambassador to Canada.
Pending congressional approval, Pete Hoekstra will succeed David Cohen, President Joe Biden's emissary to Ottawa, after Trump is sworn in on Jan. 20.
"Pete is well-respected in the Great State of Michigan - A state we won sizably," the U.S. president-elect wrote on social media.
"I overhauled the disastrous NAFTA Agreement, the worst Trade Deal in the History of the United States, which was switched to the USMCA (Mexico/Canada), which no one thought could be done," Trump's statement continued.
"We brought Trade with Mexico and Canada to a level playing field for our wonderful Farmers and Working Families," he said, signalling more trade tumult may loom.
"In my Second Term, Pete will help me once again put AMERICA FIRST. He did an outstanding job as United States Ambassador to the Netherlands during our first four years, and I am confident that he will continue to represent our Country well in this new role. Thank you, Pete!"
At Queen's Park, there was relief Trump had selected an ambassador from a border state that did more than $80 billion in two-way trade with Ontario in 2022 - eight per cent of the total trade between the Canada and the U.S.
Premier Doug Ford has worked closely with Michigan's Democratic Gov. Gretchen Whitmer to strengthen those ties, since the auto industry is hugely important to the economies of both the province and the state.
"There is no partnership more important to the economic success of Ontario than our close ties with the United States," Ford said Thursday at the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
"As we head into the review of the USMCA, our government is doing everything we can to strengthen and grow our historic partnership. We're continuing to build relationships with our U.S. counterparts at all levels of government and all political stripes, driving home the message that we need to reject protectionism and promote a 'Buy Can-Am' approach," said the premier.
His comments came one day after he announced the Council of the Federation of provincial and territorial leaders is urging Ottawa to do a separate trade deal with the U.S., excluding Mexico for the transshipment of Chinese-made electric vehicles to evade American and Canadian tariffs.
In a congratulatory social media message to Hoekstra, Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to Washington, noted "Canada and the United States share the strongest bilateral relationship in the world, and I look forward to working with you to make it even stronger."
Bruce Heyman, who was Democratic president Barack Obama's ambassador to Canada, said Hoekstra brings some strengths to the job.
Heyman said he's an "experienced ambassador ... (who) obviously knows Trump well and has his trust (and is) experienced with Congress."
Obama's ambassador added Hoekstra is "clearly an insider and this is who Canada will need to get (the) straight scoop and real lay of the land and work with D.C." and "comes from a border state that will have direct knowledge and understanding of Canada (and Trump's) early naming shows importance of the relationship."
That means it "should be good news for the Canada-U.S. relationship as they will have someone to work with to navigate the changes that are coming."
The Dutch-born Hoekstra, 71, served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1993 to 2011 and was chair of the House intelligence committee.
As chair of the Michigan Republican Party, he was instrumental in Trump winning that key state over Democratic Vice-President Kamala Harris on Nov. 5.
The Detroit Free Press said Hoekstra professionalized a state party that was riven by factional infighting.
CAPTION: Michigan Republican Party chair Pete Hoekstra watches the results of the U.S. presidential election in Novi, Michigan on Nov. 5, 2024.

Document 113 of 500

Trudeau government announces $250 cheques for some Canadians, plus GST cuts on food, beer, children's clothes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-announces-250-cheques-some/docview/3131474250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+announces+%24250+cheques+for+some+Canadians%2C+plus+GST+cuts+on+food%2C+beer%2C+children%27s+clothes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Persistently down in the polls and with a Parliament that has ground to a halt, the Liberals attempted to solve both problems Thursday with a two-month GST cut and $250 cheques to millions of Canadians, just in time for the holiday season.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said his government is trying to give Canadians a break on the cost of living when prices are typically highest.
"Our government can't set prices at checkout, but we can put more money in people's pockets," he said.
Under the Liberals' proposal, the GST cut would be applied to children's clothes, toys, diapers and car seats and the Christmas trees those toys would go under. All of the beer, wine and cider Canadians buy for holiday parties would be exempt and many grocery items that are currently subject to GST - like pre-made meals and salads, as well as chips and candy - would not have the tax applied from Dec. 14 until Feb. 15.
Canadians who don't feel like cooking themselves would also be covered, because the GST will not apply to restaurant meals during the two-month period.
The GST cut would cost the government an estimated $1.6 billion. For a family spending $2,000 over that period, the government estimates a $100 savings.
Pratik Parmar, owner of The Cottage Cheese, a restaurant in Kensington Market that specializes in Indian cuisine, says he is still trying to understand how Ottawa's announcement will impact his business.
"It's a good initiative, but only for some small period of time," Parmar said. "It does not solve the problem completely."
The restaurant industry was among the hardest hit by pandemic closures and still hasn't recovered.
While inflation has cooled this year, consumers went through two years of above-average increases. The Liberals have trailed the Conservatives in public opinion polls for more than a year now, sometimes by more than 20 points, with cost of living consistently ranking as a top issue.
Consumers are spending less on dining out while business owners continue to face rising labour costs, sky-high rents and debt loads.
Parmar is hopeful the GST cuts will help stimulate post-holiday season traffic at his restaurant. "January is dead, as always," he said.
Trudeau also announced Wednesday that anyone who worked in 2023 and made less than $150,000 would also qualify for a new Working Canadians Rebate of $250, expected to be delivered in April. The government estimates the rebate will go to 18.7 million Canadians and cost $4.68 billion.
Trudeau had previously rejected the suggestion his government send cheques to Canadians, arguing they would be inflationary, with prices rising right alongside any government largesse.
On Thursday, he said that is no longer the case.
"We've been close to a year within the target range that the Bank of Canada set, which is why the Bank of Canada is now dropping interest rates faster than for most of our peer countries," he said. "This is something that we're able to do because Canada has one of the strongest balance sheets in the world."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said the Liberals are offering a temporary tax holiday, while continuing to increase the carbon levy and other costs for Canadians.
"What we have is a two-month temporary tax trick that will not make up for the permanent quadrupling of the carbon tax on heat housing, food and fuel," Poilievre said.
"Ironically, in just a few months, Trudeau and the NDP plan to raise taxes on all the same items they claim they're giving you a break on directly through the carbon tax."
Poilievre called on the government to scrap the consumer price on carbon and implement his proposal for a permanent GST cut on newly constructed homes.
At Queen's Park, where Premier Doug Ford's government has promised to send $200 cheques to Ontarians early in the new year, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy accused the prime minister of being a copycat.
"Obviously that was our idea," Bethlenfalvy told reporters. "But look, the best thing he can do is cut that carbon tax ... if you really want to put money back in people's pockets."
Ford's rebate plan is expected to cost $3 billion.
The New Democrats have been proposing a broader GST cut, which would cover cellphone, internet and home heating bills and would be permanent. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said Wednesday that his party had pushed the Liberals into action, even if it was only partially what the NDP had proposed.
"This is temporary, and it doesn't include the monthly bills, but we know that middle-class families need a break, so we're going to work as hard as possible to get this done as quickly as possible," he said.
However, whether the temporary GST cuts can be implemented will now depend on the government being able to pass legislation through what has become a stalled Parliament.
The House of Commons has ground to a halt for weeks over a privilege debate that has prevented any legislation from advancing, as the opposition demands documents related to Sustainable Development Technology Canada be tabled in the house, and the government cites Charter of Rights issues and attorney-client privilege in withholding some of them.
The New Democrats are not willing to relent on the broader privilege debate, but will work with the government to pass the GST cuts.
To do so, the NDP hopes to pause - but not end - the ongoing stalemate by temporarily adjourning debate in the House of Commons for a single day.
Singh plans on making a "programming" motion, which would require the Liberals' support to pass, that would theoretically allow for the legislation to be introduced and green-lit in one day. That would allow debate on the privilege motion to continue the next sitting day.
"We don't want to let the Liberals off the hook, but we want to see some relief for middle-class families," he said.
Business groups mostly welcomed the proposed GST suspension.
Canadian Federation of Independent Business president Dan Kelly said this will be a helpful but complicated initiative for his members.
"The biggest issue members have right now is consumer demand, so anything which helps with that is good, but the administrative complexity of this could outweigh the benefits," he said.
"My biggest concern is am I going to get a bunch of calls from members in March or April because they've been told they gave a GST break on things they shouldn't and didn't do one on things they should have."
Kelly Higginson, president and CEO of Restaurants Canada, said more than half of restaurants are currently losing money or just breaking even.
Higginson said fewer Canadians are dining out, and they're spending less when they do.
"The recent drop in foot traffic has been deeply discouraging for our operators as they head into the challenging winter season," she said. "Today's announcement restores some much-needed hope to our industry and we are optimistic it will translate to increased spending at local restaurants across the country."
- With files from Raisa Patel, Rob Ferguson, and Josh Rubin

Document 114 of 500

Too many people in jails are there on bail. Playing politics is making the situation worse.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/too-many-people-jails-are-there-on-bail-playing/docview/3131147975/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Too+many+people+in+jails+are+there+on+bail.+Playing+politics+is+making+the+situation+worse.&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's an uncomfortable statistic: of the almost 9,000 inmates incarcerated in Ontario's jails and penitentiaries last year, more than 80 per cent - about 7,200 people - were behind bars on remand, i.e., charged and awaiting trial. These individuals, none of whom have been found guilty, are housed in facilities across Ontario, including two supermax prisons as well as notoriously over-crowded ones, like the Toronto South Detention Centre.
Many aren't there for violent crimes offenses. The ranks of remand inmates - all of whom have been denied bail or are being held for bail violations - has surged in the past decade, despite recent Supreme Court rulings intended to restrict the deal-making between crowns and judges that has resulted in overly restrictive bail conditions, which drive up custody levels.
A brief exchange late last month of rhetorical blows between the provincial Tories and the federal Liberals suggests bail could become a political football, again, with outcomes that may make the current situation worse.
The Ford government went first, calling on Ottawa to legislate mandatory minimum sentences and toughen up bail conditions for a wide range of crimes, including drug trafficking and car-jacking; mandate a three-strikes rule for accused who breach bail terms; and eliminate pre-trial time-served "credits" in sentencing.
Federal Liberal justice minister Arif Varani shot back, saying those proposals ran afoul of Charter protections. These ideas, he said, underscore the Ontario government's failure to manage the bail system in ways that reduce backlogs instead of adding to the ranks of those serving pre-trial time.
The Tories' signaling is unfortunate. Everyone involved in Ontario's criminal justice system knows the courts are clogged due to a judge shortage - a situation that has led appeals courts to throw out cases involving violent offences due to untenable delays.
The Ford government's gambit smells of politics, staking out a tough-on-crime stance and signalling to their federal counterparts that they'd support a return to the hard-nosed criminal justice laws passed by Stephen Harper, such as mandatory minimum sentences. (Most were overturned by the Supreme Court.)
The Charter and the Criminal Code articulate the right to bail and set out a "ladder" approach that aims to match bail conditions to the severity of the allegations, as well as factors like public safety.
Canadian law puts the onus on the Crown to demonstrate the need to attach conditions to a bail application - e.g., imposing curfews or requiring a relative to assume legal responsibility for ensuring the accused adheres to their release conditions.
Yet Crowns and courts have taken to layering on ever more complex bail conditions, e.g., abstinence from drug use, that may be difficult or impossible for the accused to obey, especially those with addictions or who are homeless. The upshot: an escalating spiral of bail-arrest-detention that has filled Ontario prisons with people who were charged with a minor crime but are now behind bars for multiple bail violations.
Earlier this year, the Canadian Civil Liberties Association released a report concluding that accused individuals continue to be released on bail with "extraneous or unnecessary conditions" that violate the basic principle of proportionality laid out in the bail provisions in the Criminal Code. To add salt to the wound, the CCLA also found that the "constitutional right to a timely bail hearing" is being regularly violated due to back-ups in the court system.
As both ends of the Queen's Park/Ottawa nexus know, the Supreme Court in 2017 issued a landmark ruling, R. vs Antic, that reminded provincial courts and Crowns to obey the law and the Charter instead of indulging in the horse-trading that mars bail hearings.
The federal Liberals responded to Antic two years later with legislation designed to mitigate the negative impact of minor bail violations and thus slow or reverse the Catch-22 that entraps accused people who may have a lot of difficulty adhering to overly strict bail conditions but otherwise haven't caused any harm to a victim or property.
A 2022 study in the Canadian Journal of Law and Society found that since the Antic ruling, bail courts have been more attentive to ensuring that release conditions reflect the severity of the alleged crime. "[D]espite the Supreme Court's judgment that the `least onerous conditions' be imposed," the authors added, "the impact of this high-level call to `follow the law' is...unclear."
Instead of investing in a solution to that problem and confronting the associated mess in the bail courts, Ford's Tories have opted instead to send Ontarians $200 cheques, even as they advocate for criminal justice `reforms' that will - ironically - drive up the public burden of locking up individuals who are, after all, presumed innocent, pending their day in court.
CAPTION: Ontario's jails have seen a dramatic increase in the number of inmates over the past year, with the majority of the facilities well over capacity. The Toronto South Detention Centre is seen in this file photo.The Central East Correctional Centre in Lindsay, Ontario in 2020.
CREDIT: John Lorinc, Contributor

Subject: Bail; Violations; Crime; Prisons; Prisoners; Politics; Trials

Document 115 of 500

Doug Ford and Olivia Chow have the means to end encampments. One thing is stopping them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-olivia-chow-have-means-end-encampments/docview/3131147862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+and+Olivia+Chow+have+the+means+to+end+encampments.+One+thing+is+stopping+them&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A chill wind is blowing across Ontario.
As winter looms, homeless people are hunkering down in tent encampments. With the ground hardening under them in public parks, rival political camps are digging in for a fight.
So politicized is this issue that we cannot even agree on how to describe the people or decide on a policy. Activists insist that people in need are not the homeless but the unhoused, or "people experiencing homelessness" - a phoney war preoccupied with terminology over policy.
Now the ground is shifting.
On one side, a new alliance has formed between Doug Ford and a group of exasperated mayors across the province demanding action and eviction - with the premier leading the way. At Ford's behest, almost on a bluff, a dozen municipal leaders signed a letter urging him to clear out encampments by any means necessary.
If that necessitates suspending Charter rights and overruling the courts - which have blocked previous removals - these mayors are onside with the premier. But not all of them.
On the other side is Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who won power last year on a promise to tackle the housing problem. Eighteen months later, she has invested more political and fiscal capital in long-term affordability than in short-term accessibility.
The problem with her strategy is that in the long run we are all dead, as any economist will tell you. In the short run, homeless people need help, as any humanitarian will remind you.
Future affordable housing won't help people who can't afford their next meal, let alone their next rent cheque. Today's problems can't wait.
Yet the city now admits that hundreds if not thousands of homeless people will be frozen out, night after night, because of a shortage of shelter space this winter. Meanwhile, the courts have ruled that police cannot evict homeless people from public parks as long as they have nowhere else to go.
There's a certain logic to the verdict - why evict people from encampments if they will simply pop up at another park? But that lets everyone off the hook while politicians point accusing fingers.
As long as municipal politicians underfund emergency shelters and bylaws go unenforced, people will camp out in public parks. If you hate shelters, with all the rules and hassles, you can live on your own, at your own risk, in propane-heated tents that are fire hazards and death traps for others.
Which is precisely what's happening in Toronto and many other cities.
Encampments inevitably encroach on public parks that were designed for public use. If you believe in urban planning and, say, public transit, do you simply stop believing when people sleep in subways and streetcars or tents move in?
This occupation - or privatization - of public space is not only unhealthy and unsustainable for the most vulnerable in our cities, it also makes our parks unusable and our transit unrideable for everyone else.
All that said, few people are talking seriously about homelessness today. A battle between Ford and Chow over bike lanes, which seems like a side issue, gets all the bandwidth.
By comparison to the housing problem, bike lanes are a wedge issue wrapped in a rounding error. Homelessness, on the other hand, is an endlessly human story, a morality tale about political failure.
Every winter, as most cyclists (like me) retreat to the warmth of public transit, homeless people brave the cold and snow. Their allies rally to the cause of encampments, defending their right to freeze to death while the fight for emergency shelters is deferred to another day, another winter, or a long-term build.
The problem is not so much a lack of fiscal wealth but political will at every level of government.
Cities could easily divert funding from lesser priorities to meet the modest needs of emergency shelters, even if that means erecting pop-up tent cities in protected and secure areas of every municipality.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government not only has the money to rip out bike lanes, it keeps doling out billions of dollars to taxpayers as pre-election giveaways - $200 ahead of a possible 2025 election, and hundreds of dollars in gas tax and licence plate rebates before the 2022 vote. The premier could easily redirect some of the $7 billion he spends every year on hidden hydro subsidies - dreamed up by the last Liberal government on the dubious grounds that ratepayers had to choose between "heating and eating."
And the federal government should surely pay its fair share for the large number of refugees who are crowding Toronto's shelters.
The problem is that many of the same people who complain about encampments in neighbouring parks wouldn't countenance an emergency shelter in their neighbourhood. In NDP strongholds across the city, I've seen earnest lawn signs on pricey homes proclaiming, "I support my neighbours in tents" - only to see them quietly removed when encampments encroached.
When it comes to tents, good intent only goes so far. What you call them - the unhoused or homeless people - matters less than what you do for them.
It's not the money, it's the morality.
CAPTION: A tent encampment is seen in St. James Park in the King and Jarvis area on Nov. 12, 2024.

Subject: Public transportation; Tents; Parks & recreation areas; Political activism; Morality; Shelters; Homeless people

Title: Doug Ford and Olivia Chow have the means to end encampments. One thing is stopping them

Full text: Giller Prize 2024: Anne Michaels wins Canada's top literary prize for 'Held', Nov. 18
The pro-Palestinian protesters of the Giller Prize cite the prize's association with Scotiabank which holds an interest in Israel's Elbit company- a company that makes weapons. If Scotiabank is the link, then why are the protesters not protesting the bank itself? I say this because I participated in the recent Scotiabank waterfront run for charity. There was not a single protest in sight.

Charter City status for Toronto is long overdueDoug Ford says $48 million estimate to remove Toronto bike lanes is 'hogwash', Nov. 20
The bike lane fiasco has a total cost - both construction and demolition of bike lanes and restoring the roadway. Add it all up, and it's a huge waste of taxpayers' money. How long is Toronto putting up with Premier Doug Ford's interference? Charter city status is long overdue. It enables major cities in the Western world to manage their own affairs and control more of their tax revenue.
Ulla Colgrass, Toronto
Province should focus on a major expansion of public transit options
The government of Ontario has the right to intervene in Toronto, but rights come with discretion about when and where to exercise them. While I have the right to operate a jackhammer daily from 9 to 5, I have never used that right. My neighbour is grateful for my discretion. Toronto has developed a broad transportation plan after the widest possible public consultations. I am unclear what else could have been done to include public input. In the meantime, what generated a backlash, and now a political intervention by the province, is the extension of bike lanes into Etobicoke.
Most weekdays, my wife and I both bike to work along the Bloor lanes. They save time, but more importantly they ensure that we arrive safely. Does this come at the expense of motorists? To a degree, but the average time added is measured in single-digit minutes (according to official statistics), and that is precisely what I see during my commute. Although longer than it used to be, the line of cars is not nearly as long as the opponents of bike lanes claim. The rest of the car network seems to have absorbed much of the extra traffic. If something was wrong with the rollout of the bike lanes, it was forgetting public transit. The Ontario line will help in some places, but not in Etobicoke. Line 2 and its capillaries need to move beyond 1970s technology and design. With fundamental improvements and a diverse array of transportation options, people will be able to get to and fro as they do in all major cities around the globe.
If the government of Ontario wants Toronto to thrive, it should indeed intervene here, but not by rolling back what the city has already developed. Instead, it should intervene by supporting a major expansion of public transit options. Not an incremental, fits-and-starts, subject-to-the-political-winds expansion, but something visionary to ensure that Toronto remains a world-class city.
Edward Schatz, Toronto
A concise list of Ford's poorly designed government policiesFord is wasting far more taxpayer money than the school boards, Nov. 20
Sonia Kurmey of Etobicoke did an excellent job of concisely reporting the fiscal excesses of poorly designed Ford government policies. Her report was plainly stated and pointed to specific failures to provide responsible government. Her story should be front page news and reprinted regularly with updates as Ford's folly continues.
Tom Edwards, Toronto
Food for thought
Food is a right, not a luxury, Nov. 20
Here's an idea. Grow more food right here in Ontario. Rip up the Highway 407 and install farms. This should satisfy Premier Doug Ford's need to rip stuff up while adding much needed farmland.
Jay Butler, Etobicoke
Primary responsibility of school boards is the students
'It's appalling': Hawaii trip prompts Doug Ford to order audit of all Ontario school boards, Nov. 7
Thanks to the provincial government for enacting Bill 98 to ensure greater transparency and accountability of school boards. Education Minister Jill Dunlop is taking an important first step in initiating an investigation regarding financial responsibility. However, the families we serve and support believe it is also necessary that the ministry investigate boards' compliance with the Education Act and in particular with Reg. 181 in improving educational opportunities and education for students with differing needs.
Financial irregularities were pointed out as far back as the early 2000s by forensic accountant Al Rosen but unfortunately, due to an election and change in government, no direct action was taken. Boards seem to have forgotten that they are corporations with employment standards that must be enforced and that their primary responsibility is to act as "in loco parentis" - in the best interests of the students. The Ontario Public School Boards' Association has stated that trustees are serious about their roles and that they are "accountable to the communities and students they serve." Forensic audits of all Ontario school boards are clearly long overdue.
Janis Jaffe-White and Reva Schafer, Toronto Family Network, North York
No better place to draw attention to atrocities than the House of CommonsNDP MP cautioned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians in the House of Commons, Nov. 18
NDP MP Heather McPherson was cautioned for wearing a pin that symbolizes sympathy to Palestinians in the House of Commons. But what better place than the seat of government to show support for the innocent children being killed every day and draw attention to the atrocities in Gaza? Canada's response to the ongoing humanitarian crisis is inhumane and painful. I hope our conscience will wake up soon.
Mehdi Rizvi, Pickering
The Palestinian exception
NDP MP cautioned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians in the House of Commons, Nov. 18
Canadians should be outraged by the actions of the Speaker of the House. It's what is known as the "Palestinian exception." Members in the House wear Ukrainian "props" all the time. Members wear the Remembrance Day poppy all the time. It seems any sort of support for Palestinians has been outlawed. This action follows the Speaker of the Ontario legislature outlawing the keffiyeh, because that too is considered support for Palestinians and is therefore considered "too political." Kudos to NDP MP Heather McPherson for standing on the right side of history.
Peter Giaschi, Penticton
