Document 117 of 500

Ford keeps Wynne's fiscal legacy intact

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-keeps-wynnes-fiscal-legacy-intact/docview/3131101855/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+keeps+Wynne%27s+fiscal+legacy+intact&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When we were kids, we were wisely told not to judge a book by its cover. Similarly, it's a mistake to assume you can predict how any government will behave in office based on its political party or campaign promises. In recent Canadian history, there are many examples of free-spending Conservative governments and many examples of fiscally disciplined and tax-cutting Liberal and NDP governments.
That said, the Ford government tabled its fall fiscal update Oct. 30, which some have branded a "mini-budget" because it contains several new initiatives. And once again, when it comes to fiscal policy, the Ford government has mostly continued the policies of its predecessor, the Liberal government led by Kathleen Wynne.
To be fair, Premier Doug Ford's mini-budget does contain one echo of Ontario's last long-serving Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris - namely, a $200 one time "rebate" cheque to all Ontarians, which is something the Harris government did before its re-election campaign in 2000.
But wherever the era, the rebates are ill-advised - they don't change economic behaviour and boost economic growth the way permanent tax cuts can. Although it's important to note that the Harris rebate was part of a much more ambitious tax reduction and reform plan that spurred growth and left more "money in people's pockets" during a time when the province was balancing its budget after a long string of deficits.
Specifically, the Harris government significantly reduced personal income and business income tax rates, setting the stage for a period of prosperity in the province. By contrast, the Ford government has broken its promises to meaningfully reduce taxes and instead has kept both personal income tax rates and business income tax rates at the same level Wynne left behind. Instead of actually cutting taxes and improving the environment for economic growth and opportunity across all income levels, Ford is relying on his $200 one-time rebate to soften the blow of Ontario's high taxes.
On spending, Ford has also been much more Wynne-like than Harris-like. According to Wednesday's mini-budget, per-person spending (adjusted for inflation) is higher today than when Wynne left office. Harris, by contrast, sharply reduced spending.
Moreover, Harris's spending reductions allowed his government to quickly eliminate the deficit (left by his predecessor Bob Rae) and enact tax cuts - at a time, incidentally, when transfers from the federal government were shrinking.
Fast-forward to the Ford government. Despite maintaining spending at Wynne levels and governing during an era of increasing federal transfers, the Ford government still won't balance the books this year. The mini-budget pegs this year's deficit at $6.6 billion. And the Ford government has added a projected $106 billion in debt since taking office in 2018.
Ford acknowledged Harris's presence at a recent speech to the Empire Club of Canada in Toronto, and his $200 pre-election rebate is a political play drawn directly from the Harris playbook. However, just as it's a mistake to judge a book by its cover, it's also unwise to judge a government's approach to policy by its political stripe or pre-election gimmicks.
In reality, Ford is not the heir to the Harris legacy of tax reform, balanced budgets and smaller government. Ford's approach simply represents a continuation of the Wynne government's high-tax and deficit-fuelled approach to fiscal management that Ontarians have endured for years.
Ben Eisen is a senior fellow and Jake Fuss is director of fiscal studies at the Fraser Institute.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss Contributors Ben Eisen is a senior fellow and Jake Fuss is director of fiscal studies at the Fraser Institute.;

Document 118 of 500

On spending, Doug Ford has been more like Kathleen Wynne than Mike Harris

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/on-spending-doug-ford-has-been-more-like-kathleen/docview/3131101809/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+spending%2C+Doug+Ford+has+been+more+like+Kathleen+Wynne+than+Mike+Harris&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When we were kids, we were wisely told not to judge a book by its cover. Similarly, it's a mistake to assume you can predict how any government will behave in office based on its political party or campaign promises. In recent Canadian history, there are many examples of free-spending Conservative governments and many examples of fiscally disciplined and tax-cutting Liberal and NDP governments.

To be fair, Premier Ford's mini-budget does contain one echo of Ontario's last long-serving Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris - namely, a $200 one time "rebate" cheque to all Ontarians, which is something the Harris government did before its re-election campaign in 2000.
But wherever the era, the rebates are ill-advised - they don't change economic behaviour and boost economic growth the way permanent tax cuts can. Although it's important to note that the Harris rebate was part of a much more ambitious tax reduction and reform plan that spurred growth and left more "money in people's pockets" during a time when the province was balancing its budget after a long string of deficits.
Specifically, the Harris government significantly reduced personal income and business income tax rates, setting the stage for a period of prosperity in the province. By contrast, the Ford government has broken its promises to meaningfully reduce taxes and instead has kept both personal income tax rates and business income tax rates at the same level Premier Wynne left behind. Instead of actually cutting taxes and improving the environment for economic growth and opportunity across all income levels, Premier Ford is relying on his $200 onetime rebate to soften the blow of Ontario's high taxes.
On spending, Ford has also been much more Wynne-like than Harris-like. According to Wednesday's mini-budget, per-person spending (adjusted for inflation) is higher today than when Wynne left office. Premier Harris, by contrast, sharply reduced spending.
Moreover, Harris' spending reductions allowed his government to quickly eliminate the deficit (left by his predecessor Bob Rae) and enact tax cuts-at a time, incidentally, when transfers from the federal government were shrinking.
Fast-forward to the Ford government. Despite maintaining spending at Wynne levels and governing during an era of increasing federal transfers, the Ford government still won't balance the books this year. The mini-budget pegs this year's deficit at $6.6 billion. And the Ford government has added a projected $106 billion in debt since taking office in 2018.
Premier Ford acknowledged Mike Harris' presence at a recent speech to the Empire Club of Canada in Toronto, and his $200 pre-election rebate is a political play drawn directly from the Harris playbook. However, just as it's a mistake to judge a book by its cover, it's also unwise to judge a government's approach to policy by its political stripe or pre-election gimmicks.
In reality, Premier Ford is not the heir to the Harris legacy of tax reform, balanced budgets and smaller government. Ford's approach simply represents a continuation of the Wynne government's high-tax and deficit-fuelled approach to fiscal management that Ontarians have endured for years.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister PeterBethlenfalvy arrive at Queen's Park on Oct. 30 with the fall economic statement.
CREDIT: Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss Contributor

Subject: Tax rates; Personal income; Budgets; Tax cuts; Economic growth; Business income; Tax reform; Politics; Income taxes

Full text: Protest the bank, not Giller Prize, over ties to Israel
Anne Michaels wins Canada's top literary prize for 'Held, 'Nov. 18
The pro-Palestinian protesters of the Giller Prize cite the prize's association with Scotiabank, which holds an interest in Israel's Elbit - a company that makes weapons. If Scotiabank is the link, then why are the protesters not protesting the bank itself? I say this because I participated in the recent Scotiabank waterfront run for charity. There was not a single protest in sight.
Miriam Hochman, Toronto
Charter status
for Toronto
is long overdue
Doug Ford says $48-million estimate to remove Toronto bike lanes is 'hogwashl,'
Nov. 20
The bike lane fiasco has a total cost - both construction and demolition of bike lanes and restoring the roadway. Add it all up, and it's a huge waste of taxpayers' money. How long will Toronto put up with Premier Doug Ford's interference? Charter city status is long overdue. It enables major cities in the Western world to manage their own affairs and control more of their tax revenue.
Ulla Colgrass, Toronto
Province should focus on transit expansion
The government of Ontario has the right to intervene in Toronto, but rights come with discretion about when and where to exercise them. While I have the right to operate a jackhammer daily from 9 to 5, I have never used that right. My neighbour is grateful for my discretion. Toronto has developed a broad transportation plan after the widest possible public consultations. I am unclear what else could have been done to include public input. In the meantime, what generated a backlash, and now a political intervention by the province, is the extension of bike lanes into Etobicoke.
Most weekdays, my wife and I both bike to work along the Bloor lanes. They save time, but more importantly they ensure that we arrive safely. Does this come at the expense of motorists? To a degree, but the average time added is measured in single-digit minutes (according to official statistics), and that is precisely what I see during my commute. Although longer than it used to be, the line of cars is not nearly as long as the opponents of bike lanes claim. The rest of the car network seems to have absorbed much of the extra traffic.
If something was wrong with the rollout of the bike lanes, it was forgetting public transit. The Ontario line will help in some places, but not in Etobicoke. Line 2 and its capillaries need to move beyond 1970s technology and design. With fundamental improvements and a diverse array of transportation options, people will be able to get to and fro as they do in all major cities around the globe.
If the government of Ontario wants Toronto to thrive, it should indeed intervene here, but not by rolling back what the city has already developed.
Instead, it should intervene by supporting a major expansion of public transit options. Not an incremental, fits-and-starts, subject-to-the-political-winds expansion, but something visionary to ensure that Toronto remains a world-class city.
Edward Schatz, Toronto
A concise list
of Ford's poorly designed policies
Ford is wasting far more taxpayer money than the school boards, Letters, Nov. 20
Sonia Kurmey of Etobicoke did an excellent job of concisely reporting the fiscal excesses of poorly designed Ford government policies. Her letter was plainly stated and pointed to specific failures to provide responsible government. Her letter should be front page news and reprinted regularly, with updates as Ford's folly continues.
Tom Edwards, Toronto
First priority
for school boards is the students
'It's appalling': Hawaii trip prompts Doug Ford to order audit of all Ontario school boards, Nov. 7
Thanks to the provincial government for enacting Bill 98 to ensure greater transparency and accountability of school boards.
Education Minister Jill Dunlop is taking an important first step in initiating an investigation regarding financial responsibility.
However, the families we serve and support believe it is also necessary that the ministry investigate boards' compliance with the Education Act and in particular with Reg. 181 in improving educational opportunities and education for students with differing needs.
Financial irregularities were pointed out as far back as the early 2000s by forensic accountant Al Rosen, but unfortunately, due to an election and change in government, no direct action was taken. Boards seem to have forgotten that they are corporations with employment standards that must be enforced and that their primary responsibility is to act as "in loco parentis" - in the best interests of the students.
The Ontario Public School Boards' Association has stated that trustees are serious about their roles and that they are "accountable to the communities and students they serve." Forensic audits of all Ontario school boards are clearly long overdue.
Janis Jaffe-White and Reva Schafer, Toronto Family
Network, North York
No better place to draw attention to Gaza atrocities
NDP MP cautioned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians in the House of Commons,
Nov. 18
NDP MP Heather McPherson was cautioned for wearing a pin that symbolizes sympathy to Palestinians in the House of Commons. But what better place than the seat of government to show support for the innocent children being killed every day and draw attention to the atrocities in Gaza?
Canada's response to the ongoing humanitarian crisis is inhumane and painful. I hope our conscience will wake up soon.
Mehdi Rizvi, Pickering
Canadians should be outraged by the actions of the Speaker of the House. It's what is known as the "Palestinian exception." Members in the House wear Ukrainian "props" all the time. Members wear the Remembrance Day poppy all the time. It seems any sort of support for Palestinians has been outlawed. This action follows the Speaker of the Ontario legislature outlawing the kaffiyeh, because that, too, is considered support for Palestinians and is therefore considered "too political."
Kudos to NDP MP Heather McPherson for standing on the right side of history.

Document 120 of 500

Our politicians are failing the homeless

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/our-politicians-are-failing-homeless/docview/3131100788/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+politicians+are+failing+the+homeless&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A chill wind is blowing across Ontario.
As winter looms, homeless people are hunkering down in tent encampments. With the ground hardening under them in public parks, rival political camps are digging in for a fight.
So politicized is this issue that we cannot even agree on how to describe the people or decide on a policy. Activists insist that people in need are not the homeless but the unhoused, or "people experiencing homelessness" - a phoney war preoccupied with terminology over policy.
Now the ground is shifting.
On one side, a new alliance has formed between Doug Ford and a group of exasperated mayors across the province demanding action and eviction - with the premier leading the way. At Ford's behest, almost on a bluff, a dozen municipal leaders signed a letter urging him to clear out encampments by any means necessary.
If that necessitates suspending charter rights and overruling the courts - which have blocked previous removals - these mayors are onside with the premier. But not all of them.
On the other side is Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who won power last year on a promise to tackle the housing problem. Eighteen months later, she has invested more political and fiscal capital in long-term affordability than in short-term accessibility.
The problem with her strategy is that in the long run we are all dead, as any economist will tell you. In the short run, homeless people need help, as any humanitarian will remind you.
Future affordable housing won't help people who can't afford their next meal, let alone their next rent cheque. Today's problems can't wait.
Yet the city now admits that hundreds if not thousands of homeless people will be frozen out, night after night, because of a shortage of shelter space this winter. Meanwhile, the courts have ruled that police cannot evict homeless people from public parks as long as they have nowhere else to go.
There's a certain logic to the verdict - why evict people from encampments if they will simply pop up at another park? But that lets everyone off the hook while politicians point accusing fingers.
As long as municipal politicians underfund emergency shelters and bylaws go unenforced, people will camp out in public parks. If you hate shelters, with all the rules and hassles, you can live on your own, at your own risk, in propane-heated tents that are fire hazards and death traps for others.
Which is precisely what's happening in Toronto and many other cities.
Encampments inevitably encroach on public parks that were designed for public use. If you believe in urban planning and, say, public transit, do you simply stop believing when people sleep in subways and streetcars or tents move in?
This occupation - or privatization - of public space is not only unhealthy and unsustainable for the most vulnerable in our cities, it also makes our parks unusable and our transit unrideable for everyone else.
All that said, few people are talking seriously about homelessness today. A battle between Ford and Chow over bike lanes, which seems like a side issue, gets all the bandwidth.
By comparison to the housing problem, bike lanes are a wedge issue wrapped in a rounding error. Homelessness, on the other hand, is an endlessly human story, a morality tale about political failure.
Every winter, as most cyclists (like me) retreat to the warmth of public transit, homeless people brave the cold and snow. Their allies rally to the cause of encampments, defending their right to freeze to death while the fight for emergency shelters is deferred to another day, another winter, or a long-term build.
The problem is not so much a lack of fiscal wealth but political will at every level of government.
Cities could easily divert funding from lesser priorities to meet the modest needs of emergency shelters, even if that means erecting pop-up tent cities in protected and secure areas of every municipality.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government not only has the money to rip out bike lanes, it keeps doling out billions of dollars to taxpayers as pre-election giveaways - $200 ahead of a possible 2025 election, and hundreds of dollars in gas tax and licence plate rebates before the 2022 vote. The premier could easily redirect some of the $7 billion he spends every year on hidden hydro subsidies - dreamed up by the last Liberal government on the dubious grounds that ratepayers had to choose between "heating and eating."
And the federal government should surely pay its fair share for the large number of refugees who are crowding Toronto's shelters.
The problem is that many of the same people who complain about encampments in neighbouring parks wouldn't countenance an emergency shelter in their neighbourhood. In NDP strongholds across the city, I've seen earnest lawn signs on pricey homes proclaiming, "I support my neighbours in tents" - only to see them quietly removed when encampments encroached.
When it comes to tents, good intent only goes so far. What you call them - the unhoused or homeless people - matters less than what you do for them.
It's not the money, it's the morality.
CAPTION: A tent encampment in St. James Park near King and Jarvis streets. Cities could easily divert funding from lesser priorities to meet the modest needs of emergency shelters, even if that means pop-up tent cities in secure areas of every municipality, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 121 of 500

Premiers support bilateral U.S. deal, Ford says: Provinces concerned about movement of Chinese goods via Mexico, PM is told

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-support-bilateral-u-s-deal-ford-says/docview/3131100641/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+support+bilateral+U.S.+deal%2C+Ford+says%3A+Provinces+concerned+about+movement+of+Chinese+goods+via+Mexico%2C+PM+is+told&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to strike a separate trade deal with the United States, excluding Mexico, says Premier Doug Ford.
Ford, chair of the Council of Federation of premiers, said "there's a clear consensus that everyone agrees that we need a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. and a separate bilateral trade deal with Mexico."
With the spectre of a more protectionist American administration under president-elect Donald Trump, the Ontario premier said Wednesday it is key that Canada insulates itself from any trade tariffs by forging closer ties to the U.S.
"That's our No. 1 trading partner, our closest ally, our closest friend," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park after a conference call with the other premiers.
"What our problem is - all the premiers - we know Mexico is bringing in cheap Chinese parts, slapping 'Made in Mexico' stickers on, shipping (them) up through the U.S. and Canada, causing American jobs to be lost and Canadian jobs," he said.
The transshipment of artificially inexpensive Chinese electric vehicle parts, subsidized by Beijing, through Mexico is a sore point in Ottawa and Washington.
An expert with Canada's chamber of commerce cautioned that tossing Mexico out of the deal before talks even begin would be rash.
"We very much think that we should be working with all three countries and that we're better together," said Catherine Fortin LeFaivre, the chamber's vice-president of strategic policy and global partnerships.
"CUSMA has been good for Canadians. It's been good for Canadian businesses," Fortin LeFaivre said, referring to the Canada-United States-Mexico free trade agreement.
Ford said the premiers will meet in person in Toronto Dec. 15-16 to further discuss the issue, which he has also raised on the phone with Trudeau.
A Team Canada trade mission to Washington is planned for February or March after Trump's inauguration.
"I had a call with the prime minister right after I sent my statement out," said the premier, referring to comments last week on concerns about Mexico being a back door to EVs in North America.
"He's very supportive, and I had an opportunity to meet (Industry) Minister (François-Philippe) Champagne as well. And we'll reach out to Chrystia, I'm sorry, Minister Freeland and the rest of the team," he said, referring to Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland.
"So we have a good relationship, all of us. That's the most important thing with all the premiers - we put all political stripes aside. We're speaking as Team Canada, and we feel the same way with the federal government. We're going to be there and take that Team Canada approach."
Ford noted Ontario does two-way trade with the U.S. worth $493 billion annually, compared with $40 billion of trade with Mexico.
"I just want them to play fair," he said of the Mexican government.
"Right now, Mexico is not playing fair. They're not playing nice in the sandbox, per se, when they're importing Chinese-made products at a much lower cost, costing U.S. and Canadian jobs. That's not fair trade."
Led by Manitoba's Wab Kinew, the premiers also want Ottawa to hit its target of two per cent of gross domestic product spending on defence in order for Canada to meet its NATO commitments.
"I want to thank ... Premier Kinew for his idea and that came at our last meeting," said Ford.
Trump has often railed against other NATO allies for not spending enough on defence and instead relying on the American military to keep them safe.
Freeland said she has heard concerns about Mexico becoming a back door for Chinese products and she highlighted that Canada is the only country in the world to fully match the steel, aluminum and EV tariffs the U.S. has in place.
"I think those are legitimate concerns for our American partners and neighbours to have. Those are concerns that I share," she said.
Trade Minister Mary Ng said China is producing an excess supply of EVs in particular and Mexico cannot be a conduit for dumping those vehicles on the North American market.
"We cannot have a back door for excess capacity into North America. We're concerned about it. We share those concerns," she said ahead of the Liberal caucus meeting Wednesday.
Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, who is currently on the campaign trail, said in a statement that he plans to focus on the U.S. trade relationship.
"The most important thing for Canadians is that our trade is protected. We have strong family and business ties to the U.S.," he said. "I will work with other premiers and the federal government to ensure that jobs and businesses in Nova Scotia that rely on U.S. trade are protected."
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has also called for a bilateral deal with the U.S.
In a statement to the Star, newly elected New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt said it was important to have a strong relationship with both countries.
"It's important to our province and economy to have strong trade agreements and create mutually beneficial agreements with both the U.S. and Mexico," she said. "We're focused on what's in our province's and New Brunswickers' best interest."
Canada and Mexico's trading relationship has been growing since the passage of the Canada-United States-Mexico agreement. In 2023, Canada shipped $8.3 billion worth of goods to Mexico, while Mexico shipped more than $46 billion in goods to Canada.
Fortin LeFaivre said it is a good time for Canada to focus on the trade irritants it has with the U.S. and also look for ways to solve those issues. She said Mexico should look at its rules for Chinese imports, but the agreement can still hold.
"The situation geopolitically has changed very much since the initial CUSMA negotiations. And factors such as China are major and we should be aligned," she said. "Mexico needs to have a position that is stronger and more aligned with the position that's been taken by the U.S. and Canada."
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 122 of 500

Too many people in jails under remand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/too-many-people-jails-under-remand/docview/3131100626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Too+many+people+in+jails+under+remand&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's an uncomfortable statistic: of the almost 9,000 inmates incarcerated in Ontario's jails and penitentiaries last year, more than 80 per cent - about 7,200 people - were behind bars on remand, i.e., charged and awaiting trial.
These individuals, none of whom have been found guilty, are housed in facilities across Ontario, including two supermax prisons, as well as notoriously overcrowded ones, like the Toronto South Detention Centre.
Many aren't there for violent offences. The ranks of remand inmates - all of whom have been denied bail or are being held for bail violations - has surged in the past decade, despite recent Supreme Court rulings intended to restrict the deal-making between Crowns and judges that has resulted in overly restrictive bail conditions, which drive up custody levels.
A brief exchange late last month of rhetorical blows between the provincial Tories and the federal Liberals suggests bail could become a political football, again, with outcomes that may make the current situation worse.
The Ford government went first, calling on Ottawa to legislate mandatory minimum sentences and toughen up bail conditions for a wide range of crimes, including drug trafficking and carjacking; mandate a three-strikes rule for accused who breach bail terms; and eliminate pre-trial time-served "credits" in sentencing.
Federal Liberal Justice Minister Arif Varani shot back, saying those proposals ran afoul of Charter protections. These ideas, he said, underscore the Ontario government's failure to manage the bail system in ways that reduce backlogs instead of adding to the ranks of those serving pre-trial time.
The Tories' signalling is unfortunate. Everyone involved in Ontario's criminal justice system knows the courts are clogged due to a judge shortage - a situation that has led appeals courts to throw out cases involving violent offences due to untenable delays.
The Ford government's gambit smells of politics, staking out a tough-on-crime stance and signalling to their federal counterparts that they'd support a return to the hard-nosed criminal justice laws passed by Stephen Harper, such as mandatory minimum sentences. (Most were overturned by the Supreme Court.)
The Charter and the Criminal Code articulate the right to bail and set out a "ladder" approach that aims to match bail conditions to the severity of the allegations, as well as factors like public safety.
Canadian law puts the onus on the Crown to demonstrate the need to attach conditions to a bail application - e.g., imposing curfews or requiring a relative to assume legal responsibility for ensuring the accused adheres to their release conditions.
Yet Crowns and courts have taken to layering on ever more complex bail conditions, e.g., abstinence from drug use, that may be difficult or impossible for the accused to obey, especially those with addictions or who are homeless. The upshot: an escalating spiral of bail-arrest-detention that has filled Ontario prisons with people who were charged with a minor crime but are now behind bars for multiple bail violations.
Earlier this year, the Canadian Civil Liberties Association released a report concluding that accused individuals continue to be released on bail with "extraneous or unnecessary conditions" that violate the basic principle of proportionality laid out in the bail provisions in the Criminal Code.
To add salt to the wound, the CCLA also found that the "constitutional right to a timely bail hearing" is being regularly violated due to backups in the court system.
As both ends of the Queen's Park/Ottawa nexus know, the Supreme Court in 2017 issued a landmark ruling, R. v. Antic, that reminded provincial courts and Crowns to obey the law and the Charter instead of indulging in the horse-trading that mars bail hearings.
The federal Liberals responded to Antic two years later with legislation designed to mitigate the negative impact of minor bail violations and thus slow or reverse the catch-22 that entraps accused people who may have a lot of difficulty adhering to overly strict bail conditions but otherwise haven't caused any harm to a victim or property.
A 2022 study in the Canadian Journal of Law and Society found that since the Antic ruling, bail courts have been more attentive to ensuring that release conditions reflect the severity of the alleged crime. "(Despite) the Supreme Court's judgment that the "least onerous conditions" be imposed," the authors added, "the impact of this high-level call to "follow the law is ... unclear."
Instead of investing in a solution to that problem and confronting the associated mess in the bail courts, Ford's Tories have opted instead to send Ontarians $200 cheques, even as they advocate for criminal justice reforms that will - ironically - drive up the public burden of locking up individuals who are, after all, presumed innocent, pending their day in court.
John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor. He is a contributor for the Star.
CREDIT: John Lorinc Contributor John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor. He is a contributor for the Star.;

Subject: Bail; Violations; Crime; Prisoners; Trials

NDP taps former mayor on 'new deal'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-taps-former-mayor-on-new-deal/docview/3131099903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+taps+former+mayor+on+%27new+deal%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles has turned to former Toronto mayor David Miller for help in drafting a "new deal" for Ontario municipalities.
With an early election possible next year, Stiles said "meddling" by Premier Doug Ford's government - now planning to remove some Toronto bike lanes - in addition to a lack of provincial funding for transit, shelters and affordable housing makes an improved relationship crucial.
"We need the province to actually be a partner," she told a news conference with Miller on Wednesday, noting municipalities are having to raise property taxes because of rising costs, such as dealing with tent encampments.
Miller was mayor from 2003 to 2010, when he was succeeded by the premier's late brother, Rob Ford.
He told reporters he will use his experience in provincial-municipal relations to consult with local leaders and hold open consultations on a new way forward that restores "mutual respect" between the two levels of government.
"The relationship has to be fixed," he added, citing consistent long-term funding as a key component of recommendations he will make to Stiles.
"If and when they become the government, they will implement the right kind of change," Miller said of the New Democrats.
The premier, whose Progressive Conservatives enjoy a strong lead over the NDP and Liberals in public opinion polls, has been hinting at an early election next year instead of waiting until the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy pushed back at NDP attacks over municipal funding this week, saying the province has increased it by 45 per cent in the last five years in addition to more money for infrastructure such as water and sewer lines to lay the groundwork for more housing.

Document 124 of 500

Ford government announces tough new rules for 'bad actor' immigration consultants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-announces-tough-new-rules-bad/docview/3130783624/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+announces+tough+new+rules+for+%27bad+actor%27+immigration+consultants&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New provincial legislation will target "bad-actor" immigration consultants who prey on newcomers with fines and lifetime bans as punishment.
Announced Wednesday in Brampton by David Piccini, minister of labour, immigration, training and skills development, the measures will set out new, tougher rules for immigration representatives that could require them to issue written contracts, show proof of their licence, and give applicants the right to see their files.
"Like so many Canadians whose parents or grandparents came here for a better life, immigrants come to this province to work hard, support their families, and contribute to a stronger Ontario," Piccini said in a written statement.
"Struggling to navigate a confusing federal immigration system, many newcomers put their trust in immigration representatives - many of whom are diligent and honest, but a few bad actors exploit vulnerable newcomers, jeopardizing their immigration status and scamming them out of tens of thousands of dollars. We will soon introduce legislation to enforce standards for immigration representatives using Ontario's immigration nominee program and crack down on fraud."
Minimum penalties for fraud will be a three- to 10-year ban from the profession, and a lifetime ban for anyone convicted of human trafficking or withholding an applicant's passport.
Fines for representatives or employers who lie during the application process - or urge their client to be dishonest - rise to $10,000 from the current $2,000.
From 2019 to April of this year, 153 people - most of them citizens or permanent residents of Canada - were charged with fraudulent immigration consultant offences, according to statistics from Canada Border Services Agency.
"Brampton is proud to be one of Canada's most diverse cities, welcoming thousands of newcomers each year who contribute to our shared prosperity. Protecting those who come here seeking better opportunities is a responsibility we take very seriously," Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said in a written statement.
"These measures are a critical step to ensuring newcomers are treated with fairness and respect as they work to build their lives in Brampton and across Ontario."
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: Labour minister David Piccini is shown with Premier Doug Ford at a funding announcement in 2023.

Document 125 of 500

Walmart Canada, Amazon, Costco, Sobeys and more grocers inform customers of carrot recall linked to deadly E.coli outbreak in the U.S., products pulled from shelves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/walmart-canada-amazon-costco-sobeys-more-grocers/docview/3130783343/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Walmart+Canada%2C+Amazon%2C+Costco%2C+Sobeys+and+more+grocers+inform+customers+of+carrot+recall+linked+to+deadly+E.coli+outbreak+in+the+U.S.%2C+products+pulled+from+shelves&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Various brands of organic baby carrots, linked to a deadly E. coli outbreak in the U.S., have been recalled in the U.S. and Canada.
If you've got some baby carrots in your fridge to nibble on, you might want to check out the packaging to see if it's part of a new recall by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA).
The CFIA has issued a recall for various brands of organic carrots due to concerns with possible E. coli contamination.
Brands included in the recall are Cal-Organic, PC Organics, Compliments Organic and Bunny-Luv. The recalling firm is Grimmway Farms.
"Grimmway Farms has issued a recall of select organic whole carrots and organic baby carrots that should no longer be in grocery stores but may be in consumers' refrigerators or freezers," according to a statement on Grimmway Farms' website.
Grimmway Farms says the products may have been contaminated with Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli (E. coli) O121:H19.
E. coli O121:H19 is a bacterium that can cause serious and sometimes fatal infections in young children, elderly people and those with a weakened immune system.
"Some infections can cause severe bloody diarrhea conditions, such as a hemolytic uremic syndrome, or the development of high blood pressure, chronic kidney disease, and neurologic problems," the company wrote in a post on their website. "Symptoms include severe stomach cramps, diarrhea, fever, nausea, and/or vomiting. The incubation period for E. coli O121:H19 in humans can range from 24 hours to as much as ten days. The average incubation period is 3 to 4 days."
Grimmway Farms said recalled products were shipped directly to retail distribution centers nationwide in the United States, Puerto Rico and Canada.
"Do not consume, use, sell, serve or distribute recalled products," the CFIA said in the recall notice dated Nov. 18.
Walmart Canada and Sobeys have confirmed the product had been for sale in their stories.
"At Walmart Canada, food safety is a top priority," said Walmart Canada in a statement. "I can confirm this product was offered for sale by Walmart Canada in-store and online. Given the recall, it is being removed from sale."
A Sobeys spokesperson confirmed the impacted products are no longer in their supply chain or in any of their stores.
"Food safety is our number one priority, and we follow all guidance issued by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency regarding investigations and recalls," said read the statement from Sobeys.
Amazon posted a recall notice for the impacted carrots sold on their platform. Costco also posted a recall notice for customers on their website.
The concern is potential exposure to the E. coli 0121 bacteria, which may not look or smell spoiled, but can still make you sick.
"This recall was triggered by a recall in another country. There have been no reported illnesses associated with the consumption of these products in Canada."
According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 18 states in the United States have reported illness believed to be caused by the carrots, sold by Grimmway Farms in California.
The recall was made Nov. 16 after 39 cases of illness were reported, and 15 people hospitalized. One person has also died in connection with the E. coli 0121 infection, according to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
The carrots include organic juice carrots, fresh carrots, organic baby-cut carrots, baby-cut carrots, organic petite carrots, and carrots sold with the brands Bunny-Luv, PC Organics, Compliments Organic and Cal-Organic.
The recall includes both single and double packages, ranging in size from 340 grams, 454 grams, 907 grams, 2.27 kilograms, 11.34 kilograms to 50 pounds.
The Cal-Organic brands have best before dates from Sept. 11 to Nov. 2, and Aug. 14 to Oct. 23.
The Bunny-Luv brands had best before dates from Aug. 14 to Oct. 23.
Compliments Organic brands had best before dates ranging from Aug. 14 or Sept. 14 to Oct. 23 and 24.
The PC Organics brands had best before dates from Aug. 14 to Oct. 23, and Sept. 11 to Nov. 2.
These products should no longer be on store shelves, but if you still have these carrot packages at your home, throw them out or return to the location where they were purchased, the Canadian Food Inspection Agency said.
E. coli symptoms can include nausea, vomiting, mild to severe abdominal cramps and watery to bloody diarrhea.
In severe cases, some may have seizures or strokes, need blood transfusions and kidney dialysis or live with permanent kidney damage. Death may also occur in extreme cases.
Here's the list of products with UPC codes:
Bunny-Luv organic juice carrots, 11.34g, 0 7878390227 9Bunny-Luv fresh carrots, 907g, 03338366001 1Cal-Organic organic baby-cut carrots 454g, 0 3338390205 0Cal-Organic organic baby cut carrots, two-pack 907g, 0 7878390207 1Cal-Organic organic baby cut carrots, 907g, 0 7878390206 4Cal-Organic organic carrots, 907g, 0 3338390202 9Cal-Organic organic carrots, 2.27 kg, 0 3338390203 6Cal-Organic organic petite carrots, 340g, 0 7878390810 3Cal-Organic carrots, 50 pound, no UPC codeCompliments Organic baby cut carrots, 454g, 0 5574250267 1Compliments Organic carrots, 907g, 0 5574250268 8PC Organics baby-cut carrots, 454g, 0 6038369288 9PC Organics baby-cut carrots, 907g, 0 6038373643 9PC Organics carrots, 907g, 0 6038369593 4
To learn more about the recall, visit recalls-rappels.canada.ca. Consumers with questions or products in their possession may contact Grimmway Farms' customer services desk at 1-800-301-3101.

Document 126 of 500

Canada's premiers want Justin Trudeau to do a trade deal with the U.S. that excludes Mexico, Doug Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-premiers-want-justin-trudeau-do-trade/docview/3130783285/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+premiers+want+Justin+Trudeau+to+do+a+trade+deal+with+the+U.S.+that+excludes+Mexico%2C+Doug+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to strike a separate trade deal with the United States, excluding Mexico, says Premier Doug Ford.
Ford, chair of the Council of Federation of premiers, said "there's a clear consensus that everyone agrees that we need a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. and a separate bilateral trade deal with Mexico."
With the spectre of a more protectionist American administration under President-elect Donald Trump, the Ontario premier said Wednesday it is key that Canada insulates itself from any trade tariffs by forging closer ties to the U.S.
"That's our number one trading partner, our closest ally, our closest friend," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park after a conference call with the other premiers.
"What our problem is - all the premiers - we know Mexico is bringing in cheap Chinese parts, slapping 'made in Mexico' stickers on, shipping (them) up through the U.S. and Canada, causing American jobs to be lost and Canadian jobs," he said.
The transshipment of artificially inexpensive Chinese electric vehicle parts, subsidized by Beijing, through Mexico is a sore point in Ottawa and Washington.
Ford said the premiers will meet in person in Toronto on Dec. 15-16 to further discuss the issue, which he has also raised on the phone with Trudeau.
A Team Canada trade mission to Washington is planned for February or March after Trump's inauguration.
"I had a call with the prime minister right after I sent my statement out (last week on concerns about Mexico being a back door to EVs in North America)," said the premier.
"He's very supportive and I had an opportunity to meet (Industry) Minister (François-Philippe) Champagne as well. And we'll reach out to Chrystia, I'm sorry, Minister Freeland and the rest of the team," he said, referring to Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland.
"So we have a good relationship all of us. That's the most important thing with all the premiers, we put all political stripes aside. We're speaking as Team Canada, and we feel the same way with the federal government. We're going to be there and take that Team Canada approach."
Ford noted Ontario does two-way trade with the U.S. worth $493 billion annually compared with $40 billion of trade with Mexico.
"I just want them to play fair," he said of the Mexican government.
"Right now, Mexico is not playing fair. They're not playing nice in the sandbox, per se, when they're importing Chinese-made products at a much lower cost, costing U.S. and Canadian jobs. That's not fair trade."
Led by Manitoba's Wab Kinew, the premiers also want Ottawa to hit its target of two per cent of gross domestic product spending on defence in order for Canada to meet its NATO commitments.
"I want to thank ... Premier Kinew for his idea and that came at our last meeting," said Ford.
Trump has often railed against other NATO allies for not spending enough on defence and instead relying on the American military to keep them safe.
Deputy prime minister and finance minister Chrystia Freeland said she has heard concerns about Mexico becoming a backdoor for Chinese products and she highlighted that Canada is the only country in the world to fully match the steel, aluminum and EV tariffs Canada has in place.
"I think those are legitimate concerns for our American partners and neighbours to have. Those are concerns that I share."
Trade minister Mary Ng said China is producing an excess supply of EVs in particular and Mexico cannot be a conduit for dumping those vehicles on the North American market.
"We cannot have a back door for excess capacity into North America. We're concerned about it. We share those concerns," she said ahead of the Liberal caucus meeting Wednesday.
Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, who is currently on the campaign trail, said in a statement that he plans to focus on the U.S. trade relationship.
"The most important thing for Canadians is that our trade is protected. We have strong family and business ties to the U.S.," he said. "I will work with other premiers and the federal government to ensure that jobs and businesses in Nova Scotia that rely on U.S. trade are protected."
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has also called for a bilateral deal with the U.S.
In a statement to the Star, newly elected New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt said it was important to have a strong relationship with both countries.
"It's important to our province and economy to have strong trade agreements and create mutually beneficial agreements with both the U.S. and Mexico," she said "We're focused on what's in our province's and New Brunswickers' best interest."
Canada and Mexico's trading relationship has been growing since the passage of the Canada-United States-Mexico agreement (CUSMA). In 2023, Canada shipped $8.3 billion worth of goods to Mexico, while Mexico shipped more than $46 billion in goods to Canada.
Catherine Fortin LeFaivre, a vice-president with the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, said now is not the time to start jettisoning countries from the agreement.
"We very much think that we should be working with all three countries and that we're better together. CUSMA has been good for Canadians. It's been good for Canadian businesses," she said.
Fortin LeFaivre said it is a good time for Canada to focus on the trade irritants it has with the U.S. and also look for ways to solve those issues. She said Mexico should look at its rules for Chinese imports, but the agreement can still hold.
"The situation geopolitically has changed very much since the initial CUSMA negotiations. And, factors such as China are major and we should be aligned," she said. "Mexico needs to have a position that is stronger and more aligned with the position that's been taken by the U.S. and Canada."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford attend an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Document 127 of 500

First Nation calls for help to fight gang, addiction crises: Chief asks province, Ottawa for policing, mental health aid

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/first-nation-calls-help-fight-gang-addiction/docview/3130581446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=First+Nation+calls+for+help+to+fight+gang%2C+addiction+crises%3A+Chief+asks+province%2C+Ottawa+for+policing%2C+mental+health+aid&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A northern Ontario First Nation declared a state of emergency Tuesday over concurrent crises involving crime, gang activity, addiction and mental health problems that have plagued the community for years.
Netmizaaggamig Nishnaabeg First Nation has asked the province and the federal government for policing and mental health help, Chief Louis Kwissiwa said at a news conference at Queen's Park.
"Organized crime and drug trafficking has created an ever-growing crisis in our community," Kwissiwa said. "Addiction, violence, and trauma is spreading. Overdoses are increasing. Mental health crises are pushing our resources to their breaking point."
The First Nation, about 350 kilometres north of Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., is $5 million in debt after trying for several years to fix the problems, the chief said. They've installed security cameras, bought licence plate readers and have brought in outside security on an emergency basis.
"Our call rate for emergency services is 35 times higher per capita than the city of Thunder Bay," Kwissiwa said.
Suicides, addictions and untreated mental health issues are on the rise in the community, he added.
"We cannot continue to shoulder this crisis alone," Kwissiwa said. "We are calling on the federal and provincial governments, law enforcement agencies and health services to act now."
The closest detox and treatment beds are a four-hour drive away in Sault Ste. Marie or Thunder Bay.
"Without immediate intervention, more lives will be lost, and the fabric of our community will continue to unravel," said Joe Moses, the CEO of the First Nation.
He said 64 of the community's 121 homes have some type of substance use occurring inside, a figure that has risen in recent years with predatory gangs lurking with drugs.
There are at least three such gangs within the community, Moses said. There have been violent home invasions, stabbings and human trafficking, he said.
Netmizaaggamig Nishnaabeg, with an on-reserve population around 400, must wait at least 45 minutes for police, fire or ambulance response in an emergency.
The nearby Anishinabek Police Services department has struggled with staffing and has only five officers available, Moses said. An Ontario Provincial Police detachment is supposed to backfill for Anishinabek Police Services, but the OPP is struggling with staffing, too, he said.
Sol Mamakwa, NDP deputy leader and the only First Nation representative in the Ontario legislature, brought the issue to question period Tuesday and asked if the province would help.
"This is a serious matter," said Solicitor General Michael Kerzner. "That's why earlier this morning I spoke with Chief Jeff Skye of Anishinabek Police Services to discuss just how serious this is."
Federal Indigenous Services Minister Patty Hajdu has been in touch with Netmizaaggamig Nishnaabeg First Nation to find solutions, her office said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Anishinabek police Chief Jeff Skye, shown last year, has been in contact with Ontario Solicitor General Michael Kerzner to discuss the issues facing Netmizaaggamig Nishnaabeg First Nation, Kerzner said at Queen's Park Tuesday.Sean KilpatrickTHE CANADIAN PRESSfile photo
CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Subject: Workforce planning; Mental health; Addictions; Community policing; Gangs; Drug overdose; State of emergency; Criminal records; Crisis intervention

But such scans aren't always good for patients - or our health care system, Nov. 16 Video captures moment of impact as stolen BMW hits TTC bus, injuring five people, Nov. 18

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3130581402/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford+government+disputes+Toronto%27s+%2448-million+bike+lane+removal+cost%3A+%27It+can%27t+cost+double%2C+Nov.+18+Province-led+survey+suggests+higher+cycling+rates+in+Toronto+than+Ford+government+has+cited%3A+city+staff+report%2C+Nov.+14+Ford%27s+education+minister+directly+warns+school+boards+against+%27unnecessary+or+extravagant+expenses%2C+Nov.+14+I+paid+%241%2C500+for+an+MRI+and+got+an+appointment+in+days.+But+such+scans+aren%27t+always+good+for+patients+-+or+our+health+care+system%2C+Nov.+16+Video+captures+moment+of+impact+as+stolen+BMW+hits+TTC+bus%2C+injuring+five+people%2C+Nov.+18&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stop using
city streets as a construction yard
Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18
I have a simple suggestion for Premier Doug Ford. Instead of wasting $48 million tax dollars to remove the bike lanes that provide increased safety and convenience to a significant - and increasing - percentage of Toronto taxpayers, maybe you should focus on a different cause of congestion. Perhaps you could pass a different piece of legislation: one that would force your developer buddies to stop using city streets as a convenient and low-cost "yard" for their construction activities. I'm pretty sure that those lane takeovers do more to slow traffic and cause congestion than do well-designed bike lanes. Even better, ending the practice wouldn't cost the province $48 million.
Neil R. Thomlinson,Toronto
Make traffic flow faster to ease congestion
A flow in any system is only determined by its choke points. These are the intersections. You can rip up bicycle lanes until the cows come home, all you get is more space for idling cars.Downtown, every traffic light starts with cars already blocking the intersection. With luck, one or two may get though on each full cycle. Effective street speed? Near none. So, focus on speeding up all intersections. We have a good size fine to wield. Now, make the drivers pay, and Toronto will soon be like Stockholm or Berlin, where drivers stop before the pedestrian crossing stripes all the time. We all win. Traffic flow will increase in orders of magnitude, less frustration, more money for the city coffers, and pedestrians can cross without snaking around dirty cars. No Bike paths gone and happiness for all.
Bengt,Lindvall, Toronto
Ford's actions should worry
all Ontarians
If Doug Ford can override Toronto's municipal government and tear up bike lanes, he can do that anywhere. What will he do next? Maybe he'll turn a park in North Bay into a parking lot. Or turn a community centre in Sudbury into a casino. Or remove dedicated bus lanes in Ottawa or Mississauga in the name of "reducing congestion." Ontarians all across the province should be worried about the precedent Bill 212 could set.
RichardSpiegel, Toronto
Councillor Holyday is part
of the problem
Province-led survey suggests higher cycling rates in Toronto than Ford government has cited: city staff report, Nov. 14
City Coun. Stephen Holyday suggests that the lack of local newspapers covering things like development proposals and bike lanes is a cause of citizens becoming detached from their local government. If Holyday is concerned about this detachment, you would think that in an effort to engage constituents, his website would be chock full of information, that his office would be sending robust e-newsletters on a monthly basis, that he would find a way to reach out to seniors, hold coffee with your councillor mornings. But he does none of the above and some residents subscribe to Coun. Amber Morley's newsletter to learn more about Etobicoke. I suggest that Holyday is part of the problem.
JanetGriffiths-Maxymiw, Toronto
Ford wasting far more money than school boards
Ford's education minister directly warns school boards against 'unnecessary or extravagant expenses, Nov. 14
The Ford government is warning the school boards to rein in spending. What hypocrisy!This from our government that has wasted more taxpayer money than any governing body.Here's a spending list (and I'm sure there is more that I've missed):
$30 million to fight the federal carbon fee;
$231 million to cancel the green renewable contracts as soon as they took office;
$30 million for gas pump stickers;
$225 million to cancel the contract with the Beer Store, a year before it was to expire, which would have cost nothing;
$800 million to build a parking lot for a spa at Ontario Place no one wants;
Proposed $4 billion (but more likely $10 billion) to build a highway we don't need.
This is not a government that thinks or acts fiscally responsible. Maybe start by cleaning up their own messes. Just think of what all this wasted money could do if it was invested in health care and education which right now are both in shambles.
Sonia Kurmey,Etobicoke
Requiring retinal scans seems unnecessary
I paid $1,500 for an MRI and got an appointment in days. But such scans aren't always good for patients - or our health care system, Nov. 16
I am 69 and wanted to test my eyesight. I went to an optometrist clinic that was new to me and they said, unsurprisingly, they would have to do a full work up on me. I asked what that entailed and they listed a few things. At the end I was informed "they required" that I have a retinal scan - $160. I had never heard of that before so I asked if it was a necessary test. The receptionist answered that they required it. Yes, but is it necessary? I asked. She asked me to discuss it with the doctor. While I was waiting for the doctor, I went to Dr. Google and it explained the test and said it was "nice to do but not necessary" for generally healthy patients. I didn't have the $160, so I told the doctor I wasn't going to have that procedure done. She was quite perturbed with me and long story short, I left with my prescription and the admonishment that the next time I came, I would be required to do the scan. I won't be going back.
Bruce Van Dieten,Toronto
Let's treat
car theft like a serious crime
Video captures moment of impact as stolen BMW hits TTC bus, injuring five people, Nov. 18
Yet again we see on the news that a stolen car has crashed into a bus injuring several people. When are the courts going to get serious about this pervasive crime wave? These guys were out on bail (again) and because there are no serious penalties or accountability for their crimes, they get out on bail and start all over again. The courts should keep these perpetrators locked up so that they cannot harm anyone while waiting for trial. Car theft is a very serious crime, let's treat it as such.
Jane White,Scarborough
CREDIT: ;

Document 129 of 500

Justin Trudeau's digital tax is hated by America. Here's why Donald Trump may use it against us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-digital-tax-is-hated-america/docview/3130581393/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+digital+tax+is+hated+by+America.+Here%27s+why+Donald+Trump+may+use+it+against+us&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It's set to bring in billions of dollars the federal government is keen to have. But Ottawa must first fend off a resistant United States before it can collect.
The Trudeau government's digital services tax - to be paid by the world's digital giants operating in Canada - kicks in this year. However, a trade challenge from the U.S. and the re-election of Donald Trump has business groups and even Premier Doug Ford calling on Ottawa to back down out of fear of retaliation.
"We cannot put millions of Canadian jobs and our historic economic partnership with the U.S. at risk because of a stubborn refusal to listen to the concerns of our American friends," Ford said last month.
Now, the stakes could rise with the return of a Trump administration threatening tough tariffs with trade deal negotiations on the horizon.
Here's what you need to know about the digital services tax.
What's the digital services tax?
For several years, there have been global negotiations on how to tax the revenues from economic activity generated online, by large companies operating in other countries.
Supporters in Canada have argued the tax is necessary because global digital giants aren't paying their fair share of taxes. The Liberals first promised a tax in 2019. But Canada has always said its priority is a global agreement.
Those negotiations, however, have stalled.
Impatient with that process after several delays, Canada and other countries decided to impose their own taxes until there is progress on the global efforts. The U.K., France, Italy and India are among countries which have already put a digital services tax in place. Others have agreed to hold off until at least 2025.
Foreign digital giants that make more than $20 million in Canada and more than 750 million euros globally in annual revenues will now have to pay a three per cent tax on revenues earned within Canada.
It is expected to generate $900 million per year, but Ottawa will get $2.3 billion in its first year because it would apply retroactively to revenues generated since 2022.
Katrina Miller, the executive director for Canadians for Tax Fairness, told the Star that having our own digital services tax puts pressure on other countries to get a global deal done and could foster increased competition in Canada.
But the U.S. sees things differently.
Why is it a sensitive issue for the U.S.?
The U.S. has argued that unilateral digital service taxes "discriminate against U.S. companies," because it's mostly American companies that meet the revenue threshold.
The previous Trump administration launched investigations against countries which made the move, and even imposed retaliatory measures in some cases. But President Joe Biden's White House was more open to putting retaliatory measures on hold in an effort to reach a global deal.
When Canada announced its own tax would proceed this summer, arguing it was unfair for Canada to wait as negotiations stalled, the U.S. challenged it under the two countries' trade agreement, launching a period of dispute settlement consultations - a less heavy-handed approach in which the two countries hold official conversations in hopes of resolving the issue.
The deadline for those consultations quietly passed last week without a resolution - Canada remains committed to the tax in the absence of an international agreement - giving the U.S. the option to proceed further by requesting a dispute settlement panel.
But given the Biden administration's lame-duck position, some observers and a government official who spoke to the Star suggested it could be left to the incoming Trump administration to deal with Canada's tax. Even if the Biden administration proceeds with its complaint, that process is unlikely to be complete before Trump takes power in January - and the U.S.' argument is not "their strongest against Canada," said William Pellerin, an international trade lawyer with McMillan LLP.
"Ultimately, it's one that's going to be ... probably best dealt with politically rather than through the litigation process," Pellerin told the Star.
A spokesperson for United States Trade Representative Katherine Tai suggested the U.S. has no immediate plans to move further in an emailed statement to the Star.
"We continue to consult with Canada to address our concerns, and we reserve our rights to further proceedings, including under the USMCA, (United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement) should those concerns not be resolved," the spokesperson said.
What's at stake for Canadians?
Since the Canadian government announced the tax, fears of devastating retaliatory measures has had business groups here calling for the Trudeau government to back off.
And those warnings are taking on new urgency as Canada prepares for a Trump administration that has resorted to strong-armed political responses in the past.
Canada will have to renegotiate the U.S. and Mexico agreement with a Trump administration that has slapped tariffs on Canadian goods before and promised during the last election to implement a 10 per cent tariff on all goods coming into America.
"Trump is ready to use what we would call in the trade world the 'nuclear option,'" Pellerin said.
Jessica Brandon-Jepp, the senior director of fiscal and financial services policy at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, told the Star she worries Canada's new tax could threaten the nearly $3.6 billion (2023 figures) of goods and services that cross the border each day.
"This particular tax is not going to bring in nearly as much as the damage that it has the potential to do for the Canadian economy and our trading relationship," she said in an interview.
Pellerin said the federal government could be open to negotiating the terms of its tax during trade talks with the U.S., but the large revenues may be too difficult to give up.
The Trump transition team did not return a request for comment about how it plans to handle this issue.
Asked several times in the last few weeks about how she will deal with a Trump administration on the issue, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland would only point to the countries with their own digital services taxes, including France and Italy which have not faced retaliatory measures from the U.S. On Tuesday, she suggested it would be unfair to Canada if we faced retaliatory measures for doing the same as other countries.
"We simply believe that Canada should be on a level playing field, on an even footing, with our G7 partners," Freeland told reporters in Ottawa. "We think that Canada, with our very strong relationship with the United States, with our free trade agreement with the United States, should not be discriminated against relative to the U.K, to France, to Italy. That's our position."
CAPTION: Minister of Finance and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland speaks to reporters after a meeting of the Cabinet Committee on Canada-U.S relations following the election of President-elect Donald Trump on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Nov. 8, 2024.Freeland isn't flinching now that the U.S. can move forward with a trade challenge against the Liberal government's digital services tax.

Subject: Taxes; Trade agreements; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Trade agreements Tariffs

Location: Italy; Mexico; United States--US; Canada; France

People: Trump, Donald J; Freeland, Chrystia

Title: Justin Trudeau's digital tax is hated by America. Here's why Donald Trump may use it against us

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 20, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 130 of 500

America refuses to elect a woman. Canada has no reason to gloat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/america-refuses-elect-woman-canada-has-no-reason/docview/3130581224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=America+refuses+to+elect+a+woman.+Canada+has+no+reason+to+gloat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After the U.S. election it's clear that women need no longer apply to be president. Two well-qualified female candidates, Hillary Clinton and Kamala Harris, ran against the worst man ever. They both lost.
The Democrats didn't see it coming this time. But the most unmanly of men gathered his bros and ran a hyper-masculine campaign designed to pull in the male vote. Young men, old men and plenty of white women voted for Donald Trump. Evangelicals and those who don't give a damn about religion voted for him too. Latino and Black men increased their share of his vote. In total about half of U.S. voters decided that a convicted felon, sexual assaulter, an insurrectionist, and a liar was a better choice than either of those two women.
Since what happens in U.S. politics soon arrives in Canada, what does this mean for our next election? What does it mean for women in politics? Will we backslide just like the U.S.?
Not that we have made great gains. Kim Campbell was our one and only one female prime minister and she was in office for only six months, when she was elected leader of the Progressive Conservatives in 1993. The PCs at the time were barely breathing - always a good time to elect a woman leader. She went down to ignominious defeat - only two PCs were elected in total - at the hands of Jean Chrétien and resigned shortly after.
We've done somewhat better with female premiers but not much. Since 1992 there have been 11 female premiers. Rita Johnson of British Columbia was the first, who managed to stay in office for six months before being turfed by Gordon Campbell. Christy Clark, another west coaster, held on for the longest. She was in office for six years and then resigned in 2017 shortly after an election due to a vote of non-confidence. Kathleen Wynne of Ontario held office for five years and then went down to defeat in 2018 to Doug Ford in the biggest loss for the Liberals since Confederation.
There were also four women premiers of the territories, among them Nellie Cournoyer of the Northwest Territories. Three of the female premiers are from Alberta.
Currently there are two female premiers: Danielle Smith in Alberta and the recently elected Susan Holt in New Brunswick.
But so far none of these premiers have been in office for more than one term. Their predecessors were all defeated by male candidates, most of whom stayed in office much longer.
Unless Justin Trudeau has a change of heart it will be an all-male slate for the next federal election, too - except for Elizabeth May of the Green Party who must be getting tired of running by now.
It would seem that here in Canada we have gotten used to things the way they are. Men run politics and women stay in the background. Trudeau has certainly elevated many women to cabinet posts and important civil service and staff positions. But he can't seem to get out of the way so one of them could run for the leadership of the Liberals.
It is said that women don't necessarily vote for other women; they are more driven by policy or something more intangible such as values or appeal. In the case of Trump it seems most Americans were swayed by his ugly words about immigrants and illegal migrants coming across the southern border.
Given our current situation - three male federal political leaders - it looks like it will be a long time before we have a female prime minister. On that front, Canada is significantly behind its peers. England has already had three. So has Finland. Germany had the second longest-serving chancellor in German history in Angela Merkel.
Meanwhile, the United States has had zero women presidents, Canada is barely better. It speaks volumes. America should feel ashamed for electing Donald Trump, but Canada has more than enough reason for its own shame.
Gillian Stewardis a Calgary-based writer and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. Follow her on
Bluesky:gillian2.blsk.social
CREDIT: GILLIAN STEWARD OPINION Gillian Stewardis a Calgary-based writer and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. Follow her on Bluesky:gillian2.blsk.social;

People: Trump, Donald J

CREDIT: Gillian Steward, Contributor

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Females; Women; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: America refuses to elect a woman. Canada has no reason to gloat

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 20, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors
