Document 132 of 500

Trump may use digital tax against Ottawa: Trudeau government will have to fend off a resistant U.S. before it can collect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-may-use-digital-tax-against-ottawa/docview/3130580578/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+may+use+digital+tax+against+Ottawa%3A+Trudeau+government+will+have+to+fend+off+a+resistant+U.S.+before+it+can+collect&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's set to bring in billions of dollars the federal government is keen to have. But Ottawa must first fend off a resistant United States before it can collect.
The Trudeau government's digital services tax - to be paid by the world's digital giants operating in Canada - kicks in this year. However, a trade challenge from the U.S. and the re-election of Donald Trump has business groups and even Premier Doug Ford calling on Ottawa to back down out of fear of retaliation.
"We cannot put millions of Canadian jobs and our historic economic partnership with the U.S. at risk because of a stubborn refusal to listen to the concerns of our American friends," Ford said last month.
Now, the stakes could rise with the return of a Trump administration threatening tough tariffs with trade deal negotiations on the horizon.
Here's what you need to know about the digital services tax.
What's the digital services tax?
For several years, there have been global negotiations on how to tax the revenues from economic activitygenerated online, by large companies operating in other countries.
Supporters in Canada have argued the tax is necessary because global digital giants aren't paying their fair share of taxes. The Liberals first promised a tax in 2019. But Canada has always said its priority is a global agreement.
Those negotiations, however, have stalled.
Impatient with that process after several delays, Canada and other countries decided to impose their own taxes until there is progress on the global efforts. The U.K., France, Italy and India are among countries which have already put a digital services tax in place.Others have agreed to hold off until at least 2025.
Foreign digital giants that make more than $20 million in Canada and more than 750 million euros globally in annual revenues will now have to pay a three per cent tax on revenues earned within Canada.
It is expected to generate $900 million per year, but Ottawa will get $2.3 billion in its first year because it would apply retroactively to revenues generated since 2022.
Why is it a sensitive issue for the U.S.?
The U.S. has argued that unilateral digital service taxes "discriminate against U.S. companies," because it's mostly American companies that meet the revenue threshold.
The previous Trump administration launched investigations against countries which made the move, and even imposed retaliatory measures in some cases. But President Joe Biden's White House was more open to putting retaliatory measures on hold in an effort to reach a global deal.
When Canada announced its own tax would proceed this summer, arguing it was unfair for Canada to wait as negotiations stalled, the U.S.challenged it under the two countries' trade agreement, launching a period of dispute settlement consultations- a less heavy-handed approach in which the two countries hold official conversations in hopes of resolving the issue.
The deadline for those consultations quietly passed last week without a resolution- Canada remains committed to the tax in the absence of an international agreement- giving the U.S. the option to proceed further by requesting a dispute settlement panel.
But given the Biden administration's lame-duck position, some observersand agovernment official who spoke to the Star suggested it could be left to the incoming Trump administration to deal with Canada's tax. Even if the Biden administration proceeds with its complaint, that process is unlikely to be complete before Trump takes power in January- and the U.S.' argument is not "their strongest against Canada," said William Pellerin, an international trade lawyer with McMillan LLP.
What's at stake for Canadians?
Since the Canadian government announced the tax, fears of devastating retaliatory measures has had business groups here calling for the Trudeau government to back off.
And those warnings are taking on new urgency as Canada prepares for a Trump administrationthat has resorted to strong-armed political responses in the past.
Canada will have to renegotiate the U.S. and Mexico agreement with a Trump administration that has slapped tariffs on Canadian goods before and promised during the last election to implement a 10 per cent tariff on all goods coming into America.
"Trump is ready to use what we would call in the trade world the 'nuclear option,'" Pellerin said.
Jessica Brandon-Jepp, the senior director of fiscal and financial services policy at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, told the Star she worries Canada's new tax could threaten the nearly $3.6 billion (2023 figures) of goods and services that cross the border each day.
"This particular tax is not going to bring in nearly as much as the damage that it has the potential to do for the Canadian economy and our trading relationship," she said in an interview.
Pellerin said the federal government could be open to negotiating the terms of its tax during trade talks with the U.S., but the large revenues may be too difficult to give up.
The Trump transition team did not return a request for comment about how it plans to handle this issue.
Asked several times in the last few weeks about how she will deal with a Trump administration on the issue, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland would only point to the countries with their own digital services taxes, including France and Italy which have not faced retaliatory measures from the U.S. On Tuesday, she suggested it would be unfair to Canada if we faced retaliatory measures for doing the same as other countries.
"We simply believe that Canada should be on a level playing field, on an even footing, with our G7 partners," Freeland told reporters in Ottawa.
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia has said that countries like France and Italy have their own digital services taxes and have not faced retaliatory measures from the U.S.Spencer Colby The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Stop using city streets as a construction yard and save $48 million and the bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stop-using-city-streets-as-construction-yard-save/docview/3130579475/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stop+using+city+streets+as+a+construction+yard+and+save+%2448+million+and+the+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford government disputes Toronto's $48-million bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double, Nov. 18
I have a simple suggestion for Premier Doug Ford. Instead of wasting $48 million tax dollars to remove the bike lanes that provide increased safety and convenience to a significant - and increasing - percentage of Toronto taxpayers, maybe you should focus on a different cause of congestion. Perhaps you could pass a different piece of legislation: one that would force your developer buddies to stop using city streets as a convenient and low-cost "yard" for their construction activities. I'm pretty sure that those lane takeovers do more to slow traffic and cause congestion than do well-designed bike lanes. Even better, ending the practice wouldn't cost the province $48 million.
Neil R. Thomlinson, Toronto
Make traffic flow faster to ease congestion
A flow in any system is only determined by its choke points. These are the intersections. You can rip up bicycle lanes until the cows come home, all you get is more space for idling cars. Downtown, every traffic light starts with cars already blocking the intersection. With luck, one or two may get though on each full cycle. Effective street speed? Near none. So, focus on speeding up all intersections. We have a good size fine to wield. Now, make the drivers pay, and Toronto will soon be like Stockholm or Berlin, where drivers stop before the pedestrian crossing stripes all the time. We all win. Traffic flow will increase in orders of magnitude, less frustration, more money for the city coffers, and pedestrians can cross without snaking around dirty cars. No Bike paths gone and happiness for all.
Bengt, Lindvall, Toronto
All Ontarians should worry about what Ford will do next
If Doug Ford can override Toronto's municipal government and tear up bike lanes, he can do that anywhere. What will he do next? Maybe he'll turn a park in North Bay into a parking lot. Or turn a community centre in Sudbury into a casino. Or remove dedicated bus lanes in Ottawa or Mississauga in the name of "reducing congestion." Ontarians all across the province should be worried about the precedent Bill 212 could set.
Richard Spiegel, Toronto
Councillor Holyday is part of the problem
Province-led survey suggests higher cycling rates in Toronto than Ford government has cited: city staff report, Nov. 14
City Councillor Stephen Holyday suggests that the lack of local newspapers covering things like development proposals and bike lanes is a cause of citizens becoming detached from their local government. If Holyday is concerned about this detachment, you would think that in an effort to engage constituents, his website would be chock full of information, that his office would be sending robust e-newsletters on a monthly basis, that he would find a way to reach out to seniors, hold coffee with your councillor mornings. But he does none of the above and some residents subscribe to Councillor Amber Morley's newsletter to learn more about Etobicoke. I suggest that Holyday is part of the problem.
Janet Griffiths-Maxymiw, Toronto
Ford is wasting far more taxpayer money than the school boardsFord's education minister directly warns school boards against 'unnecessary or extravagant expenses, Nov. 14
The Ford government is warning the school boards to rein in spending. What hypocrisy! This from our government that has wasted more taxpayer money than any governing body. Here's a list and I'm sure there is more that I've missed.
$4.3 million in legal fees to defend their wage cap law which they lost$30 million to fight the federal carbon fee $231 million to cancel the green renewable contracts as soon as they took office$30 million for gas pump stickers$225 million to cancel the contract with the Beer Store, a year before it was to expire which would have cost nothing.$800 million to build a parking lot for a spa at Ontario Place no one wantsProposed $4 billion (but more likely 10 billion) to build a highway we don't need
This is not a government that thinks or acts fiscally responsible. Maybe start by cleaning up their own messes. Just think of what all this wasted money could do if it was invested in health care and education which right now are both in shambles.
Sonia Kurmey, Etobicoke
Retinal scans are another unnecessary procedure for healthy patientsI paid $1,500 for an MRI and got an appointment in days. But such scans aren't always good for patients - or our health care system, Nov. 16
I am 69 and wanted to test my eyesight. I went to an optometrist clinic that was new to me and they said, unsurprisingly, they would have to do a full work up on me. I asked what that entailed and they listed a few things. At the end I was informed "they required" that I have a retinal scan - $160. I had never heard of that before so I asked if it was a necessary test. The receptionist answered that they required it. Yes, but is it necessary? I asked. She asked me to discuss it with the doctor. While I was waiting for the doctor, I went to Dr. Google and it explained the test and said it was "nice to do but not necessary" for generally healthy patients. I didn't have the $160, so I told the doctor I wasn't going to have that procedure done. She was quite perturbed with me and long story short, I left with my prescription and the admonishment that the next time I came, I would be required to do the scan. I won't be going back.
Bruce Van Dieten, Toronto
Car theft is a serious crime. Let's treat it that way
Video captures moment of impact as stolen BMW hits TTC bus, injuring five people, Nov. 18
Yet again we see on the news that a stolen car has crashed into a bus injuring several people. When are the courts going to get serious about this pervasive crime wave? These guys were out on bail (again) and because there are no serious penalties or accountability for their crimes, they get out on bail and start all over again. The courts should keep these perpetrators locked up so that they cannot harm anyone while waiting for trial. Car theft is a very serious crime, let's treat it as such.
Jane White, Scarborough
The Swifties are celebrating. Glue and sparkles to the critics
Taylor Swift charade shows how much our society values individual gain and not the collective good, Nov. 16
If one more person tells me how wrong it is to spend so much money to see Taylor Swift, I will spray glue and throw sparkles at them. The Swifties are celebrating a young woman who has worked very hard and is making her mark in our screwed-up world. They are enjoying her success and her music. It is a happy time for a lot of people who do not experience that many. November in Toronto has never looked so good. Go Taylor and your Swifties.
Rochelle Hatton, Sudbury

Document 134 of 500

The GTA is making key reductions in emissions, but a provincial policy is cancelling them out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gta-is-making-key-reductions-emissions-provincial/docview/3130579436/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+GTA+is+making+key+reductions+in+emissions%2C+but+a+provincial+policy+is+cancelling+them+out&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Millions of people across Toronto and this region made changes that reduced their personal carbon emissions last year, but those efforts were cancelled out by the provincial government's policy of ramping up natural gas-fired electricity generation, according to the most recent accounting of emissions.
A report that found emissions rose by two per cent in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area points out that reductions in emissions from heating and EV adoption were nullified by the increased reliance on gas plants.
"Electricity emissions increased 30 per cent in 2023 for the GTHA region, and there was no increase in electricity consumption at all. So that whole 30 per cent increase in carbon pollution was due to more intensive use of gas plants to provide the same amount of electricity," said Bryan Purcell, vice-president of policy and programs at The Atmospheric Fund, a non-profit climate agency set up by the three levels of government, which compiled the data.
Emissions from generating electricity went up by 885,000 tonnes, nearly all of the one-million-tonne increase between 2022 and 2023, according to the 2023 edition of the report, put out annually.
Even amid population growth and post-pandemic bounce back, "If electricity emissions had not increased this year, emissions would have been nearly flat for the GTA," Purcell said.
Grid emissions have more than doubled over the past four years, according to data from the Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO), which projects they will double again over the next three years and stay at that increased level for the next decade. The province has been using gas plants to make up for electricity shortfalls while nuclear reactors are offline for refurbishment.
Ontario's electricity was 96 per cent non-emitting as recently as 2017. Now, due to increased use of gas plants, it is only 87 per cent carbon-free.
As a result, emissions in the region have been rising every year since the pandemic. They need to fall by an average of 11 per cent per year to reach the 2030 target set by cities across the region.
While the province announced a major renewable energy procurement last year, new Energy Minister Stephen Lecce has opened up the door for new natural gas plants to bid for the contracts. All this could significantly erode Ontario's supply of clean energy and undermine emission reductions made across the economy.
"It is clear the government has no interest in real climate action," said NDP climate and energy critic Peter Tabuns. "People are doing their share but the premier isn't. In fact, he's wiping out their efforts."
Joseph Colella, a spokesperson for Lecce, said in a statement: "Ontario continues to have one of the cleanest electricity systems in the world with a plan to build new clean generation, including nuclear and hydro, to further reduce emissions from the sector. Our clean grid is also powering other bold investments in electric vehicle charging, clean steel production and gridlock-reducing transit, which is driving unprecedented emissions reductions and putting the province on track to meet our 2030 emission targets - unlike the federal government."
Most of the province's emissions reductions came from the closure of coal plants, and were done before Premier Doug Ford assumed office.
Bright spots in the report
The bright points in the 2023 emissions data came in the two biggest emitting sectors: buildings and transportation.
For buildings, where the vast majority of the emissions are produced by heating, there was a small reduction in emissions - about 100,000 tonnes, or 0.6 per cent - from 2022.
This occurred despite a three per cent growth in population of the region, which means tens of thousands of new homes to heat.
"It is really remarkable that we didn't see building sector emissions go up. Even with the big increase from electricity factored in, there was enough of a decrease in the direct use of natural gas in buildings. So that's fantastic and a good sign of progress," Purcell said.
Much of this can be chalked up to a mild winter, but the green building standards that have been adopted by municipalities across the region are starting to have an impact, as new houses are more energy efficient, and produce fewer emissions, than the existing housing stock.
There was also significant energy efficiency work in existing homes, as evidenced by the wild popularity of the Greener Homes Program, which provided rebates to homeowners for heat pumps and green renovations. The program was abruptly ended early this spring, after it burned through its budget two years ahead of schedule.
The report noted that industrial and commercial buildings also recorded a significant reduction in emissions per square foot, even with the weather factored in, meaning businesses are making upgrades to their spaces that are starting to achieve results.
In transportation, emissions rose by 4.3 per cent, or almost one million tonnes, because of increased road use, which continues a bounce back from the pandemic lows. This was tempered, however, by a marked increase in the adoption of electric vehicles. A record 72,563 EVs were registered in the GTHA in 2023, a 50 per cent increase over the previous year. Transit ridership was up 28 per cent, though it was still 20 per cent below pre-pandemic levels.
'A strong appetite for climate action'
"The data shows there is a strong appetite for climate action among households and businesses with investments being made," said Purcell. "More support from policymakers is really needed to enable and accelerate that."
He emphasized that even with the grid getting dirtier, electric vehicles, heat pumps and other kinds of electrification still have significant emission reductions and shouldn't be abandoned.
Every individual action builds the case for the government to do its part, he said.
While Ford came to office openly hostile to renewable energy and EVs, that's changed in recent years, with the government promoting EV manufacturing for economic revival and renewable energy as a way to attract business investment.
But the province's electrical grid remains a point of concern for climate advocates, as no new renewables have been brought online in the six and a half years since Ford's first election. With electricity demand soaring thanks to climate action, ensuring new generation is non-emitting will be essential, they say.
While natural gas has long been touted as the least-polluting fossil fuel, and an improvement over coal, recent research has cast this into doubt.
"I don't think it's accurate to call gas a cleaner or transition fuel anymore," Purcell said. "When you account for the upstream emissions from natural gas production, all the leakage that is occurring through the mining of gas ... as well as transmission and distribution system leaks, a lot of studies are saying that natural gas is similar to coal from a carbon perspective."
How-Sen Chong, the climate campaigner at Toronto Environmental Alliance, said there are a lot of positives to be found in the TAF emissions data, if you dig beneath the top-line results.
"The results are kind of what you'd expect when you start bending the curve," he said. "I can't wait to see next year's numbers to see if these trends accelerate."
CAPTION: Wisps of greenish gasses are seen during the noon hour coming out of the large smoke stacks of the Portland's gas generating station in the Leslie Street and Unwin Avenue area.

Subject: Energy; Electricity; Emission standards; Climate action; Carbon; Electric vehicles; Pandemics; Coal; Renewable resources; Steel production; Natural gas

Document 135 of 500

Premier slams $48M estimate: Ford calls city's tally to remove 22 kilometres of infrastructure 'hogwash'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-slams-48m-estimate/docview/3130579434/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+slams+%2448M+estimate%3A+Ford+calls+city%27s+tally+to+remove+22+kilometres+of+infrastructure+%27hogwash%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Hogwash."
That's Premier Doug Ford's blunt assessment of city bureaucrats' $48-million estimate for removing bike lanes that cost $27 million to install on Yonge Street, Bloor Streetand University Avenue.
Asked by reporters Tuesday for his thoughts on the City of Toronto's tally to dismantle up to 22 kilometres of cycling infrastructure, Ford did not mince words.
"You know it doesn't cost $50 million. That's a bunch of hogwash," the premier said.
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," he said, arguing the lanes contribute to traffic congestion.
Ford's comments came the morning after a legislative committee studying his controversial plan to scrap three bike lanes heard repeated pleas for them to be retained.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, told the all-party panel Monday the scheme would mean "multiple years of successive construction that would disrupt other commuters, harm businesses and incur costs for years."
"While the intent of removing these lanes may be to improve traffic, the construction process to dismantle these lanes would mean considerable delays and disruption for drivers," she warned.
Hayward estimated it would cost $20 million to remove the lanes along 3.5 kilometres of University Avenue and Queen's Park Crescent as well as part ofBloor Streetdowntown.
The remaining $28 million would be for removing curbs, lane markings and signs and repaving the other 18.5 kilometres of lanes on Bloor Street West and Yonge Street.
But Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria noted the city spent $300,000 to remove about three kilometres of bike lanes on Jarvis Street in 2011 and $80,000 to dismantle them on a four-kilometre stretch of Brimley Road in 2020.
The Jarvis lanes cost $86,000 to build, while those on Brimley were $160,000 to install.
Sarkaria, who has promised Queen's Park would foot the tab for taking out bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University, acknowledged Monday the province has not completed any costing for the removal.
With a potential spring election in the offing, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have pounced on bike lanes as a potential wedge issue against the opposition New Democrats and Liberals that could help them hold some of their dozen Toronto seats.
The Tories' internal polling apparently reflects publicly available research -included a recent Abacus Data survey for the Star- that shows support for the plan.
Abacus found 55 per cent approved of Ford's edict that municipalities must get provincial approval for bike lanes that would interfere with vehicular traffic. That's about twice the 28 per cent that disagreed with the scheme with 17 per cent unsure.
The company polled 998 Ontarians from Oct. 31 through Nov. 5 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
But at Monday's legislative hearings, there was overwhelming support for keeping the lanes intact.
Presenters to the committee repeatedly urged Tory MPPs to rethink their plans.
Association of Ontario Municipalities executive director Brian Rosborough, expressing concern about Queen's Park infringing upon city councils' turf, said gridlock in Toronto is "complex" and can't be solved by getting rid of a few bike lanes.
Scott Butler, executive director of Good Roads, the 130-year-old municipal association that focused on the quality and design of Ontario roads, likened bike lanes to the forward-looking policy-making that led to a provincial building code and other universally accepted safety standards.
Butler pointed out some European cities were once as focused on cars and trucks as Toronto, but have since redesigned their streets to accommodate safe cycling, which has led to cleaner, greener communities.
CAPTION: Cyclists gathered this month at Christie Pits to protest the province's plan to remove up to 22 kilometres of city bike lanes. A city transportation official told a legislative panel this week that the removal would mean years of construction and traffic delays.Michelle Mengsu ChangToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 136 of 500

Gas-powered plants drive up emissions: Rising pollution from electricity sector cancels out other reductions, report finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gas-powered-plants-drive-up-emissions/docview/3130579413/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gas-powered+plants+drive+up+emissions%3A+Rising+pollution+from+electricity+sector+cancels+out+other+reductions%2C+report+finds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Millions of people across Toronto and this region made changes that reduced their personal carbon emissions last year, but those efforts were cancelled out by the provincial government's policy of ramping up natural-gas-fired electricity generation, according to the most recent accounting of emissions.
A report that found emissions rose by two per cent in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area points out that reductions in emissions from heating and electric vehicle adoption were nullified by the increased reliance on gas plants.
"Electricity emissions increased 30 per cent in 2023 for the GTHA region, and there was no increase in electricity consumption at all. So that whole 30 per cent increase in carbon pollution was due to more intensive use of gas plants to provide the same amount of electricity," said Bryan Purcell, vice-president of policy and programs at the Atmospheric Fund, a non-profit climate agency set up by the three levels of government, which compiled the data.
Emissions from generating electricity went up by 885,000 tonnes, nearly all of the one-million-tonne increase between 2022 and 2023, according to the 2023 edition of the report, published annually.
Even amid population growth and a post-pandemic bounce-back, "if electricity emissions had not increased this year, emissions would have been nearly flat for the GTA," Purcell said.
Grid emissions have more than doubled over the past four years, according to data from the Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO), which projects that they will double again over the next three years and stay at that increased level for the next decade. The province has been using gas plants to make up for electricity shortfalls while nuclear reactors are offline for refurbishment.
Ontario's electricity was 96 per cent non-emitting as recently as 2017. Now, due to increased use of gas plants, it is only 87 per cent carbon-free.
As a result, emissions in the region have been rising every year since the pandemic. They need to fall by an average of 11 per cent per year to reach the 2030 target set by cities across the region.
While the province announced a major renewable energy procurement last year, new Energy Minister Stephen Lecce has opened up the door for new natural gas plants to bid for the contracts. All this could significantly erode Ontario's supply of clean energy and undermine emission reductions made across the economy.
"It is clear the government has no interest in real climate action," said NDP climate and energy critic Peter Tabuns. "People are doing their share but the premier isn't. In fact, he's wiping out their efforts."
Joseph Colella, a spokesperson for Lecce, said in a statement: "Ontario continues to have one of the cleanest electricity systems in the world with a plan to build new clean generation, including nuclear and hydro, to further reduce emissions from the sector. Our clean grid is also powering other bold investments in electric vehicle charging, clean steel production and gridlock-reducing transit, which is driving unprecedented emissions reductions and putting the province on track to meet our 2030 emission targets - unlike the federal government."
Most of the province's emissions reductions came from the closure of coal plants, and were done before Premier Doug Ford assumed office in 2018.
The bright points in the 2023 emissions data came in the two biggest emitting sectors: buildings and transportation.
For buildings, where the vast majority of the emissions are produced by heating, there was a small reduction in emissions - about 100,000 tonnes, or 0.6 per cent - from 2022.
This occurred despite a three per cent growth in population in the region, which means tens of thousands of new homes to heat.
Much of this can be chalked up to a mild winter, but the green building standards that have been adopted by municipalities across the region are starting to have an impact, as new houses are more energy-efficient, and produce fewer emissions, than the existing housing stock.
There was also significant energy efficiency work in existing homes, as evidenced by the wild popularity of the Greener Homes Program, which provided rebates to homeowners for heat pumps and green renovations. The program was abruptly ended early this spring, after it burned through its budget two years ahead of schedule.
The report noted that industrial and commercial buildings also recorded a significant reduction in emissions per square foot, even with the weather factored in, meaning businesses are making upgrades to their spaces that are starting to achieve results.
In transportation, emissions rose by 4.3 per cent, or almost one million tonnes, because of increased road use, which continues a bounce-back from the pandemic lows. This was tempered, however, by a marked increase in the adoption of electric vehicles. A record 72,563 EVs were registered in the GTHA in 2023, a 50 per cent increase over the previous year. Transit ridership was up 28 per cent, though it was still 20 per cent below pre-pandemic levels.
"The data shows there is a strong appetite for climate action among households and businesses with investments being made," said Purcell. "More support from policy-makers is really needed to enable and accelerate that."
While Ford came to office openly hostile to renewable energy and EVs, that's changed in recent years, with the government promoting EV manufacturing for economic revival and renewable energy as a way to attract business investment.
But the province's electrical grid remains a point of concern for climate advocates, as no new renewables have been brought online in the six-and-a-half years since Ford's first election. With electricity demand soaring thanks to climate action, ensuring new generation is non-emitting will be essential, they say.
While natural gas has long been touted as the least polluting fossil fuel, and an improvement over coal,recent researchhas cast this into doubt.
"I don't think it's accurate to call gas a cleaner or transition fuel anymore," Purcell said.
"When you account for the upstream emissions from natural gas production, all the leakage that is occurring through the mining of gas ... as well as transmission and distribution system leaks, a lot of studies are saying that natural gas is similar to coal from a carbon perspective."
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Green buildings; Electric vehicles; Renewable resources; Energy efficiency; Emission standards; Climate action; Climate change; Coal; Natural gas; Emissions control; Electricity generation; Alternative energy sources; Energy policy

Document 137 of 500

Doug Ford says $48M estimate to remove Toronto bike lanes is 'hogwash'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-48m-estimate-remove-toronto-bike/docview/3130932071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+%2448M+estimate+to+remove+Toronto+bike+lanes+is+%27hogwash%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Hogwash."
That's Premier Doug Ford's blunt assessment of city bureaucrats' $48-million estimate for removing bike lanes that cost $27 million to install on Yonge Street, Bloor Street and University Avenue.
Asked by reporters Tuesday for his thoughts on the City of Toronto's tally to dismantle up to 22 kilometres of cycling infrastructure, Ford did not mince words.
"You know it doesn't cost $50 million. That's a bunch of hogwash," the premier said.
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," he said, arguing the lanes contribute to traffic congestion.
Ford's comments came the morning after a legislative committee studying his controversial plan to scrap three bike lanes heard repeated pleas for them to be retained.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, told the all-party panel Monday the scheme would mean "multiple years of successive construction that would disrupt other commuters, harm businesses and incur costs for years."
"While the intent of removing these lanes may be to improve traffic, the construction process to dismantle these lanes would mean considerable delays and disruption for drivers," she warned.
Hayward estimated it would cost $20 million to remove the lanes along 3.5 kilometres of University Avenue and Queen's Park Crescent as well as part of Bloor Street downtown.
The remaining $28 million would be for removing curbs, lane markings and signs and repaving the other 18.5 kilometres of lanes on Bloor Street West and Yonge Street.
But Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria noted the city spent $300,000 to remove about three kilometres of bike lanes on Jarvis Street in 2011 and $80,000 to dismantle them on a four-kilometre stretch of Brimley Road in 2020.
The Jarvis lanes cost $86,000 to build, while those on Brimley were $160,000 to install.
Sarkaria, who has promised Queen's Park would foot the tab for taking out bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University, acknowledged Monday the province has not completed any costing for the removal.
With a potential spring election in the offing, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have pounced on bike lanes as a potential wedge issue against the opposition New Democrats and Liberals that could help them hold some of their dozen Toronto seats.
The Tories' internal polling apparently reflects publicly available research - included a recent Abacus Data survey for the Star - that shows support for the plan.
Abacus found 55 per cent approved of Ford's edict that municipalities must get provincial approval for bike lanes that would interfere with vehicular traffic. That's about twice the 28 per cent that disagreed with the scheme with 17 per cent unsure.
The company polled 998 Ontarians from Oct. 31 through Nov. 5 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
But at Monday's legislative hearings, there was overwhelming support for keeping the lanes intact.
Presenters to the committee repeatedly urged Tory MPPs to rethink their plans.
Association of Ontario Municipalities executive director Brian Rosborough, expressing concern about Queen's Park infringing upon city councils' turf, said gridlock in Toronto is "complex" and can't be solved by getting rid of a few bike lanes.
Scott Butler, executive director of Good Roads, the 130-year-old municipal association that focused on the quality and design of Ontario roads, likened bike lanes to the forward-looking policy-making that led to a provincial building code and other universally accepted safety standards.
Butler pointed out some European cities were once as focused on cars and trucks as Toronto, but have since redesigned their streets to accommodate safe cycling, which has led to cleaner, greener communities.
CAPTION: Hundreds of cyclists gather on Saturday afternoon at Christie Pits on Nov. 9, 2024 to protest the Ford government's move to remove bikes lanes in Toronto.

Subject: Traffic

Poilievre can't say anything positive about Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievre-cant-say-anything-positive-about-canada/docview/3129924070/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+can%27t+say+anything+positive+about+Canada&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is the difference between the political moment that swept Justin Trudeau into leadership and the one that will push him out, Nov. 17
Jamie Watt is correct that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will continue his efforts to bash the Trudeau government. He is releasing a new attack video every few days. Has anyone actually sat and listened to his videos one after the other? I just tried it on the weekend. The Trudeau attacks are there. The misinformation is there. But there is also something lurking in the background of his constant "everything is broken" refrain. Poilievre can't seem to say anything positive about Canada. Does he even like Canada and Canadians? He talks glowingly about the U.S. Why not just move there and quit trying to remake Canada into the 51st state?
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Promises made is accurate. Promises kept, not so much
Jaime Watt noted in his column that former Ontario Premier Mike Harris used the phrase "promises made, promises kept." Our current Premier Doug Ford also refers to "promises made, promises kept" but he understandably doesn't want to refer to those promises that he has failed to keep to the detriment of Ontario residents. He promised years ago to end hallway medicine. As someone who has had a loved one remain on a stretcher in the emergency department for several days waiting to be admitted, along with dozens of others, I know that this promise was not kept. Another promise was related to improved access to housing. Minister Stan Cho delayed an assisted housing project on Cummer Avenue for years. This delay cost the city of Toronto millions of dollars. The only accurate part of "promises made, promises kept" is the promises made.
David Ash, North York
Loss of the CBC - our national voice - would be a huge blow to cultural identity
The columnist mentions that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre wants to "shutter the CBC." This would be a violation of freedom of speech It would be his attempt to control the newsfeed for the country. The loss of our national voice would be a huge blow to cultural identity and to reporting of events in our country. We are already bombarded with American news. Let's preserve our important national treasure, the CBC.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Women are leading the way in the Catholic Church
For the Catholic Church equality for women is a bridge too far, Nov. 15
It's unfortunate that the recent opinion piece from Janice Kennedy is so far out of touch with Roman Catholicism and women. Had she attended a recent evening sponsored by the Archdiocese of Toronto under the direction of Archbishop Francis Leo (soon to be elevated to Cardinal) and named "Unpacking of the Synod on Synodality" (the Rome event referenced in Kennedy's article), she might have learned that nuns are doing more than the dishes these days. The moderator was a Catholic lay woman and mother, Dr. Josephine Lombardi, Assistant Dean for Academic Affairs at St. Augustine's Seminary. The panel consisted of three well-informed Catholic women including two nuns. One of these Sisters, Natalie Bequard, Xavieres Missionaries of Christ Jesus, from France, had been conferred an honorary doctorate earlier in the week at St. Michael's College (U of T) and was undersecretary of the Synod of Bishops at the Vatican. Going back in church history, several women have been named doctors of the Church including the brilliant Jewish convert to Catholicism, Edith Stein, who ultimately died at Auschwitz.
Anne MacCarthy, Toronto
TBA Trailblazers have created something worthy In Toronto, a Biennial that's looking for its agenda, Nov. 16
Tatum Dooley vehemently compares the fledgling Toronto Biennial of Art (TBA, established 2019) to the long-established Venice Biennale (La Biennale di Venezia, established 1895) and the Whitney Biennial funded by the fortunes of American robber barons Vanderbilt and Whitney . It would be easy to denigrate Dooley's opinion as another self-loathing example of "it's Toronto, it can't be any good," or is it the grass is greener on the other side? But I would rather sing the praise of those TBA trailblazers who have the foresight, tenacity, dedication and elbow grease, who saw the potential and created something worthy for the rest of us to enjoy, to learn and to share. And did I mention they did it all on a limited budget? During these days of diminishing arts infrastructure experienced in Toronto, TBA precariously hangs onto the beachhead it created post-COVID and manages to bring us joy.
Andrew Lee, Toronto
For profit health firms are promising more than they can deliverI paid $1,500 for an MRI and got an appointment in days. But such scans aren't always good for patients - or our health care system, Nov. 16
Thanks to journalist Kenyon Wallace for personally researching this for-profit health-care service. The private firms are promising far more than they can deliver. The public is easily lured then disappointed. This article delivers important information we should all be aware of.
Joan Wood, Markham
Release the names of known war criminals, not the others Canada must confront its shameful history of harbouring Nazi war criminals, Nov. 18
One cannot but sympathize with the concerns of Jewish Canadians as expressed in this article. That monsters such as Helmut Rauca lived among us is terrible, and we cannot forget that we, along with other democratic nations, refused entry to Jewish refugees in the 1930s and beyond. There was a great outcry when the Canadian Parliament gave a standing ovation to Yaroslav Hunka, who, it was subsequently discovered, had been a member of the Nazi SS. But let's put this in perspective; in 1941, when Hunka was a teenager or at most a very young man, Ukraine had just lived through the Holodomor in which millions of people had died of starvation intentionally inflicted by the Soviets. Starvation is a terrible way to die. Is it a surprise that young Ukrainians would flock to the banner of an army attacking the Soviets? What would any of us have done in the same circumstances? If known war criminals are included in the list, release those names. Not the others. With those few exceptions, we can have no idea of the life experiences of others identified in the Deschenes Commission report. It would be a grave injustice against people dead or in their late dotage who were swept up in circumstances beyond their control, and to their descendants.
Barrie Reynolds, Toronto
EV rebates and tariffs
I can't afford to buy an EV car, but I could if China's EV cars were allowed into Canada without a 100 per cent duty imposed on them. This doesn't seem like a good way for Canada to fight climate change. At least the government of Canada offers a $5,000 rebate. Ontario Premier Doug Ford and his government offer nothing.
Herbert Hyman, Oakville

Document 139 of 500

Doug Ford and Justin Trudeau are talking to the wrong audience about Donald Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-justin-trudeau-are-talking-wrong/docview/3129923450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+and+Justin+Trudeau+are+talking+to+the+wrong+audience+about+Donald+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Donald Trump takes power - and overpowers Canada - Doug Ford has a rescue plan:
Re-elect me. The sooner the better, for better or for worse.
It's a variation on the survival plan hatched by Justin Trudeau:
Re-elect me, too. The later the better, for the alternative is far worse.
That puts the premier and the prime minister seemingly on the same wavelength, albeit separate timelines, in search of renewed mandates. We need stability, they say, to right the ship of state in turbulent Trumpian times.
No longer a tag team, they are riding decidedly different waves. Ford hopes to surf an Ontario wave toward re-election, if he can get out ahead of a Greenbelt police probe; Trudeau is trying to keep his head above water before a national electoral deluge overwhelms him.
Facing headwinds from the south, both politicians are promising to serve and protect. They speak of trade protectionism (via tariffs) and border protection (from immigrant incursions); they're also talking up defence spending - not so much to protect us from overseas invasions as from American reverberations over NATO.
But will either of these federal or provincial playbooks protect us from the president-elect's wrath? Or are they merely self-serving re-election strategies, based on outdated bilateral tactics with little traction in Trump's America?
Ford cheerfully threw Mexico under the bus when he accused it of unfair trade practices by Chinese-owned EV carmakers in that country. He suggested we forget Fortress North America in favour of "Buy Can-Am" - replacing the current trilateral trade pact once known as NAFTA with a bilateral free trade treaty:
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them."
He boasts of his past success in pushing Ottawa for retaliation after Trump imposed tariffs on Ontario steel, claiming credit for the U.S. ultimately backing down. And he peppers most speeches with references to the nine million American workers whose jobs depend on exports to Ontario.
The premier opined boldly on federal tax policy, suggesting that Canada should cancel the controversial digital sales tax to calm bilateral waters. Like other premiers, Ford has also admonished the federal government to control the flow of irregular or illegal migrants across the border into Canada.
Trudeau, too, has cast himself as well-placed to navigate the next Trump administration, based on his handling of the last one. He has reactivated a cabinet committee on bilateral relations, reprised Deputy PM Chrystia Freeland in a leadership role and reassured Canadians on border questions.
The problem with all of the above is that the premier and the PM remain largely invisible to an incoming U.S. president with his eye on the world. Ford runs a province, not a country, and unless he becomes a regular on Fox News, he won't get on Trump's radar.
Much of the lobbying and persuading from Ford and Trudeau is also premised on the old playbook that Canadians need only remind Americans of their economic interests at the local level and word will get through to Washington.
But this notion of triangulation from the state and local level to the White House has limits. For one thing, many Southern U.S. states look to Mexico for their economic interests more than any Canadian connection.
Given those political realities, much of the posturing from the premier and prime minister seems intended as much for their domestic constituencies as any American audiences. That's especially true of the rarefied immigration rhetoric in Canada today.
Much of the alarmism about migrants is inward-looking, rather than looking at it from the American point of view. With Trump threatening to deport one million illegal migrants a year, the premiers of Quebec, Ontario and Alberta are clamouring for protection against waves of people trying to sneak into Canada, while worrying whether the Trump administration will take them back (as required by our latest bilateral agreement).
Rather than focusing solely on the northward flow of migrants into Canada, however, the federal government should be thinking hard about how to stop the southward flow into America which is much higher. After all, that's what has grabbed the attention of the incoming U.S. border czar, Tom Homan, who happens to be from upstate New York, near the Ontario border.
He called the Canada-U.S. border an "extreme national security vulnerability," predicting "tough conversations" with Ottawa. "There has to be an understanding from Canada that they can't be a gateway to terrorists coming into the United States."
While Canadians obsess about the notorious Roxham Road path into Canada, it turns out that borders are in the eye of the beholder - and very much a two-way street.
Ford and Trudeau are right to turn their minds to Trump. But the test of their tactics isn't how persuasive-sounding they seem when speaking to Canadians, but how effective they are in winning over the Americans in difficult negotiations ahead.
So far, both are following their re-election playbooks. But both will be judged by Canadian voters on how they play their hands with the Americans, not how they sound on the domestic campaign trail.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queens Park as the Ontario legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Trade agreements; Prime ministers; Protectionism; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade agreements Protectionism Tariffs

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Doug Ford and Justin Trudeau are talking to the wrong audience about Donald Trump

Document 140 of 500

My family needed the shelter system when we arrived in Toronto. Today the need is greater than ever. Doug Ford needs to quit sweeping it under the rug

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/my-family-needed-shelter-system-when-we-arrived/docview/3129922817/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=My+family+needed+the+shelter+system+when+we+arrived+in+Toronto.+Today+the+need+is+greater+than+ever.+Doug+Ford+needs+to+quit+sweeping+it+under+the+rug&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1974, my family and I arrived in Canada. As refugees from Chile, our first home was a shelter near Union Station. This service, coupled with a supportive community, made it possible for my parents to eventually find an apartment and jobs. Without the shelter, our story would have had a different ending.
Today, Canada's housing crisis is the reason the need for shelter has grown exponentially. Canadians increasingly cite the rising cost of housing as the main economic barrier people face. And nowhere is this crisis more evident than in the record number of people who are unhoused across this country.
In Toronto alone, over 12,200 people are living in shelters, with countless more sleeping on friends' couches, living in overcrowded and often unsafe apartments, or trying to survive outdoors. One in five Toronto households is at high risk of homelessness - just one missed paycheck or family emergency away from losing shelter. The very fear of eviction puts families into panic.
While the provincial government has tools to fix this, Ontario Premier Doug Ford wants to distract from his own responsibility. He could answer municipalities' calls for more health care, housing that's affordable, and other services that are proven solutions. Instead, he's called on cities to ask him to override people's Charter rights through use of the notwithstanding clause in response to outdoor encampments.
Beyond eroding constitutional freedoms, this isn't actually a fix. I echo the words of Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, the Chair of the Ontario Big City Mayors group: if the Province uses the notwithstanding clause on encampments, but people have nowhere else to go, it's not a real solution. It's just pushing people around to try and hide the problem.
Premier Ford even suggested that people experiencing homelessness should "get off [their] ass and start working." This ignores the well-documented reality that many people who are unhoused do have employment. And for people receiving disability assistance, monthly benefits aren't enough to afford the average rent for a 1-bedroom apartment. Ford's punitive approach has laid the ground for uncaring and even dehumanizing responses by frustrated residents with respect to their unhoused neighbours.
There are no solutions to be found in a polarizing and politicized approach to a problem we can only solve together. Toronto City Council has recognized this. In 2017 it took the politics out of selecting sites for new shelters, by delegating approval to senior City staff; their location is selected and evaluated based on need, by impartial staff.
In Toronto, 230 people are turned away each night because our shelters are full and there are no beds left. At the same time, we've seen a growing number of people sleeping outdoors, in park encampments. Together this is irrefutable evidence we desperately need more indoor spaces in every corner of the city.
Toronto City Council adopted a 10-Year Shelter Infrastructure Plan to deliver new purpose-built shelters across our city that can be converted to permanent housing. Recently, though, incomplete information about a new shelter site was leaked, which has fueled local opposition even before the deal officially closed and community outreach could begin.
The arguments are the same ones we hear any time these services are proposed: we might need shelters, but they don't belong in my neighbourhood. Social housing is important, but not on my street. Support services help people, but I don't want them close to my family. This reality? It's not mine to fix.
Except it is because we're all essential to the solution. Be wary of politicians who claim that people just need to get off their butt and move along, instead of investing in real solutions. Our failure to help people who are struggling with homelessness is everyone's crisis. Let's collectively treat it that way and strengthen the ties that bind us together.
CAPTION: A homeless encampment at Clarence Square Park in Downtown Toronto, with the CN tower in the background.
CREDIT: Alejandro Bravo, Contributor

Subject: Homeless people

Location: Canada

Title: My family needed the shelter system when we arrived in Toronto. Today the need is greater than ever. Doug Ford needs to quit sweeping it under the rug

Province wants social media giants to protect students' mental health

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-wants-social-media-giants-protect/docview/3129922810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+wants+social+media+giants+to+protect+students%27+mental+health&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has met with representatives from YouTube and is hoping to soon speak to executives from other social media companies about what they are doing to help students' mental health and well-being.
"I talked to the organization about what they do to protect children and young people online," Dunlop said Monday, the first day of Bullying Awareness and Prevention Week, after announcing $4.6 million for resources and supports for kids.
Talks were held with YouTube on Oct. 23.
Dunlop said when the provincial government enacted its cellphone ban this fall, "we also blocked social media in the classrooms and schools to ensure that students were focused on the learning at hand ... so I want to meet with the companies to look at what they are doing to protect our students."
Across the province, adozen school boards and two private schools are suing social media giants TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram, alleging the popular platforms have harmed kids' mental health as well as their academic achievement. They are seeking a total of more than $8 billion.
YouTube is not named in any of the cases.
Premier Doug Ford has been critical of the court action. Last May, then-education minister Stephen Lecce said that "instead of talking about it and litigating about, we opt to act decisively with a comprehensive plan" to not only curb cellphone and social media use in schools but also to work with companies to bring about change.
Boards that have launched lawsuits include Toronto public, Toronto Catholic, Peel public, Dufferin-Peel Catholic, and York Catholic. Two private religious schools - Holy Name of Mary College School in Mississauga and Eitz Chaim day school in Toronto - have also signed on.
They have filed separate but similar cases in Ontario's Superior Court of Justice, alleging the companies' platforms have "rewired the way children think, behave and learn" that has cost them billions in supports for staff and students. They are also calling on the companies to make improvements to their platforms, say their statements of claim.
Their allegations have not been tested in court, and the social media companies have said they will defend themselves against the claims.
A spokesperson for Snapchat has previously told the Star that its platform "was intentionally designed to be different from traditional social media, with a focus on helping Snapchatters communicate with their close friends ... While we will always have more work to do, we feel good about the role Snapchat plays in helping close friends feel connected, happy and prepared as they face the many challenges of adolescence."
A Meta spokesperson, representing Facebook and Instagram, has said "we want to reassure every parent that we have their interests at heart in the work we're doing to provide teens with safe, supportive experiences" on both platforms.
The boards and two schools are not paying legal costs, but have instead agreed that the law firm Neinstein LLP will take a contingency fee from any court award.
Of the $4.6 million Dunlop announced for resources and support, almost $1.5 million will go to Big Brothers and Big Sisters for mentoring programs in elementary and secondary schools, while $800,000 is earmarked for Kids Help Phone for its around-the-clock counselling services. Another $436,500 will help Egale Canada support anti-cyberbullying efforts for LGTBQ students. About $150,000 will go to Victim Services Toronto to help youth with issues around online dating and healthy relationships.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 142 of 500

Ford, Trudeau working from old playbook

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-trudeau-working-old-playbook/docview/3129922798/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%2C+Trudeau+working+from+old+playbook&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Donald Trump takes power- and overpowers Canada- Doug Ford has a rescue plan:
Re-elect me. The sooner the better, for better or for worse.
It's a variation on the survival plan hatched by Justin Trudeau:
Re-elect me, too. The later the better, for the alternative is far worse.
That puts the premier and the prime minister seemingly on the same wavelength, albeit separate timelines, in search of renewed mandates. We need stability, they say, to right the ship of state in turbulent Trumpian times.
No longer a tag team, they are riding decidedly different waves. Ford hopes to surf an Ontario wave toward re-election, if he can get out ahead of a Greenbelt police probe; Trudeau is trying to keep his head above water before a national electoral deluge overwhelms him.
Facing headwinds from the south, both politicians are promising to serve and protect. They speak of trade protectionism (via tariffs) and border protection (from immigrant incursions); they're also talking up defence spending- not so much to protect us from overseas invasions as from American reverberations over NATO.
But will either of these federal or provincial playbooks protect us from the president-elect's wrath? Or are they merely self-serving re-election strategies, based on outdated bilateral tactics with little traction in Trump's America?
Ford cheerfully threw Mexico under the bus when he accused it of unfair trade practices by Chinese-owned EV carmakers in that country. He suggested we forget Fortress North America in favour of "Buy Can-Am"- replacing the current trilateral trade pact once known as NAFTA with a bilateral free trade treaty:
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them."
He boasts of his past success in pushing Ottawa for retaliation after Trump imposed tariffs on Ontario steel, claiming credit for the U.S. ultimately backing down. And he peppers most speeches with references to the nine million American workers whose jobs depend on exports to Ontario.
The premier opined boldly on federal tax policy, suggesting that Canada should cancel the controversial digital sales tax to calm bilateral waters. Like other premiers, Ford has also admonished the federal government to control the flow of irregular or illegal migrants across the border into Canada.
Trudeau, too, has cast himself as well-placed to navigate the next Trump administration, based on his handling of the last one. He has reactivated a cabinet committee on bilateral relations, reprised Deputy PM Chrystia Freeland in a leadership role and reassured Canadians on border questions.
The problem with all of the above is that the premier and the PM remain largely invisible to an incoming U.S. president with his eye on the world. Ford runs a province, not a country, and unless he becomes a regular on Fox News, he won't get on Trump's radar.
Much of the lobbying and persuading from Ford and Trudeau is also premised on the old playbook that Canadians need only remind Americans of their economic interests at the local level and word will get through to Washington.
But this notion of triangulation from the state and local level to the White House has limits. For one thing, many Southern U.S. states look to Mexico for their economic interests more than any Canadian connection.
Given those political realities, much of the posturing from the premier and prime minister seems intended as much for their domestic constituencies as any American audiences. That's especially true of the rarefied immigration rhetoric in Canada today.
Much of the alarmism about migrants is inward-looking, rather than looking at it from the American point of view. With Trump threatening to deport one million illegal migrants a year, the premiers of Quebec, Ontario and Alberta are clamouring for protection against waves of people trying to sneak into Canada, while worrying whether the Trump administration will take them back (as required by our latest bilateral agreement).
Rather than focusing solely on the northward flow of migrants into Canada, however, the federal government should be thinking hard about how to stop the southward flow into America which is much higher. After all, that's what has grabbed the attention of the incoming U.S. border czar, Tom Homan, who happens to be from upstate New York, near the Ontario border.
He called the Canada-U.S. border an "extreme national security vulnerability," predicting "tough conversations" with Ottawa. "There has to be an understanding from Canada that they can't be a gateway to terrorists coming into the United States."
While Canadians obsess about the notorious Roxham Road path into Canada, it turns out that borders are in the eye of the beholder- and very much a two-way street.
Ford and Trudeau are right to turn their minds to Trump. But the test of their tactics isn't how persuasive-sounding they seem when speaking to Canadians, but how effective they are in winning over the Americans in difficult negotiations ahead.
So far, both are following their re-election playbooks. But both will be judged by Canadian voters on how they play their hands with the Americans, not how they sound on the domestic campaign trail.
CAPTION: Facing headwinds from the U.S., Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, shown in 2022, are promising to serve and protect Canadians in upcoming elections, Martin Regg Cohn writes. But can they protect us from the U.S. president- elect's wrath?R.J. JohnstonToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Trade agreements; Prime ministers; Protectionism; Tariffs; Political analysis; Political leadership; International relations

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade agreements Protectionism Tariffs International relations

People: Trudeau, Justin; Ford, Doug

Document 143 of 500

NDP MP warned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians: Symbol could be viewed as 'political prop,' Speaker says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-mp-warned-wearing-pin-supporting-palestinians/docview/3129922792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+MP+warned+for+wearing+pin+supporting+Palestinians%3A+Symbol+could+be+viewed+as+%27political+prop%2C%27+Speaker+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A New Democrat MP was warned Monday that her decision to don a watermelon pin - a symbol of the Palestinian cause - could be construed as a political "prop" that has no place in the House of Commons.
During question period, Edmonton-Strathcona MP Heather McPherson took to the floor of the lower chamber to castigate Justin Trudeau's Liberal government for its response to the ongoing humanitarian crisis in Gaza.
"Entire families have been decimated. Children are starving to death. When will the Liberals live up to their obligations?" McPherson said, calling for sanctions on Israel and the implementation of an "actual arms embargo."
But before Rob Oliphant, the parliamentary secretary to the foreign affairs minister, could respond, House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus interjected.
"I just want to encourage all members, to remind members, to be very careful about what they wear in this House to ensure that it's not considered a prop," Fergus said.
McPherson was wearing a beaded watermelon pin when she delivered her remarks. Watermelon imagery has long been used as a stand-in symbol for the Palestinian flag, which shares the same colours as the fruit. Since Hamas's deadly Oct. 7 attacks on Israel and the resulting loss of lives in Gaza from Israel's retaliatory strikes, the watermelon has become a mainstream symbol to signal support for Palestinian rights. It has drawn less attention than the kaffiyeh, a Middle Eastern scarf also emblematic of the Palestinian cause, which was banned from being worn in the Ontario legislature earlier this year.
In a heated exchange over what is and is not permitted to be worn in the Commons, McPherson rose on a point of order to question Fergus's suggestion. "I have to tell you that I stand here proudly wearing the pin that stands in solidarity to Palestinian people, but people within this place are wearing pins for a various number of reasons," McPherson said.
She referred to a moose hide pin that a number of MPs wear in the Commons, which was born from an Indigenous-led movement to end violence toward women and children.
The NDP MP's reference to poppies also being worn in the chamber for Remembrance Day, however, was met with outrage from the opposition benches, with Conservative MPs expressing disbelief on social media over the comparison.
According to the House of Commons' rules on order and decorum, "props of any kind, used as a way of making a silent comment on issues" are barred from the chamber.
Political buttons and lapel pins usually fall outside those restrictions, but can similarly be prohibited if they create disorder or express specific positions. Speakers have, in the past, requested that MPs remove the so-called props from their lapels.
Fergus stopped short of requesting McPherson do the same.
MPs can frequently be seen sporting pins emblematic of causes in the Commons, such as green felt squares affixed to their lapels to protest Islamophobia and ribbons raising awareness about health conditions and diseases.
"Mr. Speaker, I have proudly worn many pins in this House. For example, tomorrow, to mark a thousand days since Putin invaded Ukraine, I will wear the Ukrainian pin," McPherson said. "I need to know whether the Conservatives will call this out as a prop."
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star
