Tories balk at $48M removal estimate: Minister says cost to scrap paths 'doesn't add up'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-balk-at-48m-removal-estimate/docview/3129922760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+balk+at+%2448M+removal+estimate%3A+Minister+says+cost+to+scrap+paths+%27doesn%27t+add+up%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Toronto's estimate that it could cost $48 million to remove $27 million of bike lanes "doesn't add up," charges Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"I just don't think that can be accurate. It can't cost double the amount to remove them as it does to put them in place," Sarkaria told reporters Monday.
But the minister, who has promised Queen's Park would foot the tab for removing bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University in order to alleviate gridlock, acknowledged the province has not completed any costing for the removal.
"We've seen similar projects across the city here - and other places across Canada - be removed for way less than that. Jarvis and Brimley are two examples that come to mind," he said, referring to city bike lanes previously removed at a cost of $300,000 and $80,000.
Sarkaria's comments came as legislative hearings began into his controversial bill with opposition parties and others criticizing Premier Doug Ford's government for wasting taxpayers' money to scrap existing infrastructure.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, warned Ford's scheme would mean "years" of disruption and reduce safety for cyclists.
"While the intent of removing these lanes may be to improve traffic, the construction process to dismantle these lanes would mean considerable delays and disruption for drivers," she said.
Hayward estimated it would cost $20 million to reconstruct the 3.5 kilometres of University Avenue and Queen's Park Crescent as well as part of Bloor Street.
The remaining $28 million would be spent on removing lane markings, signs and curbs and repaving the other 18.5 kilometres of lanes on Bloor and Yonge.
"Removing the bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University would not be a short-term inconvenience - we'd likely be facing multiple years of successive construction that would disrupt other commuters, harm businesses and incur costs for years," she told the legislative committee.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said it's "outrageous" that public money was being spent to reduce the number of bike lanes in the provincial capital.
"This is ... the people of Ontario being forced to pay the price for this premier's personal grievances, his vanity projects," said Stiles, suggesting Ford is fighting "battles that he already lost at Toronto city council when he was a councillor" from 2010 to 2014.
Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood) pointed out that six cyclists have been killed on Toronto streets this year.
"What is it they're not seeing? What is it they're not hearing? This is unbelievable when you have fatalities and we are putting (a price) on people's lives," said Hazell.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Sarkaria's legislation is "completely fiscally irresponsible."
"Why should people in Bracebridge, Owen Sound and Sudbury be paying for the cost of ripping out bike lanes in Toronto?" said Schreiner.
At the legislative committee, speaker after speaker implored the Tories to reconsider.
Association of Ontario Municipalities' executive director Brian Rosborough said it would be "misguided and ineffective" to get rid of existing bike lanes.
Brian Burchell, general manager of the Bloor Annex Business Improvement Area, noted the lanes have been positive for the 207 small businesses along the stretch of Bloor he represents.
"It's been almost 10 years. It's helped transform our area into a vibrant, green, welcoming business district," said Burchell.
Scott Butler, executive director of Good Roads, a 130-year-old municipal association concerned with the quality and design of roads in Ontario, said the government has yet to explain how its proposal would make commuting safer.
"Rather than thinking of these as cycling facilities or erroneously thinking of them as causes of congestion, we're better served by acknowledging" that the lanes contribute to road safety, said Butler, urging a "common sense, evidence-based approach to bike policy."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Commuting; Bicycling; Transportation planning; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Criticism

PCs look to close 'loophole': Municipalities would need provincial approval to launch new sites under bill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-look-close-loophole/docview/3129922443/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+look+to+close+%27loophole%27%3A+Municipalities+would+need+provincial+approval+to+launch+new+sites+under+bill&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is closing a "loophole" it fears could lead to more safe drug consumption sites in Ontario.
As the province works to ban sites within 200 metres of schools or daycares, new legislation introduced Monday would prevent municipalities or local health boards from applying for federal approval or funding, Health Minister Sylvia Jones said Monday.
The proposed bill, if passed, will require approval from Ontario's health minister before that could be done, effectively nixing the process under Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"We are shutting down that loophole," Jones said. "We don't want them bypassing and getting federal approval for something that we vehemently disagree with."
"I want to be clear - there will be no further safe injection sites in the province," she added after the Safer Streets, Stronger Communities Act was tabled in the legislature by Solicitor General Michael Kerzner.
The legislation also introduces new penalties for fraudulent changing of vehicle identification numbers in a crackdown on auto thefts and prohibits convicted sex offenders on Ontario's sex offender registry from legally changing their names while they are required to check in with police.
The push to prevent more safe consumption sites from opening was condemned by harm reduction proponents.
"That is an overreach of power," Zoe Dodd of the Toronto Overdose Prevention Society told a news conference with fellow activists at Queen's Park.
The government's closure of 10 safe consumption sites by March 31 - including five in Toronto - under the 200-metre rule means ambulance services will be swamped with overdose calls, leading to delays for anyone needing a paramedic, Dodd predicted.
"Every municipality in this province had better get an emergency plan in place because people are going to be overdosing and dying."
Ford has called safe injection sites a "failed policy" experiment that has led to dangerous conditions near homes and schools.
Instead, his government has set aside $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs, with 375 supportive housing units and addiction recovery and treatment beds. But proponents of safe consumption sites said waiting lists for addiction treatment and rehab spots are months long, which is why they are needed to reverse overdoses and save lives.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 146 of 500

Ford ignores real need for shelters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-ignores-real-need-shelters/docview/3129922432/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+ignores+real+need+for+shelters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1974, my family and I arrived in Canada. As refugees from Chile, our first home was a shelter near Union Station. This service, coupled with a supportive community, made it possible for my parents to eventually find an apartment and jobs. Without the shelter, our story would have had a different ending.
Today, Canada's housing crisis is the reason the need for shelter has grown exponentially. Canadians increasingly cite the rising cost of housing as the main economic barrier people face. And nowhere is this crisis more evident than in the record number of people who are unhoused across this country.
In Toronto alone, more than 12,200 people are living in shelters, with countless more sleeping on friends' couches, living in overcrowded and often unsafe apartments, or trying to survive outdoors. One in five Toronto households is at high risk of homelessness- just one missed paycheck or family emergency away from losing shelter. The very fear of eviction puts families into panic.
While the provincial government has tools to fix this, Premier Doug Ford wants to distract from his own responsibility. He could answer municipalities' calls for more health care, housing that's affordable, and other services that are proven solutions. Instead, he's called on cities to ask him to override people's Charter rights through use of the notwithstanding clause in response to outdoor encampments.
Beyond eroding constitutional freedoms, this isn't actually a fix. I echo the words of Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, the chair of the Ontario Big City Mayors group: if the Province uses the notwithstanding clause on encampments, but people have nowhere else to go, it's not a real solution. It's just pushing people around to try and hide the problem.
Premier Ford even suggested that people experiencing homelessness should "get off (their) ass and start working." This ignores the well-documented reality that many people who are unhoused do have employment. And for people receiving disability assistance, monthly benefits aren't enough to afford the average rent for a 1-bedroom apartment. Ford's punitive approach has laid the ground for uncaring and even dehumanizing responses by frustrated residents with respect to their unhoused neighbours.
There are no solutions to be found in a polarizing and politicized approach to a problem we can only solve together. Toronto city council has recognized this. In 2017 it took the politics out of selecting sites for new shelters, by delegating approval to senior city staff; their location is selected and evaluated based on need, by impartial staff.
In Toronto, 230 people are turned away each night because our shelters are full and there are no beds left. At the same time, we've seen a growing number of people sleeping outdoors, in park encampments. Together this is irrefutable evidence we desperately need more indoor spaces in every corner of the city.
Toronto city council adopted a 10-Year Shelter Infrastructure Plan to deliver new purpose-built shelters across our city that can be converted to permanent housing. Recently, though, incomplete information about a new shelter site was leaked, which has fuelled local opposition even before the deal officially closed and community outreach could begin.
The arguments are the same ones we hear any time these services are proposed: we might need shelters, but they don't belong in my neighbourhood. Social housing is important, but not on my street. Support services help people, but I don't want them close to my family. This reality? It's not mine to fix.
Except it is because we're all essential to the solution. Be wary of politicians who claim that people just need to get off their butt and move along, instead of investing in real solutions. Our failure to help people who are struggling with homelessness is everyone's crisis. Let's collectively treat it that way and strengthen the ties that bind us together.
Alejandra Bravo is the City Councillor for Ward 9 Davenport and Chair of the City of Toronto's Economic and Community Development Committee.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Alejandro Bravo Contributor Alejandra Bravo is the City Councillor for Ward 9 Davenport and Chair of the City of Toronto's Economic and Community Development Committee.;

Full text: Does Poilievre even like Canada?
This is the difference between the political moment that swept Justin Trudeau into leadership and the one that will push him out, Nov. 17
Jamie Watt is correct that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will continue his efforts to bash the Trudeau government. Has anyone actually sat and listened to his videos one after the other? The Trudeau attacks are there. The misinformation is there. But there is also something lurking in the background of his constant "everything is broken" refrain. Poilievre can't seem to say anything positive about Canada. Does he even like Canada and Canadians? He talks glowingly about the U.S. Why not move there and quit trying to remake Canada into the 51st state?
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge, Ont.
Premier hasn't kept promises
Jaime Watt noted in his column that former Ontario premier Mike Harris used the phrase "promises made, promises kept." Premier Doug Ford also refers to "promises made, promises kept" but he understandably doesn't want to refer to those promises that he has failed to keep to the detriment of Ontario residents. He promised years ago to end hallway medicine. As someone who has had a loved one remain on a stretcher in the emergency department for several days waiting to be admitted, I know that this promise was not kept. Another promise was related to improved access to housing. Minister Stan Cho delayed an assisted housing project on Cummer Avenue for years. This delay cost the city of Toronto millions of dollars. The only accurate part of "promises made, promises kept" is the promises made.
DavidAsh, North York
Losing the CBC would be big blow
The columnist mentions that ConservativeLeader PierrePoilievre wants to "shutter the CBC." This would be a violation of freedom of speech. It would be his attempt to control the newsfeed for the country. The loss of our national voice would be a huge blow to cultural identity and to reporting of events in our country. Let's preserve our important national treasure, the CBC.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Women leading way in church
For the Catholic Church equality for women is a bridge too far,Nov. 15
It's unfortunate that the recent opinion piece from Janice Kennedy is so far out of touch with Roman Catholicism and women. Had she attended a recent evening sponsored by the Archdiocese of Toronto under the direction of Archbishop Francis Leo (soon to be elevated to Cardinal) and named "Unpacking of the Synod on Synodality" (the Rome event referenced in Kennedy's article), she might have learned that nuns are doing more than the dishes these days. The moderator was a Catholic lay woman and mother, Dr. Josephine Lombardi, Assistant Dean for Academic Affairs at St. Augustine's Seminary. The panel consisted of three well-informed Catholic women including two nuns. One of these Sisters, Natalie Bequard,Xavieres Missionaries of Christ Jesus, from France, had been conferred an honorary doctorate earlier in the week at St. Michael's College (U of T) and was undersecretary of the Synod of Bishops at the Vatican. Going back in history, several women have been named doctors of the Church including the brilliant Jewish convert to Catholicism, Edith Stein, who ultimately died at Auschwitz.
Anne MacCarthy,Toronto
Singing praises of TBA Trailblazers
In Toronto, a Biennial that's looking for its agenda, Nov. 16
Tatum Dooley vehemently compares the fledgling Toronto Biennial of Art (TBA, established 2019) to the long-established Venice Biennale (La Biennale di Venezia, established 1895) and the Whitney Biennial funded by the fortunes of American robber barons Vanderbilt and Whitney. It would be easy to denigrate Dooley's opinion as another self-loathing example of "it's Toronto, it can't be any good," or is it the grass is greener on the other side? But I would rather sing the praise of those TBA trailblazers who have the foresight, tenacity, dedication and elbow grease, who saw the potential and created something worthy for the rest of us to enjoy, to learn and to share. Did I mention they did it all on a limited budget? During these days of diminishing arts infrastructure, TBA precariously hangs onto the beachhead it created post-COVID and manages to bring us joy.
AndrewLee,Toronto
Firms promising more than they can deliver
I paid $1,500 for an MRI and got an appointment in days. But such scans aren't always good for patients - or our health care system, Nov. 16
Thanks to journalist Kenyon Wallace for personally researching this for-profit health-care service. The private firms are promising far more than they can deliver. The public is easily lured then disappointed. This article delivers important information we should all be aware of.
Joan Wood,Markham
Release names of war criminals, not the others
Canada must confront its shameful history of harbouring Nazi war criminals, Nov. 18
One cannot but sympathize with the concerns of Jewish Canadians as expressed in this article. That monsters such as Helmut Rauca lived among us is terrible, and we cannot forget that we, along with other democratic nations, refused entry to Jewish refugees in the 1930s and beyond. There was a great outcry when the Canadian Parliament gave a standing ovation to Yaroslav Hunka, who, it was subsequently discovered, had been a member of the Nazi SS. But let's put this in perspective; in 1941, when Hunka was a teenager or at most a very young man, Ukraine had just lived through the Holodomor in which millions of people had died of starvation intentionally inflicted by the Soviets. Is it a surprise that young Ukrainians would flock to the banner of an army attacking the Soviets? If known war criminals are included in the list, release those names. Not the others. With those few exceptions, we can have no idea of the life experiences of others identified in the Deschenes Commission report.
Barrie Reynolds, Toronto
EV rebates
and tariffs
I can't afford to buy an EV car, but I could if China's EV cars were allowed into Canada without a 100 per cent duty imposed on them. This doesn't seem like a good way for Canada to fight climate change. At least the federal government offers a $5,000 rebate. Premier Doug Ford and his government offer nothing.
HerbertHyman, Oakville

Document 148 of 500

The third-party force influencing voters: Progress Toronto's role in Ward 15 byelection sparks talk of need for similar group on the right

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/third-party-force-influencing-voters/docview/3129922002/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+third-party+force+influencing+voters%3A+Progress+Toronto%27s+role+in+Ward+15+byelection+sparks+talk+of+need+for+similar+group+on+the+right&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the most important players in this month's municipal byelection in Don Valley West wasn't on the ballot.
Rachel Chernos Lin was the one who handily beat conservative rival Anthony Furey in the closely watched contest Nov. 4 to become Toronto's newest councillor. But no sooner had the votes been counted than left-wing advocacy group Progress Toronto claimed credit for Furey's defeat.
The organization boasted in a social media post that it had distributed thousands of flyers in the ward exposing the "truth" about Furey's record.
"When voters learned the truth, they rejected him," it asserted.
The extent to which Progress Toronto swung the election for Chernos Lin is a matter for debate. But even Furey's team acknowledges the group likely played a role in his defeat.
The organization's influence in Ward 15, Don Valley West - powered by hundreds of volunteer hours and strategic anti-Furey attack ads - looks like the latest coup for the organization, which has helped elect a string of progressives to municipal office in the past six years. In the wake of the results, local conservative insiders are talking about establishing an organization of their own to challenge Progress Toronto, an effort that, if successful, could reshape how city elections are fought.
Coun. Brad Bradford, the centre-right representative for Ward 19, Beaches-East York, said that while officially there are no political parties at the municipal level, Progress Toronto has been effectively operating as one for years, building up supporters' lists and training volunteers. He's critical of the group's tactics, which he describes as aggressive and misleading, but said "to their credit, they've had some success."
Although more right-leaning groups have gotten involved in previous city elections, none have had the staying power of Progress Toronto. Bradford said he isn't directly involved in efforts to change that, and declined to say who was. But he said "I definitely have those conversations" about the need for a third-party organization to challenge Progress Toronto's messaging, "and many others do as well."
"I wouldn't be surprised if that materializes," he said.
Progress Toronto was founded in 2018, after the Ontario government introduced new rules for third parties that campaign or support candidates in municipal elections.
The groups are required to register with the city, stay within spending and contribution limits, and aren't allowed to co-ordinate with any candidates.
The brainchild of political organizer Michal Hay, Progress Toronto was envisioned as a standing political force that could help elect candidates who support the public investments the non-profit believes are necessary to create a more equitable city.
It scored success in 2022 when five of the nine progressives it endorsed for city council won their seats. In 2023's byelection to replace John Tory, the group endorsed Olivia Chow, helping her become Toronto's first left-leaning mayor in 13 years. Hay took a leave from Progress Toronto to direct Chow's campaign, and is now the mayor's chief of staff. Chow's office says it has no involvement in the group's activities.
Saman Tabasinejad, Progress Toronto's executive director, told the Star the organization got involved in the Ward 15 race because it believed that if elected, Furey, a former Postmedia pundit who came fourth in last year's mayoral byelection, would "embolden the right" across Toronto and block progressive policy at city hall.
The group didn't endorse Chernos Lin - who was backed by prominent Liberals like former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne - or any other candidate, and instead chose to use the less than $9,000 third parties were allowed to spend in the byelection to attack Furey's record. It enlisted almost 100 volunteers to deliver 22,000 flyers claiming Furey had "a history of pushing dangerous myths on COVID-19, Islam, (and) gender," among other accusations.
Tabasinejad said the group targeted parts of the ward where residents have historically voted for right-wing candidates in the belief that mainstream conservatives would be turned off by Furey's past statements.It also focused on Thorncliffe Park, home to a significant Muslim community, delivering flyers in Urdu and English accusing Furey of spreading "hate about Muslims." They cited his comments after the 2017 Quebec City mosque shooting that a law to tackle Islamophobia could lead to a "compliant-with-Sharia law."
Furey has rejected accusations he holds discriminatory or hateful views against Muslims or any other group, and described Progress Toronto's allegations as "out-of-context smear attacks."
"It's rather sad that a group with the name Progress Toronto would choose to focus on negativity, fear mongering and lies," he said in an email to the Star.
"While they may be pleased with themselves that they were able to frighten and misinform some people with their malicious attacks, make no mistake about it, there were many other people who weren't fooled by it and were disgusted by it," added Furey.
Jim Burnett, co-manager of Furey's campaign, said Progress Toronto's depictions of Furey as far-right were mischaracterizations. But he said the group's tactics were probably effective, if not in determining the byelection's winner, then at least in boosting Chernos Lin's margin of victory. She secured 55 per cent of the vote in a field of 16 candidates. Furey was second with about 31 per cent.
Burnett said third-party attack ads are difficult to fight against because municipal campaign spending caps are low.
The limit for challengers in the Ward 15 race was less than $70,000, and he said candidates have to focus that money on countering their rivals' messaging.
Burnett stressed that there's nothing unfair about third-party groups taking part in elections, saying they're just following "the rules of the game."
But organizers in the middle and on the right of the political spectrum have to start considering whether"these are rules that they should also be engaged with," he said, adding that those conversations, largely informal until recently, have ramped up since the Ward 15 race.
Furey said he's involved in those talks. He told the Star that "people have reached out to me about their (interest) in organizing and funding an alternative group that presents a more forward-looking vision for the city." He added: "Stay tuned."
Myer Siemiatycki, professor emeritus of politics at Toronto Metropolitan University, said more third-party groups could be a positive thing for local democracy.
Public engagement with municipal elections is generally low, but multiple groups knocking on doors to endorse or oppose a candidate could raise awareness, and "stand a good chance of boosting voter turnout," he said.About one-third ofeligible voters cast a ballot in the Ward 15 race, which is high for a city byelection.
Siemiatycki also predicted that having competing progressive and conservative groups align themselves with candidates could help weaken the power of incumbency, often considered an unfair advantage in local elections, by "(shifting) the focus away just from name recognition to what the candidates actually stand for."
Tabasinejad said she sees talk of political rivals forming a group to challenge Progress Toronto as a "compliment" to what it's accomplished through grassroots organizing. She rejected accusations the organization's attacks against Furey were misleading or unfair.
Not everyone sees Progress Toronto's influence in Ward 15 as decisive. Chernos Lin told the Star she believes she would have won if the group hadn't gotten involved. She said she had a base of support in the ward from previously being elected Toronto District School Board trustee.
"I find it interesting that everybody is trying to take credit for the hard work that my whole campaign put into this election," she said.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Subject: Conservatism; Election results; Local elections; Grass roots movement; Third party; Negative campaigning; Candidates; Voters; Political advertising; Voter behavior; Human influences; Advocacy

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Progress Toronto; NAICS: 813319

Title: The third-party force influencing voters:   Progress Toronto's role in Ward 15 byelection sparks talk of need for similar group on the right

Document 149 of 500

NDP MP cautioned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians in the House of Commons

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-mp-cautioned-wearing-pin-supporting/docview/3130445366/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+MP+cautioned+for+wearing+pin+supporting+Palestinians+in+the+House+of+Commons&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A New Democrat MP was warned Monday that her decision to don a watermelon pin - a symbol of the Palestinian cause - could be construed as a political "prop" that has no place in the House of Commons. During question period, Edmonton-Strathcona MP Heather McPherson took to the floor of the lower chamber to castigate Justin Trudeau's Liberal government for its response to the ongoing humanitarian crisis in Gaza."Entire families have been decimated. Children are starving to death. When will the Liberals live up to their obligations?" McPherson said, calling for sanctions on the Israeli government and the implementation of an "actual arms embargo."But before Rob Oliphant, the parliamentary secretary to the foreign affairs minister, could respond, House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus interjected."I just want to encourage all members, to remind members, to be very careful about what they wear in this House to ensure that it's not considered a prop," Fergus said.McPherson was wearing a beaded watermelon pin when she delivered her remarks. Watermelon imagery has long been used as a stand-in symbol for the Palestinian flag, which shares the same colours as the fruit. Since Hamas's deadly Oct. 7 attacks on Israel and the resulting loss of lives in Gaza from Israel's retaliatory strikes, the watermelon has become a mainstream symbol to signal support for Palestinian rights. It has drawn less attention than the kaffiyeh, a Middle Eastern scarf also emblematic of the Palestinian cause, which was banned from being worn in the Ontario legislature earlier this year.In a heated exchange over what is and is not permitted to be worn in the Commons, McPherson rose on a point of order to question Fergus's suggestion."I have to tell you that I stand here proudly wearing the pin that stands in solidarity to Palestinian people, but people within this place are wearing pins for a various number of reasons," McPherson said.She referred to a moose hide pin that a number of MPs wear in the Commons, which was born from an Indigenous-led movement to end violence toward women and children. The NDP MP's reference to poppies also being worn in the chamber for Remembrance Day, however, was met with outrage from the opposition benches, with Conservative MPs expressing disbelief on social media over the comparison. According to the House of Commons' rules on order and decorum, "props of any kind, used as a way of making a silent comment on issues" are barred from the chamber. Political buttons and lapel pins usually fall outside those restrictions, but can similarly be prohibited if they create disorder or express specific positions. Speakers have, in the past, requested that MPs remove the so-called props from their lapels.Fergus stopped short of requesting McPherson do the same.MPs can frequently be seen sporting pins emblematic of causes in the Commons, such as green felt squares affixed to their lapels to protest Islamophobia and ribbons raising awareness about health conditions and diseases. "Mr. Speaker, I have proudly worn many pins in this House. For example, tomorrow, to mark a thousand days since Putin invaded Ukraine, I will wear the Ukrainian pin," McPherson said, referring to another pin commonly worn in the chamber. "I need to know whether the Conservatives will call this out as a prop."Fergus said he would take the matter under consideration and return to the House with further comments if necessary.
CAPTION: NDP MP Heather McPherson speaks in the House of Commons on Nov. 18, 2024.

Location: Gaza Strip; Israel

Ford government moves to shut 'loophole' for supervised drug consumption sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-moves-shut-loophole-supervised/docview/3130445365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+moves+to+shut+%27loophole%27+for+supervised+drug+consumption+sites&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is closing a "loophole" it fears could lead to more safe drug consumption sites in Ontario.
As the province works to ban sites within 200 metres of schools or daycares, new legislation introduced Monday would prevent municipalities or local health boards from applying for federal approval or funding, Health Minister Sylvia Jones said Monday.
The proposed bill, if passed, will require approval from Ontario's health minister before that could be done, effectively nixing the process under Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"We are shutting down that loophole," Jones said. "We don't want them bypassing and getting federal approval for something that we vehemently disagree with."
"I want to be clear - there will be no further safe injection sites in the province," she added after the Safer Streets, Stronger Communities Act was tabled in the legislature by Solicitor General Michael Kerzner.
The legislation also introduces new penalties for fraudulent changing of vehicle identification numbers in a crackdown on auto thefts and prohibits convicted sex offenders on Ontario's sex offender registry from legally changing their names while they are required to check in with police.
The push to prevent more safe consumption sites from opening was condemned by harm reduction proponents.
"That is an overreach of power," Zoe Dodd of the Toronto Overdose Prevention Society told a news conference with fellow activists at Queen's Park.
The government's closure of 10 safe consumption sites by March 31 - including five in Toronto - under the 200-metre rule means ambulance services will be swamped with overdose calls, leading to delays for anyone needing a paramedic, Dodd predicted.
"Every municipality in this province had better get an emergency plan in place because people are going to be overdosing and dying."
Ford has called safe injection sites a "failed policy" experiment that has led to dangerous conditions near homes and schools.
Instead, his government has set aside $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs, with 375 supportive housing units and addiction recovery and treatment beds.
But proponents of safe consumption sites said waiting lists for addiction treatment and rehab spots are months long, which is why they are needed to reverse overdoses and save lives.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford chats with Health Minister Sylvia Jones at Queen's Park on Feb. 26 2024.

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Sex crimes

Title: Ford government moves to shut 'loophole' for supervised drug consumption sites

Ford government disputes Toronto's $48M bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-disputes-torontos-48m-bike-lane/docview/3130445265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+disputes+Toronto%27s+%2448M+bike+lane+removal+cost%3A+%27It+can%27t+cost+double%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Toronto's estimate that it could cost $48 million to remove $27 million of bike lanes "doesn't add up," charges Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"I just don't think that can be accurate. It can't cost double the amount to remove them as it does to put them in place," Sarkaria told reporters Monday.
But the minister, who has promised Queen's Park would foot the tab for taking out bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University in order to alleviate traffic gridlock, acknowledged the province has not completed any costing for the removal.
"We've seen similar projects across the city here - and other places across Canada - be removed for way less than that. Jarvis and Brimley are two examples that come to mind," he said, referring to city bike lanes previously removed at a cost of $300,000 and $80,000 respectively.
Sarkaria's comments came as legislative hearings began into his controversial bill with opposition parties and others criticizing Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government for wasting taxpayers' money to scrap existing infrastructure.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, warned Ford's scheme would mean "years" of disruption and reduce safety for cyclists.
"While the intent of removing these lanes may be to improve traffic, the construction process to dismantle these lanes would mean considerable delays and disruption for drivers," she said.
Hayward estimated it would cost $20 million to reconstruct the 3.5 km of University Avenue and Queen's Park Crescent as well as part of Bloor Street.
The remaining $28 million would be spent on removing lane markings, signs and curbs and repaving the other 18.5 km of lanes on Bloor and Yonge.
"Removing the bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University would not be a short-term inconvenience - we'd likely be facing multiple years of successive construction that would disrupt other commuters, harm businesses and incur costs for years," she told the legislative committee.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said it's "outrageous" that public money was being spent to reduce the number of bike lanes in the provincial capital.
"This is ... the people of Ontario being forced to pay the price for this premier's personal grievances, his vanity projects," said Stiles, suggesting Ford is fighting "battles that he already lost at Toronto city council when he was a councillor" from 2010 to 2014.
Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood) pointed out that six cyclists have been killed on Toronto streets this year.
"What is it they're not seeing? What is it they're not hearing? This is unbelievable when you have fatalities and we are putting (a price) on people's lives," said Hazell.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Sarkaria's legislation is "completely fiscally irresponsible."
"Why should people in Bracebridge, Owen Sound and Sudbury be paying for the cost of ripping out bike lanes in Toronto?" said Schreiner.
At the legislative committee, speaker after speaker implored the Tories to reconsider.
Association of Ontario Municipalities' executive director Brian Rosborough said it would be "misguided and ineffective" to get rid of existing bike lanes.
Brian Burchell, general manager of the Bloor Annex Business Improvement Area, noted the lanes have been positive for the 207 small businesses along the stretch of Bloor he represents.
"It's been almost 10 years. It's helped transform our area into a vibrant, green, welcoming business district," said Burchell.
Scott Butler, executive director of Good Roads, a 130-year-old municipal association concerned with the quality and design of roads in Ontario, said the government has yet to explain how its proposal would make commuting safer.
"Rather than thinking of these as cycling facilities or erroneously thinking of them as causes of congestion, we're better served by acknowledging" that the lanes contribute to road safety, said Butler, urging a "common sense, evidence-based approach to bike policy."
CAPTION: Ontario's Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria during question period at the Ontario Legislature in Toronto on Nov. 28, 2023.

Subject: Commuting; Bicycling

Title: Ford government disputes Toronto's $48M bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double'

Good for David Olive for calling for a business overhaul in Canada. The 10 richest Canadians are worth $261 billion and they saw an increase of 20 per cent over the last four years. They need to be taxed at a higher rate. Postering and throwing paint on a Toronto store, commonly known as vandalism, on Nov. 10, 2023, the same date as Kristallnacht (unmentioned in the Star article) smacks of antisemitism and should not be dismissed as mere civil disobedience.

Our provincial government is spending OUR money on a print and media ad campaign touting how great things are in Ontario. Ontario announces $100M deal with Musk's Starlink, Nov. 14
Premier Doug Ford once again fails to follow his own rules. He recently forced the LCBO to cancel a contract for paper bags and reissue it to an Ontario company. This in spite of the fact that the chosen supplier was a Canadian company and provided a lower price. It's long overdue for many services in Canada to be designated essential services - namely the railroads, the commercial ports, sections of health care and Canada Post. Compulsory arbitration is a best solution if management and labour cannot resolve contract differences. A strike is not. Canada Post, which has lost approximately $500 million annually for the past few years due to poor management, is now on strike again causing serious problems for many people including those who receive wages, pensions and CRA communications by post. There already has been a significant shift by clients to other delivery services putting Canada Post in an even more precarious situation. Document 153 of 500

Ford government wants to work with social media giants to keep children safe, minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-wants-work-with-social-media/docview/3130445068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+wants+to+work+with+social+media+giants+to+keep+children+safe%2C+minister+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has met with representatives from YouTube and is hoping to soon speak to executives from other social media companies about what they are doing to help students' mental health and well-being.
"I talked to the organization about what they do to protect children and young people online," Dunlop said Monday, the first day of Bullying Awareness and Prevention Week, after announcing $4.6 million for resources and supports for kids.
Talks were held with YouTube on Oct. 23.
Dunlop said when the provincial government enacted its cellphone ban this fall, "we also blocked social media in the classrooms and schools to ensure that students were focused on the learning at hand ... so I want to meet with the companies to look at what they are doing to protect our students."
Across the province, a dozen school boards and two private schools are suing social media giants TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram, alleging the popular platforms have harmed kids' mental health as well as their academic achievement. They are seeking a total of more than $8 billion.
YouTube is not named in any of the cases.
Premier Doug Ford has been critical of the court action. Last May, then-education minister Stephen Lecce said that "instead of talking about it and litigating about, we opt to act decisively with a comprehensive plan" to not only curb cellphone and social media use in schools but also to work with companies to bring about change.
Boards that have launched lawsuits include Toronto public, Toronto Catholic, Peel public, Dufferin-Peel Catholic, and York Catholic. Two private religious schools - Holy Name of Mary College School in Mississauga and Eitz Chaim day school in Toronto - have also signed on.
They have filed separate but similar cases in Ontario's Superior Court of Justice, alleging the companies' platforms have "rewired the way children think, behave and learn" that has cost them billions in supports for staff and students. They are also calling on the companies to make improvements to their platforms, say their statements of claim.
Their allegations have not been tested in court, and the social media companies have said they will defend themselves against the claims.
A spokesperson for Snapchat has previously told the Star that its platform "was intentionally designed to be different from traditional social media, with a focus on helping Snapchatters communicate with their close friends ... While we will always have more work to do, we feel good about the role Snapchat plays in helping close friends feel connected, happy and prepared as they face the many challenges of adolescence."
A Meta spokesperson, representing Facebook and Instagram, has said "we want to reassure every parent that we have their interests at heart in the work we're doing to provide teens with safe, supportive experiences" on both platforms.
The boards and two schools are not paying legal costs, but have instead agreed that the law firm Neinstein LLP will take a contingency fee from any court award.
Of the $4.6 million Dunlop announced for resources and support, almost $1.5 million will go to Big Brothers and Big Sisters for mentoring programs in elementary and secondary schools, while $800,000 is earmarked for Kids Help Phone for its around-the-clock counselling services. Another $436,500 will help Egale Canada support anti-cyberbullying efforts for LGTBQ students.
About $150,000 will go to Victim Services Toronto to help youth with issues around online dating and healthy relationships.
CAPTION: Education Minister Jill Dunlop attends question period in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Students; Mental health; Bullying; Education; Youth organizations; Litigation; Social networks; School boards

Document 154 of 500

What more will it take to shut down Marineland?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-more-will-take-shut-down-marineland/docview/3129646524/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+more+will+it+take+to+shut+down+Marineland%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How long do we have to keep talking about Marineland? How many more animals have to die at the Niagara Falls marine park before something is done to protect those still languishing there?
It's hard to imagine now what would have to happen to finally force Marineland to shut down for good as yet another beluga whale has died at the park. This is the fifth beluga to die there this year, the 17th since 2019.
Dolphins, seals, penguins and Canada's last remaining captive orca, Kiska, have also all perished under Marineland's care in recent years. In March, the park was also found guilty of mistreating three black bears.
But for officials, this is seemingly not yet bad enough.
Mounting deaths at Marineland have raised concerns in recent years about water quality, sparking a government investigation. Melanie Milczynski, Ontario's chief animal welfare inspector, told The Canadian Press that enforcement officials visited the park 205 times since the province took over animal welfare enforcement from the Ontario Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals in 2020. She said that to her knowledge, the deaths are not linked to water quality. Though she also acknowledged the park's past failure to meet water standards, she insists that recent improvements meet acceptable limits.
But meeting water quality standards is really the bare minimum when it comes to caring for marine animals in captivity. We are talking about wild animals, most of whom were traumatically captured from their homes, being forced to live out the rest of their lives in tiny tanks, unable to engage in natural behaviours, until they get ill and die. Drone footage shot by advocacy group UrgentSeas in October, showed 32 belugas still remaining at the park.
While Marineland has stated in the past that deaths of animals in their care are simply the cycle of life, animal advocates aren't buying it.
Lawyer and executive director of Animal Justice, Camille Labchuk, calls the current situation at the park "as dire as it has ever been," and says "we need urgent government action to protect these whales and get as many of them as possible to sanctuaries."
An ocean sanctuary for whales is currently being developed off the east coast of Canada.
Adds Labchuk: "How long will the Ontario government stand on the sidelines while whales drop like flies at Marineland?"
NDP leader, Marit Stiles, called the recent beluga death "disgraceful," adding, "they should have shut this place down years ago."
While Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie told City News, "This is a constant theme with this provincial government that there is no accountability." She asks: "What is the plan to ensure these beautiful mammals are being cared for properly and that they're not dying?"
Doug Ford wants to turn Niagara Falls into the Las Vegas of the North, though the inclusion of Marineland remains in question.
Erin Ryan, Wildlife Campaign Manager for World Animal Protection, says "If the Government of Ontario truly wants to transform the Niagara region, any plans for an amusement park and revitalization must not include animal entertainment, but must include a transition plan for Marineland."
Canada passed landmark legislation in 2019, banning the captivity of new whales and dolphins in the country. Animal Justice was influential in getting the law enacted, but today the group's director of legal advocacy, Kaitlyn Mitchell, says "it is truly shameful that after the monumental passage of Bill S-203, we as a society can do nothing but watch as the remaining belugas at Marineland die off one by one."Marineland is closed for the season after operating in limited capacity over the summer. It claims on its website that the park is "in a transitional phase," as it seeks new ownership.
Niagara mayor Jim Diodati told media last year that he'd "like to see (Marineland) move right away from animals." The animals would surely agree.
CAPTION: Kiska, Canada's last Orca in captivity, lived alone at Marineland from 2011 until its death last year.Beluga whales swim in a tank at Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ont., Friday, June 9, 2023.A beluga whale surfaces from a tank to be fed by an employee at Marineland amusement park in Niagara Falls, Ont., June 9, 2023.
CREDIT: Jessica Scott-Reid, Contributor

Subject: Water quality; Amusement parks; Animal welfare; Whales & whaling; Animal care

Location: Canada; Niagara Falls

Company / organization: Name: Marineland; NAICS: 712130

The bike lane behind the back lash + the problem with tenanted property sales

Full text: Need to deal
with oligopolies long overdue
Anti-business talk is cheap. Does Pierre Poilievre really have the guts to take on Canada's oligopolies? Nov. 14
Good for David Olive for calling for a business overhaul in Canada. The need to deal with oligopolies is long overdue, as is the need to reduce corporate welfare handouts and charge a fair tax to corporations. But there is no need to wait for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre because Prime Minister Justin Trudeau can introduce appropriate changes. Maybe it will improve the Liberal standing with Canadians.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
Another meaningless misdirection
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's spokesperson Sebastian Skamski replied to Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow's request for immediate housing funding by stating "Incompetent and greedy NDP Mayor Olivia Chow is the model example of the failure of the Liberals' housing photo op fund."Hisresponse is obviously off-the-charts meaningless and a typical rudemisdirection. Why do Poilievre and his people need to treat theirfellow Canadians with contempt every minute of every day of the week?
Jack Bergmans,Toronto
Why aren't billionaires stepping up?
Doug Ford wanted cities to ask him to use the notwithstanding clause to end encampments. Twelve mayors just said do it, Nov. 8
There are more than 100 very rich billionaires in Canada . It would be simple for them to build enough shelters, buy enough food for the food banks and lower rents so their tenants can stay in place. But they won't because there is no gain for them. The 10 richest Canadians are worth $261 billion and they saw an increase of 20 per cent over the past four years. They need to be taxed at a higher rate. Our system has created desperate people and desperate people do desperate things. Governments need to step up to the plate and get the job done humanely.
Brian Mellor,Midland, Ont.
Vandalism
isn't civil disobedience
Indigo CEO 'reached out' to top cop, Nov. 14
There is a world of difference between civil disobedience and an act of vandalism in a context of antisemitism. In civil disobedience, it is those committing the disobedience who are inconvenienced, usually because of arrest. Postering and throwing paint on a Toronto store, commonly known as vandalism, on Nov. 10, 2023, the same date as Kristallnacht (unmentioned in the Star article) smacks of antisemitism and should not be dismissed as mere civil disobedience.
Marc Côté, Toronto
Wasting our money on
a sales pitch
Our provincial government is spending OUR money on a print and media ad campaign touting how great things are in Ontario. In the meantime, health care is in deep trouble, housing is a problem, and the list goes on. Sending out $200 to every Ontarian (despite level of financial need) and pouring money into a thinly disguised election campaign tells me something about this government's focus. Can anyone tell us how much this "new day" is costing us? I'm not alone in saying that huge numbers of Ontarians are not "feeling good."
KathrynClark,Claremont
Ford's double standards
Ontario announces $100M deal with Musk's Starlink, Nov. 14
Premier Doug Ford once again fails to follow his own rules. He recently forced the LCBO to cancel a contract for paper bags and reissue it to an Ontario company. This in spite of the fact that the chosen supplier was a Canadian company and provided a lower price. Then our premier makes a $100-million deal with a foreign company owned by the odious Elon Musk and shuts out MDA Space, an Ontario company with a long history of successful space and satellite endeavours. Ford's current rabble-rousing stance on bike lanes follows his common diversionary tactic of creating a tempest in a teapot while he sneaks through deals for foreign billionaires.
Peter Pinch,Toronto
When will we see RCMP's report on Greenbelt?
RCMP rolls out body-worn cameras for officers nationally, Nov. 15
Some people question whether RCMP officers should wear body cameras, but the real question is, when will the RCMP release its investigation report into Doug Ford's Greenbelt land deals to his developer friends?
Al Yolles, Toronto
Have trustees never heard
of Zoom?
'It's appalling': Hawaii trip prompts Doug Ford to order audit of all Ontario school boards, Nov. 11
Trips to Italy, Hawaii and New Zealand on the taxpayer dime is completely outrageous. My thinking here is that staff retreats, and all other staff meetings and outreach gestures, should be held in the school cafeteria when not in use. Anyone heard of Zoom? These trustees should be required to pay back these funds as a warning to all future employees.
InderaNarain,Scarborough
Compulsory arbitration is the best solution
Canada Post workers are on strike. Here's what you need to know about your mail, Nov. 15
It's long overdue for many services in Canada to be designated essential services - namely the railroads, the commercial ports, sections of health care and Canada Post. Compulsory arbitration is a best solution if management and labour cannot resolve contract differences. A strike is not. Canada Post, which has lost approximately $500 million annually for the past few years due to poor management, is now on strike again causing serious problems for many people including those who receive wages, pensions and CRA communications by post. There already has been a significant shift by clients to other delivery services putting Canada Post in an even more precarious situation. This has been an issue for far too many years with no satisfactory solutions. Enough is enough.Common sense must prevail. Compulsory arbitration is the way to go. Get on with it.
Cameron Finley, Lindsay, Ont.

Document 157 of 500

Trump to figure in Ford's pitch to voters: Fears of economic turbulence will help make case for early election, sources say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-figure-fords-pitch-voters/docview/3129466128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+to+figure+in+Ford%27s+pitch+to+voters%3A+Fears+of+economic+turbulence+will+help+make+case+for+early+election%2C+sources+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians are expected to go to the polls this spring in an early election that Premier Doug Ford will contend is necessary due to the spectre of Donald Trump.
Thanks to the economic uncertainty triggered by a second Trump presidency, Ford's planned pitch to voters would be a simple one, Progressive Conservative sources told the Star.
"Stability, competence and someone experienced in dealing with Trump in order to protect jobs in Ontario," said one senior official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss an election strategy that is still being finalized.
While Ford already has a stable majority government and a provincial election is not scheduled until June 2026, his team believes the North American political landscape has so radically shifted that a fresh mandate is required.
The premier's New Democratic, Liberal and Green rivals insist he is just scrambling to get ahead of any charges that could be laid as a result of the RCMP's ongoing criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
They believe he wants to face voters well before that investigation, which began 13 months ago, is concluded.
Whatever the rationale for a snap election, Ford himself has already begun setting the table to make his economic argument in a campaign that could be launched as early as March.
After Trump was elected Nov. 5, the premier was quick to point out that Ontario did $493 billion in two-way trade with the U.S. last year and is "the No. 1 trading partner to 17 states, No. 2 to 11 other states."
"The (president-elect) knows that nine million Americans wake up every morning to produce products to ship to Ontario and we're their third-largest trading partner in the world," Ford told reporters on Nov. 6 as he expressed concern about threatened U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods.
"We're going to work with president-elect Trump, as we did at the beginning of the administration," he said, referring to the incoming president's previous tenure in the White House between 2017 and 2021.
When the U.S. slapped tariffs on steel during a 2019 trade war, Ford went to Washington to help the federal Liberal government make Canada's case to then U.S. trade representative Robert Lighthizer, who is expected to again play a key role in the Trump administration.
"It was a federal government that sent me down to negotiate with Bob Lighthizer since we're the engine of Canada," the premier reminded journalists on Nov. 12.
A week earlier, Ford had noted that dispute with the U.S. "didn't last too long, because - when I talked to Chrystia (Freeland, the then foreign minister, and I) went down there, talked to Lighthizer and I went back and said, 'Tariff everything' ... they pulled the tariffs off extremely quickly after we did this."
The premier's recent unexpected blast at Mexico, the third partner with Canada and the U.S. in NAFTA, for serving as a "back door" to allow artificially cheap Chinese electric vehicles into North America, was also not unhelpful to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government.
That's because, as leader of a subnational jurisdiction, insiders say Ford is free to speak his mind in a way that would be impolitic for Trudeau or Freeland.
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them," said the premier in a Nov. 12 trial balloon that, pointedly, was not shot down by the prime minister.
Indeed, Ford and Trudeau, who presides over a shaky minority government that could be toppled at any time, spoke by phone a few hours after the Mexico gambit.
It is likely that before a spring provincial election call, the premier will travel to Washington to meet with Trump administration officials to further burnish his credentials as a steady hand on the tiller in potentially stormy times.
"This is a smart strategy," said another senior Ontario official. "The premier can speak credibly to the Trump people because he's done so before. He can be a broker to a Liberal government in Ottawa that they (the Trump administration) might not trust."
In that vein, Ford has been appealing to the federal Liberals to delay the implementation of its controversial digital sales tax, a three per cent levy on U.S. tech giants like Apple, Google, Meta, Netflix and Amazon, as a show of goodwill toward the Americans.
"They're furious with Canada for even considering what they view as an unfair tax on American companies," he told 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon last month.
"We cannot put millions of Canadian jobs and our historic economic partnership with the U.S. at risk because of a stubborn refusal to listen to the concerns of our American friends."
Such lofty statesmanlike rhetoric aside, Ford is also making more prosaic decisions that signal an election could be called more than one year ahead of schedule.
Quietly, on the afternoon of Nov. 6, with media attention focused on the U.S. presidential contest, his government announced that public funding of Ontario political parties, which was to end Dec. 31, will be extended another two years.
The annual $2.54 per-vote subsidy of parties is paid out in quarterly instalments based on the results of the 2022 election.
Every three months, Ford's governing Tories, who handily out-fundraise their rivals, receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000.
While the premier had long opposed public funding of parties, sources say he changed his tune over concerns the NDP might not be raising enough money to mount a decent campaign that would siphon votes from the Liberals, thereby helping his Tories.
That's because Ford - apparently attempting to play a kind of three-dimensional electoral chess - wants to slightly boost Marit Stiles' official Opposition New Democrats, who are currently in third place in public opinion polls, in order to hinder Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, who sit second in most surveys.
Vote splitting between the NDP and the Grits is a factor in the Tories winning many ridings across the province.
In 2022, the NDP won 31 seats with 23.7 per cent of the popular vote while the Liberals elected just eight MPPs despite receiving 23.9 per cent. The Greens won one seat with six per cent of the vote. One Independent MPP was elected.
Ford's party took 83 seats in the 124-member legislature with 40.8 per cent of the vote.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 158 of 500

'We all need to push back on this': Caledon councillors oppose premier's fast-tracking of project after senior's heartfelt plea

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-all-need-push-back-on-this/docview/3129466104/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+all+need+to+push+back+on+this%27%3A+Caledon+councillors+oppose+premier%27s+fast-tracking+of+project+after+senior%27s+heartfelt+plea&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The shadow of Highway 413 has hovered over Angela Piscitelli's life for the past decade, but now the Caledon resident fears what Doug Ford's proposed legislation to fast-track the project could mean for her future.
She said for the past few years she has become "imprisoned" in her half-century home, which sits in the path of the new 52-kilometre highway: unable to sell, unable to get a building permit to renovate it to meet the needs of her ill husband - unable to plan ahead.
Now she worries that Bill 212, Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, intended to speed up the construction of the 413, could put her in an even more precarious situation.
"Seniors like us have been imprisoned for several years by the provincial government in homes that no longer meet our needs, and we have lost our freedom to sell our homes and been deprived of the value of our homes to make other arrangements," the usually reserved Piscitelli said in a moving deputation before Caledon councillors last week.
"No one wants to buy land designated for a highway," she added. "We are running out of time," she said later, in an interview with the Star.
She said she's now also concerned about the day the expropriation notice will arrive. She will be expected to empty out her home of five decades and leave in just 90 days, as is required for expropriations.The new 413 legislation doesn't allow landowners like her to petition a judge to delay that deadline.
"Could you do it?" she said, when asked by councillors if she had the means to leave.
"Imagine a palliative senior living in his or her own home for 50 years being asked to vacate during his final days or months," Piscitelli told Tuesday's meeting. "That senior could be me or my husband."
She urged the councillors to ask the province to consider the "plight of seniors before the bill is passed. They need time to be able to prepare for such a stressful move, and such an enormous lifestyle change."
After hearing Piscitelli's story, Caledon councillors on the general committee voted unanimously to oppose the Ford government's push to accelerate construction of the 413 under the Bill 212, which is expected to become law this month. Caledon council haslong remained steadfast in its support for the highway project.
With almost half of the highway to be built in Caledon, Coun. Doug Maskell said he felt compelled to bring the motion forward after studying the proposed bill, which he called "a significant attack on the rule of law" and "gross government overreach and intrusion."
In a plea to his colleagues, Maskell said his motion was not opposed to the highway in itself, but the means being used to get there.
"I'm asking you to vote against a proposed Highway 413 built across Caledon in the manner permitted by Bill 212," he told councillors.
Maskell said he sent his motion to other municipalities on the route of the highway, such as Halton Hills,Vaughanand Mississauga, which once opposed the 413 for environmental reasons, to take a stand against the way in which the province is now plowing ahead.
"We all need to push back on this together," he told the Star.
In addition to expropriation changes, Maskell said Bill 212 will also significantly expedite the environmental assessment process for the highway. The new route would cut through the sensitive Greenbelt, at-risk species habitat and waterways, allow for 24/7 construction, enact hefty penalties on those who resist leaving their property, and allow officials to keep secret any information they choose around the highway building process.
"How can we stand by while our residents ... are put in peril?" he told councillors. "Why should the residents of Caledon and the voiceless be subject to an environmental review process that has never been used in Ontario's history?"
But it was the deputation from Piscitelli, which appeared to move the councillors to take action.
"Remember you too will be a senior one day, and ask yourselves, what would you do, how would you feel if your government treated you this way?" Piscitelli told them.
At the meeting, Mayor Annette Groves said she "believes the province really dropped the ball" on Bill 212 given the lack of community consultation. She said she would send a letter to the province expressing councillors' opposition to the legislation.
Dakota Brasier, the spokesperson for the Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria's office said the "proposed accelerated process will build upon the considerable environmental studies and consultation efforts MTO has already undertaken to advance the project."
CAPTION: After hearing from a senior resident last week, Caledon councillors voted to oppose the Ford government's push to speed up construction of Highway 413 under Bill 212, which is set to become law this month.Cole Burston THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Subject: Legislation; Bills; Roads & highways

People: Ford, Doug

Title: 'We all need to push back on this':   Caledon councillors oppose premier's fast-tracking of project after senior's heartfelt plea

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Document 159 of 500

Crash victims' families slam premier's bike lane bill at vigil: Critics contend PCs' new legislation will put more lives at risk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crash-victims-families-slam-premiers-bike-lane/docview/3129466067/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crash+victims%27+families+slam+premier%27s+bike+lane+bill+at+vigil%3A+Critics+contend+PCs%27+new+legislation+will+put+more+lives+at+risk&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At an emotionally charged memorial Sunday evening, families of cyclists and pedestrians killed and injured on Ontario roads rallied at Queen's Park to express outrage over Premier Doug Ford's plan to limit bike lanes, legislation they say will put more lives at risk.
One of the statements read before a crowd of supporters holding candles and victims' pictures was a letter from the family of Alex Amaro, a 23-year-old cyclist killed on Dufferin Street in December 2020, which was addressed directly to Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria. "Minister Sarkaria, you say that this bike lane legislation is about giving people more time with their families. Our family would give anything to have more time with Alex, to hug her, to speak with her, to celebrate birthdays and life milestones, to see what a difference she would be making in this world today."
Last month the Progressive Conservative government introduced Bill 212, the "Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act," which if passed would require municipalities to get approval from the province to install new bike lanes if they removed a lane for vehicle traffic and would "review" existing ones. The province contends that the lanes are little used, and contribute to the city's multibillion-dollar gridlock problem.
Toronto city council voted Thursday to get information about their options to launch a legal challenge against it. Mayor Olivia Chow has highlighted the issue of safety in her opposition to the move, saying that "Literally, somebikelanessave lives."
Sunday's event was held on the World Day of Remembrance for Road Traffic Victims, a day designated by the United Nations to highlight the toll of traffic collisions.
More than half of the 1.35 million annual road traffic deaths worldwide are among "vulnerable road users," according to the UN, a group that includes cyclists, pedestrians and motorcyclists.
Jess Spieker of Friends and Families for Safe Streets, who organized the memorial, told the crowd that one statistic she had been prepared to read out - that since 2006, 1,017 people in Toronto had been killed in traffic crashes - was already out of date after another pedestrian died in the last 24 hours.
On Saturday evening, in separate incidents that occurred less than an hour apart, two pedestrians were struck by cars on the same two-kilometre stretch of Sheppard Avenue East.
One victim,a 64-year-old man, died, while the other,a 94-year-old woman, was transported to hospital with "life-threatening" injuries, according to police. Later that night,a childwas taken to hospital with minor injuries after being struck near Front and John Streets downtown.
Six cyclists have died on Toronto streets so far this year, a "significant increase" above the average of two to three per year over the last decade, according to a city staff report debated at council last week. According to the same report, the number of cyclists injured in collisions dropped by more than half after the installation of bike lanes on Bloor Street, from an average of about 20 a year to an average of about 15, despite big increases in the number of cyclists using the street. Pedestrian injuries dropped even more, from 13 a year to 5, and vehicle collision injuries also lowered, "reflecting the overall safety improvement for all road users," according to the report.
The Star reached out to Ford andSarkaria's offices for comment but they did not reply by deadline.
CAPTION: Sunday's event at Queen's Park was held on the World Day of Remembrance for Road Traffic Victims, a day designated by the United Nations to highlight the toll of traffic collisions.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Kate Allen Toronto Star
