Document 160 of 500

What will it take to shut Marineland?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-will-take-shut-marineland/docview/3129466047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+will+it+take+to+shut+Marineland%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How long do we have to keep talking about Marineland? How many more animals have to die at the Niagara Falls marine park before something is done to protect those still languishing there?
It's hard to imagine now what would have to happen to finally force Marineland to shut down for goodas yet another beluga whale has died at the park. This is the fifth beluga to die there this year, the 17th since 2019.
Dolphins, seals, penguins and Canada's last remaining captive orca, Kiska, have also all perished under Marineland's care in recent years. In March, the park was also found guilty of mistreating three black bears.
But for officials, this is seemingly not yet bad enough.
Mounting deaths at Marineland have raised concerns in recent years about water quality, sparking a government investigation. Melanie Milczynski, Ontario's chief animal welfare inspector, toldthe Canadian Pressthat enforcement officials visited the park 205 times since the province took over animal welfare enforcement from the Ontario Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals in 2020. She said that to her knowledge, the deaths are not linked to water quality. Though she also acknowledged the park's past failure to meet water standards, she insists that recent improvements meet acceptable limits.
But meeting water quality standards is really the bare minimum when it comes to caring for marine animals in captivity. We are talking about wild animals, most of whom were traumatically captured from their homes, being forced to live out the rest of their lives in tiny tanks, unable to engage in natural behaviours, until they get ill and die.Drone footageshot by advocacy group UrgentSeas in October, showed 32 belugas still remaining at the park. While Marineland has stated in the past that deaths of animals in their care are simply the cycle of life, animal advocates aren't buying it.
Lawyer and executive director of Animal Justice, Camille Labchuk, calls the current situation at the park "as dire as it has ever been," and says "we need urgent government action to protect these whales and get as many of them as possible to sanctuaries."
Anocean sanctuary for whalesis currently being developed off the east coast of Canada.
Adds Labchuk: "How long will the Ontario government stand on the sidelines while whales drop like flies at Marineland?"
NDP Leader Marit Stiles called the recent beluga death"disgraceful," adding, "they should have shut this place down years ago."
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie told City News, "This is a constant theme with this provincial government that there is no accountability." She asks: "What is the plan to ensure these beautiful mammals are being cared for properly and that they're not dying?"
Premier Doug Ford wants to turn Niagara Falls into the Las Vegas of the North, though the inclusion of Marinelandremains in question.
Erin Ryan, Wildlife Campaign Manager for World Animal Protection, says "If the Government of Ontario truly wants to transform the Niagara region, any plans for an amusement park and revitalization must not include animal entertainment, but must include a transition plan for Marineland."
Canada passed landmark legislation in 2019, banning the captivity of new whales and dolphins in the country. Animal Justice was influential in getting the law enacted, but today the group's director of legal advocacy, Kaitlyn Mitchell, says "it is truly shameful that after the monumental passage of Bill S-203, we as a society can do nothing but watch as the remaining belugas at Marineland die off one by one." Marineland is closed for the season after operating in limited capacity over the summer. It claims on its website that the park is "in a transitional phase," as it seeks new ownership.
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodatitold medialast year that he'd "like to see (Marineland) move right away from animals." The animals would surely agree.
Jessica Scott-Reid is a freelance journalist and independent animal advocate. She is also a regular contributor to Sentient.
CREDIT: Jessica Scott-Reid Contributor Jessica Scott-Reid is a freelance journalist and independent animal advocate. She is also a regular contributor to Sentient.;

Document 161 of 500

How a Toronto advocacy group's attack ads in a recent council race may have changed Toronto elections for good

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-toronto-advocacy-groups-attack-ads-recent/docview/3129215887/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+a+Toronto+advocacy+group%27s+attack+ads+in+a+recent+council+race+may+have+changed+Toronto+elections+for+good&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the most important players in this month's municipal byelection in Don Valley West wasn't on the ballot.
Rachel Chernos Lin was the one who handily beat conservative rival Anthony Furey in the closely-watched contest Nov. 4 to become Toronto's newest councillor. But no sooner had the votes been counted than left-wing advocacy group Progress Toronto claimed credit for Furey's defeat.
The organization boasted in a social media post that it had distributed thousands of flyers in the ward exposing the "truth" about Furey's record. "When voters learned the truth, they rejected him," it asserted.
The extent to which Progress Toronto swung the election for Chernos Lin is a matter for debate. But even Furey's team acknowledges the group likely played a role in his defeat.
The organization's influence in Ward 15, Don Valley West - powered by hundreds of volunteer hours and strategic anti-Furey attack ads - looks like the latest coup for the organization, which has helped elect a string of progressives to municipal office in the past six years. In the wake of the results, local conservative insiders are talking about establishing an organization of their own to challenge Progress Toronto, an effort that, if successful, could reshape how city elections are fought.
Coun. Brad Bradford, the centre-right representative for Ward 19, Beaches-East York, said that while officially there are no political parties at the municipal level, Progress Toronto has been effectively operating as one for years, building up supporters' lists and training volunteers. He's critical of the group's tactics, which he describes as aggressive and misleading, but said "to their credit, they've had some success."
Although more right-leaning groups have gotten involved in previous city elections, none have had the staying power of Progress Toronto. Bradford said he isn't directly involved in efforts to change that, and declined to say who was. But he said "I definitely have those conversations" about the need for a third-party organization to challenge Progress Toronto's messaging, "and many others do as well."
"I wouldn't be surprised if that materializes," he said.
Progress Toronto was founded in 2018, after the Ontario government introduced new rules for third parties that campaign or support candidates in municipal elections.
The groups are required to register with the city, stay within spending and contribution limits, and aren't allowed to co-ordinate with any candidates.
The brainchild of political organizer Michal Hay, Progress Toronto was envisioned as a standing political force that could help elect candidates who support the public investments the non-profit believes are necessary to create a more equitable city.
It scored success in 2022 when five of the nine progressives it endorsed for city council won their seats. In 2023's byelection to replace John Tory, the group endorsed Olivia Chow, helping her become Toronto's first left-leaning mayor in 13 years. Hay took a leave from Progress Toronto to direct Chow's campaign, and is now the mayor's chief of staff. Chow's office says it has no involvement in the group's activities.
Saman Tabasinejad, Progress Toronto's executive director, told the Star the organization got involved in the Ward 15 race because it believed that if elected, Furey, a former Postmedia pundit who came fourth in last year's mayoral byelection, would "embolden the right" across Toronto and block progressive policy at city hall.
The group didn't endorse Chernos Lin - who was backed by prominent Liberals like former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne - or any other candidate, and instead chose to use the less than $9,000 third parties were allowed to spend in the byelection to attack Furey's record. It enlisted almost 100 volunteers to deliver 22,000 flyers claiming Furey had "a history of pushing dangerous myths on COVID-19, Islam, (and) gender," among other accusations.
Tabasinejad said the group targeted parts of the ward where residents have historically voted for right-wing candidates in the belief that mainstream conservatives would be turned off by Furey's past statements.
It also focused on Thorncliffe Park, home to a significant Muslim community, delivering flyers in Urdu and English accusing Furey of spreading "hate about Muslims." They cited his comments after the 2017 Quebec City mosque shooting that a law to tackle Islamophobia could lead to a "compliant-with-Sharia law."
Furey has rejected accusations he holds discriminatory or hateful views against Muslims or any other group, and described Progress Toronto's allegations as "out-of-context smear attacks."
"It's rather sad that a group with the name Progress Toronto would choose to focus on negativity, fear mongering and lies," he said in an email to the Star. "While they may be pleased with themselves that they were able to frighten and misinform some people with their malicious attacks, make no mistake about it, there were many other people who weren't fooled by it and were disgusted by it," added Furey.
Jim Burnett, co-manager of Furey's campaign, said Progress Toronto's depictions of Furey as far-right were mischaracterizations. But he said the group's tactics were probably effective, if not in determining the byelection's winner, then at least in boosting Chernos Lin's margin of victory. She secured 55 per cent of the vote in a field of 16 candidates, a result he called "outstanding." Furey was second with about 31 per cent.
Burnett said third-party attack ads are difficult to fight against because municipal campaign spending caps are low. The limit for challengers in the Ward 15 race was less than $70,000, and he said candidates have to focus that money on countering their rivals' messaging. "But now here comes the third party that comes along with a new wrinkle and new accusations" and "you just don't have the resources to punch back," he said.
He also argued that third-party groups fuel more extreme campaign rhetoric. Because they're not on the ballot themselves, they can make statements a candidate courting votes might consider too risky. Burnett noted that the language Chernos Lin used to criticize Furey was much less harsh than Progress Toronto's.
Burnett stressed that there's nothing unfair about third-party groups taking part in elections, saying they're just following "the rules of the game."
But organizers on the middle and right of the political spectrum have to start considering whether "these are rules that they should also be engaged with," he said, adding that those conversations, largely informal until recently, have ramped up since the Ward 15 race.
Furey said he's involved in those talks. He told the Star that "people have reached out to me about their (interest) in organizing and funding an alternative group that presents a more forward-looking vision for the city." He added: "Stay tuned."
Myer Siemiatycki, professor emeritus of politics at Toronto Metropolitan University, said more third-party groups could be a positive thing for local democracy.
Public engagement with municipal elections is generally low, but multiple groups knocking on doors to endorse or oppose a candidate could raise awareness, and "stand a good chance of boosting voter turnout," he said. About one-third of eligible voters cast a ballot in the Ward 15 race, which is high for a city byelection.
Siemiatycki also predicted that having competing progressive and conservative groups align themselves with candidates could help weaken the power of incumbency, often considered an unfair advantage in local elections, by "(shifting) the focus away just from name recognition to what the candidates actually stand for."
Tabasinejad said she sees talk of political rivals forming a group to challenge Progress Toronto as a "compliment" to what it's accomplished through grassroots organizing. She rejected accusations the organization's attacks against Furey were misleading or unfair.
Not everyone sees Progress Toronto's influence in Ward 15 as decisive. Chernos Lin told the Star she believes she would have won if the group hadn't gotten involved. She said she had a base of support in the ward from previously being elected Toronto District School Board trustee, and her "positive, solutions-oriented campaign" resonated with voters.
"I find it interesting that everybody is trying to take credit for the hard work that my whole campaign put into this election," she said.
CAPTION: Saman Tabasinejad, executive director of Progress Toronto, said she sees talk of political rivals forming a group to challenge her organization as a "compliment" to what it's accomplished.

Subject: Conservatism; Local elections; Election results; Grass roots movement; Mayors; Third party; Negative campaigning; Candidates; Muslims; Voters; Volunteers; Political advertising; Voter behavior

Title: How a Toronto advocacy group's attack ads in a recent council race may have changed Toronto elections for good

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 162 of 500

The Toronto bike lane that went too far and sparked a backlash from Doug Ford's government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-bike-lane-that-went-too-far-sparked/docview/3129215875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Toronto+bike+lane+that+went+too+far+and+sparked+a+backlash+from+Doug+Ford%27s+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Between 8 a.m. and 9 a.m., on an unseasonably warm, sunny weekday in October, 18 cyclists travel in the eastbound bike lane on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke, in front of a church, where elderly men and women struggle to climb the stairs to their morning sermon.
Local resident and former Toronto city councillor John Campbell watches ruefully. He voted for the extension of the Bloor West bike lanes from Shaw Street to Runnymede Road when he represented what was then Ward 4 (Etobicoke Centre) from 2014 to 2018.
But this latest extension - from Runnymede to Aberfoyle Crescent to the west in 2023 and Aberfoyle to Resurrection Road this year - he cannot fathom and does not support.
"It's not like they're never used, but they're not used anywhere near enough to justify putting them in," said Campbell.
"This is city hall, telling people what is good for them."
One week later and 800 metres west, Mark Fernando, a founder of We Belong on Bloor, waves at the newest bike lanes, extolling their virtues as part of a "complete street," that includes traffic calming measures that make roads and sidewalks safer for everyone, including pedestrians.
The measures have reduced vehicular speeds along the corridor, according to the city.
Across from Tom Riley Park, where Fernando says his eight-year-old son was nearly hit by a car running a red light last year, speeds are down to 53 km/h from 64 km/h.
"The narrative is always 'the bike lanes,' " says Fernando. "I don't see this project as a bike-lanes project. I see it as being a Bloor complete-street project to improve safety overall across the project area."
Campbell and Fernando are residents of the same neighbourhood, but they couldn't be further apart on an issue that has riven members of the community and the city - for more than a year, finally resulting in the province proposing legislation that could affect whether and how every city in Ontario builds bike lanes.
Facts related to the Bloor Street bike lanes project in Etobicoke have been hotly contested, including how many cyclists use the lanes; whether the city's consultation process was robust and meaningful; how local businesses have been affected; whether the bike lanes have increased local congestion and by how much and whether the lanes are worsening emergency service response times.
Residents and businesses who have publicly opposed the bike lanes on Bloor West in Etobicoke have been harassed. Balance on Bloor founder Cody MacRae has been inundated by crank emails, including someone who told him he'd be "sorry" for making himself a public figure.
Sam Pappas, owner of the Crooked Cue, says more than two dozen one-star reviews for his business were posted to Google after a related press conference was held there in October. The reviews have since been removed.
"There's a lot of aggressive people who sit behind their computers and come after you. That's just the way the world is," says Pappas.
"That doesn't make it right."
It is this debate, over a 4.7-kilometre stretch of Bloor between Runnymede and Resurrection Roads, fought over the past year, that compelled Premier Doug Ford to introduce provincial legislation that will affect whether and how all cities in Ontario implement plans to install bike lanes - a welcome intervention for some, an unacceptable intrusion into municipal affairs for others.
How did Toronto's cycling plan, which seemed to have slipped into a smooth new gear after COVID, crash in Etobicoke?
Until it crossed the Humber River, the city's plan to install a network of 500 kilometres of bike lanes on major arterial routes by 2042 seemed to have finally achieved critical momentum. A master cycling network plan had been approved in 2016. In December 2021, about 30 kilometres of temporary bike lanes installed during COVID as part of the city's ActiveTO pandemic response were made permanent, and a 4.7-kilometre bike lane extension on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke was approved in principle by council.
In June 2023, council overwhelmingly voted to authorize installation of the Bloor West complete street project, which included removing two lanes of vehicular traffic along Bloor from Runnymede Road to Resurrection Road to add bike lanes on the north and south side of the street, along with other traffic calming measures.
The first stretch, from Runnymede to Aberfoyle Crescent, was to be built in 2023, the second stretch, from Aberfoyle to Resurrection Road, near Islington subway station, in 2024.
The city held numerous consultations on the Etobicoke project in 2023, but at that point, officials weren't asking the community whether the bike lanes should be built, they were explaining the plan and looking for suggestions on how to fine-tune it.
This led some residents to believe they weren't being meaningfully consulted and that the bike lanes were going in over their objections.
"Consultations are not a referendum," says Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto, adding that he believes the consultations are good-faith forums to get feedback on projects.
Longtime Etobicoke Coun. Stephen Holyday (Ward 2 Etobicoke-Centre) believes some residents were surprised by the bike lane construction because when the decision was made in 2021 to make the ActiveTO bike lanes permanent and expand the cycling network along Bloor St. W. in Etobicoke, they were obviously preoccupied - with COVID. Bike lane advocates were more aware of the process that was taking place and brought their influence to bear at city meetings and consultations.
Holyday notes that it's not unusual for special interest groups and residents' associations to be more aware of what is going on at city hall and be making suggestions and submissions.
Opponents complained that the bike lanes were hideous, destroyed the village feel of the local community, and made the area more hazardous for pedestrians, especially older people, who reported trouble navigating the added infrastructure. Some changes were made to reflect their concerns, but the city was not going to back down on the project. Neither were the opponents in Etobicoke.
The members of Balance on Bloor are no ordinary crowd, counting among their ranks members of the city's business elite and backroom political organizers on a first-name basis with the premier. Their influence also cuts across party lines. Politicians of all stripes from Jack Layton, who led the federal NDP before his death in 2011, to former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper, have swung through The Crooked Cue, for a pool game, to launch a campaign, or win votes.
Holyday believes another contributing factor to the backlash was a lack of local reporting on the issue. Etobicoke used to have two local papers covering things like development proposals and bike lanes.
"I think this is a major reason why citizens have become detached from their local government, and why so much comes as an unpleasant surprise to many," said Holyday.
He says the experience on Bloor West in Etobicoke has gone so wrong that it has soured residents on other cycling projects, even simple ones that would have no impact on traffic. He cites as an example a project on Mill Road that would have linked Centennial Park to a trail in Mississauga, without reducing any vehicular lanes.
"It all made logical sense, but there was a backlash in the community because they were so worried about what they saw on Bloor Street," says Holyday.
"It's making it harder for even noncontroversial projects to advance because of the stigma associated with bikes."
Campbell says that while bike lanes make sense in more densely populated neighbourhoods, they make less sense in Etobicoke, a former Toronto suburb, now a part of the city, with an aging population. He thinks the neighbourhood is just too far from downtown for the average person to turn in their transit pass or four tires for two wheels.
"The underlying assumption is that if the traffic gets bad enough, people are going to hop on their bikes. That's not a logical inference to me," says Campbell.
"The city has taken a one-size-fits-all approach to the bike lanes. And clearly, what's happened in Etobicoke was residents are saying, 'No, one size doesn't fit all.' "
The fact that residents who opposed bike lanes felt like what they thought didn't matter or was stupid - they were compared to flat-earthers - didn't win anyone over.
Residents were motivated by a sense of injustice, MacRae says. Balance on Bloor is a grassroots organization. Cycle Toronto, which lobbies the city in support of bike lanes, is a federally registered charity with reported revenues of nearly $500,000 in 2023, including $119,000 from local governments.
Cycle Toronto holds workshops to help members make effective presentations at city hall when the topic of bike lanes is under discussion.
Longfield believes Balance on Bloor was able to garner support because it was founded during a chaotic time - when the bike lanes were going in, before the city had an opportunity to fine-tune the project.
A defining characteristic of the battle over the Etobicoke bike lanes has been the inability of the two sides to agree on even basic facts, says Longfield.
He cites as an example the city's figures on travel times through the corridor that show an increase in motor vehicle times ranging from 1.5 minutes to 4.4 minutes, depending on the time of day and direction of travel.
The fact that those figures don't align with a person's subjective experience doesn't make the data untrue, Longfield says.
"It's hard for people to wrap their head around," he says.
Pappas and others say they have seen an uptick in the number of people cycling along Bloor, but not nearly enough to justify the major changes to the local roadways.
Some of the data so far has been less than robust.
The city collected data for one day before installing the bike lanes, and over three days afterward.
"For more reliable results, we usually recommend data from at least 3-5 continuous workdays, ideally excluding any days affected by environmental (e.g., snow, rainfall) or operational (e.g., construction) factors," said Mudasser (Rafi) Seraj, founder and lead consultant, iVision Traffic Solution (iTS), an Alberta-based traffic engineering analytics firm.
Seraj reviewed the data for the Star.
The city also predicted that an additional benefit of the bike lanes would be a drop in vehicular volumes of between five and 15 per cent along the corridor. Traffic has gone down one per cent, Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation project design and management at the City of Toronto, told residents at a neighbourhood meeting held in October to inform the community about the plan for bike lanes.
"Given current trends in population growth, it (was) optimistic to anticipate such a decrease on a major arterial road," said Seraj.
For supporters of the bike lanes, extending Toronto's cycling network is not just about how many people use bike lanes today. It's about providing the infrastructure that will encourage more people to use the lanes over time, a stated city objective.
"It takes time for people to adjust their travel habits and acclimatize to changes," the city wrote in response to questions from the Star.
"The city continues to monitor the area and will publish a report by the end of the year."
CAPTION: Bike lanes on Bloor West that stretch into Etobicoke have been at the centre of local and provincial debate. Some drivers have railed against the bike lanes. Other residents said the combined traffic calming measures have made the roads and sidewalks safer.A bus stop ramp on Bloor Street West that asks bikes to yield to TTC riders as they get on the bus.Former Toronto councillor John Campbell stands outside the Royal York subway station on Bloor West, to observe how traffic is affected by bike lanes. He opposes the latest extension, from Runnymede to Aberfoyle Crescent and westward to to Resurrection Road.The bike lane on the north side of Bloor Street West in Etobicoke.

Subject: Conferences; Community; Traffic; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Title: The Toronto bike lane that went too far and sparked a backlash from Doug Ford's government

Document 163 of 500

Insiders say Doug Ford will use the spectre of Trump to justify an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/insiders-say-doug-ford-will-use-spectre-trump/docview/3129215832/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Insiders+say+Doug+Ford+will+use+the+spectre+of+Trump+to+justify+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians are expected to go to the polls this spring in an early election that Premier Doug Ford will contend is necessary due to the spectre of Donald Trump.
Thanks to the economic uncertainty triggered by a second Trump presidency, Ford's planned pitch to voters would be a simple one, Progressive Conservative sources told the Star.
"Stability, competence and someone experienced in dealing with Trump in order to protect jobs in Ontario," said one senior official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss an election strategy that is still being finalized.
While Ford already has a stable majority government and a provincial election is not scheduled until June 2026, his team believes the North American political landscape has so radically shifted that a fresh mandate is required.
The premier's New Democratic, Liberal and Green rivals insist he is just scrambling to get ahead of any charges that could be laid as a result of the RCMP's ongoing criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
They believe he wants to face voters well before that investigation, which began 13 months ago, is concluded.
Whatever the rationale for a snap election, Ford himself has already begun setting the table to make his economic argument in a campaign that could be launched as early as March.
After Trump was elected Nov. 5, the premier was quick to point out that Ontario did $493 billion in two-way trade with the U.S. last year and is "the number one trading partner to 17 states, number two to 11 other states."
"The (president-elect) knows that nine million Americans wake up every morning to produce products to ship to Ontario and we're their third-largest trading partner in the world," Ford told reporters Nov. 6 as he expressed concern about threatened U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods.
"We're going to work with President-elect Trump, as we did at the beginning of the administration," he said, referring to the incoming president's previous tenure in the White House between 2017 and 2021.
When the U.S. slapped tariffs on steel during a 2019 trade war, Ford went to Washington to help the federal Liberal government make Canada's case to then U.S. trade representative Robert Lighthizer, who is expected to again play a key role in the Trump administration.
"It was a federal government that sent me down to negotiate with Bob Lighthizer since we're the engine of Canada," the premier reminded journalists on Nov. 12.
A week earlier, Ford had noted that dispute with the U.S. "didn't last too long, because - when I talked to Chrystia (Freeland, the then foreign minister, and I) went down there, talked to Lighthizer and I went back and said, 'tariff everything' ... they pulled the tariffs off extremely quickly after we did this."
The premier's recent unexpected blast at Mexico, the third partner with Canada and the U.S. in NAFTA, for serving as a "backdoor" to allow artificially cheap Chinese electric vehicles into North America, was also not unhelpful to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government.
That's because, as leader of a subnational jurisdiction, insiders say Ford is free to speak his mind in a way that would be impolitic for Trudeau or Freeland.
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them," said the premier in a Nov. 12 trial balloon that, pointedly, was not shot down by the prime minister.
Indeed, Ford and Trudeau, who presides over a shaky minority government that could be toppled at any time, spoke by phone a few hours after the Mexico gambit.
It is likely that before a spring provincial election call, the premier will travel to Washington to meet with Trump administration officials to further burnish his credentials as a steady hand on the tiller in potentially stormy times.
"This is a smart strategy," said another senior Ontario official. "The premier can speak credibly to the Trump people because he's done so before. He can be a broker to a Liberal government in Ottawa that they (the Trump administration) might not trust."
In that vein, Ford has been appealing to the federal Liberals to delay the implementation of its controversial digital sales tax, a three-per-cent levy on U.S. tech giants like Apple, Google, Meta, Netflix and Amazon, as a show of goodwill toward the Americans.
"They're furious with Canada for even considering what they view as an unfair tax on American companies," he told 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon last month.
"We cannot put millions of Canadian jobs and our historic economic partnership with the U.S. at risk because of a stubborn refusal to listen to the concerns of our American friends."
Such lofty statesmanlike rhetoric aside, Ford is also making more prosaic decisions that signal an election could be called more than one year ahead of schedule.
Quietly, on the afternoon of Nov. 6, with media attention focused on the U.S. presidential contest, his government announced that public funding of Ontario political parties, which was to end Dec. 31, will be extended another two years.
The annual $2.54 per-vote subsidy of parties is paid out in quarterly installments based on the results of the 2022 election.
Every three months, Ford's governing Tories, who handily out-fundraise their rivals, receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000.
While the premier had long opposed public funding of parties, sources say he changed his tune over concerns the NDP might not be raising enough money to mount a decent campaign that would siphon votes from the Liberals, thereby helping his Tories.
That's because Ford - apparently attempting to play a kind of three-dimensional electoral chess - wants to slightly boost Marit Stiles' official opposition New Democrats, who are currently in third place in public-opinion polls, in order to hinder Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, who sit second in most surveys.
Vote-splitting between the NDP and the Grits is a factor in the Tories winning many ridings across the province.
In 2022, the NDP won 31 seats with 23.7 per cent of the popular vote while the Liberals elected just eight MPPs despite receiving 23.9 per cent. The Greens won one seat with six per cent of the vote. One Independent MPP was elected.
Ford's party took 83 seats in the 124-member legislature with 40.8 per cent of the vote.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends question period as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Election results; Trade agreements; Scandals; Voters; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 164 of 500

The battle of Bloor Street: How one stretch of road in Etobicoke became ground zero in the debate over bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/battle-bloor-street/docview/3129110167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+battle+of+Bloor+Street%3A+How+one+stretch+of+road+in+Etobicoke+became+ground+zero+in+the+debate+over+bike+lanes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Between 8 a.m. and 9 a.m., on an unseasonably warm, sunny weekday in October, 18 cyclists travel in the eastbound bike lane on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke, in front of a church, where elderly men and women struggle to climb the stairs to their morning sermon.
Local resident and former Toronto city councillor John Campbell watches ruefully. He voted for the extension of the Bloor West bike lanes from Shaw Street to Runnymede Road when he represented what was then Ward 4 (Etobicoke Centre) from 2014 to 2018.
But this latest extension - from Runnymede west to Aberfoyle Crescent in 2023, and from Aberfoyle to Resurrection Road this year - he cannot fathom and does not support.
"It's not like they're never used, but they're not used anywhere near enough to justify putting them in," said Campbell.
"This is city hall telling people what is good for them."
One week later and 800 metres west, Mark Fernando, a founder of We Belong on Bloor, waves at the newest bike lanes, extolling their virtues as part of a "complete street" that includes traffic-calming measures that make roads and sidewalks safer for everyone, including pedestrians.
The measures have reduced vehicular speeds along the corridor, according to the city.
Across from Tom Riley Park, where Fernando says his eight-year-old son was nearly hit by a driver running a red light last year, speeds are down to 53 kilometres per hour from 64 km/h.
"The narrative is always 'the bike lanes,'" says Fernando. "I don't see this project as a bike lanes project. I see it as being a Bloor complete-street project to improve safety overall across the project area."
Campbell and Fernando are residents of the same neighbourhood, but they couldn't be further apart on an issue that has riven members of the community and the city for more than a year, finally resulting in the province proposing legislation that could affect whether and how every city in Ontario builds bike lanes.
Facts related to the Bloor Street bike lanes in Etobicoke have been hotly contested, including how many cyclists use them; whether the city's consultation process was robust and meaningful; how local businesses have been affected; whether the bike lanes have increased local congestion and by how much; and whether the lanes are worsening emergency service response times.
Residents and businesses who have publicly opposed the bike lanes on Bloor West in Etobicoke have been harassed. Balance on Bloor founder Cody MacRae has been inundated by crank emails, including someone who told him he'd be "sorry" for making himself a public figure.
Sam Pappas, owner of the Crooked Cue, says more than two dozen one-star reviews for his business were posted to Google after a related press conference was held there in October. The reviews have since been removed.
"There's a lot of aggressive people who sit behind their computers and come after you. That's just the way the world is," says Pappas. "That doesn't make it right."
It is this debate, over a 4.7-kilometre stretch of Bloor between Runnymede and Resurrection roads, fought over the past year, that compelled Premier Doug Ford to introduce provincial legislation that will affect whether and how all cities in Ontario implement plans to install bike lanes - a welcome intervention for some, an unacceptable intrusion into municipal affairs for others.
How did Toronto's cycling plan, which seemed to have slipped into a smooth new gear after COVID-19, crash in Etobicoke?
Until it crossed the Humber River, the city's plan to install a network of 500 kilometres of bike lanes on major arterial routes by 2042 seemed to have finally achieved critical momentum. A master cycling network plan had been approved in 2016. In December 2021, about 30 kilometres of temporary bike lanes installed during COVID as part of the city's ActiveTO pandemic response were made permanent, and a 4.7-kilometre bike lane extension on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke was approved in principle by council.
In June 2023, council overwhelmingly voted to authorize installation of the Bloor West complete street project, which included removing two lanes of vehicular traffic along Bloor from Runnymede Road to Resurrection Road to add bike lanes on the north and south sides, along with other traffic-calming measures.
The first stretch, from Runnymede to Aberfoyle Crescent, was to be built in 2023; the second stretch, from Aberfoyle to Resurrection Road, near Islington subway station, in 2024.
The city held numerous consultations on the Etobicoke project in 2023, but at that point, officials weren't asking the community whether the bike lanes should be built; they were explaining the plan and looking for suggestions on how to fine-tune it.
This led some residents to believe they weren't being meaningfully consulted and that the bike lanes were going in over their objections.
"Consultations are not a referendum," says Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto, adding that he believes the consultations are good-faith forums to get feedback on projects.
Longtime Etobicoke Coun. Stephen Holyday (Ward 2, Etobicoke-Centre) believes some residents were surprised by the bike lane construction because when the decision was made in 2021 to make the ActiveTO bike lanes permanent and expand the cycling network along Bloor Street West in Etobicoke, they were obviously preoccupied - with COVID. Bike lane advocates were more aware of the process that was taking place and brought their influence to bear at city meetings and consultations.
Holyday notes that it's not unusual for special-interest groups and residents associations to be more aware of what is going on at city hall and be making suggestions and submissions.
Opponents complained that the bike lanes were hideous, destroyed the village feel of the local community, and made the area more hazardous for pedestrians, especially older people, who reported trouble navigating the added infrastructure. Some changes were made to reflect their concerns, but the city was not going to back down on the project. Neither were the opponents in Etobicoke.
The members of Balance on Bloor are no ordinary crowd, counting among their ranks members of the city's business elite and backroom political organizers on a first-name basis with the premier. Their influence also cuts across party lines. Politicians of all stripes, from Jack Layton, who led the federal NDP before his death in 2011, to former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper, have swung through the Crooked Cue, for a pool game, to launch a campaign, or win votes.
Holyday believes another contributing factor to the backlash was a lack of local reporting on the issue. Etobicoke used to have two local papers covering things like development proposals and bike lanes.
"I think this is a major reason why citizens have become detached from their local government, and why so much comes as an unpleasant surprise to many," said Holyday.
He says the experience on Bloor West in Etobicoke has gone so wrong that it has soured residents on other cycling projects, even simple ones that would have no impact on traffic. He cites as an example a project on Mill Road that would have linked Centennial Park to a trail in Mississauga, without reducing any vehicular lanes.
"It all made logical sense, but there was a backlash in the community because they were so worried about what they saw on Bloor Street," says Holyday.
"It's making it harder for even non-controversial projects to advance because of the stigma associated with bikes."
Campbell says that while bike lanes make sense in more densely populated neighbourhoods, they make less sense in Etobicoke, a former Toronto suburb, now a part of the city, with an aging population. He thinks the neighbourhood is just too far from downtown for the average person to turn in their transit pass or four tires for two wheels.
"The underlying assumption is that if the traffic gets bad enough, people are going to hop on their bikes. That's not a logical inference to me," says Campbell.
"The city has taken a one-size-fits-all approach to the bike lanes. And clearly, what's happened in Etobicoke was residents are saying, 'No, one size doesn't fit all.'"
The fact that residents who opposed bike lanes felt that what they thought didn't matter or was stupid - they were compared to flat-earthers - didn't win anyone over.
Residents were motivated by a sense of injustice, MacRae says. Balance on Bloor is a grassroots organization. Cycle Toronto, which lobbies the city in support of bike lanes, is a federally registered charity with reported revenues of nearly $500,000 in 2023, including $119,000 from local governments.
Cycle Toronto holds workshops to help members make effective presentations at city hall when the topic of bike lanes is under discussion.
Longfield believes Balance on Bloor was able to garner support because it was founded during a chaotic time - when the bike lanes were going in, before the city had an opportunity to fine-tune the project.
A defining characteristic of the battle over the Etobicoke bike lanes has been the inability of the two sides to agree on even basic facts, says Longfield.
He cites as an example the city's figures on travel times through the corridor that show an increase in motor vehicle times ranging from 1.5 minutes to 4.4 minutes, depending on the time of day and direction of travel.
The fact that those figures don't align with a person's subjective experience doesn't make the data untrue, Longfield says.
"It's hard for people to wrap their head around," he says.
Pappas and others say they have seen an uptick in the number of people cycling along Bloor, but not nearly enough to justify the major changes to the local roadways.
Some of the data so far has been less than robust.
The city collected data for one day before installing the bike lanes, and over three days afterward.
"For more reliable results, we usually recommend data from at least three to five continuous workdays, ideally excluding any days affected by environmental (e.g., snow, rainfall) or operational (e.g., construction) factors," says Mudasser (Rafi) Seraj, founder and lead consultant at iVision Traffic Solution, an Alberta-based traffic engineering analytics firm.
Seraj reviewed the data for the Star.
The city also predicted that an additional benefit of the bike lanes would be a drop in vehicular volumes of between five and 15 per cent along the corridor. Traffic has gone down one per cent, Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation project design and management at the City of Toronto, told residents at a neighbourhood meeting held in October to inform the community about the plan for bike lanes.
"Given current trends in population growth, it (was) optimistic to anticipate such a decrease on a major arterial road," says Seraj.
For supporters of the bike lanes, extending Toronto's cycling network is not just about how many people use it today. It's about providing the infrastructure that will encourage more people to use the lanes over time, a stated city objective.
"It takes time for people to adjust their travel habits and acclimatize to changes," the city wrote in response to questions from the Star.
"The city continues to monitor the area and will publish a report by the end of the year."
CREDIT: Francine Kopun Senior Writer

Title: The battle of Bloor Street:   How one stretch of road in Etobicoke became ground zero in the debate over bike lanes

Document 165 of 500

'We are running out of time': How one senior's heartfelt plea helped sway GTA councillors to oppose Doug Ford's fast-tracking of Highway 413

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-are-running-out-time-how-one-seniors-heartfelt/docview/3128965873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+running+out+of+time%27%3A+How+one+senior%27s+heartfelt+plea+helped+sway+GTA+councillors+to+oppose+Doug+Ford%27s+fast-tracking+of+Highway+413&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The shadow of the 413 has hovered over Angela Piscitelli's life for the past decade, but now the Caledon resident fears what Doug Ford's proposed legislation to fast-track the highway could mean for her future.
She said for the past few years she has become "imprisoned" in her half-century home, which sits in the path of the new 52-kilometre highway: unable to sell, unable to get a building permit to renovate it to meet the needs of her ill husband - unable to plan ahead.
Now she worries that Bill 212, Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, intended to speed up the construction of the 413, could put her in an even more precarious situation.
"Seniors like us have been imprisoned for several years by the provincial government in homes that no longer meet our needs, and we have lost our freedom to sell our homes and been deprived of the value of our homes to make other arrangements," the usually reserved Piscitelli said in a moving deputation before Caledon councillors this week..
"No one wants to buy land designated for a highway," she added. "We are running out of time," she said later, in an interview with the Star.
She said she's now also concerned about the day the expropriation notice will arrive. She will be expected to empty out her home of five decades and leave in just 90 days, as is required for expropriations. The new 413 legislation doesn't allow landowners like her to petition a judge to delay that deadline.
"Could you do it?" she said, when asked by councillors if she had the means to leave.
"Imagine a palliative senior living in his or her own home for 50 years being asked to vacate during his final days or months," Piscitelli told Tuesday's meeting. "That senior could be me or my husband."
She urged the councillors to ask the province to consider the "plight of seniors before the bill is passed. They need time to be able to prepare for such a stressful move, and such an enormous lifestyle change."
After hearing Piscitelli's story, Caledon councillors on the general committee voted unanimously to oppose the Ford government's push to accelerate construction of the 413 under the Bill 212, which is expected to become law this month. Caledon council has long remained steadfast in its support for the highway project.
With almost half of the highway to be built in Caledon, Coun. Doug Maskell said he felt compelled to bring the motion forward after studying the proposed bill, which he called "a significant attack on the rule of law" and "gross government overreach and intrusion."
In a plea to his colleagues, Maskell said his motion was not opposed to the highway in itself, but the means being used to get there.
"I'm asking you to vote against a proposed Highway 413 built across Caledon in the manner permitted by Bill 212," he told councillors.
Maskell said he sent his motion to other municipalities on the route of the highway, such as Halton Hills, Vaughan and Mississauga, which once opposed the 413 for environmental reasons, to take a stand against the way in which the province is now plowing ahead.
"We all need to push back on this together," he told the Star.
In addition to expropriation changes, Maskell said Bill 212 will also significantly expedite the environmental assessment process for the highway. The new route would cut through the sensitive Greenbelt, at-risk species habitat and waterways, allow for 24/7 construction, enact hefty penalties on those who resist leaving their property, and allow officials to keep secret any information they choose around the highway building process.
"How can we stand by while our residents ... are put in peril?" he told councillors. "Why should the residents of Caledon and the voiceless be subject to an environmental review process that has never been used in Ontario's history?"
But it was the deputation from Piscitelli, which appeared to move the councillors to take action.
"Remember you too will be a senior one day, and ask yourselves, what would you do, how would you feel if your government treated you this way?" Piscitelli told them.
At the meeting, Mayor Annette Groves said she "believes the province really dropped the ball" on Bill 212 given the lack of community consultation. She said she would send a letter to the province expressing councillors' opposition to the legislation.
Dakota Brasier, the spokesperson for the Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria's office said the "proposed accelerated process will build upon the considerable environmental studies and consultation efforts MTO has already undertaken to advance the project."
CAPTION: Doug Ford's Bill 212, Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, is intended to speed up the construction of Highway 413.

Subject: Legislation

Title: 'We are running out of time': How one senior's heartfelt plea helped sway GTA councillors to oppose Doug Ford's fast-tracking of Highway 413

Document 166 of 500

It's the $1 trillion economy, stupid: How Canadian business can stickhandle the looming Trump tariff reality

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/1-trillion-economy-stupid-how-canadian-business/docview/3128965870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+the+%241+trillion+economy%2C+stupid%3A+How+Canadian+business+can+stickhandle+the+looming+Trump+tariff+reality&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a challenge familiar to many businesses: your biggest corporate customer has a new boss who wants to re-evaluate everything.
It's even harder when they start with a bias that everyone wants to rip them off.
That's Canada's challenge as Donald Trump returns to the White House in January.
For business, the campaign was alarming. We witnessed disdain for international alliances, and promises of tariffs and trade wars. We heard anti-immigrant rhetoric and a proposal to deport some 11 million people, which would damage America's economy and risk a migrant crisis at our border. We see America going down an unsustainable, inflationary fiscal path, with deep tax cuts while deficits are at levels usually reserved for recessions or wars.
These things can seem abstract, so let's translate them into everyday realities: Imagine a car costing $8,000 more because of tariffs. Imagine running a business that depends on imports that have become prohibitively expensive. Imagine the agony of shutting down a plant or laying off workers in Ontario, Illinois, Michigan or Pennsylvania.
That's the reality when our two countries have the world's largest bilateral trading relationship. In Ontario alone, some $1 billion of goods and services cross the border every day. Across Canada, it's $3.6 billion a day. That makes the annual economic stakes to the U.S. and Canada more than $1 trillion.
The landscape is harsh but not hopeless. Campaigning and governing are very different processes - even for populists. Further, Canada has economic reality on its side. We have influential U.S. business allies. And we have products, services and capabilities of enormous value.
Here are the top ten things we can do:
Present a united front
In both approach and message, we need alignment between provincial and federal governments, opposition leaders, and the business community.
Together, we have a vast range of relationships - in Washington, in state capitals, with chambers of commerce, and with countless businesses.
Make it 'North America first'
We must consider the world the new administration inherits, and the forces it fears. Canada's strategy - previewed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford - should include proactive proposals to strengthen North America's economy and security at a time of shifting alliances and threats from America's rivals.
It's (still) the economy, stupid
For the second time in eight years, Trump will inherit a thriving U.S. economy.
Job one is not to mess it up just as ordinary people start to feel it. He needs to hear from the business community that America's prosperity depends on an open market that embraces innovation and competition.
The reality of integration
Given global trends and Trump's predispositions, tariffs are inevitable. However, they need not be universal. Given the integration between our economies and supply chains, there's an airtight case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely, and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement in 2026.
Invest in security
While America's southern border took centre stage in the election, Canada's concern is the one we share - and our own northern border with Russia.
We must strengthen our commitment to North American and global security, and meet our defence spending commitments.
Share our assets
We can make a powerful case that Canada has assets the U.S. needs: energy. Minerals. Nuclear power. AI research excellence. Doing business with us is strategically smarter than the alternatives, notably China.
Be competitive
We must ensure the cost of investing and doing business in Ontario and Canada remains competitive with the U.S. Investment will go where it's wanted, welcomed and rewarded. And with America going through uncertainty, we can attract investment in Canada, and the jobs that come with it.
Attract top talent
Canada must double down on its investments in research at our post-secondary institutions. During the first Trump presidency, we learned that when the U.S. is less welcoming to international research talent, Canada is an outstanding alternative - provided that our institutions have the funding to do the work.
Diversify our markets over time
While every business must look after its biggest customer, it must also grow business elsewhere. Canada has more trade agreements than any other G7 country; we need to leverage them. Within Canada, we also need to eliminate interprovincial trade barriers, benefiting businesses across the country.
Articulate Canadian values
The best of friends can disagree, so let's be unapologetic in standing for Canadian values.
This does not mean lecturing or vacuous virtue-signalling.
It means acting with humility; being reliable partners in international alliances; celebrating the contributions of immigrant communities; meeting our climate commitments; and upholding democracy, security and the rule of law - particularly through unflagging support for Ukraine.
Imagine, once more, that you're that business worried about its relationship with its biggest customer.
Would you sit and wait for them to make decisions on their own? Or would you take the initiative?
The smart business does the latter - and is more likely to hold the Trump card.
CAPTION: While Trump tariffs appear inevitable, writes Daniel Tisch, they needn't be universal. There's a strong case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement in 2026.
CREDIT: Daniel Tisch Contributor

Document 167 of 500

A politician with no enemies? How Lincoln Alexander became Canada's Great Consensus Maker

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/politician-with-no-enemies-how-lincoln-alexander/docview/3128965842/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+politician+with+no+enemies%3F+How+Lincoln+Alexander+became+Canada%27s+Great+Consensus+Maker&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've met a lot of politicians in my day and I can honestly say that Lincoln Alexander was the finest by far.
Among his many qualities, he's the only one I ever knew who had no enemies. None. He managed to work his way higher in life without stepping on anyone. Alexander's father, from St. Vincent and the Grenadines, was a CPR sleeping car porter.
His mother, a maid born in Jamaica, insisted her son, born in Toronto, get a good education, telling him, "Go to school, you're a little black boy." He graduated from McMaster University, then Osgoode Hall law school, becoming one of a handful of Black lawyers in Ontario in the 1960s.
But even at Osgoode, he suffered the slur of racism. During an event in his final year, a speaker referred to something as being the equivalent of "looking for a n--r in the woodpile." When questions were invited, Alexander stood up, cited the offensive words, and asked what exactly did the phrase mean? The speaker shook it off by saying that everyone used those words. Alexander told him that as someone in a position of authority, he should be showing leadership, and not be so disrespectful.
Fortunately, voters were more positive. In 1968, Alexander successfully ran for election as a Progressive Conservative in Hamilton, Ont., becoming an MP for twelve years as the first Black member of Parliament and the first Black cabinet minister.
As press secretary to Robert Stanfield in the early 1970s, I attended weekly caucus. At times, debate would get so heated I'd wonder if unity on that topic would ever be possible. And then Alexander, all six-foot-three-inches of him, would rise in his place, and say, "I can sense a consensus emerging here."
I'd think to myself, "Whaaaat?" Alexander would then take a few strands from this diatribe, a few threads from that tirade, and so on, until he had woven together a garment that everyone could wear. The strength of any political party flows from its ability to reach consensus. Alexander's leadership qualities could achieve that goal like few others.
I also watched him from the gallery in the House of Commons on that infamous day in February 1971 when Alexander and John Lundrigan, a Progressive Conservative MP from Newfoundland, questioned prime minister Pierre Trudeau about training programs for the unemployed. Exasperated by their rigorous inquisition, Trudeau mouthed the words "F-k off" at them, claiming later that all he said was "Fuddle duddle." Alexander knew exactly what he said.
In the 1990s, I was involved in organizing an event to honour the philanthropic efforts benefitting Ryerson (now Metropolitan University of Toronto) by Jack Cockwell, president of Brookfield Asset Management. Among my assignments was obtaining a suitable congratulatory message from Alexander. I called him at his Hamilton home, and asked if he would come to Toronto to film a tribute interview in front of the Ontario Legislature where he had served as lieutenant governor for six years.
Just as we were just completing our filming with the pink granite building behind him aglow in the morning sun, two busloads of students arrived, presumably for a tour.
As it turned out, they were from Hamilton. The teachers recognized Alexander and introduced him to the kids who were all about eight or nine years old. The cameraman and I organized everybody into three rows so we could get some additional footage of Alexander surrounded by the children. I then withdrew but even from where I stood about ten metres away, I could hear Alexander saying in such a low voice that it was almost as if he were speaking to himself: "Vote for Alexander, vote for Alexander."
Filming finished, as he bid goodbye to the students, he said, "When you go home tonight, don't forget to tell your parents you saw Linc Alexander today." He was ever the campaigner, even though he hadn't run for public office in about fifteen years.
Subsequently, I'd see him arriving in a wheelchair to watch the Raptors. We always had an enjoyable chat before the game. On other occasions, when I visited my daughter Alison in Hamilton and we'd pick up a few things at Denninger's Foods, we'd see Alexander, having his lunch, constantly interrupted by well-wishers.
On Jan. 21, Lincoln Alexander Day across Canada, a bust of Alexander was unveiled at the Ontario Legislature. It's certainly unusual. Rather than the normal grey, it's taupe with aspects of green. The glasses are too big and the mouth is open rather than closed as is traditional for a bust.
If Alexander were alive, I can't imagine he'd be upset. For most politicians, an ego trip is the only journey worth taking. Not Lincoln Alexander. He cared more about walking with others rather than leading any parade.
CAPTION: Erika and Marissa Alexander, look at a bust of their grandfather, during its unveiling this year on Jan. 21, Lincoln Alexander Day. For most politicians, writes Rod McQueen, an ego trip is the only journey worth taking. Not Alexander, who cared more about walking with others rather than leading any parade.Lincoln Alexander broke through many barriers, writes Rod McQueen, becoming Canada's first Black MP and cabinet minister en route to being made Lt.-Gov. for Ontario in 1985

Taylor Swift charade shows how much our society values individual gain and not the collective good

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/taylor-swift-charade-shows-how-much-our-society/docview/3128944362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Taylor+Swift+charade+shows+how+much+our+society+values+individual+gain+and+not+the+collective+good&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto fans wait hours for Taylor Swift merch ahead of Eras Tour: 'There is no budge,' Nov. 13
Young fans are spending $1,000 to $12,000 per ticket per show to see Taylor Swift in Toronto this weekend and next weekend. Hotels are charging an average of $1,600 per room. That fans would pay that amount of money for a ticket is obscene. Tens of thousands of hungry people are lining up at food banks in this city. Hundreds more are homeless and are sleeping on the street or in encampments. The fact that Taylor Swift's concerts are sold out shows that there is a lot of disposable income to go around and a tremendous amount of privilege. It's not that the money isn't there to solve our socio-economic problems, it's that it isn't equitable distributed. This whole Taylor Swift charade shows to me just how much our values as a society are focused on individual gain and not on the collective good.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto, ON
Blaming Swift for Doug Ford's FailureHow Swifties were short-shrifted by their hero, Nov. 13
Mark McQueen blames Taylor Swift for the extortionate reseller ticket prices for her concerts in Toronto. A quick look at recent history shows that it was the current Progressive Conservative provincial government in 2019 that scrapped the section of the Ticket Sales Act (brought in by the previous government) that would have limited the resale price to 50 per cent above a ticket's face value. McQueen mentions that seeing Swift perform in Europe would have been cheaper, even with the added costs of flights, while neglecting to mention the reason, that European legislation does what Ontario's won't: limit resale prices. I guess it's easier to blame a foreign artist than it is to acknowledge our own government's failure to look out for our interests.
Howard To, Markham
CAPTION: With Taylor Swift now in Toronto and her Eras Tour kicking off on Thursday, an inflatable bracelet adorns the south side of the Rogers Centre.

Our health care takes a back seat to beer sales ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/our-health-care-takes-back-seat-beer-sales/docview/3128944361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+health+care+takes+a+back+seat+to+beer+sales+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Our health care takes a back seat
to beer sales
Home care supply shortage due to mistakes by Ontario Health and Ontario Health atHome, sources say, Nov. 11
As a home-care patient since undergoing major surgery in July, I have experienced health supply shortages firsthand. In spite of the best efforts of the dedicated home-care providers it's been my good fortune to have on my case, they have been hamstrung by the province's screw-up when, in its infinite wisdom, it changed the procurement and distribution system. I have either received the wrong supplies or not received them at all. On behalf of all Ontario home-care patients affected by this incompetence, I can assure you this is a frightening situation. The health minister should be held accountable.We are witnessing corporate pharmacies milk our anorexic health-care dollars to meet their billing quotas on unnecessary procedures, to enhance their bottom lines. It's quite obvious that health care for Ontarians is taking a back seat to this government's priorities - beer in convenience stores, ripping out bike lanes, $200 vote purchases costing $3.8 billion in our money and the list goes on. Shame on both of you, Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones. Ontario deserves better.
Michelle Simson, Hastings, Ont.
Let's remove
everything that's
not in constant use
Doug Ford wants to rip up bike lanes - but how many people in Toronto actually cycle?Nov. 13
While I was biking in Toronto the other day, I thought of Premier Doug Ford. I was on Dundas Street and I saw an empty parking spot. Are you aware, premier, that there are empty parking spots in this city that are going unused? If you eliminate parking on major arteries in Toronto, think of the reduction in traffic you would see. Come to think of it, I saw several side streets that were empty of cars, they must just not get used. You could probably close those, too.
From Dundas, I turned up University Avenue. I saw an ambulance that was empty. Probably not getting used. Think of the reduction in commuter transit time you will save by removing those pesky emergency services. Who cares about making the city safer, right? Think of it: ridding the city of ambulances would help with the overburdened emergency rooms of the province - less emergent care, less wait time.
Wow, look at all that time I just saved you. If you like my ideas, call anytime. I have other great ideas for effective ways to deal with what others have claimed are very complex issues.
FelixBeaudry,Toronto
What price will Ontarians pay
for Highway 413?
Doug Ford wants to put the pedal to the metal to build Ontario's Highway 413. Here's why that may not work, Nov. 12
Even though Premier Doug Ford and Transportation MinisterPrabmeetSarkaria have tabled Bill 212 to fast-track the construction of Hwy 413, they still have not told Ontarians how much this highway is going to cost. What would go into figuring the costs anyway? Would it include the costs of expropriating peoples' homes? How about the health-care costs from increased particulates in the air? Or the cost of food security as farmers' fields are turned to asphalt? Then there's the costs of paving over Greenbelt lands. How much would that cost? Would these priceless things be included in the cost of building this unwanted highway? They should be. Their loss will cost us all.
VictoriaCreese, Stouffville
Recent interfaith symposium showed the way to harmony
There's a lot more that unites us than divides us. We must pull together to talk about what unites us, especially in the increasingly divided world we live in.
At the same time, differing opinions are healthy. They lead to growth. A willingness to hear differing beliefs is the hallmark of a democratic society. A tolerant society. A vibrant society.
I saw this on full display at the interfaith symposium I attended last month, one of the many interfaith symposiums held across the country in October to mark Islamic Heritage Month. It was hosted by the Ahmadiyya Muslim Women's Association.
Speakers from different faiths spoke one after the other on the same topic: "Suffering through the lens of my faith." Compelling arguments on how to deal with suffering were made that took into consideration different religious teachings but the underlying theme was the same. We are loved by the Being that created us, and He will and does indeed help people when they turn to Him.
A lot more interfaith symposiums need to happen. They need to become part of the fabric of society, as while they give food for thought, they radiate a bright and harmonious future for us. Together.
Maria Mehmood, Toronto
A dark future lurks
for us humans
This Ontario student accused of cheating was flagged by an AI detection program. But the software isn't always right, Nov. 10
The Star reports that a Grade 11 student has been convicted by a computer. Artificial intelligence, used by her teacher to grade student essays "decided" that she has plagiarized the work of others, and has assigned a mark of zero. No amount of reason or discussion could get it changed. No live person would do further evaluating.
Is this our future? Are our lives to be decided by programmed computers using algorithms and artificial intelligence?
Have there not been several terrifying science-fiction movies based on this future world? This bodes for a truly dark and terrifying future for humans.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington

Full text: Home care supply shortage due to mistakes by Ontario Health and Ontario Health atHome, sources say, Nov. 11
As a home-care patient since undergoing major surgery in July, I have experienced health supply shortages firsthand. In spite of the best efforts of the dedicated home-care providers it's been my good fortune to have on my case, they have been hamstrung by the province's screw-up when the province, in their infinite wisdom, changed their procurement and distribution system. I have experienced either receiving the wrong supplies or not receiving them at all. On behalf of all Ontario home-care patients affected by this incompetence, I can assure you this is a frightening situation. The health minister should be held accountable. We are witnessing corporate pharmacies milk our anorexic health-care dollars to meet their billing quotas on unnecessary procedures, to enhance their bottom lines. It's quite obvious that health care for Ontarians is taking a back seat to this government's priorities - beer in convenience stores, ripping out bike lanes, $200 vote purchases costing $3.8 billion in our money and the list goes on. Shame on both of you, Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones. Ontario deserves better.
Michelle Simson, Hastings, ON
