A dark future for humans
This Ontario student accused of cheating was flagged by an AI detection program. But the software isn't always right, Nov. 10
The Star reports that a Grade 11 student, Marissa, has just been convicted by a computer. Artificial Intelligence, used by her teacher to grade student essays has "decided" that she has plagiarized the work of others, and has assigned a mark of zero. No amount of reason or discussion could get it changed. No live person would do further evaluating. Is this our future? Are our lives to be decided by programmed computers using Algorithms and Artificial Intelligence? Have there not been several terrifying Science Fiction movies based on this future world? This bodes for a truly dark and terrifying future for humans.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
Math shows half of crossing guard budget goes to overhead costs, not salariesCity councillors stopping to look both ways in bid to rein in crossing guard costs, Nov. 12
It's hard to be an informed citizen without all the data on a topic - and we never seem to get all the data. But let's do some math with what we have. In Toronto there are 882 crossing guards earning $17.91 an hour cost the city about $15,800 an hour. A total budget of $30 million will thus provide almost 1,900 hours of crosswalk coverage every year. With 195 school days a year, crosswalks should therefore be covered for 9 1/2 hours a day. I don't know about other neighbourhoods, but here in Riverside we get considerably less coverage than 9 1/2 hours - probably about half that. So, about half the costs are wages for crossing guards and the rest is other costs - overhead. This is where data on other costs would come in handy. Alas, the article provides none. Even so, with about 50 per cent of the costs in overhead, it sure looks like there's a lot of waste in the program.
John Gavin, Toronto
Interfaith symposiums need to be the way of the future
There's a lot more that unites us than what divides us. We must pull together to talk about what unites us, especially in the increasingly divided world we live in. At the same time, differing opinions are healthy. They lead to growth. A willingness to hear differing beliefs is the hallmark of a democratic society. A tolerant society. A vibrant society. I saw this on full display at the interfaith symposium I attended last month, one of the many interfaith symposiums held across the country in October to mark Islamic Heritage Month. It was hosted by the Ahmadiyya Muslim Women's Association. Speakers from different faiths spoke one after the other on the same topic: "Suffering through the lens of my faith." Compelling arguments on how to deal with suffering were made that took into consideration different religious teachings but the underlying theme was the same. We are loved by the Being that created us, and He will and does indeed help people when they turn to Him. A lot more interfaith symposiums need to happen. They need to become part of the fabric of society, as while they give food for thought, they radiate a bright and harmonious future for us. Together.
Maria Mehmood, Toronto
CAPTION: A spokesperson for Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones, pictured in 2023, says the minister has directed the chair and CEO of Ontario Health atHometo "utilize whatever means and resources necessary to resolve this situation as quickly as possible."

Celebrating the future while saluting the past: WNBA expansion announcement included national players who never got to be pros at home

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/celebrating-future-while-saluting-past/docview/3128944065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Celebrating+the+future+while+saluting+the+past%3A+WNBA+expansion+announcement+included+national+players+who+never+got+to+be+pros+at+home&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=S.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Each year, the sports department contributes to the Toronto Star Santa Claus Fund via Proudfoot Corner. The drive allows children from financially disadvantaged families across the GTA to enjoy a gift box Christmas morning.
Proudfoot Corner is our long-standing tradition of recognizing donors. And while we do, we'll take a behind-the-scenes look at some of the stories our journalists worked on.
The official announcement in May of a WNBA expansion team in Toronto was a perfect opportunity to think about the future ramifications.
It was, as many of us wrote, a chance to be the Carter Effect moment for the women's game. The 2026 Toronto team should be a perfect springboard here and across Canada. We saw what the arrival of the Raptors in 1995, and Vince Carter four years later, did for the NBA and the men's game in this country.
I don't know whether the Toronto franchise will win a WNBA championship - it took the New York Liberty 28 seasons to win their first title, beating the Minnesota Lynx in a compelling series this fall - but at least the chance exists, and who knows who might make an impact similar to what Carter did two decades ago.
When I was at that glitzy news conference at the downtown Toronto hotel, it was easy to think about the history of the women's game in this country, and how bygone eras established a record of international success.
These were women I've written about for decades, the trailblazers in Canada. One of the enduring memories of that morning was watching them bask in the WNBA arrival, all proud of the part they'd played. Those in attendance included:
Sylvia Sweeney, once one of the top handful of women's players on the planet.
Tammy Sutton-Brown, a WNBA all-star and one of Canada's best and underappreciated players.
Stacey Dales, a true great in her time and a 2000 Olympian.
Tamara Tatham, a two-time Olympian now giving back as a high-level coach.
It was good, and important,- to see them gathered to usher in the future after the impact they had on the past. The stars of tomorrow are standing on the shoulders of those greats who helped lay the groundwork for success.
Sure, it was predictable that the politicians- Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow among them - showed up to get on the bandwagon. (Having to be at the venue two hours early for a mandatory security check took some of the good feeling away.) But the moment was, deservedly, about women's basketball.
"I just got chills," Sutton-Brown told me. "Who would have thought? Dreams really do come true."
Said Tatham: "I looked up to Michael Jordan growing up, the New York Knicks, the Chicago Bulls... now these young girls are going to get to see women in their country playing the sport they love. You get to look up to people, you get to look up to women. I think that's an amazing opportunity for our country and athletes."
I think it's long overdue and full credit to Larry Tanenbaum for making it happen.
But seeing those greats from the past, recognized for getting the game to where it is today, was a great part of the story.
On the Corner
We start off with Jim Dadson,a Proudfoot donor for more than two decades. This year, he gives $105 in honour of John Faveri, long-time member of theThursday night "Write Offs" ... There's always a lot of support from former Toronto Star family. This week: $200 from Bob Hepburn, $105 from Chris Zelkovich and $250 from John C.P. King.
James Vlahos gives $250 in memory of "My dad, Nick Vlahos, diehard Leaf fan" ... Diane and David Baker donate $50 ...Patricia Roche chips in $105 ...Cathy and Ronald Plese help out with $350 ... Another $350 comes from Maureen Moloneyin memory of Frank and Patricia Moloney ...Tom and Sandy Cloutier arrive with $250 ...Larry Green gives $40 in memory of Jays fans Wes and Annie Green ...Monique Kondziela donates $105 in memory of H. Kondziela ... Finally this week, there's $200 from Janice Robinson in memory of "my father, Maurice Marin, who loved everything about the Christmas season." ... Many thanks to all!

Document 172 of 500

Bigfoot Ford gains traction with voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bigfoot-ford-gains-traction-with-voters/docview/3128944046/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bigfoot+Ford+gains+traction+with+voters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Electorally, he's the mighty premier of Ontario.
Locally, Doug Ford acts like the aspiring mayor of Toronto.
Nationally, he talks like the unofficial prime minister of Canada.
One day,our premier is badmouthing Mexico's unfair trade practices. The next day, he's trash-talking Toronto's bike lanes.
Forget guardrails. Ford refuses to stay in his legislative lane, and why not?
Every wayward bike path or economic bump in the road is a winning wedge for him. By bigfooting Olivia Chow and stepping on Justin Trudeau's turf, the premier is taking the path of least resistance - and gaining traction on the campaign trail ahead.
Beyond local bikes and foreign cars, the premier can't resist wading in on needle exchanges, high interest rates, the digital services tax and the federal carbon levy.
While his political critics keep flailing over his overreach and overlap - I'm one of them - there's method to his madness. More than ever, Ford's job description is all over the map, but watch how he's connecting the dots and data points.
The premier who would be mayor also wants to be prime minister, but that's nothing new. What's changed is that his counterparts at city hall and on Parliament Hill keep gifting him gifts that keep on giving - openings that any opportunist can't help but exploit.
Nothing personal. In truth, Ford is fond of Trudeau and chummy with Chow.
But the premier isn't interested in being popular with the mayor or prime minister. He's a populist who reads the polls to tally his popularity where it counts - with voters.
Ford understands that regular all-weather cyclists amount to a tiny sliver of the population across the region. As my colleague Robert Benzie has reported, it's not just the census data but the polling numbers that point the way: A recent public opinion survey for the Star by Abacus Data bears out the driving divide - commuting cyclists are in the low single digits.
Full disclosure: I love my bike lanes on Yonge Street, but I don't see them as a birthright, given that I was the only cyclist in sight two chilly mornings this week (Uber Eats e-bikers were the only users I came upon in the afternoon rush hour), while cars were jammed bumper to bumper for blocks. Removing the paint and stanchions on the Yonge bike lanes should cost no more than rejigging the zigzagging CaféTO curb lanes after every summer of disruption, although costs would obviously be higher elsewhere.
Predictably, city hall is trying to win this war of words with new numbers churned out by the same municipal planners who gave us the lanes. Remarkably, they claim it will cost roughly twice as much to take down what they built up, adding up to tens of millions of dollars (albeit absorbed by Queen's Park).
Interestingly, when Toronto of its own volition removed questionable bike lanes on Jarvis Street and Brimley Road, the proportions were reversed - removal cost half as much as construction, and the tab tended to be in the low six figures. Admittedly, those car lanes were easily restored with a fresh coat of paint, but it's a helpful reminder for next time that not all bike paths deserve to be carved in stone (and cement) by city planners, lest their ridership forecasts falter.
In truth, most people could care less about which level of government is making the decision - as long as it's the right call and makes their lives easier. Remember, though, this isn't truly a fight to improve traffic; it's pre-election distraction by Ford's Tories to get people talking about bike lanes instead of big issues.
Even when the premier prattles on about the bigger issues, he tends toward gratuitous talking points that come cheap. High interest rates are a federal matter, yet Ford has fired off countless letters to Ottawa demanding action, knowing full well that the central bank operates at arm's length; the federal carbon levy is fully rebated to most Canadian families, yet the premier claims it hits commuters in the pocket.
The reason Ford keeps ranting about the problems of other politicians is that he knows bike lanes play better than the Greenbelt on the campaign trail.
Personally, I'd let the mayor make her own mistakes, but Ford loves nothing more than meddling, because he never seems to pay a political price - while cheerfully agreeing to absorb all costs of restoring car lanes.
The premier may have found a winner while the mayor is playing a losing hand, but all is not lost for Chow. After all, when all is said and done and dismantled, the province will be paying for the city's mistakes.
CAPTION: Hundreds of cyclists gathered at Christie Pits last Saturday to protest the Ford government's plan to remove some bike lanes on busy Toronto streets.MichelleMengsu Chang Toronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn Toronto Star OPINION

Document 173 of 500

A place to meet in the lonely city: A primer on how to get more pedestrianized streets in a congested city that has a love-hate relationship with its cars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/place-meet-lonely-city/docview/3128943680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+place+to+meet+in+the+lonely+city%3A+A+primer+on+how+to+get+more+pedestrianized+streets+in+a+congested+city+that+has+a+love-hate+relationship+with+its+cars&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As public space experiments go, the risks were as low as the block was short.
In 2022, the St. Lawrence Neighbourhood Association (SLNA) and the local business improvement area pitched Councillor Chris Moise on a proposal to close Market Street, just west of St. Lawrence Market, to cars for the summer.
Motivated by the isolation of the pandemic and the emphasis on outdoor activities, the group's plan was to put out tables and chairs and take advantage of the fact that the City, in 2014, had rebuilt Market Street so it didn't have curbs. The pedestrianization, with a full calendar of events, was resurrected for the summer of 2023 and again for this past summer.
This contained experiment revealed how an intentional effort to welcome city-dwellers into a lively public space can defuse the loneliness of life in the big city.
A2022 surveyconducted by the SLNA and the Old Town BIA found overwhelming public approval for the "I HEART Market Street" initiative. More than 90 per cent went further, approving or strongly approving the concept of pedestrianized streets in Toronto generally.
Yet, the City isn't acting.Torontonians who've visited Montreal, New York or European cities such as Paris, Copenhagen or Madrid, have seen how other places have pedestrianized some downtown streets, moves that invariably draw crowds.
Toronto seems to be going in the opposite direction. Contentious new legislation introduced by Doug Ford's Tory government will force municipalities to seek provincial approval to remove vehicle lanes in favour of new bike lanes and also tear up some of Toronto's existing bike lanes.
Kensington Market, of course, has been hosting Pedestrian Sundays for years, and there areactive proposalsto close a few segments of the market's streets to cars permanently, although these have drawn criticism from merchants worried about deliveries.
Toronto officials also cite decisions to ban vehicles from High Park and the proliferation of street festivals and other event-related closures- e.g., Salsa on St. Clair, Pride, TIFF- that draw large crowds as evidence of their receptiveness to such moves, adding that the City is open to pitches from local groups.
Having the City simply close a street, saysBarbara Gray, general manager of Toronto transportation services, "is not necessarily going to yield the outcome that we might want. It feels to me like it's successful if it's a co-ordinated effort. The City's supportive, the businesses are supportive, the community is supportive. Once you get these streets in place, then people see how amazing it can be."
A primer on how to get more pedestrianized streets in a congested city that has a love-hate relationship with its cars.
What are the modern origins
of the pedestrianized street?
The notion of the pedestrian mall emerged in tandem with the enclosed shopping mall in the post-war decades. "We made these pedestrian malls in the center of mostly smaller and medium sized cities, and they failed to compete with the [suburban] shopping mall," says University of Toronto planning scholarPaul Hess. "In European cities, they pedestrianized their main streets, and those kind of became the shopping mall."
The City of Toronto pedestrianized a stretch of Yonge Street south of Bloor in the early 1970s. But council cancelled the popular experiment after shopkeepers complained about theft. The other noteworthy Canadian example is Ottawa's Sparks Street mall, which still exists but is considered a failure from a public space perspective.
How does Copenhagen
factor into this story?
In the 1960s, a Danish planner named Jan Gehl pushed Copenhagen municipal officials to ban cars from a congested downtown area. In response to concerns from merchants, Gehl invented something he called "public life surveys," tallies documenting the volumes of pedestrians passing by stores, lingering in outdoor cafes or on benches.
Over time, his surveys won over skeptical shopkeepers who saw the foot traffic passing their storefronts exceeded what they'd seen before cars were banned.Those surveys also revealed that pedestrians don't vanish in the cold months.
Copenhagen's pedestrian-only zone, known as the Strøget, has inspired other cities, including Melbourne and New York, where former mayor Michael Bloomberg's transportation commissioner Janette Sadik-Khan drew on Gehl's approach when, in the late 2000s, she began topedestrianize parts of Times Squareand other pieces of Manhattan's grid formed by the intersection of north-south/east-west and diagonal streets.
What makes Montreal's pedestrian-only streets so successful?
For the past few years, Montreal has operated11 pedestrian-only streetsin seven neighbourhoods, including a festive stretch on Avenue du Mont Royal, which cuts through the Plateau and Mile End. A spokesperson says the city evaluates pedestrianization proposals from local BIAs, looking for evidence of consultations, economic impact, quality of life benefits, and consensus among those impacted. The areas targeted tend to be high streets. The City collects garbage early in the day, and emergency vehicles have access at all times.
Recent surveys show a marked increase in year-over-year foot traffic, with 83 per cent of merchants reporting increased sales. Attendees said they sought out these areas to shop, exercise and socialize. "All commercial arteries experienced a significant drop (30 per cent - 60 per cent) in pedestrian activity after the streets reopened to motorized traffic," the spokesperson said.
For 2025, the City of Montreal is putting out a call for more pedestrian zone proposals and has allocated $12 million over three years to support this initiative.
What about traffic?
From a congestion perspective, Market Street was low-hanging fruit because it doesn't go anywhere.
But complaints about traffic surface even at short-run events like TIFF, which saw a few blocks of King Street closed during the festival. The King streetcar, already delayed by Ontario Line construction, was detoured.
Hess says New York officials avoided traffic disruptions when they implemented Sadik-Khan's plan. Elsewhere, a few crusading mayors, such as Paris's Anne Hidalgo, have mounted campaigns to drastically curb road space available for cars by adding transit-only, cycling and pedestrian lanes. After the Olympics, Parispermanently closedone of the Seine bridges entirely to car traffic.
Yet in both cities, as well as Montreal, extensive rapid transit mitigates disruption, although none of these cities is exactly a model of smoothly flowing traffic.
The comparisons to Toronto's festival-related street closures are inexact because the bulk of these events take place on streetcar routes.
Neither the city nor downtown BIAs have sought to close off more localized streets, like the section of Church that runs through the Village or Cumberland, which traverses Yorkville Park and is frequently jammed with cut-through traffic.
How about plans for pedestrian spaces on Yonge, John streets?
BothJohn Street, between Grange Park and Front, and Yonge, from Queen north to College, have been identified as corridors where the pedestrian realm can be expanded so that they are "shared" streets.
Planning for the "John Street Cultural Corridor" goes back a dozen years. The latest developments include relocating underground utilities and the commencement this fall of yet another round of public consultations.
The Yonge project- which has been pithily dubbedYongeTOmorrowand involves closing Yonge where it abuts Sankofa Square and widening sidewalks north to Carlton- is also waiting on major infrastructure work.
John Lorinc is a Toronto
journalist and editor. He is a contributor for the Star.
CREDIT: John Lorinc Contributor;

Business indexing term: Subject: Transportation services Shopping centers

Document 174 of 500

A politician who had no enemies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politician-who-had-no-enemies/docview/3128943565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+politician+who+had+no+enemies&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've met a lot of politicians in my day and I can honestly say that Lincoln Alexander was the finest by far.
Among his many qualities, he's the only one I ever knew who had no enemies. None. He managed to work his way higher in life without stepping on anyone. Alexander's father, from St. Vincent and the Grenadines, was a CPR sleeping car porter.
His mother, a maid born in Jamaica, insisted her son, born in Toronto, get a good education, telling him, "Go to school, you're a little Black boy." He graduated from McMaster University, then Osgoode Hall law school, becoming one of a handful of Black lawyers in Ontario in the 1960s.
But even at Osgoode, he suffered the slur of racism. During an event in his final year, a speaker referred to something as being the equivalent of "looking for a n----r in the woodpile." When questions were invited, Alexander stood up, cited the offensive words, and asked what exactly did the phrase mean? The speaker shook it off by saying that everyone used those words. Alexander told him that as someone in a position of authority, he should be showing leadership, and not be so disrespectful.
Fortunately, voters were more positive. In 1968, Alexander successfully ran for election as a Progressive Conservative in Hamilton, becoming an MP for 12 years as the first Black member of Parliament and the first Black cabinet minister.
As press secretary to Robert Stanfield in the early 1970s, I attended weekly caucus. At times, debate would get so heated I'd wonder if unity on that topic would ever be possible. And then Alexander, all six-foot-three-inches of him, would rise in his place, and say, "I can sense a consensus emerging here."
I'd think to myself, "Whaaaat?" Alexander would then take a few strands from this diatribe, a few threads from that tirade, and so on, until he had woven together a garment that everyone could wear. The strength of any political party flows from its ability to reach consensus. Alexander's leadership qualities could achieve that goal like few others.
I also watched him from the gallery in the House of Commons on that infamous day in February 1971 when Alexander and John Lundrigan, a Progressive Conservative MP from Newfoundland, questioned prime minister Pierre Trudeau about training programs for the unemployed. Exasperated by their rigorous inquisition, Trudeau mouthed the words "F--- off" at them, claiming later that all he said was "Fuddle duddle." Alexander knew exactly what he said.
In the 1990s, I was involved in organizing an event to honour the philanthropic efforts benefitting Ryerson (now Metropolitan University of Toronto) by Jack Cockwell, president of Brookfield Asset Management. Among my assignments was obtaining a suitable congratulatory message from Alexander. I called him at his Hamilton home, and asked if he would come to Toronto to film a tribute interview in front of the Ontario Legislature where he had served as lieutenant governor for six years.
Just as we were just completing our filming with the pink granite building behind him aglow in the morning sun, two busloads of students arrived, presumably for a tour.
As it turned out, they were from Hamilton. The teachers recognized Alexander and introduced him to the kids who were all about eight or nine years old. The cameraman and I organized everybody into three rows so we could get some additional footage of Alexander surrounded by the children. I then withdrew, but even from where I stood about 10 metres away, I could hear Alexander saying in such a low voice that it was almost as if he were speaking to himself: "Vote for Alexander, vote for Alexander."
Filming finished, as he bid goodbye to the students, he said, "When you go home tonight, don't forget to tell your parents you saw Linc Alexander today." He was ever the campaigner, even though he hadn't run for public office in about 15 years.
Subsequently, I'd see him arriving in a wheelchair to watch the Raptors. We always had an enjoyable chat before the game. On other occasions, when I visited my daughter Alison in Hamilton and we'd pick up a few things at Denninger's Foods, we'd see Alexander, having his lunch, constantly interrupted by well-wishers.
On Jan. 21, Lincoln Alexander Day across Canada, a bust of Alexander was unveiled at the Ontario Legislature. It's certainly unusual. Rather than the normal grey, it's taupe with aspects of green. The glasses are too big and the mouth is open rather than closed as is traditional for a bust.
If Alexander were alive, I can't imagine he'd be upset. For most politicians, an ego trip is the only journey worth taking. Not Lincoln Alexander. He cared more about walking with others rather than leading any parade.
Rod McQueen is a freelance contributing columnist for
the Star's Business section. McQueen spent a career talking to successful CEOs and power players. In an ongoing series,
he reflects on the lessons
he learned from those past interviews. McQueen is based
in Toronto. Reach him via email: rmq@rogers.com
CREDIT: Rod McQueen Contributing Columnist

Subject: Personal profiles; Political leadership; Politicians

People: Alexander, Lincoln

Title: A politician who had no enemies

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: B.8

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 16, 2024

Section: Business

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Document 175 of 500

Canada's $1-trillion Trump tariff question

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-1-trillion-trump-tariff-question/docview/3128943432/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+%241-trillion+Trump+tariff+question&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a challenge familiar to many businesses: your biggest corporate customer has a new boss who wants to re-evaluate everything.
It's even harder when they start with a bias that everyone wants to rip them off.
That's Canada's challenge as Donald Trump returns to the White House in January.
For business, the campaign was alarming. We witnessed disdain for international alliances, and promises of tariffs and trade wars. We heard anti-immigrant rhetoric and a proposal to deport some 11 million people, which would damage America's economy and risk a migrant crisis at our border. We see America going down an unsustainable, inflationary fiscal path, with deep tax cuts while deficits are at levels usually reserved for recessions or wars.
These things can seem abstract, so let's translate them into everyday realities: Imagine a car costing $8,000 more because of tariffs. Imagine running a business that depends on imports that have become prohibitively expensive. Imagine the agony of shutting down a plant or laying off workers in Ontario, Illinois, Michigan or Pennsylvania.
That's the reality when our two countries have the world's largest bilateral trading relationship. In Ontario alone, some $1 billion of goods and services cross the border every day. Across Canada, it's $3.6 billion a day. That makes the annual economic stakes to the U.S. and Canada more than $1 trillion.
The landscape is harsh but not hopeless. Campaigning and governing are very different processes- even for populists. Further, Canada has economic reality on its side. We have influential U.S. business allies. And we have products, services and capabilities of enormous value.
Here are the top ten things we can do:
Present a united front
In both approach and message, we need alignment between provincial and federal governments, opposition leaders, and the business community.
Together, we have a vast range of relationships - in Washington, in state capitals, with chambers of commerce, and with countless businesses.
Make it 'North America first'
We must consider the world the new administration inherits, and the forces it fears. Canada's strategy - previewed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford - should include proactive proposals to strengthen North America's economy and security at a time of shifting alliances and threats from America's rivals.
It's (still) the economy, stupid
For the second time in eight years, Trump will inherit a thriving U.S. economy.
Job one is not to mess it up just as ordinary people start to feel it. He needs to hear from the business community that America's prosperity depends on an open market that embraces innovation and competition.
The reality of integration
Given global trends and Trump's predispositions, tariffs are inevitable. However, they need not be universal. Given the integration between our economies and supply chains, there's an airtight case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely, and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement in 2026.
Invest in security
While America's southern border took centre stage in the election, Canada's concern is the one we share - and our own northern border with Russia.
We must strengthen our commitment to North American and global security, and meet our defence spending commitments.
Share our assets
We can make a powerful case that Canada has assets the U.S. needs: energy. Minerals. Nuclear power. AI research excellence. Doing business with us is strategically smarter than the alternatives, notably China.
Be competitive
We must ensure the cost of investing and doing business in Ontario and Canada remains competitive with the U.S. Investment will go where it's wanted, welcomed and rewarded. And with America going through uncertainty, we can attract investment in Canada, and the jobs that come with it.
Attract top talent
Canada must double down on its investments in research at our post-secondary institutions. During the first Trump presidency, we learned that when the U.S. is less welcoming to international research talent, Canada is an outstanding alternative - provided that our institutions have the funding to do the work.
Diversify our markets over time
While every business must look after its biggest customer, it must also grow business elsewhere. Canada has more trade agreements than any other G7 country; we need to leverage them. Within Canada, we also need to eliminate interprovincial trade barriers, benefiting businesses across the country.
Articulate Canadian values
The best of friends can disagree, so let's be unapologetic in standing for Canadian values.
This does not mean lecturing or vacuous virtue-signalling.
It means acting with humility; being reliable partners in international alliances; celebrating the contributions of immigrant communities; meeting our climate commitments; and upholding democracy, security and the rule of law- particularly through unflagging support for Ukraine.
Imagine, once more, that you're that business worried about its relationship with its biggest customer.
Would you sit and wait for them to make decisions on their own? Or would you take the initiative?
The smart business does the latter- and is more likely to hold the Trump card.
Daniel Tisch is president and
CEO of the Ontario Chamber
of Commerce.
CAPTION: Although Trump tariffs appear inevitable, they needn't be universal, writes Daniel Tisch. There'sa strong case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.- Mexico Agreementin 2026.Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Daniel Tisch Contributor

Document 176 of 500

PCs plan to tighten controls: New rules would bar registered offenders from changing their names

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-plan-tighten-controls/docview/3128943337/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+plan+to+tighten+controls%3A+New+rules+would+bar+registered+offenders+from+changing+their+names&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is proposing tighter controls on registered sex offenders, helping police to keep better track of them with new measures that include prohibiting legal name changes.
"We're not going to be timid," Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said Friday at Peel Regional Police headquarters, where he was asked if he's concerned parts of the provincial sex offenders registry could be challenged in the courts, as happened with the federal registry two years ago.
"We will do what it takes."
With MPPs returning to the legislature Monday after a one-week break, Kerzner said he will introduce legislation soon making several major changes to the registry created by Christopher's Law. The law was named after 11-year-old Christopher Stephenson, who was abducted in a Brampton shopping mall by a convicted pedophile on parole, abused and killed. The legislation was passed in 2001.
Convicted sex offenders would be barred from changing their names during the time period they are required to regularly check in with police, report in if they receive a passport or driver's licence, report any changes to email addresses, social media usernames or other "digital identifiers," and report to police 14 days in advance of any trips longer than seven days.
Kerzner acknowledged there are safeguards currently in place on name changes but said the new prohibition is needed to "make it easier for police to monitor sex offenders."
But a prominent Toronto criminal lawyer told the Star that Ontario's sex offender registry - even without the proposed changes - remains "vulnerable" to the same type of challenge as the federal one.
That's because of a 2022 Supreme Court of Canada ruling that mandatory registration of all sex offenders with more than one conviction, and keeping them on the registry for the rest of their lives, violates the Constitution because it does not take into account individual circumstances.
"I suspect this is a prelude to election season," said Daniel Brown, a past president of the Criminal Lawyers Association and co-author of the legal textbook "Prosecuting and Defending Sexual Offence Cases," in reference to Premier Doug Ford's hints at an early vote next year instead of waiting to June 2026.
The name change reform was proposed last year in a private member's bill by Progressive Conservative MPPs Laurie Scott (Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock) and Laura Smith (Thornhill), who said name changes can make convicted sex offenders difficult to trace.
"This will stop the Karla Homolkas of this world from becoming Leanne Teale," said Smith. Homolka changed her name after her conviction for manslaughter for in the abduction, torture and killings of teenagers Kristen French and Leslie Mahaffy by her then-husband, Paul Bernardo, who remains in prison.
Saskatchewan and Alberta already ban the name changes.
There are more than 14,000 registered sex offenders in Ontario who are required to report in with police, and there is a high compliance rate of 94 per cent.
But in Peel Region, for example, where there are 750 registrants - including 185 considered "high risk" - 500 compliance checks this year have resulted in 36 arrests and 82 criminal charges, said deputy police chief Nick Milanovic.
He added the reporting of email addresses, social media handles or digital identifiers is important because "sexual predators are more and more using online platforms."
Jim Stephenson, the father of Christopher, told a news conference with Kerzner that he "wholeheartedly" supports the proposed reforms.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 177 of 500

How to make Toronto more livable - you know, like Paris, New York and Montreal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-make-toronto-more-livable-you-know-like-paris/docview/3128943276/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+to+make+Toronto+more+livable+-+you+know%2C+like+Paris%2C+New+York+and+Montreal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As public space experiments go, the risks were as low as the block was short.
In 2022, the St. Lawrence Neighbourhood Association (SLNA) and the local business improvement area pitched Councillor Chris Moise on a proposal to close Market Street, just west of St. Lawrence Market, to cars for the summer.
Motivated by the isolation of the pandemic and the emphasis on outdoor activities, the group's plan was to put out tables and chairs and take advantage of the fact that the City, in 2014, had rebuilt Market Street so it didn't have curbs. The pedestrianization, with a full calendar of events, was resurrected for the summer of 2023 and again for this past summer.
This contained experiment revealed how an intentional effort to welcome city-dwellers into a lively public space can defuse the loneliness of life in the big city.
A 2022 survey conducted by the SNLA and the Old Town BIA found overwhelming public approval for the "I HEART Market Street" initiative. More than 90 per cent went further, approving or strongly approving the concept of pedestrianized streets in Toronto generally.
Yet, the City isn't acting. Torontonians who've visited Montreal, New York or European cities such as Paris, Copenhagen or Madrid, have seen how other places have pedestrianized some downtown streets, moves that invariably draw crowds.
Toronto seems to be going in the opposite direction. Contentious new legislation introduced by Doug Ford's Tory government will force municipalities to seek provincial approval to remove vehicle lanes in favour of new bike lanes and also tear up some of Toronto's existing bike lanes.
Kensington Market, of course, has been hosting Pedestrian Sundays for years, and there are active proposals to close a few segments of the market's streets to cars permanently, although these have drawn criticism from merchants worried about deliveries.
Toronto officials also cite decisions to ban vehicles from High Park and the proliferation of street festivals and other event-related closures - e.g., Salsa on St. Clair, Pride, TIFF - that draw large crowds as evidence of their receptiveness to such moves, adding that the City is open to pitches from local groups.
Having the City simply close a street, says Barbara Gray, general manager of Toronto transportation services, "is not necessarily going to yield the outcome that that we might want. It feels to me like it's successful if it's a coordinated effort. The City's supportive, the businesses are supportive, the community is supportive. Once you get these streets in place, then people see how amazing it can be."
A primer on how to get more pedestrianized streets in a congested city that has a love-hate relationship with its cars.
What are the modern origins of the pedestrianized street?
The notion of the pedestrian mall emerged in tandem with the enclosed shopping mall in the post-war decades. "We made these pedestrian malls in the center of mostly smaller and medium sized cities, and they failed to compete with the [suburban] shopping mall," says University of Toronto planning scholar Paul Hess. "In European cities, they pedestrianized their main streets, and those kind of became the shopping mall."
The City of Toronto pedestrianized a stretch of Yonge Street south of Bloor in the early 1970s. But council cancelled the popular experiment after shopkeepers complained about theft. The other noteworthy Canadian example is Ottawa's Sparks Street mall, which still exists but is considered a failure from a public space perspective.
How does Copenhagen factor into this story?
In the 1960s, a Danish planner named Jan Gehl pushed Copenhagen municipal officials to ban cars from a congested downtown area. In response to concerns from merchants, Gehl invented something he called "public life surveys," tallies documenting the volumes of pedestrians passing by stores, lingering in outdoor cafes or on benches.
Over time, his surveys won over skeptical shopkeepers who saw the foot traffic passing their storefronts exceeded what they'd seen before cars were banned. Those surveys also revealed that pedestrians don't vanish in the cold months.
Copenhagen's pedestrian-only zone, known as the Strøget, has inspired other cities, including Melbourne and New York, where former mayor Michael Bloomberg's transportation commissioner Janette Sadik-Khan drew on Gehl's approach when, in the late 2000s, she began to pedestrianize parts of Times Square and other pieces of Manhattan's grid formed by the intersection of north-south/east-west and diagonal streets.
What makes Montreal's pedestrian-only streets so successful?
For the past few years, Montreal has operated 11 pedestrian-only streets in seven neighbourhoods, including a festive stretch on Avenue du Mont Royal, which cuts through the Plateau and Mile End. A spokesperson says the city evaluates pedestrianization proposals from local BIAs, looking for evidence of consultations, economic impact, quality of life benefits, and consensus among those impacted. The areas targeted tend to be high streets. The City collects garbage early in the day, and emergency vehicles have access at all times.
Recent surveys show a marked increase in year-over-year foot traffic, with 83 per cent of merchants reporting increased sales. Attendees said they sought out these areas to shop, exercise and socialize. "All commercial arteries experienced a significant drop (30 per cent - 60 per cent) in pedestrian activity after the streets reopened to motorized traffic," the spokesperson said.
For 2025, the City of Montreal is putting out a call for more pedestrian zone proposals and has allocated $12 million over three years to support this initiative. Toronto, by contrast, does not have a pedestrianization program or budget, according to a spokesperson, who adds that city officials recognize that pedestrianization "can have varying impacts on commercial activity."
What about traffic?
From a congestion perspective, Market Street was low-hanging fruit because it doesn't go anywhere. But complaints about traffic surface even at short-run events like TIFF, which saw a few blocks of King Street closed during the festival. The King streetcar, already delayed by Ontario Line construction, was detoured.
Hess says New York officials avoided traffic disruptions when they implemented Sadik-Khan's plan. Elsewhere, a few crusading mayors, such as Paris's Anne Hidalgo, have mounted campaigns to drastically curb road space available for cars by adding transit-only, cycling and pedestrian lanes. After the Olympics, Paris permanently closed one of the Seine bridges entirely to car traffic.
Yet in both cities, as well as Montreal, extensive rapid transit mitigates disruption, although none of these cities is exactly a model of smoothly flowing traffic.
The comparisons to Toronto's festival-related street closures are inexact because the bulk of these events take place on streetcar routes. Neither the City nor downtown BIAs have sought to close off more localized streets, like the section of Church that runs through the Village or Cumberland, which traverses Yorkville Park and is frequently jammed with cut-through traffic.
How about the plans for more pedestrian spaces on Yonge Street and John Street?
Both John Street, between Grange Park and Front, and Yonge, from Queen north to College, have been identified as corridors where the pedestrian realm can be expanded so that they are "shared" streets.
Planning for the "John Street Cultural Corridor" goes back a dozen years. The latest developments include relocating underground utilities and the commencement this fall of yet another round of public consultations. The Yonge project - which has been pithily dubbed YongeTOmorrow and involves closing Yonge where it abuts Sankofa Square and widening sidewalks north to Carlton - is also waiting on major infrastructure work.
Gray says the City's approach is to carry out all the road re-construction work at once so as not to be opening and closing stretches repeatedly. "Of late," she notes, "there's been a lot of pressure to fully coordinate those works so that they're happening concurrently." Gray doesn't expect John Street to be completed before the 2026 FIFA World Cup rolls into town.
CAPTION: Pedestrians on Market Street near St. Lawrence Market in 2018.
CREDIT: John Lorinc, Contributor

Thousands of Ontario university spots are in jeopardy + how many cyclists are actually in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/thousands-ontario-university-spots-are-jeopardy/docview/3128732883/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Thousands+of+Ontario+university+spots+are+in+jeopardy+%2B+how+many+cyclists+are+actually+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, November 15 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Ontario university spots in jeopardy, the actual number of cyclists in Toronto and fallout from a massive gunfight in West Queen West.
DON'T MISSThousands of Ontario university spots are in jeopardy
Some 100,000 spots for Ontario teens will be at risk over the next six years unless the province lifts its cap on domestic enrolment and provides funding for the additional students, universities say. In a report obtained by the Star ahead of its release, Ontario's public universities say they're grappling with the higher costs of salary increases and the loss of international student tuition, Kristin Rushowy reports. Take a closer look.
ICYMI: Ontario's 20 public universities previously estimated Ottawa's cap on international students will cost them almost $1 billion over two years.Tight competition: For prospective students, even a 99.5 per cent average isn't enough to get into some of the province's top universities. Take an inside look at the admissions process of Ontario's most competitive undergraduate programs.There might be more cyclists in Toronto than Doug Ford suggests
Justifying his plan to remove Toronto bike lanes, Ford's government claimed only 1.2 per cent of Torontonians commute by bike - but the actual number may be several times that. According to a confidential city staff report seen by the Star, 5.8 per cent of commuters took bikes or other forms of "micromobility" to work. Meanwhile, daily cycling trips have tripled in the last two decades. Ben Spurr breaks down the data.
Wait, what? The numbers come from a major, unreleased survey led by the province itself. The Transportation Tomorrow Survey was supposed to be published this fall; Ontario's Ministry of Transportation said its goal was still to release it this year, although it didn't say when.Martin Regg Cohn's take: Electorally, he's the mighty premier of Ontario. Locally, he acts like the aspiring mayor of Toronto. Nationally, he talks like the unofficial prime minister of Canada. Here's why Ford is trampling into municipal and federal lanes.Those charged in a massive gunfight included a 16-year-old wanted for murder
Monday's massive gunfight in West Queen West, which saw more than 100 shots exchanged between two groups, involved a 16-year-old who was involved in a previous homicide, police say. A police spokesperson said the individuals involved were apparently attending a birthday party and were seen waving their weapons around in a video posted to social media. Here are the details.
Go deeper: Police initially arrested 23 people and seized more than 16 firearms, including two assault-style rifles, after gunfire broke out late Monday night.Word from police: "It is a miracle no one was injured," Toronto police Supt. Joe Matthews said Thursday. "In my time as a police officer, this type of event we really haven't seen in the city of Toronto. It's something we see on TV and in movies."WHAT ELSE
Jagmeet Singh is promising tax cuts on "essential" items like home heating, internet and cellphone bills.Postal workers across the country are officially on strike this morning. Here's what that means for your deliveries.To survive Donald Trump, Canada must do these four things, Stephen Maher writes. Trudeau is going to hate them.Oval Office or three-ring circus? With Trump's appointments, Rosie DiManno writes that it's hard to tell.Ford's education minister directly warned school boards against "unnecessary or extravagant expenses."Trump has selected anti-vaccine activist Robert F. Kennedy Jr. as his health secretary.The FBI released a new image of the ex-Olympic snowboarder linked to a massive cocaine ring and Ontario killings.Police released body camera video from a Brampton temple protest, saying their officer acted lawfully.The Eras Tour thrilled in Toronto: "The way the city of Toronto has embraced us, we notice all of it."As Taylor Swift's Eras Tour takes off, Toronto brands are clamouring to align with the megastar.A new theatre company is launching in Toronto and it wants audiences to help develop the next great Canadian play.Alex Jones' Infowars just got bought at auction by satire publication The Onion (seriously). Check out its plans for the website.POV

Document 179 of 500

Ford government will tighten controls on registered sex offenders

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-will-tighten-controls-on/docview/3128732836/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+will+tighten+controls+on+registered+sex+offenders&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is proposing tighter controls on registered sex offenders, helping police to keep better track of them with new measures that include prohibiting legal name changes.
"We're not going to be timid," Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said Friday at Peel Regional Police headquarters, where he was asked if he's concerned parts of the provincial sex offenders registry could be challenged in the courts, as happened with the federal registry two years ago.
"We will do what it takes."
With MPPs returning to the legislature Monday after a one-week break, Kerzner said he will introduce legislation soon making several major changes to the registry created by Christopher's Law. The law was named after 11-year-old Christopher Stephenson, who was abducted in a Brampton shopping mall by a convicted pedophile on parole, abused and killed. The legislation was passed in 2001.
Convicted sex offenders would be barred from changing their names during the time period they are required to regularly check in with police, report in if they receive a passport or driver's licence, report any changes to email addresses, social media usernames or other "digital identifiers," and report to police 14 days in advance of any trips longer than seven days.
Kerzner acknowledged there are safeguards currently in place on name changes but said the new prohibition is needed to "make it easier for police to monitor sex offenders."
But a prominent Toronto criminal lawyer told the Star that Ontario's sex offender registry - even without the proposed changes - remains "vulnerable" to the same type of challenge as the federal one.
That's because of a 2022 Supreme Court of Canada ruling that mandatory registration of all sex offenders with more than one conviction, and keeping them on the registry for the rest of their lives, violates the Constitution because it does not take into account individual circumstances.
"I suspect this is a prelude to election season," said Daniel Brown, a past president of the Criminal Lawyers Association and co-author of the legal textbook "Prosecuting and Defending Sexual Offence Cases," in reference to Premier Doug Ford's hints at an early vote next year instead of waiting to June 2026.
The name change reform was proposed last year in a private member's bill by Progressive Conservative MPPs Laurie Scott (Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock) and Laura Smith (Thornhill), who said name changes can make convicted sex offenders difficult to trace.
"This will stop the Karla Homolkas of this world from becoming Leanne Teale," said Smith. Homolka changed her name after her conviction for manslaughter for in the abduction, torture and killings of teenagers Kristen French and Leslie Mahaffy by her then-husband, Paul Bernardo, who remains in prison.
Saskatchewan and Alberta already ban the name changes.
There are more than 14,000 registered sex offenders in Ontario who are required to report in with police, and there is a high compliance rate of 94 per cent.
But in Peel Region, for example, where there are 750 registrants - including 185 considered "high risk" - 500 compliance checks this year have resulted in 36 arrests and 82 criminal charges, said deputy police chief Nick Milanovic.
He added the reporting of email addresses, social media handles or digital identifiers is important because "sexual predators are more and more using online platforms."
Jim Stephenson, the father of Christopher, told a news conference with Kerzner that he "wholeheartedly" supports the proposed reforms.
CAPTION: Solicitor General Michael Kerzner, right, and Premier Doug Ford appearat a newsconference at the Toronto Police College in Etobicoke on April 25, 2023.

Full text: Taylor Swift is in Toronto tonight for the second of six shows at the Rogers Centre. Ahead of Friday's show, fans are once again expected to gather near the Rogers Centre, and at various pop-up parties around the city. This file is no longer updating.
5:20 p.m. As part of the security measures in place, drones have been banned near the Rogers Centre on concert days.
Transport Canada requested a 0.3-nautical-mile radius around the Rogers Centre, from the ground to 400 feet in the air, where drones are barred, Maryam Amini, a spokesperson for Nav Canada, confirmed to the Star.
The restrictions, in place from 4 p.m. onward, don't include any areas south of the Gardiner Expressway.
5:10 p.m. It's Taylor Swift's Toronto. We just live here
In this podcast episode, This Matters looks at the Tay-Tay-takeover of Toronto, in which the pop star's six concerts over 10 days have been estimated to bring in as many as a half a million tourists and pump hundreds of millions of dollars into the economy. Reporter Mark Colley provides some perspective on the phenomenon and all it has entailed, from massive security, transit and traffic planning, to the scene around the city. Aisling Murphy, the Star's resident Swiftie, was at the show on Thursday night.
Listen to the podcast.
4 p.m. Taylor Swift meets Toronto traffic: How we survived the gridlock with the help of some glitter and glam
If there's ever a time to get stuck in Toronto traffic, it's Taylor's Version.
Taylor Swift's first, highly anticipated show at the Rogers Centre unleashed a mass of tens of thousands - ticket holders, tourists, parents and fans who never made it inside - onto city streets Thursday night.
To see how Swiftmania would impact Toronto's already-harrowing congestion, and to prepare anyone foolish enough not to heed the city's "don't drive" advice for the next five shows, we drove to the concert before it started - and after as it let out.
Read the full article from Mahdis Habibinia David Rider
3:46 p.m. Taylor Swift tickets in Toronto, which originally sold to verified fans for as little as $160, are now being resold for thousands leaving many fans empty handed. How do Swift's ticket prices compare to popular artists of the past? Here are some examples of ticket prices from Toronto shows and what they would cost in today's dollars. - Kelsey Wilson
3:19 p.m. The line for Gates 1-3 at the Rogers Centre is already stretching close to the sidewalk on Front Street. On Thursday, the line for these gates got so long that it started mixing with the line for Taylgate, a fan event happening simultaneously at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.
3 p.m. The official lineups have opened, with stadium staff holding large blue signs with gate numbers as people start filing in.
The crowd of fans coming from Union Station through the PATH-Skywalk has also grown.
2:55 p.m. Jo Lalonde was all smiles as fans took photos as she did a chalk art drawing of Taylor Swift with the CN Tower in the background outside of the Rogers Centre.
Known on social media as @chalkchick, Lalonde said she's been doing chalk art professionally for 18 years or, as she said jokingly, since she stopped eating chalk as a kid.
She also made a friendship bracelet mural elsewhere in Canada Lands Square, and has amassed quite the connection of friendship bracelets from fans passing by.
"I've worked events for years and just like how enthusiastic and how lovely and sweet and wholesome everyone is, it has just been warming the cockles of my heart," she said.
2:38 p.m. Romy Marquez drove an hour from North York to the Rogers Centre to see all the excitement around the Eras Tour for himself.
"I've never seen so many people here for an artist," he said, a camera around his neck and another in his hand to photograph the festivities.
There are a few other locals who came out just to see the crowds, following in the footsteps of others yesterday.
2:33 p.m. Resale prices on StubHub are similar to where they stood around this time last night. The get-in price - for a single seat in the Corona Rooftop Patio, a bar in the 500 level outfield - is $2,864.
Last night, ticket prices dropped as the concert began. Hundreds of ticketless fans crowded around the Rogers Centre in the hopes of snagging a last-minute deal.
2:28 p.m. The sun is starting to peak out from behind the clouds, with about an hour until lineups officially open at the Rogers Centre, as fans fill the sidewalk across from the giant friendship bracelet hung outside the stadium.
People are exchanging friendship bracelets, trying to find their friends or family in the crowd and, of course, taking photos in their outfits representing all 11 of Taylor Swift's eras.
2:03 p.m. Lillian Dinegar and her dad, Greg, spent the whole car ride from New Jersey to Toronto talking about what songs they were most excited to see at Night 2 of the Eras Tour.
They also speculated about what the surprise songs would be. While there are many dream picks, Lillian is really hoping for "Tolerate It" from "Reputation."
"No one was more devastated when it was taken off of the set list than me," she said, dressed in a "Lover"-inspired outfit, complete with the signature "Lover" heart around her eye.
Greg is most excited to see "Blank Space," a song he's loved since seeing the music video back in 2014 - even before Lillian became a Swiftie during the "Midnights" era.
"I'm 57 years old, and I tell all my buddies like, 'Dude, you gotta listen to this girl. She's unbelievable,'" he said, dressed in a Taylor-themed fit put together by Lillian.
2 p.m. A line is already starting to form along Bremner Boulevard for tonight's Eras Tour show, stretching to Ripley's Aquarium.
"Why are people already lining up, the show's tomorrow?," one young girl asked before her mom told her that there is also a concert tonight.
Some people were lining up to trade friendship bracelets, but a Rogers staff member using a megaphone asked fans to exchange their accessories elsewhere, and the line began to shrink.
2 p.m. Taylor Swift and the Richter scale
When Taylor Swift visited Seattle last year, her fans created the equivalent of a 2.3-magnitude earthquake, the Seattle Times reported.
In Toronto, though, the Richter scale took the night off. Chris Boucher, a seismic analyst at Natural Resources Canada, told the Star his agency didn't pick up any significant seismic activity between 6 p.m. and 11 p.m. on Thursday night.
That's likely because the nearest seismometer to the Rogers Centre is in Acton, Ont., about 50 kilometres away. In Seattle, a seismometer is located in a building across the street from Lumen Field, Boucher said.
A new seismometer is being installed on the Toronto islands within the next year, according to Boucher, meaning activity at a concert like Swift's might be picked up in the future.
"But for something in the Rogers Centre at the moment," Boucher said, "it doesn't quite compare to an earthquake that we'd be able to register about 50 kilometres away."
1:40 p.m. Concert goes getting ready for the second night of the tour
Two hours ahead of lineups opening the second evening of the Eras Tour, fans decked out in sequins, cowboy boots and Taylor Swift merch are already starting to make their way toward the Rogers Centre along Front Street.
The number of people is still relatively small compared to the crowds expected in a couple hours, but their bejeweled outfits stand out among the grey and drab of a cloudy Friday afternoon in downtown. - Nathan Bawaan
1:30 p.m. Why the blue crew neck is the most coveted Eras Tour merch item in Toronto
Now that Taylor Swift's Eras Tour has kicked off in Toronto, there are new status symbols for Swifties: Friendship bracelets you got from trading at the Taylgate party outside Rogers Centre, a hoarse throat from screaming for three hours because you actually managed to get tickets to the show, and the ultra-coveted Eras Tour blue crew neck.
This piece of merch, a faded navy sweatshirt with "Taylor Swift" written in black on the front and all the Eras tour dates on the back, is the most-coveted item among fans shopping for memorabilia at the Rogers Centre during Swift's six-concert residency in the city. It's not to be confused with an earlier iteration of the blue crew neck, which only lists the dates of the first American leg of the tour.
Read the full story from Sarah Laing
1:20 p.m. Taylor Swift Eras Tour in Toronto concert review
Around the halfway point of Taylor Swift's first Toronto Eras Tour concert, the show became something bigger than itself: a sprawling, ear-splitting, life-affirming ovation.
When the applause for "Champagne Problems" hit the two-minute mark, Swift removed her in-ear monitors and took in the enormous roar of the crowd. At first, she was humble, soaking in the whoops as she gazed out at the more than 40,000 people gazing adoringly back at her.
Then she cocked her eyebrow and smirked.
The audience kept cheering. This was no ordinary crowd.
Read the full concert review from Aisling Murphy
12:10 p.m. Which celebrities came to last night's show?
The stars came out for the first evening of the Eras Tour last night. Fans posted photos online with TV and film actor Lucy Liu and Annie Murphy of "Schitt's Creek" fame. Taylor Swift's parents, Scott and Andrea, were also spotted in the crowd. -Nathan Bawaan
11:45 a.m. Taylor Swift scams likely run by "well-organized" fraudsters climbs to 190
Taylor Swift fans eager to score a last-minute ticket should be on alert for scams run by "well-organized" fraudsters.
The Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre says the number of reports of people being ripped off this year trying to see the artist has now climbed to 190.
Spokesperson Lisanne Roy Beauchamp says most victims are solicited through compromised accounts of someone they knew.
Read the full story from the Canadian Press
11:15 a.m. The city has updated its road closures list ahead of the second evening of the Eras Tour. While no new roads will be closed, streets surrounding the Rogers Centre will now be shut down an hour earlier at 1 p.m.
The city has also added a sixth designated drop-off/pick-up spot for cars heading eastbound on Front Street between Spadina Avenue and Bathurst Street. - Nathan Bawaan
11:07 a.m. Where does Taylor Swift go next?
Following Taylor Swift's first of six Toronto performances, some wonder how long she'll stay in Toronto. The Eras Tour will hit B.C. next month. Swift will perform in Vancouver on Dec. 6, 7, and 8. -Jayden Dill
10:42 a.m. What's the most coveted item of the tour?
The blue crew neck may be the most coveted item of the Eras tour. Some fans say the shirt sold out in the first hour, leaving them with the grey quarter zip as an alternative option.
"A man from the staff here said the blue crew neck literally sold out within 45 minutes," one person said in a post on X. "But then I see someone walking out with five of the same item, so no wonder."
It's been a trend in cities Swift has visited on her tour. One Instagram user, who missed the blue crewneck at Swift's Chicago and Indiana tours, raved about the Swifty fanbase when they received a blue crewneck from a fellow fan.
"This is why I love the Swifty fandom. I don't care what anybody says about us; we are here for each other," the user said in an Instagram post. "I now have the 'blue crew' in my size." -Jayden Dill
10:05 a.m. Day 2 is here
Rise and shine, Swifties! Day two of Taylor Swift's 10-day Eras Tour residency in Toronto is here.
The first of six shows at Rogers Centre on Thursday went off without a hitch. Though there was plenty of traffic in the city's downtown core, it was mostly an evening filled with positive vibes and plenty of glitter.
Inside the venue, Swift took the stage just before 8 p.m., treating the crowd to a 45-song set that lasted over three hours. The set list did not diverge significantly from the rest of the Eras Your. For the evening's surprise songs, she performed a guitar-based mashup "My Boy Only Breaks His Favorite Toys" with "This Is Why We Can't Have Nice Things," and a piano-based mashup of "False God" with "'tis the damn season."
Once again, gates open at 4:30 p.m. Organizers are reminding fans that ticket-holders are not permitted to line up before 3:30 p.m.
9:32 a.m. Taylor Swift Eras Tour set list
Since Taylor Swift's Eras Tour began way back in March of 2023, Swifties have obsessed over the mega-star's set list, poring over each date to discover differences and to see which songs she plays during the "surprise" portion of her show.
On day one of Swift's mini-residency in Toronto, Swift performed a 45-song set that lasted over three hours.
During the surprise songs section of the show, she performed a guitar-based mash-up of "My Boy Only Breaks His Favorite Toys" and "This Is Why We Can't Have Nice Things," followed by a piano-based mash-up of "False God" "'tis the damn season."

9:20 a.m. Travis Kelce Silent H rumours debunked
Social media has been buzzing that Travis Kelce was at taco restaurant Silent H on King Street West on Wednesday night ahead of Taylor Swift's first Canadian Eras Tour date.
The social media account for Silent H posted a photo of owner Patrick Marzouk with the Kansas City tight end to Instagram on Wednesday night. But when reached for confirmation, Marzouk said the photo was from a previous visit to the city.
TMZ also reported that on Wednesday night, Kelce was at the Jelly Roll concert in Kansas City.
We'll stay tuned to see if Travis goes back to Silent H - with Taylor in tow -while they're in town. - Jayden Dill

If you plan to visit the city or surrounding areas, prepare accordingly as you may run into some construction ... and more than a few Swifties.

Friday 7 a.m. Toronto brands clamour to align with the megastar
As she begins her concert run in Toronto, many businesses are trying to hitch their wagon to Taylor Swift, whether it's for marketing or to highlight their pet causes, literally.
"Generally, it's quite positive how she has this emotional appeal amongst so many people where she's able to create a sense of community," said Mohamed Khimji, a law professor at Queen's University and a self-professed Swiftie.
"I think that's really what all the brands are trying to tap into by relying upon Taylor Swift, in terms of their messaging and their public relations."

11:55 p.m. Despite the crowd of fans heading into Union Station, the flow of traffic inside is pretty smooth.
Two volunteers, armed with a giant GO sign and a megaphone, are directing people from the Great Hall down to the lower floors.
Besides security and janitorial staff, there are very few non-Swifties inside the station.
