"This is crazy," a passerby who didn't go the concert said to her friend as they moved against the crowd.
Some are stopping at poutine and hot dog trucks along the way. No one is stopping, however, for the Ubers and taxis parked all along the street. - The Star's Nathan Bawaan
11:38 p.m. Julliette, 9, and Charlotte, 11, didn't have time to prepare an outfit for tonight's show - they only got tickets earlier today thanks to their dad, Francois.
The show was emotional for everyone, with Juliette and Charlotte tearing up when Taylor Swift first came on, which then made their dad emotional.
"I had some tears when I saw you girls being so excited to go," Francois said.
When the girls go to school on Monday - tomorrow is a professional development day - they plan to wear their new Eras Tour sweatshirts. - The Star's Nathan Bawaan
11:30 p.m. Ten minutes ago, this pedestrian bridge from the Rogers Centre to John Street was completely empty.
Now it's filled with fans making their mass exodus from Night One of the Eras Tour, the ground literally shaking beneath them.
A busker's acoustic rendition of "You Belong With Me" mixes with the voices of excited fans. - The Star's Nathan Bawaan
11:20 p.m. Ellen McCrum left her 11-year-old daughter at home - supervised - in New York to see Taylor Swift tonight with her friend Jennifer Small, another New Yorker.
"It was great. It was totally worth it," McCrum said as she and Small were leaving the Rogers Centre around 11:15 p.m., almost three and a half hours after Swift went on stage.
McCrum and Small loved everything, from the outfits (from Swift and fans) to the acoustic sets.
"It was not long enough," Small said with a laugh. "We were surrounded by so much joy." - The Star's Nathan Bawaan
11 p.m. As Swift wrapped up her show, X lit up with fans excited to hear about the night's setlist. For surprise songs -- which Swift performs at every show -- she mashed up "My Boy Only Breaks His Favorite Toys" with "This Is Why We Can't Have Nice Things," and "False God" with "'tis the damn season."
10:45 p.m. Taylor Swift's Eras Tour stormed into Toronto on Thursday night, bringing the biggest tour the world has ever seen to the Rogers Centre and covering downtown in seas of sequins and sparkle. Tens of thousands of fans, many from around the world, encircled entire city blocks.
"I was thinking, 'You know, I want to spend those last shows with the most generous, encouraging, welcoming, passionate, excitable fans' - so we came to see you in Toronto," Swift said on stage as the rapturous crowd of more than 40,000 erupted. "The way the city of Toronto has embraced us and welcomed us, we notice all of it, and it's heartening. It feels so good and it feels so different than any other experience I've had before."
When Swift swept on stage a few minutes before 8 p.m., she performed songs from her various eras and albums - "Lover," "Fearless," "Red," "Reputation," "Evermore" and others - with twists for her first Canadian audience.
Read the full story from the Star's Mark Colley.
9:35 p.m. Swift has dubbed the entire "Folklore" album era as being Canada's.
"Doesn't it seem like the entire Folklore era just belongs in Canada? It just does. The kind of place that I envisioned in my mind that Folklore took place is very natural wilderness, beautiful, forests that have been there since the beginning of time. It feels like we're returning the Folklore era to where it belongs."
9:30 p.m. Taylor Swift told fans that she hadn't considered for a second not coming to Canada for the Eras Tour.
"I'm just having more fun on this tour than I've ever had on a tour in my life. And the reason for that is that you guys have turned this into something that feels more than just a concert for me. You have all these traditions you've started, you've turned it into a full event - and the way the city of Toronto has embraced us and welcomed us. We notice all of it, and it's heartening, and it feels so good and it feels so different than any other experience I've ever had before," the popstar said.
8:50 p.m. "Oh Toronto, we are sooo back!"
Those were Swift's opening remarks as she addressed her fans on opening night.
"That was so awesome. What you just did, and also, the way you've been treating me for the last five minutes... I'm feeling dangerously good right now."
Swift shared some insight about why she chose Canada to close out her tour after spending the summer in Europe and the last couple months in the U.S.
"We thought, 'You know what? If we're going to bring the Eras Tour to a close, which we are, in nine shows. I was thinking, you know, I want to spend those last shows with the most generous, encouraging, welcoming, passionate, excitable fans - so we came to see you in Toronto.'"
8:31 p.m. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau welcomed Taylor Swift to Canada for the Eras Tour's first show at the Rogers Centre on Thursday night.
It was the PM's public plea that put extra pressure on the superstar to solidify her Canadian dates.
Before Swift's Toronto concerts were announced in August last year, Trudeau posted a plea to her on X on July 5, 2023: "It's me, hi. I know places in Canada would love to have you. So, don't make it another cruel summer. We hope to see you soon."
8:28 p.m. As Swift was on stage with her concert well underway, StubHub was selling tickets for up to $17,500, with some at $1,700 - with about 150 tickets left -- just before 8:20 p.m. on Thursday.
8:05 p.m. During her performance of "The Man", Taylor Swift says: "You're making me feel like tonight's the first-ever Eras Tour show in Canada."
7:55 p.m. Taylor Swift has taken the stage, a few minutes before her expected 8 p.m. start time. Her show is expected to last approximately three hours. The Eras concert is expected to end around 11 p.m.
7:49 p.m. As 25-year-old Gracie Abrams nears the end of her opening act, the Rogers Centre is "very full and very buzzy," Star contributor Aisling Murphy reports. There's lots of friendship bracelet being traded, too.
Abrams' set has included her number-one single, "That's So True," as well as her first-ever live performance of "I Told You Things."
7:15 p.m. Abrams is now performing the Grammy-nominated song "US," which was co-written by Swift.
7:10 p.m. For Brenda MacLean, the Eras Tour is a family affair. She and daughters, Maxine and Michelle, and her granddaughter Madeline Smallman, flew in from Nova Scotia for what they described as a once-in-a-lifetime event. Maxine said her look was inspired by her favourite album, "Reputation," while Michelle went the "Bejewelled," route. Smallman went for a "Lover" vibe. "It's been my dream (to see Swift) since I was four years old," said Smallman. - The Star's Asma Sahebzada
7:05 p.m. Nicolas Bello, a volunteer with the city directing fans where to go, normally works at David Pecaut or Nathan Phillips squares doing the same thing.
Tonight, he and two others have been standing near the base of the CN Tower since 4 p.m., and will be here until 8.
"My wife's at the show, so I need something to do tonight," he said, adding that volunteer spots for tonight and the other show nights filled up pretty quickly.
"It's fun, he continued. "I've never talked to so many people at any of my prior City of Toronto volunteering gigs." - The Star's Nathan Bawaan
6:50 p.m. The concert has started. Singer-songwriter Gracie Abrams is on stage to open for Swift.
Meanwhile, fans still hoping to get tickets are huddling in the cold and rain outside, scanning resale markets and holding signs.
On StubHub, tickets have dropped to a little under $1,400 at the cheapest.
6:45 p.m. Just before the concert starts, a land acknowledgement is playing on the big screen. That's new. Previous shows in the U.S. didn't feature this.
6:40 p.m. Danielle Wong's first concert was to Taylor Swift's Fearless tour in 2010 with her sister.
Fourteen years later, she's standing outside the Rogers Centre with about 15 minutes until Gracie Abrams, tonight's opener, goes on stage to replicate this memory.
"Unfortunately, we had to sit on different floors, so it would be nice to 1) go and 2) sit together," the downtown resident told the Star.
Wong has been trying to get tickets since they first went on sale, and she said she was considering coming down to the stadium on another show night if she doesn't get in tonight. - The Star's Nathan Bawaan
6:31 p.m. A van with a sign that reads "Fan experience sponsored by Toronto education workers" has just come back along Front Street playing Swift's music out of speakers.

6:29 p.m. The line along Front that stretched from the entrance to Taylor Swift Way has now cleared completely.
6:23 p.m. The line along Front Street is starting to move quickly as stadium staff wave their arms to let fans inside.
Companies like Ricola are stationed next to the line to hand out cough drop samples - "Save you throat tomorrow," a representative says to fans passing by - while other people try to sell pink cowboy hats and unofficial tour merch to those who are waiting.
6:20 p.m. Inside the Rogers Centre, the seating bowl is about half full as fans continue to trickle in.There are "sequins as far as the eye can see," Star contributor Aisling Murphy says.
6:15 p.m. Cousins Alissia Alvanese, 18, and Tia Shea, 16, said Thursday night's show will be their first time seeing Taylor Swift live. They both chose their concert looks carefully.
"My holy grail is 'Folklore,' 'Evermore' and 'The Tortured Poets Department' - anything that reminds me of being deep in a forest," said Alvanese, right. "After 'Folklore,' I was like, 'I'm buying and putting it on my body."
"I did a dance solo to 'The Archer,' so I (chose) a 'Lover' themed look," said Shea.
Zainab, a first-year university student, has been a Swiftie since childhood. She told the Star she skipped class on Thursday and will do soagain tomorrow, after she scored tickets to all three Eras concerts this week.
"It's 'Lover' themed," she said, of her pink puffed sleeve dress, "Miss Americana" sash and tiara. "It's my favourite album, you know?" - The Star's Asma Sahebzada
6:10 p.m. The line up for the Rogers Centre entrance on Bremner Boulevard went past the aquarium, north on Simcoe, west on Front Street and down Blue Jays Way. It winded a full square around the stadium and it took Star reporters over 10 minutes to walk the length of it.
5:45 p.m. At the Taylgate, fans once again assembled at the main stage at 4 p.m. for Taylor Swift trivia and a lyrics fill in the blanks trivia. Fans answered questions about Swift's family, career, friends and more.
In yet another side of the venue stood a small "train" onboard which there was a silent disco featuring songs from not only Swift but many major pop artists such as Charli XCX, One Direction, Ariana Grande and more.
4:55 p.m. At the Michael's craft store at John and Richmond Street, about a five minute walk from the Rogers Centre, store manager Kriti Madhukar says they've been sold out of some friendship bracelet material since last week.
People have even taken to sifting through returns before they can be put back on the shelf.
"We're so low on stock, I've suggested some of our Christmas (bracelets)," said Sarah Melkert, another manager.
4:37 p.m. As the clock hits 4:30 and the gates open, the crowd lets out a cheer - matched seconds later by the faint cheer around the corner, when another gate opens.
There's still more than two hours to go until Gracie Abrams opens the show at 6:45 p.m.
4:24 p.m. Swifties being shepherded to the downtown core could were both seen and felt as far north as Willowdale at 4 p.m. But it wasn't quite the typical rush hour because everyone was already packed further south.
Streets were mostly empty around Yonge and Sheppard. What would have been gridlocked intersections any other day of the year were set free. The closest Uber driver, typically one to two minutes away, was now 15 to 20.
A subway train descending from Line 1's terminal station at Finch, a typically packed experience this time of day, had plenty of seats. You could spot the difference between a North York-er just heading downtown or home, and the bedazzled Swifties on the subway. If the merch and friendship bracelets didn't give it away, the pink feathery jackets or black sequened jumpers did.
4 p.m. Jasmine Salve, from Germany, worked as security at Swift's concert in Munich.
She decided last week to fly to Toronto - without a ticket - to try to see it again. She's walking the streets right now, hopeful someone will notice her sign.
She said she'll be disappointed if she doesn't get a ticket, but is making the most of her trip by seeing the city and visiting Niagara Falls.
3:56 p.m. The line to get in to the Rogers Centre is long. For the east entrance, it wraps down Bremner Boulevard, up Lower Simcoe Street and is spilling on to Front Street.
It's so long that there's confusion about whether it's the line for Taylgate, at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre, or the concert itself.
Chloe St. Laurent, who drove with her two friends from Sherbrooke, Que. this morning, is expecting to wait an hour or longer.
3:53 p.m. On one side of the room at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre, fans lined up to get the number 13 tattooed on the back of their hand - a tradition at Swift's shows dating back to her earliest concerts. For her first two tours, Swift would write the number on her hand before each show. Thirteen is famously the singer's lucky number.
3:38 p.m. The Rogers Centre's security perimeter has just opened.
Fans are now, officially, lining up on stadium property. They'll all have to scan their tickets to enter the perimeter. Gates officially open at 4:30 p.m.
3:37 p.m. Outside the Fairmont Royal York, across the street from Union Station, hotel workers held a rally to bring attention to their difficulties in securing a new contract. They picked the first night of Swift's shows for this protest, as they hoped to gain some sympathy from fans who had to pay increased room rates during the concerts.
3:15 p.m. Taylor Swift fans are enchanted to meet her and ahead of the concert tonight the Star asked Swifties to share their favourite photos. Reader Marcello Cabezas share a photo and a tip for other fans. "There is an amazing SWIFTIE sign (not far) away from Rogers Centre - you can snap a selfie with the CN Tower in the background and also put a bracelet on the Friendship Wall. Located at 229 Richmond St. West at RendezViews.
Have a fan photo to share? Submit it here.
3:09 p.m. An unofficial line to enter Rogers Stadium has formed on Front Street, and now extends hundreds of metres west of the venue.
2:57 p.m. Tabitha and Summer, a mother-daughter duo, won the the Metro Convention Center costume contest with their "Lover"-inspired outfits. On stage, Tabitha surprised her daughter with the news that they were going to the show tonight. Summer says that she was left speechless. She says she is "super excited" and is excited to hear Swift sing "All Too Well (Ten Minute Version)" and "Vigilante Shit."
2:52 p.m. Union Station's concourse was steady but busy just before 3 p.m. Thursday as some commuters tried to escape the oncoming crush of fans.
"It looks a little like Christmas, with all the shiny dresses and beads," joked Marc Beaulieu, who was heading home from the office.
"We've been planning our outfits for a year, said Karlee Donovan, who was with daughter, Aria and her mother Corrina Merlin. "Its all about the glitter."
2:45 p.m. We're about an hour away from lineups opening at the Rogers Centre, two hours from gates opening and four hours until opening act Gracie Abrams goes on.
Crowds are continuing to build and road closures are in effect. Just in the last half hour, the unofficial lineup on the Rogers Centre's East side has extended to Ripley's Aquarium.
A few hot dog carts are set up outside the Rogers Centre. Sales are down right now, one operator said, but they're holding out for the crowds to pick up.
2:44 p.m. Fans, with and without tickets to the concert, assembled at the Metro Convention Center all dressed up in their Swift-inspired outfits. From friendship bracelet-making stations to photo booths, a beauty bar and more - the event had something for every Swiftie.
At 2:30, all eyes were on the main stage for the costume contest. People came dressed up in costumes inspired by different Swift Eras. Many fans had self-made costumes which included outfits Swift has worn in her music videos, concerts, as well as outfits inspired by song titles and lyrics.
2:36 p.m. Outside Union Station, a Sephora pop-up a long line of people waited to get touched up and pick up free samples.
The shift from downtown Toronto commuters trying to get out and Eras fan arriving for their concert experience was noticeable.
"We're just about to check out the Taylgate at the Convention Centre," said Samantha Shields, 19, from Mississauga, who was with a group of friends. "But the we'll probably head to the show around 5 or 6 p.m."
2:30 p.m. Alana and Anthony, Swifties from Syracuse, New York, are killing time outside of Rogers Centre as they wait for the doors to open. This is the couple's first time in Toronto as adults.
"Taylor and I are around the same age, so I grew up listening to her," Alana explains. "That makes the Eras Tour super relatable to me."
As for Anthony, he "married into" becoming a Swift fan, but he became a convert after seeing her perform at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey during the "Reputation" tour. "I was blown away," he recalls. "Now I have a young daughter, and she's obsessed with her, too. That's what we're jamming to on the way to school."
So what made them take the trek to Canada?
"We're just drawn to the magnitude of this tour," Alana says. "There are so many generations coming together, and it's just a real community experience."
2:15 p.m. Your complete guide to navigating Taylor Swift Eras Tour chaos in Toronto, from TTC and GO train intel to when to arrive at the Rogers Centre
It's been a longtime coming. Taylor Swift's Eras Tour opens tonight at the Rogers Centre. It marks the first of six sold-out shows that have been more than a year in the making and which are expected to bring Toronto's downtown core to a standstill.
Toronto is the second-last stop on Swift's two-year, 149-show, multibillion-dollar tour that long ago became the highest-grossing concert series of all time. Swift, the superstar of nearly two decades and author of hits including "I Knew You Were Trouble," "Shake It Off" and "Anti-Hero," is expected to bring more than 500,000 visitors to the downtown core and more than $282 million in economic impact.
For those inside the Rogers Centre, it will be an experience that costs thousands of dollars. For those outside, it could mean a frustrating commute, swamped local bars and packed city sidewalks.
Read the essential guide to navigating the Taylor Swift chaos from the Star's Mark Colley.
2:13 p.m. While Swiftmania picks up outside the Rogers Centre, Pamela Cordeiro is at home in Etobicoke.
She and her Grade 4 daughter, Shay, couldn't get tickets to the concert, so she decided instead to make the trip on Sunday to Buffalo for the Bills-Chiefs game, which Swift may attend to see her boyfriend, Chiefs tight end Travis Kelce.
"I got tickets on both sides of the stadium," Cordeiro said, ensuring they'd see Swift regardless. "I'm actually a little wild."
It will be both Cordeiro and Shay's first NFL game.
2:07 p.m. Bojan, a Toronto resident who has lived on Front Street for five years now, says he's "never seen anything like this."
Walking his dog, Phoebe, outside the Rogers Centre on Wednesday afternoon, Bojan says he's decided to embrace the chaos, rather than avoid it. "Traffic is going to be terrible, so I'm not happy about that," he says. "But the crowd is harmless, and it's an overall positive atmosphere."
As for Phoebe, she likes strangers. "She's enjoying herself."
2 p.m. Starting now, the following roads will be restricted to local traffic (residents and businesses) only:
Bremner Boulevard between Spadina Avenue and Simcoe StreetNorthbound lanes on Rees Street will be closed. Southbound lanes will remain open for two-way traffic including an accessible entry point.Blue Jays Way between Spadina Avenue and south of Front StreetThe Marriott Hotel will be accessible from Front Street via Blue Jays Way.Navy Court WharfAccess to condos will be via a police checkpoint at the Spadina Avenue and Bremner Boulevard intersection.Lake Shore Boulevard will be reduced to two westbound lanes at the intersection with Rees Street for a drop-off/pick-up point.
1:54 p.m. While the lineup officially opens at 3:30 p.m., Andi McCord and her daughter Keriann, 11, are among the handful of people already queuing.
They don't want to miss out on merch, which they tried to get yesterday but had already sold out. Keriann also wanted to trade friendship bracelets with the other early bird fans.
McCord's daughter Lauren, nine, is also here - but isn't going to the concert. Lauren chose a trip to New York instead of the Eras Tour. Still, McCord said, she's "very upset."
1:42 p.m. Taylor Swift is almost here, and Toronto's restaurants, bars and hotels understand the assignment, and that's to get into the spirit and theme of everything Swiftian.
Speaking of spirits and themes, there are a number of bars offering Swift themed cocktails and spaces inspired by Eras. The 1 Hotel has transformed their lobby bar, Flora, into The Folklore Lounge. Taking inspiration from the album cover, it had a natural aesthetic, with a moss-covered piano and lyrics from the album on parchment-like scrolls.
"We've been waiting for this for a long time, and Flora has become Folklore Lounge, and we'll have acoustic performances from Toronto musicians from 3 to 6 every day with Taylor-inspired music for guests to enjoy," said Liza McWilliams, marketing director for the hotel. "And the cocktails are all inspired from some of her most popular songs. Very playful, colourful, there are mocktails as well."
They include The 1, which is reportedly inspired by Swift's favourite drink, the French Blonde, and has gin and elderflower syrup.
Kost, the rooftop bar at Bisha Hotel has the Lavender Haze and the Midnight Rum. Up by Yorkville, the Park Hyatt is also featuring two themed cocktails, the Karma and the Alchemist.
The message to all Swifties is clear, drink up all that Toronto has to offer.
1:33 p.m. Marsha Stagg, bundled in blankets and sitting on a folding beach chair, said she's been waiting outside the Rogers Centre since 5:30 a.m. So far, her sign - "We Need Tickets" - hasn't worked.
But she's not ready to give up. Originally from Stephenville, Nfld., she flew in from Halifax and met six of her childhood friends in Toronto for the concert.
One of them, Kristen, has stage four breast cancer. The concert is a chance to reconnect and celebrate her, Stagg said.
Stagg hopes the box office will sell off extra tickets. If that doesn't work, she said she's willing to pay $750 on the resale market.
And if the whole plan falls apart, the group still has tickets to Sunday's Bills-Chiefs game in Buffalo, where Swift's boyfriend Travis Kelce will play. So either way, Stagg said, they'll see Swift at some point.
1:28 p.m. The Pint Public House at 277 Front Street is located the epicentre of Swiftmania. But Cesar Mesen, who owns the popular sports pub, says he's ready for action.
"We're not nervous," he tells me. "There are 82 Blue Jays games a year here, plus tonnes of concerts. We understand the magnitude."
The Pint, he adds, has been preparing for this moment for six months now. "We've got DJs and live entertainment. We had a Taylor Swift trivia last night. People are looking for hospitality and a place where they feel welcome. If you can provide them with that, you are good."
"It's not like a Leafs or Jays game, where there's a good chance that the team will lose and people will come in sad or angry," he adds. "(Swift fans) are happy both before and after the show."
"I think it's fantastic to have so many international travellers here, and we're ready to put our best food forward and represent Toronto the best we can."
1:16 pm. Starting now, westbound lanes on Bremner Blvd. between Rees Street and Navy Wharf Court are closed, while eastbound lanes remain open for two-way traffic.
Starting at 2 p.m. the following roads will be restricted to local traffic (residents and businesses) only:
Bremner Boulevard between Spadina Avenue and Simcoe StreetNorthbound lanes on Rees Street will be closed. Southbound lanes will remain open for two-way traffic including an accessible entry point.Blue Jays Way between Spadina Avenue and south of Front StreetThe Marriott Hotel will be accessible from Front Street via Blue Jays Way.Navy Court WharfAccess to condos will be via a police checkpoint at the Spadina Avenue and Bremner Boulevard intersection.Lake Shore Boulevard will be reduced to two westbound lanes at the intersection with Rees Street for a drop-off/pick-up point.
1 p.m. Just hours out from the first of Taylor Swift's six shows in Toronto there are still some tickets for night one on the market, and many fans have been holding out in hopes that more will become available and prices will drop.
For those trying to snag tickets from Ticketmaster, a few were made available on the platform last night to people who had already registered as verified fans for the Toronto shows.
On StubHub, the cheapest ticket currently available is going for just over $1,900, and it's standing room only. The spot is positioned slightly behind the main stage in the TD Park Social section of the Rogers Centre. Floor seat tickets are selling for between $6,000 to upwards of $20,000.
12:30 p.m. Who gets the Taylor Swift tickets after divorce? Lawyer shares couple's dilemma as Eras Tour arrives in Toronto
When a couple separates, who gets custody of their Eras Tour tickets?
A Toronto lawyer took to LinkedIn this week to share an awkward situation one couple found themselves in, after purchasing tickets to see Taylor Swift during their marriage, but separating before the concert.
"The issue was what to do with the tickets from a family law perspective," Anna-Marie Musson, managing lawyer of M. & Co Law Firm, wrote on social media. "Do they sell them and equalize the proceeds? Or who uses the tickets?"
It's a prickly issue, given that a single ticket to one of her Toronto shows can cost thousands of dollars on resale sites. Here's what happened to the couple.

11:22 a.m. The TTC was to make sure Swift fans are ready for it. In a post on X, the transit agency announced increased service on Line 1 and Line 2 as well as on the 504, 509, 511 streetcars and the 510D Spadina and 19 Bay buses.
11:02 a.m. Shauna Mason and her daughter, Adele, along with Alayne Burn and her daughter Aria, came from Calgary for the show. Decked out in Swift merch they got yesterday, their plan was to shop during the day, before getting their hair done and prepping for the show.
They were hitting up Queen Street West for all the stores' themed offerings.
"We're doing all the Swiftie stuff," said Shauna.
They had already hit Lululemon where she got a clear bag with a friendship bracelet, that she could take into the show.
Next was Brandy Melville, which is a favourite among Swift fans and had additional security.
Then there was Pink Palm HQ, which was giving out free hoodies.
11 a.m. Among the opening remarks at city council Thursday was a tribute from deputy mayor Ausma Malik to city staff who have spent months preparing for the Swift tsunami this week and next.
"I'm looking forward to us continuing to catch the excitement from the Eras Tour, to show the world what we have to offer here in Toronto," said the Spadina-Fort York councillor, adding that the city has learned lessons from this work applicable to hosting FIFA in 2026.
"Let's show everyone what we got and that we're ready for it."
The city is keeping costs for hosting Swift secret "for operational and security reasons" until her departure but is expected to have spent millions of dollars setting up for the shows - and keeping an eye out for potential threats to safety.
Malik solicited applause for "the biggest Swiftie on council," fellow deputy mayor Jennifer McKelvie, who was behind the christening of "Taylor Swift Way" downtown, a charity stunt to support the Daily Bread Food Bank that Malik said has already raised $50,000.
Even the mayor couldn't help getting swept up in Swiftmania. She said she recently went to a Toronto Public Library friendship bracelet making event where she crafted eight for herself and traded some with fans.
"It really celebrated the spirit of kindness and sharing," said Mayor Olivia Chow, her wrist covered in eight bracelets. She offered one to Speaker Frances Nunziata.
Chow said with a laugh that she has a bracelet that says 'Taylor bike lane,' a nod to her feud with Premier Doug Ford over bike lane placement in the city. - Ben Cohen and Mahdis Habibinia
10:55 a.m. In a statement on X, Ontario's minister of tourism, culture and gaming, Stan Cho welcomed Taylor Swift and Swifties to the province's capital today. Cho said the singer's six-concert stint in Toronto is expected to be a boon to the economy.
10:52 a.m. Taylor Swift isn't the only game in town. Here's how other musicians playing Toronto are dealing with her ubiquity
For the next week and a half, Swift will not be the only game in town, though it may feel that way. Ocie Elliott is just one of the many artists playing shows at smaller venues who have to contend with the issues that come with Swift's unprecedented run of six dates at the Rogers Centre.
Ocie Elliott said they know very well how difficult it is to find a place to stay in Toronto and were aware of the high hotel costs during the period Swift would be in town - a price surge that has concerned some Swifties who are still seeking accommodations.

10:49 a.m. Two years ago, there was a gas explosion at the home of Claire Ferguson, a Swiftie from Welland. The explosion, caused by a incorrectly installed stove igniter, knocked her mother unconscious.
Following the accident, Claire and her mother received a small settlement, which they used to buy tickets to the Eras Tour in Toronto. "It's kind of like my birthday celebration," says Claire, who turns 20 next week.
"Taylor just makes me so happy," Claire says. "Whenever I'm having a hard mental time, I listen to her music."
What's she most excited about for tonight?
"Getting to spend time with my mom."

8:33 a.m. A group of hotel service workers in Toronto is set to hold a rally today outside the Fairmont Royal York to demand salary increases as hotel costs in the city skyrocket during Taylor Swift's concerts.
Unite Here Local 75, the union representing 8,000 hospitality workers in the Greater Toronto Area, says Royal York employees have not seen a salary increase since 2021, and have been negotiating a new contract with the hotel since 2022.
The rally comes as the megastar begins her series of six sold-out concerts in Toronto, with the last show scheduled for Nov. 23.

Taylor Swift's Eras Tour is finally here. A Toronto survival guide for visiting Swifties
We are excited to have you and not at all fretting about traffic or Starbucks congestion. Are you jazzed? Ready to trigger seismic activity under the Rogers Centre? Ready to scream your lungs out as Taylor Swift finally brings her Eras Tour to town?
Here are some tips and fun facts to make your stay more enjoyable.
Rule No. 1: Leave plenty of time. If you are going to Thursday's first show and the hotel concierge says it should take about 30 minutes, that person is a liar. Double all estimated travel times. Due to construction, Toronto has more lane closures than Florida has banned books. The Eglinton LRT line is now expected to open when Taylor and Travis are grandparents.
Read more from the Star's Vinay Menon
Worried about stolen Taylor Swift Toronto tickets? Here's how to protect your Ticketmaster account
Emma Schmelzle was one of the lucky ones. Last August, she and her brother snagged tickets to Taylor Swift's Eras Tour in Toronto for $91 each. The seats were partially obstructed, but the tickets were still a steal. A single Eras Toronto ticket is now selling for close to $3,000 at their cheapest on resale markets.
For more than a year, Schmelzle thought she was in the clear. Then, in September, her brother received an email: their tickets had been transferred out of his Ticketmaster account. Their chance to see one of the biggest tours in history was gone.
Working with Ticketmaster support in the week that followed, Schmelzle and her brother got the tickets back. But that scare - plus another a month later, when it appeared the tickets had been stolen again - has shaken Schmelzle's faith in Ticketmaster.
Read the full story from the Star's Mark Colley
Most Toronto events geared at Swifties not officially 'Taylor's Version'
When fans converge on a convention centre in downtown Toronto this week, they'll be talking about Taylor Swift and listening to her music, but they won't see her name or photo in any official capacity - instead, the event is both literally and figuratively adjacent to the pop star and her mega-popular Eras Tour.
Though it may seem like a sheen of Swift is coating the city of Toronto, only some of the hoopla can really be counted as "Taylor's Version."
"This is adjacent to the brand, but this is not about Taylor," said Bram Goldstein, organizer of the unofficial Swiftie event "Toronto's Version: Taylgate '24."

Everything you need to know if you're going to the Eras Tour in Toronto
Toronto will become the centre of the Taylor Swift universe as the international megastar plays the Rogers Centre for six sold-out shows.
It's been a long time coming. Rogers spent millions on renovations. Toronto renamed the path from Nathan Phillips Square to the Rogers Centre "Taylor Swift Way." City staff even activated the emergency operations centre, just in case anything goes wrong.
For good reason. The city is expecting up to 500,000 visitors during the concerts and an economic impact of more than $282 million.

Toronto moves homeless near Rogers Centre 'to help ensure their safety' ahead of Taylor Swift concerts
The city of Toronto is clearing tents belonging to homeless people near the Rogers Centre in preparation for Taylor Swift's Eras Tour. The first of Swift's six sold-out concerts is on Thursday.
"The individuals surrounding Rogers Centre are being offered a space in the city's shelter system to help ensure their safety," a city spokesperson said in an email.
The city said only two people were living in the area. One of them, Chris - who goes by Crystofur and didn't want their last name used due to their circumstances - told the Star on Tuesday they were being moved to a hotel paid for by the city.

Originally from Queensland, Australia, and now living in London, England, Bridget had flown to Toronto for the Eras Tour - her 15th time seeing the concert - with parents Lisa and Spock.

How much will Taylor Swift's visit cost the city?
Taylor Swift is expected to cost Toronto millions in extra services such as transit and policing but the exact amount will remain a secret - for now.
The TTC said it anticipates a bill of $1 million to $1.2 million across the six shows scheduled for the Rogers Centre.
That amount is going toward adding a "significant amount of extra service," said Stuart Green, spokesperson for the transit agency, in an email to the Star.

The Toronto Humane Society is celebrating the arrival of Taylor Swift with a unique cat adoption campaign inspired by the singer.
The charity has welcomed seven new kittens, Kelce, Reputation, Dorothea, Benjamin, Taylor, Karma, and Travis, all named after some of Swift's famous lyrics and themes.
These kittens join 10 shelter cats, Folklore, Red, Midnights, Willow, August, Evermore, Champagne, Swiftie, Marjorie, and Cornelia, who have also been named as a tribute to the singer.

Cost of removing bike lanes also includes visits to emergency wards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cost-removing-bike-lanes-also-includes-visits/docview/3128554836/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cost+of+removing+bike+lanes+also+includes+visits+to+emergency+wards&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Removing bike lanes will cost taxpayers $48M, city estimates, Nov.14
If the lanes are removed, cyclists will understandably use the sidewalks for their own safety - and cause danger to pedestrians.There's no way to know how many near misses there are, but perhaps someone is counting bike-pedestrian collisions. It must cost the province a lot to patch people up in emergency wards after accidents.
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
At least finish the Eglinton subway line before destroying with the bike lane network
Is it too much to hope that Premier Doug Ford would, for a change, follow scientific data instead of his gut instinct on transit plans? Where is the respect for Toronto's developed network of bicycle lanes? Ensuring that the Eglinton subway line is completed should be more important than eliminating bike lanes or building tunnels. Focusing on highways that will only encourage more automobile congestion is yesterday's playbook. Former Conservative leader Mike Harris was also unable to visualize the future when he filled in the Eglinton subway which similarly put Toronto's transit planning back many years.
Jonas Brand, Oakville
Bike travel on secondary streets is a reasonable option
The Star's reporting on bike lanes has been accompanied by several different pictures. What they have in common is the almost complete lack of bicycles and an endless line of cars. Rush hour traffic with literally two or three bicycles commandeering an entire extra lane. Designing East/West and North/South routes for bike travel on secondary streets seems more reasonable than dedicated lanes on major arteries. Common sense says it's better to add travel time to a few cyclists than it is to inconvenience thousands of daily commuters.
Don Mustill, Niagara on the Lake,
Ford should stay in his own lane
I do not understand Premier Doug Ford's focus on eliminating certain bike lanes in Toronto. Surely that is a municipal matter. Ford has enough issues to deal with at Queen's Park without interfering in Toronto's business. There is no evidence to support his claim that removal of bike lanes will ease traffic congestion, and it would be an expensive undertaking. Ford is a supposed conservative, but he does not show conservatism in his spending priorities. In any case he should stay in his own lane and leave Toronto to its elected officials.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
Synchronize traffic lights and bike lanes won't be an issue
Remove the bike lanes and this is what occurs: When drivers have two lanes, there are those who zip by, at great speed along the curb lane until a parked car blocks their lane or maybe a bike rider is in the way. Then they push their way into the line of patient, civilized drivers in the centre lane. This happened the other day as I drove easterly from west of the Humber River towards the Bloor and Jane intersection where a long line of cars waited patiently starting at the bridge. The traffic lights seem to change their rhythm to shorter intervals and all the traffic changed to moving along smoothly. It's not the bike lanes that slow traffic - they keep the bike riders safe and car drivers in line. A much simpler andlikely less expensive solution would be tosynchronize traffic lights to traffic conditions.
Helga Weick, Toronto
Wage controls have been implemented regularly. Why not price controls? Ahead of Taylor Swift Eras Tour, city moves homeless people near Rogers Centre 'to help ensure their safety,' Nov. 13
Displacing people already displaced with nowhere to call home is another illustration of how little leaders care about homelessness. For a concert? Leave them alone. They are suffering enough already. Decades of pro Big Business politics have put far too many decent people on the street. Why, when business build their products in countries with low wages, are they permitted to charge exorbitant prices far above their actual cost? Wage controls have been implemented regularly. Why not price controls? This is the reason for the ever-widening gap between prices and wages. Those we elect are supposed to prevent this type exploitation. Instead, they encourage it. It is time those responsible be held accountable for the damage they have caused to our economy and for all the human beings they continue to hurt.
Rick Lockman, Orillia
Crossing guards are more that safety beaconsCity councillors stopping to look both ways in bid to rein in crossing guard costs, Nov. 12
I sometimes drive my daughter to an 8.30 a.m. class at Centennial College and want to give a shout out to some civil servants who are very visible at that time of day. Crossing guards are at every street corner near our schools, and as well as being safety beacons for our youngsters, they seem to interact with them nicely and give a positive start to the day. Public money well spent I'd say.
Beverly Jones, Scarborough
Psychedelic medicines are highly effective treatmentsThese patients are considering MAiD. They say Canada has made it harder to access magic mushrooms - a treatment giving them reason to live, Nov. 12
I write in response to Omar Mosleh's informed and insightful article comparing the ease of getting approval for MAiD in Canada with the difficulty of getting approval for psilocybin therapy for end-of-life distress. Psilocybin, the active ingredient in magic mushrooms, should not be treated as if it is less safe than death. Hundreds of scientific studies have shown psilocybin therapy is both safe and highly effective under the supervised care of trained health-care professionals. The Psychedelic Association of Canada (PAC) concurs and supports Mr. Mosleh's analysis.
The PAC educates the public and government officials about the safety, benefits and science behind psychedelic therapies. As Mosleh pointed out, there are many challenges, delays and uncertainties in applying for medical exemptions to legally receive psilocybin therapy. This results in many Canadians travelling to jurisdictions where psilocybin can be legally accessed or seeking treatments in gray markets. Those are expensive and sometimes uncertain options. We know the government does not intend that patients should have to choose between their health and adhering to the law. We encourage more educational and informative stories like this one and active discussions with government to increase access to these medicines for those who need it most.
Jay Katz, Psychedelic Association of Canada, Toronto
CAPTION: The bike lanes along University Avenue in the approach to Queen's Park are in the cross hairs of Premier Doug Ford's Bill 212 in Toronto.

Document 182 of 500

On Trump, Ford's instincts are correct

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-trump-fords-instincts-are-correct/docview/3128554686/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+Trump%2C+Ford%27s+instincts+are+correct&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So it turns out Doug Ford is basically right. A lot of people jumped on the premier this week for pointing out that Mexico is the weak link among the Three Amigos in the North American trading partnership and suggesting Canada should work out its own two-way deal with the United States.
Ford, they said, is straying out of his lane. He's freelancing instead of acting as a loyal member of "Team Canada" in pushing back against Donald Trump's America First-ism.
But Ford was just saying out loud what others are thinking. Trump blames Mexico for allowing itself to be used as a backdoor for cheap Chinese imports into the U.S., but all he's doing is shouting what the Biden administration says in more polite tones.
If Ford was way off base, you'd expect the Trudeau government to point that out quite firmly. But it didn't. On the contrary, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland conceded the premier's main point. She said she's heard those worries about Mexico not being "aligned" against China and added: "I think those are legitimate concerns for our American partners and neighbours to have. Those are concerns that I share."
Freeland, no surprise, wouldn't be drawn into speculating about a separate Canada-U.S. deal somewhere down the road. But it might eventually come to that if Mexico doesn't clean up its act. And in the meantime it's useful for Ford to send a strong signal to Trump that he shouldn't lump Canada (Ontario, really) in with Mexico as he goes about putting up a high tariff wall against China.
In fact, Mexico was always an awkward fit with Canada and the U.S. in a continental trade deal. Part of the idea was that investment would flow into Mexico, speed development and raise wages there. But three decades after NAFTA went into effect the wage gap between Mexico and the U.S./Canada is as wide as ever.
Canada and the United States are natural partners, with similar standards of living and complementary economies. That's the message our governments have always pressed home with Washington and will continue to push with Trump. Adding Mexico to the mix muddies the waters, and it's quite possible the three-way deal won't survive a second Trump presidency.
We're learning some other things very quickly about the impact of Hurricane Donald. We knew Trump and the people around him don't particularly care for Canada and its government: too lefty, sanctimonious, "woke," etc. But it still comes as a shock to hear the depth of contempt they have for this country and for Justin Trudeau in particular.
Most of the big names Trump nominated this week for top positions have had things to say about Canada, and it's not good. Mike Waltz, his pick for national security adviser, tweeted that Pierre Poilievre will "send Trudeau packing in 2025 (finally) and start digging Canada out of the progressive mess it's in." And Tulsi Gabbard, his nominee as director of national intelligence, has called Trudeau "the autocratic leader in Canada who has resorted to genuinely authoritarian and tyrannical means" during the pandemic.
There's more: Trump's new "border czar," Tom Homan, calls the U.S. northern border a "huge national security issue." His pick as secretary of state, Marco Rubio, and his choice for UN ambassador, Elise Stefanik, have both warned about terrorists entering the U.S. through a leaky Canadian border.
All of which adds up to a generalized mistrust bordering on contempt for Canada and its leaders coming from the team Trump is forming around him. If Poilievre wins the next election, the likes of Waltz and Gabbard won't have Trudeau to kick around anymore. But there really does seem to be a deeper disdain for this country that goes way beyond disagreements over policy.
No doubt that's partly just the mean-spirited attitude Trump and his loyalists feel toward anyone or any country that doesn't fall into line behind them. There'll be plenty of contempt to go around and there's probably not much any Canadian government can do about that.
The experts will point out that the economic fundamentals mean Canada and the U.S. will keep doing business, regardless. But the early signs for the relationship are, if anything, worse than expected. No wonder Ford is scrambling to get on the good side of Team Trump.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca;

Document 183 of 500

WNBA expansion announcement in Toronto celebrated the future, while saluting the past

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wnba-expansion-announcement-toronto-celebrated/docview/3128531202/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WNBA+expansion+announcement+in+Toronto+celebrated+the+future%2C+while+saluting+the+past&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Editor's note: Each year, the sports department contributes to the Toronto Star Santa Claus Fund via Proudfoot Corner. This annual drive allows children from financially disadvantaged families across the GTA to enjoy a gift box Christmas morning.
Proudfoot Corner is our long-standing tradition of recognizing donors. This year's theme to go with this weekly feature is a behind-the-scenes look at some of the stories our journalists worked on.
The official announcement in May of a WNBA expansion team in Toronto was a perfect opportunity to think about the future ramifications.
It was, as many of us wrote, a chance to be the "Carter Effect" moment on the women's game here in years to come. The 2026 Toronto team should be a perfect springboard for the women's game and the WNBA, here and across Canada. We saw what the arrival of the Raptors in 1995, and Vince Carter four years later, did for the NBA and the men's game.
I don't know whether the Toronto franchise will contest a WNBA championship - it took the New York Liberty 28 seasons to win their first title when they beat the Minnesota Lynx in a compelling series this fall - but at least the chance exists, and who knows who could make a similar impact as Carter did two decades ago.
When I was at that glitzy news conference at the downtown Toronto hotel,it was easy to think about the history of the women's game in this country, and how bygone eras established a record of international success.
These were women I've written about for decades, the trailblazers of the game in Canada. One of the enduring memories of that morning was watching them bask in the WNBA arrival, all proud of the part they'd done to bring the day alive. Those in attendance included:
 Sylvia Sweeney, at one time one of the top handful of women's players on the planet.
 Tammy Sutton-Brown, a WNBA all-star and one of Canada's best and under-appreciated players.
 Stacey Dales, a truly great player in her time and a 2000 Olympian.
 Tamara Tatham, a two-time Olympian now giving back as a high-level coach.
It was good - and also important - to see those greats gathered together to usher in the future after such an impact they had on the past in Toronto and Canada. The stars of tomorrow are standing on the shoulders of those greats who helped lay the groundwork for their future success.
Sure, it was predictable that the politicians - including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow - showed up to get on the bandwagon (having to be at the venue two hours early for a mandatory security check took some of the good feeling away). But the moment was, deservedly, about basketball. And women's basketball.
"I just got chills," Sutton-Brown told me. "Who would have thought? Dreams really do come true."
Said Tatham: "I looked up to Michael Jordan growing up, the New York Knicks, the Chicago Bulls ... now these young girls are going to get to see women in their country playing the sport they love.
"You get to look up to people, you get to look up to women, I think that's an amazing opportunity for our country and athletes."
I think it's long due that Toronto has had a WNBA franchise and full credit to Larry Tanenbaum for making it happen.
But seeing those greats from the past, happy to see them recognized for getting the game where it is today, was a great part of the story.
On the corner: We start off this week with Jim Dadson who's been a Proudfoot donor for more than two decades. This year, he gives $105 in honour of John Faveri, long-time member of the Thursday night "Write Offs" ... There's always a lot of support from former Toronto Star family and this week we get $200 from Bob Hepburn, $105 from Chris Zelkovich and $250 from John C.P. King.
James Vlahos gives $250 in memory of "My dad, Nick Vlahos, die-hard Leaf fan" ... Diane and David Baker donate $50 ... Patricia Roche chips in $105 ... Cathy and Ronald Plese help out with $350 ... and another $350 comes from Maureen Moloney in memory of Frank and Patricia Moloney ... Tom and Sandy Cloutier help out with $250 ... Larry Green gives $40 in memory of Jays fans Wes and Annie Green ... Monique Kondziela donates $105 in memory of H. Kondziela ... finally this week, we get $200 from Janice Robinson in memory of "my father, Maurice Marin, who loved everything about the Christmas season." ... Many thanks to all!
CAPTION: From left, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, WNBA commissioner Cathy Engelbert, team owner Larry Tanenbaum, Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow were among the big names at the May news conference announcing the league's expansion team in Toronto.The WNBA announced it was expanding to Toronto at a news conference in May. A panel for a Q and A was held after which included (from left): host and five-year WNBA veteran Stacey Dales, league commissioner Cathy Engelbert, WNBA Toronto president Teresa Resch and 12-year WNBA veteran Tammy Sutton-Brown.

Document 184 of 500

Ontario announces $100M deal with Musk's Starlink

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-announces-100m-deal-with-musks-starlink/docview/3128531123/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+announces+%24100M+deal+with+Musk%27s+Starlink&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has signed a $100-million deal with Elon Musk's SpaceX to deliver high-speed internet to remote residents in rural and northern Ontario.
The new program called ONSAT - which stands for Ontario Satellite - will bring SpaceX's Starlink satellite internet system to 15,000 premises, said Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
"These communities will gain access to high-speed satellite internet capable of supporting streaming video calls, online gaming and more activities that have become second nature to so many of us," Surma said.
The service will be used to connect people in the hardest-to-reach areas of the province to the internet, she said. The system will go live next June, with eligibility and registration starting in the spring.
The province will cover equipment and installation costs, but not monthly fees.
"It is an investment of close to $100 million," Surma said.
SpaceX won after a "robust and transparent and competitive and fair technical and financial evaluation of multiple qualified parties," said Michael Lindsay, CEO of Infrastructure Ontario.
Indigenous "engagement and participation" is part of the contract Infrastructure Ontario signed with SpaceX, he said.
"SpaceX is going to engage directly with Indigenous communities to ensure equal access to the program and to create socio-economic opportunities through employment contracting and training opportunities," Lindsay said.
Installation is relatively straightforward and fast, said Joel Cherkis, who runs the business operations for Starlink.
The move is part of the province's $4-billion plan to deliver high-speed internet to every corner of Ontario. It has so far invested $2.5 billion in 270 projects, Surma said.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 185 of 500

Trump ally thinks Canada-U.S. bond will remain strong: Steel firm CEO says tough talk shouldn't apply to neighbour

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-ally-thinks-canada-u-s-bond-will-remain/docview/3128531115/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+ally+thinks+Canada-U.S.+bond+will+remain+strong%3A+Steel+firm+CEO+says+tough+talk+shouldn%27t+apply+to+neighbour&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chrystia Freeland has called him a "proof point" of the enduring Canada-U.S. relationship. Fortune Magazine has asked if he could be the Elon Musk of steel.
But Lourenco Goncalves says Canada's trade partnership with the U.S. can thrive during Donald Trump's second administration because of the close ties between the two nations.
"I really have conviction that the economic, political and social relationship between our two countries is the real deal," he said in an interview with the Star.
Goncalves is the chair, CEO and president of Cleveland-Cliffs, a major steel producer in the U.S. that just this month completed a purchase of Canadian steel company Stelco. He said he's committed to keeping Stelco's name and the company's headquarters in Hamilton.
Born in Brazil, Goncalves rose through the metals industry there before moving to several U.S. firms.
When he took over Cleveland-Cliffs in 2014, the company was a small player, but is now the largest flat rolled steel company in North America.
During the first Trump administration, Canada faced tariffs on steel and aluminum that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau called insulting.
On the campaign trail this time, Trump pledged 10 per cent tariffs on all goods entering the U.S.
The day after Trump's re-election, Canada's finance minister said Goncalves' acquisition of Stelco is proof that the trade relationship will be fine.
"He has been a long-standing supporter of President Trump. He chose to acquire a major Canadian steelmaker. Now, would he do that if he did not believe in the fundamentals of the economic relationship between our two countries? I don't think so," Freeland said.
Cleveland-Cliffs' political action committee has supported both Democratic and Republican candidates. In an interview on CNBC just days before the 2024 election, he said both Trump and U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris understand the value of his industry.
Goncalves told the Star Freeland is representing him appropriately, and that Trump's tough talk on trade shouldn't apply to Canada.
"Canada and the U.S. should not even be in the conversation when we talk about other countries. We see the two countries really as the only close ones that you could claim in this complicated world," he said.
He said Canada's economy is closely connected to the U.S. and also has critical minerals America needs, which it is currently buying from unfriendly countries like Russia and China.
"I feel like it's good practice to carve out Canada, but it's not a favour, it's a necessity."
Despite that close relationship, many of Trump's early picks for his new administration have been highly critical of Canada.
Congresswoman Elise Stefanik, who has been picked as UN ambassador, said Canada wasn't living up to its trade obligations. Mike Waltz, Trump's choice for national security adviser, cheered a clip of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre attacking Trudeau in question period, writing, "This guy is going to send Trudeau packing in 2025."
Trump's likely pick for secretary of state, Marco Rubio, was highly critical of Canada's program to allow Gazans to come to Canada on temporary visas.
Goncalves said he feels the people around Trump understand that on trade, Canada is different, and highlighted Ottawa's decision to match U.S. tariffs on Chinese steel, aluminum and electric vehicles.
"That's the type of thing that I believe the future Trump administration will appreciate," he said.
Goncalves said too many U.S. allies say the right things, but their actions try to undercut the U.S. economy.
"We don't like countries that talk out of both sides of their mouths," he said, singling out Mexico and Japan as two U.S. allies that speak positively about the U.S., but make economic moves that hurt America's economy.
"These fake friends or friends of convenience will not have a good time when President Trump goes back to the White House."
Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that Canada should consider a new trade agreement with the U.S. alone, leaving Mexico out of the trade deal. Mexico has not matched Canada and U.S. tariffs on steel, aluminum and EVs and Ford argued they were being used to skirt tariffs.
"If Mexico won't fight trans-shipment by, at the very least, matching Canadian and American tariffs on Chinese imports, they shouldn't have a seat at the table or enjoy access to the largest economy in the world," he said on social media.
Goncalves said Ford's suggestion is in line with what the new Trump administration will want.
"That's actually extremely in sync with Donald Trump's playbook. He doesn't like multilateral trade agreements. He likes bilateral trade agreements."
Freeland was more circumspect, but in a news conference Wednesday said she has heard similar concerns about Mexico from people in the Trump administration and she shares them.
"I have heard from them, actually. I've heard also from the Biden administration, and I've heard also from U.S. business leaders some real concerns about whether Mexico is fully aligned when it comes to its policies vis-à-vis China."
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star

Document 186 of 500

Here's why Doug Ford is trampling on federal and municipal territory on the campaign trail

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-why-doug-ford-is-trampling-on-federal/docview/3128531028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+Doug+Ford+is+trampling+on+federal+and+municipal+territory+on+the+campaign+trail&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Electorally, he's the mighty premier of Ontario.
Locally, Doug Ford acts like the aspiring mayor of Toronto.
Nationally, he talks like the unofficial prime minister of Canada.
One day, our premier is badmouthing Mexico's unfair trade practices. Next day, he's trash-talking Toronto's bike lanes.
Forget guardrails. Ford refuses to stay in his legislative lane, and why not?
Every wayward bike path or economic bump in the road is a winning wedge for him. By bigfooting Olivia Chow and stepping on Justin Trudeau's turf, the premier is taking the path of least resistance - and gaining traction on the campaign trail ahead.
Beyond local bikes and foreign cars, the premier can't resist wading in on needle exchanges, high interest rates, the digital services tax and the federal carbon levy.
While his political critics keep flailing over his overreach and overlap - I'm one of them - there's method to his madness. More than ever, Ford's job description is all over the map, but watch how he's connecting the dots and data points.
The premier who would be mayor also wants to be prime minister, but that's nothing new. What's changed is that his counterparts at city hall and on Parliament Hill keep gifting him gifts that keep on giving - openings that any opportunist can't help but exploit.
Nothing personal. In truth, Ford is fond of Trudeau and chummy with Chow.
But the premier isn't interested in being popular with the mayor or prime minister. He's a populist who reads the polls to tally his popularity where it counts - with voters.
Ford understands that regular all-weather cyclists amount to a tiny sliver of the population across the region. As my colleague Robert Benzie has reported, it's not just the census data but the polling numbers that point the way: A recent public opinion survey for the Star by Abacus Data bears out the driving divide - commuting cyclists are in the low single digits.
Full disclosure: I love my bike lanes on Yonge Street, but I don't see them as a birthright, given that I was the only cyclist in sight two chilly mornings this week (Uber Eats e-bikers were the only users I came upon in the afternoon rush hour), while cars were jammed bumper to bumper for blocks. Removing the paint and stanchions on the Yonge bike lanes should cost no more than rejigging the zigzagging CaféTO curb lanes after every summer of disruption, although costs would obviously be higher elsewhere.
Predictably, city hall is trying to win this war of words with new numbers churned out by the same municipal planners who gave us the lanes. Remarkably, they claim will cost roughly twice as much to take down what they built up, adding up to tens of millions of dollars (albeit absorbed by Queen's Park).
Interestingly, when Toronto of its own volition removed questionable bike lanes on Jarvis Street and Brimley Road, the proportions were reversed - removal cost half as much as construction, and the tab tended to be in the low six figures. Admittedly, those car lanes were easily restored with a fresh coat of paint, but it's a helpful reminder for next time that not all bike paths deserve to be carved in stone (and cement) by city planners, lest their ridership forecasts falter.
In truth, most people could care less about which level of government is making the decision - as long as it's the right call and makes their lives easier. Remember, though, this isn't truly a fight to improve traffic, it's pre-election distraction by Ford's Tories to get people talking about bike lanes instead of big issues.
Even when the premier prattles on about the bigger issues, he tends toward gratuitous talking points that come cheap. High interest rates are a federal matter, yet Ford has fired off countless letters to Ottawa demanding action, knowing full well that the central bank operates at arm's length; the federal carbon levy is fully rebated to most Canadian families, yet the premier claims it hits commuters in the pocket.
The reason Ford keeps ranting about the problems of other politicians is that he knows bike lanes play better than the Greenbelt on the campaign trail. Personally, I'd let the mayor make her own mistakes, but Ford loves nothing more than meddling, because he never seems to pay a political price - while cheerfully agreeing to absorb all costs of restoring car lanes.
The premier may have found a winner while the mayor is playing a losing hand, but all is not lost for Chow. After all, when all is said and done and dismantled, the province will be paying for the city's mistakes.
CAPTION: Hundreds of cyclists gathered on Nov. 9, 2024 at Christie Pits to protest against the Ford government's move to remove bike lanes in Toronto.

Document 187 of 500

Survey suggests cycling rates are higher than cited

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/survey-suggests-cycling-rates-are-higher-than/docview/3128530835/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Survey+suggests+cycling+rates+are+higher+than+cited&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A major survey led by the province suggests cycling rates in Toronto are higher than Premier Doug Ford's government has asserted, according to a confidential city staff report seen by the Star.
The question of how many people use bicycles to get around the city has become a topic of heated debate as the Ontario PCs pursue legislation to dismantle bike lanes on some of Toronto's major streets.
The provincial bill was the subject of debate at a council meeting Thursday, where members voted to get information about their options to launch a legal challenge against it.
Ford's government has cited a figure that just 1.2 per cent of Toronto residents commute by bike as justification for the plan, which would remove lanes on sections of Bloor Street, Yonge Street, and University Avenue.
The 1.2 per cent figure was drawn from the 2011 census and applies to the Toronto Census Metropolitan Area, which encompasses municipalities outside the city proper, includingsmall towns and semirural locations, such as Uxbridge and King, as well as car-centric suburbs such as Brampton.
According to city staff, results of the Transportation Tomorrow Survey tell a different story.
The survey is conducted every five years in partnership with the province, TTC, Metrolinx and municipalities in the Greater Golden Horseshoe, and is used by the Ontario Ministry of Transportation to "support planning for transportation infrastructure," according to the project website.
The ministry has yet to publicly release results from the most recent edition, which is supposed to be published this fall. Last week the ministry told the Star its goal is still to release it this year, but didn't say when.
However, as a participant in the study, the city has access to its data, and staff compiled a report based on the results. It was included as a confidential attachment to a public report that went to council Thursday.
According to the attachment, which has been reviewed by the Star, the survey found that, in 2022, 5.8 per cent of commuters in the city took bikes or other forms of "micromobility" to work.
Micromobility can refer to electric bikes, mopeds, e-scooters and other small devices. About 45 per cent of commuters drove or were passengers in cars, while 37 per cent took transit.
Looking not just at commuting journeys, cycling and micromobility accounted for 4.4 per cent of all trips in the city, according to the survey, while drivers and passengers accounted for about 57 per cent.
Cycling and micromobility rates were significantly higher for trips ending downtown, where many of Toronto's bike lanes are concentrated. Just more than nine per cent of all trips that started in the city and ended downtown were taken by bike or micromobility device, compared to about 40 per cent for transit and 25 per cent for car drivers and passengers.
The survey also found the number of cycling trips taken in the city every day has more than tripled in the past two decades, from about 55,000 in 2001 to more than 172,000 in 2022.
A spokesperson for the ministry said they couldn't immediately respond to the figures because they hadn't seen the confidential report.
Asked about the figures, Coun. Josh Matlow (Ward 12, Toronto-St. Paul's), said it was "shameful" that Ford's government "buried evidence that doesn't fit his narrative."
In the public portion of the report debated Thursday, city staff asserted that removing the three bike lanes on the province's list and replacing them with car lanes would cost an estimated $48 million.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation project design, told the meeting the city figures were "very preliminary," but estimated about $20 million would be spent on reconstructing about 3.5 kilometres of University Avenue, Queen's Park Crescent and Bloor Street that were recently overhauled to add bike lanes.
The other $28 million would be spent to remove lane markings, signs and curbs and on performing resurfacing, on 18.5 kilometres of roadways that hadn't recently been rebuilt.
The province, which has promised to pay for the cost of bike lane removal, disputed the city's numbers.
Ford's office noted that removing bike lanes on Jarvis Street cost just $300,000, and the city had estimated the price tag for resurfacing a 300-metre portion of Ossington Avenue at only $805,000.
Although the premier's office didn't provide its own estimate for removing the lanes, it pointed out that by the city's own projections it would cost $21 million more to dismantle them than the $27 million they had cost to install.
No matter the cost of removal, it would be "significantly lower than the $11 billion the Toronto Region Board of Trade estimates the city loses every year due to congestion," Ford's office said.
A large majority of councillors approved a motion from Mayor Olivia Chow that formally expressed council's opposition to Ontario's bike lane bill, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, and requested the city solicitor to review "the potential for commencing litigation" against it.
Chow described the legislation as a violation of council's authority to make decisions about its road network.
"No matter where you stand, whether you support bike lanes or don't support bike lanes, I think it's important that this council and the people of Toronto have the respect they deserve," she said.
The mayor said her motion didn't mean that "tomorrow we're going to go to court," but she wanted council to know its options.
The province generally has broad power to impose decisions on the city.
Etobicoke Centre Coun. Stephen Holyday, who voted against Chow's motion, argued council had invited Ford's intervention by ignoring local residents' anger at the western extension of the Bloor West bike lanes.
He argued Etobicoke drivers had been stuck in "traffic paralysis," but the city wasn't taking their concerns seriously, and the province had stepped in to put it right.
"Council broke it. They broke Bloor. They broke it real bad. That's why we're here today," Holyday said.
Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson for Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, said in a statement that gridlock has reached "crisis levels" in Toronto, and, "The removal of lanes of traffic on our busiest roads ... has only made gridlock worse."
She encouraged the city "to listen to the thousands of drivers to help clear our major roads and get people out of traffic."
With files from Kate Allen, Robert Benzie and Ben Cohen
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's government has cited a figure that just 1.2 per cent of Toronto residents commute by bike as justification for removing bike lanes on some city streets. But a more recent survey puts the number at 5.8 per cent.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief
