Boards warned to watch expenses: No room for unnecessary or extravagant costs, minister says in call with Ontario school officials

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/boards-warned-watch-expenses/docview/3128530712/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Boards+warned+to+watch+expenses%3A+No+room+for+unnecessary+or+extravagant+costs%2C+minister+says+in+call+with+Ontario+school+officials&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has warned school boards there is "no room for unnecessary or extravagant expenses" amid new reports of questionable international trips taken by staff and trustees.
In a Thursday morning call with school board directors and chairs, Dunlop - who last week ordered audits of all boards' discretionary spending at the urging of Premier Doug Ford following public outrage over pricey trips to Italy and Hawaii - said "it's important that we address some pressing issues, and without delay.
"Parents expect school boards to be responsible stewards of public education dollars to prioritize student achievement. Every dollar spent must go toward improving and supporting the student experience, primarily funding classroom resources, teacher support or programs that enhance academic outcomes," she told the education leaders.
"I want to be clear - there is no room for unnecessary or extravagant expenses that do not contribute to these core objectives."
Controversies over spending "have consequences, not only for the financial health of our education system, but also for the trust that the public places in us," Dunlop added.
Kathleen Woodcock, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said "many people on the call felt that the minister's message was direct but fair."
School boards, she added, "share Minister Dunlop's priority - ensuring that funding reaches the classroom, where it supports teachers and staff and strengthens programs with the biggest impact on student success. We know Ontarians expect transparency and accountability from all of us, and school board trustees take that responsibility seriously."
Last month, details of a $145,000 summer trip to Italy taken by four Brantford-area Catholic school trustees came to light - a price tag that included airfare, hotels, meals with alcohol and almost $100,000 spent on religious statues.
Since then, reports of other trips have surfaced, including at the Lambton-Kent public board, where $32,000 was spent to attend an Indigenous conference in Hawaii that officials described as a "once-in-a-career opportunity" for three staff members - an expenditure Dunlop called "appalling."
The Star has also learned that the Dufferin-Peel Catholic board used more than $58,500 from a board-wide Indigenous Education Grant to help pay for a March break trip to New Zealand for 13 Indigenous students, four staff and five Indigenous community members involved with the board. An additional $28,700 was donated by an Indigenous artist toward the trip - and of that, $1,550 came from schools' purchasing artwork from their operating budgets.
A spokesperson for the board said Education Ministry staff had indicated the grant could be spent on travel.
Other reports include a $16,000 staff retreat to St. Catharines attended by more than two dozen employees of the London Catholic board, and $38,000 spent on 18 Thames Valley public board staffers for three days in Toronto at the former SkyDome hotel on days the Toronto Blue Jays were playing.
On Wednesday, the CBC detailed four trips taken by a senior staffer at the Halton Catholic board to Brazil, Italy and Germany on the public dime, with another trip to the United Arab Emirates planned for next year - as part of international student recruitment efforts - at a total cost of $41,000.
Dunlop said spending on questionable international travel "raises legitimate and serious concerns, and demonstrates a serious lack of judgment," and told directors that as "the sector is facing increasing financial pressures, it's important that you are mindful of every dollar."
"It is my expectation that you lead by example, exercise sound judgment and champion fiscal responsibility," she said in the 45-minute call via Teams.
"This means that if there are surplus funds available, it should be directed to programs and initiatives that directly benefit students - not in non-essential spending."
She also asked the directors to "please be mindful of the fact that the entire province - parents, taxpayers, the media - is watching closely."
Michael Bellmore, president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association, said "as a provincial partner, we share the same goals regarding transparency, accountability and a commitment to responsible stewardship in education."
Woodcock said public boards "appreciate the minister recognizing that boards are facing increasing financial pressures," and that "by providing oversight and ensuring transparency at the community level, trustees help keep our schools aligned with the needs and expectations of the families they serve. This local accountability is one of the best ways to uphold confidence in our public schools."
A statement released late Thursday by the Halton Catholic board said details of the overseas trips will be released publicly at a board meeting next week.
The "revenue generated through the International Student Program (through tuition) exceeds the costs associated with staffing, travel, conferences, and other related expenses," the board also said.
"This financial support benefits all of our students, allowing us to continue delivering high-quality Catholic education in a way that is both sustainable and beneficial for our community."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 189 of 500

Doug Ford's instincts are right when it comes to working with Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-instincts-are-right-when-comes-working/docview/3128530688/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+instincts+are+right+when+it+comes+to+working+with+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So it turns out Doug Ford is basically right. A lot of people jumped on the premier this week for pointing out that Mexico is the weak link among the Three Amigos in the North American trading partnership and suggesting Canada should work out its own two-way deal with the United States.
Ford, they said, is straying out of his lane. He's freelancing instead of acting as a loyal member of "Team Canada" in pushing back against Donald Trump's America First-ism.
But Ford was just saying out loud what others are thinking. Trump blames Mexico for allowing itself to be used as a backdoor for cheap Chinese imports into the U.S., but all he's doing is shouting what the Biden administration says in more polite tones.
If Ford was way off base, you'd expect the Trudeau government to point that out quite firmly. But it didn't. On the contrary, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland conceded the premier's main point. She said she's heard those worries about Mexico not being "aligned" against China and added: "I think those are legitimate concerns for our American partners and neighbours to have. Those are concerns that I share."
Freeland, no surprise, wouldn't be drawn into speculating about a separate Canada-U.S. deal somewhere down the road. But it might eventually come to that if Mexico doesn't clean up its act. And in the meantime it's useful for Ford to send a strong signal to Trump that he shouldn't lump Canada (Ontario, really) in with Mexico as he goes about putting up a high tariff wall against China.
In fact, Mexico was always an awkward fit with Canada and the U.S. in a continental trade deal. Part of the idea was that investment would flow into Mexico, speed development and raise wages there. But three decades after NAFTA went into effect the wage gap between Mexico and the U.S./Canada is as wide as ever.
Canada and the United States are natural partners, with similar standards of living and complementary economies. That's the message our governments have always pressed home with Washington and will continue to push with Trump. Adding Mexico to the mix muddies the waters, and it's quite possible the three-way deal won't survive a second Trump presidency.
We're learning some other things very quickly about the impact of Hurricane Donald. We knew Trump and the people around him don't particularly care for Canada and its government: too lefty, sanctimonious, "woke," etc. But it still comes as a shock to hear the depth of contempt they have for this country and for Justin Trudeau in particular.
Most of the big names Trump nominated this week for top positions have had things to say about Canada, and it's not good. Mike Waltz, his pick for national security adviser, tweeted that Pierre Poilievre will "send Trudeau packing in 2025 (finally) and start digging Canada out of the progressive mess it's in." And Tulsi Gabbard, his nominee as director of national intelligence, has called Trudeau "the autocratic leader in Canada who has resorted to genuinely authoritarian and tyrannical means" during the pandemic.
There's more: Trump's new "border czar," Tom Homan, calls the U.S. northern border a "huge national security issue." His pick as secretary of state, Marco Rubio, and his choice for UN ambassador, Elise Stefanik, have both warned about terrorists entering the U.S. through a leaky Canadian border.
All of which adds up to a generalized mistrust bordering on contempt for Canada and its leaders coming from the team Trump is forming around him. If Poilievre wins the next election, the likes of Waltz and Gabbard won't have Trudeau to kick around anymore. But there really does seem to be a deeper disdain for this country that goes way beyond disagreements over policy.
No doubt that's partly just the mean-spirited attitude Trump and his loyalists feel toward anyone or any country that doesn't fall into line behind them. There'll be plenty of contempt to go around and there's probably not much any Canadian government can do about that.
The experts will point out that the economic fundamentals mean Canada and the U.S. will keep doing business, regardless. But the early signs for the relationship are, if anything, worse than expected. No wonder Ford is scrambling to get on the good side of Team Trump.
CAPTION: When it comes to trade talks with Donald Trump, right, Premier Doug Ford is right to distinguish Canada from Mexico.

Subject: National security; Decades

Location: China; Mexico; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Full text: Cost of removing bike lanes also adds ER visits
Removing bike lanes will
cost taxpayers $48M, city estimates, Nov.14
If the lanes are removed, cyclists will understandably use the sidewalks for their own safety - and cause danger to pedestrians. There's no way to know how many near misses there are, but perhaps someone is counting bike-pedestrian collisions. It must cost the province a lot to patch people up in emergency wards after accidents.
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
Ford should
not interfere in Toronto's affairs
I do not understand Premier Doug Ford's focus on eliminating certain bike lanes in Toronto. Surely that is a municipal matter. Ford has enough issues to deal with at Queen's Park without interfering in Toronto's business. There is no evidence to support his claim that removal of bike lanes will ease traffic congestion, and it would be an expensive undertaking. Ford is a supposed conservative, but he does not show conservatism in his spending priorities. In any case he should stay in his own lane and leave Toronto to its elected officials.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
Synchronize traffic lights to road conditions
Remove the bike lanes and this is what occurs: When drivers have two lanes, there are those who zip by at great speed along the curb lane until a parked car blocks their lane or maybe a bike rider is in the way. Then they push their way into the line of patient, civilized drivers in the centre lane. This happened the other day as I drove easterly from west of the Humber River towards the Bloor and Jane intersection where a long line of cars waited starting at the bridge. The traffic lights seem to change their rhythm to shorter intervals and all the traffic changed to moving along smoothly. It's not the bike lanes that slow traffic - hey keep the bike riders safe and car drivers in line. A much simpler and likely less expensive solution would be to synchronize traffic lights to traffic conditions.
Helga Weick, Toronto
Bike travel on secondary streets is a logical option
The Star's reporting on bike lanes has been accompanied by several different pictures. What they have in common is the almost complete lack of bicycles and an endless line of cars. Rush-hour traffic with literally two or three bicycles commandeering an entire extra lane. Designing East/West and North/South routes for bike travel on secondary streets seems more reasonable than dedicated lanes on major arteries. Common sense says it's better to add travel time to a few cyclists than it is to inconvenience thousands of daily commuters.
Don Mustill,
Niagara-on-the-Lake, Ont.
Eglinton subway must come first,
not bike network
Is it too much to hope that Premier Doug Ford would, for a change, follow scientific data instead of his gut instinct on transit plans? Where is the respect for Toronto's developed network of bicycle lanes? Ensuring that the Eglinton subway line is completed should be more important than eliminating bike lanes or building tunnels. Focusing on highways that will only encourage more automobile congestion is yesterday's playbook.
Jonas Brand, Oakville

High time
we implement price controls
Ahead of Eras Tour, city moves homeless people near Rogers Centre 'to help ensure their safety,' Nov. 13
Displacing people already displaced with nowhere to call home is another illustration of how little leaders care about homelessness. For a concert? Leave them alone. They are suffering enough already. Why, when business build their products in countries with low wages, are they permitted to charge exorbitant prices far above their actual cost? Wage controls have been implemented regularly. Why not price controls? This is the reason for the ever-widening gap between prices and wages. Those we elect are supposed to prevent this type exploitation. Instead, they encourage it. It is time those responsible be held accountable for the damage they have caused to our economy and for all the human beings they continue to hurt.
Rick Lockman, Orillia, Ont.
Crossing guards are more than safety beacons
City councillors stopping to look both ways in bid to rein in crossing guard costs, Nov. 12
I sometimes drive my daughter to an 8.30 a.m. class at Centennial College and want to give a shout-out to some civil servants who are very visible at that time of day. Crossing guards are at every street corner near our schools, and as well as being safety beacons for our youngsters, they seem to interact with them nicely and give a positive start to the day. Public money well spent I'd say.
Beverly Jones, Scarborough
Psychedelic medicines are highly effective
These patients are considering MAID. They say Canada has made it harder to access magic mushrooms - a treatment giving them reason to live, Nov. 12
I write in response to Omar Mosleh's informed and insightful article comparing the ease of getting approval for MAID in Canada with the difficulty of getting approval for psilocybin therapy for end-of-life distress. Psilocybin, the active ingredient in magic mushrooms, should not be treated as if it is less safe than death. Hundreds of scientific studies have shown psilocybin therapy is both safe and highly effective under the supervised care of trained health-care professionals. The Psychedelic Association of Canada (PAC) concurs and supports Mr. Mosleh's analysis.
There are many challenges, delays and uncertainties in applying for exemptions to legally receive psilocybin therapy. This results in many Canadians travelling to jurisdictions where psilocybin can be legally accessed or seeking treatments in grey markets. Those are expensive and sometimes uncertain options. We know the government does not intend that patients should have to choose between their health and adhering to the law. We encourage more informative stories like this and active discussions with government to increase access to these medicines.
Jay Katz, Psychedelic
Association of Canada, Toronto

Province-led survey suggests higher cycling rates than Ford government numbers: city staff report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-led-survey-suggests-higher-cycling-rates/docview/3128839994/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province-led+survey+suggests+higher+cycling+rates+than+Ford+government+numbers%3A+city+staff+report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A major survey led by the province and not yet made public suggests cycling rates in Toronto are higher than Premier Doug Ford's government has asserted, according to a confidential city staff report seen by the Star.
The question of how many people use bicycles to get around the city has become a topic of heated debate as the Ontario PCs move to dismantle bike lanes on some of Toronto's major streets.
Ford's government has cited a figure that just 1.2 per cent of Toronto residents commute by bike as justification for the plan, which would remove lanes on sections of Bloor Street, Yonge Street, and University Avenue.
The 1.2 per cent figure was drawn from the 2011 census and applies to the Toronto Census Metropolitan Area, which encompasses municipalities outside the city proper, including small towns and semirural locations such as Uxbridge and King, as well as car-centric suburbs like Brampton.
According to city staff, results of the Transportation Tomorrow Survey tell a different story.
The survey is conducted every five years in partnership with the province, TTC, Metrolinx, and municipalities in the Greater Golden Horseshoe, and is used by the Ontario Ministry of Transportation to "support planning for transportation infrastructure," according to the project website.
The ministry has yet to publicly release results from the most recent edition, which was supposed to be published this fall.
However, as a participant in the study, the city has access to its data, and staff compiled a report based on the results. It was included as a confidential attachment to a public report that went to council Thursday.
According to the attachment, which has been reviewed by the Star, the survey found that in 2022, 5.8 per cent of commuters in the city took bikes or other forms of "micromobility" to work.
Micromobility can refer to electric bikes, mopeds, e-scooters and other small devices. About 45 per cent of commuters drove or were passengers in cars, while 37 per cent took transit.
Looking not just at commuting journeys, cycling and micromobility accounted for 4.4 per cent of all trips in the city, according to the survey, while drivers and passengers accounted for about 57 per cent.
Cycling and micromobility rates were significantly higher for trips ending downtown, where many of Toronto's bike lanes are concentrated. Just more than nine per cent of all trips that started in the city and ended downtown were taken by bike or micromobility device, compared to about 40 per cent for transit and 25 per cent for car drivers and passengers.
The survey also found the number of cycling trips taken in the city every day has more than tripled in the past two decades, from about 55,000 in 2001 to more than 172,000 in 2022.
The province didn't immediately respond to a request for comment about the survey figures.
With files from Kate Allen
CAPTION: The Ontario PCs have said they will dismantle bike lanes on some of Toronto's major streets, including the one on University Avenue.

Subject: Commuting; Bicycling

Ford's education minister directly warns school boards against 'unnecessary or extravagant expenses'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-education-minister-directly-warns-school/docview/3128531111/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+education+minister+directly+warns+school+boards+against+%27unnecessary+or+extravagant+expenses%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has warned school boards there is "no room for unnecessary or extravagant expenses" amid new reports of questionable international trips taken by staff and trustees.
In a Thursday morning call with school board directors and chairs, Dunlop - who last week ordered audits of all boards' discretionary spending at the urging of Premier Doug Ford following public outrage over pricey trips to Italy and Hawaii - said "it's important that we address some pressing issues, and without delay.
"Parents expect school boards to be responsible stewards of public education dollars to prioritize student achievement. Every dollar spent must go toward improving and supporting the student experience, primarily funding classroom resources, teacher support or programs that enhance academic outcomes," she told the education leaders.
"I want to be clear - there is no room for unnecessary or extravagant expenses that do not contribute to these core objectives."
Controversies over spending "have consequences, not only for the financial health of our education system, but also for the trust that the public places in us," Dunlop added.
Kathleen Woodcock, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said "many people on the call felt that the minister's message was direct but fair."
School boards, she added, "share Minister Dunlop's priority - ensuring that funding reaches the classroom, where it supports teachers and staff and strengthens programs with the biggest impact on student success. We know Ontarians expect transparency and accountability from all of us, and school board trustees take that responsibility seriously."
Last month, details of a $145,000 summer trip to Italy taken by four Brantford-area Catholic school trustees came to light - a price tag that included airfare, hotels, meals with alcohol and almost $100,000 spent on religious statues.
Since then, reports of other trips have surfaced, including at the Lambton-Kent public board, where $32,000 was spent to attend an Indigenous conference in Hawaii that officials described as a "once-in-a-career opportunity" for three staff members - an expenditure Dunlop called "appalling."
The Star has also learned that the Dufferin-Peel Catholic board used more than $58,500 from a board-wide Indigenous Education Grant to help pay for a March break trip to New Zealand for 13 Indigenous students, four staff and five Indigenous community members involved with the board. An additional $28,700 was fundraised for the trip, using donated Indigenous artwork - of that, $1,550 came from schools' purchasing of artwork using operating funds.
A spokesperson for the board said Education Ministry staff had indicated the grant could be spent on travel.
Other reports include a $16,000 staff retreat to St. Catharines attended by more than two dozen employees of the London Catholic board, and $38,000 spent on 18 Thames Valley public board staffers for three days in Toronto at the former SkyDome hotel on days the Toronto Blue Jays were playing.
On Wednesday, the CBC detailed four trips taken by a senior staffer at the Halton Catholic board to Brazil, Italy and Germany on the public dime, with another trip to the United Arab Emirates planned for next year - as part of international student recruitment efforts - at a total cost of $41,000.
Dunlop said spending on questionable international travel "raises legitimate and serious concerns, and demonstrates a serious lack of judgment," and told directors that as "the sector is facing increasing financial pressures, it's important that you are mindful of every dollar."
"It is my expectation that you lead by example, exercise sound judgment and champion fiscal responsibility," she said in the 45-minute call via Teams.
"This means that if there are surplus funds available, it should be directed to programs and initiatives that directly benefit students - not in non-essential spending."
She also asked the directors to "please be mindful of the fact that the entire province - parents, taxpayers, the media - is watching closely."
Michael Bellmore, president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association, said "as a provincial partner, we share the same goals regarding transparency, accountability and a commitment to responsible stewardship in education."
Woodcock said public boards "appreciate the minister recognizing that boards are facing increasing financial pressures," and that "by providing oversight and ensuring transparency at the community level, trustees help keep our schools aligned with the needs and expectations of the families they serve. This local accountability is one of the best ways to uphold confidence in our public schools."
A statement released late Thursday by the Halton Catholic board said details of the overseas trips will be released publicly at a board meeting next week.
The "revenue generated through the International Student Program (through tuition) exceeds the costs associated with staffing, travel, conferences, and other related expenses," the board also said. "This financial support benefits all of our students, allowing us to continue delivering high-quality Catholic education in a way that is both sustainable and beneficial for our community."
CAPTION: Education Minister Jill Dunlop attends question period in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Document 193 of 500

Who gets the Taylor Swift tickets after divorce? Lawyer shares couple's dilemma as Eras Tour arrives in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-gets-taylor-swift-tickets-after-divorce/docview/3128300153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+gets+the+Taylor+Swift+tickets+after+divorce%3F+Lawyer+shares+couple%27s+dilemma+as+Eras+Tour+arrives+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When a couple separates, who gets custody of their Eras Tour tickets?
A Toronto lawyer took to LinkedIn this week to share an awkward situation one couple found themselves in, after purchasing tickets to see Taylor Swift during their marriage, but separating before the concert.
"The issue was what to do with the tickets from a family law perspective," Anna-Marie Musson, managing lawyer of M. & Co Law Firm, wrote on social media. "Do they sell them and equalize the proceeds? Or who uses the tickets?"
It's a prickly issue, given that a single ticket to one of her Toronto shows can cost thousands of dollars on resale sites. Here's what happened to the couple.
Taylor Swift at the centre of a couple's separation troubles
According to Musson, one big issue was the time sensitivity - the tickets had value before the Eras Tour's opener in Toronto on Thursday, but were essentially worthless afterwards.
"It raised legal issues on ownership and asset valuation you don't traditionally see with concert tickets," she wrote in the post.
In the end, her team was able to negotiate a deal, of which she wasn't able to share the details. But Musson reported both parties emerged happy with the outcome.
"This is a win in our books and maybe one or both of them will even be at the concert."
Musson and M. & Co Law Firm did not immediately respond to the Star's inquiries.
The disagreement highlighted a real challenge in family law when it came to non-traditional assets, given the emotional attachments that can form, she added in a previous LinkedIn post.
"From sports memorabilia to concert tickets, it's essential to balance the practical and sentimental elements when clients are parting ways," she wrote. "Because whether it's real estate or front-row seats, everyone deserves to protect what's meaningful to them."
Why are Eras Tour tickets so expensive?
Tickets to see Swift are worth several times their weight in gold. On StubHub, a single seat for the Eras Tour in the upper bowl of Rogers Centre costs at least $4,000 - more than 20 times the presale price.
Experts previously told the Star the exorbitant prices in secondary markets all came down to supply and demand. Because Swift's Toronto and Vancouver dates were the last two stops on the Eras Tour, Canadians also had to compete with international Swifties hoping for a last chance to see the tour in person.
It's made it incredibly profitable for scalpers, who utilize armies of bots to scoop up tickets as soon as they become available.
Ontario's previous Liberal government tried to target scalpers with legislation that outlawed such bots and cap resale markups at 50 per cent above resale value. But the resale caps were scrapped when Doug Ford came into power after being deemed "unenforceable."
Eras Tour tickets attracting scams and thieves
Unscrupulous actors have taken advantage of Swifties' scramble for tickets as scams proliferated online, offering affordable tickets in a ploy to separate buyers from their money.
Peel police have warned of the posts, which often come from hacked social media accounts. "I promise you that no one is swooping in on a white horse trying to sell you cheap T-Swizzle tickets," a police spokesperson previously said.
Even if you got your hands on genuine tickets to see Swift, there's no guarantee your passes won't get stolen right out of your Ticketmaster account.
What to know about the Eras Tour
The first of six Toronto Eras Tour concerts will open at Rogers Centre on Thursday, with the rest scheduled for Nov. 15, 16, 21, 22 and 23.
The highest-grossing concert series of all time is expected to draw more than 500,000 visitors to the downtown core and more than $282 million in economic impact.
Have you secured the highly-coveted tickets to the event? Here's everything you need to know to navigate the Eras Tour chaos.

Reversing tax cuts for the wealthy is not raising taxes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reversing-tax-cuts-wealthy-is-not-raising-taxes/docview/3128300106/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reversing+tax+cuts+for+the+wealthy+is+not+raising+taxes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The billionaires I know feel trapped by their money. We should take it away, Nov 12
There is something seriously wrong when five of the richest multi-billionaires double their wealth in a short time. Charles Dickens was a critic of the uber-wealthy of his times and creator of the greedy grasping Scrooge character. In the 1830s Dickens wrote "the rich will take the widows mite and the orphan's crumb." Never in the history of man has so much wealth been accumulated by so few while at the same time there are record numbers of homeless and record numbers of the food insecure. Billionaires own trillion-dollar companies like Amazon, Apple, Nvidia and too many more to mention here. These billionaires and their corporations have done everything they can to avoid paying taxes which enabled by their political friends they have successfully done. Dickens would have been completely horrified by our times and soundly denounced our government for allowing this to happen. History shows time and again what happens when there is a massive concentration of wealth in the hands of a few while deprivation of the many occurs at the same time. It doesn't end well. Julie Mannell is right on the money. Reversing tax cuts for the wealthy is not raising taxes; it is restoring funding to rebuild our once civil society.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
We can't escape death or taxesCrombie's Liberals pledge income tax breaks that they say Doug Ford has failed to deliver, Nov. 12
The basic fact is that when politicians throw out promises about tax reductions, they totally fail to mention that as one tax goes down, another one goes up - without fail!
Hank Bangild, Port Colborne, ON
We got what we paid for
Over the years, as the baby boom generation matured and started to make decisions, we have known that there was going to be a big problem with the size of the generation in the future. Well, the future has arrived. We knew, for instance, that health would be a difficulty, but we did nothing about it. We elected short-sighted governments more concerned about money than the realities of our situation. We knew that was going to happen but did nothing to prepare. We now have a medical problem that could have been avoided but successive governments have reduced taxes that would have paid for preparation. The opposition continually blames the governing party as the cause of all our problems. Yet, when they are in power, they, too, like to reduce expenses instead of looking for the corrupters and dealing with them.We, the boomers and subsequent generations, are responsible for the current predicament in health, education, and housing. Nobody thinks beyond today.
Phil Minty, Greely
Let's focus on Canada's issuesAn emboldened Danielle Smith has voters wondering what she's really up to, Nov. 12
Perhaps we as Canadians should focus on what is happening in our own backyards. We can't do much about the one across the southern fence.
David Steele, Saskatoon, SK
Encampments cause irreparable harm to the environment If you don't want encampments in your park, you should speak up for shelters in your neighbourhood, Nov. 10
Encampments are eroding vulnerable nature areas in public parks. For example, there is an encampment in High Park on the Savannah that is in the middle of a conservation area for birds, animals and plants. By the time the residents leave, irreparable harm to the environment may be done. We know that housing is an issue, but what happens in the meantime to the natural environment is also an issue.
Cathy Brown, Toronto
Zelenskyy better watch out
When Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy called President-elect Donald Trump to congratulate him on his Nov. 5 victory, the latter passed the phone to Elon Musk, the richest man on the planet. By letting Musk and Zelenskyy talk together, Trump wanted to send a message to Russian President Vladimir Putin's sworn enemy: If you don't negotiate, you risk losing the Starlink network, owned by Musk. We know that Musk has sent thousands of terminals to Ukrainians since February 2022 so that they have access to the internet without depending on the terrestrial network. Musk even dared to turn off Starlink antennas to prevent a Ukrainian naval drone attack on Russian warships. Musk is no longer the supreme master, because he is serving Trump now, and Zelenskyy better watch out.
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal, PQ
Trump's win was not a landslide
Election conspiracies given new life, Nov. 12
We would like to remind readers that Donald Trump won the election by a mere three million votes. Harris had 72 million votes and Trump had 75 million votes. This is not a landslide.
Sandra and Jim Hill, Guelph
Real estate industry needs to set housing pricing
BOC keeping a wary eye on housing market, Nov. 7
There is a simple solution to helping ensure house pricing does not skyrocket. It is something the government should have done years ago and that contributed heavily to the ramp up of house pricing. Eliminate bidding. Demand that industry players set pricing properly. This is mandated for retailers and car sellers. You set a price and the first to offer the amount posted gets it. Why the government does not demand this makes no sense. They are not protecting Canadians with their largest single purchase, yet offer protection on retail shelf pricing and car advertisements?
George Holovaci, Toronto
Flawed Americans voted in a flawed president
Americans are usually good at separating the personal lives from the official responsibilities of their presidents. Before Trump, there was another infamous comeback kid, Bill Clinton. Both were husbands, lovers, liars, while being presidents. Living a contradiction is not confined only within the White House. Divided, angry Americans prioritize single issues they obsess about, be it nationalism, immigration, abortion, traditional masculinity, gun ownership, deregulation, and tax cuts, and suppress the side of them that would ordinarily disapprove of a politician's bad behaviours.
Trump presented as a slick enabler. His histrionics reach people at a visceral level, from all walks of life with different daily struggles. Sound bites like "Build the wall," "Stop the steal," "Drain the swamp," and "Make America great again," liberate inner beliefs that feel neglected by mainstream media and restricted by traditional decorum and politics. By comparison, Kamala Harris' "opportunity economy" sounded vague and elitist. Closet Trump supporters, including many Jan. 6 Capitol Hill rioters who had regular lives and day jobs, made pre-election polls showing a tight race the only fake news in this election. Not sure if the U.S. as a nation will benefit from Trump and his protectionist policies. But emotions trump reasons. Game recognizes game. Flawed Americans voted in a flawed president.
Simon Wong, Mississauga

DON'T MISSDoug Ford's plan to remove bike lanes has a hefty price tag
The premier's plan to tear up Toronto's bike lanes could make commuting in the city more dangerous and achieve only "minor" improvements in traffic long term. That's according to a new report from the city, which found removing bike lanes from Yonge, Bloor and University Avenue could cost taxpayers $48 million - more than the $27 million it cost to install the lanes. Take a look at his plans.This migrant worker rebuffed a 'wage take-back' scheme - then he was fired

Document 196 of 500

What you need to know about people sleeping in tents in parks - they have nowhere else to go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-you-need-know-about-people-sleeping-tents/docview/3128140775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+you+need+to+know+about+people+sleeping+in+tents+in+parks+-+they+have+nowhere+else+to+go&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We know that homelessness in Ontario is a crisis. Encampments are in every community. But a small group of Ontario mayors have chosen a dark path to address the problem - one that should never even be taken, let alone entertained.Two weeks ago, 13 mayors - from Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Guelph, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury, and Windsor - wrote a letter to the Premier asking him to use the Charter of Rights and Freedom's notwithstanding clause to bypass the court system and allow them to evict encampments - even when there is nowhere else for people to go.For those of us on the front-lines, we truly have nowhere to refer people. We've given up on searching for shelter beds because they're full. Same with voluntary detox and crisis beds.And housing? Anyone we've put on the list for social housing languishes for years - sometimes for more than a decade. Sometimes they don't live to see their number come up.Workers like me know what people need: affordable housing, decent shelter in the interim and voluntary supports to stay healthy and well. These don't exist. And this is the case in Toronto, where I work, as it is for every municipality across the province.It's why the 444 members of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario are calling for solutions like investing in deeply affordable housing and supportive housing and expanding access to voluntary and evidence-based treatment.But instead of responding to these sensible requests, Premier Doug Ford is entertaining a handful of mayors - now expanded to 15 mayors, including Midland and St. Thomas - who would rather remove homeless people's Charter-protected rights.These mayors are referencing a 2023 ruling in Waterloo. In this decision, the court ruled that clearing the Waterloo encampment would be unconstitutional: the city couldn't remove the encampment without providing adequate shelter beds, and to do so would be a violation of homeless people's Charter-protected right to life, liberty, and security of the person.There is no human right more foundational than the right to life, and no ask more extreme than removing this right from an entire demographic group - homeless people.In fact, using the notwithstanding clause to override the right to life for homeless people would take away the responsibility of Ontario cities for homelessness altogether - for housing, for shelter, everything.Nothing about this would make the issue of encampments better, it would mean fewer resources available to a growing number of Ontarians - 234,000 and counting - who are currently or at risk of homelessness. The problem would become more prolific, more visible, and ultimately more criminalized in public spaces.The request is too ludicrous to be entertained. We elect our leaders to do difficult work, including solving the housing and homelessness crisis - not to take away the Charter rights of those most impacted by the crisis.These 13 individual mayors do not represent the majority of their mayoral colleagues, let alone their respective councils and constituents, none of whom were consulted on their letter. The Premier can't seriously consider their request - it's undemocratic and un-Canadian.Indeed, the issue is bigger than just homelessness: this is about protecting the integrity of the Charter and the foundations of Canadian society.If we fully remove the right to life for one group of people, who's next? What does the Charter mean if the elected leaders of the day can blithely abuse the notwithstanding clause - which was meant to be used in the rarest circumstances - to take away the very rights the Charter protects?These are big questions and they supersede political affiliation. Whether you support encampment residents, or you want them gone from your communities, or you care about the implications to your rights, everything about this request is reckless, abusive, and dangerous.To these 15 mayors and the Premier I say: solve the homelessness crisis. Protect the Charter. And do your damned jobs.
CAPTION: A tent encampment is seen in St. James Park in the King St. E. and Jarvis St. area on Tuesday.
CREDIT: Diana Chan McNally

Subject: Affordable housing; Homeless people

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Document 197 of 500

Removing bike lanes will cost taxpayers $48M, city estimates: Report finds move will lessen road safety

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/removing-bike-lanes-will-cost-taxpayers-48m-city/docview/3128140365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Removing+bike+lanes+will+cost+taxpayers+%2448M%2C+city+estimates%3A+Report+finds+move+will+lessen+road+safety&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's bid to tear up Toronto bike lanes could cost taxpayers tens of millions of dollars, make commuting more dangerous and achieve only "minor" long-term improvements to traffic speed, a new report from the city contends.
It estimates that ripping up bike lanes on Yonge Street, Bloor Street and University Avenue would cost Ontario taxpayers $48 million - on top of the $27 million the city spent installing the lanes.
The report, released Wednesday by the city manager's office and attached to a motion from the mayor, marks an escalation in the municipality's dispute with the province over the future of local cycling infrastructure. City council, which resumes Thursday, is set to debate the report.
Ford has vowed to dismantle bike lanes on sections of Bloor, Yonge and University, which his government has said contribute to Toronto's traffic woes. The province has said it will pay for the work. Ford is also moving to make Ontario municipalities ask for permission to construct new bikeways going forward.
Ford has blamed bike lanes for the "crowded streets that are just absolutely insanity right now on Bloor, on Yonge, on University."
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, who introduced the bike lane removal legislation last month, has argued it will stanch Toronto's annual loss of $11 billion due to congestion, according to regional board of trade data.
The bill is awaiting royal assent, the final step before becoming law.
After the city report was published Wednesday evening,Sarkaria's office reiterated to the Star the province's rationale for removing the lanes from major arterial roads.
"Bike lanes should only go where they make sense," said Dakota Brasier, Sarkaria's director of media relations. "It's clear that the city's approach isn't working and we encourage them to listen to the thousands of common-sense drivers to help clear our major roads and get people out of traffic."
In an email to the Star on Wednesday night, Mayor Olivia Chow said the money needed to remove the lanes could be better spent elsewhere.
"With family budgets squeezed across our city, the millions it's estimated to cost to remove these lanes (and make people less safe on our roads) could be better spent on feeding all kids in our city through our school food program or help to build more housing."
Speaking to reporters Wednesday morning ahead of the report's release, Chow called the province's plan "arbitrary" and said the city intended to put forward evidence of the impact of removing bike lanes.
"They need to look at our numbers," she said.
"Ripping up our roads will make our congestion worse, especially during the time that you're ripping it up. It's costly and it will make our roads less safe for cyclists."
She reiterated her argument that Ford's bike lane legislation is provincial overreach that violates the city's oversight of its roadways. The cycling network "has been planned for many years, and the City of Toronto and Torontonians need respect," Chow said.
Cost estimatesfrom the city donot include additionalfees it said could be incurred to identify, design and construct alternative cycling routes. There would also be other staff and infrastructure costs for redesigning and reconstructing these affected roads to add space for cars where the lanes used to be.
According to the report, the $48-million estimate for removing the bike lanes and replacing them with vehicle lanes includes the cost of road reconstruction for sections that "were recently reconstructed and would need to be rebuilt," as well as road resurfacing on other sections.
The report stressed that the figure was a "preliminary order of magnitude" estimate excluding staff time and was produced after the province posted details of its legislation late last month. It said more information would be required before the estimates could be "relied upon for other purposes."
"The removal of existing bike lanes will be a complex project that will be challenging to implement within a short time period, due to planning, design and construction requirements," the report said.
Removing the lanes has the potential to decrease safety for pedestrians, cyclists and drivers, according to the report. Drivers will be at greater risk of crashing and cyclists are injured far more frequently on roads without bike lanes.
In the past 10 years, 28 people have been killed and 380 people have been "seriously injured" while cycling in Toronto, the report said.
It added that 68 per cent of cyclist collisions happened on streets without "safe cycling accommodations."
Precisely how many Torontonians bicycle to work is a matter up for debate. Based on recent, albeit imprecise, surveys, it appears some amount fewer than four per cent of the city commutes on two wheels.By contrast, 61 per cent of Torontonians drive to work. There would be significant benefits for traffic and the climate, the city report said, if some of these folks instead picked up a bike.
It is "well documented" that even small reductions in the number of vehicles on a road can produce "large improvements" in traffic flow, according to the report.
With files from Ben Spurr
and Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star With files from Ben Spurr and Robert Benzie

Subject: Commuting; Legislation; Estimates; Roads & highways; Bicycling; Traffic

People: Ford, Doug

This Donald Trump ally thinks Canada's relationship with the U.S. will continue to be strong. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-donald-trump-ally-thinks-canadas/docview/3128140294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+Donald+Trump+ally+thinks+Canada%27s+relationship+with+the+U.S.+will+continue+to+be+strong.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Chrystia Freeland has called him a "proof point" of the enduring Canada-U.S. relationship. Fortune Magazine has asked if he could be the Elon Musk of steel.
But Lourenco Goncalves says Canada's trade partnership with the U.S. can thrive during Donald Trump's second administration because of the close ties between the two nations.
"I really have conviction that the economic, political and social relationship between our two countries is the real deal," he said in an interview with the Star.
Goncalves is the chairman, CEO and president of Cleveland-Cliffs, a major steel producer in the U.S. that just this month completed a purchase of Canadian steel company Stelco. He said he's committed to keeping Stelco's name and the company's headquarters in Hamilton.
Born in Brazil, Goncalves rose through the metals industry there before moving to several U.S. firms.
When he took over Cleveland-Cliffs in 2014, the company was a small player, but is now the largest flat rolled steel company in North America.
During the first Trump administration, Canada faced tariffs on steel and aluminum that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau called insulting. On the campaign trail this time, Trump pledged ten per cent tariffs on all goods entering the U.S.
The day after Trump's re-election, Freeland, Canada's finance minister, said Goncalves' acquisition of Stelco is proof that the trade relationship will be fine.
"He has been a long-standing supporter of President Trump. He chose to acquire a major Canadian steelmaker. Now, would he do that if he did not believe in the fundamentals of the economic relationship between our two countries? I don't think so."
Cleveland-Cliffs' political action committee has supported both Democratic and Republican candidates. In an interview on CNBC just days before the 2024 election, he said both Trump and Vice-President Kamala Harris understand the value of his industry.
"As far as steel goes, we are going to be OK no matter who is the next president of the United States."
Goncalves told the Star Freeland is representing him appropriately, and that Trump's tough talk on trade shouldn't apply to Canada.
"Canada and the U.S. should not even be in the conversation when we talk about other countries. We see the two countries really as the only close ones that you could claim in this complicated world," he said.
He said Canada's economy is closely connected to the U.S. and also has critical minerals America needs, which it is currently buying from unfriendly countries like Russia and China.
"I feel like it's good practice to carve out Canada, but it's not a favour, it's a necessity."
Despite that close relationship, many of Trump's early picks for his new administration have been highly critical of Canada.
Congresswoman Elise Stefanik, who has been picked as UN ambassador, said Canada wasn't living up to its trade obligations. Mike Waltz, Trump's choice for national security adviser, cheered a clip of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre attacking Trudeau in question period, writing, "This guy is going to send Trudeau packing in 2025."
Trump's likely pick for Secretary of State, Marco Rubio, was highly critical of Canada's program to allow Gazans to come to Canada on temporary visas.
Goncalves said he feels the people around Trump understand that on trade, Canada is different, and highlighted Ottawa's decision to match U.S. tariffs on Chinese steel, aluminum and electric vehicles.
"That's the type of thing that I believe the future Trump administration will appreciate," he said.
Goncalves said too many U.S. allies say the right things, but their actions try to undercut the U.S. economy.
"We don't like countries that talk out of both sides of their mouths," he said, singling out Mexico and Japan as two U.S. allies that speak positively about the U.S., but make economic moves that hurt America's economy.
"These fake friends or friends of convenience will not have a good time when President Trump goes back to the White House."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that Canada should consider a new trade agreement with the U.S. alone, leaving Mexico out of the trade deal. Mexico has not matched Canada and U.S. tariffs on steel, aluminum and EVs and Ford argued they were being used to skirt tariffs.
"If Mexico won't fight transshipment by, at the very least, matching Canadian and American tariffs on Chinese imports, they shouldn't have a seat at the table or enjoy access to the largest economy in the world," he said on social media.
Goncalves said Ford's suggestion is in line with what the new Trump administration will want.
"That's actually extremely in sync with Donald Trump's playbook. He doesn't like multilateral trade agreements. He likes bilateral trade agreements."
Freeland was more circumspect, but in a news conference Wednesday said she has heard similar concerns about Mexico from people in the Trump administration and she shares them.
"I have heard from them, actually. I've heard also from the Biden administration, and I've heard also from U.S. business leaders some real concerns about whether Mexico is fully aligned when it comes to its policies vis-à-vis China."
CAPTION: U.S. President-elect Donald Trump speaks at a House Republicans Conference meeting at the Hyatt Regency on Capitol Hill on Nov. 13, 2024 in Washington, DC.

Taylor Swift fans will spend hundreds of millions in Toronto. Why does the city get so little of it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/taylor-swift-fans-will-spend-hundreds-millions/docview/3128139493/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Taylor+Swift+fans+will+spend+hundreds+of+millions+in+Toronto.+Why+does+the+city+get+so+little+of+it%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hey Swifties ... and those coming to Toronto for FIFA 2026! Welcome! We always do our best for to make sure people visiting Toronto have lots to see and do.
We hope you enjoy your stay. When you check out, you will see on your bill a charge for a "Municipal Accommodation Tax." For anyone not travelling a lot in North America, I want to tell you quite simply exactly what this is: It's not a tax grab, we aren't trying to raid your wallet.
It is in fact one of the few ways the City of Toronto will actually make any money from big events like these. A lot of the tourism tax revenue goes to other levels of government, it doesn't stay in our city.We look for other revenue sources try to keep it this city full of fun things to do and see so we can get you to come back.
Not only in Ontario ... but across much of North America, this tax is becoming increasingly popular amongst all municipalities. It's levied at check-out and collected by the accommodation provider: hotels, motels, bed and breakfasts and AirBnBs.
Ontario's Municipal Act, 2001 mandates that we have to revenue share the funds generated by the tax (which is about $98 million annually) with an "eligible tourism entity" - here it goes to Destination Toronto, which promotes our city to tourists, companies and associations hosting conferences here. The rest is turned over to the City of Toronto's municipal government to assist in balancing the budget and providing necessary services to all our residents.
While the tax rate and implementation vary from one municipality to another, in Toronto the levy is 6 per cent of the total accommodation fee. While much has been said about how much revenue federal and provincial tax levies - the HST - will generate, here's how that money breaks down: the total of taxes and fees raised by all three levels of government annually in Toronto sees 47 per cent going to the Federal government, 44 per cent to the Province, and just 9 per cent to the City of Toronto.
Taylor Swift concerts in November will help generate increased demand for hotel rooms when bookings are typically lower than peak season (November is a 'slower' month), although the additional tax revenue the City will receive from the 6 days of concerts will not be too significant. The City of Toronto estimates all levels governments are expected to take in around $40 million in tax revenue from the events, but the City will only receive about $250,000 in total from this added municipal tax.
Toronto, like all Ontario municipalities, has been facing challenges with its long-term financial sustainability for years. Our municipal government has seen a significant transfer of responsibilities and services over time, and the expectations and complexity of our residents has also increased, from challenges with infrastructure to homelessness. These challenges are exacerbated by unforeseen events such as COVID and the effects of climate change which have put pressure on our municipal budget.
Toronto is limited in the ways we collect revenue, and as municipal costs have increased, revenue tools have not kept up. Traditional revenue streams include taxation, including property taxes, special area rates, payments in lieu of taxation, user fees, fees for licenses, permits and rents, fines and penalties, investment income and development charges.
Another key source of revenue are conditional and unconditional transfers from other levels of government. These transfers are currently a mixed bag of project-based and formula-based funding. This type of funding does not allow for long-term financial planning.
Premier Doug Ford stated, "Toronto is critical to Ontario and Canada's economic success, and we need all levels of government working together to deliver solutions that protect services and put the City on a path towards long-term financial sustainability. When Toronto succeeds, Ontario and Canada succeed."
How do we do this when we only receive 9 per cent of all levied taxes and fees? As a municipal government we look at every source of revenue while we work with organizations like the Federation of Canadian Municipalities and Association of Ontario Municipalities to encourage have our Federal and Provincial governments step up to the financial plate and support our cities both large and small.
So thanks, Swifties ... hope you come back again, or for the FIFA World Cup events in 2026. We're happy to have you.
CAPTION: With Taylor Swift now in Toronto and her Eras Tour kicking off on Thursday, an inflatable bracelet adorns the south side of the Rogers Centre.
CREDIT: Paul Ainslie, Contributor

Subject: Fines & penalties; Taxation; Taxes; Tax rates; Tax revenues; Tourism; Soccer; Municipalities
