Live updates: Taylor Swift tickets to Eras Tour in Toronto still selling for thousands; doors officially open at Roger Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/live-updates-taylor-swift-tickets-eras-tour/docview/3128139185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Live+updates%3A+Taylor+Swift+tickets+to+Eras+Tour+in+Toronto+still+selling+for+thousands%3B+doors+officially+open+at+Roger+Centre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Taylor Swift is in Toronto for six shows starting Thursday. The 34-year-old singer is playing at the Rogers Centre on Nov. 14, 15, 16 and then again on Nov. 21, 22, and 23.Doors open at 4:30. Opener Gracie Abrams takes the stage 6:45, followed by Taylor Swift at 8 p.m. The concert is expected to last over three hours. Follow the Star's live updates today.
5:45 p.m. At the Taylgate, fans once again assembled at the main stage at 4 p.m. for Taylor Swift trivia and a lyrics fill in the blanks trivia. Fans answered questions about Swift's family, career, friends and more.
In yet another side of the venue stood a small "train" onboard which there was a silent disco featuring songs from not only Swift but many major pop artists such as Charli XCX, One Direction, Ariana Grande and more.
4:55 p.m. At the Michael's craft store at John and Richmond Street, about a five minute walk from the Rogers Centre, store manager Kriti Madhukar says they've been sold out of some friendship bracelet material since last week.
People have even taken to sifting through returns before they can be put back on the shelf.
"We're so low on stock, I've suggested some of our Christmas (bracelets)," said Sarah Melkert, another manager.
4:37 p.m. As the clock hits 4:30 and the gates open, the crowd lets out a cheer - matched seconds later by the faint cheer around the corner, when another gate opens.
There's still more than two hours to go until Gracie Abrams opens the show at 6:45 p.m.
4:24 p.m. Swifties being shepherded to the downtown core could were both seen and felt as far north as Willowdale at 4 p.m. But it wasn't quite the typical rush hour because everyone was already packed further south.
Streets were mostly empty around Yonge and Sheppard. What would have been gridlocked intersections any other day of the year were set free. The closest Uber driver, typically one to two minutes away, was now 15 to 20.
A subway train descending from Line 1's terminal station at Finch, a typically packed experience this time of day, had plenty of seats. You could spot the difference between a North York-er just heading downtown or home, and the bedazzled Swifties on the subway. If the merch and friendship bracelets didn't give it away, the pink feathery jackets or black sequened jumpers did.
4 p.m. Jasmine Salve, from Germany, worked as security at Swift's concert in Munich.
She decided last week to fly to Toronto - without a ticket - to try to see it again. She's walking the streets right now, hopeful someone will notice her sign.
She said she'll be disappointed if she doesn't get a ticket, but is making the most of her trip by seeing the city and visiting Niagara Falls.
3:56 p.m. The line to get in to the Rogers Centre is long. For the east entrance, it wraps down Bremner Boulevard, up Lower Simcoe Street and is spilling on to Front Street.
It's so long that there's confusion about whether it's the line for Taylgate, at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre, or the concert itself.
Chloe St. Laurent, who drove with her two friends from Sherbrooke, Que. this morning, is expecting to wait an hour or longer.
3:53 p.m. On one side of the room at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre, fans lined up to get the number 13 tattooed on the back of their hand - a tradition at Swift's shows dating back to her earliest concerts. For her first two tours, Swift would write the number on her hand before each show. Thirteen is famously the singer's lucky number.
3:38 p.m. The Rogers Centre's security perimeter has just opened.
Fans are now, officially, lining up on stadium property. They'll all have to scan their tickets to enter the perimeter. Gates officially open at 4:30 p.m.
3:37 p.m. Outside the Fairmont Royal York, across the street from Union Station, hotel workers held a rally to bring attention to their difficulties in securing a new contract. They picked the first night of Swift's shows for this protest, as they hoped to gain some sympathy from fans who had to pay increased room rates during the concerts.
3:15 p.m. Taylor Swift fans are enchanted to meet her and ahead of the concert tonight the Star asked Swifties to share their favourite photos. Reader Marcello Cabezas share a photo and a tip for other fans. "There is an amazing SWIFTIE sign (not far) away from Rogers Centre - you can snap a selfie with the CN Tower in the background and also put a bracelet on the Friendship Wall. Located at 229 Richmond St. West at RendezViews.
Have a fan photo to share? Submit it here.
3:09 p.m. An unofficial line to enter Rogers Stadium has formed on Front Street, and now extends hundreds of metres west of the venue.
2:57 p.m. Tabitha and Summer, a mother-daughter duo, won the the Metro Convention Center costume contest with their "Lover"-inspired outfits. On stage, Tabitha surprised her daughter with the news that they were going to the show tonight. Summer says that she was left speechless. She says she is "super excited" and is excited to hear Swift sing "All Too Well (Ten Minute Version)" and "Vigilante Shit."
2:52 p.m. Union Station's concourse was steady but busy just before 3 p.m. Thursday as some commuters tried to escape the oncoming crush of fans.
"It looks a little like Christmas, with all the shiny dresses and beads," joked Marc Beaulieu, who was heading home from the office.
"We've been planning our outfits for a year, said Karlee Donovan, who was with daughter, Aria and her mother Corrina Merlin. "Its all about the glitter."
2:45 p.m. We're about an hour away from lineups opening at the Rogers Centre, two hours from gates opening and four hours until opening act Gracie Abrams goes on.
Crowds are continuing to build and road closures are in effect. Just in the last half hour, the unofficial lineup on the Rogers Centre's East side has extended to Ripley's Aquarium.
A few hot dog carts are set up outside the Rogers Centre. Sales are down right now, one operator said, but they're holding out for the crowds to pick up.
2:44 p.m. Fans, with and without tickets to the concert, assembled at the Metro Convention Center all dressed up in their Swift-inspired outfits. From friendship bracelet-making stations to photo booths, a beauty bar and more - the event had something for every Swiftie.
At 2:30, all eyes were on the main stage for the costume contest. People came dressed up in costumes inspired by different Swift Eras. Many fans had self-made costumes which included outfits Swift has worn in her music videos, concerts, as well as outfits inspired by song titles and lyrics.
2:36 p.m. Outside Union Station, a Sephora pop-up a long line of people waited to get touched up and pick up free samples.
The shift from downtown Toronto commuters trying to get out and Eras fan arriving for their concert experience was noticeable.
"We're just about to check out the Taylgate at the Convention Centre," said Samantha Shields, 19, from Mississauga, who was with a group of friends. "But the we'll probably head to the show around 5 or 6 p.m."
2:30 p.m. Alana and Anthony, Swifties from Syracuse, New York, are killing time outside of Rogers Centre as they wait for the doors to open. This is the couple's first time in Toronto as adults.
"Taylor and I are around the same age, so I grew up listening to her," Alana explains. "That makes the Eras Tour super relatable to me."
As for Anthony, he "married into" becoming a Swift fan, but he became a convert after seeing her perform at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey during the "Reputation" tour. "I was blown away," he recalls. "Now I have a young daughter, and she's obsessed with her, too. That's what we're jamming to on the way to school."
So what made them take the trek to Canada?
"We're just drawn to the magnitude of this tour," Alana says. "There are so many generations coming together, and it's just a real community experience."
2:15 p.m. Your complete guide to navigating Taylor Swift Eras Tour chaos in Toronto, from TTC and GO train intel to when to arrive at the Rogers Centre
It's been a longtime coming. Taylor Swift's Eras Tour opens tonight at the Rogers Centre. It marks the first of six sold-out shows that have been more than a year in the making and which are expected to bring Toronto's downtown core to a standstill.
Toronto is the second-last stop on Swift's two-year, 149-show, multibillion-dollar tour that long ago became the highest-grossing concert series of all time. Swift, the superstar of nearly two decades and author of hits including "I Knew You Were Trouble," "Shake It Off" and "Anti-Hero," is expected to bring more than 500,000 visitors to the downtown core and more than $282 million in economic impact.
For those inside the Rogers Centre, it will be an experience that costs thousands of dollars. For those outside, it could mean a frustrating commute, swamped local bars and packed city sidewalks.
Read the essential guide to navigating the Taylor Swift chaos from the Star's Mark Colley.
2:13 p.m. While Swiftmania picks up outside the Rogers Centre, Pamela Cordeiro is at home in Etobicoke.
She and her Grade 4 daughter, Shay, couldn't get tickets to the concert, so she decided instead to make the trip on Sunday to Buffalo for the Bills-Chiefs game, which Swift may attend to see her boyfriend, Chiefs tight end Travis Kelce.
"I got tickets on both sides of the stadium," Cordeiro said, ensuring they'd see Swift regardless. "I'm actually a little wild."
It will be both Cordeiro and Shay's first NFL game.
2:07 p.m. Bojan, a Toronto resident who has lived on Front Street for five years now, says he's "never seen anything like this."
Walking his dog, Phoebe, outside the Rogers Centre on Wednesday afternoon, Bojan says he's decided to embrace the chaos, rather than avoid it. "Traffic is going to be terrible, so I'm not happy about that," he says. "But the crowd is harmless, and it's an overall positive atmosphere."
As for Phoebe, she likes strangers. "She's enjoying herself."
2 p.m. Starting now, the following roads will be restricted to local traffic (residents and businesses) only:
Bremner Boulevard between Spadina Avenue and Simcoe StreetNorthbound lanes on Rees Street will be closed. Southbound lanes will remain open for two-way traffic including an accessible entry point.Blue Jays Way between Spadina Avenue and south of Front StreetThe Marriott Hotel will be accessible from Front Street via Blue Jays Way.Navy Court WharfAccess to condos will be via a police checkpoint at the Spadina Avenue and Bremner Boulevard intersection.Lake Shore Boulevard will be reduced to two westbound lanes at the intersection with Rees Street for a drop-off/pick-up point.
1:54 p.m. While the lineup officially opens at 3:30 p.m., Andi McCord and her daughter Keriann, 11, are among the handful of people already queuing.
They don't want to miss out on merch, which they tried to get yesterday but had already sold out. Keriann also wanted to trade friendship bracelets with the other early bird fans.
McCord's daughter Lauren, nine, is also here - but isn't going to the concert. Lauren chose a trip to New York instead of the Eras Tour. Still, McCord said, she's "very upset."
1:42 p.m. Taylor Swift is almost here, and Toronto's restaurants, bars and hotels understand the assignment, and that's to get into the spirit and theme of everything Swiftian.
Speaking of spirits and themes, there are a number of bars offering Swift themed cocktails and spaces inspired by Eras. The 1 Hotel has transformed their lobby bar, Flora, into The Folklore Lounge. Taking inspiration from the album cover, it had a natural aesthetic, with a moss-covered piano and lyrics from the album on parchment-like scrolls.
"We've been waiting for this for a long time, and Flora has become Folklore Lounge, and we'll have acoustic performances from Toronto musicians from 3 to 6 every day with Taylor-inspired music for guests to enjoy," said Liza McWilliams, marketing director for the hotel. "And the cocktails are all inspired from some of her most popular songs. Very playful, colourful, there are mocktails as well."
They include The 1, which is reportedly inspired by Swift's favourite drink, the French Blonde, and has gin and elderflower syrup.
Kost, the rooftop bar at Bisha Hotel has the Lavender Haze and the Midnight Rum. Up by Yorkville, the Park Hyatt is also featuring two themed cocktails, the Karma and the Alchemist.
The message to all Swifties is clear, drink up all that Toronto has to offer.
1:33 p.m. Marsha Stagg, bundled in blankets and sitting on a folding beach chair, said she's been waiting outside the Rogers Centre since 5:30 a.m. So far, her sign - "We Need Tickets" - hasn't worked.
But she's not ready to give up. Originally from Stephenville, Nfld., she flew in from Halifax and met six of her childhood friends in Toronto for the concert.
One of them, Kristen, has stage four breast cancer. The concert is a chance to reconnect and celebrate her, Stagg said.
Stagg hopes the box office will sell off extra tickets. If that doesn't work, she said she's willing to pay $750 on the resale market.
And if the whole plan falls apart, the group still has tickets to Sunday's Bills-Chiefs game in Buffalo, where Swift's boyfriend Travis Kelce will play. So either way, Stagg said, they'll see Swift at some point.
1:28 p.m. The Pint Public House at 277 Front Street is located the epicentre of Swiftmania. But Cesar Mesen, who owns the popular sports pub, says he's ready for action.
"We're not nervous," he tells me. "There are 82 Blue Jays games a year here, plus tonnes of concerts. We understand the magnitude."
The Pint, he adds, has been preparing for this moment for six months now. "We've got DJs and live entertainment. We had a Taylor Swift trivia last night. People are looking for hospitality and a place where they feel welcome. If you can provide them with that, you are good."
"It's not like a Leafs or Jays game, where there's a good chance that the team will lose and people will come in sad or angry," he adds. "(Swift fans) are happy both before and after the show."
"I think it's fantastic to have so many international travellers here, and we're ready to put our best food forward and represent Toronto the best we can."
1:16 pm. Starting now, westbound lanes on Bremner Blvd. between Rees Street and Navy Wharf Court are closed, while eastbound lanes remain open for two-way traffic.
Starting at 2 p.m. the following roads will be restricted to local traffic (residents and businesses) only:
Bremner Boulevard between Spadina Avenue and Simcoe StreetNorthbound lanes on Rees Street will be closed. Southbound lanes will remain open for two-way traffic including an accessible entry point.Blue Jays Way between Spadina Avenue and south of Front StreetThe Marriott Hotel will be accessible from Front Street via Blue Jays Way.Navy Court WharfAccess to condos will be via a police checkpoint at the Spadina Avenue and Bremner Boulevard intersection.Lake Shore Boulevard will be reduced to two westbound lanes at the intersection with Rees Street for a drop-off/pick-up point.
1 p.m. Just hours out from the first of Taylor Swift's six shows in Toronto there are still some tickets for night one on the market, and many fans have been holding out in hopes that more will become available and prices will drop.
For those trying to snag tickets from Ticketmaster, a few were made available on the platform last night to people who had already registered as verified fans for the Toronto shows.
On StubHub, the cheapest ticket currently available is going for just over $1,900, and it's standing room only. The spot is positioned slightly behind the main stage in the TD Park Social section of the Rogers Centre. Floor seat tickets are selling for between $6,000 to upwards of $20,000.
12:30 p.m. Who gets the Taylor Swift tickets after divorce? Lawyer shares couple's dilemma as Eras Tour arrives in Toronto
When a couple separates, who gets custody of their Eras Tour tickets?
A Toronto lawyer took to LinkedIn this week to share an awkward situation one couple found themselves in, after purchasing tickets to see Taylor Swift during their marriage, but separating before the concert.
"The issue was what to do with the tickets from a family law perspective," Anna-Marie Musson, managing lawyer of M. & Co Law Firm, wrote on social media. "Do they sell them and equalize the proceeds? Or who uses the tickets?"
It's a prickly issue, given that a single ticket to one of her Toronto shows can cost thousands of dollars on resale sites. Here's what happened to the couple.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang.
11:22 a.m. The TTC was to make sure Swift fans are ready for it. In a post on X, the transit agency announced increased service on Line 1 and Line 2 as well as on the 504, 509, 511 streetcars and the 510D Spadina and 19 Bay buses.
11:02 a.m. Shauna Mason and her daughter, Adele, along with Alayne Burn and her daughter Aria, came from Calgary for the show. Decked out in Swift merch they got yesterday, their plan was to shop during the day, before getting their hair done and prepping for the show.
They were hitting up Queen Street West for all the stores' themed offerings.
"We're doing all the Swiftie stuff," said Shauna.
They had already hit Lululemon where she got a clear bag with a friendship bracelet, that she could take into the show.
Next was Brandy Melville, which is a favourite among Swift fans and had additional security.
Then there was Pink Palm HQ, which was giving out free hoodies.
11 a.m. Among the opening remarks at city council Thursday was a tribute from deputy mayor Ausma Malik to city staff who have spent months preparing for the Swift tsunami this week and next.
"I'm looking forward to us continuing to catch the excitement from the Eras Tour, to show the world what we have to offer here in Toronto," said the Spadina-Fort York councillor, adding that the city has learned lessons from this work applicable to hosting FIFA in 2026.
"Let's show everyone what we got and that we're ready for it."
The city is keeping costs for hosting Swift secret "for operational and security reasons" until her departure but is expected to have spent millions of dollars setting up for the shows - and keeping an eye out for potential threats to safety.
Malik solicited applause for "the biggest Swiftie on council," fellow deputy mayor Jennifer McKelvie, who was behind the christening of "Taylor Swift Way" downtown, a charity stunt to support the Daily Bread Food Bank that Malik said has already raised $50,000.
Even the mayor couldn't help getting swept up in Swiftmania. She said she recently went to a Toronto Public Library friendship bracelet making event where she crafted eight for herself and traded some with fans.
"It really celebrated the spirit of kindness and sharing," said Mayor Olivia Chow, her wrist covered in eight bracelets. She offered one to Speaker Frances Nunziata.
Chow said with a laugh that she has a bracelet that says 'Taylor bike lane,' a nod to her feud with Premier Doug Ford over bike lane placement in the city. - Ben Cohen and Mahdis Habibinia
10:55 a.m. In a statement on X, Ontario's minister of tourism, culture and gaming, Stan Cho welcomed Taylor Swift and Swifties to the province's capital today. Cho said the singer's six-concert stint in Toronto is expected to be a boon to the economy.
10:52 a.m. Taylor Swift isn't the only game in town. Here's how other musicians playing Toronto are dealing with her ubiquity
For the next week and a half, Swift will not be the only game in town, though it may feel that way. Ocie Elliott is just one of the many artists playing shows at smaller venues who have to contend with the issues that come with Swift's unprecedented run of six dates at the Rogers Centre.
Ocie Elliott said they know very well how difficult it is to find a place to stay in Toronto and were aware of the high hotel costs during the period Swift would be in town - a price surge that has concerned some Swifties who are still seeking accommodations.
Read the full story from the Star's Asma Sahebzada
10:49 a.m. Two years ago, there was a gas explosion at the home of Claire Ferguson, a Swiftie from Welland. The explosion, caused by a incorrectly installed stove igniter, knocked her mother unconscious.
Following the accident, Claire and her mother received a small settlement, which they used to buy tickets to the Eras Tour in Toronto. "It's kind of like my birthday celebration," says Claire, who turns 20 next week.
"Taylor just makes me so happy," Claire says. "Whenever I'm having a hard mental time, I listen to her music."
What's she most excited about for tonight?
"Getting to spend time with my mom."

"Some of us are Swifties, so we just came up with a few drinks, that were pink and inspired by her," said Joanna, who was working behind the counter.
"The morning's been busy, we've had fans from all over the world."

Amy, 21, has just one, which she got it after the two superfans from Edmundston, New Brunswick, saw the Eras Tour in Pittsburgh last summer. "I Had the Time of My Life With You," it reads, along with the date of the concert.

9:55 a.m. Swift fans Noelle Rogers and her daughter, Evie, arrived in Toronto on Wednesday. The merch line, Noelle says, was longer than their flight from New York City. But she and Evie are thrilled for the big show tonight.
After grabbing selfies outside Rogers Centre, Noelle and Evie plan to hit up Ripley's Aquarium, before heading back to the hotel for a bracelet-making party.
Evie, who turns 8 this week, says she's most excited to see Taylor perform "Lover" and "Midnights."
9:05 a.m. It's only 9 a.m., but the excitement is starting to slowly bubble up outside the Rogers Centre, where a Taylor Swift impersonator decked out in a bright pink fur coat is regaling a small crowd of young fans with an energetic rendition of "Shake It Off."
Zaylor Zwift is spending the day touring around the city with a small group of fans.Decked out in a sparkling dress and a bright pink fur coat - Zwift led her troupe in a dance challenge soundtracked by "Shake It Off" underneath the CN Tower. 8:45 a.m. The Hasty Market on Bremner Blvd is located very close to Rogers Centre and its staff say they are fully prepared for the influx of Swifties this weekend. Maitri, an employee at the convenient store, says they've stocked up on extra candy and extra beer.
"It will be good for business," she says. "And good for us, because time will fly."
8:33 a.m. A group of hotel service workers in Toronto is set to hold a rally today outside the Fairmont Royal York to demand salary increases as hotel costs in the city skyrocket during Taylor Swift's concerts.
Unite Here Local 75, the union representing 8,000 hospitality workers in the Greater Toronto Area, says Royal York employees have not seen a salary increase since 2021, and have been negotiating a new contract with the hotel since 2022.
The rally comes as the megastar begins her series of six sold-out concerts in Toronto, with the last show scheduled for Nov. 23.

7 a.m.: After 15 months, it's finally here.
Taylor Swift's Eras Tour will open Thursday at the Rogers Centre. It marks the first of six sold-out shows that have been more than a year in the making and which are expected to bring Toronto's downtown core to a standstill.
Toronto is the second-last stop on Swift's two-year, 149-show, multibillion-dollar tour that long ago became the highest-grossing concert series of all time. Swift, the superstar of nearly two decades and author of hits including "I Knew You Were Trouble," "Shake It Off" and "Anti-Hero," is expected to bring more than 500,000 visitors to the downtown core and more than $282 million in economic impact.

We are excited to have you and not at all fretting about traffic or Starbucks congestion. Are you jazzed? Ready to trigger seismic activity under the Rogers Centre? Ready to scream your lungs out as Taylor Swift finally brings her Eras Tour to town?
Here are some tips and fun facts to make your stay more enjoyable.

Due to construction, Toronto has more lane closures than Florida has banned books. The Eglinton LRT line is now expected to open when Taylor and Travis are grandparents.
Read more from the Star's Vinay Menon
Worried about stolen Taylor Swift Toronto tickets? Here's how to protect your Ticketmaster account
Emma Schmelzle was one of the lucky ones. Last August, she and her brother snagged tickets to Taylor Swift's Eras Tour in Toronto for $91 each. The seats were partially obstructed, but the tickets were still a steal. A single Eras Toronto ticket is now selling for close to $3,000 at their cheapest on resale markets.
For more than a year, Schmelzle thought she was in the clear. Then, in September, her brother received an email: their tickets had been transferred out of his Ticketmaster account. Their chance to see one of the biggest tours in history was gone.
Working with Ticketmaster support in the week that followed, Schmelzle and her brother got the tickets back. But that scare - plus another a month later, when it appeared the tickets had been stolen again - has shaken Schmelzle's faith in Ticketmaster.
Read the full story from the Star's Mark Colley
Most Toronto events geared at Swifties not officially 'Taylor's Version'
When fans converge on a convention centre in downtown Toronto this week, they'll be talking about Taylor Swift and listening to her music, but they won't see her name or photo in any official capacity - instead, the event is both literally and figuratively adjacent to the pop star and her mega-popular Eras Tour.
Though it may seem like a sheen of Swift is coating the city of Toronto, only some of the hoopla can really be counted as "Taylor's Version."
"This is adjacent to the brand, but this is not about Taylor," said Bram Goldstein, organizer of the unofficial Swiftie event "Toronto's Version: Taylgate '24."

Here are Toronto's 10 biggest concerts of all time
A rolling stone may gather no moss, but the Rolling Stones certainly gather the masses, especially in or near Toronto.

Swift's stadium history here has been impressive - she entertained 287,220 fans during six sellout Rogers Centre concerts between 2013 and 2018 - and she'll easily surpass the half-million mark with her upcoming shows.

Toronto moves homeless near Rogers Centre 'to help ensure their safety' ahead of Taylor Swift concerts
The city of Toronto is clearing tents belonging to homeless people near the Rogers Centre in preparation for Taylor Swift's Eras Tour. The first of Swift's six sold-out concerts is on Thursday.
"The individuals surrounding Rogers Centre are being offered a space in the city's shelter system to help ensure their safety," a city spokesperson said in an email.
The city said only two people were living in the area. One of them, Chris - who goes by Crystofur and didn't want their last name used due to their circumstances - told the Star on Tuesday they were being moved to a hotel paid for by the city.

Originally from Queensland, Australia, and now living in London, England, Bridget had flown to Toronto for the Eras Tour - her 15th time seeing the concert - with parents Lisa and Spock.

How much will Taylor Swift's visit cost the city?
Taylor Swift is expected to cost Toronto millions in extra services such as transit and policing but the exact amount will remain a secret - for now.
The TTC said it anticipates a bill of $1 million to $1.2 million across the six shows scheduled for the Rogers Centre.
That amount is going toward adding a "significant amount of extra service," said Stuart Green, spokesperson for the transit agency, in an email to the Star.

Document 201 of 500

Nowhere to relocate people living in tents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nowhere-relocate-people-living-tents/docview/3128139168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nowhere+to+relocate+people+living+in+tents&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We know that homelessness in Ontario is a crisis. Encampments are in every community. But a small group of Ontario mayors have chosen a dark path to address the problem - one that should never even be taken, let alone entertained.
Two weeks ago, 13 mayors - from Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Guelph, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury, and Windsor - wrote a letter to the Premier asking him to use the Charter of Rights and Freedom's notwithstanding clause to bypass the court system and allow them to evict encampments - even when there is nowhere else for people to go.
For those of us on the front-lines, we truly have nowhere to refer people. We've given up on searching for shelter beds because they're full. Same with voluntary detox and crisis beds.
And housing? Anyone we've put on the list for social housing languishes for years - sometimes for more than a decade. Sometimes they don't live to see their number come up.
Workers like me know what people need: affordable housing, decent shelter in the interim and voluntary supports to stay healthy and well. These don't exist. And this is the case in Toronto, where I work, as it is for every municipality across the province.
It's why the 444 members of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario are calling for solutions like investing in deeply affordable housing and supportive housing and expanding access to voluntary and evidence-based treatment.
But instead of responding to these sensible requests, Premier Doug Ford is entertaining a handful of mayors - now expanded to 15 mayors, including Midland and St. Thomas - who would rather remove homeless people's Charter-protected rights.
These mayors are referencing a 2023 ruling in Waterloo. In this decision, the court ruled that clearing the Waterloo encampment would be unconstitutional: the city couldn't remove the encampment without providing adequate shelter beds, and to do so would be a violation of homeless people's Charter-protected right to life, liberty, and security of the person.
There is no human right more foundational than the right to life, and no ask more extreme than removing this right from an entire demographic group - homeless people.
In fact, using the notwithstanding clause to override the right to life for homeless people would take away the responsibility of Ontario cities for homelessness altogether - for housing, for shelter, everything.
Nothing about this would make the issue of encampments better, it would mean fewer resources available to a growing number of Ontarians - 234,000 and counting - who are currently or at risk of homelessness. The problem would become more prolific, more visible, and ultimately more criminalized in public spaces.
The request is too ludicrous to be entertained. We elect our leaders to do difficult work, including solving the housing and homelessness crisis - not to take away the Charter rights of those most impacted by the crisis.
These 13 individual mayors do not represent the majority of their mayoral colleagues, let alone their respective councils and constituents, none of whom were consulted on their letter. The Premier can't seriously consider their request - it's undemocratic and un-Canadian.
Indeed, the issue is bigger than just homelessness: this is about protecting the integrity of the Charter and the foundations of Canadian society.
If we fully remove the right to life for one group of people, who's next? What does the Charter mean if the elected leaders of the day can blithely abuse the notwithstanding clause - which was meant to be used in the rarest circumstances - to take away the very rights the Charter protects?
These are big questions and they supersede political affiliation. Whether you support encampment residents, or you want them gone from your communities, or you care about the implications to your rights, everything about this request is reckless, abusive, and dangerous.
To these 15 mayors and the Premier I say: solve the homelessness crisis. Protect the Charter. And do your damned jobs.
Diana Chan McNally is a front-line worker supporting homeless people.@Diana_C_McNally
CREDIT: Diana Chan McNally Contributor Diana Chan McNally is a front-line worker supporting homeless people.@Diana_C_McNally;

Subject: Affordable housing; Homeless people

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Title: Nowhere to relocate people living in tents

Document 202 of 500

City won't earn much revenue from Swift or World Cup

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-wont-earn-much-revenue-swift-world-cup/docview/3128139089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+won%27t+earn+much+revenue+from+Swift+or+World+Cup&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hey Swifties ... and those coming to Toronto for FIFA 2026! Welcome! We always do our best for to make sure people visiting Toronto have lots to see and do.
We hope you enjoy your stay. When you check out, you will see on your bill a charge for a "Municipal Accommodation Tax." For anyone not travelling a lot in North America, I want to tell you quite simply exactly what this is: It's not a tax grab, we aren't trying to raid your wallet.
It is, in fact, one of the few ways the city of Toronto will actually make any money from big events like these. A lot of the tourism tax revenue goes to other levels of government, it doesn't stay in our city. We look for other revenue sources to keep the city full of fun things to do and see, so we can get you to come back.
Not only in Ontario ... but across much of North America, this tax is becoming increasingly popular among municipalities. It's levied at checkout and collected by the accommodation provider: hotels, motels, bed and breakfasts and AirBnBs.
Ontario's Municipal Act, 2001, mandates that we have to revenue share the funds generated by the tax (which is about $98 million annually) with an "eligible tourism entity"- here it goes to Destination Toronto, which promotes our city to tourists, companies and associations hosting conferences here. The rest is turned over to the city of Toronto's municipal government to assist in balancing the budget and providing necessary services to all residents.
While the tax rate and implementation vary from one municipality to another, in Toronto the levy is 6 per cent of the total accommodation fee. While much has been said about how much revenue federal and provincial tax levies - the HST - will generate, here's how that money breaks down: the total of taxes and fees raised by all three levels of government annually in Toronto sees 47 per cent going to the federal government, 44 per cent to the province, and just 9 per cent to the city of Toronto.
Taylor Swift's concerts will help generate increased demand for hotel rooms when bookings are typically lower than peak season (November is a "slower" month), although the additional tax revenue the city will receive from the six days of concerts will not be too significant. The city estimates all levels of governments are expected to take in around $40 million in tax revenue from the events, but the city will only receive about $250,000 in total from this added municipal tax.
Toronto, like all Ontario municipalities, has been facing challenges with its long-term financial sustainability for years. Our municipal government has seen a significant transfer of responsibilities and services over time, and the expectations and complexity of our residents has also increased, from challenges with infrastructure to homelessness. These challenges are exacerbated by unforeseen events such as COVID and the effects of climate change, which have put pressure on our municipal budget.
Toronto is limited in the ways we collect revenue, and as municipal costs have increased, revenue tools have not kept up. Traditional revenue streams include taxation, including property taxes, special area rates, payments in lieu of taxation, user fees, fees for licenses, permits and rents, fines and penalties, investment income and development charges.
Another key source of revenue are conditional and unconditional transfers from other levels of government. These transfers are currently a mixed bag of project-based and formula-based funding. This type of funding does not allow for long-term financial planning.
Premier Doug Ford stated, "Toronto is critical to Ontario and Canada's economic success, and we need all levels of government working together to deliver solutions that protect services and put the city on a path toward long-term financial sustainability. When Toronto succeeds, Ontario and Canada succeed."
How do we do this when we only receive 9 per cent of all levied taxes and fees? As a municipal government, we look at every source of revenue while we work with organizations like the Federation of Canadian Municipalities and Association of Ontario Municipalities to encourage our federal and provincial governments to step up to the financial plate and support our cities, both large and small.
So thanks, Swifties ... hope you come back again, or for the FIFA World Cup events in 2026. We're happy to have you.
Paul Ainslie is the city councillor for Ward 24,
Scarborough-Guildwood.
CAPTION: The City of Toronto estimates all levels of governments areexpected to take in around $40 million in tax revenue from theTaylor Swift concerts, Paul Ainslie writes, but the city will onlyreceive about $250,000 of that.Richard LautensToronto Star
CREDIT: Paul Ainslie Contributor Paul Ainslie is the city councillor for Ward 24, Scarborough-Guildwood.;

Removing Toronto bike lanes will cost an estimated $48 million: City

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/removing-toronto-bike-lanes-will-cost-estimated/docview/3128382437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Removing+Toronto+bike+lanes+will+cost+an+estimated+%2448+million%3A+City&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new report from the city Wednesday estimates that ripping up bike lanes on Yonge Street, Bloor Street and University Avenue will cost taxpayers $48 million - on top of the $27 million it cost to install the lanes.
This figure does not include additional costs the city said could be incurred to identify, design and construct alternative cycling routes.
There will also be other staff and infrastructure costs for redesigning and reconstructing these affected roads to add space for cars where the lanes used to be.
"The removal of existing bike lanes will be a complex project that will be challenging to implement within a short-time period, due to planning, design and construction requirements," says the report from the city manager.
Removing the lanes also has the potential to decrease safety for pedestrians, cyclists and drivers, according to the report.
Premier Doug Ford has vowed to dismantle bike lanes on sections of Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue, which his government has said contribute to Toronto's traffic woes. He is also moving to make Ontario municipalities ask for permission to construct new lanes going forward.
More to come
CAPTION: Legislation put forward by Premier Doug Ford's government restricts Toronto council's power to approve bike lanes, and allows the removal of bike lanes already in place.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, November 13 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Olivia Chow's beef with Doug Ford over bike lanes, a massive gunfight in West Queen West and city residents unable to make ends meet.
DON'T MISSDon't expect Olivia Chow to war with Doug Ford over bike lanes
Toronto's mayor faces an uphill and potentially lonely battle to get Ford to stay in his legislative lane, David Rider reports. City council will debate this week how to convince the premier to shelve a proposed law empowering the province to rip out Toronto bike lanes. Chow would have to battle in the court of public opinion - but there are two reasons you shouldn't expect her to go to war on the issue.
Wait, what? The Ford government's 2021 Supreme Court victory established the province had the right to slash the size of Toronto council mid-election and, by extension, do pretty much whatever it wants with Canada's largest city.By the numbers: How many Torontonians actually cycle? Statistics vary wildly, going as low as one per cent to as high as 70 per cent. Here's why the numbers are so different, and what the actual answer might be.A massive gunfight broke out at a Toronto recording studio
It was supposed to be a routine bail check, police say. Instead, plainclothes officers found themselves in the middle of a gunfight between two rival groups Monday night, during which more than 100 shots were exchanged. Remarkably, no one was hurt. "It sounded like war," one resident said of the battle that saw a West Queen West grocery store and unmarked police vehicle riddled with bullet holes. Here's what happened.
Moments earlier: Around 11:20 p.m. Monday, three suspects in a stolen vehicle arrived in the area of Queen Street West and Sudbury Street and opened fire at a recording studio on the second floor of a building, where a crowd of people had gathered outside. The shooting quickly escalated from there.The aftermath: Police say they seized more than 16 firearms, including two assault-style rifles, and arrested 23 people at the scene. Two suspects escaped and remain at large.Toronto saw a big jump in residents unable to make ends meet

The increased financial vulnerability is corroding Torontonians' physical and mental health, while institutions helping the city's most vulnerable are wearing thin. Omar Mosleh explores the ramifications of the concerning trend, and what it means for you.

Document 205 of 500

Don't expect Olivia Chow to go to war with Doug Ford over bike lanes. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dont-expect-olivia-chow-go-war-with-doug-ford/docview/3127645860/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+expect+Olivia+Chow+to+go+to+war+with+Doug+Ford+over+bike+lanes.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow faces an uphill and potentially lonely battle trying to get Ontario Premier Doug Ford to stay in his legislative lane.
City council will this week debate how to convince Ford to shelve a proposed law that would empower his government to remove specific Toronto bike lanes. Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act, would also give the province a veto over any proposed new bikeways that would remove a traffic lane.
Chow, a cycling advocate elected in June 2023 after beating a third-place rival who vowed to rip out bike lanes, says Toronto must fight this provincial "overreach."
Pressed Tuesday on how she will do that, Chow instead answered why she feels the city should try to thwart the unusual intervention by a one-term city councillor who tried and failed to become mayor before becoming the much more powerful premier in 2018.
"Local democracy matters and we need to make sure everyone is safe," Chow told reporters after an unrelated event. She noted motorists have killed six cyclists on Toronto roads so far this year and suggested Ford fight congestion by finally opening the Eglinton LRT.
"I am not one that would back down when it comes to safety," the mayor said. "Literally, some bike lanes save lives."
Pressed on ways to fight the Progressive Conservative majority government, Chow said she's waiting for a city staff report that will be tabled sometime before council debates her "Respecting Local Democracy and Cities" motion, likely on Thursday.
Ford, for his part, says Toronto's notorious gridlock demands the removal of sections of bike lane on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue. Neither Ford nor his transportation minister, Prabmeet Sarkaria, have offered any data to prove that those separated lanes are causing traffic any more than road construction or condo projects that occupy vehicle lanes, sometimes for years.
But they don't have to. The Ford government's Supreme Court of Canada win in 2021 established that the province had the right to unilaterally slash the size of Toronto council midelection and, by extension, do pretty much whatever it wants with its legislative offshoot which happens to be Canada's biggest city.
The court's 5-4 decision included a sharply worded dissent calling Ford's intervention unlawful. However, barring some kind of novel legal challenge, perhaps based on personal safety concerns, little doubt exists that the Ford government has the legal power to rip out bike lanes approved by elected Toronto officials and funded by city taxpayers.
That leaves Chow the court of public opinion, something to which the premier pays a lot of attention.
Most observers believe that Ford's out-of-the-blue move on cycling infrastructure is driven by internal polling that suggests, with talk of an early 2025 provincial election brewing, the move will help him more with fed-up drivers than it will hurt him with furious bike lane lovers.
A wedge issue could swing some Toronto motorists their way.
"I think the government has a pretty good idea that the politics work for them on this, especially in Etobicoke," where the PCs currently hold three seats, said Jim Burnett, a former city hall political staffer who works on PC political campaigns.
"They're convinced that the politics work quite strong for them, they've done polling on it, they hear about bike lanes."
Ford has lost to city hall before. In 2019, before any hint of a looming pandemic, his government announced deep cuts to funding for provincial health, with Toronto inexplicably hit the hardest. John Tory and Joe Cressy, then the mayor and public health chair, respectively, went door to door in PC ridings, urging residents to give their MPPs an earful for Ford.
Their public relations war, augmented by firepower from mayors across the province, won the day. Ford retreated and saved himself a massive political problem when COVID-19 hit the following year.
There are two reasons, however, to not expect Chow cycling door to door to lobby homeowners to fight Ford and save bike lanes.
First, the Ford government last year agreed to a new deal for Toronto to rescue the city's pandemic-ravaged finances. Negotiations on everything from uploading the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway to helping fund new subway cars for Line 2 are, city staff say, expected to produce a deal in the new year with "ongoing collaboration and partnership."
Chow, a former NDP MP with a list of socially conscious goals for her first term, can't afford to do much of anything without that deal.
Second, even many cyclists will acknowledge that there are enough bike lane haters to convince Ford he is on the right track.
"I don't think there is enough sentiment on bike lanes to change (Ford's) direction," said Coun. Josh Matlow, a progressive who lost the mayor's race to Chow but has fought more public battles with the premier. "Public opinion within the city is mixed on bike lanes. For people who care about them, they care passionately, and the people who don't passionately despise them."
Matlow believes the mayor and council should instead try to galvanize Torontonians against Ford's micromanagement of city issues, asking voters if he should be usurping the power of the local politicians they elected, and whom they can hold accountable in the next civic election.
Matlow is a proponent of Toronto seeking to become a charter city - a long-standing global concept that involves providing specific legal jurisdiction to cities under the constitution, allowing them to manage their own affairs. It's needed more than ever, he says.
"When Doug Ford gave John Tory 'strong mayor' powers, everyone believed the mayor and the city had stronger powers, but that was never true," Matlow said. "The reality is that, thanks to Premier Ford's many interventions, we just have a weaker council, we have a weaker city." Another reality for Chow is that she is an unabashedly progressive mayor leading a centrist city council. While downtown councillors might be eager to mix it up, the mayor is not guaranteed a majority from across the city will back more than a sternly worded request. Some councillors friendly to Chow, but who also know Ford from his days at city hall, told the Star on Tuesday that they don't sense a great appetite on council for Toronto to go toe-to-toe with the premier over bike lanes. For now, the lane appears to be his.
CAPTION: Then mayor-elect Olivia Chow cycles with supporters on the morning of her swearing in July of last year.

Subject: Public opinion; Local elections; Mayors; Automobile drivers; Councils; Politics; Pandemics; Bicycling

Title: Don't expect Olivia Chow to go to war with Doug Ford over bike lanes. Here's why

Canada needs to get serious about climate change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-needs-get-serious-about-climate-change/docview/3127645845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+needs+to+get+serious+about+climate+change&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's priorities at the global climate talks, and the Trump impact, Nov. 11
Canada and the provinces are not taking climate change seriously enough.
With silly slogans such as Axe the Tax and Scrap the Cap we are sleep-walking into darkness. Every level of government and individual Canadians should be examining how we all can make adjustments now to our way of life to make climate change the No. 1 priority.
I highly recommend reading the the book "Fire Weather: The Making of a Beast" for everyone to see what climate change is doing. Albertans especially should read it, because the book deals with the burning down of Fort McMurray in 2016. This will continue to happen and probably with increasing frequency and intensity.
We need to act ... now.
Gary Johnson, Stouffville
Provincial government is wasting our tax dollars
Millions of provincial infrastructure tax dollars are being spent on the reconstruction of an overpass in Oshawa at the 401 and Albert Street.
However, Albert Street goes nowhere, as it's not a main street. The reconstruction makes no sense. When the old bridge, built in the late 1940s, was demolished, replacing it with a new bridge was unnecessary.
I reached out to an Oshawa city councillor whose response was: "I have made the exact same comment to our staff and to the province. They give no justification. A total waste of money."
I have no idea if the city of Oshawa had any more involvement with the province on this matter, or if the province and city consulted with each other before the project was started. A costly mistake if they didn't.
It's disgusting how the Ford Conservatives are wasting taxpayer dollars.
Robert Dingman, Oshawa
Doug Ford has his transit priorities all wrongFord government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads, Oct. 18
If the Doug Ford government is serious about fighting traffic congestion, how about spending the millions of dollars it would cost to remove bike lanes on improving public transit instead?
Instead of going backwards, that money, time and resources could go towards increasing frequency of bus routes, adding new bus routes or buying the much needed new subway cars for Line 2.
These are steps that would actually reduce traffic.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
Compassionate leadership sorely lacking in housing crisisWhy haven't Toronto's efforts to fight homelessness been enough? These numbers tell the story, Oct. 25
This excellent article notes some solutions to homelessness, such as rent subsidies and eviction prevention programs. We also desperately need to recognize that our affordable housing crisis will not be solved by the market.
Rather, we need all levels of government, especially the provincial and federal governments, to invest in building masses of affordable housing province and nationwide. The Canadian federal government adopted this policy after the Second World War and built affordable housing all across the country. That staved off the housing crisis at the end of the war and is why these tiny bungalows are still called 'wartime houses.'
It is high time our provincial and federal governments step up and show leadership and compassion to help house Canadian citizens.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
Consumer roaming options shouldn't concern CRTCWireless carriers tell CRTC they're already offering flexible roaming options, Nov. 11
Why does the CRTC express concern regarding roaming options when, as reported by the Toronto Star, carriers intend to transfer these charges to other areas?
Travellers have ample options for staying connected. E-SIM cards are available at a cost as low as $6 for a 30-day period, contingent upon the data requirements. Additionally, travellers can purchase SIM cards from various countries.
These consumer options are sufficient to effectively regulate the industry, thereby liberating the CRTC to address other pressing matters.
Patricia Goldthorpe, Toronto
Minimum wage should be higher in citiesI've dated a billionaire and lived on minimum wage. This is the one, radical solution to the inequalities I've seen, Nov. 11
Once again, an article about the minimum wage in Ontario. Once again, it ignores the fact that in some towns and small cities of Ontario, $17.60 is very adequate.
For those living in Toronto, it isn't.
Not all companies are giants like Walmart, Canadian Tire, McDonald and some just can't pay more than that. In wanting higher wages for Ontario workers, we must not kill small businesses.
Many jurisdictions in the U.S. and Europe have a higher minimum wage in and around large cities and a lower minimum wage further away from those cities. It's time we do the same here in Ontario.
The Torontonian should earn more than his counterpart in Thunder Bay.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Canadians must hold corporations accountable
While the cost of food, gas and housing is top of mind, other more hidden costs and ghost fees imposed by large corporations contribute towards our cost of living concerns.
Thankfully, governments and regulatory bodies appear increasingly willing to crack down on corporate malfeasance. This is happening in large part because individuals are calling out unfair corporate practices and demanding action.
I have recently contacted our federal government and the Canadian Transport Agency to urge them to expand upon the Canada Air Passengers Protection Regulations. They can do this by initiating a thorough review of General Conditions of Carriage rules employed by airlines to shield themselves from reasonable complaints, and to monetize situations which, without such arbitrary conditions, could easily be resolved fairly and without punitive costs and fees.
It is up to each of us to take individual responsibility to point out unfair business practices whenever and wherever we encounter them. And we must put pressure on public officials and regulatory bodies to intervene and ban such practices, for the benefit of us all.
Peter Eriksen, Richmond, B.C.
CAPTION: The "beast" of a wildfire that charred Fort McMurray, Alta., in 2016 is a reminder to all Canadians of the devastating effects of climate change.

Subject: Minimum wage; Affordable housing; Climate change; Cities

Document 207 of 500

Ford takes aim at Mexico: Premier pushes for bilateral trade deal with United States

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-takes-aim-at-mexico/docview/3127519609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+takes+aim+at+Mexico%3A+Premier+pushes+for+bilateral+trade+deal+with+United+States&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is taking a run at the border- the Mexican border.
With the prospect of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump slapping hefty tariffs on Canadian products after he takes office in January, Ford is moving to insulate Ontario from American levies.
The premier, who has been advocating a "Buy Can-Am mindset" that would more closely tie Canada with the U.S., took aim at Mexico on Tuesday.
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S.," said Ford, pointing toward upcoming renegotiations on the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA).
"If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them," he said at a campaign-style event in Oro-Medonte, 125 kilometres north of Toronto.
Ford's comments come amid mounting concern that Ontario will be collateral damage as the Trump administration imposes hefty tariffs on imports to protect American industry, triggering a trade war.
University of Calgary economics professor Trevor Tombe has warned a 10 per cent American tariff would result in the equivalent of an average Canadian's annual income dropping by $1,100 and cause a five per cent plunge in Canada's gross domestic product.
That's a nightmare scenario for Ford, who noted that Ontario is the economic "engine of Canada and 95 per cent of all the jobs that were created just last month were here."
"Free trade needs to be fair," the premier said.
"Since signing on to the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, Mexico has allowed itself to become a backdoor for Chinese cars, auto parts and other products into Canadian and American markets, putting Canadian and American workers' livelihoods at risk while undermining our communities and doing enormous harm to our shared economic success," he said.
Ford was one of the Canadian leaders who convinced Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to match U.S. President Joe Biden's 100 per cent tariff on artificially cheap Chinese electric vehicles, which are subsidized by Beijing, in order to protect Ontario's burgeoning domestic EV industry.
The premier and the prime minister were also concerned that Canada might serve as a back door to import inexpensive Chinese EVs into the U.S. if matching tariffs weren't introduced.
"If Mexico won't fight transshipment by, at the very least, matching Canadian and American tariffs on Chinese imports, they shouldn't have a seat at the table or enjoy access to the largest economy in the world," said Ford.
"Instead, we must prioritize the closest economic partnership on Earth by directly negotiating a bilateral U.S.-Canada free trade agreement that puts U.S. and Canadian workers first."
Asked about Ford's comments during a news conference in Fredericton with newly elected New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt, Trudeau said China's trade policies are a concern among most of Canada's allies.
"All of our partner democracies around the world have expressed varying degrees of concern about China's overcapacity and around unfair trade practices that the Chinese economy is inflicting on the world," he said, adding it is an issue he intends to raise with Trump.
"We're going to continue to work with partners like the United States and, hopefully Mexico as well, to make sure that we are united in our desire to protect good jobs," he said.
The office of Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland - a friend of Ford who conferred with the premier as the U.S. election results poured in on Nov. 5 - underscored that.
"Canada firmly believes that action is necessary to level the playing field for Canadian workers in the face of China's intentional, state-directed policy of overcapacity," said spokespersonKatherine Cuplinskas.
"That is why we have put strong tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles, steel and aluminum. Canada will not be a back door to other markets, especially to our most important friend and ally, the United States."
Canadian officials are bracing for the prospect of a second Trump presidency that would be even more protectionist than when he was in office between 2017 and 2021.
Flavio Volpe, president of theAutomotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who works closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on trade issues, said Ford "is hitting a note that's been making the rounds in Washington for some time."
"The Chinese are relentless in their strategies to access the North American market and all partners have a share in the responsibility to protect it," said Volpe, noting the domestic industry is integrated with Mexico's automotive sector.
"Canadian suppliers are heavily invested in Mexico to serve carmakers where they build cars," he saidin an interview Tuesday.
"We've been working closely with Mexican officials for sometime now as local investors on navigating this issue to our collective benefit."
With files from Ryan Tumilty
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says that Mexico has become a "backdoor for Chinese cars, auto parts and other products into Canadian and American markets."Chris Young THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Ryan Tumilty

Document 208 of 500

Doug Ford wants to rip up bike lanes - but how many people in Toronto actually cycle?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-rip-up-bike-lanes-how-many-people/docview/3127516521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+rip+up+bike+lanes+-+but+how+many+people+in+Toronto+actually+cycle%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's often said that numbers don't lie. But do they bike to work, or do they drive?
Since the Ford government announced plans to limit new bike lanes and "review" some of Toronto's existing ones, one number has received a lot of scrutiny: how many Torontonians actually cycle. Wildly different statistics have been tossed out. Those who go looking could come up with numbers as low as one per cent, or as high as 70 per cent, depending on the source.
Why are these numbers so different - and what's the most accurate one? The answer, and how it changes going forward, will influence not just the current debate over bike lanes and gridlock, but policies as consequential as the City of Toronto's plan to fight the climate crisis.
Taking the narrow view
Since Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives unveiled legislation last month that would limit new bike lanes and review existing ones, he and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria have returned to the same statistic: that only 1.2 per cent of Toronto residents commute by bike. The Star has also cited this number.
The statistic comes from a sterling source, but it has three problems: It is old, and it is - stay with us - both overly broad and overly narrow.
Here's the 'old' part: Statistics Canada surveys how Canadians get to their jobs through the census, which is collected every five years. The 1.2 per cent number comes from the 2011 version of this survey - three census cycles ago, and a time, some have pointed out, before Toronto had any significant biking infrastructure.
It's unclear why Ford and Sarkaria have touted this statistic when the most current one, from the 2021 census, might seem to prove their point better. After ticking up slightly in the 2016 census to 1.4 per cent, the number of cyclist commuters in the Toronto region dipped to 0.97 per cent in 2021.
So is that the best number? That brings us to the 'broad' part. This data comes from a geographic region that Statistics Canada calls a "census metropolitan area." Toronto's CMA covers an enormous swath around the city proper. It includes a number of small towns and their semirural surroundings, such as Uxbridge and King, which have 10 cyclist commuters each. It also includes big, car-centric suburbs such as Brampton, where Sarkaria's riding is, and where the cycling rate is just a notch above zero - 0.1 per cent of its 238,000 commuters bike.
Luckily for us, Statistics Canada has also calculated the same figure for what we would call the city of Toronto proper (and what StatCan, bless its heart, would call a "census subdivision," not to be confused with the above-mentioned "census metropolitan area"). Excluding all those suburban and exurban areas and looking just at Toronto, the percentage of cyclist commuters in 2021 was two per cent, or about 16,000 people.
But wait. Now comes the 'narrow' part. The question on the long-form census is very specific: It asks workers to choose their "main mode of commuting," and they can only pick one. If you bike to a GO station, you choose the mode you used for longest distance (probably the GO train). If you drive from fall to spring but cycle in the summer, you pick the mode you used the first week of May (probably car).
To capture more information, the 2021 survey added an extra question on commuters' other modes of transportation. Counting these sometimes-cyclists nearly doubles the number: In 2021, 3.8 per cent, or nearly 30,000 commuters in the city of Toronto used a bicycle as one of their modes of commuting.
Thank you for joining us on this ride: If you want to use a Statistics Canada source for the number of Toronto residents who cycle to work, the best one is probably 3.8 per cent.
But with apologies to the good and rigorous folks at Statistics Canada, there is reason to think we should keep looking for our answer.
Cyclists are more than just commuters
If we're counting cyclists, who qualifies as one? Getting to work isn't the only reason people hop on a bicycle.
Different polls have tried to tackle a more expansive definition of a "cyclist," which bumps the number higher still.
In 2019, the city of Toronto commissioned Nanos to undertake a cycling-related survey, which sampled 1,516 people across all parts of the city on their bike habits. The "70 per cent" figure is from this poll: Nanos found that seven in 10 respondents cycled for some reason, whether for fitness or recreation, getting to school or work, to go shopping or visiting friends.
Like the 0.97 per cent statistic from StatCan, this number isn't wrong, it's just the result of asking the question in a particular way: In this survey, someone who bikes for fun a couple of times a year counts as much as someone who cycles to work daily.
Then, there is yet another survey - perhaps the Holy Grail of transportation surveys.
For almost 40 years, the Ministry of Transportation and a group of municipalities and transit agencies have partnered on the Transportation Tomorrow Survey. Collected every five years, these are "the largest travel surveys ever undertaken anywhere," according to the University of Toronto data management group. The 2016 version involved more than 160,000 interviews.
The survey asks every household to count all trips in the past 24 hours, and are a richly detailed source of information.
One thing it shows is how varied residents' transportation choices are, even within the city of Toronto. In Etobicoke and North York, during the morning rush hour, only eight per cent of respondents either walked or cycled (both modes of "active transportation" are bundled together). Within the old city of Toronto borders, that number more than triples, to 26 per cent. One of the city's climate goals is for three-quarters of school and work trips under five kilometres to be walked, biked or by transit by 2030.
Statistics Canada's data also shows enormous variation between neighbourhoods. Some Toronto census tracts in Palmerston-Little Italy, the Annex and Casa Loma have bike-commuting rates between 15 and 19 per cent, compared to the regionwide one per cent. (The highest is the census tract that covers the Toronto Islands, where personal cars aren't allowed: 43 per cent. Census tracts with no cyclist commuters are overrepresented in the city's north, including more than 10 in north Etobicoke, which Ford represents.)
These figures were taken from the 2016 Transportation Tomorrow Survey, but another survey was completed in 2022 after being delayed by the pandemic. Those results were supposed to be published in "fall 2024," which should be about now. Asked when the results would be released, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Transportation said that "the target date for releasing the Transportation Tomorrow 2022 survey data and reports is still 2024."
As we wait, it's worth asking what number would settle this debate - if any would.
Whatever the true number of cyclists, it will not be bigger than the 61 per cent of Torontonians and 76 per cent in the region who drive to work. Those who oppose bike lanes will likely still argue cyclists are a minority whose interests are being prioritized above the much bigger majority who drive, and who are stuck in productivity-depressing gridlock. Those who support bike lanes will probably say we will never find our way out of the congestion problem, or make a dent our contributions to the climate crisis, or stop seeing cyclists killed on our roads, without creating a safe and efficient cycling network that encourages residents to ditch their cars for bikes.
with files from David Rider
CAPTION: The bike lanes on University Avenue are in the cross-hairs of Premier Doug Ford's Bill 212, along with those on Yonge and Bloor Streets.

Subject: Automobiles; Statistics; Polls & surveys; Commuting; Censuses; Bicycles; Bicycling

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Title: Doug Ford wants to rip up bike lanes - but how many people in Toronto actually cycle?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 13, 2024

Section: news/gta

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 209 of 500

Crombie's Liberals pledge income tax breaks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombies-liberals-pledge-income-tax-breaks/docview/3127507039/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%27s+Liberals+pledge+income+tax+breaks&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals are promising a $2.8-billion tax cut plan ahead of an expected early provincial electionwith an income tax breakPremier Doug Ford has not delivered since pledging it in 2018.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Tuesday she would cut middle-class income taxes 22 per cent if elected premier in addition to axing the eight per cent provincial portion of the Harmonized Sales Tax on home heating fuels and hydro bills.
The measures would save the average family $1,150 a year in "permanent financial relief," she said, contrasting the breaks to the $200 tax rebate cheques Ford's Progressive Conservative government will send to 15 million Ontarians in January to defray the cost of living after a bout of high inflation.
"Doug Ford thinks a one-time $200 rebate cheque makes up for the cost of living crisis he created. Well, I don't," Crombie said Tuesday at a Gabby's restaurant location in Scarborough.
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation applauded her move.
"Premier Doug Ford promised income tax relief for the middle class six years ago, but taxpayers are still waiting," said Jay Goldberg, the organization's Ontario director.
"While the federal government's carbon tax keeps making those basics more expensive, Crombie's promise to take the provincial portion of the HST off home energy bills will make a big difference."
Ford wouldn't commit to matching the Liberal tax cuts at a campaign-style stop in the Barrie area to announce funding for a new facility to train crane operators for Ontario's construction industry.
He accused Crombie of planning to give municipalities broader taxing powers while revoking his temporary gasoline tax cut of 10.7 cents per litre, elimination of tolls on Highways 412 and 418 and scrapping of licence plate renewal fees.
"What Bonnie Crombie doesn't understand is there's an affordability crisis right now. Taxing people does not work," Ford said.
"They're going to have a very clear choice," he added, referring to Ontarians. "Vote for a government that's creating opportunities, creating jobs, getting people trained, building infrastructure ... and making sure we're prudent fiscal managers."
The premier's income tax cut never materialized as the province was engulfed in the COVID-19 pandemic, which sent Ontario's deficit skyrocketing as the government was forced to dramatically increase spending on health.
Ford's $200 rebate cheques, which opposition parties have dubbed a pre-election bribe, will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion this fiscal year as the province runs a deficit of $6.6 billion.
Crombie said her tax cut plan would cost $2.8 billion, but there is no clear accounting of where the money will come from.
The Liberals said it will be paid for by ending "shady backroom deals" that Ford has made with "wealthy friends and insiders over the last six years ... at the expense of the real people of Ontario."
Crombie cited the abrupt closure of the Ontario Science Centre and its planned move to Ontario Place with a parking garage, a deal with the European company Therme to build a spa at Ontario Place, up to $1 billion spent to put beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails into convenience stores a year ahead of schedule, and the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
"We'll just stop the giveaways," said Crombie.
The Liberals now have nine seats and hold a distant third place in the legislature behind the Official Opposition NDP. Ford's PCs have a solid lead in public opinion polls.
Crombie's tax cut plan comes a couple of weeks after Ford began touting his rebate cheques and taunting her with them.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers ... she wants to increase taxes," Ford said late last month.
Crombie's income tax break would reduce the tax rate on taxable income between $51,446 and $75,000 to 7.15 per cent from the current 9.15 per cent.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
