Document 211 of 500

Chow faces roadblocks in fight over bike lanes: Limited by legal options, mayor may also find it hard to sway public opinion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-faces-roadblocks-fight-over-bike-lanes/docview/3127506865/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+faces+roadblocks+in+fight+over+bike+lanes%3A+Limited+by+legal+options%2C+mayor+may+also+find+it+hard+to+sway+public+opinion&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow faces an uphill and potentially lonely battle trying to get Ontario Premier Doug Ford to stay in his legislative lane.
City council will debate this week how to convince Ford to shelve a proposed law that would empower his government to remove specific Toronto bike lanes. Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act, would also give the province a veto over any new bikeways that would remove a traffic lane.
Chow, a cycling advocate elected in June 2023 after beating a third-place rival who vowed to rip out bike lanes, says Toronto must fight this provincial "overreach."
Pressed Tuesday on how she will do that, Chow instead answered why she feels the city should try to thwart the unusual intervention by a one-term city councillor who tried and failed to become mayor before becoming the much more powerful premier in 2018.
"Local democracy matters and we need to make sure everyone is safe," Chow told reporters after an unrelated event. She noted motorists have killed six cyclists on Toronto roads so far this year and suggested Ford fight congestion by finally opening the Eglinton LRT.
"I am not one that would back down when it comes to safety," the mayor said.
"Literally, some bike lanes save lives."
Pressed on ways to fight the Progressive Conservative majority government, Chow said she's waiting for a city staff report that will be tabled sometime before council debates her "Respecting Local Democracy and Cities" motion, likely on Thursday.
Ford, for his part, says Toronto's notorious gridlock demands the removal of sections of bike lane on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue. Neither Ford nor his transportation minister, Prabmeet Sarkaria, have offered any data to prove that those separated lanes are causing traffic any more than road construction or condo projects that occupy vehicle lanes, sometimes for years.
But they don't have to. The Ford government's Supreme Court of Canada win in 2021 established that the province had the right to unilaterally slash the size of Toronto council midelection and, by extension, do pretty much whatever it wants with its legislative offshoot that happens to be Canada's biggest city.
The court's 5-4 decision included a sharply worded dissent calling Ford's intervention unlawful. However, barring some kind of novel legal challenge, perhaps based on personal safety concerns, little doubt exists that the Ford government has the legal power to rip out bike lanes approved by elected Toronto officials and funded by city taxpayers.
That leaves Chow the court of public opinion, something to which the premier pays a lot of attention.
Most observers believe Ford's out-of-the-blue move on cycling infrastructure is driven by internal polling that suggests, with talk of an early 2025 provincial election brewing, the move will help him more with fed-up drivers than it will hurt him with furious bike lane lovers.
A wedge issue could swing some Toronto motorists their way.
"I think the government has a pretty good idea that the politics work for them on this, especially in Etobicoke," where the PCs currently hold three seats, said Jim Burnett, a former city hall political staffer who works on PC political campaigns.
"They're convinced that the politics work quite strong for them, they've done polling on it, they hear about bike lanes."
Ford has lost to city hall before. In 2019, before any hint of a looming pandemic, his government announced deep cuts to funding for provincial health, with Toronto inexplicably hit the hardest. John Tory and Joe Cressy, then the mayor and public health chair, respectively, went door to door in PC ridings, urging residents to give their MPPs an earful for Ford.
Their public relations war, augmented by firepower from mayors across the province, won the day. Ford retreated and saved himself a massive political problem when COVID-19 hit the following year.
There are two reasons, however, to not expect Chow cycling door to door to lobby homeowners to fight Ford and save bike lanes.
First, the Ford government last year agreed to a new deal for Toronto to rescue the city's pandemic-ravaged finances. Negotiations on everything from uploading the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway to helping fund new subway cars for Line 2 are, city staff say, expected to produce a deal in the new year with "ongoing collaboration and partnership."
Chow can't afford to do much of anything without that deal.
Second, even many cyclists will acknowledge that there are enough bike lane haters to convince Ford he is on the right track.
"I don't think there is enough sentiment on bike lanes to change (Ford's) direction," said Coun. Josh Matlow, a progressive who lost the mayor's race to Chow but has fought more public battles with the premier.
"Public opinion within the city is mixed on bike lanes. For people who care about them, they care passionately, and the people who don't passionately despise them."
Matlow believes the mayor and council should instead try to galvanize Torontonians against Ford's micromanagement of city issues.
Matlow is a proponent of Toronto seeking to become a charter city - a long-standing global concept that involves providing specific legal jurisdiction to cities under the constitution, allowing them to manage their own affairs.
"When Doug Ford gave John Tory 'strong mayor' powers, everyone believed the mayor and the city had stronger powers, but that was never true," Matlow said. "The reality is that, thanks to Premier Ford's many interventions, we just have a weaker council, we have a weaker city."
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Full text: Need to get serious about climate change
Canada's priorities at the global climate talks, and the Trump impact, Nov. 11
Canada and the provinces are not taking climate change seriously enough. With silly slogans such as Axe the Tax and Scrap the Cap we are sleep-walking into darkness. Every level of government and individual Canadians should be examining how we all can make adjustments now to our way of life to make climate change the No. 1 priority. I highly recommend reading the book "Fire Weather: The Making of a Beast" for everyone to see what climate change is doing. Albertans especially should read it, because the book deals with the burning down of Fort McMurray in 2016. This will continue to happen and probably with increasing frequency and intensity. We need to act ... now.
Gary Johnson, Stouffville
Province is wasting our
tax dollars
Millions of provincial infrastructure tax dollars are being spent on the reconstruction of an overpass in Oshawa at the 401 and Albert Street. However, Albert Street goes nowhere, as it's not a main street. The reconstruction makes no sense. When the old bridge, built in the late 1940s, was demolished, replacing it with a new bridge was unnecessary. I reached out to an Oshawa city councillor whose response was: "I have made the exact same comment to our staff and to the province. They give no justification. A total waste of money." I have no idea if the city of Oshawa had any more involvement with the province on this matter, or if the province and city consulted with each other before the project was started. A costly mistake if they didn't. It's disgusting how the Ford government is wasting taxpayer dollars.
Robert Dingman, Oshawa
Ford has his transit priorities all wrong
Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads,
Oct. 18
If the Doug Ford government is serious about fighting traffic congestion, how about spending the millions of dollars it would cost to remove bike lanes on improving public transit instead? Instead of going backwards, that money, time and resources could go towards increasing frequency of bus routes, adding new bus routes or buying the much needed new subway cars for Line 2. These are steps that would actually reduce traffic.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
Compassionate leadership is sorely lacking
Why haven't Toronto's efforts to fight homelessness been enough? These numbers tell the story, Oct. 25
This excellent article notes some solutions to homelessness, such as rent subsidies and eviction prevention programs. We also desperately need to recognize that our affordable housing crisis will not be solved by the market.
Rather, we need all levels of government, especially the provincial and federal governments, to invest in building masses of affordable housing province and nationwide. The Canadian federal government adopted this policy after the Second World War and built affordable housing all across the country. That staved off the housing crisis at the end of the war and is why these tiny bungalows are still called "wartime houses." It is high time our provincial and federal governments step up and show leadership and compassion to help house Canadian citizens.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
Roaming options shouldn't concern CRTC
Wireless carriers tell CRTC they're already offering flexible roaming options, Nov. 11
Why does the CRTC express concern regarding roaming options when, as reported by the Toronto Star, carriers intend to transfer these charges to other areas? Travellers have ample options for staying connected. E-SIM cards are available at a cost as low as $6 for a 30-day period, contingent upon the data requirements.
Additionally, travellers can purchase SIM cards from various countries.
These consumer options are sufficient to effectively regulate the industry, thereby liberating the CRTC to address other pressing matters.
Patricia Goldthorpe, Toronto
Minimum wage should be
higher in cities
I've dated a billionaire and lived on minimum wage. This is the one, radical solution to the inequalities I've seen, Nov. 11
Once again, an article about the minimum wage in Ontario. Once again, it ignores the fact that in some towns and small cities of Ontario, $17.60 is very adequate. For those living in Toronto, it isn't. Not all companies are giants like Walmart, Canadian Tire, McDonald and some just can't pay more than that. In wanting higher wages for Ontario workers, we must not kill small businesses.
Many jurisdictions in the U.S. and Europe have a higher minimum wage in and around large cities and a lower minimum wage further away from those cities. It's time we do the same here in Ontario.
The Torontonian should earn more than his counterpart in Thunder Bay.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Corporations should be held accountable
While the cost of food, gas and housing is top of mind, other more hidden costs and ghost fees imposed by large corporations contribute towards our cost of living concerns.
Thankfully, governments and regulatory bodies appear increasingly willing to crack down on corporate malfeasance. This is happening in large part because individuals are calling out unfair corporate practices and demanding action.
I have recently contacted our federal government and the Canadian Transport Agency to urge them to expand upon the Canada Air Passengers Protection Regulations. They can do this by initiating a thorough review of General Conditions of Carriage rules employed by airlines to shield themselves from reasonable complaints, and to monetize situations which, without such arbitrary conditions, could easily be resolved fairly and without punitive costs and fees.
It is up to each of us to take individual responsibility to point out unfair business practices whenever and wherever we encounter them. And we must put pressure on public officials and regulatory bodies to intervene and ban such practices, for the benefit of us all.
Peter Eriksen, Richmond, B.C.

Yves Rocher has landed at Shoppers Drug Mart and Pharmaprix stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/yves-rocher-has-landed-at-shoppers-drug-mart/docview/3127753285/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Yves+Rocher+has+landed+at+Shoppers+Drug+Mart+and+Pharmaprix+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We couldn't be more thrilled to share the news that Yves Rocher has announced its expansion into Shoppers Drug Mart and Pharmaprix stores across Canada. Beginning this fall, you can now shop and discover more products from the natural beauty brand at select Shoppers Drug Mart stores and online.
This collaboration, more than two years in the making, marks a true milestone for Yves Rocher as it will be the first time the brand offers its products outside of its own in-house distribution channels.
It's an exciting partnership that allows Yves Rocher to make its natural and sustainable beauty solutions more easily accessible while giving Shoppers Drug Mart customers a more convenient way to incorporate natural ingredients into their skincare-with the bonus of getting even more PC Optimum points.
The global leader in dermo-botanical beauty, Yves Rocher is the number one beauty brand in France. What sets Yves Rocher apart is its innovation as a pioneer in natural cosmetics. Founded in the small village of La Gacilly in Brittany in 1959, Yves Rocher manufactures its products in its own factories and with ingredients grown on 60 hectares of organically farmed fields.
Using the village's native plants as the basis for its formulas, each product begins with an essential botanical ingredient-a concept that revolutionized the cosmetics industry when it was introduced.
At participating Shoppers Drug Mart locations, you'll find a curated assortment of Yves Rocher's top skincare, body and hair products. New to Yves Rocher? You'll want to start with their best-selling Anti-Âge Global skincare collection, the most premium skincare range under the Yves Rocher umbrella.
Its powerful ingredients restore the skin's moisture barrier to combat all signs of aging, working deep below the surface to smooth, firm, and plump the skin to restore radiance and even the complexion. Its hero ingredient is Botanical Bud Nectar, selected for its ability to improve the skin's cohesion and recreate perfect harmony.
As a leader in bioscience innovation, Yves Rocher is always working on new ways to keep your skin in its best shape. Existing and new fans alike will want to check out its latest anti-wrinkle innovation, the Lift Pro-Collagène collection, which was 12 years in the making.
This groundbreaking line features six products designed to lift, smooth and target wrinkles through the powerful combination of natural hyaluronic acid and Ice Plant-which has the capacity to form millions of microvesicles filled with active molecules, retaining all its freshness, vitality and youth.
With a visible improvement in less than four weeks, the combination of natural hyaluronic acid, collagen and the Ice Plant strengthens the skin barrier and targets the appearance of wrinkles.
You'll find Yves Rocher's dermo-botanical beauty products and more at a Shoppers Drug Mart or Pharmaprix location near you, or online at shoppersdrugmart.ca.
This content was created by The Kit; Yves Rocher funded and approved it.

Document 214 of 500

Crombie's Liberals pledge income tax breaks that they say Doug Ford has failed to deliver

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/crombies-liberals-pledge-income-tax-breaks-that/docview/3127361707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%27s+Liberals+pledge+income+tax+breaks+that+they+say+Doug+Ford+has+failed+to+deliver&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals are promising a $2.8 billion tax cut plan ahead of an expected early provincial election, offering income tax breaks Premier Doug Ford has failed to deliver since pledging them in 2018.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Tuesday she would cut middle-class income taxes 22 per cent if elected premier in addition to axing the eight per cent provincial portion of the Harmonized Sales Tax on home heating fuels and hydro bills.
The measures would save the average family $1,150 a year in "permanent financial relief," she added, contrasting the breaks to the one-time $200 tax rebate cheques Ford's Progressive Conservative government will send to 15 million Ontarians in January to defray the high cost of living after years of high inflation, which is now abating.
"He told us he'd lower taxes but he never got it done," Crombie said in a statement Tuesday before making her announcement at a Scarborough restaurant.
"Just like Ford has failed to fix the deadly crisis in our health-care system, he has failed to cut your taxes," she added, referring to hospital overcrowding, a doctor shortage and the "hallway medicine" Ford also pledged to end in the 2018 campaign that brought him to power.
The premier's income tax cut never materialized as the province was engulfed in the COVID-19 pandemic, which sent Ontario's deficit soaring as the government was forced to dramatically increase spending on health.
But Ford has eliminated licence plate renewal fees, tolls on Highways 412 and 418 and temporarily cut gasoline taxes. His $200 rebate cheques, which opposition parties have dubbed a pre-election bribe, will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion this fiscal year as the province runs a deficit of $6.6 billion.
Crombie said her tax cut plan would cost $2.8 billion, but there is no clear accounting of where the money will come from.
The Liberals said in a statement it will be paid for by ending "shady backroom deals" that Ford has made with "wealthy friends and insiders over the last six years...at the expense of the real people of Ontario."
As examples, Crombie cited: the abrupt closure of the Ontario Science Centre and its planned move to Ontario Place, with a parking garage to be built; a deal with the European company Therme to build a spa on the west island of Ontario Place; a "sweetheart deal" to put ServiceOntario centres into Staples stores; $1 billion spent to put beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails into convenience stores in the last couple of months, a year ahead of schedule; and the government's $8.28 billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
Crombie promised a "comprehensive investigation" into those deals within 30 days of taking office if she is elected premier. The Liberals now have nine seats and hold a distant third place in the legislature behind the Official Opposition NDP. Ford's PCs have a solid lead in public opinion polls.
The premier has been touting his rebate cheques and taunting Crombie with them.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers...she wants to increase taxes," Ford said late last month.
Crombie's income tax break would reduce the tax rate on taxable income between $51,446 and $75,000 to 7.15 per cent from the current 9.15 per cent.
CAPTION: Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie (left) and Premier Doug Ford.

Subject: Tax rates; Tax cuts; Income taxes

Toronto, the city where fun goes to die

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-city-where-fun-goes-die/docview/3127361706/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%2C+the+city+where+fun+goes+to+die&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Removal of driftwood sculptures seems a misguided overstepThis artist built a whimsical collection of driftwood creatures along Toronto's waterfront. Then the city told him to take them down, Nov. 8
Seriously? Our city has the will, the time and the funds to remove Damian Agostini's quirky, fleeting beach-find sculptures from Humber Bay Shores Park?
Toronto really is the place where fun goes to die.
Sally Cobb, Toronto
Failed housing program a sad reflection of the times
A Toronto program is offering new condos to public housing residents, no down payment required. Why is it struggling to find takers? Nov. 8
The failed Alexandra Park program to entice subsidized renters to get into home ownership says it all.
One, if you remove from home ownership the opportunity to build wealth, renting is the best option.
Two, if you can't afford to rent without subsidy, you can't afford to buy a house (or once bought, maintain it).
Three, even large, well-resourced non-profit organizations like Habitat for Humanity can't afford to absorb the escalation of the price of housing so lesser non-profits haven't a chance.
Fay Martin, Minden, Ont.
Voting strategically is one way to approach elections
How Donald Trump won - again, Nov. 6
Reading much of the analysis of the recent U.S. election outcomes made me think through why I vote as I do.
My preference is to have different parties in power federally and provincially. Because, in my mind, there is a hope that one can balance the more nonsensical aspects of the other. I prefer the Liberals at the federal level, as they have less impact on my personal life.
Provincially, the PCs are more likely to line up with my sense of how far any politicians should interfere with my day-to-day living. Lastly I look to the local municipal council to do as much as they can, within a reasonable budget, to make living where I do as pleasant as possible for the majority.
I am usually most affected by the municipal decisions and least by the federal ones.
Keeping a balance fits my way of thinking, and has for eight decades.
R. Holloway, Oakville
Trump's election may cause buyer's remorse in four years
Trump weaponized 'fake news.' Now, we're all going to be living in his version of reality, Nov. 7
There is an old saying: "Be careful what you wish for, you may get it."
I think four years from now there will be many people asking themselves one simple question: "What were we thinking?"
Allan Mantel, Victoria Harbour, Ont.
Working class and rural voters were underestimated
Here's what we missed on Trump's path to victory, Nov. 8
Canada's commitment to "free trade" and "free trade agreements" does lower the cost of living, leaving people here better off than without free trade.
In the United States, Americans are proud of their world-beating economic power, military power, self-sufficiency, innovation and technological leadership.
To American working men, Latinos, Blacks and, apparently, a majority of U.S. voters, more jobs, better jobs, jobs kept in the U.S. is what they want.
Sounds good, right?
A key driver of Trump's victory surely is his manufacturing policy; his commitment to bring manufacturing jobs back to American soil.
Economists will, or should, admit that even though tariffs drive up prices and create inflation, protectionist tariffs will bring jobs back to the U.S. This expectation is in accord with Keynesian economic theory.
What was missed on Trump's path to victory? Evidently, it was underestimating the savvy of working class and rural voters. These voters expect tariffs will bring more manufacturing jobs with better pay to Americans.
It's a reasonable expectation, and it is what they want.
Jay Butler, Etobicoke
Is there a place for rule of law in Trump's world?
I learned the rule of law can also be summed up by the phrase, "No one is above the law."
But if president-elect Donald Trump is not bothered by the law, can we really maintain that the United States is still a state of law? Will the partial immunity granted by the conservative Supreme Court to the president-elect not dangerously weaken the "immune system" of the rule of law?
Today, president of the United States.
Tomorrow, monarch?
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
Bipartisan politics key to fixing T.O.'s housing crisisThe housing crisis is a threat to our communities. It's time for the government to take bold action
I am worried about the future because of the housing crisis in Toronto, as it does not appear to be getting any better.
This housing crisis is not new, but it has never been this severe before. Toronto ranks globally as one of the most liveable cities yet housing issues might jeopardize that position.
Lack of affordable housing is not just a financial issue, it is a matter of social equity and economic sustainability. High housing costs push essential workers and young professionals to the fringes of our city, threatening the diversity and vibrancy which define Toronto.
I think municipal authorities need to prioritize affordable housing alternatives such as accelerating development approvals, reforming and lowering municipal fees when buying a house, and transforming existing properties into low-cost living spaces. These procedures are critical in making Toronto accessible to all its citizens.
Toronto does not need superficial solutions to its housing crisis; it needs comprehensive, sustainable policies that address the root causes of affordability and availability.
We need a strong mayor or a premier who is willing to put aside their partisan agenda for the future of our community.
Ahmed Hassan, Brampton
Let's not let the Santa Claus parade fall by the wayside'We'll be here from dark to dark': Preparations for annual Santa Claus parade are underway, Nov. 8
I was dismayed to read about the financial woes of this venerable Toronto/GTA institution.
Thankfully, the parade will go ahead this year, as planned, on Nov. 24 thanks to the hard work of the army of volunteers. However, to think this might be the last year the parade can be mounted is inconceivable. The annual Santa Claus parade kicks off the holiday season and fuels the dreams of sugar plums that children experience at this time of year.
Somehow, Santa's parade must be saved in 2025 and beyond. Since the problem identified is the drying up of corporate donations and funding, why doesn't Galen Weston (and his fellow grocery magnates at the top three chains) spearhead this campaign?
With the obscene profits flowing into their pockets due to inflated grocery prices, they can afford to fund the parade in perpetuity. But they needn't do it alone! They can easily rally their corporate pals in other industries, such as communications (Edward Rogers, I am looking at you), and pharma to name a couple.
Step up to the plate, people, for the sake of the children.
Meanwhile, individually, we can all be charitable at this time of year, if we have the means. Let's commit to donating our $200 cheques, which Premier Doug Ford will send us in January, to our local food banks (or other worthy causes) and let's donate other amounts of our own to the Toronto Star Santa Claus Fund and other charities.
Pam Tomasevic, Mississauga
CAPTION: Damian Agostini had been putting up animal sculptures made out of driftwood and other materials he finds on the beach atHumber Bay Park Shores Park.

Bonnie Crombie's plans for a major tax cut + the ugly battle over a new Catholic school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombies-plans-major-tax-cut-ugly-battle/docview/3127361297/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, November 12 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Bonnie Crombie's plans for a major tax cut, parents battling over a new Catholic school location and the escalating impact of the climate crisis.
DON'T MISSBonnie Crombie's about to unveil a major tax plan
The Ontario Liberal leader will reveal her plan for a new tax cut today that includes lifting the provincial levy on home heating fuels, the Star has learned. "People need something lasting and not just a one-time handout," a senior Liberal campaign source told Rob Ferguson, taking aim at Doug Ford's $200 rebate cheques. Here's what this would mean for your wallet.
Looking to the polls: Crombie's "More For You" tax package proposal comes as Ford muses a spring election - at least a year ahead of the scheduled vote in June 2026.Meanwhile: Ford said his promised rebate cheques will hit the mailboxes of 15 million Ontarians early in the new year, at a cost of $3 billion to the provincial treasury. Crombie has accused Ford of using "taxpayers' money to buy their vote."The battle over a new Catholic school location is getting ugly
The repurposing of a former public school has divided two Etobicoke communities, sparking a debate coloured with undertones of racism and classism. Central Etobicoke parents want the now-empty Scarlett Heights Entrepreneurial Academy to become the new high school in the area, Isabel Teotonio reports. But south Etobicoke parents want it as the temporary location for Bishop Allen Academy as it undergoes construction. Here's what you need to know.
Go deeper: The Toronto Catholic District School Board is looking to send Bishop Allen students to the former Don Bosco Catholic Secondary School - a move some parents have petitioned against, citing crime statistics that others have called false and fear mongering.On the other side: Other parents want the newly-acquired TCDSB facility converted into a new high school to alleviate demand and fear central Etobicoke's plans would set the project back by years.Climate disaster is fuelling conflicts and displacing millions around the world
The climate crisis has increasingly become a driving force behind global displacement, fuelling conflicts and deepening the existing humanitarian crisis, Nicholas Keung reports. The first climate report from the UN Refugee Agency released today paints a grim picture of how extreme climate-related hazards are forcing millions from their homes - and how, if nothing is done, these hazards will wreak further havoc across global borders. Take a look at what's at stake.
More: By 2040, the number of countries facing extreme climate hazards is set to rise from three - Bangladesh, United Arab Emirates and Oman - to 65, the vast majority of which are currently hosting displaced populations.In the U.S.: Donald Trump's election victory spells grim news for the mitigation of the climate crisis, with the president-elect expected to dismantle American climate action and promote further oil, natural gas and coal development.WHAT ELSE

Document 217 of 500

Doug Ford takes aim at Mexico in order to protect Ontario's auto sector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-takes-aim-at-mexico-order-protect/docview/3127359983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+takes+aim+at+Mexico+in+order+to+protect+Ontario%27s+auto+sector&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is taking a run at the border - the Mexican border.
With the prospect of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump slapping hefty tariffs on Canadian products after he takes office in January, Ford is moving to insulate Ontario from American levies.
The premier, who has been advocating a "Buy Can-Am mindset" that would more closely tie Canada with the U.S., took aim at Mexico on Tuesday.
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S.," said Ford, pointing toward the upcoming USMCA renegotiations.
"If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them," he said at a campaign-style event in Oro-Medonte, 125 kilometres north of Toronto.
Ford's comments come amid mounting concern that Ontario will be collateral damage as the Trump administration imposes hefty tariffs on imports to protect American industry, triggering a trade war.
University of Calgary economics professor Trevor Tombe has warned a 10 per cent American tariff would result in the equivalent of an average Canadian's annual income dropping by $1,100 and cause a five per cent plunge in Canada's gross domestic product.
That's a nightmare scenario for Ford, who noted that Ontario is the economic "engine of Canada and 95 per cent all the jobs that were created just last month were here."
"Free trade needs to be fair," the premier said.
"Since signing on to the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, Mexico has allowed itself to become a backdoor for Chinese cars, auto parts and other products into Canadian and American markets, putting Canadian and American workers' livelihoods at risk while undermining our communities and doing enormous harm to our shared economic success," he said.
Ford was one of the Canadian leaders who convinced Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to match President Joe Biden's 100 per cent tariff on artificially cheap Chinese electric vehicles, which are subsidized by Beijing, in order to protect Ontario's burgeoning domestic EV industry.
The premier and the prime minister were also concerned that Canada might serve as a back door to import inexpensive Chinese EVs into the U.S. if matching tariffs weren't introduced.
"If Mexico won't fight transshipment by, at the very least, matching Canadian and American tariffs on Chinese imports, they shouldn't have a seat at the table or enjoy access to the largest economy in the world," said Ford.
"Instead, we must prioritize the closest economic partnership on Earth by directly negotiating a bilateral U.S.-Canada free trade agreement that puts U.S. and Canadian workers first."
Asked about Ford's comments during a news conference in Fredericton with newly elected New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt, Trudeau said China's trade policies are a concern among most of Canada's allies .
"All of our partner democracies around the world have expressed varying degrees of concern about China's overcapacity and around unfair trade practices that the Chinese economy is inflicting on the world," he said, adding it is an issue he intends to raise with Trump.
"We're going to continue to work with partners like the United States and, hopefully Mexico as well, to make sure that we are united in our desire to protect good jobs," he said.
The office of Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland - a friend of Ford who conferred with the premier as the U.S. election results poured in on Nov. 5 - underscored that.
"Canada firmly believes that action is necessary to level the playing field for Canadian workers in the face of China's intentional, state-directed policy of overcapacity," said spokesperson Katherine Cuplinskas.
"That is why we have put strong tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles, steel and aluminum. Canada will not be a back door to other markets, especially to our most important friend and ally, the United States."
Canadian officials are bracing for the prospect of a second Trump presidency that would be even more protectionist than when he was in office between 2017 and 2021.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who works closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on trade issues, said Ford "is hitting a note that's been making the rounds in Washington for some time."
"The Chinese are relentless in their strategies to access the North American market and all partners have a share in the responsibility to protect it," said Volpe, noting the domestic industry is integrated with Mexico's automotive sector.
"Canadian suppliers are heavily invested in Mexico to serve carmakers where they build cars," he said in an interview Tuesday.
"We've been working closely with Mexican officials for sometime now as local investors on navigating this issue to our collective benefit."
With files from Ryan Tumilty
CAPTION: In a statement, Premier Doug Ford said free trade needs to be fair.

Document 218 of 500

Doug Ford wants a Toronto designed for cars. His own housing plans reveal why that's a mistake

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-toronto-designed-cars-his-own/docview/3126872893/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+a+Toronto+designed+for+cars.+His+own+housing+plans+reveal+why+that%27s+a+mistake&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Mayor Olivia Chow and members of Toronto council get together for their monthly meeting Wednesday, much of the attention will be on the mayor's Ford-fighting strategy.
Chow has held a spot on the agenda for discussion of how city hall should push back against Bill 212. That's the legislation put forward by Premier Doug Ford's government that would, if passed, restrict council's power to approve bike lanes. It will also enable Queen's Park to remove lanes already installed on routes like Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue. Regardless of what you think about bike lanes, it's an overreach onto city hall's turf - it needs to defend itself.
So the strategy discussion will be important. But it is truly ridiculous that it's even necessary. Because there are 31 other items on council's agenda that highlight just how absurd Ford's anti-bike lane push really is.
By my count, with these 31 items - a mishmash of various planning reports asking for approval to approve or continue negotiations on various developments - council is set to vote on 15,590 new housing units. Most of them condos. And collectively, those new units will come with just 6,071 new parking spaces.
The math is clear: the vast majority of new homes voted on by council this week won't have any parking at all. Zip. Zilch. Zero.
The numbers are starker if you think in terms of people, not units. A 2021 city planning report says about 1.97 people live in the average condo or apartment unit, but let's assume these new units - which tend to be pretty darn small - will average a bit lower, at 1.75. That still means these units will house about 27,283 people.
And only about 22 per cent of them will have a space to park a car.
I was inspired to add up these numbers after reading a column the writer and advocate Dylan Reid published last month for Spacing arguing that Ford's bike lane legislation isn't just about transportation - it's also very much about housing.
Consider the context. In 2021, council abolished its previous planning policy that required developers to include a minimum amount of parking in each new development. The motivation was twofold. First, underground parking spaces are enormously expensive - up to $165,000 per space, according to a 2019 estimate - and only add to the cost of already costly housing in the city.
Second, there's no conceivable future where Toronto can be a functional city where new residents drive as their primary means of transportation. Toronto can handle a lot more people. It can't handle a lot more cars. (To that end, council in some areas replaced parking minimums with parking maximums - preventing some developers from building parking spaces even if they want to.)
And so, motivated in part by Ford's housing targets, Toronto is approving thousands of new units but not too many new parking spaces. To further make it clear they expect new residents to live car-free, Toronto city hall also routinely approves bylaws making residents of new buildings ineligible for on-street parking permits.
But this isn't just a Toronto strategy. Getting rid of parking requirements in new buildings has been a focus for Ford's government too. In 2022, Ford's Housing Task Force recommended eliminating parking requirements for any development on a street with transit service, including bus service. Ford's latest housing bill, passed this spring, took a step toward that and blocked municipalities from requiring parking in developments near transit stations.
This isn't just a downtown thing, either. Near Bloor and Kipling in the premier's beloved Etobicoke, city hall's real estate agency CreateTO is planning for just 199 parking spaces in a 710-unit complex, enough for less than 30 per cent of units to come with a space.
Private developers in the area are getting even more aggressive. A plan for Weston Village up for debate at council this week boasts 556 units and only 115 spaces. A Dundas West project near Highway 427 has 600 units but only 109 parking spots. And a recently updated plan for a site near the Long Branch GO Station now includes 549 units, but only 72 parking spaces.
Yes, these new residents will be close to transit stations and that will help with car-free living. But transit can't just be the only answer for getting around. Service won't always be available when you need it. Disruptions happen. Destinations won't always line up with available routes. Some trips are always better handled by active transportation, like walking or cycling.
Municipal and provincial housing policy has understood and embraced the fact that cars aren't and won't ever again be king. It's been codified in bylaws and legislation. Ford, though, seems to believe Toronto should be designed for cars even as its population of new residents won't have cars. He's trying to prevent a future that's already under construction.
CAPTION: Legislation put forward by Premier Doug Ford's government restricts Toronto council's power to approve bike lanes, and allows the removal of bike lanes already in place.

Subject: Automobiles; Legislation; Councils; Planning; Condominiums

Title: Doug Ford wants a Toronto designed for cars. His own housing plans reveal why that's a mistake

Document 219 of 500

There's one lesson the Trudeau Liberals must learn from Trump's victory

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-one-lesson-trudeau-liberals-must-learn/docview/3126872711/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+one+lesson+the+Trudeau+Liberals+must+learn+from+Trump%27s+victory&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Standing on the lawn of Howard University in Washington, D.C on Wednesday, listening to Vice President Kamala Harris deliver her concession speech, I tuned my ears for a note of introspection or contrition.
These speeches are rarely a time for self-flagellation. But I was taken aback when Harris began congratulating herself on her biggest failure: "We have been intentional," she said, "about building community and building coalitions."
In fact, the Democrats spent election night watching their rainbow coalition - of union members, immigrants, young people - fall apart. Many of those voters, particularly men, defected to President-elect Donald Trump. They opted for the former president despite, well, everything.
Here in Canada, a similar political reorientation is in process. And in the wreckage of Harris' campaign, there are some critical lessons for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and his one-time governing buddy NDP leader Jagmeet Singh - which they don't have much time to learn.
The post-mortem of Harris's stunning loss is still ongoing, but it's incontrovertible that Trump is returning to the White House because he won over the working class, particularly men. Trump mimicked the anger of working people, offered them a return to optimism, and reached them on the channels they listen to. He campaigned on memorable, impactful economic policies, like ending taxation on tips and overtime. He shot for the stars, literally, in promising to help Elon Musk get to Mars. He vowed to end foreign wars and bring manufacturing back to America. He endorsed Bitcoin, mocked wokeism, and became a fixture in male-dominated online communities of comedians, sports fans, and gamers.
Listeners of the Joe Rogan Experience, the biggest podcast in the world, had the ex-president in their ears for three hours shortly before the election. (Plus another three hours with his vice-presidential candidate J.D. Vance.)
Trump's policies, almost unanimously, were unserious, cruel, or economic quackery. His rhetoric was steeped in egregious lies and represents a genuine danger to democracy. Many, myself included, thought voters would see Trump for the charlatan that he is. But the former "The Apprentice" star won over working class voters by selling the message that he, a consummate outsider, was the only one equipped to break a rigged system.
The president-elect stalked around the country, savaging the institutions which inspire distrust, fairly or not. People are angry at rising homelessness, failing healthcare, the opioid crisis, poor infrastructure, declining state services, a strained education system, and much more. At a time when technological innovation is widening our imagination of what is possible, this kind of decay feels inexcusable.
In trying to paint Trump as a danger to democracy, the Democrats twisted themselves in knots to become guardians of the status quo. They avoided, even shut down, difficult conversations on pressing social issues because they are fearful of how bigots might weaponize those discussions. They avoided trying to break into, or compete with, the toxic information environment in which Trump lives. Harris spent the final days of the campaign with Liz Cheney because it signified a return to bipartisanship - and refused to consider that it looked like she was palling around with the daughter of a war criminal.
Harris spent the campaign bombarding voters with tightly-messaged scripts about why they should vote against Trump. By election day, Americans had few good reasons to vote for the vice president. And yet it's this strategy that Trudeau looks set to replicate.
Trudeau's party has long looked to the Democrats for inspiration, and that's become particularly true as his numbers have gone from bad to worse.
A recent Liberal Party ad directly lifted Harris' "we won't go back" slogan. Not long after Harris called Trump a "fascist," Liberal MP Adam van Koeverden claimed that Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre "poses an existential threat to Canada's democracy." They even took to calling the Conservatives "weird." They gave up on trying to win, and now hope to make the Conservatives lose.
The Liberals even told themselves a Republican victory - because of the pall it would cast on the Conservatives - might be bad for Canada, but good for the Liberal Party.
This is all delusional. Harris' loss should be a wake-up call for Trudeau: He needs to actually win people over again.
This doesn't mean that Trudeau ought to start hawking Bitcoin or denigrating transgender people. But it does mean that he needs to figure out how to convince working Canadians that his party is still capable of getting stuff done, instead of merely managing decline.
There are positive lessons to be learned from the United States, but they do not come from Kamala Harris. Look, instead, to Senator Bernie Sanders, a progressive populist; and Transport Secretary Pete Buttigieg, an excellent salesman for the Biden administration's industrial policies. Sanders' 2019 sit-down with Joe Rogan was so impactful that he won the podcaster's endorsement. (Rogan's endorsement went to Trump this year.) Buttigieg, meanwhile, participated in some remarkably watchable, informative social media sit-downs with undecided, sometimes hostile, voters.
Trudeau is neither innovating new policy nor reaching out to unlikely places. Their most significant accomplishments of late, national pharmacare and dentalcare plans, won't cover most working people and come as medicare plunges further into crisis.
The Liberals' lack of vision or ambition ought to leave an open lane for the NDP to offer a bold, uncompromising, and ambitious agenda for change. Yet they are the architects of the dental and pharmacare plans, and now seem bereft of other ideas. Despite their anti-billionaire rhetoric, Jagmeet Singh's most ambitious policy is to implement price controls for groceries (also a riff on a Harris policy.) Voters are, understandably, disinterested.
Singh and Trudeau and stuck in their scripts, damned by the anxieties, purity, and dourness of their parties. The only tactic left at their disposal is to compare Poilievre to Trump, unconvincingly and to the frustration of the Conservatives' new-found working-class base.
Liberals and progressives need to build actual coalitions, not just micro-target voters' anxieties. They need credible, optimistic, plans, and a willingness to market them in unfriendly spaces. If they can't or won't do that, they should concede now.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump, left, and Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talk prior to a NATO meeting in England in 2019.

Subject: Digital currencies; Political campaigns; Working class; Presidential elections; Democracy; Voters; Endorsements; Prime ministers; Wage & price controls

Business indexing term: Subject: Digital currencies Working class Wage & price controls

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala; Trump, Donald J; Singh, Jagmeet; Trudeau, Justin; Rogan, Joe

Title: There's one lesson the Trud eau Liberals must learn from Trump's victory

Then the city told him to take them down, Nov. 8 A Toronto program is offering new condos to public housing residents, no down payment required. Why is it struggling to find takers? Nov. 8 How Donald Trump won - again, Nov. 6 Trump weaponized 'fake news.' https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3126871518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+This+artist+built+a+whimsical+collection+of+driftwood+creatures+along+Toronto%27s+waterfront.+Then+the+city+told+him+to+take+them+down%2C+Nov.+8+A+Toronto+program+is+offering+new+condos+to+public+housing+residents%2C+no+down+payment+required.+Why+is+it+struggling+to+find+takers%3F+Nov.+8+How+Donald+Trump+won+-+again%2C+Nov.+6+Trump+weaponized+%27fake+news.%27+Now%2C+we%27re+all+going+to+be+living+in+his+version+of+reality%2C+Nov.+7+Here%27s+what+we+missed+on+Trump%27s+path+to+victory%2C+Nov.+8+%27We%27ll+be+here+from+dark+to+dark%27%3A+Preparations+for+annual+Santa+Claus+parade+are+underway%2C+Nov.+8&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A misguided overstep by city
This artist built a whimsical collection of driftwood creatures along Toronto's waterfront. Then the city told him to take them down, Nov. 8
Seriously? Our city has the will, the time and the funds to remove Damian Agostini's quirky, fleeting beach-find sculptures from Humber Bay Shores Park? Toronto really is the place where fun goes to die.
Sally Cobb, Toronto
A sad reflection of the times
A Toronto program is offering new condos to public housing residents, no down payment required. Why is it struggling to find takers? Nov. 8
The failed Alexandra Park program to entice subsidized renters to get into home ownership says it all. One, if you remove from home ownership the opportunity to build wealth, renting is the best option. Two, if you can't afford to rent without subsidy, you can't afford to buy a house (or once bought, maintain it). Three, even large, well-resourced non-profit organizations like Habitat for Humanity can't afford to absorb the escalation of the price of housing so lesser non-profits haven't a chance.
Fay Martin, Minden, Ont.
Strategic voting
is one approach
How Donald Trump won - again, Nov. 6
Reading much of the analysis of the recent U.S. election outcomes made me think through why I vote as I do.
My preference is to have different parties in power federally and provincially. Because, in my mind, there is a hope that one can balance the more nonsensical aspects of the other. I prefer the Liberals at the federal level, as they have less impact on my personal life.
Provincially, the PCs are more likely to line up with my sense of how far any politicians should interfere with my day-to-day living. Lastly I look to the local municipal council to do as much as they can, within a reasonable budget, to make living where I do as pleasant as possible for the majority.
I am usually most affected by the municipal decisions and least by the federal ones.
Keeping a balance fits my way of thinking, and has for eight decades.
R. Holloway, Oakville
Trump win may lead to regret
Trump weaponized 'fake news.' Now, we're all going to be living in his version of reality, Nov. 7
There is an old saying: "Be careful what you wish for, you may get it." I think four years from now there will be many people asking themselves one question: "What were we thinking?"
Allan Mantel, Victoria Harbour, Ont.
Some voters were underestimated
Here's what we missed on Trump's path to victory, Nov. 8
Canada's commitment to "free trade" and "free trade agreements" does lower the cost of living, leaving people here better off than without free trade.
To American working men, Latinos, Blacks and, apparently, a majority of U.S. voters, more jobs, better jobs, jobs kept in the U.S. is what they want. Sounds good, right?
A key driver of Trump's victory surely is his commitment to bring manufacturing jobs back to American soil.
Economists will, or should, admit that even though tariffs drive up prices and create inflation, protectionist tariffs will bring jobs back to the U.S. This expectation is in accord with Keynesian economic theory.
What was missed on Trump's path to victory? Evidently, it was underestimating the savvy of working class and rural voters. These voters expect tariffs will bring more manufacturing jobs with better pay to Americans. It's a reasonable expectation, and it is what they want.
Jay Butler, Etobicoke
Is Trump's U.S.
still a state of law?
I learned the rule of law can also be summed up by the phrase, "No one is above the law."
But if U.S. president-elect Donald Trump is not bothered by the law, can we really maintain that the United States is still a state of law? Will the partial immunity granted by the conservative Supreme Court to the president-elect not dangerously weaken the "immune system" of the rule of law?
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
Bipartisanship can help fix crisis
This housing crisis is not new, but it has never been this severe before. Toronto ranks globally as one of the most liveable cities yet housing issues might jeopardize that position.
Lack of affordable housing is not just a financial issue, it is a matter of social equity and economic sustainability. High housing costs push essential workers and young professionals to the fringes of our city, threatening the diversity and vibrancy which define Toronto.
I think municipal authorities need to prioritize affordable housing alternatives such as accelerating development approvals, reforming and lowering municipal fees when buying a house, and transforming existing properties into low-cost living spaces.
Toronto needs comprehensive, sustainable policies that address the root causes of affordability and availability.
We need a strong mayor or a premier who is willing to put aside their partisan agenda for the future of our community.
Ahmed Hassan, Brampton
Don't let the Santa parade fall by the wayside
'We'll be here from dark to dark': Preparations for annual Santa Claus parade are underway, Nov. 8
I was dismayed to read about the financial woes of this venerable Toronto/GTA institution.
Thankfully, the parade will go ahead this year, as planned, on Nov. 24 thanks to the hard work of the army of volunteers. However, to think this might be the last year the parade can be mounted is inconceivable. The annual Santa Claus parade kicks off the holiday season and fuels the dreams of sugar plums that children experience at this time of year.
Somehow, Santa's parade must be saved in 2025 and beyond. Since the problem identified is the drying up of corporate donations and funding, why doesn't Galen Weston (and his fellow grocery magnates) spearhead this campaign? But they needn't do it alone! They can easily rally their corporate pals in other industries, such as communications (Edward Rogers, I am looking at you), and pharma to name a couple.
Meanwhile, individually, we can all be charitable at this time of year, if we have the means. Let's commit to donating our $200 cheques, which Premier Doug Ford will send us in January, to our local food banks (or other worthy causes) and let's donate other amounts of our own to the Toronto Star Santa Claus Fund and other charities.
Pam Tomasevic, Mississauga

Document 221 of 500

Why is it so expensive to see Taylor Swift?: Some resale tickets selling for 20 times the presale price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-is-so-expensive-see-taylor-swift/docview/3126871426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+it+so+expensive+to+see+Taylor+Swift%3F%3A+Some+resale+tickets+selling+for+20+times+the+presale+price&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before Taylor Swift tickets went on presale, Maha Ansari asked about 10 family members and friends to sign up as verified fans on her behalf to help secure tickets. A year later, she is still on the wait-list.
While checking secondary ticketing platforms and Facebook groups every day, Ansari, a Toronto lawyer who lives minutes away from Rogers Centre, was put off by the skyrocketing prices of resale tickets.
For Swift's first show in Toronto on Thursday, tickets for seats in the upper bowl were offered for at least $4,000 on StubHub, more than 20 times the presale price.
"I still don't think I can justify spending $4,000 on one night of my life to see a concert," said Ansari.
With a budget of $2,000, her hopes grew dim as the concert date drew closer. Ansari said she and many fans desperate to see the star continued to receive scam messages on social media.
"As a Swiftie, I am heartbroken ... As a lawyer, I'm disappointed in the Canadian government, Ontario's government, their failure to really regulate the ticket resale and the ticket pricing market," said Ansari.
Ontario's previous Liberal government introduced legislation in 2018 to outlaw computer "bots" that scoop up huge blocks of tickets and cap resale markups at 50 per cent above face value. However, the resale markup cap was scrapped after Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives came into office.
Ansari said she was disheartened that not much progress had been made on the legal front since the cap was lifted. On the other hand, she saw Swifties flying overseas to Europe to see the superstar perform as they found the resale tickets there less costly and there is legislation against for-profit resale.
Is the removal of the resale cap to blame for the high resale prices?
Supply and demand explain the exorbitant price of the resale tickets in the secondary market, said Catherine Moore, a music professor at the University of Toronto.
What's different from previous shows, Moore added, is that Toronto and Vancouver dates are the last two shows in The Eras Tour.
Kelly Cronin from New Hampshire is one of the American fans who want to secure a Toronto ticket. She saw Swift perform in Los Angeles last year by buying a last-minute resale ticket at $850 (U.S.).
"The appeal to go again is that she changed her set list," said Cronin.
Cronin booked the hotel and flight to Toronto but hasn't found a ticket that fit her $2,500 budget.
While many fans are in favour of a cap on the price of resale tickets, researchers warned that in practice, people can always find ways to get around the legislation.
When overpriced tickets can't be sold on resale platforms, transactions can easily flow into a grey market where ticket sellers charge extra, and people agree to higher prices, said Pascal Courty, an economist at the University of Victoria.
In a statement, Ticketmaster said it believes capping concert resale prices is the only way to remove the incentive, adding that the company supports further regulations.
The government "requires those who facilitate the resale of a ticket for more than its face value to provide the purchaser with either a confirmation from the primary seller that the ticket is valid or a money-back guarantee," said Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement spokesperson Matthew D'Amico.
For-profit ticket resale is banned in some European countries.
While for-profit ticket resales are not banned in Germany, event promoters can include a clause in the ticket's terms and conditions prohibiting resale above 25 per cent of its face value. These tickets must be personalized with a box on the ticket for the buyer's name.
When the Tories reviewed the Ticket Sales Act in 2019, they kept the bot prohibition intact and increased fines for non-compliance with the act from $10,000 (Canadian) to $25,000, but it's hard to tell how effective the ban has been.
Hours after tickets to Swift's Toronto show went on sale on Ticketmaster in 2023, tickets appeared on resale site, with prices ranging from thousands to hundreds of thousands of dollars.
It's not clear if any of this activity is inappropriate, but enforcing the law requires co-operation from ticket platforms, said Courty.
Ticketmaster required a presale code to purchase tickets through the Ticketmaster Verified program, which is aimed at cracking down on resellers. Following a surge in reported scams, the platform implemented a new rule that ticket transfers for Swift's concerts can only begin 72 hours before the event.
One approach China has taken to eliminate for-profit ticket resale that could also apply in Canada is to tie tickets to ID for major, rare events like the Eras Tour, which have excess demand, Courty said.
"The best way to do that is to have limited tickets with one ticket, one name, one admission, and you check identity at the gate," he said, thus completely prohibiting the resale and exchange of tickets while still allowing people to get refunds.
Cyrenne agrees with the approach, saying it would take a lot of manpower and resources to police the rule, and he was unsure if concert promoters would have the incentive to go through the hassle.
CAPTION: Some Toronto Eras Tour resale tickets are going for at least $4,000.Scott A Garfittinvision/the associated press file photo
CREDIT: Estella Ren Toronto Star

Document 222 of 500

Lt. Gen. Rohmer gives Ford an order

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lt-gen-rohmer-gives-ford-order/docview/3126871384/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lt.+Gen.+Rohmer+gives+Ford+an+order&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The general has given an order.
Lt. Gen. Richard Rohmer, Canada's most decorated citizen, wants Premier Doug Ford to engrave the name of the Ontario Veterans' Memorial on the north side of the black granite monument at Queen's Park.
Rohmer, 100, who chaired the committee that got the 30-metre memorial built in 2006 under premier Dalton McGuinty, delivered some instructions to Ford at Monday's Remembrance Day ceremony.
"I want to say to the premier there's one other thing I want him to do," said the retired general, whose honours and medals include the highest awards of Canada, France and Belgium, among others.
"There's one final task that I tried to get done to this wall. On the north side of the wall, it is a blank black, black, black, black," he said from the dais in front of the wall he championed. "And I tried fruitlessly - I don't like the word fruitlessly - but I tried without result in getting the name of this wall put on the north side of the wall so that the people coming here to pay respect ... to this monument ... will know what this wall is."
Turning to Ford, who was seated nearby, Rohmer said: "A few words, letters ... premier, on that side would be helpful. At the moment, it's just a blank wall."
Since its opening 18 years ago, the monument - designed by Charlottetown-born artist Allan Harding MacKay and Vancouver-based landscape architects Phillips Farevaag Smallenberg after a national competition - has become a major landmark at Queen's Park.
With a glimmer in his eye, the centenarian - who has advised premiers dating back John Robarts in the 1960s - reminded the gathered crowd of several hundred people in front of the Ontario legislature of Ford's campaign mantra.
"The premier is the kind of man who likes to 'get things done.' I'm giving him another good suggestion."
Ford's office said later Monday that it is looking into the general's request.
Lest anyone forget Rohmer's powers of persuasion, the P-51 Mustang fighter plane he flew on D-Day in 1944 is one of the engraved scenes of war depicted on the veterans memorial.
At Ford's behest in 2020, a new monument was added to complement the memorial to commemorate the 158 Canadian Armed Forces personnel killed in the war in Afghanistan.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 223 of 500

Status quo defence a failing strategy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/status-quo-defence-failing-strategy/docview/3126871326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Status+quo+defence+a+failing+strategy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Standing on the lawn of Howard University in Washington on Wednesday, listening to Vice-President Kamala Harris deliver her concession speech, I tuned my ears for a note of introspection or contrition.
These speeches are rarely a time for self-flagellation. But I was taken aback when Harris began congratulating herself on her biggest failure: "We have been intentional," she said, "about building community and building coalitions."
In fact, the Democrats spent election night watching their rainbow coalition - of union members, immigrants, young people - fall apart. Many of those voters, particularly men, defected to president-elect Donald Trump. They opted for the former president despite, well, everything.
Here in Canada, a similar political reorientation is in process. And in the wreckage of Harris' campaign, there are some critical lessons for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and his one-time governing buddy NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh - which they don't have much time to learn.
The post-mortem of Harris' stunning loss is still ongoing, but it's incontrovertible that Trump is returning to the White House because he won over the working class, particularly men. Trump mimicked the anger of working people, offered them a return to optimism, and reached them on the channels they listen to. He campaigned on memorable, impactful economic policies, like ending taxation on tips and overtime. He shot for the stars, literally, in promising to help Elon Musk get to Mars. He vowed to end foreign wars and bring manufacturing back to America. He endorsed bitcoin, mocked wokeism, and became a fixture in male-dominated online communities of comedians, sports fans, and gamers.
Listeners of "the Joe Rogan Experience," the biggest podcast in the world, had the ex-president in their ears for three hours shortly before the election. (Plus another three hours with his vice-presidential candidate J.D. Vance.)
Trump's policies, almost unanimously, were unserious, cruel, or economic quackery. His rhetoric was steeped in egregious lies and represents a genuine danger to democracy. Many, myself included, thought voters would see Trump for the charlatan that he is. But the former "The Apprentice" star won over working class voters by selling the message that he, a consummate outsider, was the only one equipped to break a rigged system.
The president-elect stalked around the country, savaging the institutions which inspire distrust, fairly or not. People are angry at rising homelessness, failing health care, the opioid crisis, poor infrastructure, declining state services, a strained education system, and much more. At a time when technological innovation is widening our imagination of what is possible, this kind of decay feels inexcusable.
In trying to paint Trump as a danger to democracy, the Democrats twisted themselves in knots to become guardians of the status quo. They avoided, even shut down, difficult conversations on pressing social issues because they are fearful of how bigots might weaponize those discussions. They avoided trying to break into, or compete with, the toxic information environment in which Trump lives. Harris spent the final days of the campaign with Liz Cheney because it signified a return to bipartisanship - and refused to consider that it looked like she was palling around with the daughter of a war criminal.
Harris spent the campaign bombarding voters with tightly messaged scripts about why they should vote against Trump. By election day, Americans had few good reasons to vote for the vice-president. And yet it's this strategy that Trudeau looks set to replicate.
Trudeau's party has long looked to the Democrats for inspiration, and that's become particularly true as his numbers have gone from bad to worse.
A recent Liberal Party ad directly lifted Harris' "we won't go back" slogan. Not long after Harris called Trump a "fascist," Liberal MP Adam van Koeverden claimed that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre "poses an existential threat to Canada's democracy." They even took to calling the Conservatives "weird." They gave up on trying to win, and now hope to make the Conservatives lose.
The Liberals even told themselves a Republican victory - because of the pall it would cast on the Conservatives - might be bad for Canada, but good for the Liberal Party.
This is all delusional. Harris' loss should be a wake-up call for Trudeau: he needs to actually win people over again.
This doesn't mean that Trudeau ought to start hawking bitcoin or denigrating transgender people. But it does mean that he needs to figure out how to convince working Canadians that his party is still capable of getting stuff done, instead of merely managing decline.
There are positive lessons to be learned from the United States, but they do not come from Kamala Harris.
Look, instead, to Sen. Bernie Sanders, a progressive populist; and Transport Secretary Pete Buttigieg, an excellent salesman for the Biden administration's industrial policies. Sanders' 2019 sit-down with Joe Rogan was so impactful that he won the podcaster's endorsement (Rogan's endorsement went to Trump this year). Buttigieg, meanwhile, participated in some remarkably watchable, informative social media sit-downs with undecided, sometimes hostile, voters.
Trudeau is neither innovating new policy nor reaching out to unlikely places. Their most significant accomplishments of late, national pharmacare and dental-care plans, won't cover most working people and come as medicare plunges further into crisis.
The Liberals' lack of vision or ambition ought to leave an open lane for the NDP to offer a bold, uncompromising, and ambitious agenda for change. Yet they are the architects of the dental and pharmacare plans, and now seem bereft of other ideas. Despite their anti-billionaire rhetoric, Jagmeet Singh's most ambitious policy is to implement price controls for groceries (also a riff on a Harris policy.) Voters are, understandably, disinterested.
Singh and Trudeau and stuck in their scripts, damned by the anxieties, purity, and dourness of their parties. The only tactic left at their disposal is to compare Poilievre to Trump, unconvincingly and to the frustration of the Conservatives' new-found working-class base.
Liberals and progressives need to build actual coalitions, not just micro-target voters' anxieties. They need credible, optimistic, plans, and a willingness to market them in unfriendly spaces. If they can't or won't do that, they should concede now.
Justin Ling is an independent investigative journalist based in Montreal and a contributing columnist for the Star. Reach via email: justinling@proton.me
CAPTION: Justin Trudeau's Liberals told themselves a Republican victory might be bad for Canada, but good for their party. This isall delusional, Justin Ling writes. Donald Trump's victory should be a wake-up call for Trudeau: He needs to actually win people over again.Frank Augstein The Associated Press file photo

Subject: Digital currencies; Political campaigns; Working class; Presidential elections; Democracy; Voters; Endorsements; Wage & price controls
