Document 224 of 500

Ford wants the car-centric city of yesterday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-wants-car-centric-city-yesterday/docview/3126871301/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+wants+the+car-centric+city+of+yesterday&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Mayor Olivia Chow and members of Toronto council get together for their monthly meeting Wednesday, much of the attention will be on the mayor's Ford-fighting strategy.
Chow has held a spot on the agenda for discussion of how city hall should push back against Bill 212. That's the legislation put forward by Premier Doug Ford's government that would, if passed, restrict council's power to approve bike lanes. It will also enable Queen's Park to remove lanes already installed on routes like Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue. Regardless of what you think about bike lanes, it's an overreach onto city hall's turf - it needs to defend itself. So the strategy discussion will be important. But it is truly ridiculous that it's even necessary. Because there are 31 other items on council's agenda that highlight just how absurd Ford's anti-bike lane push really is.
By my count, with these 31 items - a mishmash of various planning reports asking for approval to approve or continue negotiations on various developments - council is set to vote on 15,590 new housing units. Most of them condos. And collectively, those new units will come with just 6,071 new parking spaces.
The math is clear: The vast majority of new homes voted on by council this week won't have any parking at all. Zip. Zilch. Zero.
The numbers are starker if you think in terms of people, not units. A 2021 city planning report says about 1.97 people live in the average condo or apartment unit, but let's assume these new units - which tend to be pretty darn small - will average a bit lower, at 1.75. That still means these units will house about 27,283 people.
And only about 22 per cent of them will have a space to park a car.
I was inspired to add up these numbers after reading a column the writer and advocate Dylan Reid published last month for Spacing arguing that Ford's bike lane legislation isn't just about transportation - it's also very much about housing.
Consider the context. In 2021, council abolished its previous planning policy that required developers to include a minimum amount of parking in each new development. The motivation was twofold. First, underground parking spaces are enormously expensive - up to $165,000 per space, according to a 2019 estimate - and only add to the cost of already costly housing in the city.
Second, there's no conceivable future where Toronto can be a functional city where new residents drive as their primary means of transportation. Toronto can handle a lot more people. It can't handle a lot more cars. (To that end, council in some areas replaced parking minimums with parking maximums - preventing some developers from building parking spaces even if they want to.)
And so, motivated in part by Ford's housing targets, Toronto is approving thousands of new units but not too many new parking spaces. To further make it clear they expect new residents to live car-free, Toronto city hall also routinely approves bylaws making residents of new buildings ineligible for on-street parking permits.
But this isn't just a Toronto strategy. Getting rid of parking requirements in new buildings has been a focus for Ford's government too. In 2022, Ford's Housing Task Force recommended eliminating parking requirements for any development on a street with transit service, including bus service. Ford's latest housing bill, passed this spring, took a step toward that and blocked municipalities from requiring parking in developments near transit stations.
This isn't just a downtown thing, either. Near Bloor and Kipling in the premier's beloved Etobicoke, city hall's real estate agency CreateTO is planning for just 199 parking spaces in a 710-unit complex, enough for less than 30 per cent of units to come with a space.
Private developers in the area are getting even more aggressive. A plan for Weston Village up for debate at council this week boasts 556 units and only 115 spaces. A Dundas West project near Highway 427 has 600 units but only 109 parking spots. And a recently updated plan for a site near the Long Branch GO Station now includes 549 units, but only 72 parking spaces.
Yes, these new residents will be close to transit stations and that will help with car-free living. But transit can't just be the only answer for getting around. Service won't always be available when you need it. Disruptions happen. Destinations won't always line up with available routes. Some trips are always better handled by active transportation, like walking or cycling.
Municipal and provincial housing policy has understood and embraced the fact that cars aren't and won't ever again be king. It's been codified in bylaws and legislation. Ford, though, seems to believe Toronto should be designed for cars even as its population of new residents won't have cars. He's trying to prevent a future that's already under construction.
CAPTION: Municipal and provincial housing policy has understood and embraced the fact that cars aren't and won't ever again be king. Doug Ford, though, seems to believe Toronto should be designed for cars even as its population of new residents won't have them, Matt Elliott writes.Michelle Mengsu Chang Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Document 225 of 500

Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will pledge tax cut on home heating fuels

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-liberal-leader-bonnie-crombie-will-pledge/docview/3126871277/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Liberal+Leader+Bonnie+Crombie+will+pledge+tax+cut+on+home+heating+fuels&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will unveil a major tax cut plan that includes lifting the provincial levy on home heating fuels, the Star has learned.
Taking aim at Premier Doug Ford's $200 tax rebate cheque scheme, a senior Liberal campaign source said "people need something lasting and not just a one-time handout."
The Liberals' "More For You" tax package proposal will be released Tuesday as Ford is considering an early election next spring instead of waiting until the scheduled vote in June 2026.
"She's going to deliver tax cuts that Doug Ford promised but didn't deliver and that the New Democrats and Greens would never touch," added the source, speaking confidentially Monday in order to discuss the plan before its release.
While Ford pledged a middle-class income tax cut in the 2018 campaign that brought him to power, he has not followed through.
However, his Progressive Conservative government has eliminated licence plate renewal fees, tolls on Highways 412 and 418, and has a temporary cut on gasoline taxes.
"The whole plan will cost less than Doug Ford's cheques but bring ... more relief on an annual basis," said the Liberal source.
The $200 cheques, which will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion as the province runs a deficit of $6.6 billion this year, were a cornerstone of the government's fall economic statement Oct. 30.
Ford accused the Liberal leader of planning to raise taxes when he made the cheque announcement in Scarborough.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers," the premier said in a partisan boast. "She wants to increase taxes, increase the carbon tax."
Crombie shot back with a reminder "the only reason" Ontarians pay the federal carbon levy is because Ford axed the province's cap-and-trade environmental pact with Quebec and California in 2018.
"We will not implement a carbon tax," she added.
The Liberals and other opposition parties have accused Ford's PCs, who have a solid lead in public opinion polls, of using the $200 rebate cheques to bribe Ontarians into voting for them as the government faces an RCMP investigation into its $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Under Crombie's proposal, the eight per cent provincial portion of the 13 per cent Harmonized Sales Tax on home heating fuel would be eliminated.
The Liberal source said other consumer friendly tax relief measures will be included in a "fiscally responsible" package paid for by ending the government's "multibillion-dollar deals" that will benefit a private spa at Ontario Place, Staples stores into which ServiceOntario counters are being moved, and as much as $1 billion being spent to move beer, wine and pre-mixed cocktails into convenience stores a year earlier than planned.
Ford's tax-free $200 cheques will be sent to all taxpayers 18 and over. Families who qualify for federal Canada Child Benefit payments receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks at a party meeting and policy convention in London, Ont., on Sept. 21, 2024.

Document 227 of 500

Canada's most decorated citizen gives Doug Ford an order on Remembrance Day: 'There's one other thing I want him to do'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-most-decorated-citizen-gives-doug-ford/docview/3127397775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+most+decorated+citizen+gives+Doug+Ford+an+order+on+Remembrance+Day%3A+%27There%27s+one+other+thing+I+want+him+to+do%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The general has given an order.
Lt. Gen. Richard Rohmer, Canada's most decorated citizen, wants Premier Doug Ford to engrave the name of the Ontario Veterans' Memorial on the north side of the black granite monument at Queen's Park.
Rohmer, 100, who chaired the committee that got the 30-metre memorial built in 2006 under premier Dalton McGuinty, delivered some instructions to Ford at Monday's Remembrance Day ceremony.
"I want to say to the premier there's one other thing I want him to do," said the retired general, whose honours and medals include the highest awards of Canada, France and Belgium, among others.
"There's one final task that I tried to get done to this wall. On the north side of the wall, it is a blank black, black, black, black," he said from the dais in front of the wall he championed.
"And I tried fruitlessly - I don't like the word fruitlessly - but I tried without result in getting the name of this wall put on the north side of the wall so that the people coming here to pay respect ... to this monument ... will know what this wall is."
Turning to Ford, who was seated nearby, Rohmer said: "A few words, letters ... premier, on that side would be helpful. At the moment, it's just a blank wall."
Since its opening 18 years ago, the monument - designed by Charlottetown-born artist Allan Harding MacKay and Vancouver-based landscape architects Phillips Farevaag Smallenberg after a national competition - has become a major landmark at Queen's Park.
With a glimmer in his eye, the centenarian - who has advised premiers dating back John Robarts in the 1960s - reminded the gathered crowd of several hundred people in front of the Ontario legislature of Ford's campaign mantra.
"The premier is the kind of man who likes to 'get things done.' I'm giving him another good suggestion."
Ford's office said later Monday that it is looking into the general's request.
Lest anyone forget Rohmer's powers of persuasion, the P-51 Mustang fighter plane he flew on D-Day in 1944 is one of the engraved scenes of war depicted on the veterans memorial.
"The challenge that was given to me at one point was, 'you can't do that.' I said, 'oh really, watch me.' It's in the wall."
At Ford's behest in 2020, a new monument was added to complement the memorial to commemorate the 158 Canadian Armed Forces personnel killed in the war in Afghanistan.
It features a zigzagging bronze ribbon to symbolize Afghanistan's mountainous terrain and includes a stone from an inukshuk dedicated to the fallen that was erected by Canadian soldiers at the Kandahar air field.
CAPTION: Former Lt. Gen. Richard Rohmer describes fighting the Germans in the Second World War and warns of the Russian threat in Canada's north. Rohmer helped lead the Remembrance Day ceremonies outside the legislature in Toronto. (Nov. 11, 2024 / The Canadian Press)Richard Rohmer, left, one of the most decorated Canadian veterans, is shown at the Government of Canada ceremony to mark the 80th anniversary of D-Day, at Juno Beach, in Courseulles-sur-Mer, Normandy, France, on June 6, 2024.

Document 228 of 500

Tarion proposal that would penalize pre-construction buyers is fatally flawed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tarion-proposal-that-would-penalize-pre/docview/3126727475/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tarion+proposal+that+would+penalize+pre-construction+buyers+is+fatally+flawed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tarion - Ontario's consumer protection organization for newly built homes - wants to reduce the amount of deposit protection for buyers of pre-construction freehold homes who fail to register their purchase agreements with the program.
Deposit protection covers buyers in cases where a builder goes bankrupt or fundamentally breaches an agreement - such as by not completing the house before the final construction deadline - and does not return the deposit.
The stated intention of Tarion's new proposal is to help combat illegal sales of new homes and ensure deposit coverage for current and future new-home purchasers.
In my opinion, the proposal is fatally flawed and a breach of the regulator's obligation to protect the public. Consumers should not be forced to become the police for the industry regulators, and should not be penalized for failing to register their agreements.
Currently, deposits of up to $60,000 are protected for a pre-construction freehold home priced at up to $600,000. For homes priced over $600,000, deposits are protected for up to 10 per cent of the purchase price to a maximum of $100,000.
The new proposal would require purchasers to notify Tarion about their new-home purchase within a specific period of time after signing an agreement with a builder. The notice to Tarion would include providing information about the purchaser, the builder and the amount of the deposit paid. This will be a requirement for buyers in order to qualify for the maximum deposit coverage.
Since 2021, the regulation and licensing of new-home builders in Ontario are the responsibility of the Home Construction Regulatory Authority (HCRA). All matters related to homeowner warranty protection remain the responsibility of Tarion.
Until now, Tarion and the HCRA have been tracking unlicensed builders and prosecuting them where appropriate.
Under the new proposal, purchasers will be tasked with policing the industry, and buyers who fail to register their contracts will be subject to an unspecified reduction in their deposit coverage.
In my experience, perhaps half of Ontario's new-home buyers do not have their contracts reviewed by a lawyer. Expecting those buyers to take the additional step of registering their contracts with Tarion is totally unrealistic.
Penalizing buyers for failure to register their agreements by reducing their deposit protection is a breach of Tarion's mandate to ensure buyers will receive the coverage to which they are entitled.
Tarion has an obligation to verify builders comply with their licensing requirements. It should not require consumers to police the industry so that it can mitigate losses on deposit claims and combat illegal marketing.
Based on my experience as a real estate lawyer, I think the proposal is bound to fail and will only end up reducing deposit protection to less sophisticated buyers.
Tarion's discussion guide on this proposal is available on the Tarion website at www.tarion.com. Feedback on the proposed changes may be sent to submissions@tarion.com by Nov. 25, 2024.
Todd McCarthy, Ontario's Minister of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement, is charged with oversight of Tarion. Let him know what you think at Todd.McCarthy@pc.ola.org.
CAPTION: Construction sites near Dundas Street and Trafalgar Road.Deposit protection covers buyers in cases where a builder goes bankrupt or fundamentally breaches an agreementand does not return the deposit.

Subject: Construction; Consumers; Purchasing; Consumer protection

Document 229 of 500

How long it will actually take to build Highway 413 + fiery Tesla crash highlights failures of electronic doors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-long-will-actually-take-build-highway-413/docview/3126727463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+long+it+will+actually+take+to+build+Highway+413+%2B+fiery+Tesla+crash+highlights+failures+of+electronic+doors&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, November 11 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
As we mark Remembrance Day, consider why the Netherlands continues to honour the sacrifices of Canadian soldiers.
Here's the latest on Doug Ford's Highway 413 plans, the risk of electronic car doors and the impact of Taylor Swift's arrival in Toronto.
DON'T MISSOntario's next highway may be more complicated than Doug Ford expects
Doug Ford's government aims to fast-track Highway 413 construction by streamlining the environmental assessment process - but there are still several steps and years of work ahead, Noor Javed reports. The project was stuck in the environmental assessment stage for three years after the federal government decided to oversee the process. But even after the federal oversight was removed, much remains to be done.
Friends with benefits: Highway 413 is a four-to-six-lane highway connecting Vaughan to Milton, passing through Caledon and Brampton. It also passes thousands of acres of land owned by powerful developers connected to the Ford government.Damage assessment: Critics say the highway will cause environmental damage by paving through thousands of acres of prime farmland, and cutting across the Greenbelt and dozens of waterways, further jeopardizing already at-risk species. A fiery, fatal Tesla crash highlights the risk of electronic doors
Questions are swirling around why the four young victims of a fatal crash last month couldn't get out of their Tesla after it caught fire. A fifth passenger only survived after a passerby, Rick Harper, smashed the window with a metal pole: "You couldn't open the doors," he told Marco Chown Oved. Teslas, like some Mazdas, GMs and Fords, have electronic doors that require power, leaving the doors inoperable following a power failure. Here's what that means for you.
A dangerous road: On Oct. 24, the driver of a Tesla struck a guardrail on Lake Shore Boulevard East and careened into a concrete barrier before bursting into flame, killing three men and one woman. Their deaths make that stretch of road Toronto's deadliest this year.Deadly design: Several news reports chronicled Tesla's door failures after an accident, including a Florida anesthesiologist who died in 2019 when his Model S caught fire and bystanders couldn't open the doors to get him out.Taylor Swift arrives in Toronto this week. Are we ready for it?
Taylor Swift's Eras Tour is landing in Toronto Thursday, marking a cultural moment unlike any other in the city's history. The megastar's six sold-out shows are expected to draw half a million visitors and more than $282 million in economic impact, Mark Colley writes. Roads will close, subways will be packed and the entertainment district will grind to a standstill. Take a look at what's to come.
A look back: While Swift may be the hottest ticket in town, Toronto has seen no shortage of iconic performances. Check out 10 of the city's biggest concerts of all time.Swift's night out: With Toronto's hottest spots vying for the singer-songwriter's attention, Shinan Govani compiled six of the city's best restaurants and bars Swift might actually visit.WHAT ELSE
Israel killed dozens of people in Lebanon and isolated northern Gaza while Netanyahu and Trump speak.Pharmacists say the power to prescribe often comes with pressure to prescribe - and generate profits.Toronto's housing market will boom in 2025, the head of Royal LePage says - making it the most expensive market in Canada.The province's home care supply shortage is due to mistakes by Ontario Health and Ontario Health atHome, sources say.Three people died and a child was injured after a collision on Highway 401 near Quinte West.Three people were rushed to hospital after a small plane crashed near London, Ont.You've saved it, now spend it: Why are some retirees so reluctant to spend money when they can afford to?The economic scenarios for the coming year are all over the map, David Aston writes. Document 230 of 500

Scarborough pays the price of transit 'foot-dragging' a year after the TTC shuttered the SRT

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/scarborough-pays-price-transit-foot-dragging-year/docview/3126727291/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scarborough+pays+the+price+of+transit+%27foot-dragging%27+a+year+after+the+TTC+shuttered+the+SRT&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The long articulated TTC bus rattles and hums on its way down Brimley Road. It's headed toward Kennedy station from Scarborough Centre station, on an express route that could take as little as 10 minutes on Scarborough Rapid Transit, the defunct above-ground light rail line that ran on its own right of way.
Now, during a typical weekday rush hour, the commute can take much longer, despite the dedicated priority transit lanes where buses sometimes have to compete with construction equipment or dump trucks parked in the red-painted curb lanes.
"On a good day with no traffic, it's maybe 20 to 30 minutes," said Janay Samai of her commute by bus to Kennedy station.
Like many Scarborough residents, Samai was disappointed when the former borough's only rapid transit line shut down last year after a derailment that sent five people to hospital - just months before the TTC had planned to decommission the line.
Before the shutdown, Samai used the SRT to get to Kennedy from Scarborough Town Centre. Now she has to catch a bus near her house and transfer to another bus at Kennedy station to get to Centennial College.
More than a year later, she and others living in the area are still waiting for the construction of a dedicated busway that is supposed to run along part of the former SRT route - a busway that could have been built years ago because the TTC knew the rail line was nearing the end of the line.
The SRT shut down "wasn't unexpected, because we all saw the signs," said Samai.
"We know the TTC is not the best at change," added Samai. "But the reason people liked the SRT is because we got to avoid the bus routes," which were already crowded.
A map produced by the TTC for a board meeting in February shows that from Jan.22-26 this year, numerous major bus routes in Scarborough were at or near capacity, even in the middle of the day.
A familiar feeling in Scarborough
The disappointment felt over the abrupt shutdown of an SRT long past its planned lifespan and the overcrowding on other forms of public transit is a familiar feeling for Scarborough commuters, who have seen themselves shortchanged repeatedly by transit service.
Olivia Chow, newly elected as mayor in June of 2023, seemed to take the former borough's transit woes to heart. She called the loss of the SRT, without a replacement, "symbolic of the decades of underinvestment in our city and the people who live here."
The mayor presented a draft budget in February in which she proposed a historic property tax hike that would include money to fully fund the busway, estimated to cost $67.9 million.
In an interview last month , Chow said that before she became mayor, the busway had been delayed because of wrangling over who would pay for it - the city or the province.
"I finally said, 'Enough already. We need it. We'll build it,'" said Chow, who noted the busway could save Scarborough commuters 20 minutes a day.
"We started designing it. And then the budget season was upon us," added Chow. "I didn't want to lose another year."
The mayor said she has also been vocal about the need for provincial and federal funding for the Eglinton East LRT, which would extend the much-delayed Eglinton Crosstown further east and north into Scarborough. Chow said the city will come up with its third of the cost - around $1.2 billion - once the other levels of government commit to contributing their share.
"I want the Scarborough Eglinton East LRT to be a priority," said Chow.
Busway makes headway
Meanwhile, demolition work began in October to make way for the new busway. Track and signals from the SRT corridor are being removed and the work should be finished by the end of this year, according to the TTC chief executive officer's report from October. The busway's design is also expected to be completed by the end of 2024.
The TTC runs express buses from Scarborough Centre to Kennedy station to replace the SRT and has extended eight other Scarborough routes, such as those on Finch and Sheppard Avenues, to Kennedy station so that there is no need for riders to transfer at the Scarborough Centre station. Dedicated bus-only lanes along the express route were also installed.
The TTC CEO's report noted that average commute times on the replacement buses are down three to five minutes with the introduction of the bus-only lanes.
Daniel McIntosh, a member of the transit advocacy group TTCriders who lives in Scarborough, said Kennedy station is "absolute chaos" any time there is a Line 2 subway shutdown because of the volume of replacement buses and shuttle buses vying to get in the station.
Waiting on transit relief
When the derailment occurred, it set off a storm of negative reaction from the public, said Scarborough Centre city councillor Michael Thompson in an interview before his current trial on sexual assault charges.
"Had the derailment not taken place, I think it would have taken a lot longer" to build the busway, he said. It's now scheduled to be completed in 2027, a timeline which Thompson said would have stretched to 2030 without the derailment.
That would have meant that nearly a decade had passed since the TTC originally approved decommissioning the SRT in February of 2021.
Opened in 1985, the SRT had a life expectancy of about 30 years, said Steve Munro, a longtime transit advocate, which meant it was on its last legs as early as 2014-15.
As far back as 2006, Toronto city staff had recommended updating the SRT, and adding newer and bigger cars.
But the plan was scrapped after then-mayor David Miller's Transit City plan was approved, which would have replaced and extended the SRT to Malvern, and built several other LRT lines in the city.
Transit City, which could have been built by now, included a different LRT line that would have included a stop at U of T Scarborough.
A staff member at the university remembers Miller telling students they would have an LRT by the 2015 Pan Am Games if they agreed to a student levy to help shoulder the cost of the Toronto Pan Am Sports Centre on campus, which they did.
"I think a lot of politicians have this idea that Scarborough is still anti-commuter, anti-bus and anti-subway," said Omar Mousa, a U of T Scarborough student who is vice-president, external, for the Scarborough Campus Students' Union.
"And because of that, they're perfectly fine with doing budget cuts where it affects Scarborough," he said. "And they'll try their best to keep the ones in downtown Toronto up and running. Or introduce faster lines for downtown Toronto.
"It's very disappointing that we can see it happening to this day," said Mousa, "with the delays for the busway, with the delays to the (Eglinton Crosstown) LRT, with the shutting down of the SRT."
Subway or busway?
The Transit City plan, which was fully funded, met an untimely death after Rob Ford was elected mayor. He proposed a more expensive subway extension of the Bloor-Danforth line to McCowan, part of his rhetoric to "stop the war on the car," a plan council eventually endorsed, even though there was no money to build it.
Two light rail lines originally proposed as part of Transit City - the Eglinton Crosstown and the Finch West LRT - were later funded, but have yet to open.
Tunnelling for a Scarborough subway line finally began in June 2021 after Premier Doug Ford announced more than $11 billion to fund four transit projects in Toronto.
The subway is expected to be complete by 2030, just three years after the busway is to be up and running.
That timing has transit advocates such as Munro doubting whether the busway, with its proximity to the new Scarborough subway line, will ever be built.
"The foot dragging on that project is typical of everything that's gone on with Scarborough planning," said Munro. "I'm fully expecting someone to come along and say, 'Well, you know, this thing's going to cost us $60 million and the subway is going to be open within X number of years of when we're finally going to get this thing finished,'" he said. "Do we really need it?"
The route of the Scarborough subway extension is similar to the proposed busway, although parts of it are about two kilometres further east. The busway will run from Kennedy station up the same north-south corridor as the former SRT to priority bus lanes on Ellesmere Road between Ellesmere and Scarborough Centre stations. The trip from Scarborough Centre to Kennedy station is expected to take 15 minutes.
The TTC said it intends to continue using the busway after the subway is built to "enhance surface transit for TTC commuters in Scarborough," according to spokesperson Stuart Green. But how it will be used and specific routings have yet to be determined.
Munro, however, expects service on the busway will be reduced once the subway is built and demand shifts to the new line.
Commuters make changes
For now, Scarborough transit users have adjusted how they commute.
According to the latest TTC figures, about 17,300 riders use the replacement buses each day - down from the roughly 23,000 daily riders on the SRT, The transit agency also said that daily ridership on surrounding bus routes increased by 8,000.
McIntosh believes the TTC is doing the best it can, but he thinks the city needs to do more long-term transit planning.
"We should really be thinking 20 to 40 years out," said McIntosh, who joined TTCriders because of his love of transit and climate concerns. "Even if we're not building it now, let's think about where we need the land for the next expansions.
"And we didn't really do a very good job of that with the Scarborough RT."
CAPTION: Scarborough RT was more than a decade past its expected life span when it derailed in July 2023.Newly elected MPP Andrea Hazell addresses a crowd gathered for a "funeral" for the Scarborough RT after it derailed in July 2023, leaving commuters in the former borough to ride the bus.Signage at the TTC Scarborough Centre bus stop at Scarborough Town Centre shows priority transit lanes in red, the express route that allows replacement buses for the SRT to use dedicated bus lanes to get to the Kennedy subway station.A plan under Transit City to extend a light rail transit line along Sheppard Avenue was ended by then-mayor Rob Ford and city council in favour of a subway extension.According to the latest TTC figures, about 17,300 riders use the replacement buses each day - down from the roughly 23,000 daily riders on the SRT.After the SRT derailment last year, the TTC started running express buses on dedicated lanes as replacement. A complete dedicated busway is still years away.A replacement bus headed for Scarborough Town Centre pulls into Kennedy subway station.Express buses along Kennedy road.

Subject: Light rail transportation; Budgets; Buses

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Ontario is criminalizing poverty

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-is-criminalizing-poverty/docview/3126727273/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+is+criminalizing+poverty&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is criminalizing poverty
Doug Ford wanted cities to ask him to use the notwithstanding clause to end encampments. Twelve mayors just said do it, Nov. 1
Workers owed $60M in unpaid wages Ontario failed to collect since 2017, Nov. 8
I am a volunteer with Out of the Cold. Some of the guests have jobs. Obviously, their jobs don't pay a living wage. There are people relying on food banks who a year ago were donating to food banks. How long before they, too, will be living in tents? This is the criminalization of poverty.
Elizabeth Block, Toronto
For many unhoused people, addiction is a result of being homeless
Doug Ford wanted cities to ask him to use the notwithstanding clause to end encampments. Twelve mayors just said do it, Nov. 1
In Sudbury, homelessness is the primary issue at hand. Sudbury Mayor Paul Lefebvrer, who signed an open letter encouraging Premier Doug Ford to invoke the notwithstanding clause, says it isn't his intent to target those experiencing homelessness or addiction. Then what does he plan to do with it?
The lack of housing and the barriers to accessing shelter in the city of Sudbury are not being addressed. Forty new beds will barely make an impact on our list of 500 people currently outdoors living in encampments. Many folks believe that the people they see openly using drugs in our downtown core are homeless as a result of their addictions. The truth is many are falling into addiction as a result of being homeless.
When you are homeless it is extremely difficult to navigate sobriety, or a path to sobriety. If you are surviving outdoors using drugs insulates you. It dulls the cold, the trauma, and some drugs keep you awake and alert should you be attacked or robbed. When someone does present at detox they only have five or so days before they must leave, so if a bed isn't ready at a rehabilitation facility, that individual is going to go back to the outdoors, at great risk of using again. If downtown businesses, the public and the mayor are sick of seeing people use outdoors, get them housed. Once people have their basic needs met then look to support them in other areas.
Deborah Josipovic, Sudbury
What matters is how Torontonians feel about bike lanesMajority support Doug Ford's plan for bike lanes, poll suggests, Nov.7
Please stop with the anti-bike narrative. How do you not know your audience? Of course, the majority of Ontarians don't want bike lanes in the city. They don't live here and want to get downtown as fast as possible. What matters is how the people in Toronto feel about bike lanes.
Ashleigh Sullivan, Etobicoke
Gig economy profiteers offer no real value to the Canadian economyNew tax on Airbnb and other short-term rental property sales is causing some to look to the U.S. instead, Nov. 8
I have no sympathy for these investor "hosts" who are griping about paying HST on the sale of their short-term rental income properties. These gig economy profiteers offer no real value to the Canadian economy, disrupt the residential housing market, and undermine real businesses that pay taxes and provide jobs. I hope they sell their properties to people who will live in them and raise families.
James McDonald, Mississauga
Doug Ford's costly, questionable tactics are being normalized
Don't Feel Smug, Canada, Nov. 7
Should we in Ontario stoop to smugness in the aftermath of the U.S. election, look no further than recent polls placing Doug Ford's government in a large majority over any other provincial party. Every time we in Ontario merely shrug at costly, questionable and heartless Ford initiatives, we are making this government's tactics the new norm. Unless we are well-informed, vigilant and suitably outraged, we are contributing to the blinkered mindset that eventually gave America Donald Trump. Beware, Ontario!
Judith Butler, Toronto
Why were so many attracted by Trump's offensive campaign?
How misogyny, racism and character impacted Trump's return to the White House, Nov. 8
Racism and misogyny were huge factors in the American election, but barrels of ink (or more accurately watt-hours of electricity) are being used to suggest other excuses. Do people only ignore character during elections? Try to find a restaurant owner who wants to hire a waiter who brags about grabbing women. Try to find an employee who is happy working for someone who demeans them and tries to shortchange their wages. People care about cost of living? Trump's main promises, raising tariffs , will decrease household after-tax income according to the Wall Street Journal. Deporting large numbers of undocumented workers will be devastating to U.S. agriculture sector where 45 per cent of workers are undocumented. Incumbent governments are in trouble worldwide but to ignore the impact of racism and misogyny in the election of Donald Trump is ridiculous. Perhaps the question isn't why were more people not repulsed by the offensive Trump campaign, but why so many people were attracted by it?
David Steele, Saskatoon, SK
Trudeau needs to take a page from Trump's playbook
The votes have been counted and the United States will deal with the outcome for the next four years, but Canada has a lot to take away from these results. The Liberals have had to deal with similar economic and environmental concerns during their tenure and have been blamed for the situation the middle class finds itself in. The repercussions of Donald Trump's administration will be huge for us and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will be the scapegoat unless he starts to address the day-to-day problems most people are grappling with . To tackle these concerns, he must have the support of the working middle class which the Conservatives are courting. Open your eyes Trudeau and take a page from Trump's playbook.
Susan Ditchburn, Toronto
Cut the red tape and allow American's best and brightest in
Americans mull move to Canada but that's easier said than done, Nov. 8
This is a wonderful opportunity for Canada - a brain drain from America as a result of the U.S. voting Donald Trump in as president for a second term. We should cut the red tape to allow the best and brightest to become Canadian citizens and boost our struggling economy.
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
A diminished star
As if news from south of the border wasn't bad enough, today I learned that Wayne Gretzky attended the election celebration at Mar-a-Lago. For me a Canadian star in the northern sky has truly dimmed.
Bruce Kerr, Toronto
CAPTION: Dufferin Grove Park has one of Toronto's biggest encampments, but the parks playgrounds, sports facilities and even communal fire pits are used by the neighbourhood.

Try to find a restaurant owner who wants to hire a waiter who brags about grabbing women. Try to find an employee who is happy working for someone who demeans them and tries to shortchange their wages. People care about cost of living? Trump's main promises, raising tariffs, will decrease household after-tax income according to the Wall Street Journal. Deporting large numbers of undocumented workers will be devastating to U.S. agriculture sector where 45 per cent of workers are undocumented. Perhaps the question isn't why were more people not repulsed by the offensive Trump campaign, but why so many people were attracted by it?
David Steele, Saskatoon, Sask.
CREDIT: ;

Business indexing term: Subject: Gig economy

Document 233 of 500

Hwy. 413 faces a long road ahead: Several steps and years to go before rubber hits road, experts say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hwy-413-faces-long-road-ahead/docview/3126679403/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hwy.+413+faces+a+long+road+ahead%3A+Several+steps+and+years+to+go+before+rubber+hits+road%2C+experts+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's government is pushing ahead with new legislation it says will kick construction of Highway 413 into high gear by streamlining the environmental assessment process, allowing for around-the-clock construction and eliminating the right to appeal for expropriations.
The 52-kilometre highway project has been stuck in gridlock at the environmental assessment (EA) stage for the past three years, after the federal government decided in 2021 to oversee the province's work.
Earlier this year, the two parties struck a deal removing the federal oversight - for now. That move propelled the province to push through legislation last month called Bill 212 Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, in the hopes of getting the project to the next stage.
But experts say while a streamlined EA may hasten this phase of the process, there are still several steps - and years to go - before the highway can become close to a driveable reality.
"The government has positioned this as if the environmental assessment is the one thing standing in the way of the highway," said Alexander Wray, a research associate in the department of geography and environment at Western University.
"They are positioning that with the finger snap, you can build a highway, but there are many steps within an EA and afterwards before any highway can be built," said Wray, who has worked on EAs for the government on other infrastructure projects.
Highway 413, or the GTA West Highway, is a four- to six-lane road that will connect Vaughan to Milton, passing through Caledon and Brampton. The highway has become a key part of the province's election platform and its regional transportation plan, but has also generated strong opposition from environmental groups. The government says it will shorten commute times by more than 30 minutes, and help keep the region moving. Critics say the project will cause environmental damage, by cutting through farmland, Greenbelt forests and dozens of waterways across the GTA.
But highway building in Ontario is a lengthy process that according to the province's own estimates can take upwards of a decade to plan, design, expropriate land and construct, with each phase taking an estimated two to three years to complete. On a website describing the provincial highway building process, it says it takes about two to three years to build a 10-kilometre section of four-lane highway.
"It's not a simple project," said Wray, considering it will go through the Oak Ridges Moraine, and cut through numerous sensitive environmental areas including the Greenbelt and Oak Ridges Moraine. "On an Ontario scale, it's complex engineering."
Tamer El-Diraby, a professor in the department of civil and mineral engineering at the University of Toronto, said there are three main parts to highway building, which include "planning and decision-making process," the detailed design process and then construction. The EA is included in the first part, he said.
Currently, the 413 project is in Stage 2 of the environmental assessment, which according to the province, includes "refining the route, determining interchange crossing and preliminary design" in addition to carrying out extensive field studies to understand the local environment, and how to mitigate potential concerns.
Once the EA is finished, made public for feedback and approved by the minister - which can take months - the project would then proceed to the detailed design process.
"The design process is long, but generally very methodical," said El-Diraby, adding it can take time - often years to finalize - as it involves fine-tuning the details of how the project will look, and operate. "It's the construction stage where the rubber hits the road ... someone has to come up with the money to keep the project going. It often happens that politicians will say during the planning stage that we will get this money. But in the construction phase, sometimes there are budgetary concerns, or there is no political will, and the money is not there. So that can become the sticking point."
In February, the province allocated $28 billion over the next 10 years to "build, repair, and expand roads and highways." No new money was added for the 413 in this October's provincial budget, which lists dozens of other proposed highway projects across Ontario.
The province has yet to give an estimate of how much the 413 will cost.
Wray said that even after an EA is completed, the highway still needs to be designed in detail, land has to be expropriated, the project has to be resourced and logistics such as determining if there's enough sand and gravel to build the roadway needs to be determined.
"In my opinion, the accelerated EA may speed up the entire process by at most a few months, and not a few years, which I think the government is trying to position this as," said Wray.
And now, in what appears to be a last-ditch effort, environmental groups have once again petitioned federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault for the project to be designated for environmental oversight under the Impact Assessment Act.
Last month, hours after the 413 bill was introduced, advocacy group Environmental Defence made the request, citing a slew of adverse impacts to species and areas within federal jurisdiction. The highway will cut through large portions of the Greenbelt, and dozens of water ways, including the Humber River, Credit River and Etobicoke Creek, and will impact the habitat of several species of federal concern such as a minnow called the Redside Dace.
Guilbeault has roughly until mid-January to reply to the request.
In March, Ottawa and Queen's Park agreed to cancel a federal review of the province's environmental assessment of the 413 after a Supreme Court decision cast doubt on its constitutionality.
Environmental Defence's Phil Pothen and his group believe the legislation has been amended since that ruling to allow for a new federal review.
If the federal government intervenes, the project could be delayed by several years, said El-Diraby.
If the federal government doesn't step in, the provincial legislation will fast-track the process, but it's hard to know by how much, said El-Diraby.
"I would be shocked if the project starts in the next year," he added.
Dakota Brasier, director of media relations for Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, said the accelerated EA will build on studies done over the past few decades.
"As part of the proposed legislation, MTO will build upon the considerable studies and consultation efforts already undertaken, over the past 30 years to advance the project under the existing environmental assessment process," said Brasier.
"While MTO is accelerating the process - we will be maintaining environmental protection throughout the detail design of Highway 413."
Earlier this year, the province said it is planning to issue construction contracts for "early works," which include enhancements on roadways and bridges that will connect to the 413, in 2025. The new proposed legislation exempts early works from having to go through any EA review.
Pothen said by allowing the early work to start before the EA, or detailed design or route is finalized "gives an impression to the public that the highway is a done deal before we know what the highway will look like or the exact route," he said.
El-Diraby said that, even if the province is able to clear the EA process, the courts could also add to the delays. He said once an EA is finalized, including the route, it can trigger stakeholders to turn to courts to appeal the province's decisions.
The proposed legislation does try to pre-empt some of those changes, including eliminating the ability of landowners to dispute decisions around expropriations.
Such provisions will take some time off the length of the entire process, but cannot be avoided entirely, said Wray. For example, in the case of the 407 East extension, which was 22 kilometres long, it took 30 months to acquire the almost 700 properties along the route.
Wray said that the current messaging appears to be about making clear what the priorities are for this government - something not uncommon for governments of the day.
"It's clear that this highway is important to the Ford government," he said. "This is about making it a priority for the public but also those who work there."
CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Subject: Construction; Legislation; Court decisions; Planning; Roads & highways

Document 234 of 500

Residents pay price of transit 'foot-dragging': More than a year after decrepit light-rail train derailed, commuters still waiting for replacement

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/residents-pay-price-transit-foot-dragging/docview/3126679357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Residents+pay+price+of+transit+%27foot-dragging%27%3A+More+than+a+year+after+decrepit+light-rail+train+derailed%2C+commuters+still+waiting+for+replacement&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The long articulated TTC bus rattles and hums on its way down Brimley Road. It's headed toward Kennedy station from Scarborough Centre station, on an express route that could take as little as 10 minutes on Scarborough Rapid Transit, the defunct above-ground light rail line that ran on its own right of way.
Now, during a typical weekday rush hour, the commute can take much longer, despite the dedicated priority transit lanes where buses sometimes have to compete with construction equipment or dump trucks parked in the red-painted curb lanes.
"On a good day with no traffic, it's maybe 20 to 30 minutes," said Janay Samai of her commute by bus to Kennedy station.
Like many Scarborough residents, Samai was disappointed when the former borough's only rapid transit line shut down last year after a derailment that sent five people to hospital - just months before the TTC had planned to decommission the line.
Before the shutdown, Samai used the SRT to get to Kennedy from Scarborough Town Centre. Now she has to catch a bus near her house and transfer to another bus at Kennedy station to get to Centennial College.
More than a year later, she and others living in the area are still waiting for the construction of a dedicated busway that is supposed to run along part of the former SRT route - a busway that could have been built years ago because the TTC knew the rail line was nearing the end of the line.
The SRT shutdown "wasn'tunexpected, because we all saw the signs," said Samai.
"We know the TTC is not the best at change," added Samai. "But the reason people liked the SRT is because we got to avoid the bus routes," which were already crowded.
A map produced by the TTC for a board meeting in February shows that from Jan. 22-26 this year, numerous major bus routes in Scarborough were at or near capacity, even in the middle of the day.
A familiar feeling in Scarborough
The disappointment felt over the abrupt shutdown of an SRT long past its planned lifespan and the overcrowding on other forms of public transit is a familiar feeling for Scarborough commuters, who have seen themselves shortchanged repeatedly by transit service.
Olivia Chow, newly elected as mayor in June 2023, seemed to take the former borough's transit woes to heart. She called the loss of the SRT, without a replacement, "symbolic of the decades of underinvestment in our city and the people who live here."
The mayor presented a draft budget in February in which she proposed a historic property tax hike that would include money to fully fund the busway, estimated to cost $67.9 million.
In an interview last month, Chow said that before she became mayor, the busway had been delayed because of wrangling over who would pay for it - the city or the province.
"I finally said, 'Enough already. We need it. We'll build it,' " said Chow, who noted the busway could save Scarborough commuters 20 minutes a day. "We started designing it. And then the budget season was upon us," added Chow. "I didn't want to lose another year."
The mayor said she has also been vocal about the need for provincial and federal funding for the Eglinton East LRT, which would extend the much-delayed Eglinton Crosstown further east and north into Scarborough. Chow said the city will come up with its third of the cost - around $1.2 billion - once the other levels of government commit to contributing their share.
"I want the Scarborough Eglinton East LRT to be a priority," said Chow.
Busway makes headway
Meanwhile, demolition work began in October to make way for the new busway. Track and signals from the SRT corridor are being removed and the work should be finished by the end of this year, according to the TTC chief executive officer's report from October. The busway's design is also expected to be completed by the end of 2024.
The TTC runs express buses from Scarborough Centre to Kennedy station to replace the SRT and has extended eight other Scarborough routes, such as those on Finch and Sheppard avenues, to Kennedy station so that there is no need for riders to transfer at the Scarborough Centre station. Dedicated bus-only lanes along the express route were also installed.
The TTC CEO's report noted that average commute times on the replacement buses are down three to five minutes with the introduction of the bus-only lanes.
Daniel McIntosh, a member of the transit advocacy group TTCriders who lives in Scarborough, said Kennedy station is "absolute chaos" any time there is a Line 2 subway shutdown because of the volume of replacement buses and shuttle buses vying to get in the station.
Waiting on transit relief
When the derailment occurred, it set off a storm of negative reaction from the public, said Coun. Michael Thompson (Ward 21, Scarborough Centre) in an interview before his current trial on sexual assault charges.
"Had the derailment not taken place, I think it would have taken a lot longer" to build the busway, he said. It's now scheduled to be completed in 2027, a timeline which Thompson said would have stretched to 2030 without the derailment.
That would have meant that nearly a decade had passed since the TTC originally approved decommissioning the SRT in February 2021.
Opened in 1985, the SRT had a life expectancy of about 30 years, said Steve Munro, a longtime transit advocate, which meant it was on its last legs as early as 2014-15.
As far back as 2006, Toronto city staff had recommended updating the SRT, and adding newer and bigger cars.
But the plan was scrapped after then-mayor David Miller's Transit City plan was approved, which would have replaced and extended the SRT to Malvern, and built several other LRT lines in the city.
Transit City, which could have been built by now, included a different LRT line that would have included a stop at U of T Scarborough.
A staff member at the university remembers Miller telling students they would have an LRT by the 2015 Pan Am Games if they agreed to a student levy to help shoulder the cost of the Toronto Pan Am Sports Centre on campus, which they did.
"I think a lot of politicians have this idea that Scarborough is still anti-commuter, anti-bus and anti-subway," said Omar Mousa, a U of T Scarborough student who is vice-president, external, for the Scarborough Campus Students' Union.
"And because of that, they're perfectly fine with doing budget cuts where it affects Scarborough," he said.
"And they'll try their best to keep the ones in downtown Toronto up and running. Or introduce faster lines for downtown Toronto.
"It's very disappointing that we can see it happening to this day," said Mousa, "with the delays for the busway, with the delays to the (Eglinton Crosstown) LRT, with the shutting down of the SRT."
Subway or busway?
The Transit City plan, which was fully funded, met an untimely death after Rob Ford was elected mayor. He proposed a more expensive subway extension of the Bloor-Danforth line to McCowan, part of his rhetoric to "stop the war on the car," a plan council eventually endorsed, even though there was no money to build it.
Two light rail lines originally proposed as part of Transit City - the Eglinton Crosstown and the Finch West LRT - were later funded, but have yet to open.
Tunnelling for a Scarborough subway line finally began in June 2021 after Premier Doug Ford announced more than $11 billion to fund four transit projects in Toronto.
The subway is expected to be complete by 2030, just three years after the busway is to be up and running.
That timing has transit advocates such as Munro doubting whether the busway, with its proximity to the new Scarborough subway line, will ever be built.
"The foot dragging on that project is typical of everything that's gone on with Scarborough planning," said Munro. "I'm fully expecting someone to come along and say, 'Well, you know, this thing's going to cost us $60 million and the subway is going to be open within X number of years of when we're finally going to get this thing finished,' " he said. "Do we really need it?"
The route of the Scarborough subway extension is similar to the proposed busway, although parts of it are about two kilometres further east. The busway will run from Kennedy station up the same north-south corridor as the former SRT to priority bus lanes on Ellesmere Road between Ellesmere and Scarborough Centre stations. The trip from Scarborough Centre to Kennedy station is expected to take 15 minutes.
The TTC said it intends to continue using the busway after the subway is built to "enhance surface transit for TTC commuters in Scarborough," according to spokesperson Stuart Green. But how it will be used and specific routings have yet to be determined.
Munro, however, expects service on the busway will be reduced once the subway is built and demand shifts to the new line.
Commuters make changes
For now, Scarborough transit users have adjusted how they commute.
According to the latest TTC figures, about 17,300 riders use the replacement buses each day - down from the roughly 23,000 daily riders on the SRT. The transit agency also said daily ridership on surrounding bus routes increased by 8,000.
McIntosh believes the TTC is doing the best it can, but he thinks the city needs to do more long-term transit planning.
"Weshould really be thinking 20 to 40 years out," said McIntosh, who joined TTCriders because of his love of transit and climate concerns. "Even if we're not building it now, let's think about where we need the land for the next expansions. And we didn't really do a very good job of that with the Scarborough RT."
CAPTION: The Scarborough RT was more than a decade past its expected life span when it derailed in July 2023.MPP Andrea Hazell addresses a crowd gathered for a "funeral" for the Scarborough RT after it derailed last year, leaving commuters in the former borough to ride the bus.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photoDan Pearce Metroland file photo
CREDIT: Patty Winsa staff Reporter

Document 235 of 500

Tired of tents? Speak up for shelters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tired-tents-speak-up-shelters/docview/3126679145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tired+of+tents%3F+Speak+up+for+shelters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're wondering what it's like to live in Toronto, this is my neighbourhood: Within sight of my home I can see a shelter and a daycare across the street from one another. They have been there for a few years, the toddlers playing mere metres from the shelter. They are both our neighbours and they are fine.
We often pass by the shelter with our own nine-month-old and soon he'll play near it. I see this scene every day and often think about how this kind of thing is routinely opposed by some residents of our city. Recently, word that a shelter is planned for 2535 Gerrard St. E. was met with local resident objections. A prominent Toronto sportscaster's social media post opposing it went viral, beginning with, "If you're wondering what it's like to live in Toronto ...This is my neighbourhood," going on to describe reasons the shelter shouldn't be there: it's near a daycare; a school is two blocks away; there's a family restaurant nearby.
It never occurred to me that restaurants shouldn't be near shelters, but we also have those in our neighbourhood and they, too, are fine. The sportscaster's post aside, there were enough other neighbours who shared the sentiment that politicians got involved.
Local NDP MPP Doly Begum wrote a letter to Coun. Alejandra Bravo, chair of council's economic and community development committee, which oversees shelter expansion, expressing concerns over the site selection, demonstrating it isn't just Doug Ford's provincial PCs who feel entitled to meddle in municipal matters.
Coun. Parthi Kandavel also got involved, saying more homework and due diligence is required to determine the shelter's location. Back in February, however, Kandavel voted in favour of a council item that, among other things, delegated authority (and homework, and due diligence) to city staff in establishing new shelters.
It reaffirmed a 2017 council decision delegating authority to professional and trained staff, both wise moves to depoliticize the process of establishing desperately needed shelters because they are habitually opposed.
But now it seems local politicians are enabling the usual objections instead of standing firm for what is right.
It's like the last few years haven't taught us anything. As if we didn't see that the city's shelters were at capacity while encampments filled parks and public spaces on a scale that hasn't been seen since the Great Depression. Nobody likes that encampments exist, but the only way to truly fix the situation is by establishing more shelters and, in the longer term, affordable housing.
Related to this is Doug Ford's recent offer to use the notwithstanding clause so Ontario cities can clear encampments. Fighting for and defending those kinds of rights and freedoms are, in part, why Canadians wear poppies right now. Are they important or not?
In response, 13 Ontario mayors signed a letter asking that the notwithstanding clause be a possible tool to clear encampments. Cleared to where, exactly, you might reasonably ask?
Some of those same signatory mayors have also made building housing difficult in various ways, from exclusionary zoning and taxing new projects to the mayor of Windsor giving up $70 million in federal funding by refusing to make necessary housing reforms. The Ford government itself has proven to be the province's chief NIMBY gatekeeper, baulking at real reforms and funding, despite all their bluster otherwise.
Building housing is hard, but blithely taking charter rights away is easy. Remember that on Remembrance Day.
It's all part of a dismal cycle that avoids, blocks or holds up shelter and housing creation. The problem is clear and so are the solutions. It isn't complicated, but the politics are cowardly.
Back in my neighbourhood there's a modular, supportive housing site for recently homeless men a few blocks away on Dovercourt Road. Opened in 2021, it too is fine, and is a block away from another daycare that we have our eye on as a possibility for our kid in the near future. Other nearby shelters and supportive or affordable housing projects will mitigate the nearby Dufferin Grove Park encampment, one of Toronto's biggest.
The situation there has been far from perfect or ideal, but despite claims otherwise, the playgrounds, sports facilities and even the communal fire pits are still busy and used by the neighbourhood.
The city is doing the right thing in boldly establishing more shelters, but it doesn't take many voices for a politician's spider senses to be alerted to disgruntled squeaky wheel residents. My household wrote a letter of support for our shelter neighbour and attended a community meeting to voice the same. Others did too.
The squeaky wheels get the attention, but to solve this problem it's necessary to actively squeak in support of shelters, because otherwise the politicians just listen to the squelchers.
CAPTION: Dufferin Grove Park has one of Toronto's biggest encampments, but the park's playgrounds, sports facilities and even communal fire pits are used by the neighbourhood. Nobody likes that encampments exist, but the only way to truly fix the situation is by establishing more shelters and, long term, affordable housing, Shawn Micallef writes.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Document 236 of 500

Tarion proposal is fatally flawed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tarion-proposal-is-fatally-flawed/docview/3126679053/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tarion+proposal+is+fatally+flawed&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tarion-Ontario's consumer protection organization for newly built homes-wants to reduce the amount of deposit protection for buyers of pre-construction freehold homes who fail to register their purchase agreements with the program.
Deposit protection covers buyers in cases where a builder goes bankrupt or fundamentally breaches an agreement- such as by not completing the house before the final construction deadline - and does not return the deposit.
The stated intention of Tarion's new proposal is to help combat illegal sales of new homes and ensure deposit coveragefor current and future new-home purchasers.
In my opinion, the proposal is fatally flawed and a breach of the regulator's obligation to protect the public. Consumers should not be forced to become the police for the industry regulators, and should not be penalized for failing to register their agreements.
Currently, deposits of up to $60,000 are protected for a pre-construction freehold home priced at up to $600,000. For homes priced over $600,000, deposits are protected for up to 10 per cent of the purchase price to a maximum of $100,000.
The new proposal would require purchasers to notify Tarion about their new-home purchase within a specific period of time after signing an agreement with a builder. The notice to Tarion would include providing information about the purchaser, the builder and the amount of the deposit paid. This will be a requirement for buyers in order to qualify for the maximum deposit coverage.
Since 2021, the regulation and licensing of new-home builders in Ontario are the responsibility of the Home Construction Regulatory Authority (HCRA). All matters related to homeowner warranty protection remain the responsibility of Tarion.
Until now, Tarion and the HCRA have been tracking unlicensed builders and prosecuting them where appropriate.
Under the new proposal, purchasers will be tasked with policing the industry, and buyers who fail to register their contracts will be subject to an unspecified reduction in their deposit coverage.
In my experience, perhaps half of Ontario's new-home buyers do not have their contracts reviewed by a lawyer. Expecting those buyers to take the additional step of registering their contracts with Tarion is totally unrealistic.
Penalizing buyers for failure to register their agreements by reducing their deposit protection is a breach of Tarion's mandate to ensure buyers will receive the coverage to which they are entitled.
Tarion has an obligation to verify builders comply with their licensing requirements. It should not require consumers to police the industry so that it can mitigate losses on deposit claims and combat illegal marketing.
Based on my experience as a real estate lawyer, I think the proposal is bound to fail and will only end up reducing deposit protection to less sophisticated buyers.
Tarion's discussion guide on this proposal is available at tarion.com. Feedback on the proposed changes may be sent to submissions@tarion.com by Nov. 25.
Todd McCarthy, Ontario's Minister of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement, is charged with oversight of Tarion. Let him know what you think at Todd.McCarthy@pc.ola.org.
CAPTION: Ontario's consumer protection organizationfor newly built homes wants to reduce the amount of deposit protection for buyers of pre-construction freehold homes who fail to register their purchase agreements.Toronto Starfile photo
