Document 237 of 500

Doug Ford wants to put the pedal to the metal to build Ontario's next highway. Here's why that may not work

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-put-pedal-metal-build-ontarios/docview/3126679025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+put+the+pedal+to+the+metal+to+build+Ontario%27s+next+highway.+Here%27s+why+that+may+not+work&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's government is pushing ahead with new legislation it says will kick construction of Highway 413 into high gear by streamlining the environmental assessment process, allowing for around-the-clock construction and eliminating the right to appeal for expropriations.
The 52-km highway project has been stuck in gridlock at the environmental assessment (EA) stage for the past three years, after the federal government decided in 2021 to oversee the province's work.
Earlier this year, the two parties struck a deal removing the federal oversight - for now. That move propelled the province to push through legislation last month called Bill 212 Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, in the hopes of getting the project to the next stage.
But experts say while a streamlined EA may hasten this phase of the process, there are still several steps - and years to go - before the highway can become close to a driveable reality.
"The government has positioned this as if the environmental assessment is the one thing standing in the way of the highway," said Alexander Wray, a research associate in the department of geography and environment at Western University, who has worked on EAs for the government on other infrastructure projects.
"They are positioning that with the finger snap, you can build a highway, but there are many steps within an EA and afterwards before any highway can be built," he said.
A complex construction job
Highway 413, or the GTA West Highway, is a four- to six-lane highway that will connect Vaughan to Milton, passing through Caledon and Brampton. The highway has become a key part of the province's election platform and its regional transportation plan, but has also generated strong opposition from environmental groups. The government says it will shorten commute times by more than 30 minutes, and help keep the region moving. Critics say the project will cause environmental damage, by cutting through farmland, Greenbelt forests and dozens of waterways across the GTA.
But highway building in Ontario is a lengthy process that according to the province's own estimates can take upwards of a decade to plan, design, expropriate land and construct, with each phase taking an estimated two to three years to complete. On a website describing the provincial highway building process, it says it takes about two to three years to build a 10-kilometre section of four-lane highway.
"It's not a simple project," said Wray, considering it will go through the Oak Ridges Moraine, and cut through numerous sensitive environmental areas including the Greenbelt and Oak Ridges Moraine. "On an Ontario scale, it's complex engineering."
Tamer El-Diraby, a professor in the department of civil and mineral engineering at University of Toronto, said there are three main parts to highway building, which include "planning and decision-making process," the detailed design process, and then construction. The EA is included in the first part, he said.
Currently, the 413 project is in Stage 2 of the environmental assessment, which according to the province, includes "refining the route, determining interchange crossing and preliminary design" in addition to carrying out extensive field studies to understand the local environment, and how to mitigate potential concerns.
Once the EA is finished, made public for feedback and approved by the minister - which can take months - the project would then proceed to the detailed design process.
"The design process is long, but generally very methodical," said El-Diraby, adding that it can take time - often years to finalize - as it involves fine-tuning the details of how the project will look, and operate.
"It's the construction stage where the rubber hits the road ... someone has to come up with the money to keep the project going," said El-Diraby. "It often happens that politicians will say during the planning stage that we will get this money. But in the construction phase, some times there are budgetary concerns, or there is no political will, and the money is not there. So that can become the sticking point."
Cost of 413 a mystery
In February, the province allocated $28 billion over the next 10 years to "build, repair, and expand roads and highways." No new money was added for the 413 in this October's provincial budget, which lists dozens of other proposed highway projects across Ontario.
The province has yet to give an estimate of how much the 413 will cost.
Wray said that even after an EA is completed, the highway still needs to be designed in detail, land has to be expropriated, the project has to be resourced, and logistics such as determining if there's enough sand and gravel to build the roadway needs to be determined.
"In my opinion, the accelerated EA may speed up the entire process by at most a few months, and not a few years, which I think the government is trying to position this as," said Wray.
A new curve in the road
And now, in what appears to be a last-ditch effort, environmental groups have once again petitioned federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault for the project to be designated for environmental oversight under the Impact Assessment Act.
Last month, hours after the 413 bill was introduced, advocacy group Environmental Defence made the request, citing a slew of adverse impacts to species and areas within federal jurisdiction. The highway will cut through large portions of the Greenbelt, and dozens of water ways, including the Humber River, Credit River and Etobicoke Creek, and will impact the habitat of several species of federal concern such as a minnow called the Redside Dace.
Guilbeault has roughly until mid-January to reply to the request.
In March, Ottawa and Queen's Park agreed to cancel a federal review of the province's environmental assessment of the 413 after a Supreme Court decision cast doubt on its constitutionality.
Environmental Defence's Phil Pothen and his group believes the legislation has been amended since that ruling to allow for a new federal review.
If the federal government intervenes, the project could be delayed by several years, said El-Diraby.
If the federal government doesn't step in, the provincial legislation will fast-track the process, but it's hard to know by how much, said El-Diraby.
"I would be shocked if the project starts in the next year," he added.
Dakota Brasier, director of media relations for Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said that the accelerated EA will build on studies done over the past few decades.
"As part of the proposed legislation, MTO will build upon the considerable studies and consultation efforts already undertaken, over the past 30 years to advance the project under the existing environmental assessment process," said Brasier.
"While MTO is accelerating the process - we will be maintaining environmental protection throughout the detail design of Highway 413."
Earlier this year, the province said it is planning to issue construction contracts for "early works", which include enhancements on roadways and bridges that will connect to the 413, in 2025. The new proposed legislation exempts early works from having to go through any EA review.
Pothen said by allowing the early work to start before the EA, or detailed design or route is finalized "gives an impression to the public that the highway is a done deal before we know what the highway will look like or the exact route," he said.
El-Diraby said that even if the province is able to clear the EA process, the courts could also add to the delays. He said once an EA is finalized, including the route, it can trigger stakeholders to turn to courts to appeal the province's decisions.
The proposed legislation does try to pre-empt some of those changes, including eliminating the ability of landowners to dispute decisions around expropriations.
Such provisions will take some time off the length of the entire process, but cannot be avoided entirely, said Wray.For example, in the case of the 407 East extension, which was 22 kilometres long, it took 30 months to acquire the almost 700 properties along the route.
Wray said that the current messaging appears to be about making clear what the priorities are for this government - something not uncommon for governments of the day.
"It's clear that this highway is important to the Ford government," he said. "This is about making it a priority for the public but also those who work there."
CAPTION: Critics say the 413 will cause environmental damage, by cutting through farmland, Greenbelt forests and dozens of waterways across the GTA.Ontario Premier Doug Ford during an announcement for the building of 413, in Caledon earlier this year. Even once it passes the EA, the highway has to go through detailed design stage before construction can start.The province has said it is planning to issue construction contracts for "early works" on the 413, which will happen even before the route is finalized.Premier Doug Ford says his government's new legislation is designed to kick 413 construction into high gear, after being stuck in an environmental assessment for the past three years.Members of 'GASP' Grandmothers Act to Save the Planet as well as other supporters, like Fred Wieler and Charlene Pauls protested the proposed GTA West Highway 413 at the four points of the intersection of Trafalgar road and Cornwall/Speers Road. Document 238 of 500

If you don't want encampments in your park, you should speak up for shelters in your neighbourhood

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-you-dont-want-encampments-your-park-should/docview/3126546593/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+you+don%27t+want+encampments+in+your+park%2C+you+should+speak+up+for+shelters+in+your+neighbourhood&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're wondering what it's like to live in Toronto, this is my neighbourhood: within sight of my home I can see a shelter and a daycare across the street from one another. They have been there for a few years, the toddlers playing mere metres from the shelter. They are both our neighbours and they are fine.
We often pass by the shelter with our own nine-month-old and soon he'll play near it. I see this scene every day and often think about how this kind of thing is routinely opposed by some residents of our city. Recently, word that a shelter is planned for 2535 Gerrard St. E. was met with local resident objections. A prominent Toronto sportscaster's social media post opposing it went viral, beginning with "If you're wondering what it's like to live in Toronto ...This is my neighbourhood," going on to describe reasons the shelter shouldn't be there: it's near a daycare; a school is two blocks away; there's a family restaurant nearby.
It never occurred to me that restaurants shouldn't be near shelters, but we also have those in our neighbourhood and they, too, are fine. The sportscaster's post aside, there were enough other neighbours who shared the sentiment that politicians got involved.
Local NDP MPP Doly Begum wrote a letter to Coun. Alejandra Bravo, chair of council's economic and community development committee, which oversees shelter expansion, expressing concerns over the site selection, demonstrating it isn't just Doug Ford's provincial PCs who feel entitled to meddle in municipal matters.
Coun. Parthi Kandavel also got involved, saying more homework and due diligence is required to determine the shelter's location. Back in February, however, Kandavel voted in favour of a council item that, among other things, delegated authority (and homework, and due diligence) to city staff in establishing new shelters.
It reaffirmed a 2017 council decision delegating authority to professional and trained staff, both wise moves to depoliticize the process of establishing desperately needed shelters because they are habitually opposed.
But now it seems local politicians are enabling the usual objections instead of standing firm for what is right.
It's like the last few years haven't taught us anything. As if we didn't see that the city's shelters were at capacity while encampments filled parks and public spaces on a scale that hasn't been seen since the Great Depression. Nobody likes that encampments exist, but the only way to truly fix the situation is by establishing more shelters and, in the longer term, affordable housing.
Related to this is Doug Ford's recent offer to use the notwithstanding clause so Ontario cities can clear encampments. Fighting for and defending those kinds of rights and freedoms are, in part, why Canadians wear poppies right now. Are they important or not? In response, 13 Ontario mayors signed a letter asking that the notwithstanding clause be a possible tool to clear encampments. Cleared to where, exactly, you might reasonably ask?
Some of those same signatory mayors have also made building housing difficult in various ways, from exclusionary zoning and taxing new projects to the mayor of Windsor giving up $70 million in federal funding by refusing to make necessary housing reforms. The Ford government itself has proven to be the province's chief NIMBY gatekeeper, baulking at real reforms and funding, despite all their bluster otherwise.
Building housing is hard, but blithely taking charter rights away is easy. Remember that on Remembrance Day.
It's all part of a dismal cycle that avoids, blocks or holds up shelter and housing creation. The problem is clear and so are the solutions. It isn't complicated, but the politics are cowardly.
Back in my neighbourhood there's a modular, supportive housing site for recently homeless men a few blocks away on Dovercourt Road. Opened in 2021, it too is fine, and is a block away from another daycare that we have our eye on as a possibility for our kid in the near future. Other nearby shelters and supportive or affordable housing projects will mitigate the nearby Dufferin Grove Park encampment, one of Toronto's biggest.
The situation there has been far from perfect or ideal, but despite claims otherwise, the playgrounds, sports facilities and even the communal fire pits are still busy and used by the neighbourhood.
The city is doing the right thing in boldly establishing more shelters, but it doesn't take many voices for a politician's spider senses to be alerted to disgruntled squeaky wheel residents. My household wrote a letter of support for our shelter neighbour and attended a community meeting to voice the same. Others did too.
The squeaky wheels get the attention, but to solve this problem it's necessary to actively squeak in support of shelters, because otherwise the politicians just listen to the squelchers.
CAPTION: Dufferin Grove Park has one of Toronto's biggest encampments, but the parks playgrounds, sports facilities and even communal fire pits are used by the neighbourhood.

Document 239 of 500

Pharmacists say power to prescribe often comes with pressure to prescribe, and generate profits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pharmacists-say-power-prescribe-often-comes-with/docview/3126546586/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacists+say+power+to+prescribe+often+comes+with+pressure+to+prescribe%2C+and+generate+profits&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Ontario government proposes expanding the list of "minor ailments" that pharmacists can treat - a plan the province says will ease strain on family doctors and emergency rooms - pharmacists themselves are sounding the alarm over the business practices of major chains, which they say pose risks to patient safety.
Fuelling the concerns are accusations that large chain pharmacies, which dominate the profession in Ontario, have stripped back support staff and issued quotas to pharmacists for OHIP-billable services. A February CBC exposé reported that pharmacists at Shoppers Drug Mart and other chains pressure patients to access unnecessary services including medication reviews, or "MedsChecks," an Ontario program allowing pharmacists to bill the government up to $75 for what often amounts to a phone call completed within a few minutes.
Earlier this year, Shoppers billed OHIP $1,423,900 for MedsChecks alone, in just one week.
Kathleen Leach, a Hamilton pharmacist, said expanding pharmacists' roles could degrade the care at major chains, as pharmacists working under service quotas are compelled to rush through their work, or face losses to their staffing and job security.
"Things get missed," said Leach, adding there is already widespread worry among pharmacists over the lack of support and staffing in corporate pharmacies.
A recent survey of nearly 4,300 Ontario pharmacy workers showed that over a third have felt pressure to rush through minor ailment assessments in under five minutes. According to Leach, the issues with MedsChecks are just one symptom of a bigger problem: growing corporate influence over the pharmacy profession.
The Ontario College of Pharmacists, the body responsible for regulating the province's pharmacists, is trying to address the issue. Surveys and town halls held by the OCP revealed that 85 per cent of pharmacists feel pressure to meet service quotas; a sizable majority of them, according to an OCP report, work at Loblaw and Shoppers pharmacies.
A separate report from B.C. found that these issues occurred in chain pharmacies at more than twice the rate of independent pharmacies. And Saskatchewan's pharmacy regulator concluded that "focus on (business) targets leads to errors and increased patient risks."
In April, pharmacists in Ontario filed a class-action lawsuit against Shoppers and its parent company, Loblaw, arguing that corporate practices such as stripping back staff and enforcing service quotas pose significant risks to patients.
"The class action lawsuit has no merit whatsoever and we will vigorously defend against it," said Loblaw, in an email. Pharmacists bear sole responsibility in deciding whether to provide a professional service, the company said, adding that in the wake of public concern, it has worked toward "a more collaborative approach to patient care."
Corporate planning documents sent to store owners confirm Shoppers continues to issue service and revenue targets for pharmacists, with targets in-place for 2025, including for MedsChecks, Naloxone kits, and minor ailment assessments.
Kyro Maseh, an independent pharmacist in Scarborough, says he's had to "rehabilitate" pharmacy students coming from corporate pharmacies, as they're used to rushing through their work.
"If you're doing a MedsCheck in three minutes, you're false billing," he said, noting that a proper MedsCheck should take 20 minutes to an hour to complete. Rushed assessments, he warns, can leave patients with a false sense of security, risking missed medical issues.
Annie Jaeger, a Shoppers Drug Mart customer in Peterborough, Ont., said lately she's felt increased pressure from her pharmacist to undergo health assessments, even for things she usually manages on her own.
"A lot of the things they're going to be prescribing for are things we can already manage over the counter," Jaeger said, adding that as opportunities for revenue-generation increase, so too does the potential for overbilling, unnecessary assessments and "lots of abuse."
Many pharmacists, including those worried about the effects of corporate quotas on their work, welcome an expanded role.
"We genuinely want to help," said Leach, who says she's excited to provide care to patients without having to refer them to clinics for every issue.
NDP Opposition Health Critic France Gélinas says she has no issue with giving pharmacists more responsibilities, but letting them bill OHIP for health services is problematic. Gélinas pointed out that nurse practitioners provide the same services to patients without OHIP billing fees attached.
Her fellow NDP MPP Chris Glover said "Pharmacists have a lot of knowledge and skills which should be put to use. But that's not what's happening. What (Premier Doug) Ford has done is find a way to divert more tax dollars out of vital services and into the hands of companies like Loblaw."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference at a Shoppers Drug Mart pharmacy in Etobicoke, Ont., on Wednesday, January 11, 2023. THE CANADIAN PRESS/ Tijana Martin
CREDIT: Nick Tsergas

Subject: Workforce planning; Patients; Drug stores

Document 240 of 500

Tory leader is changing party's relationship with corporations: Poilievre ripped into Canada's business elite for their cosiness with the Liberal government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-leader-is-changing-partys-relationship-with/docview/3126483292/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+leader+is+changing+party%27s+relationship+with+corporations%3A+Poilievre+ripped+into+Canada%27s+business+elite+for+their+cosiness+with+the+Liberal+government&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The crowd gathered on the 54th floor of Toronto's TD Bank Tower, an imposing glass-and-steel edifice, was a who's who of the Canadian business elite. There were CEOs and tech moguls and bankers who had paid as much as $1,725 each for a chance to hear from Pierre Poilievre.
Poilievre isn't your traditional Conservative Party of Canada leader - even when addressing with his supporters in the C-suite. Weeks before this June fundraiser on Bay Street, he publicly ripped into CEOs in an opinion piece for failing to push back against the environmental policies of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the man he appears poised to defeat at the polls next year. "Gutless executives," he'd written.
So when he asked the group for questions, it soon got tense.
Blake Hutcheson, the chief executive officer of a powerful Ontario pension fund, stepped forward, clutching the newspaper column, and launched into Poilievre for attacking the very people who were trying to help him get elected. It was wrong, Hutcheson suggested. "He told Pierre he wasn't being polite," said Jim Balsillie, the former co-CEO of BlackBerry Ltd., who witnessed the exchange.
Poilievre stood his ground and made it clear that he's tired of Canadian politeness. If you agree with me, he said, go out and say publicly what you tell me in private - that you hate Trudeau's policies.
The scene that evening, described to Bloomberg by several people who attended the Conservative Party-hosted event, underscores the harder edge Poilievre is bringing to Canadian politics as he moves closer to gaining power.
He has refashioned his party, which has governed Canada just four times since the Second World War, into a feared political force, while changing the party's relationship with business. As in Donald Trump's Republican Party, genteel pro-business conservatism is out. Pointed attacks against those who cross the leader are in.
Poilievre is courting working-class voters with an anti-elite message that sometimes paints a darker picture of the country. "Everything feels broken in Canada," he says in television ads. Business groups don't have the solutions - they only know how to hold "pointless luncheons and meetings," Poilievre has written. He lambasted the CEO of Canada's largest telecommunications company as "overpaid" and accused him of draining the firm's resources "to pay his wealthy friends" high dividends.
It's working: the Conservatives hold a huge lead in the polls, implying a future Parliamentary majority that would give Poilievre, 45, the ability to make sweeping changes to the direction of the world's 10th largest economy, and he's hinting that's exactly what he wants to do. He has slammed some of Canada's biggest companies, accusing them of being coddled by the state and too eager to pursue taxpayer subsidies.
While many executives want to see the end of Trudeau's government, Hutcheson's comments at the June event also reflect a sense of unease about what Poilievre might do - and how he might behave if given the keys to the prime minister's office. Poilievre's office did not answer questions from Bloomberg.
"Blake Hutcheson is a proud Canadian who is known to value and drive respectful, open, and honest conversations and for consistently promoting and supporting diverse perspectives and discourse," a spokesperson for the pension fund, the Ontario Municipal Employees' Retirement System, said by email. "He takes this approach because he respects our political leaders, including Mr. Poilievre, their dedication to our country, and their openness to feedback."
'Crony capitalism'
In 2022, Poilievre easily won the leadership of the Conservatives after its elected members ejected Erin O'Toole, a polished ex-military man who was criticized for being too centrist and not tough enough on Trudeau.
Nobody says that about Poilievre. He has long been known in Ottawa for his combative style, but since becoming leader, his willingness to occasionally sling insults at corporate leaders has troubled even some members of his own caucus - a party that has generally presented itself as a pro-growth, business-friendly alternative to the Liberals.
One illustration was his recent spat with BCE Inc., owner of CTV. When a CTV news program stitched together clips of Poilievre, presenting his remarks out of context, he accused the network of being biased against him. CTV apologized and fired two people involved in producing the story.
But then the Conservative leader kept going, attacking BCE CEO Mirko Bibic directly and mocking the company for having its credit rating downgraded. "This is the crony capitalism of Trudeau's state-run economy," Poilievre wrote in a social-media post on X. "Politicians protect big oligopolies against competition and those oligopolies use their media arms to give politicians glowing coverage."
A spokesperson for BCE declined to comment, referring questions to a telecom industry association. (BCE's media arm also owns and operates BNN Bloomberg Television, which has a content partnership with Bloomberg LP.)
Poilievre is following an election strategy that appeals to parts of his conservative base, as well as unaligned voters who are skeptical of big companies and the mainstream media. But those who've known him for years say he genuinely believes parts of corporate Canada have grown lazy and dependent on government regulation, protection or financial aid.
Risk tolerance
In the Conservative leader's view, instead of trying to improve their firms' competitiveness, too many business leaders spend their time lobbying government for tax credits and other favours. That message finds a receptive audience in a country where there's frustration with recently sluggish economic growth, weak productivity, high housing prices and soft private-sector investment.
If Poilievre wins power, he intends to tackle Canada's feeble record on business investment and competition, according to people familiar with the Conservative leader's thinking - though how far he's willing to go is the big question.
For example: Canadian law restricts foreign ownership in parts of the telecom, media, financial and transportation industries, which has resulted in a consolidation of power in a handful of companies such as BCE, Rogers Communications, Air Canada and the major banks. Previous governments have tried at times to stoke more competition, but they've mostly stopped short of major reforms to liberalize protected sectors.
It would be a mistake to assume Poilievre is making idle threats, according to Sean Speer, a former adviser to then-Prime Minister Stephen Harper, who's now an influential conservative commentator. Poilievre's speeches over the years show he deeply believes Canada needs a more competitive corporate sector, Speer said.
That may mean less protection for big telecoms and banks under a Conservative government, perhaps through foreign capital or other measures that would loosen the control of the largest players. "His risk tolerance when it comes to possibly opening up some of these industries might be higher than, say, the Harper government," Speer said. "This does seem to be a core part of his world view."
Balsillie, who said he's not a member of any political party but has been an outspoken proponent of a more ambitious approach to economic policy in Canada, said Poilievre and his team are "trying to figure out what to do" - but "I don't think status quo is on the table."
'Captured by Bay Street'
Poilievre's "crony capitalism" charge rings true to people like Balsillie, who has been critical of the close links between Trudeau's government and some executives at big companies in highly regulated industries. He cites the example of Navdeep Bains, who was Trudeau's industry minister, left politics in 2021, quickly landed at Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce and now works as a senior executive at Rogers - a job announced just weeks after the government allowed Rogers to acquire a large rival cable company.
The government has been slow to move on open banking, a regulatory framework that makes it easier for consumers to move around their financial information and use the services of new financial technology startups.
"Why after nine years do we still not have open banking?" Balsillie said.
"Who's had total keys to the throne on fixing that for nine years? It's clear that the Liberals have been captured by Bay Street."
But others who are critical of Canada's competition policies are also wary of how Poilievre may try to address them.
Simply trying to loosen government regulation might result in handing more power to the biggest companies, said Vass Bednar, an expert on digital public policy and a professor at McMaster University. She advocates for tougher competition rules and beefed-up regulatory agencies to enforce it.
"I don't think politicians should use competition policy to go after oligopolies that they don't like for political reasons," Bednar said about Poilievre's rhetoric on this issue.
"But I do think if he and his team are serious about challenging corporate power in a smart way, then it has to be more coherent or holistic - a more consumer-centric view, empowering independent business."
"I think those elements start to become a winning strategy," she said.
'Fix the budget'
Poilievre, as a clear front-runner entering the next election, is trying to keep his message disciplined. He doesn't give rambling speeches, stray off script or attack immigrants. He has defined the Conservatives' core issues as housing, taxes and crime - and left most of the details for later.
A Conservative government will get rid of a carbon tax on fossil fuels, but it's not clear what might replace it in the way of climate policy. His three-word fiscal policy slogan is "Fix the budget," but so far the Conservatives have produced no plans for how they might balance a budget that has been in deficit nearly every year since the financial crisis of 2008.
Within corporate Canada, many are plainly done with Trudeau, who has raised taxes on businesses and higher earners and imposed stricter environmental rules that have constrained the energy sector. So plenty of executives are willing to defend Poilievre.
"I don't think he's anti-business in the least," said Kim Moody, founder of Moodys Private Client and Moodys Tax, who describes himself as a friend of the Conservative chief.
"If anything, he's pro-private business and pro-worker."
The Canadian Federation of Independent Business, which focuses on representing small- and medium-sized companies, was on the sharp end of a Poilievre attack against "useless and overpaid lobbyists" earlier this year. Dan Kelly, its president, just shrugs. He has known the Conservative leader for more than a decade and has met with him dozens of times.
"He views the current government as an existential threat to the country and has expected the business community to be out there with that same message," Kelly said, adding Poilievre has repeatedly asked him to endorse the Conservatives. The CFIB refuses to do so, he said, because it needs to work with any elected government on issues affecting small business.
"While I didn't like some of the word choice, his comments are not a shock," Kelly said.
Some believe Poilievre will moderate his tone as he gets closer to winning government - but that may be a mistaken assumption.
"He's not constrained at all by public opinion on this kind of stuff," said David Coletto, who runs the polling firm Abacus Data. His public opinion research shows a large number of Canadians believe big companies are making unreasonable profits - making corporations a political target at a time when the cost of living is still one of the most important issues for voters.
Poilievre "is willing to push bounds and to describe things and to challenge orthodoxies," the pollster said, "in a way that I don't think we've seen in another individual who's likely to become prime minister."
Bloomberg
CREDIT: Brian Platt, Thomas Seal and Christine Dob Bloomberg

Document 241 of 500

'The person using that bike lane could be your doctor': Hundreds rally to protest Ford government's plan to remove bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/person-using-that-bike-lane-could-be-your-doctor/docview/3126483284/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+person+using+that+bike+lane+could+be+your+doctor%27%3A+Hundreds+rally+to+protest+Ford+government%27s+plan+to+remove+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loudly ringing their bells, and chanting "save our bike lanes," hundreds of cyclists rode along Bloor Street on Saturday afternoon to protest the Ford government's plan to rip out Toronto bike lanes and keep more from getting built.
The noisy but peaceful protest against the province's Bill 212 began with a rally at Christie Pits Park, with cyclists holding up signs saying "bike lanes save lives," and "bike lanes keep cars moving."
The bill - introduced in the legislature last month - would require municipalities to seek provincial approval to install bike lanes that mean removing a lane of vehicle traffic. Both Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria have also said the province could force the removal of existing bike lanes, including those on Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street, to reduce gridlock.
In one of the more sombre moments of the afternoon, NDP MPP Jessica Bell read an open letter to Ford and Sarkaria from Karen Orme, whose daughter Alex Amaro was struck and killed while cycling on Dufferin Street north of College Street in 2020.
"Minister Sarkaria, you say that this ... legislation is about giving people more time with their families. Our family would give anything to have more time with Alex. To hug her, to speak with her, to celebrate holidays and life milestones," Orme said in the letter read by Bell, as many in the crowd became emotional.
Radiologist Derek Tsang also told the crowd that bike lanes allow him to navigate between various downtown hospitals where he sees patients.
"I was getting around by bicycle so I can deliver timely care to the patients who need it," said Tsang. "Ontarians need to remember ... that the person using that bike lane could be your doctor, your personal support worker, or your DoorDash delivery person."
While most drivers on Saturday waited patiently as the cyclists rode past after the rally - in the bike lanes and on the road - some expressed their frustration.
"Hurry up. People have places to go!" shouted one driver, leaning on their horn.
"Lady, where do you think we're going to be riding when the bike lanes are gone?" one cyclist retorted while pedalling past, shaking their head.
For James Macfarlane, the fight against the province's Bill 212 is a matter of life and death for Toronto cyclists.
"Removing bike lanes won't remove cyclists. It will just put them in front of cars, in harm's way," said Macfarlane, who has vivid memories of run-ins with angry motorists.
"I had some driver try to run me off the road because I swore at him for parking in the bike lane," said Macfarlane, who cycled from his east-end home to join the protest.
Rahul Sharma, who bikes to his job at Yonge Street and St. Clair Avenue from his downtown home, said "For me, the bike lanes are about safety and access. When I lived in Scarborough, it really wasn't an option."
Organizers Eva Stanger-Ross and Zev Godfrey, who both bike to classes at the University of Toronto, pleaded for the provincial government to reconsider Bill 212, officially known as the "Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act."
Stanger-Ross argued that bike lanes make Toronto more accessible, and said the city should be allowed to proceed with its planned expansion of bikeways.
"I'm confident that the city's plans to build 100 kilometres of new bike lanes would've opened up the city to many others," Stanger-Ross said, to cheers from the crowd at Christie Pits.
Godfrey said cyclists and drivers need to have a better understanding of each other's needs.
"There's a really dangerous effort to frame the issue as us vs. them, and this just isn't productive or true," Godfrey told the crowd at the rally, to more loud cheers and bell-ringing.
"For many people, driving just is the best way to get around, just as for many of us here biking is the only or most-viable option."
CAPTION: Protesters hold signs at Saturday's rally at Christie Pits Park in support of bike lanes.A sign on a protester's bike as he joins hundreds of other cyclists along Bloor Street on Saturday afternoon.A cyclist brought his corgi dogs to Saturday's rally in support of bike lanes in Toronto.Cyclists fill the bike lane and roadway on Bloor Street after a rally on Saturdayto protest the Ford government's plans to remove bikeways and require municipalities to get approval before installing more.

Document 242 of 500

'I'm not concerned about hurting someone's feelings': New union leader promises 'aggressive' voice for officers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/im-not-concerned-about-hurting-someones-feelings/docview/3126483258/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+not+concerned+about+hurting+someone%27s+feelings%27%3A+New+union+leader+promises+%27aggressive%27+voice+for+officers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Planting himself smack in front of city hall, longtime Toronto cop Clayton Campbell looked straight into the camera, raised his index finger, and issued his warning to the politicians inside.
Pay officers "what they deserve" and properly resource the police force and you'll have no better friend than the union, he said.
But if you don't?
"I will expose you publicly every chance I get."
The message - delivered in his recent campaign ad for president of the Toronto Police Association - apparently resonated with its intended audience, the city's 8,000 front-line officers and civilians. Last month Campbell sailed to victory in a three-way race to be Toronto's new union boss, earning 65 per cent of the vote and clinching one of the most powerful policing positions in the country, complete with ready access to the ears of politicians including Premier Doug Ford.
After years of a kinder, gentler union approach, the city's cops are ready for a change in tone - and Campbell says he's going to give it to them.
"If it's aggressive, if it's more adversarial, if that needs be, that's the way it's going to be," he said in a recent interview with the Star. "Because I'm not concerned about hurting someone's feelings."
That hardball approach is prompting concerns of a chilling effect on efforts to rein in the ballooning police budget - and the timing is no coincidence. For the first time in more than 15 years, the union and the Toronto police board are heading into arbitration this month after contract negotiations broke down over the key issue of officer pay. Relations have soured in the weeks since, and a no-holds-barred union ad campaign, Keep Toronto Safe, warns there will be an exodus of officers because the board won't pay competitive wages.
"When our officers leave Toronto, it's communities like yours that are impacted," a radio ad released last week says, featuring an actor playing an exasperated homeowner who waited 10 hours for police after a break-in.
On Wednesday, Campbell launched his strongest offensive yet, calling for the resignation of Toronto police board chair Ann Morgan for allegedly breaching bargaining confidentiality in a letter to councillors about the contract dispute - an allegation the board denies.
Tack on recent incidents of officers hurt on the job - such as the shooting of a cop last month during a robbery investigation - and persistent public concerns such as long 911 wait times and Campbell says it's high time to restore the union to what it "once was and should be," as he wrote on social media during his campaign: "the most POWERFUL and INFLUENTIAL association in the country."
He believes he's the one to do it. From small-town northern Ontario, Campbell comes from a family of cops (his dad, two brothers and wife have all worn a badge). He joined Toronto police in 2000, after completing a biology degree at the University of Toronto, eventually becoming a sergeant in east-end's 55 division.
That's where, in 2017, Clayton got a taste for rabble-rousing, helping spur a grassroots protest at police headquarters over what he called "atrociously low" staffing levels, amid a short-lived hiring freeze that sought to rein in the budget and overhaul policing. Concerned about the safety of cops on his platoon, he and colleagues pushed back, lobbying for more staff by confronting senior leadership, including then-chief Mark Saunders.
Challenging the top brass extinguished his own senior officer ambitions - "I kinda knew that was the end of that career," he said - but it opened the door to union work. He joined the association as a director soon after, much of his time spent under the leadership of past-president Jon Reid.
Notwithstanding a handful of high-profile spats with Mayor Olivia Chow - the union and Toronto police launched an aggressive and successful fight against a $12-million cut to the police budget this year - Reid's time at the helm marked a period of relative calm for police union-city relations. Stressing he learned a lot from Reid, Campbell said his predecessor's style "was not to be as vocal."
Campbell's campaign catchphrase? "We are done being silent."
It hasn't been so long since the union voice boomed. Mike McCormack, the son of the late former police chief Bill McCormack who was TPA president until 2020, was a ready advocate, unafraid to call out politicians and leadership. In 2018, the union ran a full-page ad in the Star about emergency wait times, showing a laughing then-mayor John Tory alongside Saunders and the police board chair saying: "These guys are putting your safety on hold." "911" was written in spattered blood behind it.
That was tame compared to earlier union tactics. Craig Bromell, the controversial union leader in the late '90s and early 2000s, became known as the "Bully in Black," alarming politicians with his so-called "True Blue" telemarketing campaign. The initiative was widely perceived as an effort to raise public funds to smear councillors who weren't friendly to them, prompting then mayor Mel Lastman to accuse the "evil" union of holding the city hostage. Chow was a city councillor and police board member at the time.
Campbell told the Star his aim to bring back an in-your-face union will not include "any dirty type stuff. ... We are a professional organization, and we're going to stay that way," he said. But he will individually call out politicians who "are not making sure public safety is a priority," he said, noting he believes most Torontonians don't support reduced spending on policing.
"The constituents can then make a decision at election time," he said.
The public call-outs have started with those on the police board who, in Campbell's telling, are letting cops in Barrie and London collect bigger paychecks while working in smaller, more peaceful communities - ones without Taylor Swift concerts and major protests. Toronto officers need to be the highest paid in Ontario to attract and retain "honest, hard working, educated people with life experience," Campbell said.
"As more and more of your voters wait longer to have a 911 call answered, or wait 20-plus minutes for an officer to respond, or become victims of random acts of violence, do you really want to be on the side of a police service board that refused to do everything they can to invest in policing?" Campbell asks in a letter sent to all city councillors last week.
In her own letter to councillors last week, Morgan, the police board chair, said an offer declined by the union this year would have seen Toronto cops make the most in Ontario for two years of a proposed three-year contract.
Asked about Campbell's more "aggressive" approach, a spokesperson for the police board said Thursday they would "continue to work with the TPA and other stakeholders in a collaborative spirit to ensure adequate and effective policing, community safety, and the safety and wellness of our service members."
Critics say pointing to other services' contracts is an age-old tactic used by unions to leapfrog each other to become the highest-paid cops - and keep police budgets sky high. For years, the Toronto police budget has been the city's biggest expense, now well north of $1 billion. About 90 per cent of that goes to officer salaries and benefits.
Akwasi Owusu-Bempah, a University of Toronto associate professor of criminology who researches policing, said he is in favour of a well-paid police service, in part because it minimizes the likelihood of corruption by officers. But he stressed cops need to be fairly compensated in comparison to other public servants, and unions cannot assume the sky is the limit.
"Police salaries cannot escalate indefinitely, as they seem to do," he said.
Draining the public purse is a central way cop unions across North America are powerful barriers to change, and their potent advocacy can secure increased funding for police even while other public-sector work is cut back, York University researchers Mark Thomas and Steven Tufts wrote in a 2020 paper on police unions. Their conclusions included that police unions "repress racial justice movements that challenge police authority."
Unions were, indeed, the biggest opponents of calls to "defund the police" and redesign public safety during the racial reckoning following the 2020 police murder of George Floyd. When the global movement prompted a group of Toronto city councillors to push to slash the cop budget by 10 per cent, McCormack, then TPA president, blasted the move as "grandstanding" and warned 500 officers could be cut from the force if the motion passed (it failed 16-8).
John Sewell, the former Toronto mayor now with the Toronto Police Accountability Coalition, said the power yielded by cop unions means politicians can be scared off challenging police spending. Campbell's finger pointing outside city hall will hinder ongoing efforts to combat the central causes of disorder in Toronto, including homelessness and a growing mental health crisis, Sewell said - issues that cry out for social and community workers, not cops.
"The union throws its weight around so much that it means that politicians, elected people, are very, very frightened to speak out," he said.
Campbell says he is open to addressing social issues like mental health and addictions through solutions other than police; when officers are responding to these calls, it's often because other social agencies have failed, he said.
"We're always willing to look at alternatives, it's certainly not an either/or scenario," he said.
CREDIT: Wendy Gillis Toronto Star

Subject: Leadership; Presidents; Police reform; Public safety; Budgets; Murders & murder attempts; Contract negotiations; Politicians

Document 243 of 500

Pharmacists say new power comes with pressure: As government expands their role, the people behind the counter feel the need to make money

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacists-say-new-power-comes-with-pressure/docview/3126483202/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacists+say+new+power+comes+with+pressure%3A+As+government+expands+their+role%2C+the+people+behind+the+counter+feel+the+need+to+make+money&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Ontario government proposes expanding the list of "minor ailments" that pharmacists can treat - a plan the province says will ease strain on family doctors and emergency rooms - pharmacists themselves are sounding the alarm over the business practices of major chains, which they say pose risks to patient safety.
Fuelling the concerns are accusations that large chain pharmacies, which dominate the profession in Ontario, have stripped back support staff and issued quotas to pharmacists for OHIP-billable services. A February CBC exposé reported that pharmacists at Shoppers Drug Mart and other chains pressure patients to access unnecessary services including medication reviews, or "MedsChecks," an Ontario program allowing pharmacists to bill the government up to $75 for what often amounts to a phone call completed within a few minutes.
Earlier this year, Shoppers billed OHIP $1,423,900 for MedsChecks alone, in just one week.
Kathleen Leach, a Hamilton pharmacist, said expanding pharmacists' roles could degrade the care at major chains, as pharmacists working under service quotas are compelled to rush through their work, or face losses to their staffing and job security.
"Things get missed," said Leach, adding there is already widespread worry among pharmacists over the lack of support and staffing in corporate pharmacies.
A recent survey of nearly 4,300 Ontario pharmacy workers showed that over a third have felt pressure to rush through minor ailment assessments in under five minutes. According to Leach, the issues with MedsChecks are just one symptom of a bigger problem: growing corporate influence over the pharmacy profession.
The Ontario College of Pharmacists, the body responsible for regulating the province's pharmacists, is trying to address the issue. Surveys and town halls held by the OCP revealed that 85 per cent of pharmacists feel pressure to meet service quotas; a sizable majority of them, according to an OCP report, work at Loblaw and Shoppers pharmacies.
A separate report from B.C. found that these issues occurred in chain pharmacies at more than twice the rate of independent pharmacies. And Saskatchewan's pharmacy regulator concluded that "focus on (business) targets leads to errors and increased patient risks."
In April, pharmacists in Ontario filed a class-action lawsuit against Shoppers and its parent company, Loblaw, arguing that corporate practices such as stripping back staff and enforcing service quotas pose significant risks to patients.
"The class action lawsuit has no merit whatsoever and we will vigorously defend against it," said Loblaw, in an email. Pharmacists bear sole responsibility in deciding whether to provide a professional service, the company said, adding that in the wake of public concern, it has worked toward "a more collaborative approach to patient care."
Corporate planning documents sent to store owners confirm Shoppers continues to issue service and revenue targets for pharmacists, with targets in-place for 2025, including for MedsChecks, Naloxone kits, and minor ailment assessments.
Kyro Maseh, an independent pharmacist in Scarborough, says he's had to "rehabilitate" pharmacy students coming from corporate pharmacies, as they're used to rushing through their work.
"If you're doing a MedsCheck in three minutes, you're false billing," he said, noting that a proper MedsCheck should take 20 minutes to an hour to complete. Rushed assessments, he warns, can leave patients with a false sense of security, risking missed medical issues.
Annie Jaeger, a Shoppers Drug Mart customer in Peterborough, Ont., said lately she's felt increased pressure from her pharmacist to undergo health assessments, even for things she usually manages on her own.
"A lot of the things they're going to be prescribing for are things we can already manage over the counter," Jaeger said, adding that as opportunities for revenue-generation increase, so too does the potential for overbilling, unnecessary assessments and "lots of abuse."
Many pharmacists, including those worried about the effects of corporate quotas on their work, welcome an expanded role.
"We genuinely want to help," said Leach, who says she's excited to provide care to patients without having to refer them to clinics for every issue.
NDP Opposition Health Critic France Gélinas says she has no issue with giving pharmacists more responsibilities, but letting them bill OHIP for health services is problematic.
Gélinas pointed out that nurse practitioners provide the same services to patients without OHIP billing fees attached.
Her fellow NDP MPP Chris Glover said "Pharmacists have a lot of knowledge and skills which should be put to use. But that's not what's happening. What (Premier Doug) Ford has done is find a way to divert more tax dollars out of vital services and into the hands of companies like Loblaw."
Nick Tsergas is the editor of Canada Healthwatch and
a fellow in the Dalla Lana
Fellowship in Journalism and Health Impact at the University of Toronto.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's government proposes expanding the list of "minor ailments" that pharmacists can treat.Tijana MartinThe Canadian Pressfile photo
CREDIT: Nick Tsergas Special to the Star Nick Tsergas is the editor of and Canada Healthwatch a fellow in the Dalla Lana Fellowship in Journalism and Health Impact at the University of Toronto.;

Hundreds rally to save bike lanes: Noisy but peaceful protest at Christie Pits urges Ford government to reconsider new restrictions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hundreds-rally-save-bike-lanes/docview/3126483195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hundreds+rally+to+save+bike+lanes%3A+Noisy+but+peaceful+protest+at+Christie+Pits+urges+Ford+government+to+reconsider+new+restrictions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loudly ringing their bells, and chanting "save our bike lanes," hundreds of cyclists rode along Bloor Street on Saturday afternoon to protest the Ford government's plan to rip out Toronto bike lanes and keep more from getting built.
The noisy but peaceful protest against the province's Bill 212 began with a rally at Christie Pits Park, with cyclists holding up signs saying "bike lanes save lives," and "bike lanes keep cars moving."
The bill - introduced in the legislature last month - would require municipalities to seek provincial approval to install bike lanes that mean removing a lane of vehicle traffic. Both Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria have also said the province could force the removal of existing bike lanes, including those on Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street, to reduce gridlock.
In one of the more sombre moments of the afternoon, NDP MPP Jessica Bell read an open letter to Ford and Sarkaria from Karen Orme, whose daughter Alex Amaro was struck and killed while cycling on Dufferin Street north of College Street in 2020.
"Minister Sarkaria, you say that this ... legislation is about giving people more time with their families. Our family would give anything to have more time with Alex. To hug her, to speak with her, to celebrate holidays and life milestones," Orme said in the letter read by Bell, as many in the crowd became emotional.
Radiologist Derek Tsang told the crowd that bike lanes allow him to navigate between various downtown hospitals where he sees patients.
"I was getting around by bicycle so I can deliver timely care to the patients who need it," said Tsang. "Ontarians need to remember ... that the person using that bike lane could be your doctor, your personal support worker, or your DoorDash delivery person."
While most drivers on Saturday waited patiently as the cyclists rode past after the rally - in the bike lanes and on the road - some expressed frustration.
"Hurry up. People have places to go!" shouted one driver, leaning on their horn.
"Lady, where do you think we're going to be riding when the bike lanes are gone?" one cyclist retorted while pedalling past, shaking their head.
For James Macfarlane, the fight is a matter of life and death for Toronto cyclists.
"Removing bike lanes won't remove cyclists. It will just put them in front of cars, in harm's way," said Macfarlane, who has vivid memories of run-ins with angry motorists.
"I had some driver try to run me off the road because I swore at him for parking in the bike lane," said Macfarlane, who cycled from his east-end home to join the protest.
Rahul Sharma, who bikes to his job at Yonge Street and St. Clair Avenue from his downtown home, said "For me, the bike lanes are about safety and access. When I lived in Scarborough, it really wasn't an option."
Organizers Eva Stanger-Ross and Zev Godfrey, who both bike to classes at the University of Toronto, pleaded for the provincial government to reconsider Bill 212, officially known as the "Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act."
Stanger-Ross argued that bike lanes make Toronto more accessible, and said the city should be allowed to proceed with its planned expansion of bikeways.
"I'm confident that the city's plans to build 100 kilometres of new bike lanes would've opened up the city to many others," Stanger-Ross said, to cheers from the crowd at Christie Pits.
Godfrey said cyclists and drivers need to have a better understanding of each other's needs.
"There's a really dangerous effort to frame the issue as us vs. them, and this just isn't productive or true," Godfrey told the crowd, to more loud cheers and bell-ringing.
"For many people, driving just is the best way to get around, just as for many of us here biking is the only or most-viable option."
CAPTION: Cyclists fill the bike lane and roadway on Bloor Street after a rally on Saturday to protest the Ford government's plans to remove bikeways and require municipalities to get approval before installing more.Michelle Mengsu Chang Toronto Star

CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star

Doug Ford stop this crazy circus. We don't find it amusing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-stop-this-crazy-circus-we-dont-find/docview/3126412992/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+stop+this+crazy+circus.+We+don%27t+find+it+amusing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques, Oct. 30
What kind of a clown act is this - sending us back our own money intended for our welfare? Thanks, but no thanks. That $3 billion is our tax money. That money would go a long way in areas in desperate need of funding.
Frances Sedgwick, Toronto
Doug Ford doesn't understand cities
Lots of ink has been spilled over the years trying to understand Premier Doug Ford's ideology as right-wing, populist, or simply in it for the benefit of his friends. I'd venture into that discussion by adding that the former Toronto City Councillor doesn't understand cities or what makes them great. When Doug Ford blames cyclists for congestion, he reveals his ignorance of just how much less space a bicycle takes up compared to a car. The promise of the city is that, by sharing space with one another, we can have more while consuming less. We pool our commutes together on public transit and in bike lanes, and in doing so save money and time, while reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
While paying lip service to building more homes, he cannot wrap his head around this principle of sharing space with your neighbours. The premier referred to gentle density as "four-storey towers," claiming they would destroy "tight-knit communities." But there is more to a community than white picket fences and big garages. How can you love your neighbour, and insist that they stay as far away from you as possible? Doug Ford has too often meddled in the affairs of municipalities only to insist that they remain stagnant and under-represented. Now, after years of letting the housing crisis spiral out of control, he seems intent on invoking the notwithstanding clause to criminalize homelessness rather than build homes. Those of us who love our cities must punish him electorally for his anti-urban ideology.
Gabriel Blanc, Toronto
If Ford cared about our Greenbelt, he would do his utmost to protect it Ford government aims to fast-track construction of Highway 413, Bradford Bypass and Garden City Skyway, Oct. 17
In this context, to "fast track" sounds like to "disregard" any environmental impact assessment or any other important research that needs to be done before bulldozing our Greenbelt. If Premier Doug Ford genuinely cared about our Greenbelt, he would do his utmost to protect it and would do everything possible to determine what risks/hazards are involved with bulldozing it. (There's already an investigation being conducted by the RCMP of Ford's treatment of our precious Greenbelt.) This fast tracking of the proposed highway is bad news because it will destroy thousands of acres of farmland and hundreds of acres of our Greenbelt (wetlands, watersheds and wildlife habitat).
There is a worthy alternative to gouging the Greenbelt. Highway 407 is located only 15 km to the south of the planned route and follows the same east-west path with intersections at Highway 401 at Milton and Highway 400. The 407 is underused, could accommodate a lot of trucks (not a lot use it now because of the extreme toll costs).We could save billions of dollars by opening the 407 toll free to trucks and it would make traffic better on the 401 too. These billions could be better spent on important things like our health care.
Donna Deneault, Whitchurch-Stouffville
The Conservatives used to be the party of fiscal responsibility
A wise man once said, "taxes are the price we pay for a civilized society."Interesting, then, that Premier Doug Ford (head tax collector) is so against them that he prefers to hand out $200 pre-election bribes instead of using that money, and the rest of the taxes we pay, to actually improve Ontario and the lives of its residents with shorter wait times in our hospital emergency rooms, more teachers in our classrooms, more care in our long-term-care homes, and adequate social supports to eliminate the need for food banks and to actually deal with chronic homelessness and substance abuse.
Rarely has a government shown such a callous disregard for its citizens. If Ford's good governance had resulted in a budget surplus, or even a balanced budget, I could understand his desire to celebrate. But Ontario is still struggling with a massive deficit, at a time when the economy is limping along, at best. The Progressive Conservatives used to be the party of fiscal responsibility and good financial management. Now it's the party of silly tunnels, illegible licence plates, private spas on public parkland, beer in corner stores ... and $200 "vote for me" cheques.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto

Document 246 of 500

There are warning signs for Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre in Donald Trump's win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-are-warning-signs-justin-trudeau-pierre/docview/3126412827/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+are+warning+signs+for+Justin+Trudeau+and+Pierre+Poilievre+in+Donald+Trump%27s+win&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Don't let anyone tell you the re-election of Donald Trump is not a jolt to Canada's political landscape. Or that we've been there, done that.
The fallout is about to overwhelm the Canadian government's agenda for the next four years.
Just how it all plays out in a Canadian election expected at any time in the next year with a deeply unpopular Liberal prime minister and a right-leaning Conservative opposition leader way ahead in the polls is a central question.
There are certainly tactical lessons to be learned by Canadian political practitioners.
What's less clear is whether Donald Trump's victory represents a lasting political realignment south of the border and whether it signals a shift in Canadian political coalitions to come.
First, the early takeaways.
Liberals and Conservatives, along with other political parties, can draw three lessons from the United States election results.
Addressing cost of living anxiety is the political play that matters right now. A moralizing campaign - whether it's signalling how virtuous you are or how detestable your opponent is - doesn't cut it.And finally, "change" is a powerful vote driver. Incumbents are dropping around the world.
Those are the tactical takeaways that many politicos identified in the halls of Canada's Parliament, on television panels and analysis pieces, and in hot takes on social media.
Yet a surprising number of MPs and advisers trying to chart the path for leaders aspiring to the prime minister's seat warn against drawing too many comparisons between the Canadian and American political landscape.
In conversation after conversation with the Star most, including those who were granted anonymity because they were not authorized to publicly discuss political strategy, said the two countries, the campaigns, and the personalities of the main party leaders are too different to read too much into Trump's win and the Democrat's defeat.
But Fred DeLorey, the Conservative party's 2021 campaign director and a longtime party strategist, said that's "complete and utter nonsense."
DeLorey said the biggest lessons are for the Liberals, who he said are "heading down the exact same path the Democrats went down. They're trying to run the same playbook, the same messaging, the same everything. And it didn't work there. It's not going to work here."
The Biden-Harris Democrats "lacked a strong, powerful narrative on why they should be elected. Instead, it was like, things are bad, but our opponent's really bad."
DeLorey said negative comparisons between Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Trump won't work, as they are patently not the same. The Democrats, he said, had "real material to work with. The problem is, though, the positive message - why vote for us - for the Liberals and the Democrats is almost the same, in that there isn't one. There is no narrative (other) than we've got your back."
For voters, including the Black men, Latinos and women who shifted votes to Trump, who were worried mostly about the economy, Trump didn't offer a detailed plan, Delorey acknowledged, but at least he "talked about issues that they felt he would champion."
In fact, a senior Liberal cabinet minister who assessed the results early on the morning the Trump victory was called agreed with the broader point: that the Liberal party must focus its re-election bid on cost of living and affordability, and forget the rest.
"Inflation's a killer," he said. Voters will be driven by their household economic concerns and no campaign that relies on "moral superiority" - anything that looks like tone-policing, virtue-signalling, or culture wars - will succeed, the insider said.
Bill Clinton's campaign strategist James Carville coined the slogan "it's the economy, stupid." The phrase has found a whole new generation's ears in the past week.
Two other senior Liberal sources agreed that is the party's biggest takeaway from the U.S. election: that, as one put it, the economy is "number one, number one, number one."
"And it's not just in the United States where you've seen an election result like that, it's hard for incumbents everywhere right now," he said.
However, those insiders don't accept there is no value in attacking your opponent.
The second insider said it is "it is not good enough, although you have to do it, to only try to demonize your opponent. You also have to have a positive offering of your own which meets the moment, and you can't only have one of those things these days. You have to have both."
The bigger question about whether voting coalitions have irreversibly shifted is more complicated. Analysts with AP's VoteCast who combed through a deep dive on voter data said "big shifts within small groups and small shifts within big groups helped propel" Trump back to the White House and eroded the Democrats' voter base.
Some Conservatives here believe that is a sign of what's to come.
A Conservative MP said Poilievre has already expanded the Conservative coalition in the two years since becoming leader, drawing in more voters from Canada's working class, from unionized labour, and from ethnic or visible minority groups by focusing on inflation, the cost of living, jobs and affordability almost to the exclusion of all else.
Howard Anglin, a top staffer to then-Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper and to then-Alberta premier Jason Kenney, said Latino and East and South Asian voters, especially the men, "voted like issue voters rather than as 'ethnic' block voters," adding it bolsters the theory that there is a "multi-ethnic groundswell against out-of-touch progressive social policy." But Anglin said that was secondary to the overwhelming drive for change.
First and foremost, Anglin said, "it was a change election and Trump was the candidate of change and Kamala was the candidate of continuity."
"In 2020, Biden won independents in key swing states; in 2024, Trump won them. Trump also won the votes of "double haters" - people who said they didn't like either candidate - by 20 points.
"People voted for change ... because of policies that they can feel making their lives worse: illegal immigration, cost of living, crime, and general quality of life. Those are progressive Democratic policies and Kamala said she wouldn't change them."
André Turcotte, a pollster with Pollara Insights and an associate professor at Carleton University, agrees the anti-incumbent wave is strong, but takes a slightly different view, saying it's "too simplistic to simply say it's all about the economy."
"Identity was probably also a factor in the shifts that saw young men turn up and vote in greater numbers for Trump, Black men and Latinos supporting Trump," he said in an interview. As for whether that is a trend likely to be seen here, Turcotte said "I don't think we play identity politics the same way they do it."
He said Canada doesn't have "big racial - for lack of a better word - groups" that can be mobilized in the same way the U.S. taps into racial voting blocs, while identity in Canadian politics tends to fall along gender or social identity lines - for example, LGBTQ identity.
Turcotte believes the U.S. result makes it easier for Trudeau to resist calls for him to leave, because he can argue it didn't help Democrats to ditch Biden. It also complicates Poilievre's bid to replace Trudeau.
Pollara's research shows that half of Conservative supporters don't like Trump, but the other half does. So he's got to be careful, when for example he's chewing on an apple as he chews out a reporter. "He has to be conscious that he has many people in this coalition that really don't like that."
And yet, Turcotte observes, Democrat strategists bet big - but wrongly, it turns out - on the old axiom that "hope is better than anger." Instead, Trump's win shows that anger works. For the past few years, Pollara's polling has tracked a Rage Index in Canada. It shows Canadians are angry - about governments, institutions, media, wars, the housing market, you name it. So he suggests the Canadian political market is ripe for political parties to try to use that to political advantage.
"The anger is there," he said. "We say we're civil, until we're not."
In the end, what voter coalition can a Liberal government seeking its fourth mandate under Trudeau mobilize to resist the anti-incumbent forces, and not face Kamala Harris' fate?
Even the Liberals are wondering. Their base is still in urban and suburban Ontario, Quebec and B.C., with some semi-rural and rural support in Atlantic Canada, less so in Quebec. The Liberals have to recover support among the under 35-year-old youth vote to win the next election. Polls show it's going to be a tough hill to climb.
Conservative Fred DeLorey said voter coalitions are shifting, but he cautions all shifts are transactional at the outset, and can be elusive.
"For sure, there is a political realignment happening across the world, it seems, coming out of post-pandemic," said DeLorey. "But at the end of the day, it's going to be results based. It's going to be- will Trump and the Republicans deliver what these voter groups want. And that's what will determine whether this solidifies.
"Voters lend you their vote. They can take it back next election and people can move around. So it's all about whether they can deliver or not. And that's the same thing Poilievre is going to have when he wins. He's going to have to deliver. If they (voters) don't get the change they want or are happy with, they'll move back somewhere else."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will be looking to see what worked and what didn't for Donald Trump and Kamala Harris.

Subject: Hispanic Americans; Conservatism; Cost of living; Black people; Political parties; Anger; Political campaigns; Negative campaigning; Incumbency; Voters; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost of living

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Title: There are warning signs for Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre in Donald Trump's win

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 9, 2024
