Document 264 of 500

The Don River restoration took decades of dreaming - and was nearly derailed by Doug Ford. Inside the marvel of engineering that will transform the Port Lands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/don-river-restoration-took-decades-dreaming-was/docview/3126045572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Don+River+restoration+took+decades+of+dreaming+-+and+was+nearly+derailed+by+Doug+Ford.+Inside+the+marvel+of+engineering+that+will+transform+the+Port+Lands&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the world's biggest civil engineering projects reached a long-anticipated, critical milestone on Friday when the Don River was finally reunited with Lake Ontario through Toronto's inner harbour. Workers removed the last pieces of a concrete wall in a new river valley that had temporarily separated the bodies of water during construction.
"It really is a rebirth," said Mayor Olivia Chow at a ceremony Friday in the Port Lands marking the final connection of the Don to the lake. "We now have new fish species coming back, wildlife, indigenous plants coming back."
Chow was among almost 100 attendees - some of whom arrived by boat - who stood in the crisp fall air at the newly built mouth of the Don.
"Today we're standing on the shoulders of so many people," said local Coun. Paula Fletcher, referring to the advocates for the river's regeneration in the 1980s, some of whom were at Friday's ceremony. "If they hadn't cleaned the river up, we would really not want it to be flowing out to the harbour, would we?"
"I'd also like to recognize the humility of making a river," said Laura Solano, partner at Michael Van Valkenburgh Associates, the firm that designed the Don's new river valley. It's impossible to truly make nature from scratch. We can only dedicate ourselves to making the circumstances for nature to take hold."
Restoring the river to the course it originally followed for millennia is a feat of human engineering 35 years in the making. It's part of a $1.35-billion flood protection project in the Port Lands on the eastern waterfront to unseal more than 600 acres of land for development, including about 9,000 new homes. It also means 100 acres of much needed greenspaces and parks for downtown.
To get to this point has meant having to dig up 1.5 million cubic metres of soil - about a Rogers Centre's worth - to make room for a new 1.3-kilometre river valley created from scratch.
Getting it all done took decades' worth of advocacy, politics, transformative engineering and unconventional thinking.
The Don of timeFormed thousands of years ago by an ice age, the Don River is a 38-kilometre watercourse flowing from the Oak Ridges Moraine near Richmond Hill emptying into Lake Ontario through the Keating Channel in Toronto's eastern waterfront.
It's one of the most urbanized watersheds in Canada, covering a large swath of central Toronto, North and East York, Scarborough and York Region. That intense urbanization means roads, homes and paved surfaces cover soil and vegetation, leaving fewer permeable areas to absorb water. Instead, when it rains, stormwater runs off and finds its way into the Don River, often resulting in floods.
Urbanization has also meant the loss of the river's natural routes into the lake. Before the late 1800s, the Don originally ended with two mouths into Toronto Harbour: one near today's Keating Channel, the second farther south. Just east was Ashbridges Bay and its marsh - one of the largest wetlands in Eastern Canada, spanning more than five square kilometres.
Decline of the DonIn the 1880s, the southern mouth of the Don was cut out to allow for more harbour and industrial dock spaces. The Keating Channel was created in the late 1890s as an emergency response to connect Ashbridges Bay to the harbour. That allowed the bay's pollution - sewage, industrial and animal wastes that risked causing another cholera outbreak in Toronto - to be dispersed more quickly.
It meant the Don was strong-armed into flowing only into the Keating Channel in an unnatural 90-degree turn, increasing the risk of flooding in the area, as there was significantly less room for it to flow into the harbour.
In 1912, a joint federal-municipal government agency decided to transform Ashbridges Bay Marsh into a massive industrial and shipping district. Within 10 years, what is now known as the Port Lands filled that marsh and its shores, lining it with oil refineries, coal facilities and various factories.
Over the years, the soil became so severely contaminated and the river so polluted from industrial activity that a group of university professors and students hosted a funeral in 1969, marking the Don's decay.
A trickle becomes a flood
Friday's successful regeneration of the Don's mouth started with a group of volunteer advocates in 1989.
An event at the Ontario Science Centre with presentations about the potential for the river's rebirth attracted hundreds of people interested in cleaning up the Don.
As part of an environmental push at city hall, then city councillor Jack Layton announced, "'We're convening a group of citizens down at city hall, so come on down,'" recalled Mark Wilson, who later became chair of the group. "So I did."
Marie Day, who was also at the Science Centre event, said she played in the Don River when she was 10, decades before the Don Valley Parkway ran through the Lower Don, collecting fossils with her siblings and swinging on nearby sumac trees - like her father, former Toronto mayor Ralph Day, who also played in the ravine as a child.
"I didn't realize that it was being used as a dump," she said about why she took up Layton's offer. "I remember (as a kid) we actually found a salmon in the river ... all these creatures were desperately trying to hold on."
Soon after, 20 citizens ranging from architects and historians to activists and cartographers, and three city councillors formed a task force to figure out how to clean the polluted watershed and help Toronto see what the Don was and what it could be.
The group's ideas included recruiting volunteers, planting trees along the lower Don Valley and convincing high-profile corporate representatives to do the same.
But, "as we looked at the physical river, it became obvious you needed to do something more dramatic to bring back nature" than just de-polluting it, Wilson said, noting the river was inaccessible south of Riverdale and only became natural north of Gerrard. "How do we bring the natural river north of Gerrard to the south?"
The efforts evolved into today's massive restoration project, but it meant years of fighting naysayers and convincing politicians through smaller projects, such as revitalizing Chester Springs Marsh along the Lower Don Trail.
It also meant generating publicity and engaging the public: "We'd invite children to come down and walk along the Don trail," said Day. "Their parents would send angry letters saying, 'oh, my kids running shoes got soaked.'"
Sketching the vision
By 1991, the task force was an official city committee and its first report titled "Bringing Back the Don" hit council floor that summer. It was the first time anybody sketched a vision to reclaim the mouth of the river by rerouting it through the Port Lands, Wilson said.
Most thought the idea was "amazing, inspirational," he said, but also "there was this, 'You guys are nuts. This is totally impractical' from the technocrats and the bureaucrats."
That fall, a "pivotal" and "emotional" moment legitimized the idea, according to Wilson. During public consultations for the Royal Commission on the Future of the Toronto Waterfront, Wilson and Paula Davies, another member of the task force, played a recording of opera singer Mark DuBois' "Come to the Vale of the Beautiful Don" during their pitch to redirect the river.
It captured the imagination of the head of the commission, former Toronto mayor David Crombie. He ended up making the Don regeneration the centrepiece of his report.
The idea then became a question not of "if," but of when and how, said Wilson.
Around that same time, the Port Lands idea got a big boost from an unexpected source.
On an unrelated trip to Toronto, a 42-year-old Prince Charles was looking to support a community group advocating for the environment.
"Crombie and the gang recommended us," Wilson said, who ended up giving Charles a personal tour of the area. "Suddenly we had all the glamour of royalty supporting the Don regeneration."
But there were still the logistics - including the cost - of getting it done, which were daunting, Wilson said.
It would be another decade and a half before the vision inched closer to reality. The city held an international design competition in 2007 requiring four teams to give the Don "an iconic identity" in a "bold and comprehensive" design for the river mouth that protected the area from flooding, restored the habitat and integrated development.
These ideas on paper were brought to life by competition winner Michael Van Valkenburgh Associates (MVVA), a U.S. landscape architecture firm whose vision for the Port Lands included new parklands, a new island, a new river valley that can carry large volumes of stormwater - and the Don Greenway, another outlet for the Don during heavy floods.
When presented with what seems like an impossible task like redirecting a major river through Canada's largest city, "you hedge your bets," said Laura Solano, project director and partner at MVVA. "You surround yourself with people that can tell you whether or not your idea is even possible, and if so, how to make it enduring and graceful."
Fighting the Ford factor
Then, suddenly, politics nearly upended the whole project in 2011.
During his brother Rob's mayoralty, then-councillor Doug Ford announced on a radio show an alternative vision for the mouth of the Don because the accepted plans were taking too long: seize control of the Port Lands and build a monorail, a megamall and the world's largest Ferris wheel. A Star editorial then called it a "blatant waterfront cash grab."
It blindsided almost everyone.
Coun. Paula Fletcher (Ward 14, Toronto-Danforth) said it was the "most tumultuous" time in her 21 years on council, noting the Ford brothers' proposal was developed with external architects and developers in private, without council's approval or public input.
"We were going to grit our teeth and fight like hell," said John Wilson (no relation to Mark), who was chair of the Don task force when Rob Ford dismantled it just months before Doug's announcement.
Some dozen task force members spontaneously formed a new group that worked around the clock collectively, said Wilson, meeting with councillors, sitting around kitchen tables and board rooms after hours.
After two weeks of press conferences, spilled ink and a public uproar, city hall was inundated with thousands of letters, emails and signatures on petitions begging council to not "sell out our waterfront" for a "collection of ideas developed by an untested agency in the back rooms."
In the end, council voted unanimously to scrap the Fords' plan but called on Waterfront Toronto, the tripartite agency overseeing the project, to speed up development as a compromise.
The mouth takes shape
After money from the three levels of government started with $65 million in 2016, the first phase of construction began with the realigning of Cherry Street. The following year, $1.25 billion was allotted to the project, and Waterfront Toronto began preparations to break ground on the new river valley.
Between 2017 and 2018, Herb Sweeney, principal at MVVA and team captain for the project, said "a game changer" moment came when they visited Rouge River trying to understand the landscape there relative to their designs for the Don.
Initially they planned for the Don to empty into the lake in a curved L-shape. Rouge River made them realize that shape was "vastly insufficient" to allow enough space to build wetlands and match what was in other river mouths, Sweeney said.
Instead, making the river valley curve more would thicken and widen the wetlands bordering it, strengthening the available surface area into larger, more contiguous wetlands which better serve its necessary functions including habitat creation and stability in case of floods - exactly what was needed at the mouth of the Don.
So, they changed the river valley into more of an S-shape, Sweeney said, "immediately increasing the habitat value" and allowing a "rich, robust foundation for four large new wetlands."
Marshes act like filters and restoring them around the new mouth of the Don meant water that left the river through those wetlands would also come out cleaner into the harbour.
Digging in the dirt
After settling on the valley's shape, the next step was figuring out soil conditions. If workers were going to dig up a Rogers Centre worth of dirt, they had to know how badly it was contaminated, whether it could be repurposed once cleaned - and how stable its foundation was, said Steve Desrocher, Waterfront Toronto's director of soil and groundwater management.
"You have materials like concrete, brick, rubble, household waste, glass bottles and things like that mixed into (the soil)," on top of a long legacy of industrial activity, said Desrocher.
Workers looked at everything - from a 2004 environmental assessment that posed as the watershed's report card to fire insurance maps from the late 1800s.
But most important were the thousands of soil samples taken from the roughly 500 holes workers drilled into the ground, samples sent to a laboratory for chemical and physical analysis. This gave an idea of where and how bad the contamination was - and whether the soil should be sent to landfill or cleaned up before being reused.
To do this for every square inch of the site would take forever and be too expensive, said Desrocher. So using the samples and their lab results, workers created a reliable 3D computer model that predicted what the soil would be like at any given point.
"When we start digging, we use that blueprint to separate out those soils," said Desrocher.
While the techniques aren't rare, "it's not common for it to happen at this level of detail over this scale," he added.
About 65 per cent of the roughly 1.5 million cubic metres of soil excavated from the river valley was reused in the Port Lands, such as at the parks, while 35 per cent was sent to landfill.
Almost half of the soil that was reused did not require decontamination, while less than one per cent was subject to intense heat in custom-made steel boxes like giant ovens (called thermal remediation) and 16 per cent was oxygenated with various nutrients and compounds (bioremediation).
With bioremediation, "we're trying to increase the digestive rate of the microbes that are in the soil naturally," said Don Forbes, Waterfront Toronto's project director overseeing remediation and earthworks. "So we're adding nutrients, surfactants, moisture, oxygen to that soil to keep the microbes consuming the hydrocarbons that are the contaminants in the soil, like residual oils and gases."
All of this soil remediation, given the volume, "played an important role in our ability to reuse it, divert it from landfill and manage costs," said Forbes. "It also meant we had to import less soil."
Down in the valley
MVVA's Sweeney compares carving out the river valley to building a bathtub - it needs barriers to keep the excavation area dry and stable. After all, it's bounded by the Don River to the north, Lake Ontario to the south, Toronto Inner Harbour to the west and Ashbridges Bay to the east.
So the river valley was temporarily sealed off from Toronto Harbour, the Keating Channel, and the Shipping Channel using concrete walls, or plugs, leaving the construction site relatively dry, allowing them to use conventional methods, such as a bulldozer, to carve the river valley in the "most cost effective, efficient and expedient" way, said Sweeney. Construction in a wet area would require extra work and more complicated machinery.
Vertical walls along the entire length of the valley on both sides keep contaminated soil and groundwater out, while protective horizontal barriers at the bottom of this "bathtub" keeps contaminated groundwater out.
If contaminants get through these "impermeable, durable" barriers, Forbes said, there is another barrier made up of grains of gravel coated with bentonite (a highly absorbent, viscous plastic clay) and carbon-activated layers, like filters, to absorb them.
The river valley's proximity to Lake Ontario and the elevated groundwater meant workers had to install an underdrain system beneath the "bathtub" as well. This pumped water from underneath the valley to an on-site treatment plant and tossed that clean water into the lake during the digging. It also prevented the environmental barrier and river finishes from popping out of the ground before the river water was reconnected above.
"We couldn't remove all of the contaminated soils out of the Port Lands - that would've just been impossible, costly and irresponsible, to do," said Sweeney. "By creating that barrier, we're able to then bring stones, boulders, wood, planting soils and plants (above it) ... to stabilize the river channel bank (when water is poured into it)."
Construction of the river valley started in late 2020 and continued until early 2024 when it was filled with water.
So how are the lands and parks around the river valley able to survive if not all of the soil was decontaminated? A different, and more simple, environmental barrier was used. On top of it, workers dumped piles of scooped and cleaned soil (which once sat at the river valley site) to physically raise the parklands above flood levels.
"There is a three-foot layer cap on top of the contaminated soil that is made up of clean soil," which keeps contaminants from migrating, said MVVA's Solano.
Building bridges
With the river valley cutting across a massive piece of land, the portion north of the river valley (south of the Keating Channel) becomes an island - informally known as Villiers Island, Indigenous elders recently proposed naming it Ookwemin Minising, meaning "place of the black cherry trees."
That island, which will be developed into a new neighbourhood, requires new bridges and streets.
In fact, roads were worked on even before the river valley was dug up. First, workers had to shift Cherry Street so they could build the western temporary wall, removed this summer, that helped keep water out of the river valley construction site.
So the street was shifted a bit to the west and the new Cherry Street South Bridge built over the Polson Slip. The street now extends north over the Keating Channel, through the new Cherry Street North Bridge (the old one was demolished) and onto the mainland.
The newly opened Commissioners Street Bridge connects the island and the east downtown shoreline.
Shannon Baker, Waterfront Toronto's project director overseeing parks and public realm, said these three bridges - "architectural icons" painted red, yellow and orange on the inside - are also landmarks for the parks opening next year.
"When they're open, you'll always be able to orient yourself within the river valley system," said Baker.
Co-ordinating a whole host of construction sequences for the streets, river valley and parks was "one of the major challenges because everything impacted something else," added Baker.
Watching the river flow
By February this year, the river valley was ready to be filled with water after a year's worth of meetings, inspections and "a long list of what we need to think about," said MVVA's Solano. That included making sure the river slopes were stable, inspecting the environmental barriers, getting final approvals from regulatory agencies and preparing contingency plans for the possibility of winter bringing ice floes.
"Commissioning a building is normal. Commissioning a river is rare," said Solano. "You can't just Google it."
This meant devising a game plan for how workers would eventually remove the three temporary walls holding back the surrounding water.
"You have to hydrostatically balance the river level with the level of Toronto Harbour and the Don River," said Solano. "So there were some careful calculations about that relative to how fast can we fill the river? How long does it take?"
The west wall was essentially a dam to hold back Lake Ontario during construction, Sweeney said. Earlier this year it was sliced, partly under water, with a wire saw and scooped out in pieces.
The south wall near the Don Greenway was significantly smaller, with a gate to keep carp and other big fish out of the wetland, while protecting the habitat and allowing "desirable fish" to enter and spawn, Sweeney said.
The north wall near Villiers Street, the latest removed, was constructed differently. Up until Friday , workers had been grinding away at the wall and the steel beams holding it up.
A sign of hope
For decades, the Don's task force had to persuade multiple cycles of mayoral administrations, prime ministers and provincial dignitaries that the project was worth completing. "It doesn't just happen by magic," said the task force's Mark Wilson.
John Wilson, who joined the group in 1994 and later became chair, said he thought at the time re-naturalizing the mouth of the Don was "a pipe dream 50 years out."
For him, balancing nature with civilization and intensification, concepts that rarely go hand-in-hand in dense urban settings, is "a sign of hope that we're not doomed."
The restoration of the Don River, he said, means "humans don't necessarily have to be a plague on the environment."

Document 265 of 500

Faith leaders speak out in support of homeless shelters after opposition to new Scarborough site

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/faith-leaders-speak-out-support-homeless-shelters/docview/3126043576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Faith+leaders+speak+out+in+support+of+homeless+shelters+after+opposition+to+new+Scarborough+site&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A trio of faith leaders spoke at city hall Thursday in support of plans to build new homeless shelters - and to remind people concerned about them to love thy neighbour, no matter who they might be.
"We know from all our traditions that we have a duty to serve the needy," said Abdul Patel, an imam and Toronto District School Board trustee. "The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, said that a person whose neighbours go hungry is not among us (believers)."
Patel was joined at the podium by bishop Kevin Robertson and rabbi Shalom Schachter. Behind them stood about 30 of their peers from other religions, as well as the mayor and half a dozen councillors. They were invited by Coun. Alejandra Bravo (Ward 9 Davenport), who stayed in a shelter after arriving in Toronto as a refugee from Chile in 1974.
Thursday's news conference comes after 12 Ontario mayors agreed to Premier Doug Ford's offer to use the notwithstanding clause to help clear homeless encampments. Mayor Olivia Chow has refused, saying it would only serve to shuffle people between parks.
It also follows opposition from local residents to a new men's shelter at Gerrard Street East and Victoria Park Avenue, the location of which was leaked in a media report last week and confirmed to the Star by the area councillor. In the next nine years, Toronto aims to build 20 new shelters.
Bishop Kevin Robertson said that across religions, one of the most crucial commands from the divine is to care for the destitute, whom he said Jesus calls us "to love as ourselves, and serve as if we are serving Jesus Himself."
Fear of homeless people is unwarranted, the bishop said, adding that they are most frequently victims of violence, rather than perpetrators of it.
He and Chow agreed that forgoing shelter construction would only exacerbate the issue of homeless encampments in parks.
"Some of the same people who object to a homeless encampment in their local park are often the first to object when a shelter or affordable housing project opens nearby," said Robertson. "We must resist those tendencies in our city."
Local politicians have questioned the site selection for the Scarborough shelter, saying residents have raised "significant concerns" about it with them.
"My perspective on the shelter is that it is poorly sited and poorly situated to deliver key services to those it serves," said Coun. Parthi Kandavel (Ward 20 Scarborough Southwest) in a statement to the Star Thursday.
Kandavel said the site chosen by the city for the shelter, which is an empty storefront across from a field in a residential neighbourhood, is too close to an area daycare and preschool.
Doly Begum, NDP MPP for Scarborough Southwest, said in a statement posted to Instagram last week that while residents are worried about the "growing population of unsheltered and vulnerable people" in their community, they want some sort of "safety plan" involving police officers in order to feel comfortable with a shelter nearby. They also want to know the shelter's admission policies.
Bruce Davis, a housing consultant who has written reports for the city on shelters, said Torontonians who would otherwise be supportive of emergency housing can become "harsh" when a shelter is proposed near them.
Davis, who is also a former TDSB chair and trustee, recommended in 2017 that selection of the sites should be taken away from councillors and given to city staff, who aren't beholden to constituents and could act impartially. City council adopted his suggestion that same year.
"Councillors were in a completely untenable position," said Davis in an interview with the Star on Thursday. "You used to end up with lineups outside their offices, hours and hours of angry people."
Davis said the solution to neighbourhood discontent is found in respectful, regular dialogue with people he calls shelter "antagonists" - a term he said he prefers over NIMBY.
This consultation must happen independently of shelter site selection, which Davis said the public can be unhelpful in.
"Everyone wants to talk about the site - everyone will say, 'There's a better site,' but when you go to the 'better site,' the people there will say there's an even better one somewhere else," he said.
CAPTION: Faith leaders at city hall on Thursday said that forgoing shelter construction would only exacerbate the issue of homeless encampments in Toronto.

An audit of all Ontario school boards + his new home was denied a warranty over a baffling 'loophole'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/audit-all-ontario-school-boards-his-new-home-was/docview/3126042334/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, November 8 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on an audit of all provincial school boards, a "loophole" in Ontario's new home warranty and Olivia Chow fighting Doug Ford's bike lane plan.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford is ordering an audit of all Ontario school boards
Provincial school boards' discretionary spending will be put under a microscope after three employees of the Lambton Kent District School Board took a $32,000 trip to a conference in Hawaii. The visit was paid for by a fund for Indigenous education and characterized as a "once-in-a-career opportunity," Kristin Rushowy reports. But officials would rather the money be spent in schools instead.
Wait, what? Four boards have recently landed in hot water over pricey trips, including a $145,000 visit to Italy taken by four Brantford Catholic trustees, a $40,000 retreat for senior staff from the Thames Valley board and a $16,000 staff retreat by the London Catholic Board.Meanwhile: Twelve Ontario school boards and two private schools are suing Snapchat, TikTok, Facebook and Instagram for more than $8 billion, alleging the social media platforms harmed kids' mental health and wellbeing.His new house was denied a warranty over a baffling 'loophole'
Kyrollos Maseh was relieved to learn he qualified for compensation after he said his contractor left his new home riddled with defects. But that compensation was denied two months later because his builder hadn't done enough work on the house, Sheila Wang reports. Maseh sought protection for the deficiencies he says were left by the contractor - but those same deficiencies meant he didn't qualify for a warranty. What the heck is going on?
A bizarre "loophole": All new homes in Ontario are provided with a warranty backstopped by Tarion, Ontario's protection agency. But a contract home is only entitled to a payout if it's 97 per cent complete - a benchmark not stipulated in the Ontario New Home Warranties Plan Act or on Tarion's website.A deeper issue: Tarion says it's been reformed since a scathing auditor general report in 2019 found it put the interests of builders ahead of consumers. But these seven new home buyers say otherwise.Olivia Chow is mulling how to fight Ford's bike lane plan
Toronto's mayor is looking for options to fight Ford's plans to tear up Toronto's bike lanes, Ben Spurr writes. In a council motion Thursday, Chow said a new bill granting the province power to remove bikeways would, "at tremendous cost to the taxpayer and without collaboration, undo another government's work." The motion didn't outline how the city could confront Ford's decision, however. Take a look at what's being done.
ICYMI: Ford recently posted a blueprint for tearing out bike lanes on sections of Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue. Here's how it could affect your commute.Public opinion: A new poll suggests a majority of Ontarians support Ford's plan to curb municipal bike lanes by a two-to-one margin. Take a closer look at the numbers.WHAT ELSE
Conservatives are seeing a slight dip in polls, but maintain their commanding lead over the Liberals. Here are the latest numbers.Toronto's police union is calling for the resignation of the police board chair over a letter they say "breached" confidentiality.The NDP is demanding a right-wing influencer be called before Parliament. Here's why.City councillors are asking for a salary review in the wake of a shelved pay hike.Toronto is offering new condos to public housing residents, no down payment required. So why aren't there any takers?Donald Trump says he will "stop wars." The world is waiting to see how he follows through.A 13-year-old Ajax cyclist has died after being hit at a crosswalk by a driver.One person has been shot dead and an OPP officer was stabbed in Orillia, the SIU reports.Try these four Toronto restaurants celebrity chef Matty Matheson misses when he's out of the city.Do you have Taylor Swift fatigue? Sick of hearing about the Eras Tour? These local Swift skeptics share your pain.Taylor Swift scavenger hunts at the ROM, cyclists protest and GO train closures: Here's what's happening in Toronto this weekend.Three people have been charged in the death of ex-One Direction member Liam Payne.POV
Trump won the working-class vote - and Poilievre's Conservatives are well on their way to doing just that.CLOSE-UP
HUMBER BAY SHORES PARK: Artist Damian Agostini charmed locals with his collection of whimsical driftwood sculptures along the waterfront. But the city has ordered him to take them down. Take a look at the beloved creations before they're gone.

Document 267 of 500

Toronto councillors ask for salary review in the wake of shelved pay hike recommendation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-councillors-ask-salary-review-wake/docview/3125955437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+councillors+ask+for+salary+review+in+the+wake+of+shelved+pay+hike+recommendation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto councillors are asking city staff to review their office salaries, a proposal that could eventually lead to a raise for council members and their staff.
In a motion headed to next week's council meeting, Coun. James Pasternak (Ward 6, York Centre) asks staff to complete an updated review of councillors' pay and make recommendations to "adjust" it to a "level in line with counterparts" in other cities.
The motion seconded by Coun. Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre) also requests staff to report back on "options to modernize the job descriptions, salary bands and the compensation envelope for staff" in councillors' offices.
The motion, which will require the support of two-thirds of members to be put to a vote at next week's meeting, follows an Oct. 28 Star story that revealed Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson quietly shelved a report last year that recommended councillors get a raise of more than 22 per cent.
The report was based on a 2023 jurisdictional review using findings of third-party consultants. It determined that on a per-constituent basis, Toronto councillors made less than municipal representatives of any other city included in the study. Councillors in Canada's largest municipality earned about $128,000 last year, and the last time council approved increases beyond automatic inflationary adjustments was in 2006.
In an interview, Pasternak said he and other councillors were "taken aback" to learn from the Star that there had been an "internal decision" not to bring the report to council last year.
His motion notes that in 2018 Premier Doug Ford's government slashed the size of council from 44 to 25 wards, making each councillor responsible for a much larger area. The average population of each ward is about 120,000. That year council voted to ask staff to recommend "an appropriate level of compensation" for councillors, but a recommendation was never brought back for a vote.
Pasternak stressed that his motion "does not ask for a raise" and that he would have to see the staff recommendations before deciding whether to vote for an increase.
The councillor acknowledged "there is no good time" for elected officials to talk about getting paid more, and "these are not easy times" for many residents. But he said it's important that councillors and their staff are paid competitively in order to attract people to run for office and ensure that employees responsible for serving constituents don't leave for the private sector.
"We're simply asking for a report that that was due, well, six years ago," Pasternak said.
His motion asks staff to report back on councillors' pay at the December council meeting, and on office staff pay by the end of next year. It also directs staff to come up with a plan to "regularly review councillor compensation."
Chow told the Star last month that she and Johnson stopped the earlier report from going to council soon after she took office in 2023 because she didn't think the city should be raising councillors' salaries during a budget crisis. She also argued it was inappropriate for members to vote on their own pay.
Asked Thursday whether the mayor would support Pasternak's motion, Chow's press secretary Arianne Robinson declined to comment.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson quietly shelved a report last year that recommended city councillors get a raise of more than 22 per cent.

Document 268 of 500

Take it from the leaders of these top unions: The workplace is not what it once was

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/take-leaders-these-top-unions-workplace-is-not/docview/3125955353/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Take+it+from+the+leaders+of+these+top+unions%3A+The+workplace+is+not+what+it+once+was&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They're not your typical union "brothers" from central casting.
Nor does Doug Ford deride them as union "bosses" any longer. Times change.
Today, these three union veterans at the top of the labour movement represent a new wave of democratically elected leaders fighting for working women and men:
Lana Payne is president of Unifor, Canada's biggest private sector union with 315,000 members.
Bea Bruske heads the Canadian Labour Congress, representing three million workers in unions across the country.
JP Hornick helms the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, with 180,000 workers.
All three were onstage together at the Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University to talk about the future of work for women and men. And the fate of the union movement.
From artificial intelligence to remote work, the workplace is not what it once was (or where it once was, if you're working from home). Future changes will impact the ability of organized labour to organize workers - sign up members - while still protecting the jobs of existing members.
Payne describes AI as "tech change on steroids right now - and it's happening in every single workplace that we have."
The impact is undeniable, whether it's workers being edged out by machines or bots. But based on Unifor's experience in the auto sector, Payne believes that reflexively opposing technological change leads to a dead end.
"I don't think there's a sexy solution," she said. "Ultimately, workers have to be part of these discussions, and I'd say the change is happening so quickly that in some cases they're not - and so they're being used as guinea pigs."
The CLC's Bruske agrees that unions should remain open to technology, but they must also open their eyes to the risks of technology being turned against people. The problem arises when AI is used for surveillance of workers.
There's a similar paradox with the rise of remote work. The battle lines are drawn between employers who want workers back in the office versus public sector unions that are pushing back.
When I asked how these three presidents deal with their own support staff wanting to work from home, the answers were instructive: Be careful what you wish for.
"I like seeing my coworkers at work, I've got to be honest," Bruske replied. "I like being able to have a quick conversation as you're grabbing that next cup of coffee ... having that collaboration."
Payne told our campus audience that about one in five Canadians now work remotely in some form, compared to four per cent pre-pandemic. Personally, she's conflicted.
"Just needing to have human interaction in your day, I think is really important," the Unifor leader mused. "We have to be cautious of how remote work can be used against us as a way to outsource work to other parts of the world."
At Unifor, workers are often on the job remotely, "but I do like seeing them in the office, I have to say."
At OPSEU, most members are front-line workers, which means remote work remains a remote possibility.
"Remote work tends to be a very class-based phenomenon," Hornick observed. "I've got corrections officers. I'm pretty sure none of us want them working remotely."
Another challenge for unions: If workers aren't in the workplace, union organizers miss out on the chance to sign up members in one convenient location.
Fewer than 29 per cent of all Canadian workers belong to a union today. In the private sector, where the labour movement got its start, barely 15 per cent are union members - roughly half the penetration rate in 1981.
Beyond that discouraging historical trend line, the recent past has also been unkind to union leaders. Despite a charm offensive by the premier in recent years, Hornick remains unmoved:
"Doug Ford is the premier of stuff, not people," Hornick said flatly.
When Ford tried to legislate away the right to strike for CUPE education workers in late 2022 - by suspending Charter rights - unions banded together. Even though Unifor wasn't directly involved, Payne told the Democracy Forum how she confronted the government with bracing language:
"What the f-k do you think you're doing here?" she demanded of the premier's office.
The Unifor president warned that workers all across the province would walk off the job in a solidarity strike.
"We were headed towards a general strike in Ontario," she said. "Unifor was all in - and that meant industry was going to feel the pain."
Facing an ultimatum, Ford blinked.
But labour solidarity only goes so far. While the CLC supposedly represents all unionized workers, Unifor hasn't been part of the umbrella organization for several years.
"Listen, I love sitting right next to my union sister here and ... when it counts, we are all together," Bruske said diplomatically from the stage.
Payne smiled broadly but said little about the institutional estrangement.
All that said - and left unsaid - when the TMU Democracy Forum was done, Bruske and Payne walked out together for lunch. Their unspoken negotiating strategy was to break bread and build unity at their own bargaining table for two.
(Disclosure: Toronto Star journalists are represented by Unifor. I'm a senior fellow at TMU's Dais.)
CAPTION: Labour's New Leadership: Unions and the Future of WorkToronto Star coiumnist Martin Regg Cohn, left, speaks with (left to right) Unifor national president Lana Payne, Canadian Labour Congress president Bea Bruske and Ontario Public Service Employees Union president JP Hornick at the TMU Democracy Forum on Oct. 30, 2024.

Subject: Work at home; Presidents; Democracy; Union leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Work at home Union leadership

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Labour Congress; NAICS: 813930; Name: Ontario Public Service Employees Union; NAICS: 813930

Title: Take it from the leaders of these top unions: The workplace is not what it once was

Document 269 of 500

'We have a duty to serve the needy': Faith leaders speak out in support of homeless shelters after opposition to new Scarborough site

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-have-duty-serve-needy/docview/3125955033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+have+a+duty+to+serve+the+needy%27%3A+Faith+leaders+speak+out+in+support+of+homeless+shelters+after+opposition+to+new+Scarborough+site&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A trio of faith leaders spoke at city hall Thursday in support of plans to build new homeless shelters - and to remind people concerned about them to love thy neighbour, no matter who they might be.
"We know from all our traditions that we have a duty to serve the needy," said Abdul Patel, an imam and Toronto District School Board trustee. "The prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, said that a person whose neighbours go hungry is not among us (believers)."
Patel was joined at the podium by Bishop Kevin Robertson and rabbi Shalom Schachter. Behind them stood about 30 of their peers from other religions, as well as the mayor and half a dozen councillors. They were invited by Coun. Alejandra Bravo (Ward 9 Davenport), who stayed in a shelter after arriving in Toronto as a refugee from Chile in 1974.
Thursday's news conference comes after 12 Ontario mayors agreed to Premier Doug Ford's offer to use the notwithstanding clause to help clear homeless encampments. Mayor Olivia Chow has refused, saying it would only serve to shuffle people between parks.
It also follows opposition from local residents to a new men's shelter at Gerrard Street East and Victoria Park Avenue, the location of which was leaked in a media report last week and confirmed to the Star by the area councillor. In the next nine years, Toronto aims to build 20 new shelters.
Robertson said that across religions, one of the most crucial commands from the divine is to care for the destitute, whom he said Jesus calls us "to love as ourselves, and serve as if we are serving Jesus himself." Fear of homeless people is unwarranted, the bishop said, adding that they are most frequently victims of violence, rather than perpetrators of it.
He and Chow agreed that forgoing shelter construction would only exacerbate the issue of homeless encampments in parks.
"Some of the same people who object to a homeless encampment in their local park are often the first to object when a shelter or affordable housing project opens nearby," said Robertson. "We must resist those tendencies in our city."
Local politicians have questioned the site selection for the Scarborough shelter, saying residents have raised "significant concerns" about it with them.
"My perspective on the shelter is that it is poorly sited and poorly situated to deliver key services to those it serves," said Coun. Parthi Kandavel (Ward 20 Scarborough Southwest) in a statement to the Star Thursday.
Kandavel said the site chosen by the city for the shelter, which is an empty storefront across from a field in a residential neighbourhood, is too close to an area daycare and pre-school.
Doly Begum, NDP MPP for Scarborough Southwest, said in a statement posted to Instagram last week that while residents are worried about the "growing population of unsheltered and vulnerable people" in their community, they want some sort of "safety plan" involving police officers in order to feel comfortable with a shelter nearby. They also want to know the shelter's admission policies.
Bruce Davis, a housing consultant who has written reports for the city on shelters, said Torontonians who would otherwise be supportive of emergency housing can become "harsh" when a shelter is proposed near them.
Davis, who is also a former TDSB chair and trustee, recommended in 2017 that selection of the sites should be taken away from councillors and given to city staff, who aren't beholden to constituents and could act impartially. City council adopted his suggestion that same year.
"Councillors were in a completely untenable position," said Davis in an interview with the Star on Thursday. "You used to end up with lineups outside their offices, hours and hours of angry people."
Davis said the solution to neighbourhood discontent is found in respectful regular dialogue with people he calls shelter "antagonists" - a term he said he prefers over NIMBY. This consultation must happen independently of shelter site selection, which Davis said the public can be unhelpful in.
"Everyone wants to talk about the site - everyone will say, 'There's a better site,' but when you go to the 'better site,' the people there will say there's an even better one somewhere else," he said.
CAPTION: Faith leaders speaking Thursday at city hall said forgoing shelter construction would only exacerbate the issue of homeless encampments in Toronto.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

Subject: Homeless encampments; Site selection; Mayors; Parks & recreation areas; Shelters; Affordable housing; Homeless people

Document 270 of 500

Trump won the working class vote - and Poilievre could too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-won-working-class-vote-poilievre-could-too/docview/3125954406/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+won+the+working+class+vote+-+and+Poilievre+could+too&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like a lot of Canadians, I thought I knew quite a bit about the United States.
After all, I lived there for five years. I reported from almost half the states and travelled in most of the others. I was married to an American for 45 years and spent time with her family in Hawaii, California and Colorado. My son is a dual citizen. I even speak their language, more or less.
Obviously, I don't know as much as I thought I did. I thought Kamala Harris had a good shot at winning on Tuesday. Donald Trump's take-that victory came as a surprise - not so much that he won, but that the voters' verdict was so decisive. I'm struggling to figure out why.
There are basically two choices if you're in this situation: condemn or try to understand.
I see quite a few people taking door number one, what I'd call the "Apocalypse Now" approach. You're going to read a lot of this stuff over the next few weeks - adjective-laden pieces lambasting Trump as a lying, bullying, race-baiting, fascistic, etc. ... Or blaming his victory on flaws in the American people themselves, on the racism, misogyny and sheer ignorance allegedly baked into the benighted population beyond the reach of the New York Times, Washington Post and so on.
Right now some of that is taking the form of beating up on tranches of voters who didn't follow the script progressives had laid out for them. Sizable numbers of Black men and especially Latinos went for Trump. And many women, in particular white women, did the same despite the Democrats' call to vote on abortion rights above all.
Blaming voters for a result you don't like is obviously a dead end. As the New York Times writer David Brooks observes, the people indulging in that apparently "love losing and want to do it again and again and again." So, not smart.
The alternative is trying to understand electoral choices that seem inexplicable from afar. This comes with the risk of being seen to justify or "normalize" support for that lying, bullying, race-baiting and so on candidate, or for the misguided world view of his supporters. But it has to be done.
That discussion is just starting. It's dawning on Democrats that their mainly coastal, urban, affluent party is fundamentally out of tune with American voters. They've become the voice of the establishment, defending institutions at a time when the popular mood favours tearing them down and starting over. Somehow, they let Trump become the champion of those who feel cast aside or, worse, disrespected by the system.
They didn't take that message on board when Trump won the first time. But after this second victory, when no one could claim to be ignorant of the risks, it better sink in.
One strain of this thinking comes from the likes of Bernie Sanders, who has long urged Democrats to stop defending a status quo that isn't working for many Americans. "It should come as no great surprise," he says, "that a Democratic Party which has abandoned working class people would find that the working class has abandoned them."
But it will take more than different economic policies to woo ordinary people away from the likes of Trump. Many feel they've been cast off by the system and their concerns have been ignored. Brooks calls it a "crisis of respect," and it will take a fundamental rethink to address it.
There are lessons for Canadian politicians in all this. Of course, the personalities are very different and our political system doesn't work the same way America's does. Pierre Poilievre is no Donald Trump but he's trying to do something along the same lines as Trump - steal the votes of working-class "ordinary Canadians" away from parties that should be speaking to their concerns, the Liberals and NDP.
Polls show Poilievre's Conservatives are well on the way to doing that by focusing relentlessly on basic issues like housing and the cost of living. If those trends continue into the next election, Canada's progressive parties will find themselves having to do the same kind of painful soul-searching as their cousins to the south.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach via email: aphillips@thestar.ca;

Document 271 of 500

Organized labour's new challenges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/organized-labours-new-challenges/docview/3125954370/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Organized+labour%27s+new+challenges&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They're not your typical union "brothers" from central casting.
Nor does Doug Ford deride them as union "bosses" any longer. Times change.
Today, these three union veterans at the top of the labour movement represent a new wave of democratically elected leaders fighting for working women and men:
Lana Payne is president of Unifor, Canada's biggest private sector union with 315,000 members.
Bea Bruske heads the Canadian Labour Congress, representing three million workers in unions across the country.
JP Hornick helms the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, with 180,000 workers.
All three were onstage together at the Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University to talk about the future of work for women and men. And the fate of the union movement.
From artificial intelligence to remote work, the workplace is not what it once was (or where it once was, if you're working from home). Future changes will impact the ability of organized labour to organize workers - sign up members - while still protecting the jobs of existing members.
Payne describes AI as "tech change on steroids right now - and it's happening in every single workplace that we have."
The impact is undeniable, whether it's workers being edged out by machines or bots. But based on Unifor's experience in the auto sector, Payne believes that reflexively opposing technological change leads to a dead end.
"I don't think there's a sexy solution," she said. "Ultimately, workers have to be part of these discussions, and I'd say the change is happening so quickly that in some cases they're not - and so they're being used as guinea pigs."
The CLC's Bruske agrees that unions should remain open to technology, but they must also open their eyes to the risks of technology being turned against people. The problem arises when AI is used for surveillance of workers.
There's a similar paradox with the rise of remote work. The battle lines are drawn between employers who want workers back in the office versus public sector unions that are pushing back.
When I asked how these three presidents deal with their own support staff wanting to work from home, the answers were instructive: Be careful what you wish for.
"I like seeing my coworkers at work, I've got to be honest," Bruske replied. "I like being able to have a quick conversation as you're grabbing that next cup of coffee ... having that collaboration."
Payne told our campus audience that about one in five Canadians now work remotely in some form, compared to four per cent pre-pandemic. Personally, she's conflicted.
"Just needing to have human interaction in your day, I think is really important," the Unifor leader mused. "We have to be cautious of how remote work can be used against us as a way to outsource work to other parts of the world."
At Unifor, workers are often on the job remotely, "but I do like seeing them in the office, I have to say."
At OPSEU, most members are front-line workers, which means remote work remains a remote possibility.
"Remote work tends to be a very class-based phenomenon," Hornick observed. "I've got corrections officers. I'm pretty sure none of us want them working remotely."
Another challenge for unions: If workers aren't in the workplace, union organizers miss out on the chance to sign up members in one convenient location.
Fewer than 29 per cent of all Canadian workers belong to a union today. In the private sector, where the labour movement got its start, barely 15 per cent are union members - roughly half the penetration rate in 1981.
Beyond that discouraging historical trend line, the recent past has also been unkind to union leaders. Despite a charm offensive by the premier in recent years, Hornick remains unmoved:
"Doug Ford is the premier of stuff, not people," Hornick said flatly.
When Ford tried to legislate away the right to strike for CUPE education workers in late 2022 - by suspending charter rights - unions banded together. Even though Unifor wasn't directly involved, Payne told the Democracy Forum how she confronted the government with bracing language:
"What the f--k do you think you're doing here?" she demanded of the premier's office.
The Unifor president warned that workers all across the province would walk off the job in a solidarity strike.
"We were headed towards a general strike in Ontario," she said. "Unifor was all in - and that meant industry was going to feel the pain."
Facing an ultimatum, Ford blinked.
But labour solidarity only goes so far. While the CLC supposedly represents all unionized workers, Unifor hasn't been part of the umbrella organization for several years.
"Listen, I love sitting right next to my union sister here and ... when it counts, we are all together," Bruske said diplomatically from the stage.
Payne smiled broadly but said little about the institutional estrangement.
All that said - and left unsaid - when the TMU Democracy Forum was done, Bruske and Payne walked out together for lunch. Their unspoken negotiating strategy was to break bread and build unity at their own bargaining table for two.
DisclosureToronto Star journalists are represented by Unifor. I'm a senior fellow at TMU's Dais.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 272 of 500

Most back bike-lane law, poll finds: Residents across province support Ford's limits, along with promised $200 'tax rebate' cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/most-back-bike-lane-law-poll-finds/docview/3125954343/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Most+back+bike-lane+law%2C+poll+finds%3A+Residents+across+province+support+Ford%27s+limits%2C+along+with+promised+%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+cheques&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians support Premier Doug Ford's controversial bid to curb municipal bike lanes by a two-to-one margin, a new poll suggests.
Abacus Data also found 72 per cent of respondents back Ford's $200 "tax rebate" cheques, although about half believe the $3 billion would be better spent on improving public services such as health care or education.
A majority - 55 per cent - approved of the premier's proposal to force municipalities to get provincial approval for bike lanes that would interfere with vehicular traffic.
Ten per cent said it's a "very good idea" with 15 per cent saying it was a "good idea" and 30 per cent an "acceptable idea."
Only 28 per cent disagreed - 16 per cent said the provincial edict, which will result in existing bike lanes being removed on University Avenue, Bloor Street and Yonge Street in Toronto, was a "bad idea" and 12 per cent said it was a "very bad idea" - with 17 per cent unsure.
Just one per cent of Torontonians surveyed said they bike to work.
Abacus president David Coletto said the premier, who is known to pore over his Progressive Conservatives' own internal polling by Campaign Research, is clearly addressing the "scarcity mindset."
"If you look at these two issues - both the bike lane question and the rebate - Ford is so far being very effective at inoculating himself and his government from these forces that are causing governments across the developed world to lose vote share," said Coletto.
"These two decisions go right at the heart of a government that is relentlessly focused on responding to what people are saying are the most important things for them - and it's all tied to scarcity," the pollster said.
"I say scarcity of money in terms of affordability ... and then on scarcity of time, which is tied to traffic and the chaos that is Toronto and the GTA."
Abacus surveyed 998 Ontarians from Oct. 31 through Tuesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford's Tories, who are considering an election next spring, were at 42 per cent support - ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
The poll found 68 per cent of Ontarians commute in their personal car or truck, while 18 per cent take public transit, nine per cent walk, four per cent take Uber or a taxi and two per cent cycle.
In Toronto, 44 per cent drive, 36 per cent take transit, 15 per cent walk, four per cent take a ride share and one per cent bike.
In the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, 75 per cent drive, 12 per cent use transit, nine per cent walk, three per cent take a ride share and one per cent cycle.
(The provincial average for biking is higher than in Toronto or the GTHA because four per cent of Eastern Ontario respondents, most of them in Ottawa, take their bicycles to work.)
When asked "how often do you ride a bike and share a road with cars," 10 per cent of Ontarians said "regularly," 22 per cent said "occasionally," 21 per cent said "rarely" and 47 per cent said "never."
In Toronto, 12 per cent said "regularly," 30 per cent said "occasionally," 21 per cent said "rarely" and 37 per cent said "never."
In the GTHA, nine per cent said "regularly," 18 per cent said "occasionally," 19 per cent said "rarely" and 54 per cent said "never."
Respondents were also asked if they thought "preventing new bike lanes or removing bike lanes on major roads will help ease traffic congestion."
About one in five provincewide - 22 per cent - said it would help "a lot" with 41 per cent saying "a little" and 37 per cent responding "not much at all."
In Toronto, 27 per cent said it would help "a lot" with 45 per cent saying "a little" and 28 per cent responding "not much at all."
In the GTHA, 23 per cent said it would help "a lot" with 43 per cent saying "a little" and 34 per cent responding "not much at all."
However, across Ontario 41 per cent believed bike lanes are "essential for safety and provide necessary alternatives to car travel" while 38 per cent said "they contribute to traffic congestion and should be more strategically placed" and 20 per cent had no opinion.
In Toronto, 45 per cent said the lanes are essential while 39 per cent said they add to gridlock and 17 per cent were unsure.
In the GTHA, 37 per cent said they are essential with 42 per cent blaming them for contributing to traffic congestion and 21 per cent had no view.
Coletto said Ford has also tapped into something with the $200 rebate cheques for 15 million Ontario adults and children, first revealed by the Star last month, that will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion.
Nearly three-quarters of respondents - 72 per cent - approve of scheme the premier's political rivals have dismissed as a "gimmick" to "bribe voters with their own money" before an election. About one-quarter - 27 per cent - said it was a "very good idea" with 19 per cent saying the cheques are a "good idea" and 26 per cent an "acceptable idea."
One fifth - 21 per cent - disapproved, with 11 per cent calling the payments a "bad idea" and 10 per cent a "very bad idea." Seven per cent had no opinion.
But 48 per cent said they would prefer the $3 billion go toward improving public services like health care and education.
"If the opposition parties can galvanize people's focus and say, 'Wait a second, he's making poor choices' with taxpayers' money ... that could be a sleeper issue for them," said Coletto.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 273 of 500

Premier Doug Ford s plan to tear up Toronto bike ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-doug-ford-s-plan-tear-up-toronto-bike/docview/3125954319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Doug+Ford+s+plan+to+tear+up+Toronto+bike+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's plan to tear up Toronto bike lanes has Mayor Olivia Chow looking for options to fight provincial "overreach" in city affairs.
In a council motion published Thursday, Chow said new Ontario legislation that would empower Ford's government to remove bikeways reducing space for cars would "overturn the decisions and work of our locally elected Toronto city council."
If the bill passes "one government would, at tremendous cost to the taxpayer and without collaboration, undo another government's work," warned the motion, which is entitled "Respecting Local Democracy and Cities."
Chow wrote that as mayor "it is my responsibility to stand up for the decision-making authority of city council," and she's consulting with staff "to review the city's options." She said recommendations will come forward before the start of council's meeting on Nov. 13.
The motion didn't provide details about what, if anything, the city could do to confront Ford's plans on bike lanes - which include demolishing those on sections of Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street - and other issues the municipality considers outside provincial jurisdiction. In general, Queen's Park has near complete legislative authority over Ontario cities.
Chow's press secretary Arianne Robinson would only say the mayor is looking for ways to defend council's decision-making powers "in light of recent overreach" by the province.
The mayor's motion is the latest sign that Ford's bike lane salvo is straining his relationship with Chow. Early in her administration the Conservative premier and former New Democrat MP teamed up on issues like a "new deal" to bail out Toronto's finances.
But in recent weeks Chow has hit Ford's government with sharper criticism over its bikeway gambit, which many observers see as an effort by the premier to shore up public support ahead of an early election call next spring.
The province's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act, was introduced last month and would require cities to seek provincial permission to install bike lanes that require removing a lane of vehicle traffic.
Last week, the province posted a further amendment to the Environmental Registry of Ontario that "would require the province to remove sections of the Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue bike lanes in the City of Toronto and return them to a lane of traffic for motor vehicles."
Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria has argued bike lanes should be removed from major streets because they cause gridlock and only a small minority of residents cycle regularly. That's disputed by cycling advocates and experts who argue the bikeways have minimal effects on traffic and are necessary to keep riders safe and help meet climate goals.
Asked about Chow's motion Thursday, the Ford government signalled it was within its rights to make decisions about Toronto's road network.
"Toronto is the economic driver of this country, and gridlock has reached a crisis levels - costing the economy $11 billion in lost productivity each year," said Sarkaria's spokesperson Dakota Brasier, citing a figure from the Toronto Region Board of Trade.
She said the Ford government supports "a common-sense approach to bike lanes - one that doesn't involve removing vital traffic lanes on some of our busiest roads."
"The city's current approach is clearly not working. Our government is stepping up to address the problem head-on and paying the costs to fix the mess," she added.
The province has promised to reimburse cities for the cost of removing bike lanes it deems to be impeding car traffic. But earlier this week, Toronto City Manager Paul Johnson said that if Queen's Park goes ahead with the plan, the municipality would demand Ontario also pay the city back for money it spent to design and install the bikeways.
"I'm not prepared to say well, all the staff time and all the planning time and all the other time we had working on this as well, that's out," Johnson told CBC's Metro Morning on Tuesday.
"I don't think the city of Toronto taxpayers should pay twice for this."
Johnson predicted the bill would run into the millions of dollars.
Sarkaria has suggested that the value of increased productivity from easing gridlock would outweigh the municipality's sunk costs on the bikeways, and the province says it has no intention of compensating the city for money spent to install the lanes.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Document 274 of 500

Province gives $3M to job training for veterans: Non-profit helps service members transition to construction sector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-gives-3m-job-training-veterans/docview/3125954306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+gives+%243M+to+job+training+for+veterans%3A+Non-profit+helps+service+members+transition+to+construction+sector&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Remembrance Day approaches, Ontario is giving the non-profit organization Helmets to Hardhats $3 million over three years to help Canadian Armed Forces members and veterans train for construction jobs on civvy street.
The money is enough to help an additional 650 people as they leave the service, speeding their move to civilian life by two to three months with apprenticeships in a booming construction sector hungry for skilled workers.
"We have a social contract with our military servicemen and women who protect our country and preserve our freedoms, which means we have a responsibility to ensure they have a civilian life to transition to when their service is complete," Labour Minister David Piccini said Thursday.
Remembrance Day is Monday and about 8,000 members leave the armed forces every year.
The announcement came as Premier Doug Ford's government corrected an apparent oversight, adding Canadian Armed Forces reservists to a new program giving free travel on GO Transit and the UP Express to full-time members and veterans starting early in 2025. Details are being worked out but those taking advantage of the rides will need a Presto card.
Working with unions, the hard hats program provides "job-ready training to veterans that leverages their expertise and experience and lands them a new career," Piccini added in a statement.
To date, Helmets to Hardhats has helped more than 2,600 former service members across Canada get unionized careers in construction in addition to offering peer counselling, the government said.
"This investment ensures that our programs remain sustainable, empowering those who served our country to access employment and training resources that will lead them to in-demand careers as a skilled trade professional," said James Hogarth, executive director of the organization.
Ontario also provides funding to Helmets to Hardhats through the province's skills development fund, with almost $1 million earmarked.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria confirmed Thursday that reservists in the Canadian Armed Forces will be able to take advantage of free transit on GO and the UP Express train service, which runs between union station and Pearson International Airport.
"We've directed Metrolinx to make sure they're included," he told reporters, speaking of the provincial transit agency.
But Sarkaria, when asked, did not explain how reservists were left out of the initial plan announced Tuesday, days after it was first reported by the Star's Robert Benzie.
The oversight was formally raised Thursday in the legislature by Liberal MPP Karen McCrimmon (Kanata-Carleton), a veteran who introduced a motion to include reservists. It was quickly passed unanimously by all parties.
CAPTION: Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Thursday that reservists in the Canadian Armed Forces will be able to take advantage of free transit on GO and the UP Express train service.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 275 of 500

City councillors ask staff for salary review

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-councillors-ask-staff-salary-review/docview/3125954156/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+councillors+ask+staff+for+salary+review&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto councillors are asking city staff to review their office salaries, a proposal that could eventually lead to a raise for council members and their staff.
In a motion headed to next week's council meeting, Coun. James Pasternak (Ward 6, York Centre) asks staff to complete an updated review of councillors' pay and make recommendations to "adjust" it to a "level in line with counterparts" in other cities.
The motion, seconded by Coun. Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre), also requests staff to report back on "options to modernize the job descriptions, salary bands and the compensation envelope for staff" in councillors' offices.
The motion, which will require the support of two-thirds of members to be put to a vote at next week's meeting, follows an Oct. 28 Star story that revealed Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson quietly shelved a report last year that recommended councillors get a raise of more than 22 per cent.
The report was based on a 2023 jurisdictional review using findings of third-party consultants. It determined that on a per-constituent basis, Toronto councillors made less than municipal representatives of any other city included in the study. Councillors in Canada's largest municipality earned about $128,000 last year, and the last time council approved increases beyond automatic inflationary adjustments was in 2006.
In an interview, Pasternak said he and other councillors were "taken aback" to learn from the Star that there had been an "internal decision" not to bring the report to council last year.
His motion notes that in 2018 Premier Doug Ford's government slashed the size of council from 44 to 25 wards, making each councillor responsible for a much larger area. The average population of each ward is about 120,000. That year council voted to ask staff to recommend "an appropriate level of compensation" for councillors, but a recommendation was never brought back for a vote.
Pasternak stressed that his motion "does not ask for a raise" and that he would have to see the staff recommendations before deciding whether to vote for an increase.
The councillor acknowledged "there is no good time" for elected officials to talk about getting paid more, and "these are not easy times" for many residents. But he said it's important that councillors and their staff are paid competitively to attract people to run for office and ensure that employees responsible for serving constituents don't leave for the private sector.
His motion asks staff to report back on councillors' pay at the December council meeting, and on office staff pay by the end of next year. It also directs staff to come up with a plan to "regularly review councillor compensation."
Asked Thursday whether the mayor would support Pasternak's motion, Chow's press secretary, Arianne Robinson, declined to comment.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Subject: Compensation; Councils; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Compensation Wages & salaries
