Document 276 of 500

Premier Doug Ford has ordered an audit of discretionary spending ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-doug-ford-has-ordered-audit-discretionary/docview/3125954116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Doug+Ford+has+ordered+an+audit+of+discretionary+spending+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has ordered an audit of discretionary spending at all Ontario school boards after another pricey international trip - this time $32,000 for a conference in Hawaii that officials defended as a "once-in-a-career opportunity" for three staff members - came to light.
"Honestly, it's appalling," Education Minister Jill Dunlop said Thursday when asked about the trip to the Pacific islands taken by the trio of employees at the Lambton Kent District School Board, as well as reports of a $16,000 staff retreat to St. Catharines attended by more than two dozen employees of the London Catholic board.
In a written statement, the Lambton Kent board said the trip was for front-line Indigenous education staff members to learn about the latest trends and keep up to date on best practices and technologies to "address the changing needs of students and learners."
But, upset with the expense, Dunlop warned that now is the time to fess up to any additional international or questionable travel.
"I'm hearing from taxpayers. This is money that is supposed to be spent in the schools, on teachers and student support," Dunlop said. "The premier has directed me to do audits of discretionary funding in the school boards, and we'll be proceeding with that."
Dunlop said Education Ministry officials will "meet with the directors of education to discuss this with them, to let them know this is happening, and to also give them a heads up that maybe it's time to come forward and let us know about some of the appalling events that have been going on."
Four boards have landed in hot water for costly travel - including a $145,000 trip to Italy taken by four Brantford Catholic trustees, a price tag that included the purchase of religious artwork, as well as a $40,000 retreat for senior staff at the Thames Valley board.
The Lambton Kent travel was first reported by the CBC, and based on documents released under a freedom-of-information request.
The ministry says the audits are being conducted under the greater transparency expected by boards outlined in last year's Bill 98, Better Schools and Student Outcomes Act, with the specific order from Ford to include discretionary spending.
But news of them came as a surprise to the Ontario Public School Boards' Association.
"School boards have not yet received the full details regarding a potential audit and look forward to understanding how this process will unfold," said association president Kathleen Woodcock.
"School board trustees across Ontario take their roles very seriously, particularly their commitment to being financially responsible, transparent, and accountable to the communities and students they serve."
"We fully support the minister's focus on putting resources where they matter most: to benefit students directly."
Dunlop said the ministry will be looking into whether attending the conferences can be justified, and noted that an online option was available for the Hawaii trip.
"That money was spent to travel to Hawaii on something that could have been done right here at (home)," Dunlop told reporters at Queen's Park.
A spokesperson for the Lambton Kent District School Board said staff members attended an international conference last January in Hawaii, adding "we maintain the quality of our staff by investing in them."
Three front-line Indigenous education staff members were approved to go to the meeting as it "offers a unique platform for sharing innovative ideas, research findings, and best practices in the field of Indigenous education. In a commitment to our efforts towards truth and reconciliation, and given the ever-evolving landscape of education, it is crucial for professionals on our Indigenous education team to stay updated on the latest trends, strategies and technologies to address the changing needs of students and learners," public relations officer Caress Lee said in a written statement, noting the cost of the trip came out of a fund specifically for Indigenous education.
"Attending this conference provided (Lambton Kent) Indigenous education staff with the invaluable, once-in-a-career opportunity to elevate their professional development and actively contribute to the ongoing growth and improvement of the Lambton-Kent District School Board. Participants engaged in unique land-based learning experiences on the island and had the rare opportunity to connect with and learn from esteemed elders. This positioned the conference as a transformative experience that shaped the future of Indigenous education in our community and the Indigenous educational opportunities for our students."
Lee did not confirm the amount spent. The report from CBC cited the cost at more than $32,000 for travel, hotel and meals.
The premier has said he is frustrated with such spending.
It's "like the cheese slipped off the cracker with these guys," Ford said last month.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 277 of 500

Majority support Doug Ford's plan for bike lanes, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/majority-support-doug-fords-plan-bike-lanes-poll/docview/3126092706/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Majority+support+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+for+bike+lanes%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians support Premier Doug Ford's controversial bid to curb municipal bike lanes by a two-to-one margin, a new poll suggests.
Abacus Data also found 72 per cent of respondents back Ford's $200 "tax rebate" cheques, although about half believe the $3 billion would be better spent on improving public services such as health care or education.
A majority - 55 per cent - approved of the premier's proposal to force municipalities to get provincial approval for bike lanes that would interfere with vehicular traffic.
Ten per cent said it's a "very good idea" with 15 per cent saying it was a "good idea" and 30 per cent an "acceptable idea."
Only 28 per cent disagreed - 16 per cent said the provincial edict, which will result in existing bike lanes being removed on University Avenue, Bloor Street and Yonge Street in Toronto, was a "bad idea" and 12 per cent said it was a "very bad idea" - with 17 per cent unsure.
Just one per cent of Torontonians surveyed said they bike to work.
Abacus president David Coletto said the premier, who is known to pore over his Progressive Conservatives' own internal polling by Campaign Research, is clearly addressing the "scarcity mindset."
"If you look at these two issues - both the bike lane question and the rebate - Ford is so far being very effective at inoculating himself and his government from these forces that are causing governments across the developed world to lose vote share," said Coletto.
"These two decisions go right at the heart of a government that is relentlessly focused on responding to what people are saying are the most important things for them - and it's all tied to scarcity," the pollster said.
"I say scarcity of money in terms of affordability ... and then on scarcity of time, which is tied to traffic and the chaos that is Toronto and the GTA," he said.
Abacus surveyed 998 Ontarians from Oct. 31 through Tuesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford's Tories, who are considering an election this spring, were at 42 per cent support - ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
The poll found 68 per cent of Ontarians commute in their personal car or truck while 18 per cent take public transit, nine per cent walk, four per cent take Uber or a taxi and two per cent cycle.
In Toronto, 44 per cent drive, 36 per cent take transit, 15 per cent walk, four per cent take a ride share and one per cent bike.
In the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, 75 per cent drive, 12 per cent use transit, nine per cent walk, three per cent take a ride share and one per cent cycle.
(The provincial average for biking is higher than in Toronto or the GTHA because four per cent of Eastern Ontario respondents, most of them in Ottawa, take their bicycles to work.)
When asked "how often do you ride a bike and share a road with cars," 10 per cent of Ontarians said "regularly," 22 per cent said "occasionally," 21 per cent said "rarely" and 47 per cent said "never."
In Toronto, 12 per cent said "regularly," 30 per cent said "occasionally," 21 per cent said "rarely" and 37 per cent said "never."
In the GTHA, nine per cent said "regularly," 18 per cent said "occasionally," 19 per cent said "rarely" and 54 per cent said "never."
Respondents were also asked if they thought "preventing new bike lanes or removing bike lanes on major roads will help ease traffic congestion."
About one in five provincewide - 22 per cent - said it would help "a lot" with 41 per cent saying "a little" and 37 per cent responding "not much at all."
In Toronto, 27 per cent said it would help "a lot" with 45 per cent saying "a little" and 28 per cent responding "not much at all."
In the GTHA, 23 per cent said it would help "a lot" with 43 per cent saying "a little" and 34 per cent responding "not much at all."
However, across Ontario 41 per cent believed bike lanes are "essential for safety and provide necessary alternatives to car travel" while 38 per cent said "they contribute to traffic congestion and should be more strategically place" and 20 per cent had no opinion.
In Toronto, 45 per cent said the lanes are essential while 39 per cent said they add to gridlock and 17 per cent were unsure.
In the GTHA, 37 per cent said they are essential with 42 per cent blaming them for contributing to traffic congestion and 21 per cent had no view.
Coletto said Ford has also tapped into something with the $200 rebate cheques for 15 million Ontario adults and children, first revealed by the Star last month, that will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion.
Nearly three-quarters of respondents - 72 per cent - approve of scheme the premier's political rivals have dismissed as a "gimmick" to "bribe voters with their own money" before an election. About one-quarter - 27 per cent - said it was a "very good idea" with 19 per cent saying the cheques are a "good idea" and 26 per cent an "acceptable idea."
One fifth - 21 per cent - disapproved, with 11 per cent calling the payments a "bad idea" and 10 per cent a "very bad idea." Seven per cent had no opinion.
But 48 per cent said they would prefer the $3 billion go toward improving public services like health care and education while 43 per cent would opt for the $200 rebate cheque and 10 per cent had no opinion.
"If the opposition parties can galvanize people's focus and say, 'Wait a second, he's making poor choices' with taxpayers' money ... that could be a sleeper issue for them," said Coletto.
CAPTION: Westbound vehicle traffic is backed up next to an empty bike lane on Bloor Street West in Toronto on Oct. 25, 2024.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Public services; Traffic congestion; Car pools; Bicycling

Document 278 of 500

Ford government helps Armed Forces members transition to civilian jobs with $3 million

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-helps-armed-forces-members/docview/3126092702/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+helps+Armed+Forces+members+transition+to+civilian+jobs+with+%243+million&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Remembrance Day approaches, Ontario is giving the non-profit organization Helmets to Hardhats organization $3 million over three years to help Canadian Armed Forces members and veterans train for construction jobs on civvy street.
The money is enough to help an additional 650 people as they leave the service, speeding their move to civilian life by two to three months with apprenticeships in a booming construction sector hungry for skilled workers.
"We have a social contract with our military servicemen and women who protect our country and preserve our freedoms, which means we have a responsibility to ensure they have a civilian life to transition to when their service is complete," Labour Minister David Piccini said Thursday.
Remembrance Day is Monday and about 8,000 members leave the armed forces every year.
The announcement came as Premier Doug Ford's government corrected an apparent oversight, adding Canadian Armed Forces reservists to a new program giving free travel on GO Transit and the UP Express to full-time members and veterans starting early in 2025. Details are being worked out but those taking advantage of the rides will need a Presto card.
Working with unions, the hard hats program provides "job-ready training to veterans that leverages their expertise and experience and lands them a new career," Piccini added in a statement.
To date, Helmets to Hardhats has helped over 2,600 former service members across Canada get unionized careers in construction in addition to offering peer counselling, the government said.
"This investment ensures that our programs remain sustainable, empowering those who served our country to access employment and training resources that will lead them to in-demand careers as a skilled trade professional," said James Hogarth, executive director of the organization.
Ontario also provides funding to Helmets to Hardhats through the province's skills development fund, with almost $1 million earmarked.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria confirmed Thursday that reservists in the Canadian Armed Forces will be able to take advantage of free transit on GO and the UP Express train service, which runs between union station and Pearson International Airport.
"We've directed Metrolinx to make sure they're included," he told reporters, speaking of the provincial transit agency.
But Sarkaria, when asked, did not explain how reservists were left out of the initial plan announced Tuesday, days after it was first reported by the Star's Robert Benzie.
The oversight was formally raised Thursday in the legislature by Liberal MPP Karen McCrimmon (Kanata-Carleton), a veteranwho introduced a motion to include reservists. It was quickly passed unanimously by all parties.
"Metrolinx should not be dividing our armed forces by creating two tiers of recognition," said McCrimmon, who retired as a lieutenant-colonel after 31 years of service that saw her become the first woman to command a flying squadron. During a 2004 tour of duty in Afghanistan, she was responsible for NATO airlift co-ordination.
"Reservists are a key part of the Canadian Armed Forces' presence in our communities. Not only do they assist and even get deployed abroad, but are also first to help when disaster strikes a community, including fighting forest fires, recovery after storms and during COVID-19."
CAPTION: Ontario's Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria saidreservists in the Canadian Armed Forces will be able to take advantage of free transit on GO and the UP Express train service.

Document 279 of 500

Olivia Chow reviewing options to fight 'overreach' posed by Doug Ford's plan to tear up bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chow-reviewing-options-fight-overreach/docview/3125955180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow+reviewing+options+to+fight+%27overreach%27+posed+by+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+tear+up+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's plan to tear up Toronto bike lanes has Mayor Olivia Chow looking for options to fight provincial "overreach" in city affairs.
In a council motion published Thursday, Chow said new Ontario legislation that would empower Ford's government to remove bikeways reducing space for cars would "overturn the decisions and work of our locally elected Toronto city council."
If the bill passes "one government would, at tremendous cost to the taxpayer and without collaboration, undo another government's work," warned the motion, which is entitled "Respecting Local Democracy and Cities."
Chow wrote that as mayor "it is my responsibility to stand up for the decision-making authority of city council," and she's consulting with staff "to review the city's options." She said recommendations will come forward before the start of council's meeting on Nov. 13.
The motion didn't provide details about what, if anything, the city could do to confront Ford's plans on bike lanes - which include demolishing those on sections of Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street - and other issues the municipality considers outside provincial jurisdiction. In general, Queen's Park has near complete legislative authority over Ontario cities.
Chow's press secretary Arianne Robinson would only say the mayor is looking for ways to defend council's decision-making powers "in light of recent overreach" by the province.
The mayor's motion is the latest sign that Ford's bike lane salvo is straining his relationship with Chow. Early in her administration the Conservative premier and former New Democrat MP teamed up on issues like a "new deal" to bail out Toronto's finances. But in recent weeks Chow has hit Ford's government with sharper criticism over its bikeway gambit, which many observers see as an effort by the premier to shore up public support ahead of an early election call next spring.
The province's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act, was introduced last month and would require cities to seek provincial permission to install bike lanes that require removing a lane of vehicle traffic.
Last week, the province posted a further amendment to the Environmental Registry of Ontario that "would require the province to remove sections of the Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue bike lanes in the City of Toronto and return them to a lane of traffic for motor vehicles."
Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria has argued bike lanes should be removed from major streets because they cause gridlock and only a small minority of residents cycle regularly. That's disputed by cycling advocates and experts who argue the bikeways have minimal effects on traffic and are necessary to keep riders safe and help meet climate goals.
Asked about Chow's motion Thursday, the Ford government signalled it was within its rights to make decisions about Toronto's road network.
"Toronto is the economic driver of this country, and gridlock has reached a crisis levels - costing the economy $11 billion in lost productivity each year," said Sarkaria's spokesperson Dakota Brasier, citing a figure from the Toronto Region Board of Trade.
She said the Ford government supports "a common-sense approach to bike lanes - one that doesn't involve removing vital traffic lanes on some of our busiest roads."
"The city's current approach is clearly not working. Our government is stepping up to address the problem head-on and paying the costs to fix the mess," she added.
The province has promised to reimburse cities for the cost of removing bike lanes it deems to be impeding car traffic. But earlier this week, Toronto City Manager Paul Johnson said that if Queen's Park goes ahead with the plan, the municipality would demand Ontario also pay the city back for money it spent to design and install the bikeways.
"I'm not prepared to say well, all the staff time and all the planning time and all the other time we had working on this as well, that's out," Johnson told CBC's Metro Morning on Tuesday. "I don't think the city of Toronto taxpayers should pay twice for this."
He predicted the bill would run into the millions of dollars.
Sarkaria has suggested that the value of increased productivity from easing gridlock would outweigh the municipality's sunk costs on the bikeways, and the province says it has no intention of compensating the city for money spent to install the lanes.
CAPTION: One of the bikeways the Ford government said it will remove is the cycline lane on University Avenue.

Subject: Councils; Decision making; Cities

Document 280 of 500

'It's appalling': Hawaii trip prompts Doug Ford to order audit of all Ontario school boards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/appalling-hawaii-trip-prompts-doug-ford-order/docview/3125954299/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+appalling%27%3A+Hawaii+trip+prompts+Doug+Ford+to+order+audit+of+all+Ontario+school+boards&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has ordered an audit of discretionary spending at all Ontario school boards after yet another pricey international trip - this time $32,000 for a conference in Hawaii that officials defended as a "once-in-a-career opportunity" for three staff members - came to light.
"Honestly, it's appalling," Education Minister Jill Dunlop said Thursday when asked about the trip to the Pacific islands taken by the trio of employees at the Lambton Kent District School Board, as well as reports of a $16,000 staff retreat to St. Catharines attended by more than two dozen employees of the London Catholic board.
And, she warned, now is the time for boards to fess up to any additional international or questionable travel.
"I'm hearing from taxpayers. This is money that is supposed to be spent in the schools, on teachers and student support," Dunlop said. "The premier has directed me to do audits of discretionary funding in the school boards, and we'll be proceeding with that."
Dunlop said Education Ministry officials will "meet with the directors of education to discuss this with them, to let them know this is happening, and to also give them a head's up that maybe it's time to come forward and let us know about some of the appalling events that have been going on."
Four boards have landed in hot water for costly travel - including a $145,000 trip to Italy taken by four Brantford Catholic trustees, a price tag that included the purchase of religious artwork, as well as a $40,000 retreat for senior staff at the Thames Valley board.
The Lambton Kent travel was first reported by the CBC, and based on documents released under a freedom-of-information request.
The ministry says the audits are being conducted under the greater transparency expected by boards outlined in last year's Bill 98, Better Schools and Student Outcomes Act with the specific order from Ford to include discretionary spending.
But news of them came as a surprise to the Ontario Public School Boards' Association.
"School boards have not yet received the full details regarding a potential audit and look forward to understanding how this process will unfold," said association president Kathleen Woodcock.
"School board trustees across Ontario take their roles very seriously, particularly their commitment to being financially responsible, transparent, and accountable to the communities and students they serve," Woodcock said.
"We fully support the minister's focus on putting resources where they matter most: to benefit students directly."
Dunlop said the ministry will be looking into whether attending the conferences can be justified, and noted that an online option was available for the Hawaii trip.
"That money was spent to travel to Hawaii on something that could have been done right here at (home)," Dunlop told reporters at Queen's Park.
A spokesperson for the Lambton Kent District School Board said staff members attended an international conference last January in Hawaii, adding "we maintain the quality of our staff by investing in them."
Three front-line Indigenous education staff members were approved to go to the meeting as it "offers a unique platform for sharing innovative ideas, research findings, and best practices in the field of Indigenous education. In a commitment to our efforts towards truth and reconciliation, and given the ever-evolving landscape of education, it is crucial for professionals on our Indigenous education team to stay updated on the latest trends, strategies and technologies to address the changing needs of students and learners," public relations officer Caress Lee said in a written statement, noting the cost of the trip came out of a fund specifically for Indigenous education.
"Attending this conference provided (Lambton Kent) Indigenous education staff with the invaluable, once-in-a-career opportunity to elevate their professional development and actively contribute to the ongoing growth and improvement of the Lambton-Kent District School Board. Participants engaged in unique land-based learning experiences on the island and had the rare opportunity to connect with and learn from esteemed elders. This positioned the conference as a transformative experience that shaped the future of Indigenous education in our community and the Indigenous educational opportunities for our students."
Lee did not confirm the amount spent. The report from CBC cited the cost at more than $32,000 for travel, hotel and meals.
The London Free Press, meanwhile, reported on the $16,000 overnight trip taken by 26 employees of the London District Catholic School Board in St. Catharines.
News of that expenditure comes on the heels of an auditor being sent to investigate the Thames Valley District School Board after its officials took a three-day trip to Toronto, staying at the former SkyDome hotel on the same days the Blue Jays played home games.
The premier has said he is frustrated with such spending.
It's "like the cheese slipped off the cracker with these guys," Ford said last month at an unrelated announcement, adding school boards are "the first to scream they need more money."
The provincial government, he added is "pouring money into school boards, and they're out partying and acting like a bunch of yahoos. Unacceptable. You're elected - you have to respect the taxpayers' money."
The government is also investigating a trip taken by four trustees at the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board. The trustees in question, including the chair, have said they will repay their approximate $45,000 travel costs and are looking for outside support to cover the cost of the religious artwork they bought for schools.
CAPTION: Education Minister Jill Dunlop attends question period in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Students; Travel; Trends; Employees; Education; School boards

Business indexing term: Subject: Employees

Location: Hawaii; United States--US

Document 281 of 500

With the U.S. election results, I have lost hope in my fellow man

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-u-s-election-results-i-have-lost-hope-my/docview/3125452259/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+the+U.S.+election+results%2C+I+have+lost+hope+in+my+fellow+man&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How Donald Trump won - again, Nov. 6
A piece of me died with the U.S. election results. It took a while to figure out what was gone, I was in such a fog. But then I realized it was HOPE. Throughout the U.S. election campaign, the differences between Kamala Harris and Donald Trump became absolutely clear. It was like day and night, good and evil, order and chaos, decency and incivility, sanity and insanity. I struggled to understand the attraction so many Americans have for the former president who wreaked havoc and chaos on their nation four years earlier. Harris seemed to be a breath of fresh air. She is honest, has common sense and laid out a plan to help those in need and bring the country together. She recognized the danger of a second Trump presidency and tried to warn the voting public. She ran what appeared to be a flawless campaign, though now I'm sure the pundits will dissect flaws in her message delivery, her character or her platform. I am so completely and utterly disappointed in the American people for choosing a convicted felon, a wannabe dictator who will undoubtedly wreak havoc on not only the U.S.A., but the rest of the world in the near future. Where I had put faith in Americans to make the right choice, I have now lost hope in my fellow man. There will be no charity.
Pat Hertzberg, Caledon
Donald Trump's victory in the U.S. presidential elections cannot be discounted as a fluke or blamed purely on racist or misogynistic voters, much as that may be tempting for disappointed American progressives. This time around, Trump won the popular vote and made important and undeniable inroads in the Black and Latino communities. This should be a time for introspection for a Democratic party that needs to ask itself what it is, in its policies and ideologies, that turned off such a large swath of the electorate. And the same questions should also be asked, pre-emptively, by the Canadian Liberals, if they want to have a chance against our own homegrown populist, Pierre Poilievre, and a Conservative party that smells blood in the water. Some basic considerations come to mind that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau may want to ponder: do not despise the working classes and abandon them to the sirens of populism. Do not count on the exclusive appeal of identity politics and of performative shows of moral superiority. Special interest groups do not add up to reliable voting blocks. Most of all, do something to rein in the cost-of-living increases and bring the price of groceries down to reflect the worries of a cash-strapped, insecure workforce. Failing to address these issues with concrete actions in Canada, may very well result in a version of what happened yesterday in the U.S.
Vittorio Frigerio, Toronto
Our health-care system is stretched to the limit Uninsured patients in Ontario are finding it harder to get health care, Nov. 5
While Ontarians with mental-health issues do need help to ensure that their OHIP card is maintained, I do agree that we need to cut programs for people who are not citizens or permanent residents in this country. Our health-care system is stretched to the limit and Canadians must come first. Proof of health insurance should be a condition to obtain a work or student visa. When my son went abroad for a university exchange program, a private health insurance for the duration of his stay was a condition of his acceptance to the program. Canadians get medical insurance when travelling abroad and student or work visa recipients coming to our country are no different. We should invest in our own citizens first .
Claude Gannon, Markham
We need to reform debt and bankruptcy systems to consider human dignity
Mortgage renewals could spur homeowners to sell, Nov. 5.
I urge everyone to take a moment to reflect on the troubling realities of our current debt and bankruptcy systems, which often fail to protect the most vulnerable. It's important to appreciate that the conditions cannot entirely be attributed to individual recklessness and that systemic issues also play a role alongside poor investment decisions. Scholars such as Elizabeth Warren, Chrystin Ondersma and Melissa B. Jacoby's works have shown how our bankruptcy systems must evolve to consider human dignity and so going forward we must reform this alongside prioritizing financial literacy.
Harjaap S. Ahluwalia, Brampton
Use rebate funds for better mental-health resources
Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques, Oct. 30
Premier Doug Ford recently announced a $200 taxpayer rebate to "help" Ontarians or is it to earn some votes in the upcoming election? Taxpayers expect their money to be put into much needed resources that benefit the population. As a psychology student and researcher for Breaking Stigmas, a club promoting mental health awareness at York University, it is evident that mental health issues continue to rise particularly in youth. These funds will be more useful for mental health intervention and treatments as well as making these resources more accessible to the public, especially to marginalized and low-income communities. Due to increased housing and food prices, Ontarians have no choice but to use their salary on physical necessities which leaves little to nothing for therapy, assessments, or medications. It is essential that the mental health of Ontarians is made a priority rather than a triviality.
Pardis Raoofnia, Richmond Hill
Ford needs to up the ante to $500 if he wants my vote
Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques, Oct. 30
In response to Premier Doug Ford's intention to mail out $200 cheques to every person in Ontario, we the public, should send the premier a letter stating that $200 is not sufficient, and that Ford should up the ante to $500 if he wants to bribe the people of Ontario to vote for him. No extra money, then no vote. If the premier wants to play games, then the public too should be able to play.
Paul Kurm, Toronto
Is Tiff over?Here's why Poilievre's attack on the Bank of Canada is absolute nonsense, Oct. 8
The inflation rate has come down due to policy and is somewhere around 1.6 per cent. Is Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre still going to fire the Governor of the Bank of Canada?
Mike Walker, Washago, ON
CAPTION: President elect Donald Trump during an election night event at the Palm Beach Convention Center on Nov. 6, 2024 in West Palm Beach, Fla.

How will Canada handle Trump 2.0 + the fate of Toronto's FIFA training facility

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-will-canada-handle-trump-2-0-fate-torontos/docview/3125449574/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+will+Canada+handle+Trump+2.0+%2B+the+fate+of+Toronto%27s+FIFA+training+facility&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, November 7 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a new chapter in Canada-U.S. relations, the fate of Toronto's FIFA training facility and an impending TikTok business ban.
DON'T MISSIt's time to see how Canada will handle Trump 2.0
The U. S. President-elect put a call with Justin Trudeau in the queue - and it was a long lineup. When the two finally connected more than 13 hours after AP projected Trump's victory, officials say they shared a friendly chat, talking supply chains and "unfair trading practices," Tonda MacCharles reports. Thus begins a fascinating new chapter in U.S.-Canada relations; take a look at where we go from here.
The price of friendship: Trump's plans to hike tariffs and demands for heightened defence spending could end up costing Canada billions. Here's how it could affect your income.John Semley's take: As Canadians, it's hard not to feel smug over what's happening south of the border. But the reality is, Canadian culture is American culture, and our identity is a reaction to America. Don't get complacent.Meanwhile: In conceding to Trump yesterday afternoon, Kamala Harris acknowledged that "sometimes the fight takes a while. That doesn't mean we won't win." Here's what else she said in her 10-minute speech. Toronto's training sites for FIFA 2026 are being scaled back - again
A planned soccer training facility and local legacy of the upcoming FIFA World Cup will be smaller than intended. Ben Spurr reports that the facility at Centennial Park will be reduced from two pitches and two field houses to one of each, after FIFA revised its hosting requirements for Toronto. The city already scrapped plans for a training facility at a second site in 2022; here's what's happening with this one.
FIFA's legacy: The "world class" recreation facility helped catalyze work on a master plan for Centennial Park, including a playground, water play area, cricket pitch and upgraded sports fields.Bruce Arthur's take: Toronto is sinking at least $380 million into hosting six games over three weeks. But just because the city is hosting the world, doesn't mean Toronto will score.Ottawa is banning TikTok's business operations in Canada
The clock is ticking on TikTok. Justin Trudeau's government is ordering the "wind up" of the social media giant's business operations in the country, Mark Ramzy reports, but it will not block Canadians' access to the app. The decision comes in response to "specific national security risks" related to the Chinese parent company of TikTok. Here's what it means for you.
Word from TikTok: "Shutting down TikTok's Canadian offices and destroying hundreds of well-paying local jobs is not in anyone's best interest," a spokesperson said. The company said it will challenge Ottawa's decision in court.Security concerns: Canada's top spy and Trudeau have urged Canadians not to use TikTok, citing concerns that user data collected by the app is being funnelled to the Chinese government "potentially for nefarious purposes."WHAT ELSE
MPPs have quietly agreed to extend public funding of Ontario political parties until the end of 2026.Ontario Trillium Benefit payments go out on Friday. Here's how much you can expect.The Bank of Canada is keeping a wary eye on the housing market - but rate cuts will likely continue.For progressives, there's a silver lining to Trump's election victory, Susan Delacourt notes.Doug Ford is urging Donald Trump to "bet big on the U.S.-Ontario relationship."Trump's re-election could be devastating for the world's fight against climate change.The world's most dangerous regimes now have a friend in the White House, Shannon Gormley writes.Will Trump's victory cause a surge of asylum seekers fleeing to Canada? Here's what's different from 2016.This Toronto shooter - once Canada's most-wanted man - claims he was "attacked for no reason" before killing.So, you're a disillusioned American who now wants to move to Canada? Here's what you need to know."I'm not hot. I'm old and grumpy." Hugh Grant chats about getting nasty for his new horror film, "Heretic.""Succession" star Kieran Culkin charms in the fine cringe comedy "A Real Pain."POV
Trump can do no wrong. Anger is a virtue. This is the version of reality American voters accepted.CLOSE-UP
PAIPORTA: A woman clears out mud among piles of debris in eastern Spain Wednesday, in the aftermath of the worst floods the country's seen in a generation. A week after the event, at least 218 are dead and 89 are still unaccounted for. Take a look at how the country is recovering.

Full text: Trump victory destroys all hope in my fellow man
How Donald Trump won - again, Nov. 6
A piece of me died with the U.S. election results. It took a while to figure out what was gone, I was in such a fog. But then I realized it was HOPE.
Throughout the election campaign, the differences between Kamala Harris and Donald Trump became absolutely clear. It was like day and night, good and evil, order and chaos, decency and incivility, sanity and insanity.
I struggled to understand the attraction so many Americans have for the former president who wreaked havoc and chaos on their nation four years earlier. Harris seemed to be a breath of fresh air. She is honest, has common sense and laid out a plan to help those in need and bring the country together.
She recognized the danger of a second Trump presidency and tried to warn the voting public. She ran what appeared to be a flawless campaign, although now I'm sure the pundits will dissect flaws in her message delivery, her character or her platform.
I am so completely and utterly disappointed in the American people for choosing a convicted felon, a wannabe dictator who will undoubtedly wreak havoc on not only the U.S., but also the rest of the world in the near future. Where I had put faith in Americans to make the right choice, I have now lost hope in my fellow man.
Pat Hertzberg, Caledon
A warning sign for Canadian Liberals and PM
Donald Trump's victory cannot be discounted as a fluke or blamed purely on racist or misogynistic voters, much as that may be tempting for disappointed American progressives. This time around, Trump won the popular vote and made important and undeniable inroads in the Black and Latino communities.
This should be a time for introspection for a Democratic party that needs to ask itself what it is, in its policies and ideologies, that turned off such a large swath of the electorate.
And the same questions should also be asked, pre-emptively, by the Canadian Liberals, if they want to have a chance against our own homegrown populist, Pierre Poilievre, and a Conservative party that smells blood in the water.
Some basic considerations come to mind that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau may want to ponder. Do not despise the working classes and abandon them to the sirens of populism. Do not count on the exclusive appeal of identity politics and of performative shows of moral superiority. Special-interest groups do not add up to reliable voting blocks.
Most of all, do something to rein in the cost-of-living increases and bring the price of groceries down to reflect the worries of a cash-strapped, insecure workforce.
Failing to address these issues with concrete actions in Canada, may very well result in a version of what happened yesterday in the U.S.
Vittorio Frigerio, Toronto
Our health-care system stretched to the limit
Uninsured patients in Ontario are finding it harder to get health care, Nov. 5
While Ontarians with mental-health issues do need help to ensure their OHIP card is maintained, I agree that we need to cut programs for people who are not citizens or permanent residents in this country. Our health-care system is stretched to the limit and Canadians must come first.
Proof of health insurance should be a condition to obtain a work or student visa. When my son went abroad for a university exchange program, a private health insurance for the duration of his stay was a condition of his acceptance to the program.
Canadians get medical insurance when travelling abroad and student or work visa recipients coming to our country are no different. We should invest in our own citizens first.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Reform systems for debt and bankruptcy
Mortgage renewals could spur homeowners to sell, Nov. 5.
I urge everyone to take a moment to reflect on the troubling realities of our current debt and bankruptcy systems, which often fail to protect the most vulnerable.
It's important to appreciate that the conditions cannot entirely be attributed to individual recklessness and that systemic issues also play a role alongside poor investment decisions.
Scholars such as Elizabeth Warren, Chrystin Ondersma and Melissa B. Jacoby's works have shown how our bankruptcy systems must evolve to consider human dignity and so going forward we must reform this alongside prioritizing financial literacy.
Harjaap S. Ahluwalia, Brampton
Use rebate funds for mental-health resources
Doug Ford is spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques,
Oct. 30
Premier Doug Ford recently announced a $200 taxpayer rebate to "help" Ontarians, or is it to earn some votes in the upcoming election?
Taxpayers expect their money to be put into much-needed resources that benefit the population. As a psychology student and researcher for Breaking Stigmas, a club promoting mental health awareness at York University, it is evident that mental health issues continue to rise, particularly in youth.
These funds will be more useful for mental health intervention and treatments, as well as making these resources more accessible to the public, especially to marginalized and low-income communities.
It is essential that the mental health of Ontarians is made a priority rather than a triviality.
Pardis Raoofnia, Richmond Hill
Ford needs to up the ante to $500 to win my vote
We the public should send the premier a letter stating that $200 is not sufficient, and that Ford should up the ante to $500 if he wants to bribe the people of Ontario. No extra money, then no vote. If the premier wants to play games, then the public should also be able to play.
Paul Kurm, Toronto
Should Macklem still be fired?
Here's why Poilievre's attack on the Bank of Canada is absolute nonsense, Oct. 8
The inflation rate has come down due to policy and is somewhere around 1.6 per cent. Is Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre still going to fire Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem?
Mike Walker, Washago, Ont.

Subject: Political campaigns; Election results; Bankruptcy; State elections; Mental health; Health care policy; Health insurance

Business indexing term: Subject: Bankruptcy Health insurance

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Document 284 of 500

With Trump in power, Canada may lose billions: Ottawa urged to do all it can to blunt proposed U.S. tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/with-trump-power-canada-may-lose-billions/docview/3125039958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+Trump+in+power%2C+Canada+may+lose+billions%3A+Ottawa+urged+to+do+all+it+can+to+blunt+proposed+U.S.+tariffs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President-elect Donald Trump's victory Tuesday paves the way for an agenda which could cost Canada billions, including demands for higher defence spending and steep new tariffs on every shipment into the U.S.
Trump will return to the White House in January after beating Vice-President Kamala Harris in a closely fought election. He is promising tariffs of at least 10 per cent, potentially as much as 20 per cent, on all goods entering America. He has also demanded that NATO countries, like Canada, boost defence spending.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, said Canada has to take the threat seriously.
"We learned in 2016 to 2020 to take his tariff threats at face value. If he says he is going to do it he is going to do it," Volpe said.
Canada does approximately $3.5 billion in trade with the U.S. every day, which could mean millions in tariffs charged to Canadian companies.
Volpe said the U.S. has concerns about Canada's digital services tax and about access Chinese companies have to the Canadian market, but overall, it is a good trade relationship. He said he expects most tariffs on Canada would be short-lived because they would only hurt American consumers.
Canadian Chamber of Commerce president Candace Laing implored the Liberal government to do everything it can to ensure the tariffs don't come to pass.
"Canada's government must collaborate effectively with the Trump administration to preserve and strengthen our bilateral economic partnership," Laing said in a written statement.
"By resisting tariffs and trade barriers that will only raise prices and hurt consumers in both countries, Canada and the United States can strengthen resilient cross-border supply chains that enhance our shared economic security."
The chamber commissioned University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe to study Trump's proposed tariffs. He found they could add up to a five per cent drop in Canada's GDP and reduce Canadians' income by $1,100 a year on average, while also hurting Americans in the process.
On Parliament Hill Wednesday, government ministers seemed to downplay the threat. Trade Minister Mary Ng congratulated Trump and said she was eager to work with the new administration.
"We look forward to working with them, to make sure that this really important trade relationship between Canada and the United States continues to be strong," Ng said.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said the government is prepared to deal with the new administration, and learned from trade negotiations that took place when Trump was previously in power.
"The good thing for Canadians is, we're ready. I was already texting folks in the United States and they're responding," he said. "Many of us served during the former Trump administration, but I think now we move from just being the friendly neighbour of the North to a strategic partner."
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland noted that the current trade deal between Canada and the U.S. is one Trump negotiated. She said beyond relationships and past associations, Canada offers the U.S. a better deal on trade than most other countries.
"At the end of the day, the reason that we were successful in getting a win-win outcome in the NAFTA negotiations was because the Canada-U.S. economic relationship is beneficial to both parties," Freeland said. "We build things together and Canadian workers do not undercut U.S. workers. We have similar labour and environmental standards and we are both high-wage countries."
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford said he conferred with Freeland on Tuesday night about the spectre of Trump's tariffs.
"I had a conversation last night with Minister Freeland and we're united. We're united with the federal government and we're going to be united with all the premiers and we'll get this done, but I'm very confident things will work out," Ford told reporters Wednesday.
The premier noted that when Trump slapped tariffs on some Canadian products in 2019, Canada retaliated.
"We did it last time. They put tariffs on our steel. Didn't last too long, because - when I talked to Chrystia (at the time and I) went down there, talked to (Trump's then trade representative Robert) Lighthizer and I went back and said, 'Tariff everything.' That doesn't work. It doesn't work for either country - I think they realized that. They pulled the tariffs off extremely quickly after we did this," said Ford.
Asked if he was concerned about the impact of a second Trump presidency on Canada's electric vehicle strategy, the premier said "I believe the market will dictate" demand for such cars and trucks and their batteries.
Trump's policies could save the government money in one respect. He has speculated about cancelling production subsidies for electric vehicle production. The Biden administration brought in massive subsidies for EV production.
Canada and Ontario matched those production subsidies to lure Volkswagen to London, Ont., which could cost $13 billion. It offered the same deal to Stellantis for its Windsor, Ont., plant at a cost of potentially $15 billion.
Both of those deals included provisions that Canada would not pay out the subsidies if the U.S. cancelled its subsidies.
Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli said the world's automakers will be "watching closely" but Ontario could benefit, citing the province's deal last April to land $15 billion in investment from Honda for four new factories without that specific type of incentive.
"That was a pretty clear signal that we're just the right place to be for some of these manufacturers," added Fedeli.
Fedeli said provincial officials will be heading to Washington again in the coming months to continue their "charm offensive" by reminding U.S. policymakers that nine million Americans make products shipped to Ontario.
In addition to potentially denting Canada's economy with tariffs, Trump is also likely to lean on Canada to spend more on defence. He has argued all NATO countries need to meet the alliance's target of two per cent of GDP on military spending.
Without specific details, the government has said it will reach that target by 2032. Its forecast spending to 2030 would get Canada to 1.7 per cent of GDP. But the Parliamentary Budget Officer has recently found those numbers are misleading, because the government was predicting a very low growth scenario for Canada.
The PBO found Canada would only be at 1.58 per cent by 2030 and would need to be spending nearly $82 billion by 2032, twice what Canada spends now, to meet the NATO target.
Defence Minister Bill Blair insisted Canada will meet the NATO target. "We're investing significantly in our defence capabilities. We've made a commitment to ... all of our NATO allies, including the United States."
CAPTION: Federal Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said Wednesday the current trade deal between Canada andthe U.S. is one Donald Trump negotiated in his first presidential term, adding that Canada offers the U.S. a better deal on trade than most other countries.Sean KilpatrickTHE CANADIAN PRESS

CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star Rob Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 285 of 500

There's a silver lining for progressive voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/theres-silver-lining-progressive-voters/docview/3125038363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+a+silver+lining+for+progressive+voters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive-leaning voters are going to be looking for some kind of silver lining in Donald Trump's return to power in the United States.
Here is one: all those people whose faith in democracy was shaken by Trump's loss four years ago, braced to declare this 2024 election corrupt too, can no longer complain that the system is rigged against them.
Democracy, in other words, has prevailed, even with a leader who is poised to shake it to its foundations and even with Trump's declared promise to be a dictator on day one of his presidency.
Trump had been carefully laying the groundwork to declare any victory by Kamala Harris as proof that Democrats cheat.
"The only thing that can stop us is the cheating. It's the only thing that can stop us," Trump told supporters late last week in Arizona. Judging by the person-in-the-street interviews being broadcast on the news in recent days, that allegation was being swallowed whole by Trump's base.
So had Harris won, it was almost inevitable that Trump supporters would rebel, perhaps in larger and more violent ways than the Jan. 6, 2021, storming of Capitol Hill. Trump's victory, then, has at least spared the world that kind of spectacle.
But there will be a different kind of chaos. Trump himself forecast it during his flurry of end-of-campaign appearances over the weekend.
"You watch. It's going to be so good. It's going to be so much fun. It'll be nasty a little bit at times, and maybe at the beginning, in particular," Trump said in Georgia, one of the many states that helped send him back to office. "But it's going to be something."
Here in Canada, there will be reverberations for Justin Trudeau's government, on the policy and political front.
Trudeau and his cabinet have been preparing for a possible Trump victory. Immigration Minister Marc Miller delivered a flat "yes" when asked in the Commons last week whether he was braced for a post-Trump flood of asylum seekers to this country.
Trudeau's team has also kept its lines of communication open with Trump's circle, even with him out of power, and those conversations will intensify in the days and weeks ahead.
There's an assumption that there's personal antagonism between Trump and Trudeau, but in fact, the two men had many cordial conversations after the 2018 blow-up, when the former president called Trudeau "weak" and "dishonest." This summer, they had a friendly chat when Trudeau reached out to convey sympathy for the assassination attempt on Trump.
The prime minister was prepared this week, naturally, with two sets of remarks to make in the wake of Tuesday's election. Had Harris won, Trudeau probably would have been more expansive on the progressive alliance between Democrats and Liberals.
His statement on Trump's victory, though, was also convivial. "The friendship between Canada and the U.S. is the envy of the world," the prime minister posted on X early Wednesday morning. "I know President Trump and I will work together to create more opportunity, prosperity, and security for both of our nations."
They're going to need all the friendliness they can muster - Trump's promises for sweeping tariffs could hit Canada hard.
The hard-won, renegotiated free-trade deal between Canada, the U.S. and Mexico is also up for review in 2026, halfway through Trump's next presidency, and that could prompt another existential crisis in the Canada-U.S. trading relationship.
But if the current polls hold in Canada, it won't be Trudeau overseeing that review, but Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Poilievre is going to be facing a lot of questions in the coming days about how he would deal with Trump, and why Canadians should expect him to handle a disruptive president with minimal damage to this country.
Conservatives will, of course, be taking heart from the fact that the United States was ready for a rightward turn and hoping Canada is in the same mood. They will also realize it is a bit more politically awkward for Trudeau and his team to cast Conservatives as the northern branch of Trump Republicanism, given that Canada has to maintain civil ties with the re-elected president.
On the other hand, if Trump's return to power does unleash the chaos that seems likely, Canadians could be looking for the kind of stability that an incumbent government, experienced at dealing with Trump, could provide. That will be a faint hope that Liberals will cling to in the days ahead.
Someone recently offered me this metaphor for how Canada might react to a Trump victory.
"It will be like seeing your neighbour go off the wagon again," he said.
For progressive voters, that feels about right. At least there won't be another storm-the-Capitol party.
CAPTION: People comfort each otheras U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris speaks at Howard University in Washington on Wednesday. Had Harris won, writes Susan Delacourt, it was almost inevitable that Trump supporters would rebel, perhaps in larger and more violent ways than the Jan. 6, 2021, storming of Capitol Hill.BRENDAN SMIALOWSKIAFP via GETTY IMAGES

Document 286 of 500

Trump won with lies voters wanted to hear

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-won-with-lies-voters-wanted-hear/docview/3125038256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+won+with+lies+voters+wanted+to+hear&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the results rolled in on Tuesday night and it became clear that the United States was moving back toward Donald Trump, I thought about Doug Ford and bike lanes.
The Ontario premier has moved with great haste to implement a quasi-ban on the construction of new bike lanes in the province, and in the city of Toronto he has gone further, moving to tear out existing lanes on three busy downtown streets. No one can seriously believe that removing some bike lanes will make a noticeable difference on traffic. If anything, it will just invite more cars into an already-choked downtown. What will the Premier say when the gridlock doesn't improve?
It's a typically Trumpian pledge: Here is an illogical solution to an intractable problem. But it sounds pleasing.
On a much broader scale, Trump has wooed his voters with similarly easy-sounding solutions to complex problems. Much of his campaign was devoted to the notion that America had descended into economic and social chaos in the years of the Biden administration, and that only he could save it.
Most visibly, he vowed that his pledge to round up millions of undocumented migrants would "liberate" America's cities from invading forces that commit crimes with abandon and steal jobs from natural-born citizens. The implicit suggestion was that problems in the country's inner cities that were decades in the making could be solved by swooping in and getting rid of foreigners.
On the economy, Trump's promises were even more simplistic. Where countries the world over suffered from high inflation as part of the post-COVID recovery, he has insisted that he could wave it away almost instantly. This is not an exaggeration: At his rallies, Trump has said inflation would be "over" as soon as he took office, as though Joe Biden has spent the last four years personally going around and raising prices on everything from bacon to automobiles to housing. Inflation has already eased, thanks to the same market forces that caused consumer prices to rise in the first place, but Trump has insisted that everything will be cheaper under his watch.
Meanwhile, he's promised to bolster U.S. manufacturing by putting punitive tariffs on all imports, a strategy that countless economists have said will only serve to increase the price of goods purchased by Americans. Completely unbothered by this logic, Trump vows that his tariffs will be paid by foreigners - and also that foreign countries will drop their tariffs on American imports. None of this makes any sense, but again Trump has insisted that American industry will be booming within moments of his return to the White House.
It goes on and on. Trump's new favourite adviser, the billionaire Tesla chief executive Elon Musk, says he can slash federal spending by a third, which amounts to a $2-trillion cut, a feat that would be impossible without reductions to big-ticket programs like defence and Medicare. The president-elect, though, has said that Musk is such an expert at efficiency that he will "cut costs without anybody even knowing it." It's such an absurd claim, this idea of spending cuts that are both unprecedented in size and scope and yet completely painless, that no politician but Trump would think to offer it.
In some of his later rallies, the signage displayed a simple message: Trump Will Fix It. Quite what the "it" stood for was, it seems, up to the reader. Unfulfilling job, lousy apartment, lack of romantic prospects, whatever: Trump would fix it for you. Evidently, the message resonated. Whatever the specific reasons for why the vote broke the way it did in different parts of the United States, it's undeniable that a lot of unhappy people bought into the idea that Trump would make their lives better, quickly and easily. It didn't matter how many fact checks or debunks were offered in response, because that skepticism wasn't nearly as appealing as the original message.
Trump has vowed that he will make everything better in a hurry. As ridiculous as that sounds coming from any politician, America has decided to let him try.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Document 287 of 500

Subsidies for political parties will continue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/subsidies-political-parties-will-continue/docview/3125038228/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Subsidies+for+political+parties+will+continue&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Against the backdrop of a spring election - and with attention focused on the U.S. presidential campaign - MPPs have quietly agreed to extend public funding of Ontario political parties.
In a news release Wednesday afternoon without any fanfare, Attorney General Doug Downey's ministry announced that the per-vote subsidy of parties that was to have been eliminated on Dec. 31 will continue until the end of 2026.
"With the support of all parties in the legislature, the Ontario government is proposing amendments to the Election Finances Act that would ... extend the payment of quarterly allowances to registered political parties and constituency associations for another two years," Downey's department said.
Each quarter, the Conservatives receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000. That's 63.6 cents per vote, based on the results of the 2022 election.
While Premier Doug Ford has long said he opposed the public funding of political parties, his party benefits the most from the subsidies, which were introduced by the previous Liberal government.
The payments go to political parties whose candidates received at least two per cent of valid votes cast across the province, or five per cent of those cast in the ridings in which the party fielded a candidate.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 288 of 500

U.S. result a big win for Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/u-s-result-big-win-trudeau/docview/3125038220/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+result+a+big+win+for+Trudeau&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump piled-up the votes last night, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must have been pinching himself in anticipation. For months, Trudeau and his PMO palace guard will have followed the U.S. presidential race polls more closely than most. Despite the whispers to the Star's Ottawa Bureau, I'm dubious that the Liberals actually preferred a Kamala Harris victory.
How so? Trump's political rebirth presents our prime minister with a career-saving opportunity: Trudeau gets to spend the next eleven months playing the delicious role of Escamillo, the brave bullfighting Toreador from Bizet's timeless opera Carmen.
Trump is his bull, and we'll soon be bombarded with stories about how Trudeau is preparing to defend Canada's interests from a snarling, vicious, horned bovine. The President-elect didn't intend to play the role, but by broaching the prospect of diverting water from Canada, and with the scheduled review of USMCA in 2026, Trudeau's woeful polling results call for different tactics than in 2016.
Back then, our rookie PM went to great lengths to build bridges with the newly-elected president and his key family members. Trudeau even flew down to New York to take First Daughter Ivanka Trump to a Broadway showing of the Canadian musical "Come From Away."
Those chummy days are over. Trump won't be on the ballot in our upcoming federal election, but Trudeau will try and run against him just the same.
We got a taste of this brewing strategy a few days ago when the Liberal government introduced legislation "to crack down" on the charitable status of community groups offering pregnancy counselling services that "push women away from accessing abortion services." I'm not sure how Pope Francis and his Canadian flock might be impacted, but the true target was Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre - with Liberal Cabinet Minister Ya'ara Saks claiming Poilievre "will use the notwithstanding clause to take away women's reproductive rights."
That's all hogwash, but with the impending U.S. vote, the Liberals were trying - once again - to leverage the never-ending American struggle over abortion for their own political ends.
Poilievre has long made clear that Conservatives will not restrict abortion access in the event he wins the next election, leaving Liberals no choice but look to Trump's MAGA-land for evidence of a hidden global right-wing agenda.
If they'd stick closer to home, Team Trudeau could take inspiration from former Liberal Leader Jean Chrétien, who proved that being critical of both the U.S. government, and even the sitting President, can prop up your support at home.
As Prime Minister, Chrétien famously "attacked" George W. Bush's administration for its large budget deficit, and he was openly critical of America's decision to go after Saddam Hussein in 2003. The cross-border animosity peaked when Chretien's spokesperson called then-president Bush a "moron" in the presence of journalists. Not to be outdone, Bush's White House featured a hallway of photos of what he called "great North American leaders" - featuring only pictures of himself and Mexican president Vicente Fox.
Canada/US relations survived such slights.
With Trump's election, Toreador Trudeau now gets to protect Canadian water, fight for dairy farmers in the coming USMCA renegotiation, and shield Canadian women from the overarching influence of U.S. Republican legislators.
It's hard to believe it was only a few days ago that a cabal of grumpy Liberal caucus members were calling for Trudeau's resignation, for fear that he had no viable path to political salvation. I guess they were wrong.
In 2012, Trudeau sought out a boxing ring to "restore his own identity." Almost a decade later, the PM leveraged a shouting match outside of a hospital with anti-vaccine mandate protesters to help turn around his flailing 2021 re-election campaign. Now comes a battle with Trump.
The Grits have done a terrible job with our economy, but interest rates are dropping, the TSX Index is at an all-time high, and pollster Nik Nanos reports that consumer confidence in vote-rich Ontario just hit "a new 30-month high."
Trudeau's remaining opportunity for electoral deliverance may just come from throwing some well-aimed banderillas in Trump's general direction.
What's he got to lose?
Mark McQueen is a Toronto-based entrepreneur who has worked on Bay Street for more than 30 years. He is the former board chair of two federal agencies and was an adviser in the Brian Mulroney PMO. He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Donald Trump won't be on the ballot in our upcoming federal election, but Justin Trudeau will try and run against him just the same, Mark McQueen writes.Sean KilpatrickThe Canadian Press file photo

Document 289 of 500

MPPs quietly agree to continue subsidizing Ontario political parties

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mpps-quietly-agree-continue-subsidizing-ontario/docview/3125038274/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPPs+quietly+agree+to+continue+subsidizing+Ontario+political+parties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Against the backdrop of a spring election - and with attention focused on the U.S. presidential campaign - MPPs have quietly agreed to extend public funding of Ontario political parties.
In a news release Wednesday afternoon without any fanfare, Attorney General Doug Downey's ministry announced that the per-vote subsidy of parties that was to have been eliminated on Dec. 31 will continue until the end of 2026.
"With the support of all parties in the legislature, the Ontario government is proposing amendments to the Election Finances Act that would ... extend the payment of quarterly allowances to registered political parties and constituency associations for another two years," Downey's department said.
Each quarter, the governing Progressive Conservatives receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000.
That's 63.6 cents per vote every three months, based on the results from the 2022 election.
While Premier Doug Ford has long said he opposed the public funding of political parties, his party benefits the most from the subsidies, which were introduced by the previous Liberal government.
The payments go to registered political parties whose candidates received at least two per cent of valid votes cast across the province, or five per cent of those cast in the ridings in which the party fielded a candidate.
CAPTION: Attorney General Doug Downey speaks in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 26, 2024.
