Document 315 of 500

'Canada has lost a giant': Indigenous leader Murray Sinclair - judge, senator and chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission - dies at 73

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-has-lost-giant/docview/3123996838/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Canada+has+lost+a+giant%27%3A+Indigenous+leader+Murray+Sinclair+-+judge%2C+senator+and+chair+of+the+Truth+and+Reconciliation+Commission+-+dies+at+73&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Murray Sinclair, former chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, died surrounded by family on Monday in Winnipeg at age 73.
"The impact of our dad's work reached far across the country and the world. From Residential School Survivors, to law students, to those who sat across from him in a courtroom, he was always known as an exceptional listener who treated everyone with dignity and respect," said Sinclair's family in a statement.
"We know that stories of his kindness, generosity, and fairness will circulate for generations to come."
Sinclair had been in poor health in recent years and had been battling congestive heart failure.
Manitoba's first Indigenous judge and the second in Canada, Sinclair lived a life dedicated to public service. An Anishinaabe leader in the justice system, he leaves a legacy that will have a lasting impact felt by Indigenous people.
He is known best for his work in the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, where he served as chair alongside Chief Wilton Littlechild and Marie Wilson. Their final report documented the stories and legacies of survivors of Canada's Indian residential schools, and its 94 calls to action have become a road map for Canada on proceeding toward reconciliation with Indigenous Peoples.
He is survived by his five children and several grandchildren. His wife, Katherine, died in June.
Reactions to Sinclair's death quickly spread across the country.
In the Ontario legislature Monday morning, NDP member Sol Mamakwa broke the news to his colleagues to audible gasps of shock from members of all parties. A moment of silence was held.
The House of Commons followed suit with many members issuing their own statements on the late Justice's passing, including Minister of Crown Indigenous Relations Gary Anandasangaree, who called Sinclair a "Canadian icon."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau expressed his condolences on X, formerly Twitter, stating, in part: "With his passing, Canada has lost a giant - a brilliant legal mind, a champion of Indigenous rights, and a trusted leader on our journey of Reconciliation."
"We are deeply saddened by the loss of a friend and prominent leader in Canada who championed human rights, justice and truth," Gov. Gen. Mary Simon said in a statement.
"A kind, wise and generous soul, he had an exceptional ability to inspire people and to touch hearts."
A sacred fire was lit outside the Manitoba Legislative Building on Monday for members of the public who wished to make offerings of tobacco and pay their respects.
Born Calvin Murray Sinclair in Selkirk, Man., on Jan. 24, 1951, on the former St. Peter's Indian Reservation, Sinclair was a member of the Peguis First Nation. His name in Ojibwe, MazinaGiizhik, means "the one who speaks of pictures in the sky."
Sinclair's mother died when he was one, and he was raised by his grandparents. He graduated from high school as valedictorian and later studied sociology and history at the University of Winnipeg before pursuing a law degree.
For 25 years working in the justice system, Sinclair focused on civil and criminal litigation as well as Indigenous and human rights law.
In March 1988, he was appointed associate chief judge of the Provincial Court of Manitoba. He would serve as a judge in the province until 2016.
That same year, Sinclair was asked to serve as co-commissioner of the Public Inquiry into the Administration of Justice and Aboriginal Peoples of Manitoba with Associate Chief Justice Al Hamilton. Their study produced almost 300 recommendations that had a profound impact on Canada's justice system. A few years later, Sinclair would also direct the Manitoba Pediatric Cardiac Surgery Inquest into the death of 12 children at Winnipeg's Health Sciences Centre in 1994.
"It's no coincidence that at virtually the exact same time I was appointed to Manitoba's court in March 1988, Indigenous leader John Joseph 'J.J.' Harper was shot by Winnipeg police Const. Robert Cross," Sinclair told the Star's Deborah Dundas in September. The death was one cause of the public inquiry he conducted with Hamilton.
"There is still much work to do; Canada is still infected with a great deal of racism that influences policy and attitudes and undermines attempts for change - but we need to see that the country has learned some things and needs to continue on this path."
The Aboriginal justice inquiry was the first time Cindy Blackstock heard of the justice. The Gitxsan activist and executive director of the First Nations Child and Family Caring Society of Canada would go on to know and work with the former senator over the next several decades.
"Murray was often first, but that wasn't the reason he did things. He became a first so that we can become the second and the third and the fourth and the fifth," Blackstock said.
"Murray was always a beacon of justice, of dignity and hope, and was laser focused on the responsibilities that we had to this generation of children and all generations of children to follow."
Beyond his career in justice, Sinclair also served as a cultural leader in the Anishinaabe community alongside his wife and life partner, Katherine Morrisseau-Sinclair, an Anishinaabek woman, artist and educator from Ste. Rose du Lac, Man., for more than 40 years.
Blackstock said Sinclair and his family were always at community events and that "his family has been so incredibly generous, not only in sharing Murray with all of us, but they all follow the example he and Katherine put down for them by really evolving themselves in the world in a way that brings light and justice to everyone.
"He set a legacy in his own family and his own community, and he inspired one in each of us who are lucky enough to know him and many who didn't know him but were inspired by his example."
Sinclair was a member of the fish-clan and an Ojibwe speaker. He and Morrisseau-Sinclair were deeply involved as spiritual leaders, both Road Chiefs of the Three Fires Midewiwin Lodge, as well as community advocates. Sinclair was also a second-degree member of the Midewiwin, or Grand Medicine Society.
In 2009, Sinclair took on his most notable role as head commissioner of Canada's Truth and Reconciliation Commission. The commission was tasked with documenting the abuse and cultural genocide administered by the federal government and church bodies during the 150 years of Canada's Indian residential school system. Nearly 4,000 Indigenous children are recorded to have died at the schools, and experts say the numbers could be thousands higher.
The commission began a year after then prime minister Stephen Harper delivered his apology on behalf of the federal government for Canada's role in the schools, and was part of the 2007 final settlement from the class-action lawsuit brought by the Assembly of First Nations and Indian Residential School Survivors.
For six years, Sinclair and his co-chairs travelled the country listening to testimonies of more than 6,500 survivors.
In 2015, the commission released its landmark final report and 94 "Calls to Action."
"As commissioners, we, and those who accompanied us, have been touched by all that we have witnessed, all that we have heard, and all that has been shared with us and with the rest of Canada," Sinclair said at the closing ceremony in December 2015.
Upon receiving the report, Trudeau stated the government's commitment to fully implementing the calls to action. As of 2023, only 13 of the 94 calls are reported to have been fully implemented.
Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak said Sinclair has been a "household name for First Nations" her entire life.
Speaking to the Star on Monday, she continued, "This year, as we look ahead and we focus on the 10th-year anniversary of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, I wish he could have stayed with us longer to commemorate that and remind Canadians that we have a lot of work to do on the calls to action and the calls to justice."
Upon news of his death, the National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation released a statement that called Sinclair "forever the mediator and negotiator who owned a powerful voice as one of the most prolific leaders in this land."
In April 2016, Sinclair was appointed by Trudeau to the Senate, the 16th Indigenous person to be appointed to the institution, which Sinclair would later refer to as "a council of Elders."
While in the Senate, Sinclair also returned to his law career in 2020, joining Cochrane Saxberg LLP in Winnipeg, the largest Indigenous law firm in Manitoba.
Sinclair retired from the Senate in January 2021, going on to accept a position as Queen's University's 15th - and first Indigenous - chancellor. In 2022, Sinclair was invested as a companion of the Order of Canada for dedicating his life to "truth, justice and reconciliation" and for championing Indigenous Peoples' rights and freedoms.
He stepped down from that role this past June, staying on at Queen's as the special adviser to the principal on reconciliation.
In September, Sinclair published his memoir, "Who We Are: Four Questions For a Life and a Nation," centred around Indigenous ways of knowing as well as Canada's future with reconciliation.
"I framed my story as a pursuit of trying to understand myself as an Indigenous person in Canada while at the same time inviting readers to consider how, by asking their own four questions, they will realize their creation story intersects with mine," Sinclair told Dundas.
"Together, we are one beautiful - albeit complicated - story."
Sinclair has received multiple honours for his work, including the National Aboriginal Achievement Award in 1994, Manitoba Bar Association's Equality Award in 2001 and Distinguished Service Award in 2016, as well as several honorary degrees.
He has also been named to the Order of Manitoba in 2024 and named a recipient of the Meritorious Service Cross in 2017.
"It will be a long time before our nation produces another person the calibre of Murray Sinclair," Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew - the first First Nations to hold the position in the province - said in a statement.
"He showed us there is no reconciliation without truth. We should hold dear in our hearts his words that our nation is on the cusp of a great new era and we must all 'dare to live greatly together.' "
In lieu of flowers, the family is asking for donations to The Murray Sinclair Memorial Fund at The Winnipeg Foundation, to "prioritize Indigenous women, children, families, and Survivors."
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Joy SpearChief-Morris Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Document 316 of 500

Another Tory MP wants Liberal housing money that Pierre Poilievre has pledged to cut

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/another-tory-mp-wants-liberal-housing-money-that/docview/3124165619/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+Tory+MP+wants+Liberal+housing+money+that+Pierre+Poilievre+has+pledged+to+cut&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser is exposing another Opposition Conservative MP who has voiced a desire for federal money from a program the party promises to cut, as the Liberals and Tories dashed off duelling letters over housing policy to mayors and premiers across the country.
On Monday, Fraser's office shared a letter from Conservative MP Frank Caputo, who wrote to the housing minister in April to express his disappointment over the fact that the biggest city in his British Columbia riding did not get any money from the federal Housing Accelerator Fund.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promised last week to slash that fund to help cover the lost revenue from his plan to cut the federal sales tax from newly-built homes that sell for less than $1 million.
"Kamloops is in dire need for federal housing funds and the people of Kamloops are tired of being left out of funding announcements," Caputo wrote in the letter, which Fraser's office provided to the Star.
Caputo did not respond to a request for comment from the Star on Monday.
Fraser's disclosure comes days after his office revealed how five other Conservative MPs - B.C's Dan Albas, Alberta's Michael Cooper, Ontario's Adam Chambers and Lianne Rood, and Rob Moore from New Brunswick - had written to Fraser seeking federal dollars for their communities from the Housing Accelerator Fund. The Canadian Press first reported on the letters.
In a statement to the Star on Monday, Fraser defended the fund and claimed that some of Poilievre's own MPs disagree with his desire to cut the program.
"In fact, they've been going behind his back to support the Housing Accelerator Fund," Fraser's statement said.
"I hope more Conservative MPs will add their names to the list of representatives who are standing up for their communities by standing against Mr. Poilievre's proposed cuts to housing in their hometown."
Poilievre's spokesperson did not directly address Fraser's claims that Tories support the accelerator fund, but echoed the party leader's criticism that Liberal policies have failed to rein in rising housing costs.
"Unlike their multibillion dollar so-called "housing accelerator" photo op fund, this common sense policy benefits all Canadians without bureaucratic applications and costly photo ops designed for Liberal political gain," Sebastian Skamski said by email, referring to Poilievre's promised tax cut.
With Fraser's office hinting it has more letters of support from Conservative MPs, a Tory official suggested the party isn't trying to hide anything when they provided a list of 15 Conservative MPs whom the official said have written similar things as Caputo did to the housing minister.
Poilievre told reporters last week that a future Conservative government could save $3 billion by cutting the accelerator fund, and another $5 billion by slashing the Liberal government's Housing Infrastructure Fund, a program that is meant to start doling out money next year to help pay for water and sewer systems for housing developments.
Those savings would help cover the lost revenues of Poilievre's pledge to eliminate the federal sales tax from new homes and condos that sell for less than $1 million - a policy that was welcomed by many housing experts, including the Smart Prosperity Institute's Mike Moffatt, who has presented to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his cabinet about Canada's housing crunch on at least two occasions.
Poilievre said last week that the tax cut would spur construction of an additional 30,000 homes, and cost roughly $4 billion, each year.
In the days since, Fraser has jumped on Poilievre's pledge to cut the Housing Accelerator Fund, claiming the pledge jeopardizes more than $3 billion in funding that has been committed to municipalities who have agreed to change zoning rules and local policies to speed up and densify housing construction. According to Fraser's office, the minister had sent 141 letters as of Monday to municipal leaders across Canada - including Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and officials from at least 10 other cities in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area - warning them about Poilievre's planned cut.
A spokesperson for Fraser explained that $1.2 billion of the accelerator's $4.4 billion budget has already been distributed, with the remainder set to be rolled out to municipalities over the next three years if they continue to hit milestones agreed to in more than 170 Housing Accelerator Fund deals that have been signed across the country.
Fraser's letter to Chow, dated Oct. 30, noted how the city inked an agreement to get more than $471 million in federal funding through this program, which the missive said is supposed to lead to an additional 53,000 permits for new homes over 10 years.
During question period, Conservative MP Kyle Seeback raised questions about how this money is going to Toronto while housing starts have dropped. Seeback claimed that Liberal policies are "funding bureaucracies" while raising development charges and not getting more new homes built, prompting Liberal MP Peter Fragiskatos to respond that the Conservative plan would "only" see cuts to programs.
But while Fraser writes to municipalities, Poilievre has penned his own letter to all the premiers, using them to match the Conservatives' plan to eliminate the federal sales tax from new homes that sell for under $1 million by similarly cutting provincial sales taxes.
"Across the country, taxes and government fees add unnecessary costs to homebuyers," said Poilievre's letter, which was provided to the Star, and outlines the Conservatives' long-standing criticism of how housing costs have jumped significantly since the Trudeau Liberals took office in 2015.
"I understand that all provinces are different. But if you match my announcement with a provincial sales tax cut of your own on new homes, you will save your residents tens of thousands of dollars," the letter said.
Last year, when the Liberal government announced it would cut the federal sales tax on new rental buildings to make it cheaper to build them, provinces like Ontario followed suit.
Colin Blachar, a spokesperson for Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, told the Star by email Monday that the province already provides a 75-per-cent rebate on provincial sales tax on new homes. But the value of that tax break is capped at $24,000.
Though less generous than what Poilievre is proposing, Blachar noted the provincial rebate is larger than the current rebate for federal sales tax that was created in 1991, which covers 36 per cent of the federal sales tax for homes that sell for less than $350,000.
"We will continue to work with all levels of government to get more homes built faster so that more people can afford to buy a home," Blachar said.
CAPTION: Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser rises during question period, in Ottawa on Oct. 29, 2024.

Housing minister wants former staffer to hand over personal emails related to Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/housing-minister-wants-former-staffer-hand-over/docview/3124165614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Housing+minister+wants+former+staffer+to+hand+over+personal+emails+related+to+Greenbelt&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's housing minister is calling on the former political staffer at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal to hand over any emails from his personal account that may relate to the province's controversial and since-reversed decision to open up the protected area to development.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra spoke to reporters at Queen's Park on Monday after the Star published an article about how Ryan Amato, the former chief of staff to Calandra's predecessor, Steve Clark, had refused to provide any Greenbelt-related emails he may have sent or received from his personal Gmail account. Amato's refusal was in response to a freedom of information request from the Star.
Amato did not respond to questions for this story. He resigned from the government in August 2023 in the wake of a scathing Auditor General's report, which denounced the use of personal email by political staff in the housing ministry involved in Greenbelt decision-making.
On Monday, Calandra said his ministry will co-operate with all authorities - including the RCMP, which is still investigating the ill-fated scheme - to provide whatever information it can.
"If there's anything outstanding that the ministry needs to provide, we will provide it," he said.
Amato's refusal to share his personal emails came to light in submissions by the housing ministry to the Information and Privacy Commissioner (IPC) as part of an appeal by the Star, which filed an FOI in August 2023 for Amato's personal emails related to the Greenbelt.
The province initially only provided a handful of emails Amato had forwarded from his personal account to his government account. The Star is arguing that additional records exist, something Amato's lawyers have described as "merely speculation."
Lawyers for the province said they have formally asked Amato to hand over any Greenbelt-related emails he may have sent or received from his personal account and that Amato "twice refused." They told the IPC they have no "legal mechanism" to force their former employee to co-operate.
Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles also asked about the Star's story in Question Period on Monday, directing a question to Premier Doug Ford about why political staff used personal email to communicate with Greenbelt lobbyists in the first place.
Responding in Ford's place, PC MP Will Bouma said the Ford government has "zero tolerance" for any wrongdoing and the province will "pursue every avenue to request that any relevant records be turned over."
CAPTION: Ryan Amato, former chief of staff of then housing minister Steve Clark.

Document 318 of 500

'Canada has lost a giant': Murray Sinclair, judge, senator and chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, dies at age 73

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-has-lost-giant-murray-sinclair-judge/docview/3124165296/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Canada+has+lost+a+giant%27%3A+Murray+Sinclair%2C+judge%2C+senator+and+chair+of+the+Truth+and+Reconciliation+Commission%2C+dies+at+age+73&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Murray Sinclair, former chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, has died at age 73.
"Peacefully and surrounded by love" from his family, Sinclair died Monday in Winnipeg after battling congestive heart failure and subsequently being hospitalized over the past few months.
"The impact of our dad's work reached far across the country and the world. From Residential School Survivors, to law students, to those who sat across from him in a courtroom, he was always known as an exceptional listener who treated everyone with dignity and respect," said Sinclair's family in a statement.
"We know that stories of his kindness, generosity, and fairness will circulate for generations to come."
Sinclair, who was Manitoba's first Indigenous judge and the second in Canada, lived a life dedicated to public service. An Anishinaabe leader in the justice system, Sinclair's legacy will have a lasting impact felt by Indigenous people.
He is known best for his work in the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, where he served as chair alongside Chief Wilton Littlechild and Marie Wilson. Their final report documented the stories and legacies of survivors of Canada's Indian residential schools, and its 94 calls to action have become a road map for Canada on proceeding toward reconciliation with Indigenous Peoples.
A dedicated husband, father and grandfather, Sinclair leaves behind his five children Gazheek, Niigaanwewidam (James), Dené, Kizhay and Miskodagwaaginikwe, as well as several grandchildren. Sinclair had lost his wife, Katherine, to cancer in June.
Reactions to Sinclair's death have begun to spread across the country.
In the Ontario legislature Monday morning, NDP member Sol Mamakwa broke the news his colleagues to audible gasps of shock from members of all parties. A moment of silence was held.
"The Honourable Murray Sinclair dedicated his life to repairing Canada's relationship with Indigenous Peoples. As the Chief Commissioner of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, he challenged us to confront the darkest parts of our history - because he believed we could learn from them, and be better for it," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said on X, formerly Twitter.
"With his passing, Canada has lost a giant - a brilliant legal mind, a champion of Indigenous rights, and a trusted leader on our journey of Reconciliation."
"We are deeply saddened by the loss of a friend and prominent leader in Canada who championed human rights, justice and truth." Governor General Mary Simon said in a statement.
"A kind, wise and generous soul, he had an exceptional ability to inspire people and to touch hearts.
Sinclair's family has asked for privacy at this time. A sacred fire will be lit outside the Manitoba Legislative Building on Monday for members of the public who wish to make offerings of tobacco and pay their respects.
Early life and career
Sinclair was born Calvin Murray Sinclair in Selkirk, Man., on Jan. 24, 1951, on the former St. Peter's Indian Reservation, to his mother, Florence, and father, Henry. A member of the Peguis First Nation, his name in Ojibway, Mazina Giizhik, means "the one who speaks of pictures in the sky."
Sinclair's mother died of a stroke when he was one year old, and he was raised by his grandparents, Catherine and Jim Sinclair, alongside his three siblings, Richard, Henry Jr. (known as Buddy) and Dianne. Sinclair's grandmother was Saulteaux-French Métis from the Fort Alexander area and taught him to speak Michif.
Sinclair showed great promise from a young age. As a teenager, he served with the 6 Jim Whitecross Royal Canadian Air Cadet Squadron, earning top awards for leadership. In 1968, at 16, Sinclair graduated from high school at Selkirk Collegiate as valedictorian and athlete of the year his senior year.
He studied sociology and history at the University of Winnipeg. After two years, Sinclair took a leave from school to return home and look after his ailing grandmother and worked at the Selkirk Indian and Métis Friendship Centre.
In 1971, he was elected vice-president for the Interlake Region of the Manitoba Métis Federation and served until 1973, leaving to work as a special assistant for Manitoba's attorney general. After two years, Sinclair returned to school, graduating in 1975, and then entered law school at the University of Manitoba.
During 25 years working in the justice system, Sinclair focused on civil and criminal litigation as well as Indigenous and human rights law, representing many First Nations bands, Indigenous child and welfare agencies, friendship centres, Métis organizations and Indigenous corporations, as well as serving as legal counsel for the Manitoba Human Rights Commission. In 1981 he became legal counsel for the Four Nations Confederacy and began teaching courses on Indigenous issues and law at the University of Manitoba and giving lectures throughout the country.
In March 1988, Sinclair was appointed associate chief judge of the Provincial Court of Manitoba. He would serve as a judge in the province until 2016.
That same year, Sinclair was asked to serve as co-commissioner of the Public Inquiry into the Administration of Justice and Aboriginal Peoples of Manitoba with Associate Chief Justice Al Hamilton. Their study produced almost 300 recommendations that had a profound impact on Canada's justice system. A few years later, Sinclair would also direct the Manitoba Pediatric Cardiac Surgery Inquest into the death of 12 children at Winnipeg's Health Sciences Centre in 1994.
"It's no coincidence that at virtually the exact same time I was appointed to Manitoba's court in March 1988, Indigenous leader John Joseph "J.J." Harper was shot by Winnipeg police Const. Robert Cross," Sinclair told the Star's Deborah Dundas in an interview in September. The death of Joseph was one cause of the public inquiry he conducted with Hamilton.
"There is still much work to do; Canada is still infected with a great deal of racism that influences policy and attitudes and undermines attempts for change - but we need to see that the country has learned some things and needs to continue on this path."
The Aboriginal justice inquiry is the first time Cindy Blackstock had heard of the Justice. The Gitxsan activist and executive director of the First Nations Child and Family Caring Society of Canada would go on to know and work with the former senator over the next several decades.
"Murray was often first, but that wasn't the reason he did things. He became a first so that we can become the second and the third and the fourth and the fifth," Blackstock said.
"Murray was always a beacon of justice, of dignity and hope, and was laser focused on the responsibilities that we had to this generation of children and all generations of children to follow."
Beyond his career in justice, Sinclair also served as a cultural leader in the Anishinaabe community alongside his wife and life partner, Katherine Morrisseau-Sinclair, an Anishinaabek woman, artist and educator from Ste. Rose du Lac, Man., for over 40 years.
Blackstock said Sinclair and his family were always at community events and that "his family has been so incredibly generous, not only in sharing Murray with all of us, but they all follow the example he and Katherine put down for them by really evolving themselves in the world in a way that brings light and justice to everyone."
"He set a legacy in his own family and his own community, and he inspired one in each of us who are lucky enough to know him and many who didn't know him but were inspired by his example."
Sinclair is a member of the fish-clan and an Ojibwe speaker. He and Morrisseau-Sinclair were deeply involved as spiritual leaders, both Road Chiefs of the Three Fires Midewiwin Lodge, as well as community advocates. Sinclair is also a second degree member of the Midewiwin (Grand Medicine) Society of the Ojibway.
"At the basis of most Indigenous teachings about the path of life is love," Sinclair told Dundas. "Love is at the centre of all things in the earth, in the water, in our relationships. If we begin, and even if we end, there we will be OK."
In 2014, the couple, their daughters and granddaughters took part in the Lake Winnipeg Water Walk, a 1,400-kilometre walk over 28 days to raise awareness of the lake's health.
Truth and Reconciliation Commission
In 2009, Sinclair would take on his most notable role as the head commissioner of Canada's Truth and Reconciliation Commission, serving as chair alongside Littlechild and Wilson. The commission was mandated to investigate and document the abuse and cultural genocide administered by the federal government and church bodies during the 150 years of Canada's Indian residential school system. Nearly 4,000 Indigenous children are recorded to have died while at the schools, and experts say the numbers could be thousands higher.
The commission began a year after then Prime Minister Stephen Harper delivered his apology on behalf of the federal government for Canada's role in the schools, and was part of the 2007 final settlement from the class-action lawsuit brought by the Assembly of First Nations and Indian Residential School Survivors against the Canadian government and administering church bodies.
For six years, Sinclair and his co-chairs travelling the country listening to the testimonies of more than 6,500 survivors, their families and communities. Sinclair also oversaw the multimillion-dollar fundraising program that supported various TRC events and helped survivors travel to events.
In 2015, the commission released its landmark final report and 94 "Calls to Action."
"As commissioners, we, and those who accompanied us, have been touched by all that we have witnessed, all that we have heard, and all that has been shared with us and with the rest of Canada," Sinclair said at the closing ceremony in December 2015.
"We cannot say we are the same as when we started - we are not; but then again, neither is this country."
Upon receiving the report, Trudeau released a statement saying he hoped it would "help heal some of the pain caused by the Indian residential school system and begin to restore the trust lost so long ago," and stated the government's commitment to fully implementing the calls to action.
Only 13 of the 94 calls are reported to have been fully implemented as of 2023.
"You have to remember that we're writing for the future, not just for this government," Sinclair said at the ceremony. "Our view is that this report is going to have to stand the test of time and when people look back not just at the report today and see how it impacted today, they should also be looking at this report in the future and using it to guide their activities into the future."
Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak said Sinclair has been a "household name for First Nations" her entire life, adding that "he was a trailblazer for all of us."
"This year, as we look ahead and we focus on the 10th year anniversary of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, I wish he could have stayed with us longer to commemorate that and remind Canadians that we have a lot of work to do on the calls to action and the calls to justice," she said.
Upon news of his death, the National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation released a statement that called Sinclair "forever the mediator and negotiator who owned a powerful voice as one of the most prolific leaders in this land."
"His invaluable contributions have left an enduring legacy that will continue to guide and inspire future generations towards a more just and equitable society.
'A council of Elders'
In April 2016, Sinclair was appointed by Trudeau to the Senate, the 16th Indigenous person to be appointment to the institution, which Sinclair would later refer to as "a council of Elders."
"I approach this appointment with hope for the future, and remain committed to reconciliation between Indigenous and non-Indigenous people, something I believe in my heart is possible," he said.
While in the Senate, Sinclair also returned to his law career in 2020 joining Cochrane Saxberg LLP in Winnipeg, the largest Indigenous law firm in Manitoba.
Sinclair retired from the Senate in January 2021, going on to accept a position as Queen's University's 15th - and first Indigenous - chancellor. In 2022, Sinclair was invested as a companion of the Order of Canada for dedicating his life to "truth, justice and reconciliation" and for championing Indigenous Peoples' rights and freedoms.
Sinclair stepped down from that role this past June, staying on at Queen's as the special adviser to the principal on reconciliation.
In September, Sinclair published his memoir, "Who We Are: Four Questions For a Life and a Nation," centred around Indigenous ways of knowing as well as Canada's future with reconciliation.
"I framed my story as a pursuit of trying to understand myself as an Indigenous person in Canada while at the same time inviting readers to consider how, by asking their own four questions, they will realize their creation story intersects with mine," Sinclair told Dundas.
"Together, we are one beautiful - albeit complicated - story."
Sinclair has received multiple honours for his work, including the National Aboriginal Achievement Award in 1994, Manitoba Bar Association's Equality Award in 2001 and Distinguished Service Award in 2016, Indspire's Lifetime Achievement Award in 2017, President's Award from the Canadian Bar Association in 2018, the Adrienne Clarkson Prize for Global Citizenship in 2020, as well as several honourary degrees.
He has also been named to the Order of Manitoba in 2024 and named a recipient of the Meritorious Service Cross in 2017.
Sinclair had long been dealing with health issues. In 2007, Sinclair has suffered a minor stroke, something he later referred to as "a wake-up call." He made a full recovery and began writing his memoirs as letters to his granddaughter, Sarah. Last winter, Sinclair and Morrisseau-Sinclair moved into assisted living together in Winnipeg. Sinclair had been living with diagnoses of congestive heart failure and lymphodema. Morrisseau-Sinclair died of cancer on June 27.
According a family update on Facebook, Sinclair had been in the hospital in Winnipeg for the past few months, battling illness before being moved into the intensive care unit about a week ago when his health took a turn.
In lieu of flowers, the family is asking for donations to The Murray Sinclair Memorial Fund at The Winnipeg Foundation, to "prioritize Indigenous women, children, families, and Survivors."
With report from Robert Benzie.
CAPTION: Sinclair shakes hands with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau during the release of the final report of the Truth and Reconciliation commission on Dec. 15, 2015, in Ottawa.Murray Sinclair, far left, with his three siblings.Sinclair's mother died of a stroke when he was one, and he was raised by his grandparents.Judge Murray Sinclair in his office in 1999 with documents from an inquiry into the deaths of 12 babies during or after heart surgery at Winnipeg's largest hospital.Truth and Reconciliation commissioners Murray Sinclair and Marie Wilson are recognized in the visitors' gallery of the House of Commons on June 2, 2015.Murray Sinclair, as chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, in 2015. The lawyer and former senator died at age 73.Murray Sinclair in this 2015 file photo.Murray Sinclair, chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission,in Vancouver in 2013.

Document 319 of 500

Liberal MP spars with Jagmeet Singh over weekend clash at Hindu temple in Brampton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-mp-spars-with-jagmeet-singh-over-weekend/docview/3123997554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MP+spars+with+Jagmeet+Singh+over+weekend+clash+at+Hindu+temple+in+Brampton&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A Liberal MP's claim that pro-Khalistan "extremists" may have infiltrated Canadian politics and law enforcement agencies parrots the lines of an Indian government accused of threatening and killing Canadians, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said Monday.
But backbencher Chandra Arya fired back, accusing Singh of refusing to acknowledge the presence of "violent Khalistani extremism" in Canada and saying he has "zero credibility" on the issue.
Their comments followed a violent clash between Sikh separatists and Hindu worshippers outside a Hindu temple in Brampton on Sunday, which garnering condemnation from Canadian political leaders and a swift rebuke from the Modi government amid simmering tensions between Canada and India over explosive allegations that linked Indian officials in Canada to homicides and threats against Canadians, which New Delhi has denied.
The pro-Khalistan Sikh groups said they were protesting the presence of Indian consular officials at the temple when the violence erupted, leading to three arrests and the suspension of a police officer who attended the demonstration while off-duty.
Arya said the pro-Khalistan protesters had crossed a "red line," blaming them for the violence that he said "shows how deep and brazen Khalistani violent extremism has become in Canada."
"I begin to feel that there is a small grain of truth in the reports that in addition to Canadian political apparatus, Khalistanis have effectively infiltrated into our law enforcement agencies," Arya wrote Sunday on X. "No wonder that under the 'freedom of expression' Khalistani extremists are getting a free pass in Canada."
As Singh also condemned the weekend violence, he accused Arya of escalating tensions with conspiracy theories.
"It is very problematic that an elected Canadian official is parroting the lines of a foreign government that engaged in allegations of serious violence and terror against Canadians, so he should stop," Singh told reporters Monday.
"What we need to see is an end to the violence, we need to see a firm stance against Indian government, we need to be united in our condemnation of violence happening at a place of prayer."
Arya fired back in a written statement to the Star, saying Singh "has zero credibility when it comes to speaking on extremism in Canada." He said the NDP leader "refuses to acknowledge the presence of violent Khalistani extremism in Canada, despite the RCMP's clear statement during their Thanksgiving Day press conference that the national task force is actively investigating Khalistani violent extremism, among other threats," referring to an announcement last month that intensified diplomatic tensions between Canada and India.
A spokesperson for the Prime Minister's Office said Monday that the PMO would "let MP Chandra Arya speak to his tweet," but added that "Canada stands ready to work with India to advance the relationship between our peoples - anchored in human rights, diversity, and the rule of law."
Earlier in the day, Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi had called on the Trudeau government to "ensure justice and uphold the rule of law" in the wake of what he characterized as the "deliberate attack on a Hindu temple in Canada."
Police have not commented on how the violence started.
"Equally appalling are the cowardly attempts to intimidate our diplomats," Modi said. "Such acts of violence will never weaken India's resolve.
Sikhs for Justice, a diaspora group pushing for the creation of a Sikh state called "Khalistan" in Punjab, India, said the protest was over concerns that Indian officials have attended similar events in the past to find informants and target Sikh separatists.
They claimed the demonstration had been peaceful before the worshippers attacked the protesters, and have accused Indian officials and Arya of encouraging violence against them. Videos posted on social media show protesters fist fighting, attacking one another with flags and throwing what appear to be stones at one another.
In social media posts on Sunday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford were quick to condemn the violence at a place of worship.
"The acts of violence at the Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton today are unacceptable," Trudeau said in a post on X. "Every Canadian has the right to practice their faith freely and safely."
Ford said on X that "the violence at the Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton this afternoon is completely unacceptable and must be condemned. No one should feel unsafe in their place of worship." Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre posted that the "violence targeting worshippers" was "completely unacceptable."
Green Party Leader Elizabeth May, like Singh, has the security clearance to read an explosive report alleging some MPs are complicit in foreign interference. She told reporters Monday she did not believe pro-Khalistan extremism has infiltrated law enforcement, but said it "remains a stain on all Canadians, that we have permitted extremism to exist."
"We are strongly in favour of immigration to Canada, of welcoming people from all corners of the world, but it is important that diaspora communities leave their domestic political grievances in their home country, and don't bring them to Canada," May said.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Oct. 30, 2024.

Document 320 of 500

Three arrested after violence outside a Hindu temple in Brampton over the weekend

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/three-arrested-after-violence-outside-hindu/docview/3123786289/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Three+arrested+after+violence+outside+a+Hindu+temple+in+Brampton+over+the+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three people have been arrested after a clash outside a Hindu temple in Brampton on Sunday.
One person was arrested during the protest, which led to an officer sustaining minor injuries.
The incident happened outside Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton.
A video posted to X by Chandra Arya, Member of Parliament representing Nepean, appears to show demonstrators with banners supporting the Khalistan movement, a separatist movement pushing for an independent Sikh homeland in northern India, engaging with others holding India's national flag.
Police said protesters relocated to the Westwood Mall areas in Mississauga and then to Airport Road and Derry Road.
Sikhs for Justice, an international advocacy group, say pro-Khalistan Sikhs were protesting the presence of Indian consulate officials and alleged that Indian officials use events like these to find informants to target Sikh separatists.
The organization is asking that Indian consular officials be barred from undertaking work outside of their diplomatic premises, arguing external site visits "directly endanger the safety and security of pro-Khalistan citizens in Canada."
The Indian high commission in Ottawa did not immediately respond to these claims.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre, Jagmeet Singh and Premier Doug Ford all condemned the violence.
Trudeau said in a post on X that "the acts of violence at the Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton today are unacceptable. Every Canadian has the right to practice their faith freely and safely."
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown also took to X Sunday afternoon to say he was "disappointed to hear about acts of violence outside" the temple. "Religious freedom is a foundational value in Canada. Everyone should feel safe in their place of worship," said Brown. "I strongly condemn any acts of violence outside of a place of worship."
Brown later in the evening told CP24 he hopes "those who are found responsible for violence will be held accountable to the full extent of the law, and they can be made as an example, that if you commit violence, if you're going to engage in vulgar behaviour like this, that there are consequences.
"I know the police are investigating the incident," said Brown. "They're looking at the (social media) videos, they're taking witness statements, but I hope that those responsible are held fully accountable, and I think that will be a chilling effect for those that want to copycat this incident.
"There are limits to the right to protest. It does not include violence, it does not include intimidation."
Last month, Canada expelled six Indian diplomats over allegations they used their positions to collect information on Canadians in the pro-Khalistan independence movement and then passed the details on to criminal gangs who targeted the individuals directly.
India has long accused Canada of harbouring pro-Khalistan terrorists that New Delhi has asked Ottawa to extradite, though Canadian officials say those requests often lack adequate proof.
On Friday, a British Columbia Supreme Court judge granted an order establishing a buffer zone surrounding a large Sikh temple in the province expecting confrontations ahead of Indian consular officials visiting this weekend.
Leaders of the Sikh temple made the request predicting "intense protests" at two Indian consular events.
The incident comes on the 40th anniversary of the 1984 anti-Sikh riots where 2146 Sikh's were killed in Delhi according to a 2005 report by India's Ministry of Home Affairs.
With files from The Canadian Press
Clarification - Nov. 4, 2024
This article was edited from a previous version to make clear that Khalistan is a proposed independent Sikh state that the Khalistan separatist movement supports.
CAPTION: Peel police were made aware of reports of violence at a demonstration outside a Brampton temple.

Is Remembrance Day a stat holiday in Ontario? Most provinces give the day off or provide holiday pay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-remembrance-day-stat-holiday-ontario-most/docview/3123786152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Remembrance+Day+a+stat+holiday+in+Ontario%3F+Most+provinces+give+the+day+off+or+provide+holiday+pay&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the weather cools and boots crunch the fallen leaves on the sidewalk, another sign of the seasonal change is the emergence of distinct red poppy pins on the lapels of coats to mark Remembrance Day.
Nov. 11 marks the anniversary of the armistice that ended the First World War, but has since evolved into a day to commemorate all Canadian Armed Forced and RCMP veterans who have been killed in the line of duty. A moment of silence at 11 a.m. is usually observed at schools and workplaces to mark the time when the armistice went into effect.
But the day isn't like other holidays - it's marked on calendars and there are official ceremonies - but it isn't a statutory holiday, at least not for Ontarians.
Here's what you need to know about why you probably aren't getting the day off.
Is Remembrance Day a statutory holiday in Canada?
Remembrance Day is a stat holiday and workers get holiday pay or the day off in all the provinces and territories except for Ontario, Manitoba, and Quebec. While the day isn't a federal stat holiday in Nova Scotia, the province gives most workers the day off through its own provincial act.
Across the country, workers of federally regulated businesses, like banks and Canada Post offices, get the day off as well.
British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, Yukon, Northwest Territories, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador and Prince Edward Island treat Remembrance Day like a federal stat, allowing workers either holiday pay or the day off.
When Remembrance Day falls on a weekend or a non-working day for employees working in certain provinces like Newfoundland and Labrador or Saskatchewan, or for federally regulated businesses, they can get holiday pay or a day off in lieu - but that's not the case for Ontario.
Why isn't Remembrance Day a stat holiday in Ontario?
Besides certain exceptions, Ontario remains one of the few provinces that continues to have schools and businesses open on Remembrance Day. And while there have been attempts to change that, the Royal Canadian Legion opposes making the day a stat, fearing that the holiday would transform into just another long weekend instead of a day of reflection.
Both the federal and provincial governments have tried to change Remembrance Day into a stat. In 2010, Tory MPP Lisa MacLeod (Nepean) put forward a private member's bill proposing to swap the Family Day stat holiday for Remembrance Day. A few years later in 2015, NDP MP Dan Harris (Scarborough Southwest) put forward a bill to change Remembrance Day to a stat holiday across the country. Neither bill made it past a second reading.
Where can I get a poppy?
The Royal Canadian Legion distributes poppies to large businesses that usually carry them in-store. Certain banks like RBC, BMO and HSBC, or big box stores like Bulk Barn, Costco, Home Depot, Shoppers Drug Mart, Best Buy and Michaels usually carry poppies at some locations. Many LCBO locations also have poppies available. The Royal Canadian Legion also sells a reusable pin online for $2.
While poppies are free, the Legion "gratefully accepts donations to the Poppy Fund," a fund that provides supports for veterans.
Poppies, according to the Royal Canadian Legion, should be worn on the left side, over the heart. The Legion also encourages people to wear the poppy through the last Friday of October to Remembrance Day.
CAPTION: Remembrance Day marks the anniversary of the armistice that ended the First World War.

Doug Ford and Prabmeet Sarkaria will be responsible for the next cyclist killed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-prabmeet-sarkaria-will-be-responsible/docview/3123785956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+and+Prabmeet+Sarkaria+will+be+responsible+for+the+next+cyclist+killed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A proposition for Doug Ford and Prabmeet Sarkaria on their first cyclist kill
Doug Ford takes next step toward removing bike lanes on Yonge, Bloor and University, Oct. 31
After these bike lanes have been removed, I propose that Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria attend the funeral of the first cyclist who dies by getting hit by a car on Yonge Street, Bloor Street or University Avenue. I speculate this will not be far in the future. I propose that they sit with the grieving widow/widower, parents, children, friends and family. And I propose that Ford and Sarkaria look each of them in the eye and tell them the number of minutes drivers have saved by the lane removals.
Mike Boeckler, Toronto
Get ready for the wrongful death and injury claims
There is one aspect of Premier Doug Ford's removal of bike lanes that does not seem to be part of the conversation. Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the right to life, liberty, and security of the person. Right now, cyclists are protected from injury or death while travelling in the bike lanes established by the city of Toronto. Ford's removal of the bikelanes places these cyclists in harm's way. There could be liability consequences for the province and us poor taxpayers, who ultimately foot the bill. The province could be facing a slew of wrongful death and injury claims if they proceed with pulling the bike lanes.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Make bike lanes bypass lanes for emergency vehicles
The city could counter the provincial mandate to remove bike lanes by taking one single vehicle lane, separating it from traffic with a concrete barrier and designating that lane as a bypass lane for emergency vehicles so that they can get past congested traffic. Such an emergency bypass lane would be wide enough that it could also be used for two-way bike traffic. And because bikes are far more nimble than cars, they can clear out of the way of emergency vehicles much easier than cars and trucks stuck in traffic, thus possibly improving emergency response times. It would be hard to argue against dedicating a lane for emergency bypass.
Paul Kern, Toronto
Turn bike lanes into HOV lanes
How about making the ripped-up bike lanes High Occupancy Lanes (HOV) for two or more passengers, similar to what exist on the highways. Taxis could be exempt.
Dorothy Low, Richmond Hill
Bike lanes make travel between hospitals safe and efficient
I am an oncologist working at multiple hospitals, including Toronto Western, Princess Margaret, SickKids and St Michael's Hospital. I regularly use the lanes to rapidly travel between hospital sites to see in-patients and outpatients who require urgent care. Removing the lanes will delay transportation and delay access to care. Please keep the lanes so I can commute safely and efficiently between hospital sites.
Dr. Derek Tsang, Toronto
Usage doesn't warrant bike lanes on Bloor Street West
I invite the mayor or any other councillor to come to my condo and see the bumper-to-bumper traffic jams all day that the Bloor Street bike lanes have created. From 6 a.m. to 6 p.m., one would be lucky to see 10 bike riders in the lanes. The only time you see the bike lanes used is on Saturdays, and half the riders are on scooters.The rest of the week the residents and commuters are left in constant traffic jams and gridlock. Emergency vehicles are stuck in traffic and commuting times for many have almost tripled as even the traffic lights don't seem to be co-ordinated.
Sheila Gaal, Etobicoke
Why is Chow worried about bike lanes but not High Park?
Premier Doug Ford would like to remove bike lanes from major arterial roads where a lane of traffic has been allocated to bike lanes to reduce gridlock and facilitate the movement of traffic. Toronto Mayor Chow finds this unreasonable because it is an "arbitrary decision and not based on evidence."
Meanwhile, for more than a year the citizens of Toronto have endured the closure of High Park to private vehicles and the deprivation it has caused - an arbitrary decision not based on evidence. Despite many requests to restore access, Chow does not find this situation unreasonable.
We do not expect removal of the expensive cycling infrastructure already installed, but deserve restoration of previously available parking spaces and access to a public space for private vehicles, which have never posed a hazard to other park users nor the ecosystem. We decry the time, energy, expenses that have gone into the plan to fix a problem that never existed and which has denied seniors, the mobility challenges, families with young children the opportunities and pleasures of High Park.
.
Günes Ege, Etobicoke
We need solutions to homelessness now
Ask me to use notwithstanding clause to end encampments, Doug Ford tells mayors, Oct. 29
What housing options is Premier Doug Ford providing to the people of Ontario who are homeless after the cities - where there are no spaces in shelters - follow his advice to evict them from encampments? Perhaps his house and garden are available? Ford needs to offer serious solutions to house people NOW.
Marcia Zalev, Toronto
Ford's $200 giveaway is sad and unfair
Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques, Oct. 30
I find it very sad and unfair that a millionaire family qualifies for the $200 a person that Premier Doug Ford is giving away, while a homeless person doesn't get anything. Maybe Ford could consider delivering them personally.
Herb Hyman, Thornbury
Rail travel has done me well throughout my life
Riding rails offers me peace of mind, Oct. 30
My experience in riding the rails goes back to the 1930s and '40s when my family would access our summer cottage at Victoria Beach on Lake Winnipeg by the only means available - an old coal-fire driven steam engine "commuter" train from Winnipeg. . That was the routine every summer for 10 or 15 years. I was there at 14 when the bomb hit Hiroshima. During the summer of 1950, the year of the Red River Big Flood, I rode the rails as a dining car waiter on the CPR. At 19, I travelled and worked 16-hour days from my Winnipeg base west to Vancouver and east to Toronto, sometimes with weekend "turnarounds" at Minaki and split trains at Sudbury sending separate sections to Toronto and Montreal.
The peace of mind in this rail ride was the good wage and tips I received, which paid my university fees. I felt very lucky to have landed that summer job. As I worked in my profession, my wife and I visited the folks in Winnipeg with our two kids many times on the "scenic route," north of Superior. Later I rode the TGV rails from Paris to Toulon in Franceand from Rome to Venice return in Italy on the speedy Eurostar. In Bangkok, we travelled their very modern subway and skytrains for unbelievably low fares . So yes, ultrafast or steady as she goes, rail travel has done me well throughout my life.
Arthur Peach, Sudbury
CAPTION: AGhost Bike is installed at the corner of Bloor Street and Avenue Road where a 24-year-old female cyclist was hit by a truck and killed on July 31 2024.

Doug Ford warns of the perils of protectionism on the eve of U.S. election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-warns-perils-protectionism-on-eve-u-s/docview/3123785950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+warns+of+the+perils+of+protectionism+on+the+eve+of+U.S.+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ahead of Tuesday's U.S. presidential election, Premier Doug Ford is warning that protectionism would hurt Ontario, America's third-largest trading partner.
Ford - who has expressed concern about the threat by former president Donald Trump, the Republican nominee, to slap hefty tariffs on foreign goods and services - underscored the importance of trade between the province and U.S. states.
"Regardless of the outcome of this week's elections, we stand ready to work with our partners south of the border," the premier said in a statement Monday.
"Above all, we must avoid falling into a 'Buy Canada' or 'Buy America' mindset that would jeopardize our existing trading relationship and instead adopt a 'Buy Can-Am' mindset that creates new jobs and opportunities for workers and businesses on both sides of the border," he said.
That was a pitch Ford used in June with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, who is now Vice-President Kamala Harris's running mate on the Democratic ticket.
While the premier has stressed he is remaining neutral in the contest between Harris and Trump, he said in August he "was happy as punch to see the governor nominated as ... (her) running mate."
"We tossed the football around a little bit in my office - we gave him a CFL football," the premier said, noting their friendly, closed-door meeting focused on trade.
"He understands that. We're stronger together."
Ontario is the largest trading partner to 17 states and the second-largest to another 11 states with two-way trade worth $493 billion last year. Only Canada and Mexico do more trade with the U.S.
"As Americans prepare to vote in this week's elections, it's more important than ever to preserve and build on our longstanding ties of friendship, trade and co-operation that unite Canada and the United States," the premier said in his Monday statement.
"Together, we can support the millions of workers on both sides of the border whose paycheques and livelihoods rely on this critical relationship," added Ford.
"As we work to build stronger economic ties with our closest ally and the largest economy in the world, Canada must remain aligned with U.S. trade policy, like we did by matching American tariffs on Chinese imports of electric vehicles and steel," he said, reiterating his call for Ottawa to hold off on its planned three per cent tax on American services like Google, Netflix and Amazon.
"Similarly, I continue to urge the federal government to delay the implantation of the digital services tax, which is putting Canadian jobs at risk."
At the Council of the Federation meeting of the premiers in Halifax in July, Ford rallied his fellow provincial and territorial leaders against U.S. tariffs.
But in a July interview with the Star's Kristin Rushowy, he conceded he himself has promoted protectionism in the past.
"I'm first to admit, I'm probably the worst culprit when I told governors 'buy Ontario, buy Ontario' - but I think we are changing the language with the U.S. governors now."
In the past year, Ontario made new "economic co-operation agreements" with Illinois, Indiana, Michigan, Nevada and New Jersey.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Fordunderscored the importance of trade between the province and U.S. states on Monday.

Subject: State elections; Presidential elections; Protectionism; Tariffs

Document 324 of 500

Don Valley West voters head to the polls today in unusually contentious council race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/don-valley-west-voters-head-polls-today-unusually/docview/3123785939/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don+Valley+West+voters+head+to+the+polls+today+in+unusually+contentious+council+race&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voters in Ward 15, Don Valley West head to the polls today to choose their next city councillor.
It's the climax of an unusually contentious special byelection that has featured behind-the-scenes jockeying by a former Liberal premier, campaigning by an Ontario PC cabinet minister and allegations of bigotry against a leading candidate. Some observers predict the result could have a big impact on how city council operates for the next two years.
The Ward 15 byelection was called after long-time representative Jaye Robinson died of cancer in May.
Anthony Furey, a right-wing former newspaper pundit, and Rachel Chernos Lin, local trustee as well as chair of the Toronto District School Board, are considered the two strongest candidates.
Fourteen others also registered for the contest, including architect Sheena Sharp, Thorncliffe Park community advocate Shakhlo Sharipova, activist Lesley Stoyan and the late councillor's son Sam Robinson. Late in the race two progressive challengers - Evan Sambasivam and Dhruv Jain - dropped out to back Chernos Lin, who they said had the best chance of beating Furey.
Municipal byelections are normally low-key affairs, because change to a single seat on Toronto's 26-member council isn't likely to affect the outcome of city hall decisions. But Furey's entrance into the Ward 15 race attracted the attention of local political heavyweights.
Former Ontario Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne, who represented Don Valley West provincially for almost 20 years, tried to unite progressives around a single candidate to stop Furey, alleging his record as a political commentator showed he had "bigoted" views against Muslims and gay people. She campaigned for Chernos Lin. Left-wing advocacy group Progress Toronto also accused Furey of "harmful" anti-Muslim rhetoric, and distributed leaflets attacking him in the ward.
Furey rejected Wynne's allegations that he held "bigoted" views, and called accusations that his record as a political commentator included Islamophobic or homophobic statements "out of context smear attacks."
He presented himself as a "common sense" conservative, vowing to fight worsening traffic, tamp down on crime, oppose tax increases, and remove bike lanes in the ward. Prominent Conservative figures like Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce and former Ontario PC leader Tim Hudak campaigned for him.
Chernos Lin pitched herself as the most experienced candidate, and vowed to ease gridlock, institute traffic calming in local neighbourhoods and give residents a greater say in the development process.
Furey ran for mayor in 2023, placing fourth, and hasn't hidden his ambition to seek the city's highest office again at the next opportunity. If elected to council, he would have a regular forum in which to challenge Mayor Olivia Chow, which some observers predict has the potential to further politicize council debates and turn them into a dress rehearsal for the 2026 mayor's race.
More than 4,000 people have already cast ballots at advance polls on Oct. 26 and 27, which was more than 18 per cent of turnout in the 2022 Ward 15 election.
Polls close Monday at 8 p.m. The Star will have coverage of the results.
CAPTION: Monday's byelection in Don Valley West was called after the death Ward 15 councillor Jaye Robinson in May.

Subject: Councils; Voters; Voter behavior; Elections

Title: Don Valley West voters head to the polls today in unusually contentious council race

Ford government knew of children's aid struggles one year before ministry audit of the sector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-knew-childrens-aid-struggles-one/docview/3123785926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+knew+of+children%27s+aid+struggles+one+year+before+ministry+audit+of+the+sector&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One year before the province launched its audit of Ontario children's aid societies, top government officials were made aware of the crisis confronting child welfare agencies struggling to care for kids with complex needs, according to documents obtained by the Star.
A memo, prepared last fall by staff at Ontario's Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services and released through access-to-information legislation, shows that officials were told children with complex health needs are being placed in unlicensed settings, such as hotels, due to a lack of suitable placements in the community.
Prepared for an assistant deputy minister, the memo states that children with complex needs in unlicensed settings are "often" in the care of CAS not because of protection concerns but because "their needs (are) not being able to be met in their home."
The memo, dated last November, also states that discussions between the ministry's regional offices and children's aid societies about "challenges finding suitable placements for children in their care" are "more prevalent" in 2023 compared to the previous year.
"Societies have specifically cited challenges with accessing publicly funded placements for children and youth with complex mental health needs," the memo states.
Child advocates and experts say the government's failure to act after being made aware that vulnerable children are not receiving appropriate care "is an abdication of its responsibility."
Kiaras Gharabaghi, dean of the Faculty of Community Services and a professor in child and youth care at Toronto Metropolitan University, said he was "disturbed" in reading the memo to see the ministry's conclusion about the use of unlicensed placements is "entirely bureaucratic in nature, focused on the criteria for licensing rather than the wellbeing of children and youth."
"It is devastating to watch how children and youth are simply discarded entirely from these conversations," Gharabaghi said in an email to the Star.
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child and youth advocate from 2008 to 2019, said the internal records show the ministry knew the extent of the problem a full year before launching an audit of the sector - and still, he added, the government refuses to publicly acknowledge that there is a crisis.
"The government saw a problem and it looked away," Elman said, calling its inaction "a complete abdication of its responsibility."
A Star investigation has revealed that children with complex health needs in the care of children's aid are being housed in unlicensed settings, including hotels, motels, Airbnbs and office buildings, due to a lack of live-in treatment options. Child welfare leaders say many of these children come into CAS care because families are unable to find adequate treatment and supports - a challenge also faced by agencies.
Children with complex needs have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression. Some have traumatic backgrounds that lead them to substance use, while others have challenging behaviours, including aggression, that exclude them from foster care, group homes or treatment programs, child welfare leaders say.
In October, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services launched an audit of child welfare agencies in the wake of the Star's reporting, including a separate investigation into the death of a four-year-old girl whose body was found in a Rosedale dumpster after children's aid closed her case.
The ministry's sector-wide review of 37 non-Indigenous children's aid societies will look into agencies' operating costs and the quality of child protection services. It will also examine funding spent by agencies on kids with complex needs.
The internal documents obtained by the Star reveal that the ministry knew about what children's aid societies have been calling a "placement crisis" for kids with complex needs nearly a year before Premier Doug Ford announced the audit.
The ministry did not respond to questions about the timing of the memo, including why the government waited almost a year to launch its audit. In a statement, a spokesperson said the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services and the Ministry of Health are "working to support easier and more co-ordinated access to services" for children with complex needs, pointing to the government's historic investments in pediatric care.
Spokesperson Kristen Tedesco said the government has increased funding to the child welfare system and brought in new legislation that prioritizes the safety, protection and needs of children and youth. She said child welfare agencies are "responsible by law" for placement decisions and for overseeing children in their care.
Tedesco said 98 per cent of children in care are in licensed settings and provided examples of unlicensed placements, including mature youth who live independently and older youth who choose to live with a friend after refusing to be placed in a licensed out-of-home program. Welfare agencies also use unlicensed settings as emergency placements, she said.
The memo obtained by the Star states 98 per cent of children in care are in licensed settings but notes that figure does not include data from all the province's children's aid societies. In Ontario, a licensed setting is subject to legal requirements and regulations under the Child, Youth and Family Services Act and is inspected by the ministry, the memo states.
According to the memo, staff prepared the "information note" after learning a Toronto children's aid society had alerted its regional ministry office that the Ombudsman's Office "may be launching a full investigation into the governance and accountability of placing agencies and ministry oversight of unlicensed residential settings."
In September, Ontario Ombudsman Paul Dubé announced an investigation into children's aid societies placing young people in unlicensed settings, such as hotels, rental apartments and office buildings, a practice he said raised serious concerns about safety, privacy and comfort.
The memo, which states the high costs of caring for children with complex needs is leading some societies to run deficits, shows ministry officials last year knew that welfare agencies were struggling to find suitable placements for children with complex needs and were relying on unlicensed settings, including hotels. The memo states the ministry "requested heightened due diligence and oversight for the safety and well-being of children" for those in unlicensed placements.
Tedesco said regulatory amendments that take effect Jan. 1 "will further enhance society oversight of children in their care, including children in unlicensed settings." She said societies will be required to visit each child in their care at least once every 30 days, an increase from the current 90-day requirement.
Gharabaghi said children's aid societies have been transparent with the government about their challenges and concerns with using unlicensed settings but have received little response.
"They know the risks are incredibly high and they're surprised the government has stood back and hoped for the best without a policy-centred solution or even a response to the issue."
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Says Irwin Elman, Ontario's former child and youth advocate: "The government saw a problem and it looked away."

What's in this former Ford staffer's Greenbelt emails? + inaction on children's aid struggles

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/whats-this-former-ford-staffers-greenbelt-emails/docview/3123785924/se-2?accountid=15090
