Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+in+this+former+Ford+staffer%27s+Greenbelt+emails%3F+%2B+inaction+on+children%27s+aid+struggles&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 327 of 500

The former Ford government staffer at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal used personal emails to do government business. He is refusing to hand them over

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-ford-government-staffer-at-centre/docview/3123785909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+former+Ford+government+staffer+at+the+centre+of+the+Greenbelt+scandal+used+personal+emails+to+do+government+business.+He+is+refusing+to+hand+them+over&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The former Ford government staffer at the heart of the Greenbelt scandal is refusing to hand over any emails he may have sent from his personal account about the ill-fated plan to develop the protected area.
Ryan Amato used his personal email in at least three interactions with lobbyists or a fellow government staffer regarding the Greenbelt project, according to records obtained by the Star.
These emails are only known about because they were forwarded to Amato's or another political staffer's government email account.
It's not known how many other times Amato used his personal email to discuss Greenbelt matters.
Ontario's auditor general denounced staffers' use of personal emails to conduct Greenbelt business, saying it "creates the perception of preferential access," and noted that any emails Amato or others sent from their personal accounts should still be subject to Freedom of Information (FOI) laws as they concern government business.
So in August 2023, shortly after the auditor general's report was published, the Star filed an FOI request for Amato's personal emails related to the Greenbelt.
The Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing said its staff asked Amato to hand over any records but he has repeatedly refused to cooperate. The ministry says it has no "legal mechanism" to compel him.
What Amato wrote - and to whom - from his personal email while he was selecting which lands would be removed from the Greenbelt is one of the unanswered questions from the scandal, which is under RCMP investigation and continues to dog the Ford government.
The fact it remains unanswered also illustrates how outdated and toothless access-to-information laws are in Canada, said Matt Malone, a Balsillie Scholar at the Balsillie School of International Affairs in Waterloo who researches government secrecy.
Government work on personal devices is 'an enormous problem'
Government officials using personal devices is "a huge weak point in the (access-to-information) system," Malone said. "It's an enormous problem when it comes to transparency and accountability."
Amato, who resigned in the wake of the auditor general's report, did not respond to the Star's requests for comment, and neither did his lawyer.
Ford himself has come under fire for using his personal cell phone for government business. For nearly two years Global News has been trying to get access to call logs for Ford's personal phone, arguing the Premier regularly uses it for work. (Records obtained by Global show Ford's government-issued phone went unused for months at a time.)
Initially, according to Global, the government said Ford's personal phone should not be subject to Freedom of Information laws. They subsequently argued his call logs should be kept confidential to protect the privacy rights of those who call him.
Ford, who often gives out his personal cell phone number to large audiences and encourages people to contact him directly with their problems, said those who call him need to be assured of confidentiality.
"Just imagine if every single politician made their personal conversations with their constituents public. It'd be a disaster. There'd be zero trust. They'd never call an elected official for help because they know the whole world would know their conversation," he said at a news conference earlier this year.
The case is currently before the province's Information and Privacy Commissioner (IPC).
Amato has twice refused government's requests for emails, province says
When the province disclosed to the Star only emails Amato had forwarded to his government account, the Star appealed to the IPC, arguing that additional records exist.
That appeal is under consideration and no decision has been made.
As part of the adjudication process, government lawyers wrote in their submissions that the ministry has sent letters to Amato - who is not named in the documents and referred to only as "the former employee" - formally requesting access to his personal email and that Amato has "twice refused to provide copies of any responsive records that may exist on [his] personal email account."
They said the only emails within their "custody and control" are those he forwarded to his government account.
Those emails include one from Ford-connected lobbyist Nico Fidani-Diker, in which he simply forwarded a Microsoft Teams meeting link, and four from lobbyist Peter Van Loan, a former federal Conservative cabinet minister who sent Amato examples of municipal councils that had passed resolutions to request Greenbelt removals in recent years.
Contacted by the Star in January about the emails, Van Loan said he shared the information with Amato "in an effort to be helpful." He said the council resolutions were not connected to any of his clients and he was not lobbying for their removal.
Van Loan said there was "no particular reason" why he used Amato's personal rather than government email address. "I did not take note at the time of the e-mail used," he said in an email to the Star. "It was not part of any file on which I was lobbying."
Lawyers for Amato provided a brief submission to the IPC, describing the Star's contention that additional records exist as "merely speculation."
The Star's submissions include evidence that at least one of Amato's personal emails related to the Greenbelt was not disclosed. Amato sent an email from his personal account to Patrick Sackville, Ford's chief of staff, which Sackville later forwarded to his own government account. The email was disclosed to the provincial NDP as part of a separate FOI request.
A spokesperson for the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing said it could not answer specific questions about the Star's FOI request or the ministry's submissions to the IPC. In a short statement, the spokesperson said the ministry is committed to its obligations under FOI laws.
"We have zero tolerance for any wrongdoing and expect anyone involved in the decision-making about the Greenbelt lands to have followed the letter of the law."
CAPTION: Ryan Amato, the former chief of staff to Ontario Minister of Municipal Affairs, is at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal.In her 2023 report, then Auditor General Bonnie Lysykdenounced Ford government staffers' use of personal emails to conduct Greenbelt business, saying it "creates the perception of preferential access."The $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal is under RCMP investigation and continues to dog Premier Doug Ford's government.

Subject: Electronic mail systems; Lobbyists; Lobbying; Freedom of information

Title: The former Ford government staffer at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal used personal emails to do government business. He is refusing to hand them over

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 328 of 500

Province was told of crisis for kids in care: Memo warned that children were placed in unlicensed settings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-was-told-crisis-kids-care/docview/3123658422/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+was+told+of+crisis+for+kids+in+care%3A+Memo+warned+that+children+were+placed+in+unlicensed+settings&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 329 of 500

Let's fast-track high-speed rail projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lets-fast-track-high-speed-rail-projects/docview/3123658266/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Let%27s+fast-track+high-speed+rail+projects&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The government is teasing and tempting us again. Just as Premier Doug Ford has offered $200 for every Ontarian in the run-up to a possible election, the federal Liberals will soon be offering trains as their own election looms in the near future, according to reports. Fast and sleek ones, in the form of high-speed rail (HSR) between Toronto and Quebec City.
It's exciting, but we've been teased with HSR before and been fooled. And fooled again. And again. Will this time be for real or is it a "fool me once, fool me the umpteenth time" tease?
High-speed rail is a Canadian ghost train, our Polar Express: only dreamers and optimists can see it. One of my earliest news memories in the 1980s is of reading about plans for HSR that would connect Windsor all the way to Quebec City. As a preteen boy transitioning between dinosaur and the train enthusiasm phases, it seemed incredibly exciting, but I was ultimately disappointed.
I still am, considering the good news these days is that Via Rail's rolling stock is being updated with new trains replacing decades-old ones. A good thing, to be clear, but those trains will still share bumpy rails with - and be at the mercy of - freight trains. They aren't high speed either.
Even the past was more futuristic-feeling than it has been lately. From the late 1960s to the early 1980s, Canada ran a few "TurboTrains" that went faster than the usual ones. It wasn't like HSR technology being built in places like Japan and France at the time, but still another shot at connecting this vast country together.
Four decades since my hopes were first raised, HSR is still a vital dream to hold on to, but we're wise to be wary of promises. I saw an apt response on social media to the most recent news: Lucy yet again swiping the football away from Charlie Brown just as he was about to kick it.
Also part of the HSR dream cycle are naysayers. We in North America are different, they say. We are car people. We are too spread out to really use rails. The irony of the latter opinion is that Canada's foundational story maintains its creation and existence was possible because of railways - the "last spike" and all that - as fraught and difficult as the project was.
There is real movement happening in the U.S. in bringing high-speed rail to some parts of the country, and a reason why current westward Canadian plans should not stop in Toronto but extend to Windsor and Niagara Falls in order to connect to possible American networks.
The naysayer's sentiments are surely not shared at Union Station where people can be found daily waiting for Via Rail trains in lines that snake all the way out into the great hall. Trains are busy and few passengers wouldn't want to get to where they're going faster. Just look, too, at all the daily flights between Canada's two biggest cities.
Just as the UP Express has diminished the unique convenience of having an airport downtown, HSR makes trips between cities an hour apart by air far less desirable and would render Billy Bishop Airport even more inessential. Considering airport hassles and how early passengers have to get there, a three-hour trip to Montreal on a stress- and pollution-free electric train from downtown to downtown is a real business case.
This isn't theoretical - it's how other places with HSR work, from China to Italy. Half of Canada's population lives between Windsor and Quebec City: our landscape, population and potential prosperity are ready for it.
It's a good nation-building policy that shouldn't be political, and one all parties should get behind and implement as quickly as possible. Putting shovels in the ground helps turn a perpetual dream into reality. Whichever government does manage to get it all started in earnest will be remembered for it the way Eisenhower is in the U.S. for the Interstate Highway System.
There are cautions though, but maybe this time we can go into it eyes wide open. Infrastructure, both renewal and building new things, is critical to Canada's success, but costs for such things have exploded. In the Star last month transit researcher and retired journalist Stephen Wickens laid out how Bloor-Danforth subway trains are coming with a world record price tag.
Here's another dream: perhaps Canadian high costs and our entire way of procuring public projects, from transit to architecture, can get an open and public rethink this time. That might get a bit political after all, but Canada exists because of big infrastructure projects of the past. Let Charlie Brown kick the football into Canada's future this time and stop all the foolin'.
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: High speed rail; Skepticism; Railway networks; Infrastructure; Urban development

Document 330 of 500

Feds to probe concerns over therapists: Promise follows investigation into program for First Nations, Inuit people

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feds-probe-concerns-over-therapists/docview/3123658114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+to+probe+concerns+over+therapists%3A+Promise+follows+investigation+into+program+for+First+Nations%2C+Inuit+people&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government will investigate concerns about therapists with troubling backgrounds approved to provide mental health care to First Nations and Inuit people under its national benefits program.
"Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) has been made aware of concerns related to specific mental health therapists" enrolled in the federal government's Non-Insured Health Benefits (NIHB) program, reads a statement Saturday from a spokesperson. "Indigenous Services Canada takes these concerns seriously ... The NIHB program will follow up immediately, prioritizing client safety (including emotional and psychological safety) and taking steps to address these."
The move comes in response to a Toronto Star/Investigative Journalism Bureau/TVO investigation published last week that found the program has approved mental health-care providers with serious criminal and professional disciplinary histories that include offences such as murder, having sex with a former patient and allegedly abandoning a suicidal patient.
Another therapist made social media posts that offended Indigenous patients and mental health experts, including one article he reposted that said residential schools had the "intent of educating children, assimilating them into the broader Canadian population, and ultimately lifting them out of poverty."
The ISC statement says officials "recognize that finding culturally safe and appropriate mental health care remains a challenge for many. However, due to privacy implications, we cannot speak to the specifics of these concerns."
Response to the investigation's findings has also been swift from Indigenous leaders.
On Friday, the Chiefs of Ontario, which advocates for 133 First Nations in the province, issued a strongly worded statement calling the revelations "deeply troubling" and saying they "call into question the very foundations of a program that is meant to heal, not hurt, First Nations people." The statement urged the federal government to act.
Ontario Regional Chief Abram Benedict said the organization will monitor the government's response to calls for "desperately needed" reforms.
"This cannot be ignored any longer."
The NIHB program covers the medical costs of a wide range of services - including therapy, dental care, medical devices and medications - for Inuit and First Nations people, who face some of the highest rates of suicide in the world. It is administered through a U.S.-based benefits company that received a $117-million, five-year contract with the government in 2018 with renewal options up to 11 years. There are about 5,000 counsellors approved to bill the program directly. Journalists reviewed the backgrounds of thousands, using professional disciplinary records, court documents and social media searches. Only about a quarter of counsellors approved by the NIHB self-reported having cultural competency or previous experience working with First Nations or Inuit clients.
The investigation also found the program prevents many qualified Indigenous therapists from treating First Nations and Inuit patients under the program, because they live in provinces without professional licensing bodies.
ISC relies on provincial licensing for NIHB eligibility and has said concerns about the conduct of counsellors and therapists fall to regulators in each province and territory.
Indigenous mental-health leaders have called that reliance on provincial licensing bodies indefensible, saying NIHB needs to take responsibility for the quality of therapists treating this uniquely vulnerable patient group.
"That said," the government said after the article published Friday, "when ISC becomes aware of a complaint against a specific health professional, we will also review the situation, in collaboration with the regulatory body."
Carol Hopkins, an Indigenous therapist who has worked in addiction and mental health in First Nation communities for over 20 years, said NIHB should go further and "ask (potential providers), have you had any disciplinary action and have you ever had any claims filed against you? ... The perpetuation of harm just continues."
Lori Idlout, federal NDP critic for Crown-Indigenous relations (Nunavut) and a member of the standing committee on Indigenous and northern affairs, said Saturday that the NIHB system needs to be "overhauled."
"There are so many gaps in this program," she said in response to the investigation. "There are too many times where First Nations and Inuit realities are still not understood, especially with academically certified mental-health providers."
CREDIT: Robert Cribb Toronto Star

Document 331 of 500

Violence erupts at Hindu temple: Visit by Indian consular officials sparks clashes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/violence-erupts-at-hindu-temple/docview/3123658082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Violence+erupts+at+Hindu+temple%3A+Visit+by+Indian+consular+officials+sparks+clashes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leaders of the three main federal parties condemned violence that erupted Sunday as Indian consular officials visited a Hindu temple in Brampton.
Videos circulating on social media appear to show demonstrators holding banners in support of a separate Sikh country called Khalistan clashing with others, including some holding India's national flag.
The videos seem to show fist fights and people striking each other with poles in what appears to be the grounds surrounding the Hindu Sabha Mandir temple.
The group Sikhs for Justice said Khalistan supporters had been protesting the presence of Indian consulate officials undertaking an announced visit to provide administrative services such as helping seniors access pensions.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh all wrote Sunday on the platform X that they condemn the violence that occurred at the temple.
"Every Canadian has the right to practice their faith freely and safely," Trudeau wrote.
Poilievre "unequivocally" condemned "violence targeting worshippers" at the temple, adding he would "end the chaos."
Singh also "unequivocally" condemned the violence.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford wrote the violence was "completely unacceptable and must be condemned."
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he was "disappointed to hear about acts of violence" outside the temple. "Those found guilty should be punished to the greatest extent of the law," he wrote on X.
A spokesperson for Peel Regional Police, which oversees law enforcement in Brampton, said nobody had been arrested or charged in relation to the protests, but said police will probe "any acts of violence or threats."
Sikhs for Justice alleged Hindu nationalists had provoked the fighting and has claimed Indian officials use visits to religious sites to find informants to target Sikh separatists.
The Indian high commission in Ottawa did not immediately respond to the claims.
The group is asking that Indian consular officials be barred from undertaking work outside of their diplomatic premises, arguing external site visits "directly endanger the safety and security of pro-Khalistan citizens in Canada."
But Liberal MP Chandra Arya accused "Canadian Khalistani extremists" of attacking Hindus, saying they are "getting a free pass in Canada."
The clashes come as Hindus celebrate the Diwali holiday and amid mounting tensions between Canada and India.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Subject: Violence; Hindus; Diplomatic & consular services; Sikhs

Letters: Doug Ford takes next step toward removing bike lanes on Yonge, Bloor and University, Oct. 31 Ask me to use notwithstanding clause to end encampments, Doug Ford tells mayors, Oct. 29 Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques, Oct. 30 Riding rails offers me peace of mind, Oct. 30

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3123657999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+takes+next+step+toward+removing+bike+lanes+on+Yonge%2C+Bloor+and+University%2C+Oct.+31+Ask+me+to+use+notwithstanding+clause+to+end+encampments%2C+Doug+Ford+tells+mayors%2C+Oct.+29+Doug+Ford+spending+nearly+half+of+Ontario%27s+tax+windfall+on+%24200+%27rebate%27+cheques%2C+Oct.+30+Riding+rails+offers+me+peace+of+mind%2C+Oct.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Removing bike lanes could have terrible cost
Doug Ford takes next step toward removing bike lanes on Yonge, Bloor and University, Oct. 31
After these bike lanes have been removed, I propose that Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria attend the funeral of the first cyclist who dies by getting hit by a car on Yonge Street, Bloor Street or University Avenue. I speculate this will not be far in the future. I propose that they sit with the grieving widow/widower, parents, children, friends and family. And I propose that Ford and Sarkaria look each of them in the eye and tell them the number of minutes drivers have saved by the lane removals.
Mike Boeckler, Toronto
There is one aspect of Premier Doug Ford's removal of bike lanes that does not seem to be part of the conversation. Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the right to life, liberty, and security of the person. Right now, cyclists are protected from injury or death while travelling in the bike lanes established by the city of Toronto. Ford's removal of the bike lanes places these cyclists in harm's way. There could be liability consequences for the province and us poor taxpayers, who ultimately foot the bill. The province could be facing a slew of wrongful death and injury claims if they proceed with pulling the bike lanes.
Clyde McDonald,
Bracebridge, Ont.
Lanes could have emergency use
The city could counter the provincial mandate to remove bike lanes by taking one single vehicle lane, separating it from traffic with a concrete barrier and designating that lane as a bypass lane for emergency vehicles so that they can get past congested traffic.
Such an emergency bypass lane would be wide enough that it could also be used for two-way bike traffic. And because bikes are far more nimble than cars, they can clear out of the way of emergency vehicles much easier than cars and trucks stuck in traffic, thus possibly improving emergency response times. It would be hard to argue against dedicating a lane for emergency bypass.
Paul Kern, Toronto
Usage doesn't justify bike lanes
on Bloor West
I invite the mayor or any other councillor to come to my condo and see the bumper-to-bumper traffic jams all day that the Bloor Street bike lanes have created. From 6 a.m. to 6 p.m., one would be lucky to see 10 bike riders in the lanes. The only time you see the bike lanes used is on Saturdays, and half the riders are on scooters. The rest of the week the residents and commuters are left in constant traffic jams and gridlock. Emergency vehicles are stuck in traffic and commuting times for many have almost tripled as even the traffic lights don't seem to be co-ordinated.
Sheila Gaal, Etobicoke
Double standard
in High Park
Premier Doug Ford would like to remove bike lanes from major arterial roads where a lane of traffic has been allocated to bike lanes to reduce gridlock and facilitate the movement of traffic. Mayor Chow finds this unreasonable because it is an "arbitrary decision and not based on evidence."
Meanwhile, for more than a year the citizens of Toronto have endured the closure of High Park to private vehicles and the deprivation it has caused - an arbitrary decision not based on evidence. Despite many requests to restore access, Chow does not find this situation unreasonable.
We do not expect removal of the expensive cycling infrastructure already installed, but deserve restoration of previously available parking spaces and access to a public space for private vehicles, which have never posed a hazard to other park users nor the ecosystem. We decry the time, energy, expenses that have gone into the plan to fix a problem that never existed and which has denied seniors, the mobility challenges, families with young children the opportunities and pleasures of High Park.
Günes Ege, Etobicoke
Where should homeless go?
Ask me to use notwithstanding clause to end encampments, Doug Ford tells mayors,
Oct. 29
What housing options is Premier Doug Ford providing to the people of Ontario who are homeless after the cities - where there are no spaces in shelters - follow his advice to evict them from encampments? Perhaps his house and garden are available? Ford needs to offer serious solutions to house people now.
Marcia Zalev, Toronto
$200 giveaway
is sad and unfair
Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques,
Oct. 30
I find it very sad and unfair that a millionaire family qualifies for the $200 a person that Premier Doug Ford is giving away, while a homeless person doesn't get anything. Maybe Ford could consider delivering them personally.
Herb Hyman, Thornbury, Ont.
Many good memories
of rail travel
Riding rails offers me peace of mind, Oct. 30
My experience in riding the rails goes back to the 1930s and '40s when my family would access our summer cottage at Victoria Beach on Lake Winnipeg by the only means available - an old coal-fire driven steam engine "commuter" train from Winnipeg. That was the routine every summer for 10 or 15 years. I was there at 14 when the bomb hit Hiroshima. During the summer of 1950, the year of the Red River Big Flood, I rode the rails as a dining car waiter on the CPR. At 19, I travelled and worked 16-hour days from my Winnipeg base west to Vancouver and east to Toronto, sometimes with weekend "turnarounds" at Minaki and split trains at Sudbury sending separate sections to Toronto and Montreal.
The peace of mind in this rail ride was the good wage and tips I received, which paid my university fees. I felt very lucky to have landed that summer job. As I worked in my profession, my wife and I visited the folks in Winnipeg with our two kids many times on the "scenic route," north of Superior. Later I rode the TGV rails from Paris to Toulon in France and from Rome to Venice return in Italy on the speedy Eurostar. In Bangkok, we travelled their very modern subway and skytrains for unbelievably low fares. So yes, ultrafast or steady as she goes, rail travel has done me well throughout my life.
Arthur Peach, Sudbury, Ont.
CREDIT: ;

Document 333 of 500

Feds vow to probe findings of Star/IJB/TVO investigation on mental health care for Indigenous people

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feds-vow-probe-findings-star-ijb-tvo/docview/3123892692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+vow+to+probe+findings+of+Star%2FIJB%2FTVO+investigation+on+mental+health+care+for+Indigenous+people&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government will investigate concerns about therapists with troubling backgrounds approved to provide mental health care to First Nations and Inuit people under its national benefits program.
"Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) has been made aware of concerns related to specific mental health therapists" enrolled in the federal government's Non-Insured Health Benefits (NIHB) program, reads a statement Saturday from a spokesperson. "Indigenous Services Canada takes these concerns seriously ... The NIHB program will follow up immediately, prioritizing client safety (including emotional and psychological safety) and taking steps to address these."

The ISC statement says officials "recognize that finding culturally safe and appropriate mental health care remains a challenge for many. However, due to privacy implications, we cannot speak to the specifics of these concerns."

On Friday, the Chiefs of Ontario, which advocates for 133 First Nations in the province, issued a strongly worded statement calling the revelations "deeply troubling" and says they "call into question the very foundations of a program that is meant to heal, not hurt, First Nations people." The statement urged the federal government to act.

"This cannot be ignored any longer."
The NIHB program covers the medical costs of a wide range of services - including therapy, dental care, medical devices and medications - for Inuit and First Nations people, who face some of the highest rates of suicide in the world. It is administered through a U.S.-based benefits company that received a $117-million, five-year contract with the government in 2018 with renewal options up to 11 years. ISC relies on provincial licensing for NIHB eligibility and has said concerns about the conduct of counsellors and therapists fall to regulators in each province and territory.
Indigenous mental health leaders have called that reliance on provincial licensing bodies indefensible, saying NIHB needs to take responsibility for the quality of therapists treating this uniquely vulnerable patient group.
"That said," the government said after the article published Friday, "when ISC becomes aware of a complaint against a specific health professional, we will also review the situation, in collaboration with the regulatory body."
Carol Hopkins, an Indigenous therapist who has worked in addiction and mental health in First Nation communities for over 20 years, said NIHB should go further and "ask (potential providers), have you had any disciplinary action and have you ever had any claims filed against you? ... The perpetuation of harm just continues."
Lori Idlout, federal NDP critic of Crown-Indigenous relations (Nunavut) and a member of the standing committee on Indigenous and Northern Affairs, said in an interview Saturday that the NIHB system needs to be "overhauled."
"There are so many gaps in this program," she said in response to the investigation. "There are too many times where First Nations and Inuit realities are still not understood, especially with academically certified mental health providers."

Subject: Licenses; Therapists; Mental health care; Native North Americans; Social networks; Inuit

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Company / organization: Name: Inuit Tribe; NAICS: 921150; Name: Indigenous Services Canada; NAICS: 921190

Title: Feds vow to probe findings of Star/IJB/TVO investigation on mental health care for Indigenous people

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 334 of 500

Police investigate violence outside of a Hindu temple in Brampton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/police-investigate-violence-outside-hindu-temple/docview/3123658058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+investigate+violence+outside+of+a+Hindu+temple+in+Brampton&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An incident at a demonstration outside a Hindu temple in Brampton is prompting heavy police presence.
Peel Regional Police posted a statement around 2:40 p.m. Sunday saying they are aware of a protest in the North East Brampton area and that "Any acts of violence, threats of violence or vandalism will be investigated."
Police have not yet indicated if any arrests have been made as a result of the incident.
A video posted to X by Chandra Arya, Member of Parliament representing Nepean, appears to show demonstrators with banners supporting Khalistan, a separate Sikh country, engaging with others holding India's national flag.
The incident happened outside of Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton, the temple declined to comment publicly at this time.
Sikhs for Justice, an international advocacy group, say pro-Khalistan Sikhs were protesting the presence of Indian consulate officials and alleges that Indian officials use events like these to find informants to target Sikh separatists.
The organization is asking that Indian consular officials be barred from undertaking work outside of their diplomatic premises, arguing external site visits "directly endanger the safety and security of pro-Khalistan citizens in Canada."
The Indian high commission in Ottawa did not immediately respond to these claims.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre, Jagmeet Singh and Premier Doug Ford have all given statements condemning the violence.
Trudeau said in a post on X that "The acts of violence at the Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton today are unacceptable. Every Canadian has the right to practice their faith freely and safely."
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown also took to X Sunday afternoon to say he was "disappointed to hear about acts of violence outside" the temple. "Religious freedom is a foundational value in Canada. Everyone should feel safe in their place of worship," said Brown. "I strongly condemn any acts of violence outside of a place of worship."
Brown later in the evening told CP24 he hopes "those who are found responsible for violence will be held accountable to the full extent of the law, and they can be made as an example, that if you commit violence, if you're going to engage in vulgar behaviour like this, that there are consequences.
"I know the police are investigating the incident," said Brown. "They're looking at the (social media) videos, they're taking witness statements, but I hope that those responsible are held fully accountable, and I think that will be a chilling effect for those that want to copycat this incident.
"There are limits to the right to protest. It does not include violence, it does not include intimidation."
Last month, Canada expelled six Indian diplomats over allegations they used their positions to collect information on Canadians in the pro-Khalistan independence movement and then passed the details on to criminal gangs who targeted the individuals directly.
India has long accused Canada of harbouring pro-Khalistan terrorists that New Delhi has asked Ottawa to extradite, though Canadian officials say those requests often lack adequate proof.
On Friday, a British Columbia Supreme Court judge granted an order establishing a buffer zone surrounding a large Sikh temple in the province expecting confrontations ahead of Indian consular officials visiting this weekend.
Leaders of the Sikh temple made the request predicting "intense protests" at two Indian consular events
The incident comes on the 40th anniversary of the 1984 anti-Sikh riots where 2146 Sikh's were killed in Delhi according to a 2005 report by India's Ministry of Home Affairs.
With files from The Canadian Press.
CAPTION: Peel police were made aware of reports of violence at a demonstration outside a Brampton temple.

Document 335 of 500

Justin Trudeau and Donald Trump had some bad moments and some oddly friendly ones - but here's what will really matter if Trump is re-elected

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-donald-trump-had-some-bad-moments/docview/3123545655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+and+Donald+Trump+had+some+bad+moments+and+some+oddly+friendly+ones+-+but+here%27s+what+will+really+matter+if+Trump+is+re-elected&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Word came unexpectedly in January 2017 that President Donald Trump wanted to talk to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Officials scrambled to connect the two leaders and racked their brains: what urgent matter had they not foreseen?
Trudeau had already had a friendly congratulatory call with the newly inaugurated Republican president. Trump, who campaigned on ripping up the North American free trade deal and forcing Canada and all NATO allies to spend more on defence, riffed on Trudeau's famous father, the former prime minister Pierre Elliot Trudeau.
When the call was patched through, tension quickly evaporated.
A jovial Trump told Trudeau it was his first time on Air Force One and he was trying out the new secure phone line, marvelling at the technology.
If politics is personal, not all personal calls are politics. No official readout from either office shows their phone chat. Yet Trump was impressed with his new ride. "Beautiful. Great plane," Trump said, according to the media pool report.
It was an early sign theirs would be no ordinary relationship.
It had highs and lows. It was marked by very public disagreements, and an oddly personal connection that appears to have survived those turbulent years and the gap since Trump was in power.
Trudeau's call to Trump after the July assassination attempt against the Republican candidate was described as genuinely warm, even though the two hadn't spoken since Trump left office in 2021. U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham, while golfing with Trump, had been texting with Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, and relayed Trudeau's wish to speak to Trump, with two sources describing the call as extraordinarily friendly.
Yet the impulsive Trump challenged Trudeau - and Canada - in ways that frequently threw the prime minister's team for a loop, according to several sources who were part of Trudeau's inner circle through the tumultuous time and who spoke to the Star on a background-only basis.
The Liberals believe if Trump wins, it will be messy, fraught and rocky. They also believe the many relationships formed then - and the lessons learned - could come in handy once more.
But is this an era where the Canada-U.S. relationship thrives because personalities align, the way it did when Brian Mulroney forged strong ties with Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush, or when Bill Clinton and Jean Chrétien clicked?
Whatever the public impression may be of a relationship, the reality is prime ministers and presidents can get things done when their political interests line up.
Stephen Harper and George W. Bush signed a seven-year softwood lumber deal not because of any ideological alignment; Conservatives say Bush pushed it over the line because he appreciated Harper's military stance on Afghanistan.
Trudeau, Trump and Mexico's president signed a new North American trade pact despite all the public flare-ups. It comes up for review in two years. What happens next is anyone's guess.
"There's no question that a personal relationship matters," said David MacNaughton, who was Canada's ambassador to Washington from 2016 to 2019. "Having said that, I think sometimes people put too much emphasis on it."
MacNaughton was an integral part of the Trudeau team that began outreach to Trump's circles well before the 2017 inauguration, and in some cases even before the election, to establish connections with Trump's family members, advisers and business associates that they leaned on in later difficult discussions.
But friendly ties are no guarantee, he said in an interview. MacNaughton recalled that when Trudeau was invited to the White House by then-president Barack Obama for a state dinner, the buzz was all about the "bromance" between two progressive leaders. MacNaughton, who had just become ambassador, said he thought Canada could strike a new deal on U.S. tariffs on Canadian softwood lumber after the previous one had expired.
The ambassador met three times with Obama's U.S. trade representative, who finally told him, "'This president is not going to do anything on softwood lumber.'"
"I said, 'Why?' and he said, 'Because he's in legacy mode, and what he wants to have done is he wants the Trans-Pacific Partnership (trade deal) to get through Congress. In order to do that, he's gotta get key senators onside and a couple of senators he needs onside are against any deal with Canada on softwood lumber. So it ain't happening.'
"No matter how warm and friendly and cosy the relationship," MacNaughton said, the U.S. will do what "the president thinks is the right politics or the right thing for the country.
"A personal relationship can help push things over the line if it's close, or stop really bad things from happening, but it isn't the be-all and end-all."
No matter who wins on Tuesday - Trump or Kamala Harris - and no matter who is Canadian prime minister throughout the next president's four-year term - Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre - MacNaughton said Canada has to understand the "substantive reasons" the U.S. takes positions it does on important matters like trade, defence and security.
The same sentiment would apply if Harris wins the election. He said American Democrats are more protectionist and isolationist now than at any time in recent history, and no Canadian politician should fail to heed their concerns.
No surprise that a senior Conservative source, with knowledge of how Conservatives dealt with the U.S. under Harper and how they might approach a Trump or Harris administration, agrees.
"Personalities do matter. Competency, backbone, charm, all of those things matter, yeah. But at the core of it, competency is the most important part. That's where I think Harper and Bush got along. Harper and Obama got along," at least to a certain extent. "They had a professional relationship, and they worked at it," the insider said.
As for whether a Poilievre-led government would get along with Trump or Harris, the Conservative insider said it would take a "level-headed and adult approach" to the relationship, no matter the stripe of the administration, or ideological affinities, if any, adding there are far fewer now between Canadian Conservatives and the Republicans than in Mulroney's day.
"I don't think American demands will change between administrations. Tone might be different, but demands don't change. National interests tend to be pretty evergreen."
Poilievre has not met either presidential candidate, nor has he attended any political conventions in the U.S., his office said.
Harris lived with her mother in Montreal for a time, but Trudeau and she didn't know each other then. As vice-president, Harris hasn't visited Canada, but the pair has met on a number of occasions, in Washington and elsewhere.
Two weeks after Russia invaded Ukraine, Trudeau and Harris crossed paths in Poland and sat down at her hotel for a meeting at a critical time when Western allies were trying to align the NATO response to Russia's aggression. It went an hour over time. Most recently, they met on the sidelines of the Service Employees International Union convention in Philadelphia in May.
According to a Canadian official, Harris said it was a "nice surprise" and showed they both support the North American labour movement. They talked about Haiti, and military issues, with Harris noting she knew Canada was "focused on the Arctic." When Trudeau said Canada was ratcheting up defence spending from 1.3 per cent of GDP to 1.7 per cent, she said "'that's good to hear.'" (That is still shy of NATO's targets for all allies - and still a sore point for Trump.)
Harris told the Canadians the election fight was important to her. It was three months before Joe Biden's disastrous performance at the election debate upended the presidential campaign, leading to his withdrawal, and her nomination.
An official recalled her analysis was Democrats were "going to win, but it's going to be bloody difficult," pointing to how aggressive Trump and the MAGA movement were in their ongoing denial of the 2020 election result, and how they represented "'unapologetic threats to democracy.'"
At Wednesday's Liberal caucus meeting, a source said, ministers and MPs were instructed not to talk about the U.S. election before the outcome is known. Brian Clow, Trudeau's deputy chief of staff, delivered the same message to ministerial chiefs of staff, telling them to ensure people make "absolutely no comment whatsoever" in social media posts or news stories, saying, "we have to work with whoever is elected."
The stakes are obviously high. The Liberal government has ramped up efforts to get ready for any outcome.
Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador in Washington, and two cabinet ministers were tasked in January with leading the outreach effort, supported by a special group at Global Affairs Canada.
Hillman's team has been criss-crossing the states, talking to governors, local officials and federal representatives. Premiers, business and union leaders were integral to Canada's charm offensive last time, and will be again as needed.
In an interview, Hillman said the relationship between political leaders is important insofar as they need to be able to talk to each other and do business together. But she emphasized the Canada-U.S. relationship is deeper and broader than just at the top. It relies on strong cabinet minister relationships with counterparts, and at other legislative levels of government.
"We have relationships at a political level across this entire country. We have relationships with governors, with senators and they represent constituencies across the whole country," she said. "And this is not a parliamentary system - the executive must have the support of Congress in order to fulfil its legislative agenda. And so we pay a lot of attention to building relationships and common purpose, yes with the administration, but also with our allies in Congress."
It pays off. Hillman had prime spots at both the Democratic and Republican national conventions this summer, seated with the extended family of Harris's spouse Doug Emhoff in Chicago, and attending key Republican events at the Milwaukee convention, including a small gathering where the keynote speaker was Donald Trump Jr., and another with Trump's former energy secretary.
During the last Trump administration, the Trudeau government's strategy deliberately focused on responding only to what Trump did, not to everything he said. But a key lesson learned was that Trump almost always did what he said he would do, several officials said.
Expect the same this time around.
It's how the Liberal government believes it survived even Trump's infamous blow-up at Trudeau after the G7 meeting in Charlevoix in 2018.
None of the Canadians - Liberal or Conservative - who spoke to the Star this week would state who they expect to win Tuesday's election.
MacNaughton's hope is that it will be a clear result whatever the final tally, and that the world does not see a close result and ensuing uncertainty that in 2000 required a Supreme Court ruling to settle the George W. Bush-Al Gore battle.
"I think we can deal with either eventuality from a presidential point of view," he said. "But what would be terribly, terribly challenging for the world would be something where the result is legitimately unclear for some period of time."
CAPTION: U.S. president Donald Trump, left, and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talk prior to a NATO round table meeting in Watford, Hertfordshire, England, on Dec. 4, 2019.

Subject: Trade agreements; Political parties; Political campaigns; Presidential elections; Union leadership; Personal relationships; Prime ministers; Professional relationships

Document 336 of 500

'Deeply troubling': Ontario chiefs call for reforms to federal health program after Star/IJB investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/deeply-troubling-ontario-chiefs-call-reforms/docview/3123545633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Deeply+troubling%27%3A+Ontario+chiefs+call+for+reforms+to+federal+health+program+after+Star%2FIJB+investigation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the largest Indigenous organizations in Canada is calling for sweeping reforms to a federal health benefits program that covers mental health care services for some of the world's most vulnerable patients.
The Chiefs of Ontario, which advocates for 133 First Nations across the province, is demanding the federal government immediately review the professional backgrounds of therapists and counsellors approved to treat First Nations and Inuit patients and remove those with questionable histories.
The call comes in response to a Toronto Star/Investigative Journalism Bureau investigation published Friday that found the federal government's Non-Insured Health Benefits (NIHB) program includes mental health-care providers with serious criminal and professional disciplinary histories that include offences such as murder, having sex with a former patient and allegedly abandoning a suicidal patient. Another therapist made social media posts that offended Indigenous patients and mental health experts, including one article he reposted that said residential schools had the "intent of educating children, assimilating them into the broader Canadian population, and ultimately lifting them out of poverty."
"The findings of this investigation are deeply troubling and call into question the very foundations of a program that is meant to heal, not hurt, First Nations people," said Ontario Regional Chief Abram Benedict in a written statement.
"When the government approves a provider, they are effectively endorsing them and saying, 'This person can help you.' But it's clear that this is not always the case. How can someone who appears to believe - or at least entertains the idea - that residential schools were implemented with noble intents, provide mental health care to someone suffering from the ongoing trauma of those very institutions?"
The NIHB program, administered by Indigenous Services Canada (ISC), is designed to cover the health-care costs for First Nations and Inuit people who face some of the highest rates of suicide in the world. It provides benefits - including counselling, dental care, medication, optometry services and medical devices - to those who would otherwise not be able to access critical services.
There are about 5,000 government-approved mental health counsellors enrolled in the program, according data the Investigative Journalism Bureau (IJB) obtained through a federal Access to Information request. Only about a quarter of counsellors approved by the NIHB self-reported having cultural competency or previous experience working with First Nations or Inuit clients.
Journalists checked the backgrounds of thousands of NIHB-approved mental health providers against professional disciplinary records, civil and criminal court documents and social media profiles.
In response to requests for comment about those with troubling backgrounds, ISC officials issued a written statement saying the program is "not responsible for the licensing and regulation of health professionals," and that such questions fall to provincial regulatory colleges that licence psychologists, psychotherapists, social workers and others.
"We invite anyone who has concerns regarding the competence of a health provider to file a complaint with the relevant provincial/territorial regulatory body."
It's a response that has failed to satisfy First Nations leaders.
"Indigenous Services Canada cannot sit idly by while this broken mental health system does further damage to vulnerable First Nation communities," reads the Chiefs of Ontario statement.
Shawanaga First Nation Chief Adam Pawis characterized the federal government's response this way: "Frankly, that's bogus. We have treaties with the Crown that must be honoured."
Neither ISC officials or federal Indigenous Services Minister Patty Hajdu responded to questions about the Chiefs of Ontario statement by deadline.
The Star/IJB investigation also found that despite a shortage of culturally sensitive therapists, thousands of experienced Indigenous counsellors are denied NIHB enrolment because they live in provinces that don't have regulated licensing bodies required for NIHB eligibility.
Lori Idlout, federal NDP critic of Crown-Indigenous relations (Nunavut) and a member of the standing committee on Indigenous and Northern Affairs, said in an interview Saturday that the NIHB system needs to be "overhauled." "There are so many gaps in this program," she said in response to the investigation. "There are too many times where First Nations and Inuit realities are still not understood, especially with academically-certified mental health providers."Indigenous provincial NDP MPP Sol Mamakwa, who represents the riding of Kiiwetinoong in Sioux Lookout, Ont., says inaccessible quality mental health services for First Nations people has been allowed to continue without action.
"These governments have the resources and they don't care. I've learned that's how oppression comes and works. It's the youth, it's the children that pay in full with their lives."
The "broken" NIHB system has been the target of Indigenous complaints for decades, says Chiefs of Ontario's Benedict.
"This investigation amplifies the voices ... Now maybe our calls for change will be met with action. Urgent reforms are needed to address the serious and harmful faults in the program."
With files from Wendy-Ann Clarke, Owen Thompson and Declan Keogh/Investigative Journalism Bureau
CAPTION: Lori Idlout, federal NDP critic of Crown-Indigenous relations and a member of the standing committee on Indigenous and Northern Affairs, said the Non-Insured Health Benefits program system needs to be "overhauled."Ontario NDP MPP Sol Mamakwa, who represents the riding of Kiiwetinoong in Sioux Lookout, says the NIHB system has been designed to fail First Nations people.Chiefs of Ontario' Regional Chief Abram Benedict says Star/IJB investigation findings about federal health-care benefits program are "deeply troubling" and calls for immediate reforms to protect vulnerable First Nations patients.

Document 337 of 500

The worst parts of American politics are creeping into Canada. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/worst-parts-american-politics-are-creeping-into/docview/3123545628/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+worst+parts+of+American+politics+are+creeping+into+Canada.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the question bedevilling progressive-leaning people north and south of the Canada-U.S. border throughout this raucous, election-filled fall - how has the race against the conservative right become so close?
You hear it from people voting for Democratic contender Kamala Harris in Tuesday's presidential vote. How on earth is Donald Trump, a convicted felon, an election denier, the man who inspired a mob to stage a deadly riot on Capitol Hill four years ago, even a serious contender?
You heard it in British Columbia's recent election, which finally produced a squeaker of a majority win for incumbent premier David Eby this week. The B.C. Conservatives came very close to denying the New Democrats that victory, even in an election peppered with racist comments by candidates and a leader, John Rustad, who has flirted with anti-vaccine sentiment.
Shachi Kurl, of the Angus Reid Institute, which has done some interesting polling on polarization in recent months, says of the B.C. election: "I've never seen the kind of negativity and polarization - in terms of language, in terms of campaign advertising."
This how-did-we-get-here question is also being asked by some of Justin Trudeau's beleaguered Liberals, incredulous that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is so far ahead in the polls, when he seems to break all the rules of civil disagreement in politics. How has the battle between Liberals and Conservatives become so polarized and personal in this country?
At least on the surface, it looks like Canada is drifting toward the deep divisions that have characterized the Trump-Harris race and have made Tuesday's result look like it's going to be too close to call. Of course we're seeing polarization creep across the border, says Kurl. "We're not insulated ... It's here already here, and it's been here for some time."
To explore why it's come to this in the United States, I talked to Canada's own David Frum, staff writer for The Atlantic and one of the many prominent Republicans who worked for George W. Bush, who is supporting Harris over Trump. Frum mailed in his vote for Harris this past week.
Cautiously, Frum wonders whether Tuesday will be all that close. Certainly, he admits, that's an optimistic view, coming from someone who has written deeply and profoundly over how Trump has damaged the Republican brand.
While he doesn't want to come out sounding totally dismissive of polling, Frum says he is thinking a lot these days about how "robust" it is, compared to even five or 10 years ago. He cites the familiar reservations - how accurate a sample can be when ordinary voters are so hard to reach and engage, and what he describes as the "herding" effect, in which pollsters tend to herd toward similar results, so as not to be an outlier.
"I'm not saying the polls are wrong. I'm just saying polls have been less reliable in the 2010s and 2020s than they were in the 1980s," Frum says.
But what if polling itself is making people more polarized, forcing them into binary choices? That sentiment has been expressed by Yale University's Timothy Snyder, a historian who delves into American political culture, including his new book, On Freedom.
Last weekend, Snyder sat down for a conversation with the CBC's Matt Galloway at a Vancouver writers' festival and their conversation was broadcast on The Current.
Snyder says that all the attention to horse-race polling forces people into making binary, either-or choices, when your average voter tends to drift toward the complicated middle.
"Most people, the vast majority, actually prefer Kamala Harris's policies, but that's not reported in America, let alone the rest of the world, because we're stuck on the polls," Snyder said.
Kurl, naturally, doesn't buy that idea, saying polls don't create the problem, they just report it. She and Frum agree that partisan politics, as it is now conducted, creates the conditions for the us-versus-them polarization.
In real life, Frum explains, people can believe in contradictory things, but partisan politics doesn't have much tolerance for contradiction. "If you're someone who's a highly connected person in politics, you know there's liberal, there's conservative, and then it makes sense" to divide the world that way, says Frum. This is also why he's skeptical of the claims that ordinary voters are polarized over policy. "They don't think it's as important as other people do, and they don't organize their policy preferences the way people more connected to politics do."
One of the most interesting polls I've seen this year - one I wrote about recently, was the Angus Reid report on the "missing middle." Without drilling again into the numbers here, it essentially found that most Canadians consider themselves political orphans - centrist-leaning people who believe political parties are either too hard left or too hard right for them.
Kurl says we saw an early warning of this in the last federal Conservative leadership race two years ago, where former Quebec premier Jean Charest's more moderate, red-Tory conservatism was trounced by Poilievre and his harder-right base.
Frum has an interesting explanation for how we got here.
"Polarization is something we see in every developed country, and it's partly the price we pay for education," he says. "As people become better informed, as people become more affluent and they're more prone to vote on the basis of cultural identity."
He describes it this way. "In 1880, you could win somebody's vote by giving them a turkey or a slug of whiskey. In 1960, you could win somebody's vote by bringing them a new dam or a new high school," he says. "Today, you win people's vote by deep social, cultural, tribal identification ... We're just in a more affluent place and our needs are less immediate, so people are much better informed and much more individualistic."
This is why Canadians should be on the lookout for spillover effects from Tuesday's historic showdown between Trump and Harris. We're not just spectators.
Kurl believes Canada could resist the excesses of that polarization before the next election here, but it would take leadership, and at present she doesn't see any leaders trying to bridge the partisan divides.
I asked Frum whether he was worried about Tuesday. "Of course," he said. He's worried even when people cautiously predict that Trump won't win, as Frum so hopes, and compares it to how humans would feel if told that a giant comet "probably" won't hit the Earth.
By that same metaphor, Canada's political culture won't be unaffected either. The impact of the too-close-to-call race will reverberate here, because when polarization hits the U.S., it hits Canada, too.
CAPTION: Republican presidential nominee and former President Donald Trump, left, and Democratic presidential nominee Vice President Kamala Harris.

Subject: Nominations; Political campaigns; Conservatism; Culture; Voters; Political leadership; Political advertising

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala; Trump, Donald J

Title: The worst parts of American politics are creeping into Canada. Here's why

Document 338 of 500

Is seeing Doug Ford's policies as satire the only way to make sense of them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-seeing-doug-fords-policies-as-satire-only-way/docview/3123545586/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+seeing+Doug+Ford%27s+policies+as+satire+the+only+way+to+make+sense+of+them%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Plato's "Republic," often considered the first work of Western political philosophy, describes the features of an ideal city-state. Many of Plato's proposals, like replacing the private family with a communal class system, appear deeply authoritarian and difficult to reconcile with Plato's emphasis on justice. Some interpreters have argued that these apparent contradictions can be resolved by understanding the "Republic," as a satire: Plato describes these supposedly "ideal" institutions in order to show careful readers how fundamentally absurd they actually are.
It turns out that this esoteric reading of Plato is very helpful for understanding our provincial politics.
Consider the premier's recent arguments against bike lanes. Aside from the health and environmental benefits of cycling, it is an odd moment for this. Toronto has already suffered more cyclist deaths in 2024 than in any of the previous five years. It is also self-defeating for two of the premier's key political priorities: making commutes more tolerable and increasing economic growth.
The single biggest contributor to long commutes is other cars. Research shows that bike lanes can shorten commute times in (at least) two ways. First, bike lanes encourage people to bike and get them out of cars. This means less traffic on the main roadway, especially since bikes take up significantly less space than cars. Second, bike lanes make traffic flow more uniform by removing bikes from the road. This makes it easier for drivers to navigate because they will have to worry less about cyclists appearing unexpectedly. Bike lanes make commuting faster, less stressful, and less dangerous for both drivers and cyclists.
Bike lanes are also proven to increase economic activity. In addition to reducing unproductive gridlock, measures that improve pedestrian or cyclist access can increase retail sales by as much as 30 per cent and each square metre of bike parking has five times the impact on retail sales when compared to car parking. It is certainly true that some businesses, particularly those selling larger goods, depend more on car traffic than others. But small local businesses (another provincial priority!) that rely on enticing new customers are the most likely to benefit from improved cycling infrastructure.
The overall result of obstructing bike infrastructure, and eliminating the little that currently exists, will be worse economic outcomes, and more dangerous road conditions. What could explain such self-defeating politics?
Perhaps the answer is concealed in this absurdity and the premier is trying to teach the province a lesson straight from Plato's "Republic": he is showing us an exaggerated version of the status quo in an effort to wake us up to its fundamental irrationality. This perspective proves very helpful for understanding many of this government's decisions that previously seemed so baffling.
Take Ontario Place and the Ontario Science Centre. Ontarians have not fully appreciated the value of our architectural treasures, cultural institutions, or public spaces. We have let so many important buildings fall into disrepair. Our cultural institutions are desperately underfunded. Our green space is rapidly disappearing. What could make us realize this more acutely than by threatening places so integral to our childhoods?
And what should we make of the proposal to build a $60+ billion tunnel under Highway 401? A traffic tunnel longer than any other, anywhere. A traffic tunnel that would almost certainly require significant and long-term closures of the 401. Initially this seemed to be a well-timed distraction to make palatable the enormous cost of buying back Highway 407. But, like with bike lanes, the real message is now clear: to show us the absurdity of doing more of the same and praying that this time the outcome will be different.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends Question Period as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21.
CREDIT: Thilo Schaefer, Contributor

Subject: Automobiles; Commuting; Retail sales; Bicycling

Title: Is seeing Doug Ford's policies as satire the only way to make sense of them?

Publication date: Nov 3, 2024

Document 339 of 500

Ford's $200 won't improve affordability

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-200-wont-improve-affordability/docview/3123496075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+%24200+won%27t+improve+affordability&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prices are high. An election is nigh.
Affordability is top of mind.
Which is why 15 million Ontarians are getting an anti-inflation consolation prize: $200 cheques in the mail soon.
Thank you, Doug Ford.
What better solution for inflation and affordability both?
Except that Ford's pre-election generosity and grandiosity may be the wrong prescription for the wrong problem.
What if our continuing obsession with inflation is a holdover from the past? Is Ford fighting yesterday's war with outdated cheques to win next year's vote?
It's a question of public perception and political calculation versus economic reality.
Public opinion polls aren't lying when they identify affordability as the top issue for voters. People assuredly believe prices are too high.
Which is why Ford is so shamelessly riding the affordability bandwagon. So, too, is federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who coined the slogan "Justinflation" as a way to pin blame for rising prices on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau alone.
No one questions that inflation surged post-pandemic, thanks mostly to supply chain disruptions caused by COVID shutdowns, plus massive government stimulus and a record influx of temporary workers (who stoked demand for housing). Inflation peaked at 8.1 per cent in mid-2022, and Canadians felt the pinch in food and housing prices.
But that was then. This is now.
The inflation rate has dropped below three per cent so far this year, according to Statistics Canada. In fact, inflation fell to 1.6 per cent in September - a historically low figure, and well below the two per cent target set by the Bank of Canada.
Which is why the central bank has revised its key lending rate steadily downward this year, and did so again last week.
So if that rock bottom inflation rate is evidence enough for the bank to conclude that inflation is increasingly under control, why do ordinary Canadians still fret about affordability? And if central bankers can get inflation under control, why can't politicians make life feel more affordable?
First, homebuyers will always feel nostalgia for the good old days when times were bad and mortgage rates reached record lows in mid-pandemic. Back then, the Bank of Canada resorted to emergency stimulus measures that dropped lending rates to near-zero, but those rates are not coming back - nor should they, barring another massive crisis. Get used to it.
Second, today's remarkably low inflation rate - which has reassured even our stickler bankers - is small comfort for ordinary consumers. People may understand that the rate of increase has slowed, but they also know that the rapid increases of recent years are now baked in - so prices absolutely feel higher than they once were.
Put another way, it's small consolation if the inflation rate has ebbed, belatedly, if today's prices are stuck at cumulatively higher levels. StatCan figures show that a basket of goods that cost $137 in September 2020 would cost $161 in September 2024 - an increase of 17.5 per cent over the past four years.
Admittedly, that was long ago. But people still recall pre-pandemic prices as if they were yesterday - and that's the benchmark that comes to mind when they see today's higher price tags.
People aren't so much fussed about the latest 1.6 per cent inflation rate; they're fuming that prices are at least 10 times higher - 17.5 per cent - than they once were.
So are we stuck with the cumulative increases of recent years?
One serious remedy is to raise wages for working people to make up for lost ground, and wages did indeed go up post-pandemic - albeit not for everyone, and the increases didn't always keep up. But wage hikes can only get you so far before you fall into an inflationary cycle of higher pay causing higher prices.
Another perception problem that economists have long observed is that workers feel that they've "earned" a pay hike by dint of hard work. They don't look upon a wage increase as compensation for inflation.
Workers understandably want to get ahead, not see their higher paycheque eaten up by rising food prices. They resent inflation - even if that's what's driving their catch-up wage increase - because it erodes their hard-won purchasing power.
Rather than increasing wages, what about rolling back price hikes? Be careful what you wish for.
Economists refer to price decrease as deflation - the evil twin of inflation - which is almost always bad news. Historically, a deflationary economy goes hand in hand with recession and depression.
Producers respond to price decreases by reducing their payroll costs - either by cutting wages or laying off workers, which in turn lowers aggregate consumer demand. Also, consumers and businesses may delay new purchases if they know prices will be lower tomorrow than they are today - further depressing demand.
All that said, questions of affordability are not imaginary. But the solutions are illusory.
People are feeling the pinch. But don't be fooled by Ford's political pitch, because $200 ain't what it used to be.
CAPTION: Questions from Ontarians about affordability are not imaginary, but Premier Doug Ford's proposed solution is illusory, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Mathew McCarthyTorstarfile photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Anti inflation; Consumers; Inflation rates; Pandemics; Housing prices; Wages & salaries; Economic conditions; Federal elections-Canada; Financial analysis; Affordable housing

Business indexing term: Subject: Anti inflation Consumers Inflation rates Housing prices Wages & salaries Economic conditions Financial analysis Affordable housing
