Document 340 of 500

A delicate balancing act: Liberal government ramps up efforts to get ready for any outcome in U.S. vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/delicate-balancing-act/docview/3123496051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+delicate+balancing+act%3A+Liberal+government+ramps+up+efforts+to+get+ready+for+any+outcome+in+U.S.+vote&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Word came unexpectedly in January 2017 that President Donald Trump wanted to talk to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Officials scrambled to connect the two leaders and racked their brains: what urgent matter had they not foreseen?
Trudeau had already had a friendly congratulatory call with the newly inaugurated Republican president. Trump, who campaigned on ripping up the North American free trade deal and forcing Canada and all NATO allies to spend more on defence, riffed on Trudeau's famous father, the former prime minister Pierre Elliot Trudeau.
When the call was patched through, tension quickly evaporated.
A jovial Trump told Trudeau it was his first time on Air Force One and he was trying out the new secure phone line, marvelling at the technology.
If politics is personal, not all personal calls are politics. No official readout from either office shows their phone chat. Yet Trump was impressed with his new ride. "Beautiful. Great plane," Trump said, according to the media pool report.
It was an early sign theirs would be no ordinary relationship.
It had highs and lows. It was marked by very public disagreements, and an oddly personal connection that appears to have survived those turbulent years and the gap since Trump was in power.
Trudeau's call to Trump after the July assassination attempt against the Republican candidate was described as genuinely warm, even though the two hadn't spoken since Trump left office in 2021. U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham, while golfing with Trump, had been texting with Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, and relayed Trudeau's wish to speak to Trump, with two sources describing the call as extraordinarily friendly.
Yet the impulsive Trump challenged Trudeau - and Canada - in ways that frequently threw the prime minister's team for a loop, according to several sources who were part of Trudeau's inner circle through the tumultuous time and who spoke to the Star on a background-only basis.
The Liberals believe if Trump wins, it will be messy, fraught and rocky. They also believe the many relationships formed then - and the lessons learned - could come in handy once more.
But is this an era where the Canada-U.S. relationship thrives because personalities align, the way it did when Brian Mulroney forged strong ties with Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush, or when Bill Clinton and Jean Chrétien clicked?
Whatever the public impression may be of a relationship, the reality is prime ministers and presidents can get things done when their political interests line up.
Stephen Harper and George W. Bush signed a seven-year softwood lumber deal not because of any ideological alignment; Conservatives say Bush pushed it over the line because he appreciated Harper's military stance on Afghanistan.
Trudeau, Trump and Mexico's president signed a new North American trade pact despite all the public flare-ups. It comes up for review in two years. What happens next is anyone's guess.
"There's no question that a personal relationship matters," said David MacNaughton, who was Canada's ambassador to Washington from 2016 to 2019. "Having said that, I think sometimes people put too much emphasis on it."
MacNaughton was an integral part of the Trudeau team that began outreach to Trump's circles well before the 2017 inauguration, and in some cases even before the election, to establish connections with Trump's family members, advisers and business associates that they leaned on in later difficult discussions.
But friendly ties are no guarantee, he said in an interview. MacNaughton recalled that when Trudeau was invited to the White House by then-president Barack Obama for a state dinner, the buzz was all about the "bromance" between two progressive leaders. MacNaughton, who had just become ambassador, said he thought Canada could strike a new deal on U.S. tariffs on Canadian softwood lumber after the previous one had expired.
The ambassador met three times with Obama's U.S. trade representative, who finally told him, "This president is not going to do anything on softwood lumber."
"I said, 'Why?' and he said, 'Because he's in legacy mode, and what he wants to have done is he wants the Trans-Pacific Partnership (trade deal) to get through Congress. In order to do that, he's gotta get key senators onside and a couple of senators he needs onside are against any deal with Canada on softwood lumber. So it ain't happening.'
"No matter how warm and friendly and cosy the relationship," MacNaughton said, the U.S. will do what "the president thinks is the right politics or the right thing for the country.
"A personal relationship can help push things over the line if it's close, or stop really bad things from happening, but it isn't the be-all and end-all."
No matter who wins on Tuesday - Trump or Kamala Harris - and no matter who is Canadian prime minister throughout the next president's four-year term - Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre - MacNaughton said Canada has to understand the "substantive reasons" the U.S. takes positions it does on important matters like trade, defence and security.
The same sentiment would apply if Harris wins the election. He said American Democrats are more protectionist and isolationist now than at any time in recent history, and no Canadian politician should fail to heed their concerns.
No surprise that a senior Conservative source, with knowledge of how Conservatives dealt with the U.S. under Harper and how they might approach a Trump or Harris administration, agrees.
"Personalities do matter. Competency, backbone, charm, all of those things matter, yeah. But at the core of it, competency is the most important part. That's where I think Harper and Bush got along. Harper and Obama got along," at least to a certain extent. "They had a professional relationship, and they worked at it," the insider said.
As for whether a Poilievre-led government would get along with Trump or Harris, the Conservative insider said it would take a "level-headed and adult approach" to the relationship, no matter the stripe of the administration, or ideological affinities, if any, adding there are far fewer now between Canadian Conservatives and the Republicans than in Mulroney's day.
"I don't think American demands will change between administrations. Tone might be different, but demands don't change. National interests tend to be pretty evergreen."
Poilievre has not met either presidential candidate, nor has he attended any political conventions in the U.S., his office said.
Harris lived with her mother in Montreal for a time, but she and Trudeau didn't know each other then. As vice-president, Harris hasn't visited Canada, but the pair has met on a number of occasions, in Washington and elsewhere.
Two weeks after Russia invaded Ukraine, Trudeau and Harris crossed paths in Poland and sat down at her hotel for a meeting at a critical time when Western allies were trying to align the NATO response to Russia's aggression. It went an hour over time. Most recently, they met on the sidelines of the Service Employees International Union convention in Philadelphia in May.
According to a Canadian official, Harris said it was a "nice surprise" and showed they both support the North American labour movement. They talked about Haiti and military issues, with Harris noting she knew Canada was "focused on the Arctic." When Trudeau said Canada was ratcheting up defence spending from 1.3 per cent of GDP to 1.7 per cent, she said "that's good to hear." (That is still shy of NATO's targets for all allies - and still a sore point for Trump.)
Harris told the Canadians the election fight was important to her. It was three months before Joe Biden's disastrous performance at the election debate upended the presidential campaign, leading to his withdrawal, and her nomination.
An official recalled her analysis was Democrats were "going to win, but it's going to be bloody difficult," pointing to how aggressive Trump and the MAGA movement were in their ongoing denial of the 2020 election result, and how they represented "unapologetic threats to democracy."
At Wednesday's Liberal caucus meeting, a source said, ministers and MPs were instructed not to talk about the U.S. election before the outcome is known. Brian Clow, Trudeau's deputy chief of staff, delivered the same message to ministerial chiefs of staff, telling them to ensure people make "absolutely no comment whatsoever" in social media posts or news stories, saying, "we have to work with whoever is elected."
The stakes are obviously high. The Liberal government has ramped up efforts to get ready for any outcome.
Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador in Washington, and two cabinet ministers were tasked in January with leading the outreach effort, supported by a special group at Global Affairs Canada.
Hillman's team has been criss-crossing the states, talking to governors, local officials and federal representatives. Premiers, business and union leaders were integral to Canada's charm offensive last time, and will be again as needed. In an interview, Hillman said the relationship between political leaders is important insofar as they need to be able to talk to each other and do business together. But she emphasized the Canada-U.S. relationship is deeper and broader than just at the top. It relies on strong cabinet minister relationships with counterparts, and at other legislative levels of government.
"We have relationships at a political level across this entire country. We have relationships with governors, with senators and they represent constituencies across the whole country," she said. "And this is not a parliamentary system - the executive must have the support of Congress in order to fulfil its legislative agenda. And so we pay a lot of attention to building relationships and common purpose, yes with the administration, but also with our allies in Congress."
It pays off. Hillman had prime spots at both the Democratic and Republican national conventions this summer, seated with the extended family of Harris's spouse, Doug Emhoff, in Chicago, and attending key Republican events at the Milwaukee convention, including a small gathering where the keynote speaker was Donald Trump Jr., and another with Trump's former energy secretary.
During the last Trump administration, the Trudeau government's strategy deliberately focused on responding only to what Trump did, not to everything he said. But a key lesson learned was that Trump almost always did what he said he would do, several officials said.
Expect the same this time around.
It's how the Liberal government believes it survived even Trump's infamous blow-up at Trudeau after the G7 meeting in Charlevoix in 2018.
None of the Canadians - Liberal or Conservative - who spoke to the Star this week would state who they expect to win Tuesday's election.
MacNaughton's hope is that it will be a clear result whatever the final tally, and that the world does not see a close result and ensuing uncertainty that in 2000 required a Supreme Court ruling to settle the George W. Bush-Al Gore battle.
"I think we can deal with either eventuality from a presidential point of view," he said. "But what would be terribly, terribly challenging for the world would be something where the result is legitimately unclear for some period of time."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Subject: Political campaigns; Presidential elections; Trade agreements; Prime ministers; Personal relationships; Professional relationships; Political parties; Canada-US relations; Political leadership; Foreign policy

Title: A delicate balancing act:   Liberal government ramps up efforts to get ready for any outcome in U.S. vote

Document 341 of 500

Premier's attack on bike lanes feels like a trap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-attack-on-bike-lanes-feels-like-trap/docview/3123495931/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier%27s+attack+on+bike+lanes+feels+like+a+trap&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Less than a week before Premier Doug Ford unveiled his grand scheme to rip out specific Toronto bike lanes in a hurry, Mayor Olivia Chow fielded a question about the prospect from the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at a Democracy Forum.
The mayor famous for the flowers on her bike basket gave an answer that might surprise some people.
She started off by talking about all the things she's been able to accomplish because of the "new deal for Toronto" she arranged with Ford, and bragged about how it helped lead to the city's recently upgraded credit rating, and why that would allow more spending on things like housing the homeless or buying new subway cars.
"In the grand scheme of things, when we look at the challenges in front of us: building affordable housing, getting people moving, feeding kids in school - all of those things, huge!" Bike lanes, she went on, are important. "But they're one small piece of it."
Against the scale of challenges the city faces - and indeed, more so in light of those the province faces - the configuration of a few stretches of road are relatively small potatoes.
I'll note the mayor's answer here may have been influenced by sharing the stage with her political mentor Marshall Ganz, whose enduring subject is how to build political movements that succeed in persuading people and making change.
Chow was directly making a point about how she needed to keep working with Ford. But she was also making a point of avoiding doing Ford's political work for him.
On the issue at hand, Doug Ford is not operating under the impression that modes of travel on University Avenue are the most important issue in the province. But he's trying to make them the biggest issue in the provincial debate. Because he thinks a campaign in which the Toronto Star and city councillors representing the downtown core march around in helmets and spandex shorts shouting "SHAME" at him is a campaign he wins.
In the immortal words of Admiral Ackbar: "It's a trap."
Admittedly, at this point, it starts to feel a little personal for a lot of us. I've written before about how the protected bike lanes, specifically the ones on Bloor Street and University Avenue, turned me into an overjoyed sometimes-cycling-commuter. If those lanes are removed, I could say my life and health would be more endangered (and those of cyclists on those routes will be), but the likely truth is that like a whole lot of others, I'll stay just as safe, but twice as miserable, crowded onto a subway train or inching away in my car. A lot of people in parts of central Toronto may feel this move is custom designed to make their lives worse.
(And in my view, to no real benefit to anyone, since I believe there are other, much bigger factors in traffic congestion on those routes. But I know the people who hate bike lanes do not agree. And frustratingly, the city has not collected enough data to conclusively prove the point. Not that I think data is driving Ford's thought process, mind you.)
It seems personal for Ford, too.
Ginning up a cars-versus-bikes political war was a chart-topping banger for his brother Rob, who was never more cheered by his supporters as mayor than when he ripped out the Jarvis bike lane in 2012. When they were raging against "bike-riding pinkos," Rob was winning! When other urgent issues took over the debate (things like slashing budgets or rebuilding the Port Lands or Rob's behaviour), they started losing and losing and losing.
Does anyone think hallway medicine is a winning debate topic for Doug Ford? The ongoing Greenbelt investigation? The speed of construction of transit lines or affordable housing? Whatever he's doing on the 413 that's getting snuck into this same bill designed to inspire shouting about bike lanes?
Like big fat cheques for everyone and a fantasyland 401 tunnel, this is an election ploy.
As Chow said last week in that Democracy Forum, "He has an election that he's facing."
There, Chow offered what seemed like hope after a few hours she'd spent on Bloor West in Etobicoke with merchants who'd opposed the bike lanes. She said they had asked about removing the landscaped median to add more car lanes if the bike lanes were staying - a possible compromise. "A-ha! OK! Why not?" Chow said. Planners were investigating to see if it could work. "Maybe we could do both," she said.
I'm skeptical all sides could be appeased. But it seemed worthwhile for Chow to look into the possibility.
In governing, a compromise in which everyone wins by getting what they want is a great outcome. But in an election campaign, there is no "everyone wins." You need to pick fights you think help you and hurt others. Which is why rather than looking into any possible compromise, a premier might prefer to ratchet up the conflict.
CAPTION: Bike lanes in downtown Toronto are not the most important issue in the province, Edward Keenan writes. But Premier Doug Ford thinks an election campaign in which city councillors march around in helmets and spandex shorts shouting "SHAME" at him is a campaign he wins.Star Staff Toronto Starfile photo
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Document 342 of 500

Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-200-cheques-are-yesterdays-solution/docview/3123363217/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+cheques+are+yesterday%27s+solution+to+today%27s+worries+about+inflation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prices are high. An election is nigh.
Affordability is top of mind.
Which is why 15 million Ontarians are getting an anti-inflation consolation prize: $200 cheques in the mail soon.
Thank you, Doug Ford.
What better solution for inflation and affordability both?
Except that Ford's pre-election generosity and grandiosity may be the wrong prescription for the wrong problem.
What if our continuing obsession with inflation is a holdover from the past? Is Ford fighting yesterday's war with outdated cheques to win next year's vote?
It's a question of public perception and political calculation versus economic reality.
Public opinion polls aren't lying when they identify affordability as the top issue for voters. People assuredly believe prices are too high.
Which is why Ford is so shamelessly riding the affordability bandwagon. So, too, is federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who coined the slogan "Justinflation" as a way to pin blame for rising prices on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau alone.
No one questions that inflation surged post-pandemic, thanks mostly to supply chain disruptions caused by COVID shutdowns, plus massive government stimulus and a record influx of temporary workers (who stoked demand for housing). Inflation peaked at 8.1 per cent in mid-2022, and Canadians felt the pinch in food and housing prices.
But that was then. This is now.
The inflation rate has dropped below three per cent so far this year, according to Statistics Canada. In fact, inflation fell to 1.6 per cent in September - a historically low figure, and well below the two per cent target set by the Bank of Canada.
Which is why the central bank has revised its key lending rate steadily downward this year, and did so again last week.
So if that rock bottom inflation rate is evidence enough for the bank to conclude that inflation is increasingly under control, why do ordinary Canadians still fret about affordability? And if central bankers can get inflation under control, why can't politicians make life feel more affordable?
First, homebuyers will always feel nostalgia for the good old days when times were bad and mortgage rates reached record lows in mid-pandemic. Back then, the Bank of Canada resorted to emergency stimulus measures that dropped lending rates to near-zero, but those rates are not coming back - nor should they, barring another massive crisis. Get used to it.
Second, today's remarkably low inflation rate - which has reassured even our stickler bankers - is small comfort for ordinary consumers. People may understand that the rate of increase has since slowed, but they also know that the rapid increases of recent years are now baked in - so prices absolutely feel higher than they once were.
Put another way, it's small consolation if the inflation rate has ebbed, belatedly, if today's prices are stuck at cumulatively higher levels. StatCan figures show that a basket of goods that cost $137 in September 2020 would cost $161 in September 2024 - an increase of 17.5 per cent over the past four years.
Admittedly, that was long ago. But people still recall pre-pandemic prices as if they were yesterday - and that's the benchmark that comes to mind when they see today's higher price tags.
People aren't so much fussed about the latest 1.6 per cent inflation rate; they're fuming that prices are at least 10 times higher - 17.5 per cent - than they once were.
So are we stuck with the cumulative increases of recent years?
One serious remedy is to raise wages for working people to make up for lost ground, and wages did indeed go up post-pandemic - albeit not for everyone, and the increases didn't always keep up. But wage hikes can only get you so far before you fall into an inflationary cycle of higher pay causing higher prices.
Another perception problem that economists have long observed is that workers feel that they've "earned" a pay hike by dint of hard work. They don't look upon a wage increase as compensation for inflation.
Workers understandably want to get ahead, not see their higher pay cheque eaten up by rising food prices. They resent inflation - even if that's what's driving their catch-up wage increase - because it erodes their hard-won purchasing power.
Rather than increasing wages, what about rolling back price hikes? Be careful what you wish for.
Economists refer to price decrease as deflation - the evil twin of inflation - which is almost always bad news. Historically, a deflationary economy goes hand in hand with recession and depression.
Producers respond to price decreases by reducing their payroll costs - either by cutting wages or laying off workers, which in turn lowers aggregate consumer demand. Also, consumers and businesses may delay new purchases if they know prices will be lower tomorrow than they are today - further depressing demand.
All that said, questions of affordability are not imaginary. But the solutions are illusory.
People are feeling the pinch. But don't be fooled by Ford's political pitch, because $200 ain't what it used to be.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Novocol Pharma in Cambridge, Ont. on Nov. 1, 2024.

Subject: Anti inflation; Consumers; Inflation rates; Pandemics; Housing prices; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Anti inflation Consumers Inflation rates Housing prices Wages & salaries

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Document 343 of 500

'Star Wars' said it best: Doug Ford's attack on bike lanes is a trap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/star-wars-said-best-doug-fords-attack-on-bike/docview/3123363152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Star+Wars%27+said+it+best%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+attack+on+bike+lanes+is+a+trap&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Less than a week before Premier Doug Ford unveiled his grand scheme to rip out specific Toronto bike lanes in a hurry, Mayor Olivia Chow fielded a question about the prospect from the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at a Democracy Forum.
The mayor famous for the flowers on her bike basket gave an answer that might surprise some people.
She started off by talking about all the things she's been able to accomplish because of the "new deal for Toronto" she arranged with Ford, and bragged about how it helped lead to the city's recently upgraded credit rating, and why that would allow more spending on things such as housing the homeless or buying new subway cars. "In the grand scheme of things, when we look at the challenges in front of us: building affordable housing, getting people moving, feeding kids in school - all of those things, huge!" Bike lanes, she went on, are important. "But they're one small piece of it."
Against the scale of challenges the city faces - and indeed, more so in light of those the province faces - the configuration of a few stretches of road are relatively small potatoes.
I'll note the mayor's answer here may have been influenced by sharing the stage with her political mentor, Marshall Ganz, whose enduring subject is how to build political movements that succeed in persuading people and making change.
Chow was directly making a point about how she needed to keep working with Ford. But she was also making a point of avoiding doing Ford's political work for him.
On the issue at hand, Doug Ford is not operating under the impression that modes of travel on University Avenue are the most important issue in the province. But he's trying to make them the biggest issue in the provincial debate. Because he thinks a campaign in which activists and city councillors representing the downtown core march around in helmets and spandex shorts shouting "SHAME" at him is a campaign he wins.
In the immortal words of Admiral Ackbar: "It's a trap."
Admittedly, at this point, it starts to feel a little personal for a lot of us. I've written before about how the protected bike lanes, specifically the ones on Bloor Street and University Avenue, turned me into an overjoyed sometimes-cycling-commuter. If those lanes are removed, I could say my life and health would be more endangered (and those of cyclists on those routes will be), but the likely truth is that like a whole lot of others, I'll stay just as safe but twice as miserable crowded onto a subway train or inching away in my car. A lot of people in parts of central Toronto may feel this move is custom designed to make their lives worse.
(And in my view, to no real benefit to anyone, since I believe there are other, much bigger factors in traffic congestion on those routes. But I know the people who hate bike lanes do not agree. And frustratingly, the city has not collected enough data to conclusively prove the point. Not that I think data is driving Ford's thought process, mind you.)
It seems personal for Ford, too.
Ginning up a cars-versus-bikes political war was a chart-topping banger for his brother Rob, who was never more cheered by his supporters as mayor than when he ripped out the Jarvis bike lane in 2012. When they were raging against "bike-riding pinkos," Rob was winning! When other urgent issues took over the debate (things like slashing budgets or rebuilding the Port Lands or Rob's behaviour), they started losing and losing and losing.
Does anyone think hallway medicine is a winning debate topic for Doug Ford? The ongoing Greenbelt investigation? The speed of construction of transit lines or affordable housing? Whatever he's doing on the 413 that's getting snuck into this same bill designed to inspire shouting about bike lanes?
Like big fat cheques for everyone and a fantasyland 401 tunnel, this is an election ploy.
As Chow said last week in that Democracy Forum, "He has an election that he's facing."
There, Chow offered what seemed like hope after a few hours she'd spent on Bloor West in Etobicoke with merchants who'd opposed the bike lanes. She said they had asked about removing the landscaped median to add more car lanes if the bike lanes were staying - a possible compromise. "A-ha! OK! Why not?" Chow said. Planners were investigating to see if it could work. "Maybe we could do both," she said.
I'm skeptical all sides could be appeased. But it seemed worthwhile for Chow to look into the possibility.
In governing, a compromise in which everyone wins by getting what they want is a great outcome. But in an election campaign, there is no "everyone wins." You need to pick fights you think help you and hurt others. Which is why rather than looking into any possible compromise, a premier might prefer to ratchet up the conflict.
CAPTION: Bike lanes in downtown Toronto, like the one on University Avenue, are not the most important issue in the province.

Document 344 of 500

Our age-old dream of high speed train service may about to become a reality - but there's a lot that can still derail it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/our-age-old-dream-high-speed-train-service-may/docview/3123363148/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+age-old+dream+of+high+speed+train+service+may+about+to+become+a+reality+-+but+there%27s+a+lot+that+can+still+derail+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The government is teasing and tempting us again. Just as Doug Ford has offered $200 for every Ontarian in the run-up to a possible election, the federal Liberals will soon be offering trains as their own election looms , according to reports. Fast and sleek ones, in the form of high speed rail (HSR) between Toronto and Quebec City.
It's exciting, but we've been teased with HSR before and been fooled. And fooled again. And again. Will this time be for real or is it a "fool me once, fool me the umpteenth time" tease?
High speed rail is a Canadian ghost train, our Polar Express: Only dreamers and optimists can see it. One of my earliest news memories in the 1980s is of reading about plans for HSR that would connect Windsor all the way to Quebec City. As a preteen boy transitioning between dinosaur and the train enthusiasm phases, it seemed incredibly exciting, but I was ultimately disappointed.
I still am, considering the good news these days is that Via Rail's rolling stock is being updated with new trains replacing decades-old ones. A good thing, to be clear, but those trains will still share bumpy rails with - and be at the mercy of - freight trains. They aren't high speed either.
Even the past was more futuristic-feeling than it has been lately. From the late 1960s to the early 1980s, Canada ran a few "TurboTrains" that went faster than the usual ones. It wasn't like HSR technology being built in places such as Japan and France at the time, but still another shot at connecting this vast country.
Four decades since my hopes were first raised, HSR is still a vital dream to hold on to, but we're wise to be wary of promises. I saw an apt response on social media to the most recent news: Lucy yet again swiping the football away from Charlie Brown just as he was about to kick it.
Also part of the HSR dream cycle are naysayers. We in North America are different, they say. We are car people. We are too spread out to really use rails. The irony of the latter opinion is that Canada's foundational story maintains its creation and existence was possible because of railways - the "last spike" and all that - as fraught and difficult as the project was.
There is real movement happening in the U.S. in bringing high speed rail to some parts of the country, and a reason why current westward Canadian plans should not stop in Toronto but extend to Windsor and Niagara Falls in order to connect to possible American networks.
The naysayer's sentiments are surely not shared at Union Station, where people can be found daily waiting for Via Rail trains in lines that snake all the way out into the great hall. Trains are busy and few passengers wouldn't want to get to where they're going faster. Just look, too, at all the daily flights between Canada's two biggest cities.
Just as the UP Express has diminished the unique convenience of having an airport downtown, HSR makes trips between cities an hour apart by air far less desirable and would render Billy Bishop Airport even more inessential. Considering airport hassles and how early passengers have to get there, a three-hour trip to Montreal on a stress- and pollution-free electric train from downtown to downtown is a real business case.
This isn't theoretical - it's how other places with HSR work, from China to Italy. Half of Canada's population lives between Windsor and Quebec City: our landscape, population and potential prosperity are ready for it.
It's a good nation-building policy that shouldn't be political, and one all parties should get behind and implement as quickly as possible. Putting shovels in the ground helps turn a perpetual dream into reality. Whichever government does manage to get it all started in earnest will be remembered for it the way Eisenhower is in the U.S. for the Interstate Highway System.
There are cautions though, but maybe this time we can go into it eyes wide open. Infrastructure, both renewal and building new things, is critical to Canada's success, but costs for such things have exploded. In the Star last month, transit researcher and retired journalist Stephen Wickens laid out how Bloor-Danforth subway trains are coming with a world record price tag.
Here's another dream: Perhaps Canadian high costs and our entire way of procuring public projects, from transit to architecture, can get an open and public rethink this time. That might get a bit political after all, but Canada exists because of big infrastructure projects of the past. Let Charlie Brown kick the football into Canada's future this time and stop all the foolin'.
CAPTION: This photograph shows a Great Speed Train (Train a Grande Vitesse - TGV) of the French railway company SNCF (Societe nationale des chemins de fer francais) on the way on the LGV Paris-Bordeaux-Nantes-Rennes, in Courtalain, north of France, on September 13, 2024. (Photo by JEAN-FRANCOIS MONIER / AFP) (Photo by JEAN-FRANCOIS MONIER/AFP via Getty Images)

Document 345 of 500

Ford abandons plan to control police boards: Appointees would have been limited to a supplied list

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-abandons-plan-control-police-boards/docview/3123262064/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+abandons+plan+to+control+police+boards%3A+Appointees+would+have+been+limited+to+a+supplied+list&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has quietly quashed a controversial plan that would have "increased provincial control and influence" over police service board appointments by municipalities, the Star has learned.
Against the backdrop of a possible spring election, sources say Ford has cancelled a scheme that would have given Queen's Park far more power in the oversight of municipal policing.
Solicitor General Michael Kerzner's ministry had been preparing amendments to the Community Safety and Policing Act, according to a draft of confidential cabinet briefing documents obtained by the Star and shared with ministers last week.
"The intended outcomes are increased provincial control in the municipal appointment of community representatives to specified police service boards, by requiring these appointments to happen from a short list of candidates provided by the Solicitor General," says the internal document.
"SOLGEN notes a strong negative reaction is anticipated from the municipal sector and stakeholders, given the proposal would reduce municipal flexibility and autonomy for appointments."
Kerzner's office said Friday that the draft proposal was "not accurate."
Progressive Conservative sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, said Ford was mindful of the potential political headaches such changes could cause.
Tory MPPs also privately warned the premier this week that the plan would be problematic in their communities.
Despite the upheaval the amendments could have triggered, they had been okayed by cabinet committees in July and September and were moving quickly through the legislative process.
They were to have been included in a bill to be tabled next week and proclaimed into law by April 1.
Under the aborted plan, Kerzner would have given municipalities an approved "short list" of candidates to serve as community representatives on police service boards.
Although there are already provincial appointees to the oversight bodies, cities also select their own candidates for the panels.
Of the seven members of the Toronto Police Service Board, for example, four were appointed by city council while three, including chair Ann Morgan, were chosen by the province.
Mayor Olivia Chow had a direct hand in three of the current city appointments: Coun. Amber Morley, Coun. Shelley Carroll and Chris Brillinger, executive director of Family Services Toronto.
The fourth, Coun. Lily Cheng, joined the board two years ago when John Tory was mayor. Her term expires on Dec. 31.
It is unclear whether elected city councillors would have automatically been included in any approved list from the solicitor general.
"SOLGEN notes that municipalities would retain the requirement to appoint a community representative, but would need to choose from a short list if the regulations end up requiring this," the document says.
"Policing stakeholders, such as the Ontario Association of Police Service Boards, are expected to have a negative reaction related to board efficiency and appointment delays," it acknowledges.
But ministry bureaucrats predicted front-line police officers might have embraced the changes.
"These amendments are expected to have limited to positive reactions from policing stakeholders and the public, since an element of the proposal for police assistance provides that chiefs of police, rather than police service boards, may request temporary assistance" for additional resources, the documents add.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

IT proposal sets a 'double standard': Province seeks vendors who use Amazon services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/proposal-sets-double-standard/docview/3123262035/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=IT+proposal+sets+a+%27double+standard%27%3A+Province+seeks+vendors+who+use+Amazon+services&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A week after Premier Doug Ford ordered the LCBO to buy Ontario-made paper bags, his government is looking to U.S.-based giant Amazon for IT services, the Star has learned.
A request for proposals to supply software to the province's call and contact centres - such as ServiceOntario and the Family Responsibility Office - seeks vendors who use Amazon Web Services.
"Bidders must demonstrate ownership or authorization as (an) AWS reseller," says a 76-page document from the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement that sets a Dec. 4 deadline for bids, with five-year contracts to begin next April.
"The bidder must provide documentation validating their status as an AWS reseller. Additionally, the bidder must maintain this status throughout the term of agreement."
The request for bids is raising concerns Ontario-based software developers already holding such contracts are being frozen out and raising questions as to why Amazon Web Services was chosen, with no known bidding process giving other companies a shot.
"It's very disappointing to see an unfair process after the government has spent so much time about supporting Ontario businesses, when they've pre-determined this is going to an American multinational corporation," said an industry source speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations.
Ontario's lobbyist registry shows four staff from Rubicon Strategy represent Amazon in the province. Rubicon founder and chief executive Kory Teneycke was campaign manager for Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the 2022 election.
The ministry headed by Todd McCarthy did not reply to a list of questions from the Star, including why the Amazon platform was selected, what it can provide that Ontario suppliers cannot, and whether existing Ontario suppliers were asked if they could match its services.
A spokesperson for McCarthy said the ministry was not prepared to respond until Monday. Late Friday, his office reached out to the Star to say a statement could be coming "shortly," but later said they would not be able to follow through.
Late last month, Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy made it clear they wanted the LCBO to buy bags from suppliers in Ontario after executives signed a $10-million contract with a Quebec company.
The premier wrote a letter to LCBO chair Carmine Nigro on the issue. Ford's office released it to the media.
"We've been very clear we want them to use Ontario companies," said Bethlenfalvy. "I think Ontario companies are the best around."
Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), an accountant, said the Amazon procurement reveals an apparent "double standard."
"It might not be unusual for a request for bids to have specific technical requirements. That being said, it is important for Ontario companies to bid on Ontario contracts because it gives them credibility in the international marketplace."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Bids; Multinational corporations; Call centers; Software development; Request for proposal; Local businesses; Information technology

'The hostages are a footnote': Canadian-Israeli is fighting to see her parents' bodies returned and surviving captives released

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hostages-are-footnote/docview/3123260957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+hostages+are+a+footnote%27%3A+Canadian-Israeli+is+fighting+to+see+her+parents%27+bodies+returned+and+surviving+captives+released&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Partway through an interview Friday, Iris Weinstein Haggai - a Canadian-Israeli who grew up on a kibbutz devastated in last year's Oct. 7 assaults - stopped to review a statement that would soon appear on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's social media page.
She frowned briefly, reading on her lawyer's phone, then smiled and shook her head. "Amazing," she said. "Amazing."
The prime minister had done what she had asked of him, one day earlier, in a private meeting on Parliament Hill. He had said her mother's name.
Judi Weinstein Haggai.
"I can't say he didn't deliver with this," Iris told the Star. "So ... that's a start."
For more than a year, Iris and her family have anguished over the absence of her parents, Judi and Gadi. Weeks after the Oct. 7 attacks, Iris said the family learned through Israeli and American intelligence that her parents were among those that Hamas assailants had killed. The organization that Canada considers a terrorist group is still believed to be holding their bodies in the war-ravaged Gaza Strip.
Judi, Iris's mother, was born in the United States but grew up in Toronto and was a Canadian citizen. This fact, to Iris, means that a Canadian remains hostage to Hamas, a fact that has been "forgotten" as the conflict grows. More than 43,000 Palestinians have been killed in Israel's offensive in the Gaza Strip since the Oct. 7 massacre, according to local health officials in the occupied territory, which has included reports of deadly airstrikes on refugee camps, hospitals and schools. With Hezbollah firing rockets into northern Israel from Lebanon, Israel also invaded and launched airstrikes against that country, where officials say 2,800 people have been killed since last October.
Hamas assailants killed more than 1,200 people in their Oct. 7 attacks on Israel, an assault that has been described as the largest attack on Jews since the Holocaust. At the same time, Hamas took more than 250 people hostage, and Israeli media have reported that 97 of them are believed to remain in Gaza, including the bodies of at least 34 who have been confirmed dead.
Iris said she wants the conflict to end, but that the plight of hostages and the bodies of those kidnapped should be addressed separately. Too often, she argued, officials in Canada and elsewhere demand the release of hostages as part of broader statements calling for a ceasefire or demanding Israel allow more aid to flow into the Gaza Strip whose borders it controls.
"The hostages are a footnote," she said. "It's always tied to something."
She was pleased to see the prime minister take up her cause and call for the release of the Oct. 7 hostages in a post on X Friday afternoon that included direct reference to her mother's killing. Iris said she wants the federal government to use Canada's moral heft in the international community to push harder for the unconditional release of the hostages, both dead and alive, and to champion that cause at every opportunity.
"This is a person that shouldn't be there," Iris said of her mother, "just as much as a poor four-year-old doesn't need to be in the middle of a building falling down on him in Gaza."
In her visit to Ottawa this week, Iris met with Trudeau and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, as well as Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and the NDP's Jagmeet Singh, she said. Iris said she pressed Canadian politicians to advocate for the Oct. 7 hostages, including by calling for the International Committee of the Red Cross to finally gain access to those Hamas kidnapped last year.
Joly's spokesperson, James Fitz-Morris, told the Star that the minister committed to Iris that she would continue raising the need to return Judi Weinstein Haggai's remains.
It's part of her effort to bring attention to the Oct. 7 hostages that has seen her visit Washington, D.C. and engage with American officials from her home in Singapore, where she now lives with her husband and children.
Iris's efforts also extend to the Canadian courts, where she is involved in two legal cases that she said are aimed at seeking "accountability" for the attacks against Israel. One is a judicial challenge of Canada's decision last March to reinstate funding for the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees (UNRWA), which the Israeli parliament voted to ban this week after the country accused 12 of its staff members - which the agency says includes about 30,000 people in the region - of involvement in the Oct. 7 attacks. UNRWA has denied knowingly aiding any armed groups and says it moves quickly to purge any suspected militants from its staff.
Iris is one of two plaintiffs involved in a lawsuit filed in Ontario Superior Court on last month's anniversary of the Oct. 7 attacks, seeking $250 million in damages from Hamas and a host of other Palestinian groups they accuse of involvement in the assault. The suit also targets the governments of Iran and Syria, which are designated state sponsors of terror under the federal Justice for Victims of Terrorism Act, a law that is meant to help people seek damages from countries that support terrorist violence.
The legal cases are occurring alongside others that include a challenge seeking to prevent Canadian military exports to Israel, as well as an effort to pressure Ottawa to try and grant visas under a special program to the family members of Canadians who are stuck in war-torn Gaza.
For Iris, she is trying to channel the trauma she has endured into action, and to make sure more Canadians know that one of their co-citizens, even in death, is believed to be in the hands of her captors and killers all these months later.
"Canada needs to join me in this fight," she said. "Everyone should know my mom's name."
With files from Star
wire services
CAPTION: Judi Weinstein Haggai and Gadi Hagi were among more than 1,200 people killed in the Oct. 7 attacks on Israel. Hamas is still believed to be holding their bodies in the war-ravaged Gaza Strip.Iris Weinstein Haggai
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Star wire services

Document 348 of 500

Why polarization is spreading

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-polarization-is-spreading/docview/3123253772/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+polarization+is+spreading&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.24&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the question bedevilling progressive-leaning people north and south of the Canada-U.S. border throughout this raucous, election-filled fall - how has the race against the conservative right become so close?
You hear it from people voting for Democratic contender Kamala Harris in Tuesday's presidential vote. How on Earth is Donald Trump, a convicted felon, an election denier, the man who inspired a mob to stage a deadly riot on Capitol Hill four years ago, even a serious contender?
You heard it in British Columbia's recent election, which finally produced a squeaker of a majority win for incumbent Premier David Eby this week. The B.C. Conservatives came very close to denying the New Democrats that victory, even in an election peppered with racist comments by candidates and a leader, John Rustad, who has flirted with anti-vaccine sentiment.
Shachi Kurl, of the Angus Reid Institute, which has done some interesting polling on polarization in recent months, says of the B.C. election: "I've never seen the kind of negativity and polarization - in terms of language, in terms of campaign advertising."
This how-did-we-get-here question is also being asked by some of Justin Trudeau's beleaguered Liberals, incredulous that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is so far ahead in the polls, when he seems to break all the rules of civil disagreement in politics. How has the battle between Liberals and Conservatives become so polarized and personal in this country?
At least on the surface, it looks like Canada is drifting toward the deep divisions that have characterized the Trump-Harris race and have made Tuesday's result look like it's going to be too close to call. Of course we're seeing polarization creep across the border, Kurl says. "We're not insulated ... It's here already here, and it's been here for some time."
To explore why it's come to this in the United States, I talked to Canada's own David Frum, staff writer for the Atlantic and one of the many prominent Republicans who worked for George W. Bush, who is supporting Harris over Trump. Frum mailed in his vote for Harris this past week.
Cautiously, Frum wonders whether Tuesday will be all that close. Certainly, he admits, that's an optimistic view, coming from someone who has written deeply and profoundly over how Trump has damaged the Republican brand.
While he doesn't want to come out sounding totally dismissive of polling, Frum says he is thinking a lot these days about how "robust" it is, compared to even five or 10 years ago. He cites the familiar reservations - how accurate a sample can be when ordinary voters are so hard to reach and engage, and what he describes as the "herding" effect, in which pollsters tend to herd toward similar results, so as not to be an outlier.
"I'm not saying the polls are wrong. I'm just saying polls have been less reliable in the 2010s and 2020s than they were in the 1980s," Frum says.
But what if polling itself is making people more polarized, forcing them into binary choices? That sentiment has been expressed by Yale University's Timothy Snyder, a historian who delves into American political culture, including his new book, "On Freedom."
Last weekend, Snyder sat down for a conversation with the CBC's Matt Galloway at a Vancouver writers' festival and their conversation was broadcast on "The Current."
Snyder says that all the attention to horse-race polling forces people into making binary, either-or choices, when your average voter tends to drift toward the complicated middle.
"Most people, the vast majority, actually prefer Kamala Harris's policies, but that's not reported in America, let alone the rest of the world, because we're stuck on the polls," Snyder said.
Kurl, naturally, doesn't buy that idea, saying polls don't create the problem, they just report it. She and Frum agree that partisan politics, as it is now conducted, creates the conditions for the us-versus-them polarization.
In real life, Frum explains, people can believe in contradictory things, but partisan politics doesn't have much tolerance for contradiction.
"If you're someone who's a highly connected person in politics, you know there's liberal, there's conservative, and then it makes sense" to divide the world that way, Frum says. This is also why he's skeptical of the claims that ordinary voters are polarized over policy. "They don't think it's as important as other people do, and they don't organize their policy preferences the way people more connected to politics do."
One of the most interesting polls I've seen this year, one I wrote about recently, was the Angus Reid report on the "missing middle." Without drilling again into the numbers here, it essentially found that most Canadians consider themselves political orphans - centrist-leaning people who believe political parties are either too hard left or too hard right for them.
Kurl says we saw an early warning of this in the last federal Conservative leadership race two years ago, where former Quebec premier Jean Charest's more moderate, red-Tory conservatism was trounced by Poilievre and his harder-right base.
Frum has an interesting explanation for how we got here.
"Polarization is something we see in every developed country, and it's partly the price we pay for education," he says. "As people become better informed, as people become more affluent and they're more prone to vote on the basis of cultural identity."
He describes it this way. "In 1880, you could win somebody's vote by giving them a turkey or a slug of whiskey. In 1960, you could win somebody's vote by bringing them a new dam or a new high school," he says.
"Today, you win people's vote by deep social, cultural, tribal identification ... We're just in a more affluent place and our needs are less immediate, so people are much better informed and much more individualistic."
This is why Canadians should be on the lookout for spillover effects from Tuesday's historic showdown between Trump and Harris. We're not just spectators.
Kurl believes Canada could resist the excesses of that polarization before the next election here, but it would take leadership, and at present she doesn't see any leaders trying to bridge the partisan divides.
I asked Frum whether he was worried about Tuesday. "Of course," he said. He's worried even when people cautiously predict that Trump won't win, as Frum so hopes, and compares it to how humans would feel if told that a giant comet "probably" won't hit the Earth.
By that same metaphor, Canada's political culture won't be unaffected either. The impact of the too-close-to-call race will reverberate here, because when polarization hits the U.S., it hits Canada, too.
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Subject: Conservatism; Political campaigns; Culture; Voters; Political leadership; Political advertising

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala

Title: Why polarization is spreading

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 2, 2024

Document 349 of 500

Mayors want tougher laws from Ford: Leaders of 12 large cities request provincial support to deal with encampments and addictions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mayors-want-tougher-laws-ford/docview/3123253203/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayors+want+tougher+laws+from+Ford%3A+Leaders+of+12+large+cities+request+provincial+support+to+deal+with+encampments+and+addictions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayors of 12 of Ontario's largest cities - including Brampton, Oshawa, Pickering and Oakville - say they want the province to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments, homelessness and addictions and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts won't be overturned by the courts.
Premier Doug Ford said this week that he wanted Ontario's big city mayors, a group of 29 municipal leaders, to show "backbone" and support using the notwithstanding clause by putting it in writing "if they really want the homeless situation to improve."
In response, the leaders of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor sent a letter to Ford on Thursday asking for the province to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments" and "strengthen the existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions," among other changes.
"We request that your government consider the (measures) ... and where necessary use the notwithstanding clause to ensure these measures are implemented in a timely and effective way."
Grace Lee, a spokesperson for the premier, said in response to the letter that "we have been clear that we will explore every legal tool available to the province to clear encampments and restore safety to public spaces.
While cities remain at the forefront of issues relating to municipally owned lands, we are examining which additional tools the province can provide to help municipalities effectively manage these ongoing challenges."
Last year in Waterloo, a judge ruled that it's a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict those in encampments if there are no shelter spaces available for them.
A similar ruling was also handed down in Kingston.
The charter's notwithstanding clause allows a government to pass legislation that overrides certain rights. Ontario's big city mayors have previously written to Ford for help, but there was no consensus among the entire group to support using the clause.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, chair of the mayors' group, wants to see "one point person, a specific minister or ministry, in charge of solving this" and a province-wide plan including more supports.
She said the "issue becomes, if you are using the notwithstanding clause to close down encampments, but people have nowhere to go, we're no farther ahead."
Ford frequently notes that he receives "endless calls about people camping out in the parks, needles being all over the park" and families feeling unsafe.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who did not sign the letter, "believes the notwithstanding clause isn't a real solution," said her spokesperson Shirven Rezvany, urging the province to create more supportive housing, boost social assistance rates and reinstate rent controls, among other things.
"These would help people experiencing homelessness get back on their feet, rather than shuffling them around from park to park," Rezvany said.
At Queen's Park, opposition parties said the solution to encampments is housing that vulnerable people can afford, not taking such a major step as invoking the notwithstanding clause.
"If the housing isn't there ... it just ends up moving the encampment, right? You're not actually solving the problem," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles.
"I would hope that the government would actually be working with municipalities to build the housing we really need."
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked with several homeless shelters, called on the Ford government to improve "affordable, wraparound and supportive housing" to solve the problem.
"As long as this government continues to fail on that, municipalities will feel as though they have no choice but to ask for things like the notwithstanding clause. It is not necessary."
The request from the mayors shows the "complete failure of the Ford government to build deeply affordable non-profit, co-operative and supportive housing," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"If they're going to take the extreme measures of taking constitutional rights away from people experiencing homelessness, where are those people going to go?" he said.
"There are no homes for them to go to, because the Ford government has failed to build homes that they can afford to live in."
CAPTION: The leaders of 12 cities sent a letter to Premier Doug Ford Thursday asking the province to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments"R.J. JohnstonToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 350 of 500

Doug Ford accused of 'double standard' over push for Amazon IT services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-accused-double-standard-over-push/docview/3123392213/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+accused+of+%27double+standard%27+over+push+for+Amazon+IT+services&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A week after Premier Doug Ford ordered the LCBO to buy Ontario-made paper bags, his government is looking to U.S.-based giant Amazon for IT services, the Star has learned.
A request for proposals to supply software to the province's call and contact centres - such as ServiceOntario and the Family Responsibility Office - seeks vendors who use Amazon Web Services.
"Bidders must demonstrate ownership or authorization as (an) AWS reseller," says a 76-page document from the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement that sets a Dec. 4 deadline for bids with five-year contracts to begin next April.
"The bidder must provide documentation validating their status as an AWS reseller. Additionally, the bidder must maintain this status throughout the term of agreement."
The request for bids is raising concerns that Ontario-based software developers already holding such contracts are being frozen out and raising questions as to why Amazon Web Services was chosen, with no known bidding process giving other companies a shot.
"It's very disappointing to see an unfair process after the government has spent so much time about supporting Ontario businesses when they've pre-determined this is going to an American multinational corporation," said an industry source speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations.
Ontario's lobbyist registry shows four staff from Rubicon Strategy represent Amazon in the province. Rubicon founder and chief executive Kory Teneycke was campaign manager for Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the 2022 election.
The ministry headed by Todd McCarthy did not immediately reply to a list of questions from the Star, including why the Amazon platform was selected, what it can provide that Ontario suppliers cannot, and whether existing Ontario suppliers were asked if they could match its services.
A spokesman for McCarthy said the ministry was not prepared to respond until Monday. On Friday evening, the minister's office reached out to the Star to say a statement could be coming "shortly," but later said they would not be able to follow through.
Supply Ontario, which is listed as a contact for potential bidders with queries, referred questions to the ministry.
Late last month, Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy made it clear they wanted the LCBO to buy bags from suppliers in Ontario after executives signed a $10-million contract with a Quebec company.
The premier, who has not ruled out an early election next year instead of waiting until the scheduled date in June 2026, wrote a letter to LCBO chair Carmine Nigro on the issue. Ford's office released it to the media.
"We've been very clear we want them to use Ontario companies," said Bethlenfalvy. "I think Ontario companies are the best around."
Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), an accountant, said the Amazon procurement reveals an apparent "double standard" in the Progressive Conservative government.
"It might not be unusual for a request for bids to have specific technical requirements. That being said, it is important for Ontario companies to bid on Ontario contracts because it gives them credibility in the international marketplace," she added.
"I've talked to a lot of Ontario companies, who say 'the first thing we get asked is ... does your own government buy from you?' And when you say no, that's a lost opportunity."
McCarthy's ministry came under fire earlier this year for moving nine ServiceOntario centres into Staples stores to offer longer hours, easy parking and save $1 million over three years.
But critics said the move ended contracts with independent "mom and pop" private operators in the centres in favour of a retail chain. The deal is under review by the province's independent Financial Accountability Office.
"We need a government that puts Ontario businesses first," said New Democrat MPP Tom Rakocevic (Humber River-Black Creek). "Instead, the Ford government continues to take opportunities away from our province, be it paper bags, handing over contracts to foreign companies, handing over ServiceOntario to Staples, and now this."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford makes an announcement at a gas station saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales, in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Document 351 of 500

Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-200-cheques-are-yesterdays-solution/docview/3123392161/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+cheques+are+yesterday%27s+solution+to+today%27s+worries+about+inflation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prices are high. An election is nigh.
Affordability is top of mind.
Which is why 15 million Ontarians are getting an anti-inflation consolation prize: $200 cheques in the mail soon.
Thank you, Doug Ford.
What better solution for inflation and affordability both?
Except that Ford's pre-election generosity and grandiosity may be the wrong prescription for the wrong problem.
What if our continuing obsession with inflation is a holdover from the past? Is Ford fighting yesterday's war with outdated cheques to win next year's vote?
It's a question of public perception and political calculation versus economic reality.
Public opinion polls aren't lying when they identify affordability as the top issue for voters. People assuredly believe prices are too high.
Which is why Ford is so shamelessly riding the affordability bandwagon. So, too, is federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who coined the slogan "Justinflation" as a way to pin blame for rising prices on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau alone.
No one questions that inflation surged post-pandemic, thanks mostly to supply chain disruptions caused by COVID shutdowns, plus massive government stimulus and a record influx of temporary workers (who stoked demand for housing). Inflation peaked at 8.1 per cent in mid-2022, and Canadians felt the pinch in food and housing prices.
But that was then. This is now.
The inflation rate has dropped below three per cent so far this year, according to Statistics Canada. In fact, inflation fell to 1.6 per cent in September - a historically low figure, and well below the two per cent target set by the Bank of Canada.
Which is why the central bank has revised its key lending rate steadily downward this year, and did so again last week.
So if that rock bottom inflation rate is evidence enough for the bank to conclude that inflation is increasingly under control, why do ordinary Canadians still fret about affordability? And if central bankers can get inflation under control, why can't politicians make life feel more affordable?
First, homebuyers will always feel nostalgia for the good old days when times were bad and mortgage rates reached record lows in mid-pandemic. Back then, the Bank of Canada resorted to emergency stimulus measures that dropped lending rates to near-zero, but those rates are not coming back - nor should they, barring another massive crisis. Get used to it.
Second, today's remarkably low inflation rate - which has reassured even our stickler bankers - is small comfort for ordinary consumers. People may understand that the rate of increase has since slowed, but they also know that the rapid increases of recent years are now baked in - so prices absolutely feel higher than they once were.
Put another way, it's small consolation if the inflation rate has ebbed, belatedly, if today's prices are stuck at cumulatively higher levels. StatCan figures show that a basket of goods that cost $137 in September 2020 would cost $161 in September 2024 - an increase of 17.5 per cent over the past four years.
Admittedly, that was long ago. But people still recall pre-pandemic prices as if they were yesterday - and that's the benchmark that comes to mind when they see today's higher price tags.
People aren't so much fussed about the latest 1.6 per cent inflation rate; they're fuming that prices are at least 10 times higher - 17.5 per cent - than they once were.
So are we stuck with the cumulative increases of recent years?
One serious remedy is to raise wages for working people to make up for lost ground, and wages did indeed go up post-pandemic - albeit not for everyone, and the increases didn't always keep up. But wage hikes can only get you so far before you fall into an inflationary cycle of higher pay causing higher prices.
Another perception problem that economists have long observed is that workers feel that they've "earned" a pay hike by dint of hard work. They don't look upon a wage increase as compensation for inflation.
Workers understandably want to get ahead, not see their higher pay cheque eaten up by rising food prices. They resent inflation - even if that's what's driving their catch-up wage increase - because it erodes their hard-won purchasing power.
Rather than increasing wages, what about rolling back price hikes? Be careful what you wish for.
Economists refer to price decrease as deflation - the evil twin of inflation - which is almost always bad news. Historically, a deflationary economy goes hand in hand with recession and depression.
Producers respond to price decreases by reducing their payroll costs - either by cutting wages or laying off workers, which in turn lowers aggregate consumer demand. Also, consumers and businesses may delay new purchases if they know prices will be lower tomorrow than they are today - further depressing demand.
All that said, questions of affordability are not imaginary. But the solutions are illusory.
People are feeling the pinch. But don't be fooled by Ford's political pitch, because $200 ain't what it used to be.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Novocol Pharma in Cambridge, Ont. on Nov. 1, 2024.

Subject: Anti inflation; Consumers; Inflation rates; Pandemics; Housing prices; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Anti inflation Consumers Inflation rates Housing prices Wages & salaries

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Title: Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 1, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited
