Document 366 of 500

As long as Doug Ford has billions to throw around, why doesn't he spend it on something worthwhile?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-long-doug-ford-has-billions-throw-around-why/docview/3122609052/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+long+as+Doug+Ford+has+billions+to+throw+around%2C+why+doesn%27t+he+spend+it+on+something+worthwhile%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several times a week - sometimes several times a day - I pass the tents. In Clarence Square across the street from the Star office, in Trinity Square near City Hall, in High Park now more and more, even in the little parkette on a residential side street out near Bloor West Village.
People camped out in the parks because they have no place to live, and because there is no room for all of them in city emergency shelters. You likely don't need me to tell you about it. In March of this year, according to the city, there were at least 202 tents in 72 different parks across the city, and an analysis of the situation since then by The Local this week showed there are more (mostly smaller) encampments spread farther out across Toronto.
You've seen it, and heard about it, and likely worried about it.
It's an extreme problem. For those sleeping there, because they have nowhere else to go, living in the rain and the cold. For the rest of us, because the encampments ruin our experience of the parks, making them feel unsafe and grubby and desperate. These encampments bring the problems faced by homeless people - including mental health and drug addiction - and put them in front of us, where we live our own lives, making them our problem, too.
On Tuesday this week, Doug Ford proposed that the problem had grown so extreme it was time to override the constitution. "Why don't the big city mayors actually put in writing that they want the province to change the homeless program, make sure that we move the homeless along - and why don't they put in the use of the notwithstanding clause or something like that," the Star reported him saying. "Let's see if they have the backbone to do it."
I note two things. First, he didn't actually say he was going to use the notwithstanding clause to override the protections of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms (after a court decision that ruled it unconstitutional to forcibly evict encampments if there was no space in shelters for people to go), but rather he asked mayors to have the backbone to ask him to do that - so that he doesn't have to have the backbone to take full responsibility for the move himself, I suppose.
Second, he chose the right words for what he was proposing, at least in part. This measure would "move the homeless along." Not house them. Not shelter them, even. Just move them along. Where would they move to? Another park? A bench on the sidewalk?
It's a spin on the old "Closing Time" refrain: You don't have a home to go to, but you can't stay here.
It seems, just on the edge of winter's harsher weather - a week after the city of Toronto acknowledged that it will not have enough shelter space for all these people through the upcoming winter - that the proposed rule for people with nowhere to go is that they must remain exposed to the elements: no shelter to go to, no tents allowed. Notwithstanding any human rights protections our constitution might otherwise require.
That feels like an extreme solution. But many would say it's an extreme problem, and (as I have written recently) an extremely difficult one to solve.
However, I personally read this news in the hours immediately after the Star's editorial board was briefed on the plan to throw a $380-million soccer party two years from now. And the premier's comments came on the same day he revealed the details of his scheme to spend $3 billion to send every Ontarian a Happy New Year pre-election cheque.
Given that the entire annual budget for sheltering 12,200 homeless people per night in Toronto is $789 million, it seems like $3.4 billion or so could go a long way to dealing with those few hundred camped out in parks. Probably a fraction of those dollars would do the trick. And with that cash, you wouldn't just "move the homeless along" - making their problems even more difficult for them to manage - you'd help them out. Making their lives better at the same time as making our cities more pleasant.
We all like receiving money in the mail - who can't use an extra couple of hundred bucks? And I like a big sports-oriented party as much as the next guy.
But if we have enough mad money stashed away to cover those things, shouldn't we look at spending at least a bit more of it on a problem so extreme we're thinking of trashing constitutional protections in order to simply move it along?
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford this week suggested the homelessness crisis had grown so extreme it was time to override the constitution - the same week he detailed a $3.4-billion pre-election handout.

Subject: Parks & recreation areas; Shelters; Homeless people

Title: As long as Doug Ford has billions to throw around, why doesn't he spend it on something worthwhile?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 31, 2024

Document 367 of 500

As taxpayers we expect government to use our tax dollars where most needed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-taxpayers-we-expect-government-use-our-tax/docview/3122609037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+taxpayers+we+expect+government+to+use+our+tax+dollars+where+most+needed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians, Oct. 29
The rationale Premier Doug Ford provided for gifting $200 per person is that as taxpayers he should return some of the money we paid out in taxes. As taxpayers should it not be our expectation that those funds be used where most needed? To send out cheques to many of us who aren't in need of the funds is not an efficient use of our tax dollars. As a volunteer at our local food bank, I see firsthand one of many examples where our dollars could be channelled for the greater good.It's not likely Ford will do an about face in this case as it is fuel for his re-election. Given that, I encourage those who don't need the money to forward a cheque to a cause of your choice.
Bob Scale, Oakville
Rebate is just more taxpayer debt
Premier Doug Ford calls his $3 billion pre-election giveaway a "rebate" of taxpayers' money. No, Ontario is running hefty deficits every year and Ford doesn't have a spare nickel for rebates. Ontario's accumulated debt exceeds $400 billion and keeps growing. The only way Ford can come up with another quick $3 billion is by more borrowing. And the unpleasant truth is that borrowed money always has to be repaid with interest. Who pays it? Us, the taxpayers. But Ford plans to be re-elected before we figure it out. Thanks for nothing but more debt.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby, ON
Quit squandering our money Premier FordMeet the guy 'off the street' behind a stunning $203-million gift to CAMH - the largest mental health donation in Canadian history, Oct. 24
What a generous gift made by Bruce McKean to a facility in need of this kind of support - as does the whole, inadequate system of mental health supports.
I don't like to be the skunk at the garden party but when I go into a hospital where every elevator wall, every poster, every flat-screen TV is begging me for dollars, I want to ask, yell, why is health care paid for increasingly by those amongst us who have lots of disposable income? It seems every door, every piece of furniture and equipment has someone's name on it. In a fancy press conference a year or so ago, the Minister of Health (our MPP here in Dufferin Caledon), to much applause, announced that the MOH would fund operating costs of an MRI. The rest of it, the machine itself, is up to the deep pockets in the riding. This is not universal health care. It's social policy by plutocracy.
For years now, we have watched this government siphon off public dollars into private pockets. Why are we not in the streets protesting this? Why does Ontario have the second lowest per capita funding of health care in the entire country? Premier Ford, quit squandering our money. You can keep this household's $400 - your latest attempt to bribe us with our own funds. And please return the licence fee for our mostly-polluting cars.
Rev'd LeeAnn McKenna, Mono
Bullet train would make a huge difference in lives of daily commutersThis megaproject could be Canada's next great nation-building exercise, Oct. 28
On a number of occasions, I have taken the GO train from Burlington to Toronto. This trip takes around an hour and the train's top speed is 144 km/h. In comparison, the average bullet train from Japan travels at 320 km/h. If I could take a bullet train to Toronto instead, I could reduce the length of my trip by more than half. If bullet trains are successfully implemented, commute times will be significantly reduced. This in turn will improve people's lives, strengthen the city, and allow for future growth. People all around the world are currently reaping the rewards of bullet trains. Why shouldn't we?
Matthew Cann, Burlington
Property taxes alone can't pay for a proper housing program
Numbers show why city is failing fight for shelters, Oct. 26.
An affordable and supportive housing program can't be financed with property taxes. The causes of the present housing crisis date back to the 1990s when the Michael Harris provincial government closed halfway houses, ended the co-op program and downloaded provincial social housing to cities without giving them the ability to tax incomes or consumption to finance them. Whatever the historic reasons for the crazy relationship between municipalities and provinces, Canada will never achieve its potential until its cities and towns have the revenue and authority to properly serve the needs of their communities.
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
Renting has more obstacles than costToronto's rents are finally falling. Here's why it's happening - and how long prices could drop, Oct. 27
The rent "situation" is not just about price. Try being a single mom with three or more children. Landlords have huge restrictions. Applications for a three-bedroom townhouse often state, "not suitable for children" and our government has no supports or resources to address unhealthy living situations such as black mould making children sick. Our government needs to examine housing issues more extensively, especially for those most vulnerable.
Sylvia Muirhead, Mississauga
Free Parking lots will reduce gridlock
One of the quickest ways to relieve Toronto's gridlock is to provide free parking lots at the Go and TTC stations from the edges of the city to the midpoint in the suburbs. Parking is expensive. For an added incentive, make the TTC trip free too.
Sarah Shartal, Toronto
We need more separated bike lanes
Bike lanes only make sense in the right places, Oct. 13
By removing bike lanes, the provincial government is only putting more people in cars, creating more traffic and a higher demand for gasoline. The provincial government should really be making bike lanes more separate, creating physical barriers to keep cyclists safe from cars, and out of their way. Drivers find cyclists can be a nuisance when they have to share the road, but every driver knows how bad traffic already is in southern Ontario. Creating more of it is the last thing anyone wants. I love riding my bike and I wish that I could more, but actions like this make it difficult and force people like me who don't enjoy driving to get in their car and get stuck in traffic.
Owen Tipple, Burlington
In the name of convenience, we will pay with our lives
Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, should be called "The Killing for Convenience Act." Simply put, Premier Doug Ford, without consulting the municipalities that make up the Association of Ontario of Municipalities, has made an uninformed and counterproductive decree that increases the chance of killing, maiming and carnage of cyclists and pedestrians on downtown city streets. By removing bike lanes and increasing driving speeds for the convenience of the suburban driver he will ensure that Toronto's "Vision Zero" for cycling and pedestrian safety is completely blindfolded - we will literally be the roadkill of his bill - collateral damage in the "war against cars." It's clear that in this war, cyclists, public transit users, and pedestrians in downtown Toronto, Ottawa, Hamilton and other cities have lost. Yes, indeed we will be paying for Ford's convenience - the tested, documented and proven need to cut two or three minutes out of a of 30 commute in a private vehicle driven more than 80 per cent of the time by a single individual - with our lives.
John Bailey, Toronto
CAPTION: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy gives the fall economic statement at Queens Park on Oct. 30. Premier Doug Ford is spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 "rebate" cheques. The provincial government has earmarked $3 billion to send cheques to 15 million people in Ontario in the new year.

Subject: Mental health; Traffic; Pedestrians; Cities; Property taxes; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Property taxes

Location: Canada

Title: As taxpayers we expect government to use our tax dollars where most needed

Document 368 of 500

Immigration system should be transparent, predictable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/immigration-system-should-be-transparent/docview/3122483313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Immigration+system+should+be+transparent%2C+predictable&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In its effort to appease voters and address Canada's housing crisis, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government has made drastic cuts to immigration rates. But the implementation of this change fails to consider the impacts on international students and their families already in Canada on temporary permits.
Students like Niño.
Niño's mother came to Canada in 2018 as a caregiver on a work permit. In 2021, when Nino turned 18, he joined her by enrolling at the University of Toronto to study anthropology and public policy. While his mother maintained her work permit, Niño as her dependent was able to register as an Ontario resident student and pay domestic tuition fees.
I met Niño in2023 in my seminar course on Southeast Asia. He was one of my top students. I learned he was from the region of Mindanao in the Philippines that has seen intractable violence and devastating poverty for the better part of the last century.
Niño wants to use his Canadian education to help bring durable stability in Mindanao. He is part of a cultural workers' collective in Mindanao where he collaborates with local artists, writers, and other like-minded individuals to heal communities fractured by conflict, neglect, and underdevelopment.
In 2023, I travelled to Mindanao for my PhD researching political violence in the Philippines. I chose Mindanao because in 2016, one of the two Canadians kidnapped and murdered by militants in the region was the father of a close friend. Niño connected me with local educators, peacebuilders, and organizations that helped me succeed in my academic journey, but now his education prospects are in jeopardy.
The result of the Liberals' policy reversal is that Niño's mother's work permit was not renewed. She must leave Canada or face deportation. That means Niño no longer qualifies as an Ontario resident and must now pay international student tuition fees to complete his final year at U of T.
Concretely, his tuition increased tenfold from $6,100 to $61,720. Niño faces the real possibility that he must now leave while at the cusp of completing his education as a straight-A student. Out of desperation, he has started a GoFundMe page.
The reason I am so sympathetic to his plight is because 25 years ago I was in a similar situation.
My mother came to Canada to escape political persecution in China thanks to the generous policies of the Chrétien government. She was fortunate to qualify as a professional and skilled immigrant who scored enough points for both of us to be granted entry. I was so grateful to live in a free society, I enrolled in the Canadian Armed Forces after high school and obtained my education simultaneously through military service.
Since then, the conversation about immigration has become muddled.
The question of how many immigrants and international students we welcome to Canada should be a different conversation from the one we need to have about how we treat those who are already here.
The pathways for temporary workers and international students who have already invested in living in Canada should be streamlined. Demonstrated commitment to Canada through work or study should be given consideration and weighed against other criteria such as work experience or qualification requirements for applications of renewal or status change to permanent residency. This could be a lengthy reform to implement, but in the short term, we can offer longer-term or renewable work permits to reduce uncertainty and provide a smoother transition from temporary to permanent status.
Instead of reacting to fluctuating labour demands or shifting public opinion during election cycles, we should create a transparent and predictable system that allows people to plan their futures with confidence, free from the threat of sudden changes. Niño and his mother have a lot to lose - education and livelihood. They are just one example of how our leaders abdicate their responsibilities to the most vulnerable and precarious among us.
Capt. (ret.) Cheng Xu is an assistant professor in the Department of Government at Smith College in Massachusetts.
CREDIT: Cheng Xu Contributor Capt. (ret.) Cheng Xu is an assistant professor in the Department of Government at Smith College in Massachusetts.;

Document 369 of 500

Tories use tax windfall to send rebates: Cheques to cost treasury $3 billion as province reports $6.6-billion deficit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-use-tax-windfall-send-rebates/docview/3122483276/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+use+tax+windfall+to+send+rebates%3A+Cheques+to+cost+treasury+%243+billion+as+province+reports+%246.6-billion+deficit&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is spending nearly half of a $6.9-billion tax windfall on $200 pre-election "rebate" cheques.
Bolstered by higher-than-projected revenue from personal income tax, corporations tax and sales tax, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have earmarked $3 billion to send cheques to 15 million Ontario adults and children in the new year.
But Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement tabled Wednesday at Queen's Park said the Tories are still running a $6.6-billion deficit. While that's lower than the surprise $9.8-billion shortfall for 2024-25 that Bethlenfalvy forecast in his March 26 budget, it's far from the $200-million surplus he had projected last year.
With Ford expected to call a spring election - one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote - Bethlenfalvy hinted to reporters that the books could be balanced as early as his March budget even though he is currently projecting a $1.5-billion deficit for 2025-26.
"I'm an optimist. I'm always hoping I can beat the numbers," he said.
In a partisan speech in the legislature, where he twice attacked "the previous Liberal government" in power from 2003 until 2018, Bethlenfalvy painted a rosy picture of Ontario's finances.
"Today we are in a position to do more," the treasurer said, insisting "this government will balance the budget" in 2026-27.
Bethlenfalvy defended the Tories' decision to mail out cheques, first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, instead of further reducing the deficit to a more manageable $3.6 billion for 2024-25.
(He said the government has not calculated the administrative cost of printing and mailing millions of them.)
"With our new, stronger fiscal position, we can now afford to provide real relief to families to help them in this time of challenge. We know that people aren't looking for some money back in time for next year's taxes," said the finance minister.
"They need real support, and they need it now. We are providing $200 per child under the age of 18 - meaning a family of five will receive $1,000," he said.
"I'm under no illusions that this will relieve all of the affordability pressures facing Ontario families, but it will help. It's real support and, most importantly, it allows the people of Ontario to choose how this money can best help them."
Questioning the wisdom of the one-time payments, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "people in Ontario are tired of gimmicks."
"Where are they not spending those dollars right now? Where people really need them," said Stiles, referring to the severe shortage of housing and family doctors and growing homeless encampments.
"People deserve a government that puts their tax dollars to work and spends (them) responsibly to make life easier. With Doug Ford, you're not getting what you paid for," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Bethlenfalvy's mini-budget fails to "address the issues that really matter to Ontario families."
"Instead of making real investments to keep emergency rooms open, helping the over 2.5 million people without a family doctor, addressing the urgent need for long-term financial relief, or making housing more affordable, Doug Ford introduced yet another failed fall economic statement that puts Doug Ford's rich friends first, and you and your family last," said Crombie.
"People are dying on wait-lists and Doug Ford is spending billions on booze, foreign spas and the Greenbelt scandal," she said, referring to the expansion of beer and wine sales, the Ontario Place redevelopment and the RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap debacle.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said there are better ways to spend $3 billion of public money for the greater good of all Ontarians.
"Two hundred dollars isn't going to buy them a home or pay the rent or boost somebody from a tent (encampment) to permanent supportive housing," said Schreiner, who plans to donate his $200 cheque to charity.
On the growing problem of homelessness, the organization representing 29 of Ontario's largest cities expressed disappointment that there was nothing new to address encampments, addictions and mental health issues.
"Municipalities have done everything we can, but we don't have the resources or expertise to take this on alone," Ontario Big City Mayors said in a statement.
Bethlenfalvy bristled at that, noting the province has already raised annual funding for the Homelessness Prevention Program by $200 million.
He said that was a 40 per cent hike "in one year upfront (that) continues for the next five years, which is about a $1-billion increase to to help municipalities as we work together on the challenges of homelessness."
The fall economic statement came against the backdrop of Ontario's debt skyrocketing to a projected $439 billion this year since Ford took office in 2018.
That's up $116 billion - or 36 per cent - from $323 billion left by the previous Liberal government six years ago.
However, interest on the provincial debt is expected to dip by $1.2 billion to $12.7 billion - more than the budget of every department except the Ministry of Health, the Ministry of Education and the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services.
The annual cost of servicing the debt, which is the largest of any subnational jurisdiction in the world, is anticipated to increase to $14.5 billion in two years.
"Ontario now has the lowest borrowing costs of any province in Canada, saving taxpayers $1.2 billion that we are now able to reinvest back into people," boasted Bethlenfalvy.
"In fact, our interest on debt relative to revenues is currently at its lowest level since the 1980s and our debt-to-GDP ratio, which is a measure of our fiscal health, remains at the best level it's been in a decade. This is very good news for Ontario taxpayers. It means that when the province needs to borrow, we can do it at a lower cost."
The costs of abiding by a court ruling that struck down the Bill 124 salary restraint legislation for public servants, which limited pay hikes to one per cent, has resulted in retroactive raises for workers totalling $6.7 billion.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 370 of 500

Better ways Ford could spend our money

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/better-ways-ford-could-spend-our-money/docview/3122483230/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Better+ways+Ford+could+spend+our+money&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several times a week - sometimes several times a day - I pass the tents. In Clarence Square across the street from the Star office, in Trinity Square near city hall, in High Park now more and more, even in the little parkette on a residential side street out near Bloor West Village.
People camped out in the parks because they have no place to live, and because there is no room for all of them in city emergency shelters. You likely don't need me to tell you about it. In March of this year, according to the city, there were at least 202 tents in 72 different parks across the city, and an analysis of the situation since then by The Local this week showed there are more (mostly smaller) encampments spread farther out across Toronto.
You've seen it, and heard about it, and likely worried about it.
It's an extreme problem. For those sleeping there, because they have nowhere else to go, living in the rain and the cold. For the rest of us, because the encampments ruin our experience of the parks, making them feel unsafe and grubby and desperate. These encampments bring the problems faced by homeless people - including mental health and drug addiction - and put them in front of us, where we live our own lives, making them our problem, too.
On Tuesday this week, Doug Ford proposed that the problem had grown so extreme it was time to override the constitution. "Why don't the big city mayors actually put in writing that they want the province to change the homeless program, make sure that we move the homeless along - and why don't they put in the use of the notwithstanding clause or something like that," the Star reported him saying. "Let's see if they have the backbone to do it."
I note two things. First, he didn't actually say he was going to use the notwithstanding clause to override the protections of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms (after a court decision that ruled it unconstitutional to forcibly evict encampments if there was no space in shelters for people to go), but rather he asked mayors to have the backbone to ask him to do that - so that he doesn't have to have the backbone to take full responsibility for the move himself, I suppose.
Second, he chose the right words for what he was proposing, at least in part. This measure would "move the homeless along." Not house them. Not shelter them, even. Just move them along. Where would they move to? Another park? A bench on the sidewalk?
It's a spin on the old "Closing Time" refrain: You don't have a home to go to, but you can't stay here.
It seems, just on the edge of winter's harsher weather - a week after the city of Toronto acknowledged that it will not have enough shelter space for all these people through the upcoming winter - that the proposed rule for people with nowhere to go is that they must remain exposed to the elements: no shelter to go to, no tents allowed. Notwithstanding any human rights protections our constitution might otherwise require.
That feels like an extreme solution. But many would say it's an extreme problem, and (as I have written recently) an extremely difficult one to solve.
However, I personally read this news in the hours immediately after the Star's editorial board was briefed on the plan to throw a $380-million soccer party two years from now. And the premier's comments came on the same day he revealed the details of his scheme to spend $3 billion to send every Ontarian a Happy New Year pre-election cheque.
Given that the entire annual budget for sheltering 12,200 homeless people per night in Toronto is $789 million, it seems like $3.4 billion or so could go a long way to dealing with those few hundred camped out in parks. Probably a fraction of those dollars would do the trick. And with that cash, you wouldn't just "move the homeless along" - making their problems even more difficult for them to manage - you'd help them out. Making their lives better at the same time as making our cities more pleasant.
We all like receiving money in the mail - who can't use an extra couple of hundred bucks? And I like a big sports-oriented party as much as the next guy.
But if we have enough mad money stashed away to cover those things, shouldn't we look at spending at least a bit more of it on a problem so extreme we're thinking of trashing constitutional protections in order to simply move it along?
CAPTION: In the same week Premier Doug Ford suggested homelessness is so extreme it was time to override the constitution, he detailed a $3.4-billion pre-election handout, Edward Keenan writes.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Letters: Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians, Oct. 29 Meet the guy 'off the street' behind a stunning $203-million gift to CAMH - the largest mental health donation in Canadian history, Oct. 24 This megaproject could be Canada's next great nation-building exercise, Oct. 28 Numbers show why city is failing fight for shelters, Keenan, Oct. 26. Toronto's rents are finally falling. Here's why it's happening - and how long prices could drop, Oct. 27 Bike lanes only make sense in the right places, Opinion, Oct. 13

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3122483223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+announces+details+of+%24200+cheques+for+15+million+Ontarians%2C+Oct.+29+Meet+the+guy+%27off+the+street%27+behind+a+stunning+%24203-million+gift+to+CAMH+-+the+largest+mental+health+donation+in+Canadian+history%2C+Oct.+24+This+megaproject+could+be+Canada%27s+next+great+nation-building+exercise%2C+Oct.+28+Numbers+show+why+city+is+failing+fight+for+shelters%2C+Keenan%2C+Oct.+26.+Toronto%27s+rents+are+finally+falling.+Here%27s+why+it%27s+happening+-+and+how+long+prices+could+drop%2C+Oct.+27+Bike+lanes+only+make+sense+in+the+right+places%2C+Opinion%2C+Oct.+13&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Direct tax dollars to where they're most needed
Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians, Oct. 29
The rationale Premier Doug Ford provided for gifting $200 per person is that he should return some of the money we paid out in taxes. Should it not be our expectation that those funds be used where most needed? To send out cheques to many of us who aren't in need of the funds is not an efficient use of our tax dollars.
As a volunteer at our local food bank, I see firsthand one of many examples of where our dollars could be channelled for the greater good. It's not likely Ford will do an about face in this case, as it is fuel for his re-election. I encourage those who don't need the money to forward a cheque to a cause of your choice.
Bob Scale, Oakville

Premier Doug Ford calls his $3-billion pre-election giveaway a "rebate" of taxpayers' money. No, Ontario is running hefty deficits every year and Ford doesn't have a spare nickel for rebates. Ontario's accumulated debt exceeds $400 billion and keeps growing.
The only way Ford can come up with a quick $3 billion is by more borrowing. And the unpleasant truth is that borrowed money always has to be repaid with interest. Who pays it? Us, the taxpayers.
But Ford plans to be re-elected before we figure it out. Thanks for nothing but more debt.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby, Ont.
Let's stop relying on private donors for health funds
Meet the guy 'off the street' behind a stunning $203-million gift to CAMH - the largest mental health donation in Canadian history, Oct. 24
What a generous gift from Bruce McKean to a facility in need of this kind of support - as does the whole, inadequate system of mental health supports.
I don't like to be the skunk at the garden party, but when I go into a hospital where every elevator wall, every poster, every flat-screen TV is begging me for dollars, I want to ask, yell, why is health care paid for increasingly by those among us who have lots of disposable income?
It seems every door, every piece of furniture and equipment has someone's name on it. In a fancy press conference a year or so ago, the Minister of Health (our MPP in Dufferin Caledon), to much applause, announced that the MOH would fund operating costs of an MRI. The rest of it, the machine itself, is up to the deep pockets in the riding. This is not universal health care. It's social policy by plutocracy.
Why does Ontario have the second-lowest per-capita funding of health care in the entire country?
Premier Ford, quit squandering our money. You can keep this household's $400 - your latest attempt to bribe us with our own funds. And please return the licence fee for our mostly polluting cars.
Rev. LeeAnn McKenna,
Mono, Ont.
Bullet trains could speed up daily commutes
This megaproject could be Canada's next great nation-building exercise, Oct. 28
On a number of occasions, I have taken the GO train from Burlington to Toronto. This trip takes about an hour and the train's top speed is 144 km/h. In comparison, the average bullet train from Japan travels at 320 km/h. If I could take a bullet train to Toronto instead, I could reduce the length of my trip by more than half.
If bullet trains are implemented, commute times will be significantly reduced. This in turn will improve people's lives, strengthen the city, and allow for future growth. People all around the world are currently reaping the rewards of bullet trains. Why shouldn't we?
Matthew Cann, Burlington
Property taxes alone can't pay for housing plan
Numbers show why city is failing fight for shelters,
Keenan, Oct. 26.
An affordable and supportive housing program can't be financed with property taxes. The causes of the present housing crisis date back to the 1990s when the Michael Harris provincial government closed halfway houses, ended the co-op program and downloaded provincial social housing to cities without giving them the ability to tax incomes or consumption to finance them.
Whatever the historic reasons for the crazy relationship between municipalities and provinces, Canada will never achieve its potential until its cities and towns have the revenue and authority to properly serve the needs of their communities.
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
Price is just one issue renters face
Toronto's rents are finally falling. Here's why it's happening - and how long prices could drop, Oct. 27
The rent "situation" is not just about price. Try being a single mom with three or more children. Landlords have huge restrictions. Applications for a three-bedroom townhouse often state, "not suitable for children" and our government has no supports or resources to address unhealthy living situations such as black mould making children sick. Our government needs to examine housing issues more extensively, especially for those most vulnerable.
Sylvia Muirhead, Mississauga
Free parking lots at transit hubs will cut gridlock
One of the quickest ways to relieve Toronto's gridlock is to provide free parking lots at the GO and TTC stations from the edges of the city to the midpoint in the suburbs. Parking is expensive. For an added incentive, make the TTC trip free, too.
Sarah Shartal, Toronto
More bike lanes will reduce traffic
Bike lanes only make sense in the right places,
Opinion, Oct. 13
By removing bike lanes, the provincial government is only putting more people in cars, creating more traffic and a higher demand for gasoline. The government should really be making bike lanes more separate, creating physical barriers to keep cyclists safe from cars, and out of their way. Drivers find cyclists can be a nuisance when they have to share the road, but every driver knows how bad traffic already is in southern Ontario. Creating more of it is the last thing anyone wants. I love riding my bike and I wish that I could more, but actions like this make it difficult and force people like me who don't enjoy driving to get in their car and get stuck in traffic.
Owen Tipple, Burlington

Title: Letters:   Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians, Oct. 29 Meet the guy 'off the street' behind a stunning $203-million gift to CAMH - the largest mental health donation in Canadian history, Oct. 24 This megaproject could be Canada's next great nation-building exercise, Oct. 28 Numbers show why city is failing fight for shelters, Keenan, Oct. 26. Document 372 of 500

Ford's gift is borrowed from all of us: Premier's cynical attempt to buy votes will add $3 billion to the provincial debt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-gift-is-borrowed-all-us/docview/3122483148/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+gift+is+borrowed+from+all+of+us%3A+Premier%27s+cynical+attempt+to+buy+votes+will+add+%243+billion+to+the+provincial+debt&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Doug Ford, everyone's a winner.
What's not to like about a $200 cheque from Ford's Tories ahead of the next election?
That's the good news. Now for the bad news: Everyone's a loser.
This $200 giveaway isn't a gift from the gods, or from the premier personally. It comes at a cost of $3 billion to the provincial treasury - which is to say, you and me, personally and individually.
Think of it as a phoney lottery without the money to back it up, and no worthy winnings to give back. Just 15 million pointless prizes for voters and suckers whose support Ford is buying with their own money, just in time for an early election.
More precisely, it's all borrowed money. Ford's Progressive Conservative government remains deep in debt and deficit: The latest budgetary projection released Wednesday shows $6.6 billion in red ink, on top of the accumulated debt of $429 billion.
It's only money.
On the one hand, it's a handout for virtually every adult and child in Ontario. On the other hand, by cutting a cheque for everyone, Ford is cutting back on the helping hand available for anyone in need - health-care services, educational investments and help for homeless people.
There's not enough money for you. Yet here's some money for you.
To understand the inanity and insanity of this gesture, consider the bizarre timing - beyond the electoral calendar and political calculus. Focus on the fiscal hocus-pocus.
This pre-election goody is the main pillar of this year's fall economic statement (FES), an annual production of the mighty Ministry of Finance. The FES is supposed to be a serious deal - a sober accounting of the province's balance sheet at the mid-point of the fiscal year, in between budgets.
Now, Ford's Tories have given new meaning - if not a new name - to the FES: Ford Electioneering on Steroids.
Vote-buying only works if customers are buying what you're selling. Happily for the premier, the voting public has a built-in bias that credits Conservatives with automatic economic acumen.
Perhaps it helps that a folksy Ford is awfully good at persuading people that he feels their pain. In pre-election mode, the premier is all about affability and affordability, invoking pocketbook politics at every turn.
He trash talks taxes, laments the federal carbon levy and rages over road tolls. It worked in 2022 when he won re-election by slashing tolls and taxes, and cutting cheques for rebated licence fees, so why not more of the same to win again in 2025?
It's not as if he hasn't tried this before. As a city councillor, he famously handed out a wad of crisp $20 bills to voters at a Toronto Community Housing building.
The difference, of course, is that Ford was spending his own money back then. Now, as premier, he's gifting us with our own borrowed money to buy our own votes.
But he's not doing it alone.
His accomplice is Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, a seemingly sensible Bay Street veteran who had a life before politics. Back then, he proudly headed Dominion Bond Rating Service, handing out credit rating downgrades to profligate politicians.
Now, he's joined the club. Rather than acting as a prudent steward of the province's finances, Bethlenfalvy has repurposed himself into a partisan enabler of Ontario's populist premier.
On Bethlenfalvy's watch, buried in this year's mini-budget, is yet another unjustifiable and unsustainable giveaway: more than $7 billion in annual subsidies from the treasury to perpetuate the fiction that electricity rates are being kept low. This uncontrolled transfer of wealth, from hapless taxpayers to hydro ratepayers, has only gotten worse since Ford took power.
But there are more Bethlenfalvy blunders that merit scrutiny. In one of his first acts as president of the Treasury Board, before taking over at Finance, he imposed a pay restraint law on public servants that never made any sense, because there was no fiscal crisis to justify it. Predictably, the courts overruled him and ordered the government to make up lost pay amounting to billions of dollars for workers across the public sector.
All that said, the FES talks up "prudence in 2024-25," as if words matter more than numbers. Yet it sugar-coats Ontario's economic stasis by noting, ever so gingerly, that growth "is projected to ease" from 1.4 per cent in 2023 to 0.9 per cent this year - because "ease" is easier said than "decline."
Oh, and unemployment? It keeps climbing under this government - from 5.6 per cent when Ford took over from the last Liberal government in 2018 to a projected 6.9 per cent this year (revised upward from the 6.7 per cent Bethlenfalvy predicted in his budget last March, it peaked at 7.1 per cent in August, according to Statistics Canada).
"The news is good!" Bethlenfalvy boasted Wednesday, as if saying so makes it so. But the bad news is that cutting $200 cheques is an empty gesture that achieves nothing beyond buying votes.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy shake hands after Wednesday's fall economic statement, which includes plans for a $200 tax rebate for every Ontario resident.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

What to expect in Doug Ford's pre-election mini-budget + the Bloc Québécois turn on the Trudeau government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-expect-doug-fords-pre-election-mini-budget/docview/3122179846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+to+expect+in+Doug+Ford%27s+pre-election+mini-budget+%2B+the+Bloc+Qu%26eacute%3Bb%26eacute%3Bcois+turn+on+the+Trudeau+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, Oct. 30 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the Ford government's mini-budget, a Bloc push to topple Trudeau and more allegations against The One developer.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford's Tories will table their pre-election mini-budgetWith cheques, tuition grants, tax credits and a stay on the provincial gas tax, the Ford government's fall budget is jam-packed with voter-friendly goodies - even though the premier hasn't called an election ... yet. The finance minister will table the budget and reveal more about how the government will spend its billions at 1 p.m. Follow along for the details as they come.
Pass go and collect $200: Sending a "rebate" cheque to most Ontarians - to the tune of $3 billion - is part of the Ford government's plans. Here's who is expected to receive them.
Ahead of schedule: When he talked to the Star over the summer, Ford said he wasn't really thinking about an early election call for 2025.
The Bloc Québécois will try to topple the Trudeau governmentEt tu, Blanchet? The Liberal government is past its "expiration date" after failing to meet the Bloc Québécois' demands. Leader Yves-François Blanchet and his party say they will side with a non-confidence motion at the next chance, taking us one step closer to an election. Here's what you need to know.
To vote or not to vote: Meanwhile, a number of Justin Trudeau's own MPs are rallying their colleagues for a secret ballot vote on the PMs leadership of the Liberals.The One's Sam Mizrahi is said to owe lenders $58.8MThe receiver of The One - a luxury skyscraper mired in financial troubles - is alleging the project's original developer overpaid his own firm millions and kept commissions from defaulted sales. Famed Toronto developer Sam Mizrahi is strongly rejecting the allegations. Ana Periera breaks down the back-and-forth.

A York school board field trip is under scrutiny after a Qur'an reading was pulled from the agenda.A new report on missing children and unmarked graves calls for Indigenous-led investigations.Toronto has revamped its vacant home tax: Here's what you need to know.You can now buy beer and wine on your next Costco trip.Canada Post workers could go on strike as early as this weekend - and small businesses are concerned.Doug Ford wants big city mayors to ask for help to end encampments.Justin Trudeau's handgun ban is not working, the police union charges.Officials have confirmed a link between a senior Modi cabinet minister and India-supported violence in Canada.The TTC's e-bike ban has been put on pause over equity concerns.Chinese electric cars cost as little as $17,000. Why can't Canadians have them?Taylor Swift in Toronto: What to expect and how the city is getting ready.The Professional Women's Hockey League says it could add as many as two teams for the 2025-26 season.POV
Immigration didn't create our housing and health care woes, Mark McQueen argues, but fixing them begins with adjusting immigration targets.

Document 375 of 500

Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-spending-nearly-half-ontarios-tax/docview/3122179756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+spending+nearly+half+of+Ontario%27s+tax+windfall+on+%24200+%27rebate%27+cheques&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is spending nearly half of a $6.9-billion tax windfall on $200 pre-election "rebate" cheques.
Bolstered by higher than projected revenue from personal income tax, corporations tax and sales tax, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have earmarked $3 billion to send cheques to 15 million Ontario adults and children in the new year.
But Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement tabled Wednesday at Queen's Park said the Tories are still running a $6.6-billion deficit.
While that's lower than the surprise $9.8-billion shortfall for 2024-25 Bethlenfalvy forecast in his March 26 budget, it's far from the $200-million surplus he had projected last year.
With Ford expected to call an election next spring - one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote - Bethlenfalvy painted a rosy picture of Ontario's finances.
"Today we are in a position to do more," the treasurer told the legislature in a partisan speech in which he twice attacked "the previous Liberal government" that was in power from 2003 until 2018.
"We have slashed our deficits and are projecting a deficit of $6.6 billion in 2024-25 - an improvement of $3.2 billion from the budget, followed by a $1.5-billion deficit next year - then ... this government will balance the budget," he said of plans to dam the river of red ink in 2026-27.
Bethlenfalvy defended the Tories' decision to mail out cheques, first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, instead of further reducing the deficit to a more manageable $3.6 billion for 2024-25. (Government officials have not calculated the administrative cost of printing and mailing the millions of cheques.)
"With our new stronger fiscal position, we can now afford to provide real relief to families to help them in this time of challenge. We know that people aren't looking for some money back in time for next year's taxes," said the finance minister.
"They need real support, and they need it now. We are providing $200 per child under the age of 18 - meaning a family of five will receive $1,000." he said.
"I'm under no illusions that this will relieve all of the affordability pressures facing Ontario families, but it will help. It's real support and, most importantly, it allows the people of Ontario to choose how this money can best help them."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the one-time payment underscores that "all Ford has to offer are stale ideas and empty promises."
"People deserve a government that puts their tax dollars to work and spends it responsibly to make life easier. With Doug Ford, you're not getting what you paid for," she said.
"After more than six years of his costly schemes and scandals, people are feeling stuck."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Bethlenfalvy's mini-budget fails to "address the issues that really matter to Ontario families."
"Instead of making real investments to keep emergency rooms open, helping the over 2.5-million people without a family doctor, addressing the urgent need for long-term financial relief, or making housing more affordable, Doug Ford introduced yet another failed fall economic statement that puts Doug Ford's rich friends first, and you and your family last," said Crombie.
"People are dying on wait-lists and Doug Ford is spending billions on booze, foreign spas and the Greenbelt scandal," she said, referring to the expansion of beer and wine sales, the Ontario Place redevelopment and the RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap debacle.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Ford "has abandoned us."
"Costs are up, homes aren't getting built, climate change is worse and our health-care system is failing those of us who need it most," said Schreiner.
"All the things that we should be able to expect from provincial governments -- to keep our neighbours housed, to make sure homes get built, that doctors are available, mental health is accessible -- the premier has walked away from. People expect better."
The cash payments come as Ontario's debt has skyrocketed to a projected $439 billion this year since Ford took office in 2018.
That's up $116 billion - or 36 per cent - from $323 billion left by the previous Liberal government six years ago.
However, interest on the provincial debt is expected to dip by $1.2 billion to $12.7 billion - more than the budget of every department except the Ministry of Health, the Ministry of Education and the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services.
The annual cost of servicing the debt, which is the largest of any subnational jurisdiction in the world, is anticipated to increase to $14.5 billion in two years.
"Ontario now has the lowest borrowing costs of any province in Canada, saving taxpayers $1.2 billion that we are now able to reinvest back into people," boasted Bethlenfalvy.
"In fact, our interest on debt relative to revenues is currently at its lowest level since the 1980s and our debt to GDP ratio, which is a measure of our fiscal health, remains at the best level it's been in a decade. This is very good news for Ontario taxpayers. It means that when the province needs to borrow, we can do it at a lower cost."
The costs of abiding by a court ruling that struck down the Bill 124 salary restraint legislation for public servants, which limited pay hikes to one per cent, has resulted in retroactive raises for workers totalling $6.7 billion.
CAPTION: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy answers questions prior to delivering the 2024 budget in the Ontario Legislature on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Economic development; Costs; Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic development Costs Budget deficits

Title: Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

____________________________________________________________

Document 376 of 500

Immigration headlines distract from what's really broken in housing and health care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/immigration-headlines-distract-whats-really/docview/3122179742/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Immigration+headlines+distract+from+what%27s+really+broken+in+housing+and+health+care&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Facing a tight housing market and with 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, Ottawa's latest faux solution is to "slash" the number of new permanent residents to 395,000 in 2025, while capping new international study permits at an eye-popping 874,000 over the next two years.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller acknowledges "the system needed an injection of discipline," although Glenn Vollebregt, the president of St. Lawrence College (SLC) in Kingston, argues that such moderation translates to "Canada saying we're closed for business."
Ottawa authorized 111,865 new international students in 2013. The revised 24-month Liberal plan provides almost 900,000 student visas - a massive increase versus a decade ago. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's newly "slashed" permanent resident target is still 30 per cent higher than it was in 2016, which was a high watermark at the time.
I don't know anything about SLC's reputation for numeric literacy, but Canada has not withdrawn the welcome mat.
The adjusted immigration targets are still at near-record highs, but some critics nevertheless see racism at play. One migrant advocate recently claimed any reduction in permanent immigration was the government "pandering to the xenophobic idea that migrants are responsible for the housing and affordability crisis."
That's not xenophobia, it's our lack of infrastructure.
Honesty is the first step in any recovery journey, and I commend Miller for being the only senior Liberal to admit that "we've got a lot of people coming in, and it's putting strain on housing prices, on infrastructure." Miller's absence from the shadow Liberal leadership campaign likely explains why he has the guts to even tinker with Trudeau's "hog wild" immigration strategy.
Nothing the Liberals have done on the immigration file will magically reverse the fact that Canadians continue to get poorer relative to our American cousins. As we say in the venture capital space, you can't cut your way to growth.
To give you a sense of how badly we've been served, consider these two inexplicable, but representative, realities: one macro, one micro.
On the macro front, Canada's labour force participation rate has stubbornly stayed around 65 per cent for the past 20 years.
Across nine years in office, the Liberal government has trumpeted a variety of policies that should have boosted that participation rate: $10 daycare, billions for improved transit, legislating against non-competes, $8 billion for "underserved" entrepreneurs. Throw in remote work, and it's troubling that more people aren't finding a productive place in our economy.
The Bank of Canada says high immigration levels should push labour force participation rates up, yet the needle hasn't moved. Demographics? Perhaps.
The accepted wisdom is that Canada needed skilled permanent residents to fill a variety of labour gaps. If that's actually what the Liberals have been doing, it defies logic that the May 2024 unemployment rate among "newcomers" was 11.7 per cent, more than double the 5.7 per cent rate of everyone else.
One explanation is that there's a labour market mismatch. Last summer, CIBC Economists identified 11 key occupations that showed consistent signs of a labour shortage, despite 3.2 million new immigrants over the past decade. Not surprisingly, health trades were high on the list.
That's where my micro datapoint comes in. According to the Registered Nurses' Association, Ontario entered the pandemic needing almost 24,000 additional nurses. The shortfall is even worse today, yet high schoolers need a 92 per cent average to get into Queen's University's nursing program - higher than, say, mechatronic engineering.
Who expects to resolve Canada's labour market imbalances when Queen's admits just 124 new nursing students each year, but enrols up to 1,000 at the esteemed Smith Engineering school? If only the Prime Minister would meet with his provincial counterparts and get this stuff right: recruit more international nurses and expand our nursing schools.
Whether you add or subtract berths on RMS Titanic, that great ship still sinks. Our challenge isn't immigration, per se - it's solving the strains in housing and health care. If we don't quickly change course, Canada as we know it faces a watery grave.
Correction - Oct. 30, 2024
This article was edited from a previous version that mistakenly referred to the RMS Titanic as the HMS Titanic.
CAPTION: Canada's Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Marc Miller in Ottawa on Oct. 8 2024.

Document 377 of 500

Ford tells cities to get tough on encampments: Premier suggests use of 'notwithstanding clause' to override ruling on rights of unhoused

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-tells-cities-get-tough-on-encampments/docview/3121965659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+tells+cities+to+get+tough+on+encampments%3A+Premier+suggests+use+of+%27notwithstanding+clause%27+to+override+ruling+on+rights+of+unhoused&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Urban mayors grappling with homelessness and encampments need to show "backbone" and toughen up their push for stronger reforms, Premier Doug Ford says.
"Why don't the big city mayors actually put in writing that they want the province to change the homeless program, make sure that we move the homeless along - and why don't they put in the use of the notwithstanding clause or something like that" so reforms can't be overturned by the courts, Ford said Tuesday.
"That's what they should be doing, rather than just going up to the edge ... Let's see if they have the backbone to do it - if they really want the homeless situation to improve."
Ford - referring to a decision last year in Waterloo in which a judge ruled that it's a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict those in encampments if there are no shelter spaces available for them - said if the mayors of the province's biggest cities "want something done, they have to put it in writing to us to make sure that they're 100 per cent behind us. You can't put your butt on both sides of the fence."
The charter's "notwithstanding clause" allows a government to pass legislation that overrides certain rights.
Ontario Big City Mayors, representing 29 of the province's largest communities, met recently in Markham and urged both the provincial and federal governments to "step up and take action now on the mental health, addictions and homeless crisis" they all face, and in particular to fund and build shelters and supportive housing.
They also want a review of the Mental Health and the Health Care Consent acts, and a ban on the public use of illegal drugs, among other measures.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, chair of Ontario Big City Mayors, said leaders have sent multiple written requests to the government about addressing the crisis, in particular asking for "one point person, a specific minister or ministry, in charge of solving this" and a provincewide plan.
While municipal leaders discussed requesting the province enact new legislation using the notwithstanding clause, they could not reach consensus, she added.
However, "we've been very specific in what we, as a caucus, believe is going to achieve the best results for our community - and the premier has received all of that in writing ... and if he is of the opinion that the notwithstanding clause is the right way to go, he has every opportunity to implement that," she said.
"We have put into our request what we think are the best outcomes."
She said the "issue becomes, if you are using the notwithstanding clause to close down encampments, but people have nowhere to go, we're no farther ahead."
Answering questions after making an unrelated announcement in Scarborough on Tuesday morning, Ford said he receives "endless calls about people camping out in the parks, needles being all over the park."
Parents, he added, are "scared to put their kids out in the park by themselves, no matter what age, and we have to make a move. But big city mayors, you want to get it done? I need that letter."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, however, accused Ford of taking "cheap shots."
"He never, ever takes true responsibility or accountability," she told reporters at Queen's Park. "The mess that we are experiencing right now, it has happened under his watch. He is responsible. He is the premier of this province and he has not done his job."
Barrie Mayor Alex Nuttall fully supports the province using the notwithstanding clause to help "restore municipal governments' ability to maintain order and safety," and especially to "ensure our children walking to school are safe in each and every park."
However, in a written statement he also acknowledged such a move "will not solve all problems, but at least give our mayors the opportunity to move forward and protect our cities' most innocent and vulnerable citizens."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau With files from Rob Ferguson

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+orders+LCBO+to+try+again+after+it+orders+paper+bags+from+Quebec%2C+Oct.+23+These+Ontarians+are+watching+how+MAID+is+being+accessed+and+used.+Here%27s+what+they+found%2C+Oct.+25+This+deputy+police+chief+was+allegedly+caught+going+50+km%2Fh+over+the+speed+limit+but+wasn%27t+ticketed%2C+Oct.+24+Food+banks+across+Canada+face+%27tipping+point%27+in+demand+with+more+than+two+million+visits+in+one+month%2C+Oct.+28+Do+cycling+corridors+really+slow+traffic%3F+We+fact-checked+the+bike+lane+blame+game%2C+Oct.+27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Did LCBO deem bags inferior?
Doug Ford orders LCBO to try again after it orders paper bags from Quebec, Oct. 23
Instead of implementing a "buy Ontario" mandate, Premier Doug Ford should be helping Ontario firms compete in Canadian and global markets. Why was a Quebec product deemed the best choice? In what aspect were Ontario products deemed inferior? These are the questions that need to be answered. The LCBO isn't all that good at running the business. Your interference may be contributing to their poor performance.
Brian Williams, Belleville, Ont.
Save Ontario's trees and BYOB
Premier Doug Ford just keeps doing the same things that make a total mess of our province. Ontarians already have all sizes of reusable bags that are strong enough to carry any amount of liquor so there is no need to cut down additional trees. When buying from the LCBO, take a material bag with you and don't use Ford-made LCBO paper bags that are only good once. Save our trees.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
Advance MAID requests should not be withheld
These Ontarians are watching how MAID is being accessed and used. Here's what they found, Oct. 25
As a recent victim of a hit and run on my bicycle that required high risk surgery at a Toronto trauma centre, I had occasion to explore the matters of MAID. I wanted to be assured that no undue life saving effort would be made in the event the surgery left me completely paralyzed and unable to speak or write. I was appalled to learn that unless I could speak and authorize MAID after the surgery, it would be withheld.
While I am pleased to report that I am still alive post-surgery and doing reasonably well, thanks to the amazing medical team at St. Michael's Hospital, I believe this situation must change. People of sound mind who authorize MAID in advance should not have it withheld because a life-altering situation suddenly leaves them unable to authorize it. We allow such authorizations in the Power of Attorney but not in MAID. I fail to see the logic and I suggest this situation will be of significant yet tragic import to a large number of Canadians over the next few years.
Barry Morrison, Etobicoke
Society suffers when laws are not enforced
This deputy police chief was allegedly caught going 50 km/h over the speed limit but wasn't ticketed, Oct. 24
Kudos to the Star for publishing its investigation of the Durham Police Service's inaction against its deputy chief for speeding 50 km/h above the speed limit. The police fraternity protecting one of its own is understandable, although not acceptable. What is more troubling is the lackadaisical approach of the Durham police board took to bring this issue to an appropriate conclusion even though the report from the Peel Police on this has been available for some time. We all suffer when the laws are not enforced.
Shahid Naeem, Ajax
Food bank lines growing longer despite harvest
Food banks across Canada face 'tipping point' in demand with more than two million visits in one month, Oct. 28
Grocery stores are overflowing with food. It being harvest time, there are loads of fresh produce. Yet these are unaffordable to a lot of families. Meanwhile the food bank lineups get longer. The contrast is so blatant. How can this happen in our society? There are headlines almost daily in our newspapers revealing this emergency situation, yet governments refuse to deal with the cause: monopoly control. Peoples' needs should be put before corporate greed.
Frances Sedgwick, Toronto
If we don't build bike lanes, they won't come
Do cycling corridors really slow traffic? We fact-checked the bike lane blame game, Oct. 27
A key piece of anecdotal evidence about travel time and the new bike lanes on Bloor Street West was the reporters' description of the difference between travelling westbound and eastbound during morning rush hour. Not surprisingly, they experienced a doubled travel time eastbound, heading downtown, as compared with westbound, against the flow of morning traffic. This problem has existed for years.
In 2019, prior to the pandemic and well before the installation of the bike lanes, I had to drive into the city from my home in Etobicoke daily as caregiver/driver for someone undergoing treatment at Princess Margaret Hospital. There was always a bottleneck eastbound on Bloor during rush hour, backing up traffic from the South Kingsway light. That bottleneck is the major cause of traffic slowdown today, just as it was in 2019. The problem is the irregular combination of a busy intersection at South Kingsway and Bloor, and another busy intersection one block east, at the foot of Jane Street. It seems that city engineers can't quite figure out how to manage these intersections because traffic smooths out and flows again east of Jane, even with the new bike lanes in place.
Two of my acquaintances have died cycling in Toronto, one of whom was very close to me. Surely a little inconvenience to motorists, with the stress on "little," is a small price to pay to keep our citizens who choose to travel by bicycle safe. To borrow from a well-known literary source, if we don't build it (cycling infrastructure), they (cyclists) won't come.
David Remski, Etobicoke
Focus should
be on keeping cyclists safe
Why are we obsessed with whether or not bike lanes slow down car traffic? Why are we not talking instead about safety for people riding bikes? I am a year-round cyclist. If Premier Doug Ford is successful in removing any of the bike lanes that I use, I will be forced to ride in the car traffic lanes. I will be riding in the middle of the lane for my own safety. Won't that slow down car traffic a lot more than if I were in a dedicated bike lane?
Ontario Transport Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria recently wrote in the Star, "I join hundreds of thousands of drivers on the roads of the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area every day. I leave my house in Brampton, say goodbye to my wife and daughters and take a leap of faith that I'll be able to get to the office on time."
When I ride my bike, I join cyclists on the roads of Toronto, and I take a leap of faith that I'll be able to get to my destination alive.
Donna Patterson, Toronto

Document 379 of 500

Health, housing fix starts at the border

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-housing-fix-starts-at-border/docview/3121965503/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health%2C+housing+fix+starts+at+the+border&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Facing a tight housing market and with 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, Ottawa's latest faux solution is to "slash" the number of new permanent residents to 395,000 in 2025, while capping new international study permits at an eye-popping 874,000 over the next two years.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller acknowledges "the system needed an injection of discipline," although Glenn Vollebregt, the president of St. Lawrence College (SLC) in Kingston, argues that such moderation translates to "Canada saying we're closed for business."
Ottawa authorized 111,865 new international students in 2013. The revised 24-month Liberal plan provides almost 900,000 student visas - a massive increase versus a decade ago. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's newly "slashed" permanent resident target is still 30 per cent higher than in 2016, which was a high watermark at the time.
I don't know anything about SLC's reputation for numeric literacy, but Canada has not withdrawn the welcome mat.
The adjusted immigration targets are still at near-record highs, but some critics nevertheless see racism at play.
One migrant advocate recently claimed any reduction in permanent immigration was the government "pandering to the xenophobic idea that migrants are responsible for the housing and affordability crisis."
That's not xenophobia, it's our lack of infrastructure.
Honesty is the first step in any recovery journey, and I commend Miller for being the only senior Liberal to admit that "we've got a lot of people coming in, and it's putting strain on housing prices, on infrastructure." Miller's absence from the shadow Liberal leadership campaign likely explains why he has the guts to even tinker with Trudeau's "hog wild" immigration strategy.
Nothing the Liberals have done on the immigration file will magically reverse the fact that Canadians continue to get poorer relative to our American cousins. As we say in the venture capital space, you can't cut your way to growth.
To give a sense of how badly we've been served, consider these two inexplicable, but representative, realities: one macro, one micro.
On the macro front, Canada's labour force participation rate has stubbornly stayed around 65 per cent for the past 20 years.
Across nine years in office, the Liberal government has trumpeted a variety of policies that should have boosted that participation rate: $10 daycare, billions for improved transit, legislating against non-competes, $8 billion for "underserved" entrepreneurs. Throw in remote work, and it's troubling that more people aren't finding a productive place in our economy.
The Bank of Canada says high immigration levels should push labour force participation rates up, yet the needle hasn't moved. Demographics? Perhaps.
The accepted wisdom is that Canada needed skilled permanent residents to fill a variety of labour gaps. If that's actually what the Liberals have been doing, it defies logic that the May 2024 unemployment rate among "newcomers" was 11.7 per cent, more than double the 5.7 per cent rate of everyone else.
One explanation is that there's a labour market mismatch. Last summer, CIBC Economists identified 11 key occupations that showed consistent signs of a labour shortage, despite 3.2 million new immigrants over the past decade. Not surprisingly, health trades were high on the list.
That's where my micro datapoint comes in. According to the Registered Nurses' Association, Ontario entered the pandemic needing almost 24,000 additional nurses. The shortfall is even worse today, yet high schoolers need a 92 per cent average to get into Queen's University's nursing program - higher than, say, mechatronic engineering.
Who expects to resolve Canada's labour market imbalances when Queen's admits just 124 new nursing students each year, but enrols up to 1,000 at the esteemed Smith Engineering school? If only the prime minister would meet with his provincial counterparts and get this stuff right: recruit more international nurses and expand our nursing schools.
Whether you add or subtract berths on HMS Titanic, that great ship still sinks. Our challenge isn't immigration, per se - it's solving the strains in housing and health care. If we don't quickly change course, Canada as we know it faces a watery grave.
Mark McQueen is a Toronto-based entrepreneur who has worked on Bay Street for more than 30 years. He is the former board chair of two federal agencies and was an adviser
in the Brian Mulroney PMO.
He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Immigration Minister Marc Miller said the international study permit system was in need of "discipline."Blair Gable Photography
