Document 380 of 500

'Rebate' cheques will be mailed next year: Ford accused of using taxpayers' money to 'buy their vote' as payments set stage for spring election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rebate-cheques-will-be-mailed-next-year/docview/3121965482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Rebate%27+cheques+will+be+mailed+next+year%3A+Ford+accused+of+using+taxpayers%27+money+to+%27buy+their+vote%27+as+payments+set+stage+for+spring+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is mailing out $200 "rebate" cheques to 15 million Ontarians in a $3-billion hit to a provincial treasury awash in red ink.
Confirming a scheme first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, Ford on Tuesday said the cheques would be in the mail early in the new year, "because life is expensive right now."
The payments - a cornerstone pledge of Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement Wednesday - set the stage for a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Striking a partisan tone at the campaign-style government announcement in a Scarborough gymnasium, Ford tried to contrast his two-term Progressive Conservatives with the opposition Liberals and the New Democrats.
"The difference between our party and the Liberals and NDP - they believe in one thing: tax the taxpayers to death; drive companies out of this province. We've taken a different approach," the premier insisted.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers. She wants to increase taxes, increase the carbon tax," he said in a swipe at the Liberal leader.
Crombie, for her part, reminded Ford "the only reason" Ontarians pay the federal carbon levy is because he scrapped the province's cap-and-trade environmental alliance with Quebec and California in 2018.
"We will not implement a carbon tax," she said, accusing the premier of using $3 billion of "taxpayers' money to buy their vote" next spring.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles dubbed the cheques, which will bear Bethlenfalvy's signature, a "cynical" ploy.
"A lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis," said Stiles.
"A $200 cheque, it might help you through groceries for that week, but it isn't going to help you the next month or the month after that," she said.
The tax-free cheques will be sent to all taxpayers 18 and over and families who qualify for federal Canada Child Benefit (CCB) payment receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
"For families with children who did not receive the CCB for 2024, the government would provide an opportunity for a taxpayer rebate payment of $200 per child through an alternative process," the PC government said in a statement.
That means 15 million payments will be sent out - 12.5 million for adults and 2.5 million for children. Only those Ontarians who were "bankrupt or incarcerated" this year will not receive a payment.
But Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "if you're going to do something like this, you should have it means-tested," so only the people most in need receive it.
"I don't understand why millionaires and billionaires like Galen Weston should get a $200 cheque when there are so many Ontarians who can't afford a home, 2.5 million can't access a doctor," said Schreiner, referring to the grocery tycoon.
"It is outrageous that people with seven-figure incomes are getting a $200 cheque."
Asked by a journalist why the rich should also be mailed a cheque, Ford, multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family, bristled: "Because they're taxpayers. These are tax dollars going back to the taxpayers and we're going to give it back to every single person in Ontario."
The cheque-mailing is a reprise of what former Tory premier Mike Harris did in 2000.
But when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques, the province's books were balanced. (That $200 payment a generation ago would be worth around $335 today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.)
Bethlenfalvy's fiscal update Wednesday is expected to show a hefty deficit, though the short-fall should be less than the surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25 he projected in the March 26 budget.
That was $10 billion more than the $200-million surplus he had forecast beforehand.
With files From Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau With files From Rob Ferguson

Document 381 of 500

Ask me to use notwithstanding clause to end encampments, Doug Ford tells mayors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ask-me-use-notwithstanding-clause-end-encampments/docview/3122287625/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ask+me+to+use+notwithstanding+clause+to+end+encampments%2C+Doug+Ford+tells+mayors&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Urban mayors grappling with homelessness and encampments need to show "backbone" and toughen up their push for stronger reforms, Premier Doug Ford says.
"Why don't the big city mayors actually put in writing that they want the province to change the homeless program, make sure that we move the homeless along - and why don't they put in the use of the notwithstanding clause or something like that" so reforms can't be overturned by the courts, Ford said Tuesday.
"That's what they should be doing, rather than just going up to the edge ... Let's see if they have the backbone to do it - if they really want the homeless situation to improve."
Ford - referring to a decision last year in Waterloo in which a judge ruled that it's a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict those in encampments if there are no shelter spaces available for them - said if the mayors of the province's biggest cities "want something done, they have to put it in writing to us to make sure that they're 100 per cent behind us. You can't put your butt on both sides of the fence."
The charter's "notwithstanding clause" allows a government to pass legislation that overrides certain rights.
Ontario Big City Mayors, representing 29 of the province's largest communities, met recently in Markham and urged both the provincial and federal governments to "step up and take action now on the mental health, addictions and homeless crisis" they all face, and in particular to fund and build shelters and supportive housing.
They also want a review of the Mental Health and the Health Care Consent acts, and a ban on the public use of illegal drugs, among other measures.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, chair of Ontario Big City Mayors, said leaders have sent multiple written requests to the government about addressing the crisis, in particular asking for "one point person, a specific minister or ministry, in charge of solving this" and a province-wide plan.
While municipal leaders discussed requesting the province enact new legislation using the notwithstanding clause, they could not reach consensus, she added.
However, "we've been very specific in what we, as a caucus, believe is going to achieve the best results for our community - and the premier has received all of that in writing ... and if he is of the opinion that the notwithstanding clause is the right way to go, he has every opportunity to implement that," she said.
"We have put into our request what we think are the best outcomes."
She said the "issue becomes, if you are using the notwithstanding clause to close down encampments, but people have nowhere to go, we're no farther ahead."
Answering questions after making an unrelated announcement in Scarborough on Tuesday morning, Ford said he receives "endless calls about people camping out in the parks, needles being all over the park."
Parents, he added, are "scared to put their kids out in the park by themselves, no matter what age, and we have to make a move. But big city mayors, you want to get it done? I need that letter."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, however, accused Ford of taking "cheap shots."
"He never, ever takes true responsibility or accountability," she told reporters at Queen's Park. "The mess that we are experiencing right now, it has happened under his watch. He is responsible. He is the premier of this province and he has not done his job."
Barrie Mayor Alex Nuttall fully supports the province using the notwithstanding clause to help "restore municipal governments' ability to maintain order and safety," and especially to "ensure our children walking to school are safe in each and every park."
However, in a written statement he also acknowledged such a move "will not solve all problems, but at least give our mayors the opportunity to move forward and protect our cities' most innocent and vulnerable citizens."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: The Tiffany Barton homeless encampment in Hamilton is shown on Oct. 9, 2024.

Document 382 of 500

Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-announces-details-200-cheques-15/docview/3121581124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+announces+details+of+%24200+cheques+for+15+million+Ontarians&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is mailing out $200 "rebate" cheques to 15 million Ontarians in a $3-billion hit to a provincial treasury awash in red ink.
Confirming a scheme first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, Ford on Tuesday said the cheques would be in the mail early in the new year, "because life is expensive right now."
The payments - a cornerstone pledge of Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement Wednesday - set the stage for a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Striking a partisan tone at the campaign-style government announcement in a Scarborough gymnasium, Ford tried to contrast his two-term Progressive Conservatives with the opposition Liberals and the New Democrats.
"The difference between our party and the Liberals and NDP - they believe in one thing: tax the taxpayers to death; drive companies out of this province. We've taken a different approach," the premier insisted.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers. She wants to increase taxes, increase the carbon tax," he said in a swipe at the Liberal leader.
Crombie, for her part, reminded Ford "the only reason" Ontarians pay the federal carbon levy is because he scrapped the province's cap-and-trade environmental alliance with Quebec and California in 2018.
"We will not implement a carbon tax," she said, accusing the premier of using $3 billion of "taxpayers' money to buy their vote" next spring.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles dubbed the cheques, which will bear Bethlenfalvy's signature, a "cynical" ploy.
"A lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis," said Stiles.
"A $200 cheque, it might help you through groceries for that week, but it isn't going to help you the next month or the month after that," she said.
The tax-free cheques will be sent to all taxpayers 18 and over and families who qualify for federal Canada Child Benefit (CCB) payment receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
"For families with children who did not receive the CCB for 2024, the government would provide an opportunity for a taxpayer rebate payment of $200 per child through an alternative process," the PC government said in a statement.
That means 15 million payments will be sent out - 12.5 million for adults and 2.5 million for children. Only those Ontarians who were "bankrupt or incarcerated" this year will not receive a payment.
But Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "if you're going to do something like this, you should have it means-tested," so only the people most in need receive it.
"I don't understand why millionaires and billionaires like Galen Weston should get a $200 cheque when there are so many Ontarians who can't afford a home, 2.5 million can't access a doctor," said Schreiner, referring to the grocery tycoon.
"It is outrageous that people with seven-figure incomes are getting a $200 cheque."
Asked by a journalist why the rich should also be mailed a cheque, Ford, multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family, bristled: "Because they're taxpayers. These are tax dollars going back to the taxpayers and we're going to give it back to every single person in Ontario."
The cheque-mailing is a reprise of what former Tory premier Mike Harris did in 2000.
But when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques, the province's books were balanced. (That $200 payment a generation ago would be worth around $335 today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.)
Bethlenfalvy's fiscal update Wednesday is expected to show a hefty deficit, though the shortfall should be less than the surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25 he projected in the March 26 budget.
That was $10 billion more than the $200 million surplus he had forecast beforehand.
With files From Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queen's Park as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Monday.

Document 383 of 500

The real reason you can't trust politicians to be in charge of their own salaries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/real-reason-you-cant-trust-politicians-be-charge/docview/3121581101/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+real+reason+you+can%27t+trust+politicians+to+be+in+charge+of+their+own+salaries&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You cannot trust politicians with approving the size of their own paycheques.
This seems kind of obvious to everyone. But, in my experience, it isn't true for the reasons most people think. We think they'll line their own pockets by overpaying themselves.
In reality, they usually underpay themselves.
On Monday, the Star's city hall bureau chief Ben Spurr wrote about a staff report he obtained claiming Toronto city councillors were drastically underpaid, which was quietly buried by Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson in August 2023.
According to that report, city councillors in Toronto represented the most constituents of any in Canada, yet were paid less than representatives in many comparable cities, especially those close by. They made at least $24,000 per year less than councillors in Markham, Vaughan, Mississauga and Brampton. Toronto councillors were paid on average $1.06 per constituent they represent, while those in Markham were paid $5.27 per constituent.
The last time city councillors gave themselves a raise other than automatic inflationary adjustments was 2006. In 2021, they voted against giving themselves a raise despite a report from city staff recommending one.
Up the road at Queen's Park, MPPs haven't seen a raise since 2008, as the Star's Robert Benzie reported early this year. Without even inflationary increases, they earn roughly $12,000 per year less than Toronto city councillors (and $78,000 per year less than federal MPs). And yet, despite some unnamed insider grumbling and a report urging a raise from the provincial integrity commissioner, Premier Doug Ford has no plans to increase salaries.
If you understand politics at all, it isn't hard to understand why this is the case. Chow was preparing to put forward a big tax increase and suggest spending increases on a bunch of things, and didn't need the political noise of people suggesting she and her colleagues were in it to make themselves rich. Ford has always been a "stop the gravy train" advocate of low taxes, who would be naturally averse to anything that looks like a benefit for government leaders (his brother, famously, paid his office expenses out of his own pocket at city hall).
Politically, it is never a good time for elected representatives of any political stripe to be seen giving themselves payments or perks. Even in Ottawa, where federal politicians are paid comparatively better, past scandals over expense-account orange juice and hotel rooms have created an atmosphere where successive prime ministers have left the official residence to rot in place rather than approve any spending to maintain it in a state suitable for human habitation.
And so, in the absence of any independent outside authority setting elected officials' pay, they don't raise it.
Many people will think that's fine. Good, even. Saving taxpayers money, and all that. Especially since they aren't exactly paid poverty wages: at $128,347 per year for Toronto city councillors and $116,550 for MPPs, both jobs pay better than the average family income in Canada.
They are important jobs. And in the world of professionals and executives, these aren't attractive salaries. It's reasonable to think that they severely limit the pool of candidates to those who are either wealthy enough as to not care about the salary (say, being born into wealth like the Ford brothers) or those who could not get an executive-level job anywhere else. There's no job security for MPPs and city councillors, and no pension. Applicants often have to take months off unpaid from other jobs to obtain them.
It is entirely reasonable to expect people won't seek public office to enrich themselves. It is less reasonable to expect that qualified management- and professional-level people who might be good candidates need to sacrifice the possibility of, say, qualifying for a Toronto mortgage, in order to do the job.
Elected officials shouldn't finance a lavish lifestyle on the public dime. But they ought to earn a competitive upper-middle-class salary that reflects the importance and difficulty of the job they do. No private sector company would expect to attract great candidates if they insisted on paying executives significantly less than they'd earn in comparable work elsewhere - even if they justified the decision by claiming to want employees who felt a sense of mission.
That logic is unlikely to change anything. No active elected official publicly quoted in the Star's recent stories advocated for a raise. After all, even if they'd secretly appreciate the money, they're already doing the job for the pay offered now. It's those who declined to run based on economics whose voices are missing. Maybe some of those are ones we'd benefit from hearing.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson shelved a report that recommended giving Toronto councillors a raise.

Subject: Politicians; Public officials; Wages & salaries; Executives

Document 384 of 500

Doug Ford is trying to put on a show of giving our own money back to us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-trying-put-on-show-giving-our-own/docview/3121580980/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+trying+to+put+on+a+show+of+giving+our+own+money+back+to+us&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Famed economist Thomas Sowell had a great line about how politicians try to con the public into voting for them. "First you take the people's money quietly," he said, "and then you give some of it back to them flamboyantly." Sowell was writing about the welfare state, but his description applies to a great many other government initiatives.
For example, the Trudeau government's carbon tax and rebate. Because the tax is not "revenue neutral," the sum of the total rebates to taxpayers is lower than the total direct costs of the tax, but the federal government is flamboyant in issuing rebates - even going so far as to dictate how banks must label these rebates.
In Ontario, the Ford government is now preparing its own flamboyant distribution of money it takes from taxpayers. As the Star first reportedahead of the government's fall economic statement scheduled for Oct. 30, the government plans to send cheques of $200 or more to every Ontarian. Reports of the $200 handouts came amid increasing speculation that Ford will call an early election in 2025. But while this flamboyant distribution of taxpayer money may help buy votes, it's terrible policy.
The Ontario government's main sources of revenue are personal incomes taxes, sales taxes, business income taxes, and employer and payroll taxes. These are all what economists call "distortionary taxes" that disincentivize and reduce work effort, earnings, business investment and employment. So the government collects money through distortionary and economically damaging taxes, then plans to distribute the money back - in the form of its $200 per person "rebate" - in a way that does not encourage or increase productivity or business activity.
While the Ford government will surely say it's putting money back into taxpayer pockets, it would make much more sense - and do far less economic harm - to simply not take that money through distortionary taxes in the first place.
Indeed, Ontario's high tax rates today remain woefully uncompetitive, contributing to slowing productivity and economic growth. The top marginal personal income tax rate is higher in Ontario than in all 50 U.S. states and higher than every other province except Newfoundland and Labrador and Nova Scotia, reducing innovation and discouraging entrepreneurship.
In fact, according to a paper published earlier this year, tax economist Bev Dahlby estimated that for the Ontario government to raise an additional $1 in income tax revenue, the cost to the private sector (what economists call the "marginal cost of public funds") would be a staggering $6.76. Payroll taxes, too, have a strong negative effect on workers and the private sector.
When it comes to business income taxes, the news is similarly discouraging. Dahlby estimated raising $1 at the margin in corporate tax revenue would cost Ontario taxpayers $3.59, up from an estimate of $2.89 a decade ago. Notably, before coming to power in 2018, the Ford government promised to reduce the business tax rate - a promise that more than six years later remains unfulfilled.
Sending $200 cheques to every Ontarian might -- as Thomas Sowell suggested -- be a successful way for the Ford government to increase public support ahead of a potential provincial election. but the economic evidence suggests, assuming personal and business income tax revenues are used to fund these rebates, that for every $1 spent on this scheme will cost taxpayers more than $3 - a horrendous deal.
Matthew Lau is an adjunct scholar with the Fraser Institute.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's $200 per person "rebate" does not encourage or increase productivity or business activity, writes Mathew Lau of the Fraser Institute.
CREDIT: Matthew Lau, Contributor

Subject: Tax rates; Rebates; Payroll taxes; Tax revenues; Business income; Taxpayers; Income taxes; Productivity

Safe city streets mean accommodating bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/safe-city-streets-mean-accommodating-bike-lanes/docview/3121580975/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Safe+city+streets+mean+accommodating+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Ford blasts bike lanes, lauds rebate, Oct. 23
Bike lanes unquestionably contribute to traffic in Toronto, but the recent significant increase in bike users makes bike lanes critical to safe city commutes. Robert Benzie writes that Premier Doug Ford is opposed to bike lanes on busy streets and urges them to be put on secondary roads.
Reducing bike lanes on the well-known busy streets such as Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue seems like an ineffective solution to the advancing traffic problem of downtown Toronto. More and more people are relying on bikes for transportation, and reducing the existing bike lanes would create a more inconvenient situation for all commuters.
According to Nanos Research in a 2019 study, "the percentage of Toronto residents who report cycling continues to increase," as the percentage of cyclists has gone from 54 per cent in 2009 to 70 per cent in 2019. As a university student in the GTA, I have witnessed a rise in people using bikes on busy streets and around university as they become more accessible through city bike share programs. Not only are bike lanes essential for the many students who do not own cars, but also for daily commuters that live and/or work in the busy downtown area of Toronto.
I am concerned that eliminating existing bike lanes would create an even more dangerous situation for bikers than what already exists today. For some people, bike lanes on main roads are the only option to safely reach their destination without sharing sidewalks with walking pedestrians or busy street lanes with cars.
Fidelia Suliman, Mississauga
Whose time does Bill 212 promise to save? Not cyclists'
Premier Doug Ford's Bill 212, Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, will take the city further away from a safe city for all, in particular, sacrificing youth mobility. As a 19-year-old undergrad student, I understand the importance of efficient and safe transportation that is accessible to youth. Whose time does the act promise to save? Not ours. Students and other people who do not use cars are equally deserving of safe and efficient transportation options. Cycling using city infrastructure like protected bike lanes is fast, affordable, and a healthy way to get around the city. Fewer bike lanes will make it slower for young people to get around and also more dangerous.
In 2023, the Toronto Police Service reported 33 cyclists involved in collisions, five of whom were between 15 and 24 years old. A city built for youth allows for walkability and cycling. StudentMoveTO, a study of student transportation found that 80 per cent of students surveyed do not drive to campus. Because the majority of youth do not use cars, they rely on public transit, cycling and walking as their modes of transportation. Not only do bike lanes help the safety of cyclists, but they also reduce the number of people driving which would help with Toronto's congestion problem. Building a safe city for all must include the youth population that often rely on cycling, walking, and public transit get around the city.
Nadia Karimjee, Toronto
Developers have a role in reducing traffic congestion
While the debate continues on the effect of bike lanes on Toronto's arteries, one aspect hasn't received much attention, yet, it could be an easy fix: force developers to include offsets in their plans so no curb lanes will be closed for several years as these buildings are under construction. Make it a condition of the plan approval process that all construction activity will be off the street. The fees developers currently pay for blocking traffic lanes (which also affect cyclists) are passed on to the occupying tenants, inflating the already hefty price. Another benefit would be a more open, less cavernous aesthetic to our downtown.
Frank Tingle, Toronto
The unintended consequences of bike lanes
Due to the increased traffic tie-ups resulting from the extension of bike lanes on Bloor St West west of the Humber River, in addition to increased traffic jams, and the almost non-existent use by bikes even in good weather, car traffic has moved from Bloor Street onto parallel residential streets. We have no sidewalks, and our street has school age children and senior citizens who now have to contend with significantly increased traffic and increasing speeds due to driver frustration.
Robert Hooton, Toronto
Thanks for the Taylor Swift explainerAre Taylor Swift fans masochists? This author believes so - and says that's part of the fun, Oct. 25
I was grateful for the recent article that pronounced Taylor Swift a "musical genius." I wasn't entirely sure as to why she was so popular but now it's all become very clear. She's a musical genius and her legions of fans know it and are celebrating her remarkable achievements. Like Mozart and Beethoven and Tchaikovsky before her Swift takes her rightful place in the pantheon of musical greats. Now if I could only sell my house asap and get me a couple of her concert tickets.
Peter Lower, Toronto
Treaty apology and compensation settlement should be front page news
Minister apologizes for government's handling of First Nations' money in 1800s, Oct. 27
The Canadian government has apologized to a group of Ontario First Nations for broken Treaties over a century ago and the Toronto Star buried this news at the back of the front section of the newspaper. This apology was accompanied by a $447.9 million compensation settlement. Apologies and compensation settlements of this nature to Canada's Indigenous people are important news and need to be front and centre in our newspapers. Indeed, these broken Treaties are just a few of hundreds of the same that the Canadian government has yet to honour with this country's Indigenous people and when agreements like this are made it should be front page news.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
No chance Maple Leafs will make a blockbuster trade this seasonNine games in, what's really changed for the Maple Leafs this season? Oct. 28
If things go from bad to worse for the Toronto Maple Leafs, there's always a blockbuster trade as an option, right? Not likely. Why not, one might ask? Six players, including the core four, have a no movement clause in their contracts: Auston Matthews, Mitch Marner, William Nylander, Morgan Rielly, John Tavares and Chris Tanev. And six other players have clauses that list teams they cannot be traded to. For example, Max Domi's contract lists 13 teams he can't be traded to. That's just over 40 per cent of the teams in the National Hockey League. Bottom line: we will just have to settle for what we have on the team and hope it wins the Stanley Cup this year. Geeze, I have been hoping ever since the Leafs last won the Stanley Cup, in 1967.
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
God works in mysterious waysPope Francis' Catholic church reform process ends without giving more equity to women, Oct. 26
The Pope is God's representative on Earth and the Pope has decided that it's not time yet for women to be equal to men. A bit of a head scratcher, but then God works in mysterious ways.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
CAPTION: Two cyclists travel in the bike lane next to backed-up cars along east-bound Bloor Street West in Etobicoke.

A new hotel tax for Toronto + Justin Trudeau prepares attack ads

Document 387 of 500

Poilievre vows to cut housing programs: Tory leader says dropping federal tax on new homes sales under $1M would lead to more builds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-vows-cut-housing-programs/docview/3121512822/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+vows+to+cut+housing+programs%3A+Tory+leader+says+dropping+federal+tax+on+new+homes+sales+under+%241M+would+lead+to+more+builds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A future Conservative government would scrap two multibillion-dollar federal housing programs the party argues have failed, to help pay for a new tax cut that could cost $4 billion per year and is designed to boost home construction.
Staging a campaign-style announcement in the suburbs of Ottawa on Monday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said he would cut the five per cent federal sales tax on newly built homes that sell for less than $1 million, and that this would "spark" construction of an additional 30,000 homes per year.
"Common-sense Conservatives will stop giving the money to bureaucrats (and) instead leave it in the pockets of homebuilders and home buyers," Poilievre said.
Several housing experts welcomed the promised tax cut, and the Ford government at Queen's Park signalled a desire to match it. Meanwhile, the federal Liberal housing minister accused the Conservatives of wanting to slash programs that are "making it easier to build homes," and suggested it would be better to cater new policies to lower-income Canadians instead of delivering a broad-based tax cut that he suggested could benefit wealthy property owners.
"He's talking about cutting the programs that are actually going to deliver housing for middle-class and low-income families," Housing Minister Sean Fraser said.
Poilievre predicted his promised tax cut would cost about $16 billion in lost federal revenue over the next four years, but claimed slashing government "bureaucracy" and increased economic activity from new home construction would make up the shortfall.
"This includes about ... $16 billion in additional revenue and lower costs to the taxpayer, which is about the cost of axing the sales tax on homebuilding over the next four years," Poilievre said.
Poilievre said the Conservatives could find $3 billion in unspent money by cancelling the existing $4.4-billion Housing Accelerator Fund, which the Tory leader argued has failed to build homes despite the Liberals' claims that the program will help construct 750,000 units through deals with municipalities across the country.
Another $5 billion would come from scrapping the government's Housing Infrastructure Fund, Poilievre said. That program, announced this spring and slated for launch next year, is meant to finance construction and upgrades to the infrastructure, like sewer and water systems, required for new home construction.
On top of that, Poilievre claimed the tax cut would generate government revenues through increased economic activity around homebuilding worth about $2 billion per year.
Asked about Poilievre's claim the two Liberal housing programs are failures, Fraser shot back that the accelerator fund - through more than 170 deals with the federal government - has helped prompt municipalities to rezone areas for greater housing density and cut red tape by adopting digital permits.
Fraser's office confirmed Monday that $1 billion of the fund has been distributed, and the remaining more-than $3 billion has been committed through these deals.
"If you want to go back to municipalities and pull that money out, that would make no sense," he said.
At Queen's Park, sources close to Premier Doug Ford said the Progressive Conservative government would "love" to match Poilievre's tax cut and remove the eight per cent provincial portion of the harmonized sales tax from new homes under $1 million.
Insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss deliberations with the federal Liberal government, said the provincial Tories have broached that change with Ottawa in the past without success.
That's because the Trudeau government prefers to earmark money toward programs like the housing accelerator fund that result in building affordable rental units as opposed to homes for purchase, sources said.
Fraser said the Conservatives were perhaps inspired by the federal government's decision last year to slash the federal sales tax on new rental apartment construction, but said Poilievre's new tax cut could give benefits to the "investor class" of property owners.
While several housing industry players on Monday said they were awaiting more details of the Conservatives' proposal, such as projected uptake, the vow was broadly welcomed.
The federal government already has a sales tax rebate for the sale of new homes priced under $450,000, a policy that was introduced in 1991. That rebate returns some of the tax paid, but not all as the Conservatives are now promising.
But Richard Lyall, president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario, said the rebate has lost its effect over the years, in part because it was "never adjusted for inflation, as was supposed to have been the case."
Housing policy expert Steve Pomeroy, a senior research fellow at Carleton University's Centre for Urban Research and Education, said "adjusting the index is good tax policy," noting how housing costs have grown over the decades since the rebate was introduced.
In a city like Toronto, or even in the surrounding 905 region, buyers looking for detached houses are still "realistically never going to qualify," said Building Industry and Land Development Association (BILD) executive Justin Sherwood.
But he sees Poilievre's tax cut as offering some relief to buyers of new condos in Toronto, as well as some stacked townhomes and other mid-sized offerings within the broader 905. BILD has been among development groups calling for such a change in recent years, added association president David Wilkes.
"Our position has been that it needed to be increased to a million dollars," he said. "In the GTA, nothing is being sold that's $350,000 or lower. Right now, no one qualifies for the current rebate."
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall, Victoria Gibson and Robert Benzie Toronto Star

Full text: Bike lanes are critical to safe commutes
Ford blasts bike lanes, lauds rebate, Oct. 23
Bike lanes unquestionably contribute to traffic in Toronto, but the recent significant increase in bike users makes bike lanes critical to safe city commutes. Robert Benzie writes that Premier Doug Ford is opposed to bike lanes on busy streets and urges them to be put on secondary roads.
Reducing bike lanes on the well-known busy streets such as Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue seems like an ineffective solution to the advancing traffic problem of downtown Toronto. More and more people are relying on bikes for transportation, and reducing the existing bike lanes would create a more inconvenient situation for all commuters.
As a university student in the GTA, I have witnessed a rise in people using bikes on busy streets and around university as they become more accessible through city bike share programs. Not only are bike lanes essential for the many students who do not own cars, but also for daily commuters that live and/or work in the busy downtown area of Toronto.
I am concerned that eliminating existing bike lanes would create an even more dangerous situation for bikers than what already exists today.
Fidelia Suliman, Mississauga
Whose time does Bill 212 promise to save?
Premier Doug Ford's Bill 212, Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, will take the city further away from a safe city for all, in particular, sacrificing youth mobility. As a 19-year-old undergrad student, I understand the importance of efficient and safe transportation that is accessible to youth. Whose time does the act promise to save? Not ours. Students and other people who do not use cars are equally deserving of safe and efficient transportation options. Cycling using city infrastructure like protected bike lanes is fast, affordable and a healthy way to get around the city. Fewer bike lanes will make it slower for young people to get around and also more dangerous.
In 2023, the Toronto Police Service reported 33 cyclists involved in collisions, five of whom were between 15 and 24 years old. Not only do bike lanes help the safety of cyclists, but they also reduce the number of people driving, which would help with Toronto's congestion problem. Building a safe city for all must include the youth population that often rely on cycling, walking, and public transit get around the city.
Nadia Karimjee, Toronto
Developers have a role in reducing traffic congestion
While the debate continues on the effect of bike lanes on Toronto's arteries, one aspect hasn't received much attention, yet, it could be an easy fix: force developers to include offsets in their plans so no curb lanes will be closed for several years as these buildings are under construction. Make it a condition of the plan approval process that all construction activity will be off the street. The fees developers currently pay for blocking traffic lanes (which also affect cyclists) are passed on to the occupying tenants, inflating the already hefty price. Another benefit would be a more open, less cavernous esthetic to our downtown.
Frank Tingle, Toronto
The unintended consequences
of bike lanes
Due to the increased traffic tie-ups resulting from the extension of bike lanes on Bloor Street West, west of the Humber River, in addition to increased traffic jams, and the almost non-existent use by bikes even in good weather, car traffic has moved from Bloor Street onto parallel residential streets. We have no sidewalks and our street has school-age children and senior citizens who now have to contend with significantly increased traffic and increasing speeds due to driver frustration.
Robert Hooton, Toronto
Thanks for the Taylor Swift explainer
Are Taylor Swift fans masochists? This author believes so - and says that's part of the fun, Oct. 25
I was grateful for the recent article that pronounced Taylor Swift a "musical genius." I wasn't entirely sure as to why she was so popular, but now it's all become very clear. She's a musical genius and her legions of fans know it and are celebrating her remarkable achievements. Like Mozart and Beethoven and Tchaikovsky before her, Swift takes her rightful place in the pantheon of musical greats. Now if I could only sell my house ASAP and get me a couple of her concert tickets.
Peter Lower, Toronto
Compensation settlement is front-page news
Minister apologizes for government's handling of First Nations' money in 1800s, Oct. 27
The Canadian government has apologized to a group of Ontario First Nations for broken treaties more than a century ago and the Star buried this news at the back of the front section of the newspaper. This apology was accompanied by a $447.9-million compensation settlement. Apologies and compensation settlements of this nature to Canada's Indigenous people are important news and need to be front and centre in our newspapers. Indeed, these broken treaties are just a few of hundreds of the same that the Canadian government has yet to honour with this country's Indigenous people and when agreements like this are made it should be front-page news.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
No chance Leafs will make a blockbuster trade
Nine games in, what's really changed for the Maple Leafs this season? Oct. 28
If things go from bad to worse for the Toronto Maple Leafs, there's always a blockbuster trade as an option, right? Not likely. Why not, one might ask? Six players, including the core four, have a no movement clause in their contracts: Auston Matthews, Mitch Marner, William Nylander, Morgan Rielly, John Tavares and Chris Tanev. And six other players have clauses that list teams they cannot be traded to. For example, Max Domi's contract lists 13 teams he can't be traded to. That's just over 40 per cent of the teams in the National Hockey League. Bottom line: we will just have to settle for what we have on the team and hope it wins the Stanley Cup this year. Geez, I have been hoping ever since the Leafs last won the Stanley Cup in 1967.
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough

Document 389 of 500

Behind Chow's frustrations with Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/behind-chows-frustrations-with-ford/docview/3121512692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+Chow%27s+frustrations+with+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Olivia Chow is all about empathy with an adversary.
Except when she's talking tough with Premier Doug Ford.
The mayor initially tried building bridges after winning power. Now she's watching Ford burning bridges by rolling back municipal bike lanes.
Why is the premier so feisty lately? And the mayor so frustrated?
Blame democracy, for better or for worse.
According to Chow, the fault lines lie with that tried and true democratic tactic known as electioneering.
"He has an election that he's facing," Chow told the Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Which means the premier is exploiting wedge issues that pit cyclists against car commuters. While squeezing the mayor on her own municipal turf.
That's the paradox of power in our democratic system: You run against your opponents, then you try to work together with your counterparts, but it's back to the barricades at election time.
"Having said that, I need him to be onside" - not just on bicycles but transit, drug treatment, mental health and housing, Chow mused. "Those are big things ... It's based on hope, but I know we can do it."
Chow's argument is that she has no choice but to find a way. The premier has most of the power.
Finding a middle way worked a year ago, when she found common ground with Ford shortly after becoming mayor. She pointed out that they'd both experienced loss - his late brother Rob, the former mayor, and her late husband Jack Layton, who worked with him on city council.
Back then, they built an "empathetic bridge," as Chow puts it. That term is borrowed from one of her political mentors, Marshall Ganz, who teaches leadership at Harvard University's Kennedy School of Government and joined her onstage at Toronto Metropolitan University last week.
"The empathetic bridge actually resulted in a lot of agreement on how we could work together," she told the audience. "One thing about Marshall's teaching is that if we connect through experience and not always fight each other on opinions ... find the core value, and then you can move ahead together."
Chow and Ford bridged the gap at first - reaching an unprecedented agreement to shore up the city's finances. Ultimately, though, it was a bridge too far for bicycle lanes, because the premier now sees them as a path to victory on the campaign trail ahead.
Ganz, whose organizing techniques have inspired generations of political activists, said he's proud of Chow, who studied his methods after she lost her first mayoral campaign in 2014. How's she doing, a decade later?
"I've got a pretty simple answer - she won," he quipped.
More seriously, power is the prerequisite to action. It's a necessary, but not sufficient condition - for if you don't lay the groundwork for substantive change, power changes little.
"Winning the thing is part of it. But coming out of it stronger is even more important. And that's what brings it back to organizing, and brings it back to developing leadership."
Ganz, who worked on Barack Obama's campaigns, said the former U.S. president didn't do enough in power to create lasting change: "We had a chance to do that in the Obama campaign ... Unfortunately, he didn't take advantage of it."
He argues for an alternative approach, both in his teachings at Harvard and in his new book, "People, Power, Change: Organizing for Democratic Renewal." It stresses grassroots organizing and people power over the power of money politics, cautioning against the "philanthropic colonization of civil society."
In a democracy, there's a big difference between winning power and wielding power, between grassroots organizing and governing, between transformational politics and transactional governing. Which is why the mayor said she invited Ganz to talk about his teachings and readings in a presentation to senior city hall staff last week.
Chow recounted how he challenged the staff: "Why are you a public servant? Why do you serve? Why are you working as a planner, a transportation manager, a communicator?"
Good questions. One question the mayor herself struggled with at our Democracy Forum, however - and for which she remains answerable and accountable - is the housing crisis in Toronto.
More than a year after she won power, the city has just conceded it doesn't have nearly enough emergency shelter space to get people off the streets. While Chow has publicly acknowledged that public parks are no place for the unhoused, she rattled off a list of policy possibilities for the future.
But her long answer on long-term solutions - which gave short shrift to the short-term crisis - prompted grumbling from an otherwise supportive audience hungry for action from the activist in chief. That, too, is democracy.

Speaking of organizing, I'll be hosting three of Canada's top labour leaders - Unifor's Lana Payne, the CLC's Bea Bruske and OPSEU's JP Hornick - at the TMU Democracy Forum on Wednesday, talking about "labour's new leadership - unions and the future of work." Free for all.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Subject: Leadership; Local elections; Grass roots movement; Mayors; Democracy; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Company / organization: Name: Toronto Metropolitan University; NAICS: 611310

Title: Behind Chow's frustrations with Ford

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Document 390 of 500

Why a pay bump is unlikely for council

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-pay-bump-is-unlikely-council/docview/3121512659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+a+pay+bump+is+unlikely+for+council&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You cannot trust politicians with approving the size of their own paycheques.
This seems kind of obvious to everyone. But, in my experience, it isn't true for the reasons most people think. We think they'll line their own pockets by overpaying themselves.
In reality, they usually underpay themselves.
On Monday, the Star's city hall bureau chief Ben Spurr wrote about a staff report he obtained claiming Toronto city councillors were drastically underpaid, which was quietly buried by Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson in August 2023.
According to that report, city councillors in Toronto represented the most constituents of any in Canada, yet were paid less than representatives in many comparable cities, especially those close by. They made at least $24,000 per year less than councillors in Markham, Vaughan, Mississauga and Brampton. Toronto councillors were paid on average $1.06 per constituent they represent, while those in Markham were paid $5.27 per constituent.
The last time city councillors gave themselves a raise other than automatic inflationary adjustments was 2006. In 2021, they voted against giving themselves a raise despite a report from city staff recommending one.
Up the road at Queen's Park, MPPs haven't seen a raise since 2008, as the Star's Robert Benzie reported this year. Without even inflationary increases, they earn roughly $12,000 per year less than Toronto city councillors (and $78,000 per year less than federal MPs). And yet, despite some unnamed insider grumbling and a report urging a raise from the provincial integrity commissioner, Premier Doug Ford has no plans to increase salaries.
If you understand politics at all, it isn't hard to understand why this is the case. Chow was preparing to put forward a big tax increase and suggest spending increases on a bunch of things, and didn't need the political noise of people suggesting she and her colleagues were in it to make themselves rich. Ford has always been a "stop the gravy train" advocate of low taxes, who would be naturally averse to anything that looks like a benefit for government leaders (his brother, famously, paid his office expenses out of his own pocket at city hall).
Politically, it is never a good time for elected representatives of any political stripe to be seen giving themselves payments or perks. Even in Ottawa, where federal politicians are paid comparatively better, past scandals over expense-account orange juice and hotel rooms have created an atmosphere where successive prime ministers have left the official residence to rot in place rather than approve any spending to maintain it in a state suitable for human habitation.
And so, in the absence of any independent outside authority setting elected officials' pay, they don't raise it.
Many people will think that's fine. Good, even. Saving taxpayers money, and all that. Especially since they aren't exactly paid poverty wages: at $128,347 per year for Toronto city councillors and $116,550 for MPPs, both jobs pay better than the average family income in Canada.
They are important jobs. And in the world of professionals and executives, these aren't attractive salaries. It's reasonable to think that they severely limit the pool of candidates to those who are either wealthy enough as to not care about the salary (say, being born into wealth like the Ford brothers) or those who could not get an executive-level job anywhere else. There's no job security for MPPs and city councillors, and no pension. Applicants often have to take months off unpaid from other jobs to obtain them.
It is entirely reasonable to expect people won't seek public office to enrich themselves. It is less reasonable to expect that qualified management- and professional-level people who might be good candidates need to sacrifice the possibility of, say, qualifying for a Toronto mortgage, in order to do the job.
Elected officials shouldn't finance a lavish lifestyle on the public dime. But they ought to earn a competitive upper-middle-class salary that reflects the importance and difficulty of the job they do. No private sector company would expect to attract great candidates if they insisted on paying executives significantly less than they'd earn in comparable work elsewhere - even if they justified the decision by claiming to want employees who felt a sense of mission.
That logic is unlikely to change anything. No active elected official publicly quoted in the Star's recent stories advocated for a raise. After all, even if they'd secretly appreciate the money, they're already doing the job for the pay offered now. It's those who declined to run based on economics whose voices are missing. Maybe some of those are ones we'd benefit from hearing.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson shelved a staff report last year that recommended a raise for councillors. Politically, it is never a good time for elected representatives to be seen giving themselves payments or perks, Edward Keenan writes.Steve RussellToronto Star

CREDIT: edward Keenan Toronto Star Opinion

Document 391 of 500

Probe urged into lack of medical supplies: PCs put agency in charge of deliveries last year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/probe-urged-into-lack-medical-supplies/docview/3121512509/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Probe+urged+into+lack+of+medical+supplies%3A+PCs+put+agency+in+charge+of+deliveries+last+year&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberals are calling on Ontario's auditor general to investigate a critical shortage in medical supplies that has left vulnerable home-care patients - some in their final days - without pain medications and other key items.
"I'm still hearing the same horror stories that we've all been hearing for the last couple weeks," MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, said Monday.
Those problems include shortages of drugs, needles and other equipment that in some cases has prompted doctors to send palliative care patients to hospital emergency rooms in their final days. As well, cancer patients have been left without IV bags and needles. Others do not have bandages for wound care after surgeries.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones has said the troubles are "unacceptable" and that ministry staff are working with suppliers of the new Ontario Health atHome agency to fix them.
Her parliamentary assistant Anthony Leardi (Essex) said Monday that there are "special assistance teams" trying to get supplies to patients and reiterated anyone purchasing supplies out of their own pockets can be reimbursed.
But complaints continue to roll in to MPPs' offices, New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said in backing the Liberal push for an investigation.
"They are still not seeing supplies. Something's still not working," she told the legislature's daily question period.
Shamji's letter to auditor general Shelley Spence requests a value-for-money audit into Ontario Health atHome's ability to procure and deliver supplies.
The agency took over the functions last year under legislation passed by Premier Doug Ford's government to change the system, making a handful of suppliers and providers responsible for delivering across the province. Previously, local pharmacies and other suppliers focused on servicing smaller geographic areas.
Shamji said he wants to know how the new suppliers were chosen and whether any political connections with the governing Progressive Conservatives were a factor.
"Ontarians need to understand why this catastrophe has occurred, how it can be stopped, who will be held accountable and the manner in which it will be prevented in the future," he wrote in a detailed three-page letter.
"As I write, countless Ontarians do not know if their scheduled home-care appointment will be honoured and, if so, whether the provider will have the necessary medications and supplies."
Spence will deliver her annual report by early December, so it is too late to include any probe into Ontario Health atHome in it. But that does not preclude her auditing teams from investigating if the office determines that's warranted.
"When selecting and planning our audits, we carefully consider all information available to us, including information that has been sent to us by members of provincial parliament and the public," auditor general spokesperson Becky Fond said after Shamji's request was received.
Liberal MPP John Fraser (Ottawa South), who recalled how he and his sisters cared for their elderly parents at home in their final days, said he could not imagine the suffering and frustration at doing it without proper supplies.
"It should make all of us angry," he said, challenging Ford to support the call for an investigation "if he is truly a man of his word."
On Friday, Ford told reporters "they had to pull me off the ceiling" when he first heard reports of the home-care supply problem.
"I actually get calls on my phone saying, 'I need gauze, I need Band Aids.' But people are going to be held accountable, as sure as I'm talking to you."
CAPTION: Shortages of drugs, needles and other equipment has in some cases prompted doctors to send palliative care patients to hospital emergency rooms in their final days. Liberal MPP Adil Shamji's letter to the auditor general seeks a value-for- money audit into Ontario Health atHome's ability to procure and deliver supplies.Frank GunnTHE CANADIANPRESS file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 392 of 500

'I need him to be onside:' Why Oliva Chow has been so frustrated with Doug Ford these days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-need-him-be-onside-why-oliva-chow-has-been-so/docview/3121721403/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+need+him+to+be+onside%3A%27+Why+Oliva+Chow+has+been+so+frustrated+with+Doug+Ford+these+days&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Olivia Chow is all about empathy with an adversary.
Except when she's talking tough with Premier Doug Ford.
The mayor initially tried building bridges after winning power. Now she's watching Ford burning bridges by rolling back municipal bike lanes.
Why is the premier so feisty lately? And the mayor so frustrated?
Blame democracy, for better or for worse.
According to Chow, the fault lines lie with that tried and true democratic tactic known as electioneering.
"He has an election that he's facing," Chow told the Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Which means the premier is exploiting wedge issues that pit cyclists against car commuters. While squeezing the mayor on her own municipal turf.
That's the paradox of power in our democratic system: You run against your opponents, then you try to work together with your counterparts, but it's back to the barricades at election time.
"Having said that, I need him to be onside" - not just on bicycles but transit, drug treatment, mental health and housing, Chow mused. "Those are big things ... It's based on hope, but I know we can do it."
Chow's argument is that she has no choice but to find a way. The premier has most of the power.
Finding a middle way worked a year ago, when she found common ground with Ford shortly after becoming mayor. She pointed out that they'd both experienced loss - his late brother Rob, the former mayor, and her late husband Jack Layton, who worked with him on city council.
Back then, they built an "empathetic bridge," as Chow puts it. That term is borrowed from one of her political mentors, Marshall Ganz, who teaches leadership at Harvard University's Kennedy School of Government and joined her onstage at TMU last week.
"The empathetic bridge actually resulted in a lot of agreement on how we could work together," she told the audience. "One thing about Marshall's teaching is that if we connect through experience and not always fight each other on opinions ... find the core value, and then you can move ahead together."
Chow and Ford bridged the gap at first - reaching an unprecedented agreement to shore up the city's finances. Ultimately, though, it was a bridge too far for bicycle lanes, because the premier now sees them as a path to victory on the campaign trail ahead.
Ganz, whose organizing techniques have inspired generations of political activists, said he's proud of Chow, who studied his methods after she lost her first mayoral campaign in 2014. How's she doing, a decade later?
"I've got a pretty simple answer - she won," he quipped.
More seriously, power is the prerequisite to action. It's a necessary, but not sufficient condition - for if you don't lay the groundwork for substantive change, power changes little.
"Winning the thing is part of it. But coming out of it stronger is even more important. And that's what brings it back to organizing, and brings it back to developing leadership."
Ganz, who worked on Barack Obama's campaigns, said the former U.S. president didn't do enough in power to create lasting change: "We had a chance to do that in the Obama campaign ... Unfortunately, he didn't take advantage of it."
He argues for an alternative approach, both in his teachings at Harvard and in his new book, "People, Power, Change: Organizing for Democratic Renewal." It stresses grassroots organizing and people power over the power of money politics, cautioning against the "philanthropic colonization of civil society."
In a democracy, there's a big difference between winning power and wielding power, between grassroots organizing and governing, between transformational politics and transactional governing. Which is why the mayor said she invited Ganz to talk about his teachings and readings in a presentation to senior city hall staff last week.
Chow recounted how he challenged the staff: "Why are you a public servant? Why do you serve? Why are you working as a planner, a transportation manager, a communicator?"
Good questions. One question the mayor herself struggled with at our Democracy Forum, however - and for which she remains answerable and accountable - is the housing crisis in Toronto.
More than a year after she won power, the city has just conceded it doesn't have nearly enough emergency shelter space to get people off the streets. While Chow has publicly acknowledged that public parks are no place for the unhoused, she rattled off a list of policy possibilities for the future.
But her long answer on long-term solutions - which gave short shrift to the short-term crisis - prompted grumbling from an otherwise supportive audience hungry for action from the activist in chief. That, too, is democracy.
* * * *
Speaking of organizing, I'll be hosting three of Canada's top labour leaders - Unifor's Lana Payne, the CLC's Bea Bruske and OPSEU's JP Hornick - at the TMU Democracy Forum on Oct. 30, talking about "labour's new leadership - unions and the future of work." Free for all.
CAPTION: People, Power and Change, with Marshall Ganz and Mayor Olivia ChowStar columnist Martin Regg Cohn, left, speaks with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, centre, and Marshall Ganz, who teaches leadership and organizing at the Harvard Kennedy School, and the Toronto Metropolitan University Democracy Forum on Oct. 25, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Local elections; Grass roots movement; Mayors; Democracy; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Company / organization: Name: Toronto Metropolitan University; NAICS: 611310

Title: 'I need him to be onside:' Why Oliva Chow has been so frustrated with Doug Ford these days

Document 393 of 500

Critical shortage of medical supplies has Ontario Liberals demanding auditor general probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/critical-shortage-medical-supplies-has-ontario/docview/3121721235/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Critical+shortage+of+medical+supplies+has+Ontario+Liberals+demanding+auditor+general+probe&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 394 of 500

Pierre Poilievre would replace Liberal housing programs with tax cut on new homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-would-replace-liberal-housing/docview/3121512533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+would+replace+Liberal+housing+programs+with+tax+cut+on+new+homes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A future Conservative government would scrap two multi-billion federal housing programs the party argues have failed, to help pay for a new tax cut that could cost $4 billion per year and is designed to boost home construction.
Staging a campaign-style announcement in the suburbs of Ottawa on Monday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said he would cut the five-per-cent federal sales tax on newly-built homes that sell for less than $1 million, and that this would "spark" construction of an additional 30,000 homes per year.
"Common-sense Conservatives will stop giving the money to bureaucrats (and) instead leave it in the pockets of homebuilders and home buyers," Poilievre said.
Several housing experts welcomed the promised tax cut, and the Ford government at Queen's Park signalled a desire to match it. Meanwhile, the federal Liberal housing minister accused the Conservatives of wanting to slash programs that are "making it easier to build homes," and suggested it would be better to cater new policies to lower-income Canadians instead of delivering a broad-based tax cut that he suggested could benefit wealthy property owners.
"He's talking about cutting the programs that are actually going to deliver housing for middle class and low-income families," Housing Minister Sean Fraser said.
Poilievre predicted his promised tax cut would cost about $16 billion in lost federal revenue over the next four years, but claimed slashing government "bureaucracy" and increased economic activity from new home construction would make up the shortfall.
"This includes about ... $16 billion in additional revenue and lower costs to the taxpayer, which is about the cost of axing the sales tax on homebuilding over the next four years," Poilievre said.
Poilievre said the Conservatives could find $3 billion in unspent money by cancelling the existing $4.4-billion Housing Accelerator Fund, which the Tory leader argued has failed to build homes despite the Liberals' claims that the program will help construct 750,000 units through deals with municipalities across the country.
Another $5 billion would come from scrapping the government's Housing Infrastructure Fund, Poilievre said. That program, announced this spring and slated for launch next year, is meant to finance construction and upgrades to the infrastructure, like sewer and water systems, required for new home construction.
On top of that, Poilievre claimed the tax cut would generate government revenues through increased economic activity around homebuilding worth about $2 billion per year.
Asked about Poilievre's claim the two Liberal housing programs are failures, Fraser shot back that the accelerator fund - through more than 170 deals with the federal government - has helped prompt municipalities to rezone areas for greater housing density and cut red tape by adopting digital permits. Fraser's office confirmed Monday that $1 billion of the fund has been distributed, and the remaining more-than $3 billion has been committed through these deals.
"If you want to go back to municipalities and pull that money out, that would make no sense," Fraser said.
At Queen's Park, sources close to Premier Doug Ford said the Progressive Conservative government would "love" to match Poilievre's tax cut and remove the eight per cent provincial portion of the harmonized sales tax from new homes under $1 million.
Insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss deliberations with the federal Liberal government, said the provincial Tories have broached that change with Ottawa in the past without success.
That's because the Trudeau government prefers to earmark money toward programs like the housing accelerator fund that result in building affordable rental units as opposed to homes for purchase, sources said.
Fraser said Monday that the Conservatives were perhaps inspired by the federal government's decision last year to slash the federal sales tax on new rental apartment construction, but said Poilievre's new tax cut could give benefits to the "investor class" of property owners.
While several housing industry players on Monday said they were awaiting more details of the Conservatives' proposal, such as projected uptake, the vow was broadly welcomed.
The federal government already has a sales tax rebate for the sale of new homes priced under $450,000, a policy that was introduced in 1991. That rebate returns some of the tax paid, but not all as the Conservatives are now promising.
But Richard Lyall, president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario, said the rebate has lost its effect over the years, in part because it was "never adjusted for inflation, as was supposed to have been the case."
Housing policy expert Steve Pomeroy, a senior research fellow at Carleton University's Centre for Urban Research and Education, said "adjusting the index is good tax policy," noting how housing costs have grown over the decades since the rebate was introduced.
In a city like Toronto, or even in the surrounding 905 region, buyers looking for detached houses are still "realistically never going to qualify," said Building Industry and Land Development Association (BILD) executive Justin Sherwood.
But he sees Poilievre's tax cut as offering some relief to buyers of new condos in Toronto, as well as some stacked townhomes and other mid-sized offerings within the broader 905. BILD has been among development groups calling for such a change in recent years, added association president David Wilkes.
"Our position has been that it needed to be increased to a million dollars," he said. "In the GTA, nothing is being sold that's $350,000 or lower. Right now, no one qualifies for the current rebate."
David Amborski, director of Toronto Metropolitan University's Centre for Urban Research and Land Development and a member of the Ontario government's recent housing affordability task force, was pleased to see the federal Conservatives turning an eye to the taxes and fees applied to new housing, which he sees as important to tackle unaffordability. He, too, sees the policy's reach as somewhat limited, but welcomed the promise.
"I'm not sure it's going to make a monumental difference - but I'd expect it to have some impact for people on the margins," he said.
Such a policy would also likely stimulate construction at a time when development has slowed, added University of Toronto Chair in Housing David Hulchanski - though he is skeptical of increased subsidies for home purchases, arguing that governments should more squarely focus on those with the most pressing housing need, including developing policies aimed at renters and lower-income households.
"There is nothing similar in terms of the value of home ownership tax system subsidies, the already more than $10 billion annually, available for Canadian households who are renters and may never be able to purchase a house," Hulchanski said Monday.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre leaves a press conference in Ottawa on Oct. 28, 2024.

Subject: Construction; Conservatism; Sales taxes; Tax cuts; Land development; Subsidies; Economic activity
