Document 423 of 500

Ford government to ban foreign students from Ontario medical schools

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-ban-foreign-students-ontario/docview/3120807152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+ban+foreign+students+from+Ontario+medical+schools&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is planning to bar new foreign students from medical schools and limit students from elsewhere in Canada while offering free tuition to 1,360 Ontarians if they agree to work as doctors in the province for five years.
In a move to tackle Ontario's growing doctor shortage, Ford is expanding "learn and stay" grants to family medicine students and directing the province's medical schools to "prioritize" 95 per cent of spots for Ontario residents starting in the fall of 2026.
"That is a game changer," Ford said Friday in announcing what Health Minister Sylvia Jones called "bold action."
The other five per cent would be students from the rest of Canada. In the unlikely event spots go unfilled, international students could be accepted.
But the impact will be felt harder by prospective out-of-province students than international ones. Government figures show just 10 or 0.26 per cent of 3,833 Ontario medical students this year are from other countries.
However, 473 students or 12 per cent are from elsewhere in Canada while the vast majority, 3,350 students or more than 87 per cent, are from Ontario.
"Let's be clear," said Liberal MPP and health critic Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, noting the tiny percentage of foreign medical students.
"Protecting more medical school spots for students in Ontario is a fair move, as other provinces do it all the time. But this won't actually add any family doctors to the workforce for six years. Where was he with this policy two, three or four years ago?"
The grants will cost $88 million over the next three years.
Ford's changes come with an early election expected next spring and the government vulnerable on the health-care front, with the Ontario Medical Association saying 2.5 million residents don't have access to a family doctor - a number expected to rise as aging physicians retire.
The premier said he gets frustrated hearing some Ontario students had to go elsewhere for medical school for lack of spaces here, with many never returning, and appeared to blame foreign students despite their low numbers.
"This has been gnawing at me for years," he said, standing with Jones in front of Queen's University students in the family medicine program on a satellite campus at Lakeridge Health in Oshawa.
Ford, whose government has already expanded medical school spots and is opening new medical schools at Toronto Metropolitan University and York University, noted he hears from Ontario parents whose sons and daughters are at medical schools abroad.
"You'd go out to events and say ... how are the kids? Well, they're studying in Ireland, or they're studying in the Caribbean, they're studying in the U.S.," he told reporters.
"And what happens next is, they meet someone and they don't come back home."
Ford suggested 18 per cent of students in Ontario medical schools are from other countries and less likely to set up shop here, but that percentage is actually for medical school graduates who are in residency training.
"Ontario students need to come first," said Jones, who has pledged that every Ontarian will have access to a family doctor within five years.
The government's problem is that family medicine is no longer a popular option, said Margaret Walton-Roberts, a professor at the Balsillie School of International Affairs at Wilfrid Laurier University in Waterloo.
"So it is not about international students. It is about making family medicine more appealing as a career," added Walton-Roberts, an expert in international migration of health-care professionals.
"Currently it is not, and most students opt for specialty training."
As Shamji pointed out, it will take years for the new measures - which include paying for the books and equipment of future family doctors - to have an impact because of the long time frame for training physicians.
Depending on the university, medical school is three or four years, followed by a two-year family medicine residency, said Dr. Dominik Nowak, a family physician who is president of the Ontario Medical Association.
He applauded the new grants as "an important, ambitious step" but urged the government to consider immediate measures, such as medical school debt forgiveness for new Ontario-trained doctors heading elsewhere if they agree to stay.
Nowak said many medical students graduate with "crushing debt" in the $100,000 to $200,000 range.
He declined to comment on the foreign medical student prohibition, saying, "It's up to the government to answer questions on that."
The Ontario Medical Students Association did not reply to a request for comment on Friday, while the Council of Ontario Faculties of Medicine said "we look forward to working with the provincial government to further understand the details with the shared goals to deliver high-quality medical education and to increase access to family physicians."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles called for more government funding for residency positions to get more doctors into service.
The "learn and stay" program was established three years ago to provide financial assistance for nursing, paramedic and medical lab technology students to work in "underserviced" areas of the province.
The government estimates medical students accepted for the "learn and stay" program will eventually be able to serve about 1.36 million patients once they become physicians.
Medical students will be able to apply for the grant program for the 2026-27 academic year.
By then, the government hopes to have up to 100 doctors who attended medical school outside Canada fully trained and cleared to serve in rural areas under the province's "Practice Ready Ontario" program.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, right, and Health Minister Sylvia Jones listen to questions from the opposition during question period at Queen's Park in Toronto on Dec. 6, 2023.

Document 424 of 500

These Ontarians are watching how MAID is being accessed and used. Here's what they found

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/these-ontarians-are-watching-how-maid-is-being/docview/3120560150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=These+Ontarians+are+watching+how+MAID+is+being+accessed+and+used.+Here%27s+what+they+found&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The cases outlined in two recently released reports on Ontario's use of medical assistance in dying are stark.In one example, a woman in her 50s with a history of depression and suicidality and a severe sensitivity to chemicals ended her life through medical assistance in dying (MAID) after failing to secure a housing arrangement that met her medical needs. That was one of six cases described in the reports, which raised new questions about how MAID is granted in the province for people who are not nearing their natural deaths.Ontario's chief coroner released the reports last week on behalf of an oversight group that reviews how MAID is accessed in the province, focusing on complex cases that saw Ontarians choose the procedure to end their suffering, in some situations, partly because of social needs that were not being met. But what did experts conclude, and what's the scope of the problem?Here's what you need to know.Why does Ontario have a MAID oversight group?In 2016, the federal government passed legislation permitting those with serious and incurable medical conditions to access medical assistance in dying if their death was "reasonably foreseeable." Quebec's Superior Court later ruled that such criteria was too restrictive and unconstitutional, prompting the Liberals in 2021 to expand access to the procedure to those whose deaths were not naturally imminent. That law created two tracks, with one set of rules for those whose natural deaths were reasonably foreseeable and another set of safeguards for those whose deaths were not. It also banned MAID for those seeking to end their lives solely due to mental illness for two years - a period that has now been extended until March 2027. That second track - for those enduring intolerable suffering but not approaching their natural death - has been met with intense scrutiny, sparking fears that people with disabilities and marginalized people who lack support and resources would be disproportionately driven to obtain the procedure. In 2023, 116 patients out of 4,644 people who received MAID in Ontario followed that track. Because it's largely up to the provinces and territories to carry out and oversee MAID, Ontario's Chief Coroner, Dr. Dirk Huyer, this year struck the MAID Death Review Committee (MDRC), a 16-member group made up of legal, medical, social work, nursing, mental health and disability experts. The purpose of the committee is to assess how MAID is being accessed and used in Ontario, and provide recommendations and advice to the different levels of government, agencies, and professionals involved in the practice. What kinds of cases were discussed in the reports?Huyer's office released the committee's first reports last week. They focused on cases in which MAID was provided to marginalized people and those with complex medical conditions, all in situations where their natural deaths were not imminent. The coroner's office chose those themes to promote discussion on providing MAID in less common situations, with the reports noting that the circumstances described "often represent divergence from typical practice."In one case, a man in his 40s with inflammatory bowel disease, few social supports and a history of mental illness was first informed about MAID from a psychiatrist, and went through with the procedure without his family being consulted or being offered treatment for his addictions. Committee members noted that the man might not have received "sufficient care through mental health and social services."In another, a man in his 80s with longtime chronic back pain who had recently been diagnosed with an adjustment disorder - in which someone has excessive reactions to stress in ways that may alter their thoughts and actions - chose MAID to end his life. Some committee members argued that psychiatric assessments properly determined that the disorder was tied to his untreatable pain. Others expressed concern that the man's adjustment disorder could have been a temporary state. In the case of the woman who could not find appropriate housing to manage her chemical sensitivity syndrome, committee members did not reach consensus on her eligibility for MAID, because they could not agree on whether her suffering was primarily caused by a medical condition, a social issue, or her mental state. What conclusions were drawn?Because the MDRC is made up of people with differing opinions on MAID, members did not arrive at unanimous conclusions in interpreting each case. Dr. Ramona Coelho, a committee member and family physician who works with marginalized patients in London, Ont., told the Star the themes laid out in the reports were "quite worrisome."Coelho said that even though the group only looked at a small number of cases, "these are people's lives," and it's imperative that those on the margins of society be offered better mental health, disability, and community supports to avoid assisted death being viewed as the only solution. Donna Stewart, another committee member with expertise in psychiatry at the University of Toronto, said she's "very concerned" that atypical cases might spark fear-mongering about the practice, telling the Star that a balance must be struck between exhausting options to ensure social needs are met and still making MAID accessible in situations where that is not always possible. She said her takeaway from the themes identified in the reports is that the "psychiatric aspects" of some conditions are not always addressed, and "that needs to change." To that end, the coroner's office issued several recommendations to the federal government, including calling for more guidance on following or reconsidering MAID protocols when mental health conditions are a significant factor in someone's suffering. Huyer also called on Health Canada to provide more advice on disability care and approaching requests when suffering is predominantly "derived from an unmet social need" like housing. Ottawa was therefore also asked to consider broad reforms, like ushering in "actionable changes" to the country's social safety net.The office of federal Health Minister Mark Holland told the Star that new regulations introduced last year now allow Health Canada to collect more data on MAID requests, including "information on living arrangements, disability, race, and Indigenous identity."The statement also said the department is exploring the relationship between socioeconomic status and MAID, and cautioned that a "lack of access to social supports or to health services" does not make someone eligible for the procedure.
CAPTION: Dr. Dirk Huyer, chief coroner for Ontario,released the reports on behalf of an oversight group that reviews how MAID is accessed in Ontario, focusing on complex cases.

Ottawa should overrule Ford and disallow Bill 212 and the tearing up of bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-should-overrule-ford-disallow-bill-212/docview/3120560075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+should+overrule+Ford+and+disallow+Bill+212+and+the+tearing+up+of+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones, Oct. 21
My family regularly uses the bike lanes that Premier Doug Ford has just promised to tear up. We use them to go to work, to shop, to visit friends, and for every part of our lives. They may not be perfect, but the bike lanes we have make our travel safer, and we appreciate them. There are many aspects of this change we're angry about. For starters, there's the waste of tens of millions of dollars and months of construction disruption, and now the promise of repeating all those same costs again, just to move backwards.
This is and has always been the responsibility of the mayor and city council. Doug Ford had his shot at being mayor, but he lost. Similarly, in both elections he's run in as leader of the PC party, his party has failed to win even a single seat in the old city of Toronto. He doesn't represent us, and he never has. Because of outdated constitutional rules, he has the power to overrule the people who are responsible for making these decisions, and there isn't anything we can do about it, since his party doesn't have any seats to lose in the affected areas.
However, just as he can overreach and violate norms of provincial conduct and respect for municipal autonomy, the federal government can and should overrule him by acting to disallow Bill 212. It's true that it's rare for the federal government to intervene in provincial legislation, but it's also rare for the province to intervene in Toronto politics the way Ford has. The premier started this, and it's time for Trudeau's government to show that it cares about the city of Toronto, and the other big cities, and disallow this bill before Ford has the chance to do any more damage.
Steven Abra, Toronto
Will bike lane boondoggle follow the same path as the Eglinton subway?
I remember from my teenage years the foresight that went into the planning and construction of a tunnelled transit line along Eglinton Ave., only to be cancelled and infilled by the next Conservative government because it was disruptive, wasteful and would be underused. It only took 30 years for the plan to be resurrected and then eventually championed by the same party that derailed it in the first place. Perhaps the disruptive, wasteful and underused bike lanes of Toronto will see a similar fate in 30 years after they are removed by the current Conservative government? Who knows, maybe it will even be a Conservative government that becomes their cheerleader then. But one wonders what innovations they will feel the need to nix when they decide the time for bike lanes has finally arrived.
Peter Drobac, Toronto
Gretzky's record is being softly assaulted
I opined last night with friends that it would be a shame if Alexander Ovechkin surpassed Wayne Gretzky's scoring record with a load of empty-net goals. Wouldn't you know it, an hour later Ovechkin scored an empty-net goal, floating around centre for a softie. How many of the 39 remaining goals he needs will be scored that way? You can bet Ovechkin will be on for a lot of empty-net situations this season even though he is not a defensive specialist. Washington Capitals should be ashamed of this insult to the game and the milestones established by incredible performances.
Ron Simpson, Toronto
Shameful police behaviour
This deputy police chief was allegedly caught going 50 km/h over the speed limit, Oct. 23
And they wonder why the public has so little faith in police and the bodies responsible for overseeing them. Shame on both.
Jennifer A. Temple, Welland ON
Liberal MPs should follow our democratic process if they want to oust their leader
Liberal MPs seek 'reflection and change' with letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down, Oct. 21
Canada is a democracy and yet some Liberal MPs seem to have forgotten that fact when they "demand" that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau resign as leader of the Liberal party. How can 24 MPs justify their behaviour when the majority of Liberal MPs continue to support the prime minister? These 24 MPs should be asking the Liberal party to establish a process by which the Liberal caucus could periodically assess their leader and through that democratic process vote on whether to keep him or fire him.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
Majority of caucus may support Trudeau but he needs the voters' support
Justin Trudeau rebuffed a dramatic challenge to his leadership, Oct. 24
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau may have the support of the majority of his caucus but what about us voters? According to the latest CIBC poll, Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party has 42.5 per cent support while the Liberals have a measly 23.2 per cent. I certainly don't want Poilievre as our next prime minister but Trudeau stubbornness and Liberal dissent are making that a likely scenario should an election be called. I live in the Scarborough-Guildwood riding in Ontario and our Member of Parliament, John McKay, doesn't plan to run in the next election. Regardless of who will represent our riding as a Liberal candidate, my vote will be determined by who leads the Liberal party. And it can't be Trudeau.
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
Is South Korea going to do what NATO members won't?
South Korea warns it can send arms to Ukraine after reports of North's troops in Russia, Oct. 22
Romeo Dallaire said very early in this conflict that allies should have sent troops and otherwise fully supported Ukraine as soon as Russian President Vladimir Putin invaded in 2022. North Korean troops are now in Russia to assist that country in its illegal invasion, but allies of Ukraine refuse to do the same to help Ukraine. The U.S. hasn't even allowed Ukraine to fire missiles into Russian territories under certain conditions. For example, despite being within range, Ukraine is not allowed to target long-range bombers before they take off from Russian bases. Sovereign nations can and should make independent decisions to assist allies in any fashion they see fit without being constrained by NATO. Let's hope non-NATO South Korea will seriously help Ukraine with weapons and troops because the "friends" of Ukraine "standing by" certainly aren't doing enough. Ukraine continues to lose territory and more importantly citizens due to Putin's ambitions.
Timothy J. Kwiatkowski, London, ON
Ontario's children's aid societies could really use those $200 chequesDoug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 18
The Ford government can't - or more accurately, won't - properly fund the Childrens Aid Societies across Ontario to protect our most vulnerable children, but he has no problem bribing Ontario citizens with $200. This is a despicable, transparent stunt.
Ginny Moeser, City of Kawartha Lakes

Document 426 of 500

Doug Ford steps into an LCBO mess of his own making - and we all may pay the price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-steps-into-lcbo-mess-his-own-making-we/docview/3120559572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+steps+into+an+LCBO+mess+of+his+own+making+-+and+we+all+may+pay+the+price&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has boxed himself in on paper bags at the LCBO.
Now watch him squirm out of a trade trap of his own making. Problem is, we may all pay a price for his folly.
When Ontario's teetotalling premier first got wind that booze bags were toast at the LCBO, he rode to the rescue of the wine-swilling, beer-guzzling, spirits-imbibing masses. Huffing and puffing, the man of the people laid down the law for liquor board's tree-hugging bean counters:
Bring back bags!
Ford fired off a letter telling the liquor board that people shouldn't have to carry bare bottles in public. Besides, Canadians are blessed with trees, which are a renewable resource, he reasoned.
"As a government, we are focused on making life easier, more convenient, and more affordable for the people of Ontario," Ford fumed last April. "The decision to remove paper bags has had the opposite effect."
And so the LCBO did as it was told. But by doing the right thing - getting the best price with a publicly tendered contract, untainted by corruption, free of favouritism and protectionism - the alcohol giant wrong-footed the premier a second time.
That's because it signed a deal with a Quebec supplier, Rosenbloom Groupe Inc., instead of a homegrown Ontario company.
Never mind that Quebec is still part of Canada. No matter that our neighbouring province happens to be the birthplace of Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, who oversees the LCBO.
The premier was apoplectic. The minister was apologetic.
How, Ford wondered, could the LCBO - which revels in protecting Niagara wines from out-of-province competition - not apply the same pugnacious, protectionist logic to local bag suppliers? If every winery is protected, why not our forestry?
The premier personally put pen to paper again, "directing the LCBO to also initiate a new tender process as soon as possible that will be limited to Ontario suppliers."
Bethlenfalvy dutifully told the arm's-length, Crown-owned LCBO that its due diligence was misplaced. When it comes to contracting, he ordered, don't go low - go local.
Now if you are a protectionist - as most progressives are - then perhaps you are onside with Ford's blustering (although not the notion of making it his top priority). Perhaps predictably, NDP Leader Marit Stiles echoed Ford's logic, arguing that "our procurement should always be focused on how we're going to actually drive Ontario's economy and create jobs here."
But if you believe in lowering trade barriers between Canadian provinces in order to foster national unity and forge economic ties, then the premier's musings make no sense.
Lest Ford forget, he is the rotating chair of the Council of the Federation until next summer, which means his mission is to bring all the premiers together to promote interprovincial procurement and trade. One can only imagine the conversation when Ford convenes Quebec Premier François Legault and his counterparts in Toronto and sheepishly confesses that Quebec bags are banned.
Is our forestry industry so feeble that it needs to be propped up at any price - even at the risk of retaliation by our trading partners? The problem with protectionism is that it's a two-way street, and not just along east-west lines.
Think north-south.
With the U.S. presidential election just days away, Ford told the Empire Club last week that the stakes are perilously high for this province: Two-way trade between Ontario and the United States exceeds $500 billion.
"If Ontario were a stand-alone country, we'd be America's third largest trading partner," he mused.
That's why it's so important for all governments, "no matter the political stripe, to reject protectionism and embrace a 'Buy Can-Am' mentality."
Ford argued against the "Fortress America" mentality that American politicians talk up on the campaign trail: "We're encouraging U.S. lawmakers to think big - to build fortress North America (my italics)."
That was last week. This week it's a different story, the premier now practising the protectionism he preaches against with a double-barrelled "Buy Ontario" and "Ban Quebec" procurement policy.
By so blatantly contradicting himself, by placing paper bags at the top of his protectionist wish list, Ford has undermined his strategy to safeguard the Ontario economy from Buy American provisions.
To be sure, there may be some strategic or sensitive sectors of the economy that might be worth going to the wall for. But building a protective wall around paper bags doesn't add up.
For the record, the brown bag contract is worth $10.9 million. That's a rounding error when measured against our $500-billion trading relationship with the U.S.
It reveals a premier who runs the province by the seat of his pants and goes by his gut. Instead of using his head to think things through, he's putting a paper bag over it.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to media at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.

Document 427 of 500

Fertility program gets $150M investment: Proposed tax credit of $5,000 will cover 25% of expenses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fertility-program-gets-150m-investment/docview/3120341421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fertility+program+gets+%24150M+investment%3A+Proposed+tax+credit+of+%245%2C000+will+cover+25%25+of+expenses&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is boosting its fertility program to help more people struggling to have children and proposing a new tax credit to defray the hefty costs of treatment.
The tax credit of up to $5,000 a year will be unveiled in next week's fall economic statement, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Thursday at an Etobicoke fertility clinic with Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
It comes as Premier Doug Ford sets the stage for an early provincial election next spring instead of waiting for the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
"More can be done to give everyone in Ontario the special chance at parenthood," Jones said, announcing an increase in funding for the Ontario fertility program to $150 million in each of the next two years.
That's more than double the $70 million now allocated annually and should triple the number of families getting government-funded in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles at more hospitals and community clinics, she added.
Jones noted fertility treatments can be "overwhelmingly expensive" and cost families tens of thousands of dollars as they ride an emotional roller coaster while trying to conceive.
"Many more women than we know have experienced this," said Progressive Conservative MPP Christine Hogarth (Etobicoke-Lakeshore.
Wait times for treatment will be "significantly" reduced by the increased funding, pledged Bethlenfalvy, who will present his fall economic statement next Wednesday.
About 13,000 people use the fertility program annually, so tripling that would put the number closer to 39,000.
Bethlenfalvy's proposed tax credit would cover up to 25 per cent of eligible expenses, including IVF cycles, fertility medications, travel for treatment and diagnostic testing starting next year.
As the Star first reported, the fall economic statement will also detail payments of about $200 each to every Ontarian to defray the cost of living following a bout of high inflation that sent prices for food, housing and other goods soaring.
Opposition parties have slammed it as a pre-election ploy as Ford seeks a third term before any potential charges in an RCMP probe of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," Ford said Wednesday on Newstalk 1010.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+should+overrule+Ford+on+bike+lanes+Doug+Ford+s+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+bill+to+limit+new+bike+lanes+will+also+%27review%27+existing+ones%2C+Oct.+21+This+deputy+police+chief+was+allegedly+caught+going+50+km%2Fh+over+the+speed+limit%2C+Oct.+23+Liberal+MPs+seek+%27reflection+and+change%27+with+letter+calling+on+Justin+Trudeau+to+step+down%2C+Oct.+21+Justin+Trudeau+rebuffed+a+dramatic+challenge+to+his+leadership%2C+Oct.+24+South+Korea+warns+it+can+send+arms+to+Ukraine+after+reports+of+North%27s+troops+in+Russia%2C+Oct.+22&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa should overrule Ford
on bike lanes
Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones, Oct. 21
My family regularly uses the bike lanes that Premier Doug Ford has just promised to tear up. They may not be perfect, but the bike lanes we have make our travel safer, and we appreciate them. There are many aspects of this change we're angry about. For starters, there's the waste of tens of millions of dollars and months of construction disruption, and now the promise of repeating all those same costs again, just to move backwards.
This is and has always been the responsibility of the mayor and city council. Ford had his shot at being mayor, but he lost. Similarly, in both elections he's run in as leader of the PC party, his party has failed to win even a single seat in the old city of Toronto. He doesn't represent us, and he never has. Because of outdated constitutional rules, he has the power to overrule the people who are responsible for making these decisions, and there isn't anything we can do about it, since his party doesn't have any seats to lose in the affected areas.
However, just as he can overreach and violate norms of provincial conduct and respect for municipal autonomy, the federal government can and should overrule him by acting to disallow Bill 212. It's true that it's rare for the federal government to intervene in provincial legislation, but it's also rare for the province to intervene in Toronto politics the way Ford has. The premier started this, and it's time for Trudeau's government to show that it cares about the city of Toronto, and the other big cities, and disallow this bill before Ford has the chance to do any more damage.
Steven Abra, Toronto
I remember from my teenage years the foresight that went into the planning and construction of a tunnelled transit line along Eglinton Avenue, only to be cancelled and infilled by the next Conservative government because it was disruptive, wasteful and would be underused. It only took 30 years for the plan to be resurrected and then eventually championed by the same party that derailed it in the first place. Perhaps the disruptive, wasteful and underused bike lanes of Toronto will see a similar fate in 30 years after they are removed by the current Conservative government? Who knows, maybe it will even be a Conservative government that becomes their cheerleader then. But one wonders what innovations they will feel the need to nix when they decide the time for bike lanes has finally arrived.
Peter Drobac, Toronto
Shameful police behaviour erodes public trust
This deputy police chief
was allegedly caught going
50 km/h over the speed limit, Oct. 23
And they wonder why the public has so little faith in police and the bodies responsible for overseeing them. Shame on both.
Jennifer A. Temple,
Welland, Ont.
Liberals should establish process to assess leader
Liberal MPs seek 'reflection and change' with letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down, Oct. 21
Canada is a democracy and yet some Liberal MPs seem to have forgotten that fact when they "demand" that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau resign as leader of the Liberal party. How can 24 MPs justify their behaviour when the majority of Liberal MPs continue to support the prime minister? These 24 MPs should be asking the Liberal party to establish a process by which the Liberal caucus could periodically assess their leader and through that democratic process vote on whether to keep him or fire him.
Charles Campisi, Oakville, Ont.
Forget caucus. Trudeau has lost voters' support
Justin Trudeau rebuffed a dramatic challenge to his leadership, Oct. 24
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau may have the support of the majority of his caucus, but what about us voters? According to the latest CIBC poll, Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party has 42.5 per cent support while the Liberals have a measly 23.2 per cent. I certainly don't want Poilievre as our next prime minister, but Trudeau's stubbornness and Liberal dissent are making that a likely scenario should an election be called. I live in the Scarborough-Guildwood riding in Ontario and our member of Parliament, John McKay, doesn't plan to run in the next election. Regardless of who will represent our riding as a Liberal candidate, my vote will be determined by who leads the Liberal party. And it can't be Trudeau.
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
A hollow way to surpass Gretzky's scoring record
I opined last night with friends that it would be a shame if Alexander Ovechkin surpassed Wayne Gretzky's scoring record with a load of empty-net goals. Wouldn't you know it, an hour later Ovechkin scored an empty-net goal, floating around centre for a softie. How many of the 39 remaining goals he needs will be scored that way? You can bet Ovechkin will be on for a lot of empty-net situations this season even though he is not a defensive specialist. Washington Capitals should be ashamed of this insult to the game and the milestones established by incredible performances.
Ron Simpson, Toronto
Is Seoul going to do what NATO members won't?
South Korea warns it can send arms to Ukraine after reports of North's troops in Russia, Oct. 22
Romeo Dallaire said very early in this conflict that allies should have sent troops and otherwise fully supported Ukraine as soon as Russia invaded in 2022. North Korean troops are now in Russia to assist that country in its illegal invasion, but allies of Ukraine refuse to do the same to help Ukraine. The U.S. hasn't even allowed Ukraine to fire missiles into Russian territories under certain conditions. For example, despite being within range, Ukraine is not allowed to target long-range bombers before they take off from Russian bases. Sovereign nations can and should make independent decisions to assist allies in any fashion they see fit without being constrained by NATO. Let's hope non-NATO South Korea will seriously help Ukraine with weapons and troops because the "friends" of Ukraine "standing by" certainly aren't doing enough. Ukraine continues to lose territory and more importantly citizens due to Vladimir Putin's ambitions.
Timothy J. Kwiatkowski,
London, Ont.

Subject: Leadership; Local elections; Voters; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Russia; Ukraine; South Korea

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Document 429 of 500

Ford in an LCBO mess he made himself

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-lcbo-mess-he-made-himself/docview/3120340914/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+in+an+LCBO+mess+he+made+himself&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has boxed himself in on paper bags at the LCBO.

Document 430 of 500

Ad campaign aims to take on Conservatives: Anonymously funded group wants to unite progressives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ad-campaign-aims-take-on-conservatives/docview/3120340594/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ad+campaign+aims+to+take+on+Conservatives%3A+Anonymously+funded+group+wants+to+unite+progressives&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA A third-party group run by former NDP and Liberal staffers has launched an advertising campaign against Pierre Poilievre, in an attempt to go "dollar for dollar" with the Conservatives on ad spending.
The campaign comes as the prospect of a snap election grows and as Liberals gripe over what they see as slow and inadequate attempts to contrast the Conservative leader's record with the Trudeau government through advertising.
The Tories, flush with cash from record fundraising, spent $8.5 million to get their message out last year - more than 20 times as much as the governing Liberals, who spent more than $380,000 on advertising.
Now, Protecting Canada, an anonymously funded group with ties to the NDP, Liberals and major unions, says it wants to "fill the vacuum" and unite progressives to stand up to the Conservative "media-machine that fuels hypocrisy."
"Our ambition is to match Conservative infrastructure, dollar for dollar, and spend as much as it takes," said spokesperson Megana Ramaswami.
But the financial backers of the group are shrouded in secrecy. Ramaswami, the only public face of the group, did not answer questions about specific funders, only saying the group was backed by progressive individuals and organizations wanting to unite Canadians against Poilievre.
Filings with the Ontario government shed some light on who's behind the non-profit group. Its directors are former senior NDP staffers Ian Wayne and David Hare; Bryan Leblanc, who worked for the Ontario Liberals in the Kathleen Wynne era and on former Liberal minister Sheila Copps's 2004 federal party leadership campaign; and former Canadian Labour Congress executive Barbara Byers.
Don Millar, an advertising specialist who has worked on federal Liberal campaigns and with former B.C. premier Christy Clark, a potential federal Liberal leadership candidate, is a former director of the group. He told the Star he is helping with advertising.
Ramaswami said Protecting Canada does not support any political party, and the NDP and federal and Ontario Liberal parties denied any connection with it. Efforts by the Star to contact the directors were either redirected to Ramaswami or not responded to.
The group, which formed in 2021 with an ad campaign against then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole in the months leading up to that year's election campaign, has launched its first anti-Poilievre televised ad, a 30-second clip attacking Poilievre's past votes for raising the retirement age and against boosting pensions, in contrast with his government pension.
Protecting Canada also plans to run ads attacking Poilievre on issues including gun control, health care, child care and abortion rights with a campaign dubbed "That's Poilievre. But it's just not Canada."
Elections Canada rules do not restrict third-party advertising until three months ahead of a scheduled election, or when an unscheduled campaign begins. Protecting Canada would only have to register - and disclose funding for its campaign - if it advertises during those periods.
The group has also spent at least $130,000 on digital ads in the last few weeks, according to Meta's ad library, which shows it outspent the Tories by $40,000 in the past week specifically. The vast majority of those ads have been in Ontario and B.C., in an attempt to target swing ridings the Tories hope can pave their way to a majority government.
The Liberals and NDP have spent less than $10,000 combined in the last month on Meta ads, though the New Democrats recently ran some anti-Poilievre ads.
CAPTION: Protecting Canada, a third-party group, says it will go "dollar for dollar" with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and his party on ad spending.
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 431 of 500

Anonymously funded anti-Poilievre ad campaign has links to former Liberal, NDP staffers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/anonymously-funded-anti-poilievre-ad-campaign-has/docview/3120654063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anonymously+funded+anti-Poilievre+ad+campaign+has+links+to+former+Liberal%2C+NDP+staffers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A third-party group run by former NDP and Liberal staffers has launched an advertising campaign against Pierre Poilievre, in an attempt to go "dollar for dollar" with the Conservatives on ad spending.
The campaign comes as the prospect of a snap election grows and as Liberals gripe over what they see as slow and inadequate attempts to contrast the Conservative leader's record with the Trudeau government through advertising.
The Tories, flush with cash from record fundraising, spent $8.5 million to get their message out last year - more than 20 times as much as the governing Liberals, who spent more than $380,000 on advertising.
Now, Protecting Canada, an anonymously funded group with ties to the NDP, Liberals and major unions, says it wants to "fill the vacuum" and unite progressives to stand up to the Conservative "media-machine that fuels hypocrisy."
"Our ambition is to match Conservative infrastructure, dollar for dollar, and spend as much as it takes," said spokesperson Megana Ramaswami.
But the financial backers of the group are shrouded in secrecy. Ramaswami, the only public face of the group, did not answer questions about specific funders, only saying the group was backed by progressive individuals and organizations wanting to unite Canadians against Poilievre.
Filings with the Ontario government shed some light on who's behind the non-profit group. Its directors are former senior NDP staffers Ian Wayne and David Hare; Bryan Leblanc, who worked for the Ontario Liberals in the Kathleen Wynne era and on former Liberal minister Sheila Copps's 2004 federal party leadership campaign; and former Canadian Labour Congress executive Barbara Byers.
Don Millar, an advertising specialist who has worked on federal Liberal campaigns and with former B.C. premier Christy Clark, a potential federal Liberal leadership candidate, is a former director of the group. He told the Star he is helping with advertising.
Ramaswami said Protecting Canada does not support any political party, and the NDP and federal and Ontario Liberal parties denied any connection with it. Efforts by the Star to contact the directors were either redirected to Ramaswami or not responded to.
The group, which formed in 2021 with an ad campaign against then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole in the months leading up to that year's election campaign, has launched its first anti-Poilievre televised ad, a 30-second clip attacking Poilievre's past votes for raising the retirement age and against boosting pensions, in contrast with his government pension.
Protecting Canada also plans to run ads attacking Poilievre on issues including gun control, health care, child care and abortion rights with a campaign dubbed "That's Poilievre. But it's just not Canada."
Elections Canada rules do not restrict third-party advertising until three months ahead of a scheduled election, or when an unscheduled campaign begins. Protecting Canada would only have to register - and disclose funding for its campaign - if it advertises during those periods.
The group has also spent at least $130,000 on digital ads in the last few weeks, according to Meta's ad library, which shows it outspent the Tories by $40,000 in the past week specifically. The vast majority of those ads have been in Ontario and B.C., in an attempt to target swing ridings the Tories hope can pave their way to a majority government.
The Liberals and NDP have spent less than $10,000 combined in the last month on Meta ads, though the New Democrats recently ran some anti-Poilievre ads. The Liberals have routinely attacked Poilievre, but without mass advertising, a point of contention among some in the party.
Trudeau recently defended the decision not to run political ads against Poilievre after he became Conservative leader, arguing it would have looked petty to voters if the Liberals took their eye off the bigger economic and financial woes facing Canadians. He has insisted there is still time to paint Poilievre as the risk to the environment, equality rights and social programs that the Liberals believe he is.
"Good luck to the prime minister to have a competitive fight if it's so lopsided," Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith said of the party's ad spending on Wednesday.
A spokesperson for Poilievre called the campaign a distraction from the federal government's "inflationary spending and carbon taxes."
"While the NDP-Liberal costly coalition and their insider friends will use dark money on their pathetic attempts to misinform Canadians, Pierre Poilievre and his common sense Conservatives will turn that hurt into hope and bring home more powerful paycheques, lower prices, and safer streets for all Canadians by axing the tax, building the homes, fixing the budget, and stopping the crime," Sebastian Skamski wrote in an email.
Unifor president Lana Payne said in a statement that "while Unifor has contributed to past campaigns," the union is not involved in the "current round of ads" from the group.
Canadian Labour Congress spokesperson Karyne Vienneau told the Star the CLC supports the message in the ad campaign and has had "chatter" about it with like-minded groups, but has not provided funding.
"The truth about Poilievre, his motivations, his policy positions on Canadians' pensions, our health-care system, needs to get out there," Vienneau said in an email.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Oct. 22, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Canadians; Pensions; Advertising expenditures; Political advertising; Elections; Advertising campaigns

Document 432 of 500

Ford government to help more families get fertility treatments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-help-more-families-get-fertility/docview/3120654061/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+help+more+families+get+fertility+treatments&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is boosting its fertility program to help more people struggling to have children and proposing a new tax credit to defray the hefty costs of treatment.
The tax credit of up to $5,000 a year will be unveiled in next week's fall economic statement, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Thursday at an Etobicoke fertility clinic with Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
It comes as Premier Doug Ford sets the stage for an early provincial election next spring instead of waiting for the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
"More can be done to give everyone in Ontario the special chance at parenthood," Jones said, announcing an increase in funding for the Ontario fertility program to $150 million in each of the next two years.
That's more than double the $70 million now allocated annually and should triple the number of families getting government-funded in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles at more hospitals and community clinics, she added.
Jones noted fertility treatments can be "overwhelmingly expensive" and cost families tens of thousands of dollars as they ride an emotional roller coaster while trying to conceive.
"Many more women than we know have experienced this," said Progressive Conservative MPP Christine Hogarth (Etobicoke-Lakeshore), who advocated for an expanded fertility program.
Wait times for treatment will be "significantly" reduced by the increased funding, pledged Bethlenfalvy, who will present his fall economic statement next Wednesday.
About 13,000 people use the fertility program annually, so tripling that would put the number closer to 39,000.
Bethlenfalvy's proposed tax credit would cover up to 25 per cent of eligible expenses, including IVF cycles, fertility medications, travel for treatment and diagnostic testing starting next year.
As the Star first reported, the fall economic statement will also detail payments of about $200 each to every Ontarian to defray the cost of living following a bout of high inflation that sent prices for food, housing and other goods soaring.
Opposition parties have slammed it as a pre-election ploy as Ford seeks a third term before any potential charges in an RCMP criminal investigation of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," Ford said Wednesday on Newstalk 1010.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the provincewill increase fertility program funding to $150 million in each of the next two years.

Subject: Fertility; Tax credits; Funding; In vitro fertilization

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax credits

Document 433 of 500

Justin Trudeau 'disappointing' MPs who want him gone but other Liberals say he is still deciding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-disappointing-mps-who-want-him/docview/3120653454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+%27disappointing%27+MPs+who+want+him+gone+but+other+Liberals+say+he+is+still+deciding&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The drama surrounding Justin Trudeau's leadership clouded another day on Parliament Hill, as dissenting Liberal MPs who want him to resign say they're disappointed the prime minister is trumpeting his intention to stay, while others suggested they don't believe he has yet made a final decision.
More than six Liberal sources have told the Star that Trudeau told his caucus he will remain as leader after he listened to roughly 20 of his MPs tell him behind closed doors on Wednesday that he should resign or at least reconsider whether his continued leadership is best for the party.
On Thursday, at a press conference on Parliament Hill, Trudeau repeated in public that he has no intention to step down, despite a demand in a letter from a group of 24 MPs asking him to respond to their call for him to resign by Monday.
Asked if he planned to stay on past that date, Trudeau simply said "yes," and later characterized the dissent over his leadership as part of his party's "robust conversations" about how best to defeat Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party, which has a strong and persistent lead in all publicly available national polls.
"We're going to continue to have great conversations about what is the best way to take on Pierre Poilievre in the next election, but that will happen with me as leader," Trudeau said.
Wayne Long, one of three Liberal MPs who have publicly confirmed they are among the dissenting group that wants Trudeau gone, told the Star on Thursday that the prime minister should take more time to consider what's best for the party.
Others who have called for Trudeau to resign include Prince Edward Island MP Sean Casey and Newfoundland's Ken McDonald. Multiple Liberal sources, who spoke on condition they not be identified since caucus deliberations are intended to be private, have told the Star that British Columbia MP Patrick Weiler and Toronto's Yvan Baker were also among those who told Trudeau this week that he should resign.
"There was a strong call yesterday asking him to step down," Long said. "I don't think 18 hours is enough time to really give pause. I honestly hoped he'd take the weekend.
"He needs to reflect more. A lot more."
Casey later told reporters that he finds Trudeau's decision "disappointing," but that he is personally moving on and focused on getting elected again in his P.E.I. riding.
"As far as I'm concerned, it's closed," he said.
Other Liberal MPs suggested Thursday that they don't believe Trudeau has truly decided on his future as leader, after hearing the grievances of so many of his parliamentarians this week.
"I'm confident he'll take the time to reflect," Baker told journalists on the Hill, after declining to say whether he supports the prime minister.
Quebec MP Alexandra Mendès - who said she has echoed her constituents' desire for Trudeau to resign, even though she thinks the opposition to him is "unfair" - told the Star that "of course" Trudeau has to tell the media he intends to stay on, because otherwise he would be a "lame duck" prime minister.
"He received a lot of information, a lot of thoughts and opinions from around the country. So, yeah - I'm sure he's going to take the time necessary to come to a decision one way or the other."
Long, meanwhile, said he is not willing to leave the Liberal caucus to sit as an independent, but that he's not sure what the dissenters can do if Trudeau refuses to resign.
"I love the Liberal party - in fact I'm trying to save it from itself," he said. "Look at Ontario. They still haven't recovered from the 2018 slaughter."
That was a reference to the provincial Liberals' crushing defeat under former premier Kathleen Wynne's leadership, when the party got walloped and dropped from 55 to seven seats at Queen's Park. The Ontario Liberals only gained one more seat in the following election in 2022, when they clinched eight ridings.
Concerns about such a bruising loss at the federal level are widespread in Liberal circles. One Liberal MP who is among those calling for Trudeau to resign - and spoke on condition of anonymity - said fears of such a defeat are the main factor behind their actions. If the Liberal government loses badly in the next election, the MP worried that a Conservative regime they view as wrong for the country could be in power for several years.
Those concerns also found voice in a grassroots petition circulating this week that declares the party is facing an "existential crisis." The petition demands that the federal caucus hold a secret ballot vote on Trudeau's leadership. It also calls for structural changes to improve party fundraising and riding-level organizing, and for a policy refresh to focus on policies to boost economic growth and wealth per capita.
Casey, the MP who has called for Trudeau to go, said this week he wished there was a formal mechanism for caucus to vote on Trudeau's leadership. And Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, the MP for Toronto's Beaches-East York, said he would support some sort of vote to provide "finality" on the question of whether Trudeau should stay.
Asked about that idea on Thursday, Health Minister Mark Holland dismissed a caucus vote as "nuts."
"That makes no sense at all," he said. "You're basically saying that a very small minority of people, a lot of whom aren't running again ... are going to impose that process ... I think that's profoundly unhelpful."
Holland suggested dissenters to Trudeau's leadership are missing how the Liberal government operates as a "team" that is more than just the person at the top.
"There is no one leader who's going to fix everything, and I think we should be very afraid of anybody who says there is," Holland said.
Several other MPs have also told the Star this week that support for Trudeau's leadership remains widespread inside the Liberal caucus. One MP, who spoke on condition of anonymity Thursday, told the Star there was a "massive round of applause" for caucus members who spoke in support of the prime minister's leadership at Wednesday's meeting.
The MP said that even some of those who called for Trudeau to resign this week said they would back him if he decides to stay.
CAPTION: Liberal MP Wayne Long saidthat the prime minister should take more time to consider what's best for the party.

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Justin Trudeau rebuffed a challenge to his leadership + Torontonians renting their homes out just for Taylor Swift

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, October 24 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on challenges to Justin Trudeau's leadership, an investigation into a Durham police official and the Torontonians profiting off Taylor Swift.
DON'T MISSJustin Trudeau rebuffed a dramatic challenge to his leadership
The prime minister is standing strong after 24 of his own MPs urged him to step down by Monday. In a "frank" and "very emotional" closed-door meeting yesterday, Trudeau made it clear to dissenters that he intends to stay while his supporters called for an end to the internal divisions. But for all his insistence, questions are still swirling around his leadership. Here's what went down at the meeting.
Meanwhile: A group of young, self-described grassroots Liberals sent a "Code Red Petition" to "tens of thousands" of voters this week, describing an "existential crisis" within the party and calling for a secret ballot vote on whether Trudeau should stay.Allen Alexandre's take: Trudeau's departure will likely unravel the delicate threads of diversity, equality and progress we fought so hard to weave over the last few years. These are the reasons Canada still needs him.This police official was allegedly caught driving way over the limit
Durham police Dep. Chief Chris Kirkpatrick was allegedly caught going more than 50 km/h over the speed limit in June. It should have led to a stunt driving charge, a license suspension and the immediate impounding of his vehicle, Brendan Kennedy reports. Instead, Kirkpatrick was let off - despite it being the second time in two days he was stopped for speeding. The case has been kept from the public until now. Take a look behind the curtain.
Under wraps: Peel Regional Police was tapped to investigate the alleged incident, and provided a report to the Durham police board this year.Under investigation: For the last five years, Durham police have faced an ongoing investigation following allegations its senior leaders regularly abused their power and created a group of "untouchables" in the top ranks. Check out the allegations against them.Torontonians are renting out their homes just for Taylor Swift's visit
If Taylor Swift were performing in your backyard, would you stay for the show - or make thousands renting your place out to Swifties? Many Torontonians are choosing the latter, placing their homes on rental platforms for up to $850 a night during Swift's Eras Tour. "Toronto brings that side-hustle out of you," joked one woman who listed her condo for $750 a night. Ana Pereira has the details.
Swiftonomics: It's not just property owners who are profiting. Swift's two-week visit is expected to inject more than $282 million into Toronto's economy.Rising rent: The price of short-term rentals across Ontario soared by roughly 70 per cent throughout Swift's six concerts in November, while searches for the rentals surged by 1,200 per cent during those dates.WHAT ELSE

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3119912040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Simu+Liu%2C+bubble+tea+and+cultural+appropriation%3A+How+an+episode+of+Dragons%27+Den+backfired+for+a+Canadian+business%2C+Oct.+17+Doug+Ford%27s+war+on+bike+lanes+may+help+him+get+re-elected%2C+but+it+won%27t+solve+gridlock%2C+Cohn%2C+Oct.+22+Toronto+will+keep+losing+treasures+like+my+favourite+magazine+shop+in+the+Annex+if+we+don%27t+figure+out+how+to+make+space+for+one+another%2C+Mallick%2C+Oct.+23+Menopause+special+section%2C+Oct.+18+This+Raptors+season+really+is+a+throwback+to+the+old+days.+Finally%2C+Toronto+is+rebuilding+again%2C+Oct.+23+Ford+government+unveils+more+details+on+plan+to+curb+new+bike+lanes+on+major+roads%2C+Oct.+18&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is drink cultural appropriation or just unhealthy?
Simu Liu, bubble tea and cultural appropriation: How an episode of Dragons' Den backfired for a Canadian business, Oct. 17
If an Asian person were to make this same pitch, would there be a problem? People can improve a product without the cultural origin coming into question. Cultural origin does not define ownership; rather, good products are built off ideas and a series of improvements over time.
The root of the problem is not cultural appropriation. It's that the entrepreneurs claimed the content of bubble tea is unknown and unhealthy when their product is just as unhealthy, especially since they mentioned having an alcoholic version of their drink. The company claims to use three main ingredients, but if you Google it, you can see that it uses chemicals as well as added sugars you would see in other boba drinks.
Although this company has to make certain adjustments to the way they advertise their products, they should never have faced hate online. Simu Liu did well to acknowledge the online threats, but I disagree with his take on cultural appropriation.
Brandon Jaundoo, Ajax
Ford must stop overruling
local politicians
Doug Ford's war on bike lanes may help him get re-elected, but it won't solve gridlock, Cohn, Oct. 22
Martin Regg Cohn scores again on his analysis of the divisive issue of bike lanes on city streets. Indeed, we ought not to take Premier Doug Ford's bait by putting our energy into a divisive and distracting debate on this issue.
We all use public spaces for transportation: sometimes we use a vehicle, sometimes we bicycle, sometimes we walk, sometimes we use public transit, and sometimes we use a specialized mobility device, like a motorized scooter or an electric bike. Public spaces ought to be available to all of us, all the time.
Cohn is absolutely correct when he writes that the responsibility for decisions on the use of roadways ought to remain at the municipal level. There are reasons for three levels of government.
Just because Ford can overrule the decisions of municipal politicians is not a good enough reason for doing so. What will it be next week?
Allan Baker, Scarborough
As landlords get richer, Toronto gets duller
Toronto will keep losing treasures like my favourite magazine shop in the Annex if we don't figure out how to make space for one another, Mallick, Oct. 23
Heather Mallick laments the impending loss of the terrific magazine shop Presse Internationale because of extortionate rent increases. So many small businesses have packed up and left after new owners jacked up the rent to impossible levels - at Presse Internationale, from $7,000 to $11,000 per month.
Fabric store The Workroom, until recently a fixture on Queen West, is another casualty of huge rent increases.
Small local retailers give us choices and delightful variety that big box stores do not offer. The landlords may be richer (well, perhaps not The Workroom's - the space was still empty a year after the store packed up), but Toronto is duller and Torontonians are certainly less well off as a result.
Dilys Bowman, Toronto
A crazy solution to tunnel vision
I have the craziest, best idea ever that will reduce traffic and make Toronto the safest place for cyclists in the world: build a 44-kilometre tunnel under Highway 401 for cyclists! Thousands of people would happily give up their cars for a weatherproof, safe ride. It could also be built at a fraction of the cost of a vehicle tunnel.
John Cowles, Oakville
Is menopause now a disease?
Menopause special section, Oct. 18
For eons, women have been going through this life stage mostly without air conditioning and moisture wicking clothes, me included. But now comes a renewed campaign to persuade women that they need a drug, HRT, to manage this normal part of life.
Pfizer, the pharma company that makes Premarin and Duavee, sources its HRT drugs from industrial pregnant mare farms. None of the articles say anything about the torturous process used to extract urine.
The mares are impregnated over and over. They spend their lives upright in tight stalls with catheter tubes, and their foals are forcibly removed right after birth. The foals are slaughtered for meat and so are the mares when they cease to be of use.
Is this abuse of horses' worth it to avoid some hot flashes? The story and ad say menopause can lead to bladder control issues and depression. I imagine men, too, get depressed when their age-related prostate problems cause bladder problems.
The point is that we now look for a pill or medication for every natural part of aging, and companies like Pfizer promote menopause as a disease with inconvenient symptoms that need to be managed.
Given all the truly life-threatening diseases that medical science has been unable to figure out, I find it incredible that a state-of-the-art facility will be built to address menopause. And given the animal abuse required to manufacture HRT, I am appalled that any woman who knows this would use it.
Igea Khan, Toronto
It is Raptor's job to try to win
This Raptors season really is a throwback to the old days. Finally, Toronto is rebuilding again, Oct. 23
Jakob Poeltl of the Toronto Raptors states that "it makes no sense for us to try to win every single game" this season. He is wrong, it is in fact his job to try to win every game. If the fans choose to buy tickets to games this year, are they supposed to cheer for losses, so the Raptors can get a high draft pick?
John Rochester, Toronto
Safe, connected bike lanes should not be deferred
Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads,
Oct. 18
As bike lanes facing little usage are taking up lanes on major thoroughfares such as Bloor Street West, viable traffic lanes are blocked. Bike lanes provide a safe, alternative transit option when strategically placed and not built on major roadways where they are barely used.
Toronto has already seen a significant increase in cyclist deaths. The implementation of safe, connected bike lanes should not be deferred.
Evelyn Rothgerber, Toronto

Document 436 of 500

'You build safe streets, or people die': protester: Cyclists rally at Queen's Park over bike lane threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/you-build-safe-streets-people-die-protester/docview/3119911887/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27You+build+safe+streets%2C+or+people+die%27%3A+protester%3A+Cyclists+rally+at+Queen%27s+Park+over+bike+lane+threat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hundreds of cyclists gathered at Queen's Park on Wednesday in protest of the Ford government's threats to use new legislation to pull out bike lanes on three major thoroughfares in Toronto.
On Monday, the government tabled a bill that would force municipalities to seek approval from the province before building new bike lanes. The bill, from Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, would also give the province a chance to "review existing bicycle lanes." And Premier Doug Ford has suggested bike lanes on Bloor and Yonge Streets, as well as University Avenue, are in his government's crosshairs.
"You build safe streets, or people die," Jess Spieker, a protest organizer with Families and Friends for Safe Streets, told the crowd of about 500 on Wednesday afternoon, including more than half a dozen opposition MPPs.
Some in the audience rang their bike bells in agreement. Elise Corbin, a student at University of Toronto, said she was biking to campus earlier Wednesday and a taxi made a U-turn into a bike lane on Bloor Street and almost hit her. Then on Spadina Avenue, she said a car turned left too soon through an intersection while she was crossing. "Again, if I hadn't skidded to a halt in time, I would have been hit."
After class, she had a few hours before the protest. She asked a hot dog vendor if she could have the cardboard lid, then found a sharpie on the ground and wrote in all caps: "I almost got ran over twice on my way here." At the protest, the sign was tied to her backpack with a piece of red yarn.
Ford defended the government's position on Wednesday, arguing that congestion on University Avenue's hospital row has caused issues for first responders.
"It's insanity, that's the way I'm going to describe it," Ford told host Deb Hutton during a Wednesday interview on Newstalk 1010. He noted his brother, Rob Ford, invested in bike lane construction when he was mayor of Toronto.
"We aren't against bike lanes," he said. "We put them on the secondary roads and let the main arterial roads flow with the traffic."
But critics have accused Ford of overstepping and meddling in municipal affairs, still focused on settling scores from his time as a city councillor when his brother was mayor.
"Yoo-hoo, Doug Ford, you're the premier of Ontario. You're not the mayor of Toronto. Stay in your lane," Mary-Margaret McMahon, the Liberal MPP for Beaches-East York, said into a microphone outside Queen's Park.
On the edges of the protest, some of the cyclists said they showed up because any changes to existing bike lanes would disrupt their daily routines.
Mary Lato rode to the protest on her bike, with her three kids in a cart attached to the front. They live near Yonge Street and anywhere they go, they use the bike lane. If it was removed, she said she couldn't bike with her kids anymore "in good conscience."
After the protest, the crowd was led on a group bike ride up to Avenue Road, near Davenport, and back to Queen's Park. Joey Schwartz, the organizer who rode at the front guiding the crowd, guessed the line of cyclists behind him stretched 800 metres.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Jake Edmiston Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Document 437 of 500

Sports briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sports-briefs/docview/3119911866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sports+briefs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Auto racing
Allison gets win 53 years later
NASCAR Hall of Fame driver Bobby Allison finally got his victory from Bowman Gray Stadium, more than five decades after he took the checkered flag. NASCAR formally recognized the 86-year-old Allison as the winner of the Meyers Brothers Memorial in Winston-Salem, N.C., in 1971. The race was one of six that season that featured a mix of Grand National (now the Cup Series) and Grand American cars. Allison, who competed in both series, chose to drive his Grand American Ford in the race and his win never counted toward his Cup Series total. He now has 85, fourth on the all-time list.
Basketball
Mystics fire coach and GM
The Washington Mystics are parting ways with coach Eric Thibault and GM Mike Thibault, the franchise announced Wednesday. Eric Thibault is the fifth WNBA coach to be let go this off-season, with Washington joining Dallas, Atlanta, Chicago and Los Angeles. He took over for his father after the 2022 season. The younger Thibault spent 10 years as an assistant coach with the Mystics, including four as associate head coach. The Mystics finished this season 14-26 after opening with 12 straight losses. Mike Thibault joined the Mystics in 2012 as GM and coach and led the team to its first title (2019).
Hockey
Blues' Thomas out six weeks
St. Louis Blues forward Robert Thomas will miss at least six weeks with a broken right ankle. The Blues put Thomas on injured reserve after he took a puck off the ankle early in the third period of their loss Tuesday night to Winnipeg. General manager Doug Armstrong said Thomas would be evaluated in six weeks. The Blues were already without defencemen Nick Leddy, who went on IR earlier in the week with a lower-body injury, and Torey Krug, who will miss the entire season after ankle surgery. Forward Oskar Sundqvist took Thomas's spot on the roster and could play Thursday in Toronto.
Tennis
Djokovic pulls out of Paris
Defending champion Novak Djokovic has pulled out of next week's Paris Masters after playing at the Six Kings Slam exhibition last week. Organizers did not give a reason for Djokovic's withdrawal. The 37-year-old said in an Instagram post he is "sorry to everyone who was hoping to see me play there." Djokovic has won a record seven titles at the Paris indoor tournament. His decision not to play could jeopardize his chances to qualify for the year-end ATP Finals, featuring the top eight players in the race. The 24-time Grand Slam champion is currently sixth.
Star wire services
CAPTION: St. Louis Blues centre Robert Thomas broke his ankle after taking a shot off of it Tuesday.
CREDIT: Star wire services
