Document 438 of 500

Ford blasts bike lanes, lauds rebate: Premier appears to tip hand on themes for a spring election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-blasts-bike-lanes-lauds-rebate/docview/3119910847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+blasts+bike+lanes%2C+lauds+rebate%3A+Premier+appears+to+tip+hand+on+themes+for+a+spring+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Road-testing his campaign slogans for a possible spring election, Premier Doug Ford is railing against bike lanes on busy streets and boasting about rebate cheques for Ontarians.
Under fire in the legislature Wednesday from NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who was peppering the premier with questions about the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, he appeared to tip his hand on themes for a spring election.
"People know life is better in Ontario than it was six years ago," Ford chided Stiles.
"They have certainty now, they have jobs now, they're bringing home a paycheque that they never had before under your left-wing regime of 'don't build anything, let's kill businesses, let's all go around and hop on our bicycle' and go down the crowded streets that are just absolutely insanity right now on Bloor, on Yonge, on University," the premier said.
That's a reference to the bike lanes on major thoroughfares the Progressive Conservatives warn contribute to gridlock, which is why they tabled legislation Monday to "review" them.
"No, people want reasonable judgment, and that's what we're giving the people of Ontario. We're putting money back into their pockets instead of your greedy little pockets," he scolded the opposition New Democrats. Speaking later Wednesday to radio host Deb Hutton on Newstalk 1010, Ford emphasized "it's not the government's money, it's taxpayers' money."
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," he said.
As first revealed by the Star last week, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will unveil payments of at least $200 to all 16 million Ontario adults and children in his fall economic statement next Wednesday.
Bethlenfalvy was tight-lipped when pressed about the rebate cheques. "I'm not going to speculate what may or may not be in the fall economic statement, but what I can tell you is that since day one, our government has been focused on affordability, cost of living," he told reporters at Queen's Park.
Stiles said the Tories are "delusional" if they believe things are better today than they were when Ford took office in 2018.
"I'll tell you how people in Ontario are feeling right now after six years of this government: They're feeling stuck; they're struggling; they're stuck on wait-lists; they're stuck, yes, in traffic; they're stuck with a government that isn't interested in fixing their problems," she said.
The premier insisted to Newstalk 1010's Hutton that "we aren't against bike lanes," but said the government has been inundated with complaints about the bike lanes on Bloor and Yonge streets and University Avenue.
"It's insanity," he said. "Put them on the secondary roads and let the main arterial roads flow with the traffic."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 439 of 500

'You build safe streets, or people die.' Hundreds rally at Queen's Park to protest plan to tear up bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-build-safe-streets-people-die-hundreds-rally/docview/3119910793/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 440 of 500

PCs eye more nuclear power to meet rising demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-eye-more-nuclear-power-meet-rising-demand/docview/3119910791/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+eye+more+nuclear+power+to+meet+rising+demand&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians can expect more nuclear power and a "significant expansion" of energy efficiency programs for families and businesses, says Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce.
The changes from Premier Doug Ford's government will come in a new energy plan being developed for release early next year to meet a forecast surge in demand for electricity by 2050.
As environmentalists call for more renewable energy like solar and wind power because of falling prices for those technologies, Lecce told reporters Wednesday that "nuclear energy will be the overarching focus of our expansion plan."
That's in addition to extensions, refurbishment and additions planned or underway to nuclear generating stations at Pickering, Bruce and Darlington, where a small modular reactor is also being built, Lecce said before introducing his proposed Affordable Energy Act in the legislature.
The bill sets a framework for developing an "integrated" energy plan for the next 25 years involving nuclear, natural gas and gasoline as well as more charging stations for electric vehicles.
But the prospect of more fossil fuel use raised concerns among critics as the world combats climate change and its effects on the environment, from air quality to higher temperatures and flooding from increasingly severe storms.
"Every time they talk about this plan, they never talk about renewable, even though the lowest-cost source of electricity generation anywhere in the world right now is solar, wind and storage," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"The lowest-hanging fruit to rapidly increase electricity supply, and to do it at low cost, is putting solar panels on all these roofs that you see all over the GTA."
Opponents of more nuclear-generated power also raise concerns about huge price tags for nuclear plants and the long-term radioactive waste they produce.But Lecce did not rule out new nuclear plants or more new reactors at existing nuclear generating stations.
"We're going to need to bring forth additional options," Lecce said, describing nuclear power as the "only way we can reduce (carbon) emissions" while increasing the electricity supply to meet expected growth in the economy.
Last week, the province's Independent Electricity System Operator - which manages the day-to-day needs of the grid to keep the lights on and handles long-term planning - forecast the need for power will soar 75 per cent by 2050.
That's up from a previous forecast of a 60 per cent increase because of an explosion in energy-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence, population growth, growing electrification of cars, public transit, home heating, electric vehicle battery plants, and steelmaking as it transitions away from coal-fired to electric arc furnaces.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Nuclear energy; Expansion; Electricity; Alternative energy sources; Electric vehicles

Document 441 of 500

Cultural appropriation isn't the problem, it's how the entrepreneurs advertise their product

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cultural-appropriation-isnt-problem-how/docview/3119910720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cultural+appropriation+isn%27t+the+problem%2C+it%27s+how+the+entrepreneurs+advertise+their+product&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Simu Liu, bubble tea and cultural appropriation: How an episode of Dragons' Den backfired for a Canadian business, Oct. 17
If an Asian person were to make this same pitch, would there be a problem? People can improve a product without the cultural origin coming into question. Cultural origin does not define ownership; rather, good products are built off ideas and a series of improvements over time.
The root of the problem is not cultural appropriation. It's that the entrepreneurs claimed the content of bubble tea is unknown and unhealthy when their product is just as unhealthy, especially since they mentioned having an alcoholic version of their drink. The company claims to use three main ingredients, but if you Google their company, you can see that they use chemicals as well as added sugars you would see in other boba drinks.
Although this company has to make certain adjustments to the way they advertise their products, they should never have faced hate online. Simu Liu did well to acknowledge the online threats, but I disagree with his take on cultural appropriation.
Brandon Jaundoo, Ajax
Just because Ford can overrule municipal decisions doesn't mean he should
Doug Ford's war on bike lanes may help him get re-elected, but it won't solve gridlock, Oct. 22
Martin Regg Cohn scores again on his analysis of the divisive issue of bike lanes on city streets. Indeed, we ought not to take Premier Doug Ford's bait by putting our energy into a divisive and distracting debate on this issue. We all use public spaces for transportation: sometimes we use a vehicle, sometimes we bicycle, sometimes we walk, sometimes we use public transit, and sometimes we use a specialized mobility device, like a motorized scooter or an electric bike. Public spaces ought to be available to all of us, all the time. Cohn is absolutely correct when he writes that the responsibility for decisions on the use of roadways ought to remain at the municipal level. There are reasons for three levels of government. Just because Ford can overrule the decisions of municipal politicians is not a good enough reason for doing so. What will it be next week?
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Landlords get richer and Toronto gets duller
Toronto will keep losing treasures like my favourite magazine shop in the Annex if we don't figure out how to make space for one another, Oct. 23
Heather Mallick laments the impending loss of the terrific magazine shop Presse Internationale because of extortionate rent increases. So many small businesses have packed up and left after new owners jacked up the rent to impossible levels -at Presse Internationale, from $7,000 to $11,000/month. The fabric store The Workroom, until recently a fixture on Queen West, is another casualty of huge rent increases. Small local retailers give us choices and delightful variety that big box stores do not offer. The landlords may be richer (well, perhaps not The Workroom's - the space was still empty a year after the store packed up), but Toronto is duller and Torontonians are certainly less well off as a result.
Dilys Bowman, Toronto
Tunnel vision
I have the craziest, best idea ever that will reduce traffic and make Toronto the safest place for cyclists in the world: build a 44-km tunnel under Highway 401 for cyclists! Thousands of people would happily give up their cars for a weatherproof, safe ride to locations all over Toronto. Not only that, but it could also be built at a fraction of the cost of a vehicle tunnel.
John Cowles, Oakville


Is menopause now a disease?
The special section on menopause, Oct. 18
For eons women have been going through this life stage mostly without air conditioning and moisture wicking clothes, me included. But now comes a renewed campaign to persuade women that they need a drug, HRT, to manage this normal part of life. Pfizer, the pharma company that makes Premarin and Duavee, sources its HRT drugs from industrial pregnant mare farms. None of the articles, including the ad from Sinai Health, say anything about the torturous process used to extract urine. The mares are impregnated over and over. They spend their lives upright in tight stalls with catheter tubes, and their foals are forcibly removed right after birth. The foals are slaughtered for meat and so are the mares when they cease to be of use. Is this abuse of horses worth it to avoid some hot flashes? The story and ad say menopause can lead to bladder control issues and depression. I imagine men, too, get depressed when their age-related prostate problems cause bladder problems. The point is that we now look for a pill or medication for every natural part of aging, and companies like Pfizer promote menopause as a disease with inconvenient symptoms that need to be managed. Given all the truly life-threatening diseases that medical science has not been unable to figure out, I find it incredible that a state-of-the-art facility will be built to address menopause. And given the animal abuse required to manufacture HRT, I am appalled that any woman who knows this would still use it.
Igea Khan, Toronto

It's your job as a Raptor to try and win This Raptors season really is a throwback to the old days. Finally, Toronto is rebuilding again, Oct. 23
Jakob Poeltl of the Toronto Raptors states that "it makes no sense for us to try to win every single game" this season. He is wrong, it is in fact his job to try and win every game. If the fans choose to buy tickets to games this year, are they supposed to cheer for losses, so the Raptors can get a high draft pick?
John Rochester, Toronto
Safe, connected bike lanes should not be deferredFord government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads, Oct. 18
As bike lanes facing little usage are taking up lanes on major thoroughfares such as Bloor Street West, viable traffic lanes are blocked. Bike lanes provide a safe, alternative transit option when strategically placed and not built on major vehicle dense roadways where they are barely used.
It is concerning that the timeline for the province to render a decision on the proposed new bike lanes is not clearly stated. Many lanes were implemented during COVID and are overdue for an evaluation of their use and traffic impact. Toronto has already seen a significant increase in cyclist deaths, with a total of six deaths this year alone. The implementation of safe, connected bike lanes should not be deferred.
The government needs to figure out where bike lanes will be utilized best by listening to the residents and commuters of these roads. We are at a crossroads, and we need this plan to be made a higher priority.
Evelyn Rothgerber, Toronto
CAPTION: Canadian actor Simu Liu accused the Canadian bubble tea brand, Bobba of cultural appropriation of Asian culture on the Dragon's Den.

Document 442 of 500

Trudeau rebuffs challenge from group of MPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-rebuffs-challenge-group-mps/docview/3119910714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+rebuffs+challenge+from+group+of+MPs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rebuffed a dramatic challenge by a group of 24 Liberal MPs who endorsed a call for him to step down by Monday and trigger a leadership race, sources say. And yet, his insistence to them that he intends to stay on failed to dispel questions about whether he can remain as Liberal leader to fight the next election.
Trudeau faced his caucus critics behind closed doors Wednesday at a critical meeting where sources say B.C. MP Patrick Weiler went first to the microphone to relay contents of a letter from 24 "concerned MPs."
None of the 24 rebels signed their names to a document, said a government source. And while about 20 MPs came forward during the meeting to urge Trudeau to leave, the number fell short of advance speculation that the dissenters numbered up to 40 or more. That led the prime minister and his team to feel the internal revolt did not have a critical mass that would drive him out.
But a question mark still looms after several MPs emerged without a clear endorsement of his grip on the embattled Liberal party. All said the caucus was united in the desire to defeat the Pierre Poilievre-led Conservatives, but not all said that is possible under Trudeau. Some dodged questions about whether he should stay on, while his supporters called for an end to the damaging internal divisions.
Over the course of a nearly three-hour meeting described as "frank" and "very emotional," MPs vented their concerns. Trudeau spoke more than once, and said clearly he intends to stay on as leader, said caucus sources who spoke on condition they not be identified since caucus deliberations are intended to be private. Striding past a gaggle of reporters after the meeting, the prime minister would only say, "The Liberal party is strong and united."
Weiler did not respond to the Star's request for comment.
One of the backers of the letter, MP Wayne Long, said the prime minister "clearly" understood the message, and while he likes Trudeau personally, he feels no better about the party's prospects under him.
"I think we've done good things together," he said, adding that "I haven't changed my mind ... I think he needs to step down, but I think if anything today, there'll be a reflection. And ... it's really his decision, right? I mean, there's no real process to remove a leader."
Long, who is not running again, said that if Trudeau's critics are not satisfied by a response expected "soon" to their demands, they will have to make a choice. "This can't go on forever. There's got to be an end date here where people are going to either get on board the train to the next election, or get off."
Even Trudeau's supporters, like cabinet minister Marci Ien, conceded that the disagreements are not over. "I think it's going to take some time. But what was good about today is that people actually had their say."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said there was no ultimatum to MPs and no instruction to dissenters not to talk, but there is an expectation, as in any family, that "once everything is done and said, you move on. You unite."
Toronto MP Nate Erskine-Smith told reporters he spoke in caucus and urged Trudeau to take the dissent seriously, and his colleagues to end "the palace intrigue."
"The prime minister has to listen to the frustrations, and in some cases, really valid frustrations, of caucus colleagues, and incorporate that into changes moving forward, whether those are big changes or small changes or both," he said.
"And the second thing is, my colleagues need to turn the knives outwards and not inwards, and we need to focus on the most important thing, which is getting things done here in Parliament and taking the fight to Pierre Poilievre, because he's a disaster for this country."
Erskine-Smith predicted the talks would go on for days to come, with the presentation of a five-point election plan that was supposed to have been delivered Wednesday sidelined because of the internal turmoil.
Many other MPs said only there was a "good discussion" behind closed doors, while others avoided answering direct questions about whether they feel their caucus stands united behind Trudeau's leadership.
Afterwards, Trudeau was defiant and combative in question period, as the Conservatives mocked his internal troubles. Poilievre taunted the prime minister to call an election, saying the Liberal leader "cannot fix what he broke because his caucus is revolting." NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called Trudeau "distracted."
Trudeau also came under fire from a group of self-described grassroots Liberals who are circulating a petition that describes an "existential crisis" inside the party. The petition demands an urgent secret-ballot vote - both inside the caucus room and at the party's national executive - on whether Trudeau really should stay on in the face of months of bad polls that place his party well behind Poilievre's Conservatives.
The document - titled "Code Red Petition" - was started by a group of mostly young Liberals, said Andrew Perez, a Liberal strategist and political consultant acting as a spokesperson for the petition organizers. The petition is accompanied by a memorandum that details how the modern Liberal party is struggling in many corners of the country, and names Poilievre as "our enemy" who has harnessed the power of the internet to win support and rake in more donations than the federal Liberals.
"We are hoping that this petition lights a fire for the party leadership," said Perez, who added that "structural changes" are needed for the Liberals to improve fundraising and grassroots organizing.
While he declined to say how many people are behind the petition, he said it has been sent to "tens of thousands" of Liberals this week.
He also said the petition "is about much more than leadership," arguing the party brass should be accountable given events of recent months - including two byelection losses in long-safe Liberal seats, the departure of campaign director Jeremy Broadhurst and a host of cabinet ministers, and the recent caucus dissent.
The document also criticizes the Trudeau government's "policy problem," saying gross domestic product (GDP) per capita is too low and demanding fresh ideas to focus on productivity and economic growth. It calls for the party to address "the leadership issue" through a secret ballot vote in caucus and at the party's national executive, and says that if Trudeau decides to resign he should announce his choice as quickly as possible.
Sean Casey, an MP from Prince Edward Island who is among the three MPs who have publicly called for Trudeau to go, told reporters on his way into the caucus meeting that he wished there were a formal mechanism to hold such a vote.
Unlike the Conservatives, who ousted Erin O'Toole as leader in a caucus vote, the Liberals decided not to adopt parliamentary rules that give their caucus that power.
Before the meeting, Erskine-Smith said he would like to see some sort of future vote, possibly in Trudeau's caucus, to provide "finality" to the question of the prime minister's leadership.
"I think a secret-ballot vote in caucus, at some time in the foreseeable future, makes a certain amount of sense," said Erskine-Smith, who has also voiced support for a partywide vote on the leader's status.
With Parliament Hill abuzz with speculation about Trudeau's future, entrances to West Block were jammed with reporters and staffers keen to press MPs on what would happen inside the morning's high-stakes Liberal caucus meeting.
Francis Drouin, a Liberal MP from eastern Ontario, was among those to denounce the MPs involved in the push against Trudeau.
"At some point, to keep knifing like this? Like, put up or shut up," Drouin said.
Judy Sgro said the dissenters have damaged the Liberal party and should have privately raised their concerns with the prime minister.
"When you look divided, you look weak," she said. "It's not necessary to do what is going on today."
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall, Tonda MacCharles, Mark Ramzy and Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Subject: Grass roots movement; Petitions; Endorsements; Prime ministers; Members of Parliament; Political dissent; Political leadership

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Ford government says Ontario needs more nuclear power to tackle rising demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-says-ontario-needs-more-nuclear/docview/3120146280/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+says+Ontario+needs+more+nuclear+power+to+tackle+rising+demand&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians can expect more nuclear power and a "significant expansion" of energy efficiency programs for families and businesses, says Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce.
The changes from Premier Doug Ford's government will come in a new energy plan being developed for release early next year to meet a forecast surge in demand for electricity by 2050.
As environmentalists call for more renewable energy like solar and wind power because of falling prices for those technologies, Lecce told reporters Wednesday that "nuclear energy will be the overarching focus of our expansion plan."
That's in addition to extensions, refurbishment and additions planned or underway to nuclear generating stations at Pickering, Bruce and Darlington, where a small modular reactor is also being built, Lecce said before introducing his proposed Affordable Energy Act in the legislature.
The bill sets a framework for developing an "integrated" energy plan for the next 25 years involving nuclear, natural gas and gasoline as well as more charging stations for electric vehicles.
But the prospect of more fossil fuel use raised concerns among critics as the world combats climate change and its effects on the environment, from air quality to higher temperatures and flooding from increasingly severe storms.
"Every time they talk about this plan, they never talk about renewable, even though the lowest-cost source of electricity generation anywhere in the world right now is solar, wind and storage," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"The lowest-hanging fruit to rapidly increase electricity supply, and to do it at low cost, is putting solar panels on all these roofs that you see all over the GTA."
Opponents of more nuclear-generated power also raise concerns about huge price tags for nuclear plants and the long-term radioactive waste they produce.
But Lecce did not rule out new nuclear plants or more new reactors at existing nuclear generating stations.
"We're going to need to bring forth additional options," Lecce said, describing nuclear power as the "only way we can reduce (carbon) emissions" while increasing the electricity supply to meet expected growth in the economy.
Last week, the province's Independent Electricity System Operator - which manages the day-to-day needs of the grid to keep the lights on and handles long-term planning - forecast the need for power will soar 75 per cent by 2050.
That's up from a previous forecast of a 60 per cent increase because of an explosion in energy-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence, population growth, growing electrification of cars, public transit, home heating, electric vehicle battery plants, and steelmaking as it transitions away from coal-fired to electric arc furnaces.
The forecast increase in demand is roughly equal to adding four-and-a-half cities the size of Toronto to the grid, said Lecce.
Consultations will soon begin on the plan with the public and interested stakeholders, but Lecce would not commit to public hearings - something Schreiner said is necessary for "transparency," especially with Ford considering an early provincial election next spring instead of waiting until June 2026 as scheduled.
"This is a major, long-term energy plan, and for them to not conduct any public hearings around it, I don't think that's adequate," the Green leader added, citing a short timeline for the process.
He called for building codes to be changed to require new homes to be "solar ready" so that a larger electrical box and wiring is in place for homeowners who want to install solar panels on the roof. Electrical panels should also be big enough to handle heat pumps instead of gas furnaces, and to provide for the installation of EV chargers, he said.
CAPTION: Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce is shown in Clarington, Ont. on June 14, 2024.

Document 444 of 500

Doug Ford orders LCBO to try again after it orders paper bags from Quebec

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-orders-lcbo-try-again-after-paper-bags/docview/3120146132/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+orders+LCBO+to+try+again+after+it+orders+paper+bags+from+Quebec&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The deal was in the bag. Until it wasn't.
After Premier Doug Ford directed the LCBO last April to end its environmentally-conscious bag ban and bring back paper bags for customers, the liquor store did so.
But a $10-million contract for bags was signed with a Quebec company, and the government - disappointed in the choice - has issued a new directive to seek bids from Ontario bag makers.
"We've been very clear we want them to use Ontario companies ... I think Ontario companies are the best around," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy told reporters Wednesday.
"People want paper bags, which I think is very popular."
The LCBO said in a statement it plans to request bids from Ontario companies "at the soonest available opportunity."
The contract for Quebec-made bags from Rosenbloom Inc. remains in place and bags will be shipped to the LCBO's 670 stores "in the coming weeks," the liquor retailer added.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles questioned why the province doesn't have an established "Ontario first" procurement process.
"Ultimately, it's the government that's responsible for these decisions ... why is it they can't seem to do anything right?"
Other opposition parties said Fordshould be more concerned about major problems facing the province as he angles for an early election next spring instead of waiting for the slated date in June 2026.
"It is ridiculous that the premier is focused on bags at the LCBO when we have a record number of people experiencing homelessness, 2.5 million people can't access a doctor, our children are having to learn in overcrowded classrooms, and our infrastructure clearly isn't ready for the kind of floods we had last summer," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"This is serious stuff," added Liberal house leader John Fraser, MPP for Ottawa South. "He's got a serious job and his job is to take care of people's schools, their hospitals, their health care, and make sure they have an affordable place to live. It's maddening."
Urging customers to bring their own bags or buy reusable ones, the LCBO stopped supplying free paper bags in September 2023, saying the move would save 188,000 trees annually and divert 2,6654 tonnes of waste from landfill. Plastic bags were phased out in 2008.
Last spring, Ford questioned the merits of the end of free paper bags, calling them "an easily recyclable alternative to single-use plastic, which is why the LCBO adopted them in the first place."
With files from Kristin Rushowy
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford stands in the house as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Liquor

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Title: Doug Ford orders LCBO to try again after it orders paper bags from Quebec

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication date: Oct 23, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Doug Ford takes aim at bike lanes, touts rebate cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-takes-aim-at-bike-lanes-touts-rebate/docview/3120146090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+takes+aim+at+bike+lanes%2C+touts+rebate+cheques&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Road-testing his campaign slogans for a possible spring election, Premier Doug Ford is railing against bike lanes on busy streets and boasting about rebate cheques for Ontarians.
Under fire in the legislature Wednesday from NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who was peppering the premier with questions about the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, he appeared to tip his hand on themes for a spring election.
"People know life is better in Ontario than it was six years ago," Ford chided Stiles.
"They have certainty now, they have jobs now, they're bringing home a paycheque that they never had before under your left-wing regime of 'don't build anything, let's kill businesses, let's all go around and hop on our bicycle' and go down the crowded streets that are just absolutely insanity right now on Bloor, on Yonge, on University," the premier said.
That's a reference to the bike lanes on major thoroughfares the Progressive Conservatives warn contribute to gridlock, which is why they tabled legislation Monday to "review" them.
"No, people want reasonable judgment, and that's what we're giving the people of Ontario. We're putting money back into their pockets instead of your greedy little pockets," he scolded the opposition New Democrats.
Speaking later Wednesday to radio host Deb Hutton on Newstalk 1010, Ford emphasized "it's not the government's money, it's taxpayers' money."
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," he said.
As first revealed by the Star last week, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will unveil payments of at least $200 to all 16 million Ontario adults and children in his fall economic statement next Wednesday.
Bethlenfalvy was tight-lipped when pressed about the rebate cheques.
"I'm not going to speculate what may or may not be in the fall economic statement, but what I can tell you is that since day one, our government has been focused on affordability, cost of living," he told reporters at Queen's Park.
Stiles said the Tories are "delusional" if they believe things are better today than they were when Ford took office in 2018.
"I'll tell you how people in Ontario are feeling right now after six years of this government: They're feeling stuck; they're struggling; they're stuck on wait-lists; they're stuck, yes, in traffic; they're stuck with a government that isn't interested in fixing their problems," she said.
The premier insisted to Newstalk 1010's Hutton that "we aren't against bike lanes," but said the government has been inundated with complaints about the bike lanes on Bloor and Yonge streets and University Avenue.
"It's insanity," he said. "Put them on the secondary roads and let the main arterial roads flow with the traffic."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends question period in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Document 446 of 500

Justin Trudeau rebuffs dramatic challenge after 24 Liberal MPs urge him to step down by Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-rebuffs-dramatic-challenge-after/docview/3119662338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+rebuffs+dramatic+challenge+after+24+Liberal+MPs+urge+him+to+step+down+by+Monday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA- Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rebuffed a dramatic challenge by a group of 24 Liberal MPs who endorsed a call for him to step down by Monday and trigger a leadership race, sources say. And yet, his insistence to them he intends to stay on failed to dispel questions about whether he can remain as Liberal leader to fight the next election.
Trudeau faced his caucus critics behind closed doors Wednesday at a critical meeting where sources say B.C. MP Patrick Weiler went first to the microphone to relay contents of a letter from 24 "concerned MPs."
None of the 24 rebels signed their names to a document, a source said. And while about 20 MPs came forward during the meeting to urge Trudeau to leave, the number fell short of advance speculation the dissenters numbered up to 40 or more. That led the prime minister and his team to feel the internal revolt did not have a critical mass that would drive him out.
But a question mark still looms after several MPs emerged without a clear endorsement of his grip on the embattled Liberal party. All said the caucus was united in the desire to defeat the Pierre Poilievre-led Conservatives, but not all said that is possible under Trudeau. Some dodged questions about whether he should stay on, while his supporters called for an end to the damaging internal divisions.
Over the course of a nearly three-hour meeting described as "frank" and "very emotional," MPs vented their concerns. Trudeau spoke more than once, and said clearly he intends to stay on as leader, sources told the Star. Striding past a gaggle of reporters after the meeting, the prime minister would only say, "The Liberal party is strong and united."
Weiler did not respond to the Star's request for comment.
One of the backers of the letter, MP Wayne Long, said the prime minister "clearly" understood the message, and while he "likes" Trudeau personally, he feels no better about the party's prospects under him.
"I think we've done good things together," he said, adding that "I haven't changed my mind ... I think he needs to step down, but I think if anything today, there'll be a reflection. And ... it's really his decision, right? I mean, there's no real process to remove a leader."
Long, who is not running again, said that if Trudeau's critics are not satisfied by a response expected "soon" to their demands, they will have to make a choice. "This can't go on forever. There's got to be an end date here where people are going to either get on board the train to the next election, or get off."
Even Trudeau's supporters, like cabinet minister Marci Ien, conceded that the disagreements are not over. "I think it's going to take some time. But what was good about today is that people actually had their say."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said there was no ultimatum to MPs and no instruction to dissenters not to talk, but there is an expectation, like in any family, that "once everything is done and said, you move on. You unite."
Toronto MP Nate Erskine-Smith told reporters he spoke in caucus and urged Trudeau to take the dissent seriously, and his colleagues to end "the palace intrigue."
"The prime minister has to listen to the frustrations, and in some cases, really valid frustrations, of caucus colleagues, and incorporate that into changes moving forward, whether those are big changes or small changes or both," he said.
"And the second thing is, my colleagues need to turn the knives outwards and not inwards, and we need to focus on the most important thing, which is getting things done here in Parliament and taking the fight to Pierre Poilievre, because he's a disaster for this country."
Erskine-Smith predicted the talks would go on for days to come, with the presentation of a five-point election plan that was supposed to have been delivered Wednesday sidelined because of the internal turmoil.
Many other MPs said only there was a "good discussion" behind closed doors, while others avoided answering direct questions about whether they feel their caucus stands united behind Trudeau's leadership.
Afterwards, Trudeau was defiant and combative in question period, as the Conservatives mocked his internal troubles. Poilievre taunted the prime minister to call an election, saying the Liberal leader "cannot fix what he broke because his caucus is revolting." NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called Trudeau "distracted."
Trudeau also came under fire from a group of self-described grassroots Liberals who are circulating a petition that describes an "existential crisis" inside the party. The petition demands an urgent secret ballot vote - both inside the caucus room and at the party's national executive - on whether Trudeau really should stay on in the face of months of bad polls that place his party well behind Poilievre's Conservatives.
The petition - titled Code Red Petition - was started by a group of mostly young Liberals, said Andrew Perez, a Liberal strategist and political consultant acting as a spokesperson for the petition organizers. The petition is accompanied by a memorandum that details how the modern Liberal party is struggling in many corners of the country, and names Poilievre as "our enemy" who has harnessed the power of the internet to win support and rake in more donations than the federal Liberals.
"We are hoping that this petition lights a fire for the party leadership," said Perez, who added that "structural changes" are needed for the Liberals to improve fundraising and grassroots organizing.
While he declined to say how many people are behind the petition, he said it has been sent to "tens of thousands" of Liberals this week.
He also said the petition "is about much more than leadership," arguing the party brass should be accountable given events of recent months - including two byelection losses in long-safe Liberal seats, the departure of campaign director Jeremy Broadhurst and a host of cabinet ministers, and the recent caucus dissent.
The document also criticizes the Trudeau government's "policy problem," saying gross domestic product (GDP) per capita is too low and demanding fresh ideas to focus on productivity and economic growth. It calls for the party to address "the leadership issue" through a secret ballot vote in caucus and at the party's national executive, and that if Trudeau decides to resign he should announce his choice as quickly as possible.
Sean Casey, an MP from Prince Edward Island who is among the three MPs who have publicly called for Trudeau to go, told reporters on his way into the caucus meeting that he wished there was a formal mechanism to hold such a vote.
Unlike the Conservatives, who ousted Erin O'Toole as leader in a caucus vote, the Liberals decided not to adopt parliamentary rules that give their caucus that power.
Before the meeting, Erskine-Smith said he would like to see some sort of future vote, possibly in Trudeau's caucus, to provide "finality" to the question of the prime minister's leadership.
"I think a secret ballot vote in caucus, you know, at some time in the foreseeable future, makes a certain amount of sense," said Erskine-Smith, who has also voiced support for a partywide vote on the leader's status.
With Parliament Hill abuzz with speculation about Trudeau's future, entrances to West Block were jammed with reporters and staffers keen to press MPs on what will happen inside the morning's high stakes Liberal caucus meeting.
Francis Drouin, a Liberal MP from eastern Ontario, was among those to denounce the MPs involved in the push against Trudeau.
"At some point, to keep knifing like this? Like, put up or shut up," Drouin said.
Judy Sgro said the dissenters have damaged the Liberal party and should have privately raised their concerns with the prime minister.
"When you look divided, you look weak," she said. "It's not necessary to do what is going on today."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to caucus on Parliament Hill on Oct. 23, 2024 in Ottawa.

A threat to Justin Trudeau's leadership + Ford's rebate cheques aren't as simple as they sound

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, October 23 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on challenges to Justin Trudeau's leadership, another assault case tossed by a Toronto judge and complications for Doug Ford's rebate cheques.
DON'T MISSJustin Trudeau is facing down the biggest threat to his leadership yet
Dissenters are expected to make their concerns known when the Liberal caucus convenes for a meeting today. With cabinet ministers having unanimously pledged their support, top officials believe the majority of caucus have the prime minister's back. But as calls for Trudeau's resignation grow, they also concede nothing is off the table. Here's what the PM is up against.
Growing dissent: More than 20 Liberal MPs have signed a letter calling for Trudeau to step down. A Toronto judge is warning that more criminal cases could be thrown out if police don't pick up the pace, Jacques Gallant reports. Justice Joseph Callaghan tossed an otherwise straightforward assault case after Toronto police "inexplicably" took seven months to hand over a video of the complainant's statement, even after repeated requests from the Crown. The police investigation was finished in a single day - so why did it take so long?
Word from police: "There are numerous challenges related to the disclosure of video evidence," a police spokesperson said. "In response, we are streamlining the redaction process, investing in better tools and creating dedicated civilian teams to manage the volume."Dismissed: The incident is the latest in a string of criminal cases tossed out in recent months, largely due to a chronic lack of judges at the Toronto Superior Court.Doug Ford's rebate cheques aren't as simple as they sound
Ford promised to send cheques to every adult and child in Ontario - but getting it done is a whole other beast. Insiders tell Robert Benzie there are hurdles in tracking down the names and addresses of all 16 million Ontarians, as privacy laws forbid the government from using data from residents' OHIP, driver's licence or birth certificate records. Take a look at what they have to do instead.
ICYMI: The Ford government intends to send at least $200 to every Ontarian by the end of January or early February, the Star previously reported. Document 448 of 500

Canada's getting national pharmacare. Here's what it means for you

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-getting-national-pharmacare-heres-what/docview/3119506675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+getting+national+pharmacare.+Here%27s+what+it+means+for+you&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new national pharmacare plan could mean free access to birth control and diabetes medication across Canada - but there's still some deal-making to be done.
With the Liberal government's pharmacare plan enacted earlier this month, the government only needs to sign individual deals with the provinces to realize widespread access to diabetes medication, like insulin, and a broad array of contraceptives.
The legislation was the result of the now-dead NDP-Liberal alliance that was canned in early September - and with the prospect of an election looming around the corner, the new national pharmacare program could have an uncertain future.
Still, experts say, while far from an all-encompassing pharmacare program, the two types of medication covered as a pilot under the new legislation is an important step toward making essential medication more accessible.
Here's what you need to know about Canada's new pharmacare program.
What will be covered by the pharmacare plan?
For now, the government-paid plan will only cover birth control and diabetes medication, though the government has hinted that it could expand the program in the future. The specifics of which medications will be covered by pharmacare will come down to the federal government's negotiations with the provinces and territories.
Birth control
The government's pharmacare plan includes a long list of oral birth control pills, copper and hormonal intrauterine devices (IUDs), contraceptive injections, hormonal implants, hormonal vaginal rings and emergency contraceptives.
Oral contraceptives, without pharmacare, cost approximately $300 per year and IUDs (which are effective for five years) cost about $500, according to the federal government.
In Ontario, OHIP+ - which covers Ontarians 24 years old or younger not covered by private insurance - currently provides coverage for some forms of contraceptives.
You can check out the full list of contraceptives under consideration for coverage under the pharmacare plan in this press release from Health Canada.
Access to contraceptives is a "necessity in universal health care," Dr. Nadia Alam, a family doctor in Georgetown and former president of the Ontario Medical Association, said.
"For a number of reasons ranging from cost to intimate partner violence, women and people with a uterus don't have access to reliable birth control," Alam said. "Managing an unplanned pregnancy is far riskier than preventing it in the first place."
Diabetes medication
The government's proposed pharmacare plan would cover insulin, Metformin, combination medications and SGLT-2 inhibitors, all of which can range from $100 per year to $1,700 per year depending on the medication, according to the government.
In Ontario, OHIP+ covers various insulins and oral diabetic medications, while the province's assistive devices program covers the full cost of an insulin pump.
The full list of diabetes medications under consideration for coverage can be found online.
Almost one in 10 Canadians over the age of 20 have been diagnosed with diabetes, a StatCan report from 2023 found, and three in four Canadians diagnosed with diabetes use medication to manage their condition. And, according to Diabetes Canada, out-of-pocket costs for people diagnosed with diabetes ranged from about $10,000 per year to over $18,000 per year.
How much will it cost?
The Liberals have said the program - budgeted at almost $1.5 billion over five years - will provide government-paid coverage for diabetes medication and birth control, and could expand in the future.
A 2015 study published in the Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology Canada found that there were more than 180,700 unintended pregnancies annually in Canada, which cost the health care system $175 million per year through births, abortions and complications.
And, a 2017 study in the journal Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention in Canada estimated that the costs due to diabetes from 2011 to 2022 was more than $16 billion across the country.
For both birth control and diabetes medication, expanded coverage "literally saves lives and money," Steve Morgan, a population and public health professor at University of British Columbia, said.
The idea is that reducing the number of unwanted or unplanned pregnancies and increasing access to treatment for people with diabetes can improve health outcomes and reduce future strains on the health-care system, Morgan explained.
For the millions of Canadians living near or in poverty - a risk factor for chronic illness - pharmacare coverage of birth control and diabetes medication "is a fantastic start," Alam said.
When will the pharmacare plan go into effect?
Earlier in October, federal Health Minister Mark Holland said that formal negotiations with provinces can start now that the pharmacare bill has passed, but that "informal" talks have already been ongoing.
Holland told reporters Friday that he's confident Ottawa can sign deals with all provinces by next spring, describing talks that have happened so far as "very" positive.
British Columbia has already signed a memorandum of understanding with Ottawa, agreeing to work together on a program to cover diabetes medication, birth control and hormone replacement therapy for menopause.
Ontario's government, however, is still mulling over the fine print of the program.
"Ontario, like many provinces, has yet to receive any details on the federal government's proposal," said Health Minister Sylvia Jones's spokesperson Hannah Jensen by email on Oct. 11.
"Instead of calling on the provinces to strike a deal with no detail," Jensen added, "the federal government should focus on working collaboratively with provinces to adequately support health-care services across the country."
CAPTION: The national pharmacare plan was the result of the now defunct Liberal-NDP deal.

Subject: Provinces; Federal government; Hormone replacement therapy; Diabetes; Insulin; Birth control; Health care; Chronic illnesses

Location: Canada

Title: Canada's getting national pharmacare. Here's what it means for you

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 23, 2024

Document 449 of 500

It doesn't matter the time of day or night, or the mode of transportation we use. Getting around Toronto is always an exhausting nightmare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doesnt-matter-time-day-night-mode-transportation/docview/3119506552/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+doesn%27t+matter+the+time+of+day+or+night%2C+or+the+mode+of+transportation+we+use.+Getting+around+Toronto+is+always+an+exhausting+nightmare&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There isn't a good way to get around this city. And, like all drivers, transit riders, cyclists and pedestrians, I'm frustrated. I will speak for us and say we're tired as well because it always takes extra time to get anywhere.
Last Saturday night, around 11:30, I was travelling east on Highway 401 towards the Don Valley Parkway. As we approached Bayview Ave., it was a sea of red brake lights. The connection to the DVP is always bumper-to-bumper traffic. It doesn't matter the time of day or night; you are going to spend some time getting on the DVP. Mysteriously, the traffic eases at Eglinton Ave.
Last week, while travelling on Eglinton Ave. E., I found myself sitting in snarled traffic past Kennedy Rd. The roadway is restricted to two lanes, with TTC buses slowly making their way in the right lane, thanks to the new Eglinton Crosstown LRT.
The LRT tracks lay empty in the middle of the street with transit riders having no clue when they'll be able to jump on board because Metrolinx won't tell us. They'll let us in on the secret three months before the trains begin to run.
So, as of today, we're looking at late January 2025 at the earliest.
Shovels went into the ground in 2011, and 13 years later, we have pretty tracks with lovely stations (the one at Eglinton and Don Mills Rd. named Science Centre - hilarious) but no working rapid transit.
Might as well call it "will-we-ever-get-there" transit or "take-its-time" transit.
As of 2022, there were more than 6.4 million people in the Greater Toronto Area. Why do I feel like I'm fighting with half of them to get to my destination? Can someone please tell me how to traverse the GTA without getting choked in traffic?
Late last month, Premier Doug Ford had an idea. He vowed to construct a tunnel under the 401 from Brampton to Scarborough. The premier said, "This tunnel and expressway will cut gridlock, support economic growth and get people moving faster."
That is approximately 55 kilometres. The Eglinton Crosstown LRT is about 19 kilometres long. If the province can't get it together for less than 20 kilometres, how can we trust them to build what would be the world's longest tunnel?
Before Ontario starts renting more boring machines, I have some ideas. A few are kind of novel, but I think they would work.
How about getting the darn Eglinton Crosstown up and running? It's been more than a decade. Can we get that done before we start burrowing under the region's main thoroughfare? It's well past time to figure out how to make the region move in a way that doesn't frustrate everyone who takes the 401, DVP, or, God forbid, the Gardiner Expressway at almost any time of the day.
While we're talking about rapid transit, as a former Scarborough resident, can we talk about the now-defunct Scarborough LRT and the lack of transit in the northeast corner of the city? Since I moved to Toronto back in 1998 - over 25 years ago - politicians of all stripes have discussed the need for transit in Scarborough and have done nothing. Ask anyone who lives east of Scarborough Town Centre how easy it is to travel around the area or to the subway without a car.
The lack of proper transit in pockets of the city and wasting time to study cockamamie ideas like tunnelling under Highway 401 ignores the real issues of traffic congestion and dearth of good, reliable public transportation.
The Ford government is planning to send out at least $200 per Ontarian sometime next year - a gift before the provincial election. Hmm ... maybe we can use the extra $200 for an Uber somewhere like your local polling station.
CAPTION: Then Ontario Premier Dalton McGuinty and then Toronto Mayor Rob Ford get a tour while they visit the Eglinton-Scarborough Crosstown LRT construction site at Keelesdale Park, 2801 Eglinton Ave West on Nov. 9, 2011.An empty Ionview station close to Kennedy Avenue in May, 2023.

Subject: Traffic

Giving 16 million Ontarians $200 isn't so simple: Privacy laws mean Ford government will have to work with CRA to collect names, addresses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/giving-16-million-ontarians-200-isnt-so-simple/docview/3119506402/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Giving+16+million+Ontarians+%24200+isn%27t+so+simple%3A+Privacy+laws+mean+Ford+government+will+have+to+work+with+CRA+to+collect+names%2C+addresses&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All signs point to Premier Doug Ford calling an early spring election.
Sources say the province is working closely with Canada Revenue Agency officials to ensure "tax rebate cheques" are mailed out at the end of January or early February.
Ford, who enjoys a healthy lead in public-opinion polls, wants the cash in people's hands before triggering an election in late March or early April, more than a year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote.
As first revealed by the Star last Wednesday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will formally unveil the payments of around $200 to all 16 million Ontario adults and children in his Oct. 30 fall economic statement.
But insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss a program that has yet to be officially announced, say collecting the names and addresses of every Ontarian is harder than it sounds.
Strict privacy laws forbid the government from using provincial health data kept by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan, drivers' licence information held by the Ministry of Transportation or birth certificate records.
That means Queen's Park will be forced to rely mostly upon the CRA for help in determining who should receive cheques and where they can be mailed.
Progressive Conservative sources say Ford has made it clear the money must flow early in the new year, when people's paycheques are smaller due to employment insurance deductions and when holiday credit card bills are coming due.
"Put money into people's pockets and they'll go out and do something as simple as going out for dinner, maybe buying your kids sneakers or clothes, or maybe putting a little renovation on your house," the premier told 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon speech last Thursday.
A spring election would take place immediately after the end of per-vote public subsidies for political parties that the premier is phasing out as of Dec. 31.
Each quarter, the Tories receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000 and the Ontario Party $53,000.
Even though his party benefits the most from the subsidies, Ford believes the PCs can continue to out-fundraise their rivals from individuals' donations.
The premier, who has long opposed public funding of political parties, would like to force his rivals to spend more of their precious time raising money to make up for the shortfall.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Tuesday that the Tories "intend to go to the polls early no matter what the people of Ontario want," and warned the plan could backfire as it did in 1990 for premier David Peterson's Liberals, who faced voters early and lost to Bob Rae's New Democrats.
"The last thing they want is the government to call an unnecessary election that's going to cost millions of dollars," said Stiles, noting Ford "already has a majority" and can pass his agenda through the house.
Peterson is now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said there's only one reason Ford would want to face voters sooner rather than later: the RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"I really believe they're trying to get ahead of the RCMP investigation, which, as we now know, is starting to speak to the core insiders. They're running frantically away from whatever could be revealed by the RCMP," she said.
Crombie said the rebate cheques are "just another gimmick" that could cost the treasury $3.2 billion.
"I've already announced that we would look at a sustainable tax cut for families, middle-income earners, so people could see savings each and every week in their paycheque rather than a one-time gimmick, which is your money being used to buy your vote," she said.
Recalling "the cautionary tale" that happened to Peterson's Liberals 34 years ago, Crombie said Ford would need to explain to voters why he needs a $150-million early election for a new mandate.
"When you have a majority government, there isn't anything - no piece of legislation - you can't get through the house, so it's going to be tough to create that narrative of why it's necessary to go now."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "the government doesn't have any rationale for an early election other than its own political self interest."
"We're in a housing crisis, our health-care system is collapsing, our students are learning in overcrowded classrooms and this government wants to call an election rather than putting forward solutions when they still have another year left in their mandate. It makes no sense."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Cost control; Scandals; Voters; Subsidies; Courthouses; Political parties

Highway 401 tunnel talk buries the real solutions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/highway-401-tunnel-talk-buries-real-solutions/docview/3119504600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Highway+401+tunnel+talk+buries+the+real+solutions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There isn't a good way to get around this city. And, like all drivers, transit riders, cyclists and pedestrians, I'm frustrated. I will speak for us and say we're tired as well because it always takes extra time to get anywhere.
Last Saturday night, around 11:30, I was travelling east on Highway 401 toward the Don Valley Parkway. As we approached Bayview Avenue, it was a sea of red brake lights. The connection to the DVP is always bumper-to-bumper traffic. It doesn't matter the time of day or night; you are going to spend some time getting on the DVP. Mysteriously, the traffic eases at Eglinton Avenue.
Last week, while travelling on Eglinton Avenue East, I found myself sitting in snarled traffic past Kennedy Road. The roadway is restricted to two lanes, with TTC buses slowly making their way in the right lane, thanks to the new Eglinton Crosstown LRT.
The LRT tracks lay empty in the middle of the street with transit riders having no clue when they'll be able to jump on board because Metrolinx won't tell us. They'll let us in on the secret three months before the trains begin to run.
So, as of today, we're looking at late January 2025 at the earliest.
Shovels went into the ground in 2011, and 13 years later, we have pretty tracks with lovely stations (the one at Eglinton and Don Mills Road named Science Centre - hilarious) but no working rapid transit.
Might as well call it "will-we-ever-get-there" transit or "take-its-time" transit.
As of 2022, there were more than 6.4 million people in the Greater Toronto Area. Why do I feel like I'm fighting with half of them to get to my destination? Can someone please tell me how to traverse the GTA without getting choked in traffic?
Late last month, Premier Doug Ford had an idea. He vowed to construct a tunnel under the 401 from Brampton to Scarborough. The premier said, "This tunnel and expressway will cut gridlock, support economic growth and get people moving faster."

Since I moved to Toronto back in 1998 - more than 25 years ago - politicians of all stripes have discussed the need for transit in Scarborough and have done nothing. Ask anyone who lives east of Scarborough Town Centre how easy it is to travel around the area or to the subway without a car.
The lack of proper transit in pockets of the city and wasting time to study cockamamie ideas like tunnelling under Highway 401 ignores the real issues of traffic congestion and dearth of good, reliable public transportation.
The Ford government is planning to send out at least $200 per Ontarian sometime next year - a gift before the provincial election. Hmm ... maybe we can use the extra $200 for an Uber somewhere like your local polling station.
Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.

Stiles says Jama won't run as NDP candidate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stiles-says-jama-wont-run-as-ndp-candidate/docview/3119504515/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stiles+says+Jama+won%27t+run+as+NDP+candidate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles has ruled out taking back her controversial former MPP Sarah Jama as a candidate.
"As far as I know, and I'm the leader, it won't be her," Stiles said Tuesday on CBC Radio's "Metro Morning."
She had been asked by host David Common about the prospect of the Independent MPP for Hamilton Centre returning to the NDP fold for the next provincial election amid speculation Premier Doug Ford will call an early vote next year instead of waiting until the scheduled June 2026 date.
Stiles ejected Jama from the New Democratic caucus last October for "acting unilaterally too many times" and "undermining our work" in the wake of the Oct. 7, 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and that country's retaliatory strikes on Gaza.
Jama has been sitting as an Independent MPP following her ouster, which split the NDP caucus and alienated staunch supporters in a riding that has been solidly New Democrat for years.
Asked last month by the Star whether she would consider returning to the New Democrats, Jama replied in a Sept. 27 email, "I am prepared to run as an Independent, though I have always kept all options open and will continue to do so."
Jama declined to comment at the legislature on Tuesday.
"I'm not talking today," she said.
Stiles, who last month refused to explicitly rule out taking back Jama as a candidate, was asked by reporters after the legislature's daily question period about her comments four hours earlier on Metro Morning.
But she repeatedly refused to use the same wording in her answer, and was less definitive.
"This is not part of the conversation right now that we're having," Stiles said of Jama.
"The member is not a member of our caucus. She's confirmed she's running as an Independent, and we will be running NDP candidates in every riding ... our aim in the next election is to defeat this Conservative government."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 453 of 500

Ford's war on bike lanes all about votes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-war-on-bike-lanes-all-about-votes/docview/3119504500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+war+on+bike+lanes+all+about+votes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has seen the enemy and it is thus:
Too many little-used bike lanes getting in the way of too many overused car lanes.
Of course, if you are a cyclist or leftist, that proposition is lunacy or heresy. But the premier is nothing if not a provocateur of progressives.
And he is not as dumb as he looks to them.
There's a reason his Progressive Conservative government introduced unprecedented legislation this week to rein in municipal bike lanes and potentially restore lost car lanes.
Ford's Tories see bike paths as political wedges that will pay dividends on the campaign trail. That's because fair-weather cyclists will never match the number of all-weather motorists stuck in the GTA's endless traffic snarls.
What makes this rightist-populist politician different from his leftist-righteous adversaries is that Ford can see around corners. By contrast, the bicycle brigade can't see what's coming down the path - which makes them their own worst enemies.
At city hall, bike lanes are seen as infinitely beautiful. The more the better.
Speaking personally, as both columnist and cyclist who uses bike lanes more than most, I know that you can have too much of a good thing in a bad location. (Fun fact: As a summer student at the Edmonton Journal decades ago, I wrote a weekly cycling column.)
I get the bicycle vision. But blinkers are bad for situational awareness - whether you're politicking or pedalling.
In the real world, traffic management is a two-way street. If you can't see the other side, you are headed for a crash.
What makes this political play so exasperating is the posturing and posing on both sides of the divide. For all the premier's huffing and puffing about fighting the war against the car, his over-the-top battle against bike lanes ultimately won't fix our underlying traffic problems.
It's more about distraction than congestion. It's a way to look away from the bigger jam that politicians refuse to face.
Bike lanes have become a symbolic bogeyman. Undoing them is not a serious solution.
As a thought experiment, suppose our all-powerful premier were to wave his wand and win this wedge, peeling bike lanes off Bloor Street West or Yonge Street. It might be the right thing to do, but by any measure it would have a marginal effect on travel times - a few minutes at most.
If the premier were serious, if Mayor Olivia Chow were serious, if commuters and voters were serious, they would steer the city towards more serious and substantive remedies instead of driving us to distraction.
Start with parking. Almost every major street is bogged down by an inert lane of locked vehicles - a parking lot shaped like an asphalt ribbon. In summer, some of those spots are repurposed into CaféTO tables whose time, like COVID, has come and gone (these spots were a good idea in mid-pandemic when traffic flows were minimal, but traffic is back).
When you add up protected bike lanes, parking lanes and CaféTO tables, you have major streets repurposed into multiplexes - leaving just a single lane of clogged traffic in each direction.
To be sure, there is a political constituency for each of these competing claims and lanes: local businesses crave the foot traffic from parking spots; restaurateurs revel in their overflow seating on asphalt; cyclists savour the freedom to sail past the congestion.
But someone has to tell everyone that you can't have it all.
Progressive proponents of protected bike lanes argue they save lives. They surely do, but if safety is so important, why does Toronto encourage the explosive growth in bike sharing knowing that so many users won't use helmets - leaving them perilously vulnerable to head injuries at intersections or crossing streetcar tracks? Why do we allow high-speed e-bike users with pretend pedals on those same bike lanes to overtake regular pedallers or menace pedestrians?
Safety, it seems, only goes so far.
To be clear, I love zooming along University Avenue's abandoned bike lanes, which are so much wider than the parallel north-south bike lanes a stone's throw away on Beverley Street. But I feel guilty looking at the traffic jams created outside our biggest hospital cluster - it feels like overkill in the name of bicycle safety.
While Ford might be right on the details, might doesn't make right in a democracy. The bottom line is that the province should stay in its own lane, rather than meddle from on high in matters of municipal jurisdiction.
If Toronto politicians want to tie traffic in knots, let them face voters and commuters at election time. Yet the premier can't resist the impulse to bigfoot the mayor, simply because he can.
Legally and constitutionally, the city is a creature of the province. He has the power to pass legislation banning bike lanes, just as he has the hubris to boast of digging a fantastical tunnel under Highway 401.
All along, Ford casts himself as the fearless defender of commuters, fending off the putative war against the car. In truth, he is waging - and winning - wedge politics, but it won't get us where we need to go.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford sees bike lanes as political wedges that will pay dividends on the campaign trail, Martin Regg Cohn writes, because fair-weather cyclists will never match the number of motorists in the GTA.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Automobile drivers; Voters; Politics; Traffic congestion; Traffic accidents & safety; Bicycling; Roads & highways
