This is the real reason our megaprojects keep going off the rails, Oct. 21 Re: Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 18 Re: We are progressive Jews who have been let down by our community leaders, Oct. 20

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3119503518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Re%3A+Ford+government+unveils+more+details+on+plan+to+curb+new+bike+lanes+on+major+roads%2C+Oct.+18+Re%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+bill+to+limit+new+bike+lanes+will+also+%27review%27+existing+ones%2C+Oct.+21+Re%3A+How+property+flippers+transformed+an+entire+street+of+Toronto+%27strawberry+box%27+homes%2C+Oct.+21+Re%3A+I+spent+12+years+as+the+public+voice+of+Metrolinx.+This+is+the+real+reason+our+megaprojects+keep+going+off+the+rails%2C+Oct.+21+Re%3A+Doug+Ford+poised+to+send+out+pre-election+cheques+to+16+million+Ontarians%2C+Oct.+18+Re%3A+We+are+progressive+Jews+who+have+been+let+down+by+our+community+leaders%2C+Oct.+20&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bike policy is ageist, disablist
Re: Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads, Oct. 18
Toronto's bike lane policy was oversold and has, unfortunately, underperformed. I was a supporter, but the evidence tells me we need a serious rethink. The bike lanes, as designed, are a dangerous barrier to the disabled and the elderly. The bike lanes make no accommodation for them. Driving a disabled or elderly relative to an appointment on a major artery is a nightmare. With their canes and walkers, they have to navigate the bike lane barriers, the bike lanes themselves, and cyclists. A 2019 staff study underpinning the bike policy suggested that 63 per cent of respondents were comfortable with bike lanes on major arteries. I was among them. I wonder how far that support has fallen since? The tragedy is that the majority of Torontonians, me included, welcomed the bike lane expansion. Unfortunately, our goodwill has been frittered away by badly executed and conceived policies implemented by tin-eared politicians. Mayor Olivia Chow should address this issue directly.
Mario Possamai, Toronto
No able-bodied Torontonian has any need for a car
Re: Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones, Oct. 21
Every single word written on the subject of traffic congestion is a fool's errand. Trying to overlay a personal vehicle structure on top of public transport, police, fire and ambulance on top of a design and build from the horse and buggy age will never work - no matter how many tunnels we build. No able-bodied Torontonian has any need for a car.
Kevin Bowen, Toronto
House flippers another reason for housing crisis
Re: How property flippers transformed an entire street of Toronto 'strawberry box' homes, Oct. 21
I really hope you are not advocating or educating on the positives of real estate flipping. While I admire the enthusiasm for making a successful career, this attitude of making profit out of housing is one of the reasons we are now in a housing crisis. Flippers are grabbing the only affordable option many of us have to own a house. Maybe we like creaky hardwood floors or maybe we would like to do some of the renovations ourselves, but one thing's for sure: As long as we allow this rampant greed, the middle class will be left with fewer and fewer options and eventually our parks will be filled with more and more tents.
Susan Ditchburn, Toronto
Why no subway service for marathoners?
For an organization that is trying to improve customer service, the TTC failed the city on Sunday morning by not starting the subway service earlier than 8 a.m., so participants in the Toronto Waterfront Marathon could easily get to the 8 a.m. start on University Avenue. This affected thousands of runners and their families.
Lorne Todgham, Toronto
Tendering process has
many flaws
Re: I spent 12 years as the public voice of Metrolinx. This is the real reason our megaprojects keep going off the rails, Oct. 21
The tendering process for public projects is long and tedious, and one is usually obliged to accept the lowest bid, even where one knows it's not the optimal one. The hoops one must jump through to be allowed to accept anything other than the lowest bid are mind-numbing. There is a valid reason for this rule, but it often bites one in the posterior. Of course, the bidders are aware of this and will lowball the cost and put in tricky or ambiguous language. This gives them an opportunity to later say things like "yes, but we didn't specifically state that it includes 100 per cent of that" or other similar excuses. This means that the organization must either put up more money or accept longer time (or both) or fight them in court. Since fighting them in court is long, expensive and tedious, the organization will usually cave in. That's been my experience. Very few come in on time and on budget. Not surprising that megaprojects "take too long and cost too much."
Rita Maio, Woodbridge
I'll donate my Ford cheque
to Crombie
Re: Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 18
I think it's a great idea and he should do this very soon. Once we all receive them and cash them, we can then turn around and send the money to Bonnie Crombie and the Liberals and bring them back to power.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
A two-state solution is the only solution
Re: We are progressive Jews who have been let down by our community leaders, Oct. 20
Overall, I was glad to see the piece by Bailey Greenspon, Jonathan Sas and Sam Hersh calling out a number of mainstream Jewish organizations for lending uncritical support to Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's conduct of the war.
I agree that Jews need all our organizations to support Israel's right and need to defend itself from a massive, seven-front Iranian-funded assault on Israel's existence, while denouncing and working against the sinister agenda of Netanyahu's messianic supporters to smash the Palestinians, resettle Gaza and complete the annexation of the West Bank. But I was dismayed to see no mention of the many major organizations and movements, in Israel-Palestine and here, that do want a ceasefire, that oppose the West Bank occupation and are fighting plans to resettle Gaza. These, I believe, represent a very large part of the diaspora and Israeli community. Yet, aside from the occasional mention of protests in Israel, who would ever know about them from our main news platforms and even this story? These are the only forces that can lead to a peaceful outcome in the Holy Land as they shape political leadership on both Israeli and Palestinian sides. It has been a source of unbelievable frustration that they remain invisible, while those uncritical of the annexationist agenda and the anti-Israel, pro-Hamas forces ("from the river to the sea," and all who protest under that banner) are given all the space. This distorts reality and massively undermines the two-state path, and it needs to change. Please feature stories about these groups.
Varda Burstyn, Peterborough

Document 455 of 500

Province secures two temporary sites: Exhibits at Sherway Gardens and Harbourfront Centre set to open in November, December

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-secures-two-temporary-sites/docview/3119503450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+secures+two+temporary+sites%3A+Exhibits+at+Sherway+Gardens+and+Harbourfront+Centre+set+to+open+in+November%2C+December&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Science Centre is moving to two temporary locations - at Harbourfront Centre and Sherway Gardens - after its Don Mills location was controversially shut down last June over concerns about its structural safety.
The Sherway location will open to the public in November, while the Harbourfront location will open in December.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said in a written state-ment that "the Ontario Science Centre has been inspiring young minds across the province for decades.
With exhibits at CF Sherway Gardens and Harbourfront Centre set to open soon, we are ensuring that more children remain engaged and excited to learn about science."
The change, however, did not sit well with critics.
The Ford government's "piecemeal policies have literally left the Science Centre in pieces," said Liberal MPP Adil Shamji, who represents Don Valley East.
"Carving the Science Centre into two and situating it along already congested traffic corridors will ensure that it becomes a shadow of its former self, and less accessible than ever before," he said of the locations in Toronto's west end and waterfront.
The Science Centre was abruptly shuttered four months ago after two reports found the building had "failing infrastructure, including critical roof issues, building systems and an inaccessible pedestrian bridge."
Critics have said while the reports cited concerns in some areas, neither called for an immediate shutdown.
Surma has said "it is unfortunate that we are in this circumstance - it is terrible. None of us wanted to make a decision like this."
But given the cost - an estimated $500 million - and length of time needed to make repairs, as well as the fact that the centre was already slated to move to a smaller space at Ontario Place in 2028, she has said it was the only choice.
Shamji, however, noted that both engineering reports said the Don Mills building could be salvaged.
The Sherway Gardens location will feature an interactive LEGO skyscraper exhibit as well as Circus!, which looks at the science behind the carnival tricks - an exhibit created by the centre that has been picked up around the globe.
KidSpark, the popular hands-on exhibit for children, will be relocated to Harbourfront.
The Science Centre will also offer pop-up exhibits across the province.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the shutdown "should never have happened in the first place."
"This has always been about (Premier) Doug Ford needed some kind of an excuse to pad his Ontario Place project, his luxury spa down there," she added.
"They've shut down a world-renowned jewel."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Shutdowns; Science

Business indexing term: Subject: Shutdowns

Company / organization: Name: Ontario Science Centre; NAICS: 712110

Toronto's bike policy is ageist and disablist

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-bike-policy-is-ageist-disablist/docview/3119503371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+bike+policy+is+ageist+and+disablist&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads, Oct. 18
Toronto's bike lane policy was oversold and has, unfortunately, underperformed. I was a supporter, but the evidence tells me we need a serious rethink.The bike lanes, as designed, are a dangerous barrier to the disabled and the elderly. The bike lanes make no accommodation for them. Driving a disabled or elderly relative to an appointment on a major artery is a nightmare. With their canes and walkers, they have to navigate the bike lane barriers, the bike lanes themselves, and cyclists, who far too often, in my experience, seem oblivious to their plight. This is much worse in winter when ice and snow add to the hazards.
A 2019 staff study underpinning the bike policy suggested that 63 per cent of respondents were comfortable with bike lanes on major arteries. I was among them. I wonder how far that support has fallen since. I doubt the Ford government would have acted if their internal polling had shown otherwise. The tragedy is that the majority of Torontonians, me included, welcomed the bike lane expansion. Unfortunately, our goodwill has been frittered away by badly executed and conceived policies implemented by tin-eared politicians. Mayor Olivia Chow should address this issue directly.
Mario Possamai, Toronto

No able-bodied Torontonian has any need for a car. Those who complain about traffic congestion are apparently those who have made monumentally bad job search/life decisions.

I spent 12 years as the public voice of Metrolinx. This is the real reason our megaprojects keep going off the rails, Oct. 21
The tendering process for public projects is long and tedious, and one is usually obliged to accept the lowest bid, even where one knows it's not the optimal one. The hoops one must jump through to be allowed to accept anything other than the lowest bid are mind-numbing. There is a valid reason for this rule, but it often bites one in the posterior. This means that the organization must either put up more money or accept longer time (or both) or fight them in court. Since fighting them in court is long, expensive and tedious, the organization will usually cave in. That's been my experience . Very few come in on time and on budget. Not surprising that megaprojects "take too long and cost too much."

Document 457 of 500

Doug Ford's government finds that giving every Ontarian a cheque isn't as simple as it sounds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-finds-that-giving-every/docview/3119781164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+finds+that+giving+every+Ontarian+a+cheque+isn%27t+as+simple+as+it+sounds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All signs point to Premier Doug Ford calling an early spring election.
Sources say the province is working closely with Canada Revenue Agency officials to ensure "tax rebate cheques" are mailed out at the end of January or early February.
Ford, who enjoys a healthy lead in public-opinion polls, wants the cash in people's hands before triggering an election in late March or early April, more than a year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote.
As first revealed by the Star last Wednesday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will formally unveil the payments of around $200 to all 16 million Ontario adults and children in his Oct. 30 fall economic statement.
But insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss a program that has yet to be officially announced, say collecting the names and addresses of every Ontarian is harder than it sounds.
Strict privacy laws forbid the government from using provincial health data kept by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan, drivers' licence information held by the Ministry of Transportation, or birth certificate records.
That means Queen's Park will be forced to rely mostly upon the CRA for help in determining who should receive cheques and where they can be mailed.
Progressive Conservative sources say Ford has made it clear the money must flow early in the new year, when people's paycheques are smaller due to Employment Insurance deductions and when holiday credit card bills are coming due.
"Put money into people's pockets and they'll go out and do something as simple as going out for dinner, maybe buying your kids sneakers or clothes, or maybe putting a little renovation on your house," the premier told 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon speech last Thursday.
"And they might do that because they have money in their pockets. The same with businesses. If you put money into businesses, they'll invest into their people, into technology, into their infrastructure, and they grow. That's what creates economic strength," said Ford.
"I risk stealing Peter Bethlenfalvy's thunder if I get into the fall economic statement. I'm sure the Toronto Star knows it already."
A spring election would take place immediately after the end of per-vote public subsidies for political parties that the premier is phasing out as of Dec. 31.
Each quarter, the Tories receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000.
Even though his party benefits the most from the subsidies, Ford believes the PCs can continue to out-fundraise their rivals from individuals' donations.
The premier, who has long opposed public funding of political parties, would like to force his rivals to spend more of their precious time raising money to make up for the shortfall.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Tuesday that the Tories "intend to go to the polls early no matter what the people of Ontario want," and warned the plan could backfire as it did in 1990 for premier David Peterson's Liberals, who faced voters early and lost to Bob Rae's New Democrats.
"The last thing they want is the government to call an unnecessary election that's going to cost millions of dollars," said Stiles, noting Ford "already has a majority" and can pass his agenda through the house.
Peterson is now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said there's only one reason Ford would want to face voters sooner rather than later: the RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"I really believe they're trying to get ahead of the RCMP investigation, which, as we now know, is starting to speak to the core insiders. They're running frantically away from whatever could be revealed by the RCMP," she said.
Crombie said the rebate cheques are "just another gimmick" that could cost the treasury $3.2 billion.
"I've already announced that we would look at a sustainable tax cut for families, middle-income earners, so people could see savings each and every week in their paycheque rather than a one-time gimmick, which is your money being used to buy your vote," she said.
Recalling "the cautionary tale" that happened to Peterson's Liberals 34 years ago, Crombie said Ford would need to explain to voters why he needs a $150-million early election for a new mandate.
"When you have a majority government, there isn't anything - no piece of legislation - you can't get through the house, so it's going to be tough to create that narrative of why it's necessary to go now."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "the government doesn't have any rationale for an early election other than its own political self interest."
"We're in a housing crisis, our health-care system is collapsing, our students are learning in overcrowded classrooms and this government wants to call an election rather than putting forward solutions when they still have another year left in their mandate. It makes no sense."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives at the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Cost control; Voters; Subsidies; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost control Subsidies

Title: Doug Ford's government finds that giving every Ontarian a cheque isn't as simple as it sounds

Document 458 of 500

NDP leader says Sarah Jama won't be her candidate in next Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-leader-says-sarah-jama-wont-be-her-candidate/docview/3119781145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leader+says+Sarah+Jama+won%27t+be+her+candidate+in+next+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles has ruled out taking back her controversial former MPP Sarah Jama as a candidate.
"As far as I know, and I'm the leader, it won't be her," Stiles said Tuesday on CBC Radio's Metro Morning.
She had been asked by host David Common about the prospect of the Independent MPP for Hamilton Centre returning to the NDP fold for the next provincial election amid speculation Premier Doug Ford will call an early vote next year instead of waiting until the scheduled June 2026 date.
Stiles ejected Jama from the New Democratic caucus last October for "acting unilaterally too many times" and "undermining our work" in the wake of the Oct. 7, 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and that country's retaliatory strikes on Gaza.
Jama has been sitting as an Independent MPP following her ouster, which split the NDP caucus and alienated staunch supporters in a riding that has been solidly New Democrat for years.
Asked last month by the Star whether she would consider returning to the NDP, Jama replied in a Sept. 27 email, "I am prepared to run as an Independent, though I have always kept all options open and will continue to do so."
Jama declined to comment at the legislature on Tuesday.
"I'm not talking today," she said.
Stiles, who last month refused to explicitly rule out taking back Jama as a candidate, was asked by reporters after the legislature's daily question period about her comments four hours earlier on Metro Morning.
But she repeatedly refused to use the same wording in her answer, and was less definitive.
"This is not part of the conversation right now that we're having," Stiles said of Jama.
"The member is not a member of our caucus. She's confirmed she's running as an Independent, and we will be running NDP candidates in every riding ... our aim in the next election is to defeat this Conservative government."
Peter Graefe, an associate professor of political science at McMaster University in Hamilton, has told the Star that running an NDP candidate against Jama could split the progressive vote and allow a Liberal or Progressive Conservative to win the riding.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on May 6, 2024.

Document 459 of 500

Doug Ford's war on bike lanes may help him get re-elected, but it won't solve gridlock

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-war-on-bike-lanes-may-help-him-get-re/docview/3119503452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+war+on+bike+lanes+may+help+him+get+re-elected%2C+but+it+won%27t+solve+gridlock&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has seen the enemy and it is thus:
Too many little-used bike lanes getting in the way of too many overused car lanes.
Of course, if you are a cyclist or leftist, that proposition is lunacy or heresy. But the premier is nothing if not a provocateur of progressives.
And he is not as dumb as he looks to them.
There's a reason his Progressive Conservative government introduced unprecedented legislation this week to rein in municipal bike lanes and potentially restore lost car lanes.
Ford's Tories see bike paths as political wedges that will pay dividends on the campaign trail. That's because fair-weather cyclists will never match the number of all-weather motorists stuck in the GTA's endless traffic snarls.
What makes this rightist-populist politician different from his leftist-righteous adversaries is that Ford can see around corners. By contrast, the bicycle brigade can't see what's coming down the path - which makes them their own worst enemies.
At city hall, bike lanes are seen as infinitely beautiful. The more the better.
Speaking personally, as both columnist and cyclist who uses bike lanes more than most, I know that you can have too much of a good thing in a bad location. (Fun fact: As a summer student at the Edmonton Journal decades ago, I wrote a weekly cycling column.)
I get the bicycle vision. But blinkers are bad for situational awareness - whether you're politicking or pedalling.
In the real world, traffic management is a two-way street. If you can't see the other side, you are headed for a crash.
What makes this political play so exasperating is the posturing and posing on both sides of the divide. For all the premier's huffing and puffing about fighting the war against the car, his over-the-top battle against bike lanes ultimately won't fix our underlying traffic problems.
It's more about distraction than congestion. It's a way to look away from the bigger jam that politicians refuse to face.
Bike lanes have become a symbolic bogeyman. Undoing them is not a serious solution.
As a thought experiment, suppose our all-powerful premier were to wave his wand and win this wedge, peeling bike lanes off Bloor Street West or Yonge Street. It might be the right thing to do, but by any measure it would have a marginal effect on travel times - a few minutes at most.
If the premier were serious, if Mayor Olivia Chow were serious, if commuters and voters were serious, they would steer the city towards more serious and substantive remedies instead of driving us to distraction.
Start with parking. Almost every major street is bogged down by an inert lane of locked vehicles - a parking lot shaped like an asphalt ribbon. In summer, some of those spots are repurposed into CaféTO tables whose time, like COVID, has come and gone (these spots were a good idea in mid-pandemic when traffic flows were minimal, but traffic is back).
When you add up protected bike lanes, parking lanes and CaféTO tables, you have major streets repurposed into multiplexes - leaving just a single lane of clogged traffic in each direction.
To be sure, there is a political constituency for each of these competing claims and lanes: local businesses crave the foot traffic from parking spots; restaurateurs revel in their overflow seating on asphalt; cyclists savour the freedom to sail past the congestion.
But someone has to tell everyone that you can't have it all.
Progressive proponents of protected bike lanes argue they save lives. They surely do, but if safety is so important, why does Toronto encourage the explosive growth in bike sharing knowing that so many users won't use helmets - leaving them perilously vulnerable to head injuries at intersections or crossing streetcar tracks? Why do we allow high-speed e-bike users with pretend pedals on those same bike lanes to overtake regular pedallers or menace pedestrians?
Safety, it seems, only goes so far.
To be clear, I love zooming along University Avenue's abandoned bike lanes, which are so much wider than the parallel north-south bike lanes a stone's throw away on Beverley Street. But I feel guilty looking at the traffic jams created outside our biggest hospital cluster - it feels like overkill in the name of bicycle safety.
While Ford might be right on the details, might doesn't make right in a democracy. The bottom line is that the province should stay in its own lane, rather than meddle from on high in matters of municipal jurisdiction.
If Toronto politicians want to tie traffic in knots, let them face voters and commuters at election time. Yet the premier can't resist the impulse to bigfoot the mayor, simply because he can.
Legally and constitutionally, the city is a creature of the province. He has the power to pass legislation banning bike lanes, just as he has the hubris to boast of digging a fantastical tunnel under the 401.
All along, Ford casts himself as the fearless defender of commuters, fending off the putative war against the car. In truth, he is waging - and winning - wedge politics, but it won't get us where we need to go.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queen's Park as the Ontario Legislature resumes on Oct.21, 2024.

Subject: Voters; Politics; Traffic accidents & safety; Traffic congestion; Bicycling

Document 460 of 500

Doug Ford's proposed bike lane legislation is just another one of the premier's empty grand gestures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-proposed-bike-lane-legislation-is-just/docview/3119181093/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+proposed+bike+lane+legislation+is+just+another+one+of+the+premier%27s+empty+grand+gestures&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Reading Doug Ford's proposed new traffic legislation, the initial relief might be that there's no immediate proposal to rip out existing bike lanes - although listening to the premier, that could well be coming next. But the restrictions on new bike lanes in places where they threaten to slow down traffic will have an immediate impact. On Tuesday, in fact, a Toronto city council committee is set to discuss new bike lanes on Parkside Drive where slowing down traffic is their stated purpose.
See, the proposal on Parkside, running along the east side of High Park between Lake Shore Blvd and Bloor St. W., is about safety. There have been more than 1,400 collisions there in the past 10 years, three of them fatal. It's a residential and parkland street that serves essentially as a highway access ramp for thousands of motorists. Cars go too fast despite the posted 40 km/h limit - in one month alone last year, 2,279 speeding tickets were issued on that little two-kilometre street by photo radar, tops of any location in the city. Bike lanes are part of a plan to slow them down.
If the province forbids the lanes, I suppose the city could implement planter boxes, or speed bumps, or additional parking lanes. But the goal is to prevent crashes, injuries and deaths, so slowing the speed of traffic - bike lanes or otherwise - is the needed remedy.
In that specific context, do Ford's new bike lane rules make a lick of sense? If you asked him, I suspect his honest answer would be a shrug. Because making a lick of sense is not one of the criteria that guides Ford's policy announcements. Feasibility, practicality, efficacy - these concepts are not in the Ford Policy Shop tool box. His point is to make a grand gesture - swing for the fences! - in a way that shows he's doing things. It's an inclination of the premier's that keeps mucking things up for the people whose job it is to try to make the city work in Toronto.
Remember when he cut the size of city council in half? Or tried to slash the budget of Toronto public health? Or when he took over all transit rail construction? Or when he decided to do something splashy at Ontario Place? Or when he shuttered safe drug consumption sites?
The flagship example - not specific to Toronto - is likely Buck-a-Beer. All these years later, no one sells beer for a buck in Ontario, of course. Beyond headline-grabbing promotions, they were never going to. No one can turn a profit selling it for one dollar. But the actual price does not matter. What matters is that Ford can shout about allowing buck-a-beer sales.
Look at the plan to tunnel under the 401, it's buck-a-beer all the way down. Patent nonsense from any practical point of view (never mind the cost: look at the road closures and traffic on Queen while we dig a subway under it, now imagine 40 years of traffic on the 401 while they tunnel under it). He's not doing it because it makes sense. He's doing it to demonstrate the absurd lengths he'll go to to move traffic.
Occasionally this compulsion can work in Toronto's favour. One day we may thank him for the Ontario Line subway that showed he was the subway Premier. The mayor and council already appreciate the budget room from when he took over the Gardiner to prove he was the highway premier.
But when Toronto's government benefits, it appears to be a happy accident. And when, so often, it doesn't, the practical, street-level concerns raised by the province's grand gestures don't even merit an afterthought. Today, Ford knows some people are very angry about bike lanes and everyone is fed up with traffic. So it's big-time gestures about highways and cycle-path bans.
Funny thing about bike lanes in Toronto. For all the audible rage you hear about them, only two city councillors voted against the hot-spot Bloor West lanes near the Kingsway Ford is now grandstanding on back when they were approved in 2020. The three major mayoral candidates who tried to make opposition to bike lanes an issue in the 2023 election got less than 15 per cent of the vote combined.
And I can tell you residents around Parkside Drive are very concerned with the safety of that street. It may be a place where Ford can get his headline about standing up for the flow of traffic, and I don't doubt it will serve his electoral needs. But it's a place where city council has every impetus to try to slow traffic down, one way or the other. As is so often the case, they're just going to have to work around the premier's grand gestures to accomplish their simple goal.
CAPTION: Not everyone wants to see the proposed bike lanes installed on Parkside Drive.

Calls are growing for Justin Trudeau to step down + Doug Ford could end these Toronto bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/calls-are-growing-justin-trudeau-step-down-doug/docview/3119181058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Calls+are+growing+for+Justin+Trudeau+to+step+down+%2B+Doug+Ford+could+end+these+Toronto+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, October 22 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on MPs calling for Justin Trudeau to step down, nursing home workers speaking out and the potential end of Toronto bike lanes.
DON'T MISSLiberal MPs signed a letter calling for Justin Trudeau to step down
The calls are coming from inside the house. Dozens of Liberal MPs have signed a private letter asking Trudeau to step down and they're prepared to make their case at Wednesday's national caucus meeting, Tonda MacCharles reports. "It's basically just a letter that's asking for reflection and change," said one signatory. Here's what it means for the future of the party.
Go deeper: Signatories are keeping their cards close to their vest, refusing to divulge its contents, its organizers or who signed it. One signatory hinted the letter received more than 20 but less than 40 signatures.Meanwhile: As the push for a new party leader grows, former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney announced he's wading into elected politics. Take a look at which high-profile Liberals might be eyeing Trudeau's job.Why nursing home workers are speaking out
Nursing home workers have had enough. Staff at one of Toronto's largest city-owned long-term care homes are slamming "chronic" staffing shortages and "mismanagement" they say are putting the quality of residents' care at risk. "We are dealing with more than just low staff levels. We are facing racism, intimidation, reprisal, retaliation, harassment," said one worker at Rexdale's Kipling Acres facility. Kenyon Wallace explores the crisis in long-term care.
Zoom out: Demand for long-term-care beds in the city has far outstripped supply following a wave of nursing home closures in recent years. Mayor Olivia Chow previously said Toronto has the capacity and willingness to help - but it lacks the funds.Retirement roadmap: Have you considered residential care as part of your retirement plan? Take a look at what options are available - and the price tag.Doug Ford could end these downtown Toronto bike lanes
The fate of Toronto's bike lanes are in the premier's hands. His Progressive Conservatives tabled sweeping legislation Monday allowing Ontario to review existing bike lanes and requiring municipalities to receive provincial approval before installing new ones. They've already hinted at stripping the lanes from Yonge and Bloor, and Eglinton and University in the name of stopping congestion. Robert Benzie and Ben Spurr break down how the changes could affect your commute.
Albert Koehl's take: Even non-cyclists have good reason to worry about Ford's scapegoating of bike lanes as the cause of congestion. He's interfering with democratically achieved municipal decisions - what local decision will he meddle in next?Word from the opposition: "Municipal politicians, they know their residents, they know their roads, let them make those decisions," said Liberal Leader and former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie. "The premier needs to stay in his lane."WHAT ELSE
New Brunswick voters have elected a Liberal majority government, ending six years of Tory rule."Women are gaslit." Menopause is a health crisis for Canadians. Why is relief out of reach?One outcome is clear in the B.C. election - Justin Trudeau lost.King Charles went to Australia and all he got were snubs and protests.The Ford government is tapping a former federal Liberal minister to tackle its family doctor shortage.A woman testified that Toronto Coun. Michael Thompson touched "under my bathing suit" without consent.Mortgage relief is on the horizon. Economists say we could see a rate cut triple the size of previous cuts on Wednesday.Toronto's homeless shelters are packed with children and babies, and hundreds more are waiting outside the system.Last-minute switches to Air Canada Rouge planes are frustrating passengers - but experts say there's not much they can do.An e-bike fire has left a Toronto woman seriously injured. Why do they keep catching fire?Eat Well Spend Almost Nothing: How to turn leftover turkey bones into bone broth, soup and even garden fertilizer.The arts in Toronto are in dire need of more money. Airbnb may be able to help fix that.POV
Racism against South Asians is becoming a scourge. Wasn't Canada supposed to be better than this?CLOSE-UP
QUEEN'S PARK: Some 200 activists staged a "die-in" in front of the provincial legislature yesterday, in protest of the Ford government's plans to shutter 10 safe-injection sites across the province. Omar Mosleh has more details from the event.
Thank you for reading First Up. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca
Sign up here to get this newsletter in your inbox.

Document 462 of 500

Chow's 25-year plan needs the Island airport

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chows-25-year-plan-needs-island-airport/docview/3119070772/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow%27s+25-year+plan+needs+the+Island+airport&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chow wants city to be a business mecca, Oct.17
Mayor Olivia Chow claims she wants to "create the most competitive business environment in North America." Yet she has all but killed any chance Billy Bishop Airport will continue to operate beyond the next decade or two. If Chow is serious about creating a business mecca of Toronto, one has to ask why she can't see the employment and economic advantage a downtown airport brings.
If Chow truly cares about growing the economy and attracting the next generation of global investment, her 25-year plan must seriously consider a long-term lease and new tri-party agreement for Billy Bishop Airport. Why ignore the thousands of jobs at stake should they close the airport, especially when keeping it would support small businesses, create yet more jobs and continue to make Toronto attractive to tech and creative companies?
Wayne Fraser, Toronto Island
Government inability to plan, forecast and budget is a disgrace
The real reason government projects take too long, Oct. 20
We should not have to accept or tolerate the fact that government "megaprojects take too long and cost too much" as Anne Marie Aikins wrote. All projects regardless of size/complexity take too long yet we're expected to both understand and accept this fact. A much smaller and simpler "miniproject" like the Glen Road Pedestrian Bridge has gone from a 19-month project to most recently a 34-month project. It is supposed to be finished "end of 2024" but this forecast will undoubtedly not be met. This inability to plan, forecast and budget is a disgrace of which every level of government should be ashamed.
Debby Neill, Toronto
How does a six-month project to open a homeless shelter turn into a six-year wait?
In 2019 the city bought an old medical building at 2299 Dundas Street West, near Bloor Street in Toronto. The plan was to turn it into a modernized shelter for men with a capacity of 80 beds and counselling on-site. Shortly after a community meeting was held with the residents of the neighbourhood. Most of the residents welcomed the idea of the shelter and saw its need. It was supposed to be open by mid-2020. Four years later it is now proposed to be opened by mid-2025. I do not see why a six-month project turns into a six-year job and maybe longer. Homeless people are sleeping in tents, and shelters are full with many people being turned away as there is no space. I think the city auditor should examine why this project is taking such a long time and the cost associated with it.
Ray Van Eenooghe, Toronto
We're donating our pre-election cheques to help the homeless
Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 16
Premier Doug Ford's blatant bribe for votes is disgusting, shameless and a very typical of his Progressive Conservative government. Our two-person household will be signing over our two cheques to the Ontario Alliance to End Homelessness. We feel the money will be of more use to those homeless folks Ford tells to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else." One wonders if homeless folks will even get a cheque, since they don't have an address. Well done, premier. More than $3 billion spent on bribery, when that money could and should go to health care, education and ending homelessness. Priorities, folks!
Nancy Van Kessel, Mississauga
An outrageous, insulting and self-centred waste of our tax dollars
Four ways to mock Doug Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love, Oct. 21
I could feel Heather Mallick's frustration as I read her article and I agree with everything she said. We taxpayers mustn't overlook where Ford's $200 generosity is coming from. It is not coming out of his personal pocket. It is coming out of our tax dollars. So, in effect, he is giving us back our own money. This is an outrageous, insulting and self-centred waste of our tax dollars. We need and deserve to have our taxes spent on necessary services that are designed to help every person in Ontario.
Patricia Steward, East York
We need to return to made-in-Canada products
What a Trump or Harris win means for trade with Canada, Oct. 19
I have often wondered why Canadians have, mostly, stopped manufacturing in Canada. When I watch "Dragon's Den," and prospective manufacturers are encouraged to have their products manufactured overseas "for more profit," I see red. I enjoy the few products manufactured in Canada (and North America for that matter) and do not mind spending more money for good quality products. I used to buy good clothing made in Canada and the USA that cost more but was definitely worth it. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should promote new Canadian businesses with seed money that doesn't need to be paid back. We don't need a "buy Canadian only" slogan. We just need a "proudly made in Canada" label on Canadian made products and perhaps a small tax credit for customers who purchase items made in Canada.
Lillian Shery, Toronto
Israel is wrong to scorch the Earth
Canada should be tougher on Israel for defying UN, Oct. 17
Why is the world standing by, watching children starve in the cold with thousands of families who cannot lay their children's heads on a pillow at bed time. Critics like me agree that Israel was right strike back against Hamas after the Oct. 7 attack. But it was wrong to go to scorched earth. Wrong is wrong. The suffering has gone over the limit. What would Golda Meir have done? Would David Ben-Gurion have drawn the line somewhere short of where we are now? Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has inked a dark document in the story of Israel. It is time to draw the line and save what they can of Israel's triumph over evil in the 20th century.
Hugh McKechnie, Sudbury
It is time to take action against Modi's IndiaIndia will pay heavily for deliberately crossing a red line in nations across the globe, Oct. 16
While I hope that Robin V. Sears is correct in stating that the government of India will pay severely for its promotion and practice of state terrorism in Canada, my only question is what took so long? India is led by Narendra Modi, a Hindu nationalist leader who is widely noted for his responsibility in the anti-Muslim genocidal massacres in the state of Gujarat as chief minister in 2002. Yet the world ignored his role once he became Prime Minister in 2015. Since becoming PM, the lynchings and pogroms of Indian Muslims as well as attacks on the country's Indian Christians by his Hindu fanatic base have increased. He has also arrested journalists, expelled Amnesty International and continued India's brutal crackdown in Kashmir - all with little international blowback. Now, as evidence has come out highlighting how Modi's India has been engaging in state terrorism worldwide by killing dissidents, threatening critics and engaging in criminal activities abroad, maybe it's time to finally impose military, economic and diplomatic sanctions? The fact that Modi even bragged about how his "new India comes into your home to kill you" indicates that the time for tough talk is over and the time for tough action has arrived.
Gohar S. Razi, Ottawa
CAPTION: Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport is pictured in 2013.

Document 463 of 500

One outcome is clear in the B.C. election - Justin Trudeau lost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-outcome-is-clear-b-c-election-justin-trudeau/docview/3119070653/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+outcome+is+clear+in+the+B.C.+election+-+Justin+Trudeau+lost&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They're still trying to figure out exactly what happened in the British Columbia election over the weekend. But one thing is clear: Justin Trudeau lost.
The prime minister wasn't on the ballot, of course. But the outcome is yet another unmistakable sign that his time is up, not that he needed any more of those.
The outcome in B.C. (my home province, and where I edited a newspaper a few years back) was awfully close. It will take days before recounts show whether the New Democrats led by David Eby can cling to power by a seat or two. But even if that happens, the story of the election is the amazing resurgence (rebirth, really) of the B.C. Conservative party - and that's where the bad news for Trudeau comes in.
The Conservative party was an empty shell in B.C. until just a year or so ago. In the last provincial election it barely registered with a minuscule 1.91 per cent of the vote. B.C. politics are sharply divided along ideological lines but the small-c conservative vote went to the B.C. Liberals (yes, it's confusing).
Those same B.C. Liberals self-immolated with a botched rebranding and left an opening on the right - which was promptly filled by a seemingly washed-up politician named John Rustad. He revived the Conservative brand in B.C. and will now be at the very least the leader of a powerful opposition (unless recounts hand him a couple more seats and the premiership itself).
Rustad managed to do that by channelling the message Pierre Poilievre has been spreading so successfully at the national level. There's no formal connection between the B.C. Conservatives and the federal Conservative party and Poilievre quite conspicuously did not endorse Rustad. But it was the same message - often word for word.
The provincial Conservative platform was titled "Common Sense Change for British Columbians" - echoing Poilievre's slogans. Rustad promised to "axe the tax" and "stop the drugs" and denounced "catch and release" bail policies. All Poilievre-isms. And he railed against the "Eby-Trudeau alliance" - linking his opponent to the unpopular PM.
Rustad's critics are obsessed with extreme positions taken by him and some of his candidates in the past - like his casting doubt on climate change. But that wasn't the key to his success on Saturday. It came from taking out Poilievre's message for a very successful test drive.
Eby obviously saw the right-wing wave coming. He reversed two of his party's signature policies - in favour of carbon pricing and decriminalization of drugs - in an effort to blunt the Conservative attack. If he hadn't done that, he almost certainly would have lost power outright.
There's a big lesson here for Trudeau. If you don't bend, you're going to break. If his government doesn't trim its sails on some of its most unpopular policies (the carbon tax above all) it's going to get run over by the Conservatives. It's almost certainly too late to avoid that, judging by the polls, but stubbornly sticking to policies the public has definitively rejected will only make things worse.
B.C. is crucial electoral ground for the Liberals. It sends the third-largest number of Liberal MPs to Ottawa, after Ontario and Quebec and it's the only western province where the Liberals show any life. The B.C. vote was a dry run for the next federal election and it was disastrous for the Liberals.
On another matter entirely: I wrote recently that Canada's medical assistance in dying, or MAID, program is being cited as the way not to do things as Britain debates whether to legalize doctor-assisted death for terminally ill people.
The Associated Press carried an eye-opening report a few days ago that will only fuel that perception. It obtained dozens of messages exchanged in confidential forums by doctors and nurses who provide MAID in Ontario. And it says they "suggest a significant number of people euthanized when they are in unmanageable pain but not about to die live in Ontario's poorest and most deprived areas."
AP reports are carried by hundreds of news outlets. This one suggests some people are requesting death because they're poor and aren't being offered alternative services. You should read it.
CAPTION: B.C. Conservative Leader John Rustad gives a thumbs up after addressing supporters on election night in Vancouver, on Saturday.Rustad revived the party in B.C. "by channeling the message Pierre Poilievre has been spreading so successfully at the national level," writes Andrew Phillips.

Liberals win majority government: Holt first woman to lead province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-win-majority-government/docview/3119063104/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+win+majority+government%3A+Holt+first+woman+to+lead+province&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick voters have elected a Liberal majority government, tossing out the incumbent Progressive Conservatives after six years in power and handing the reins to the first woman ever to lead the province.
Liberal Leader Susan Holt is a relative newcomer to the province's political scene, having won a byelection last year, eight months after she became party leader.
"Tonight's results make it clear that New Brunswickers are ready for a change," she told a boisterous crowd of supporters in downtown Fredericton during an energetic speech that showed off her skills as a fluently bilingual premier-designate.
"We don't take it lightly that you have put your trust in myself and my team and that you have hope for a brighter future."
Flanked by her three young daughters, Holt paid tribute to several women who were trailblazers in provincial politics, including former NDP leader Elizabeth Weir and Aldéa Landry, the first Acadian woman in New Brunswick named as a cabinet minister.
The 47-year-old former business advocate and public servant led the Liberals to victory after a 33-day campaign, thwarting Blaine Higgs's bid to secure a third term as Tory premier.
The Liberal win marks a strong repudiation of Higgs's pronounced shift to more socially conservative policies.
With all of the polls reporting, the Liberals won 31 of the legislature's 49 seats. The Conservatives had 16 and the Greens two.
When the Liberal win was announced, Holt's supporters stood and roared their approval. Amid the cheers and whistles, some rattled noisemakers, others thumped tables. Some women, including Aldéa Landry, used tissues to wipe tears from their eyes.
Higgs, meanwhile, lost in his southern New Brunswick riding of Quispamsis. In a speech to supporters in the riding, he confirmed that he would begin a leadership transition process.
"It's certainly not the night we hoped for," Higgs told the crowd at the St. Louis Bar and Grill in a strip mall. "Nevertheless, it is what it is. The people of New Brunswick have spoken ... Susan, you've earned the trust of the people."
During his speech Monday night, Green Party Leader David Coon pledged to continue building the party, but he then turned his sights on Higgs.
"One thing is for sure," he told the crowd at Dolan's Pub in Fredericton. "We know that Blaine Higgs is no longer the premier of this province."
The audience responded with raucous cheers.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Hina Alam The Canadian Press

Subject: Leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 465 of 500

CHANGING LANES: BIKE LANES COULD BE REMOVED FROM MAJOR TORONTO STREETS UNDER NEW LAW THAT FORD GOVERNMENT CALLS A 'REASONABLE APPROACH' TO EASING GRIDLOCK

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/changing-lanes/docview/3119063102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CHANGING+LANES%3A+BIKE+LANES+COULD+BE+REMOVED+FROM+MAJOR+TORONTO+STREETS+UNDER+NEW+LAW+THAT+FORD+GOVERNMENT+CALLS+A+%27REASONABLE+APPROACH%27+TO+EASING+GRIDLOCK&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a salvo at the city of Toronto, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are hinting at the end of bike lanes on Yonge and Bloor streets and Eglinton and University avenues.
Under sweeping new legislation tabled Monday by Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, the Highway Traffic Act is being amended to say "the Ministry may review existing bicycle lanes" and municipalities must receive provincial approval before installing new lanes.
"We've seen Toronto become one of the most congested cities in the entire world and this is really about a reasonable approach," said Sarkaria, who commutes to Brampton daily and often speaks of being stuck on University Avenue south of Queen's Park.
"We're seeing gridlock in Toronto, whether it be Bloor, University or Yonge Street," said the minister, whose office later confirmed the province would cover any costs incurred by municipalities to remove bike lanes.
His bill empowers the Ministry of Transportation to take "consideration of traffic flow" before approving any new lanes.
"When considering whether to approve the design for the construction of a bicycle lane, the Ministry may consider whether it would unduly diminish the orderly movement of motor vehicle traffic," the bill states.
Sarkaria stressed "we're not anti-bike-lane," but insisted a "reasonable approach" is needed.
"There's absolutely a place for bikes and for bike lanes," he said, adding "major arterial roads" are not the best place for such lanes.
"We don't want lanes of traffic removed at a time where we're seeing a population growth that has been significant for the province of Ontario."
Those changes could impact existing bike lanes on Yonge, Bloor, Eglinton and University, among others.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario criticized the province for "significant overreach" into civic jurisdiction.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow has also pushed back at the usurping of municipal authority. Her office declined to comment on the legislation Monday and referred the Star to her previous statement, which warned that "ripping up our roads (to remove bike lanes) will make congestion worse," and said the province should tackle gridlock by completing the delayed Finch West and Eglinton LRT lines.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford is relitigating battles from his tenure at city hall, where he was a councillor when his late brother, Rob Ford, was Toronto's mayor.
"He failed at city council ... and now he's just playing that all out again," said Stiles.
"But this is meddling in important issues that municipalities have a responsibility for. They're micromanaging municipal issues here when he's forgetting the basics of what a provincial government should be paying attention to: health care, housing, the cost of living, our schools," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford clearly "regrets not being elected mayor of the city of Toronto" in 2014 when he lost to John Tory.
"Municipal politicians, they know their residents, they know their roads. Let them make those decisions," said Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor.
"The premier needs to stay in his lane. He thinks the solution to our $13-billion congestion gridlock problem is removing bike lanes. We have very serious issues at hand that aren't going to be solved by that. We need investment in public transit," she said.
But the Tories - who are considering an early election in 2025, a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - believe curbing bike lanes would be popular with commuters.
According to Statistics Canada data from May, 71.2 per cent of Toronto-area residents took a car, truck or van to work while 22.7 per cent rode transit and 5.4 per cent used "active transportation," meaning they walked or cycled.
In 2011, the most recent year separate cycling information was available, 1.2 per cent of Torontonians commuted to work by bicycle while 69.9 per cent drove or were passengers in a car, 23.3 per cent took public transit and 4.6 per cent walked.
Sarkaria's omnibus legislation would also expedite construction of Highway 413, the 52-kilometre freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, with an "accelerated environmental assessment process" that has ecologists concerned.
It would also slap hefty fines on protesters for trying to thwart construction of Highway 413, up to $50,000 for a first offence and $100,000 for a subsequent violation.
"To disagree with the government is one thing, but to block critical infrastructure from being built is absolutely not acceptable. It wouldn't be acceptable on this project or any other project," the minister said.
On Thursday, Ford received applause from many of the 1,200 people at his Empire Club luncheon speech when he said his government was "bringing sanity back to bike lane decisions."
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes. We need to - and will - remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill," the premier said.
The Toronto and Region Board of Trade signalled openness to the province's plans, saying in a statement that "decisions limiting road capacity, including adding bike lanes to major roads, must be driven by traffic and cycling data and a clear understanding of the implications for travel and commute times."
Dr. Samantha Green, a family physician and co-founder of the Doctors for Safe Cycling advocacy group, said bike lanes have been shown to reduce deaths and injuries to cyclists, and a "robust network of connected, separated bike lanes" would encourage more people to ride.
"So removing bike lanes is a bad decision for the health and safety of people in the province," she said.
The bill was the first legislation tabled as the house resumed after an extended 137-day summer recess.
CAPTION: "Major arterial roads" such as Bloor Street are not the best places for bike lanes, said the transportation minister on Monday, introducing legislation that would allow the province to review existing lanes and approve new ones. STEVE RUSSELL TORONTO STAR

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Ben Spurr Toronto Star

One outcome of B.C. election is very clear - Trudeau lost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/one-outcome-b-c-election-is-very-clear-trudeau/docview/3119063059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+outcome+of+B.C.+election+is+very+clear+-+Trudeau+lost&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They're still trying to figure out exactly what happened in the British Columbia election over the weekend. But one thing is clear: Justin Trudeau lost.
The prime minister wasn't on the ballot, of course. But the outcome is yet another unmistakable sign that his time is up, not that he needed any more of those.
The outcome in B.C. (my home province, and where I edited a newspaper a few years back) was awfully close. It will take days before recounts show whether the New Democrats led by David Eby can cling to power by a seat or two. But even if that happens, the story of the election is the amazing resurgence (rebirth, really) of the B.C. Conservative party - and that's where the bad news for Trudeau comes in.
The Conservative party was an empty shell in B.C. until just a year or so ago. In the last provincial election it barely registered with a minuscule 1.91 per cent of the vote. B.C. politics are sharply divided along ideological lines but the small-c conservative vote went to the B.C. Liberals (yes, it's confusing).
Those same B.C. Liberals self-immolated with a botched rebranding and left an opening on the right - which was promptly filled by a seemingly washed-up politician named John Rustad. He revived the Conservative brand in B.C. and will now be at the very least the leader of a powerful opposition (unless recounts hand him a couple more seats and the premiership itself).
Rustad managed to do that by channelling the message Pierre Poilievre has been spreading so successfully at the national level. There's no formal connection between the B.C. Conservatives and the federal Conservative party and Poilievre quite conspicuously did not endorse Rustad. But it was the same message - often word for word.
The provincial Conservative platform was titled "Common Sense Change for British Columbians" - echoing Poilievre's slogans. Rustad promised to "axe the tax" and "stop the drugs" and denounced "catch and release" bail policies. All Poilievre-isms. And he railed against the "Eby-Trudeau alliance" - linking his opponent to the unpopular PM.
Rustad's critics are obsessed with extreme positions taken by him and some of his candidates in the past - like his casting doubt on climate change. But that wasn't the key to his success on Saturday. It came from taking out Poilievre's message for a very successful test drive.
Eby obviously saw the right-wing wave coming. He reversed two of his party's signature policies - in favour of carbon pricing and decriminalization of drugs - in an effort to blunt the Conservative attack. If he hadn't done that, he almost certainly would have lost power outright.
There's a big lesson here for Trudeau. If you don't bend, you're going to break. If his government doesn't trim its sails on some of its most unpopular policies (the carbon tax above all) it's going to get run over by the Conservatives. It's almost certainly too late to avoid that, judging by the polls, but stubbornly sticking to policies the public has definitively rejected will only make things worse.
B.C. is crucial electoral ground for the Liberals. It sends the third-largest number of Liberal MPs to Ottawa, after Ontario and Quebec and it's the only western province where the Liberals show any life. The B.C. vote was a dry run for the next federal election and it was disastrous for the Liberals.

The Associated Press carried an eye-opening report a few days ago that will only fuel that perception. It obtained dozens of messages exchanged in confidential forums by doctors and nurses who provide MAID in Ontario. And it says they "suggest a significant number of people euthanized when they are in unmanageable pain but not about to die live in Ontario's poorest and most deprived areas."
AP reports are carried by hundreds of news outlets. This one suggests some people are requesting death because they're poor and aren't being offered alternative services. You should read it.

Document 467 of 500

Minister slams board trustees over travel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/minister-slams-board-trustees-over-travel/docview/3119063034/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Minister+slams+board+trustees+over+travel&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has clipped the wings of jet-setting school board trustees, saying international travel should happen rarely, if at all.
Slamming a $145,000 summer trip to Italy taken by four elected officials in the Brantford Catholic board - whose governance is now under review - Dunlop said "frankly, I'm very disappointed and I'm hearing the same from taxpayers across the province."
When asked if trustees should ever take trips out of the country, Dunlop said "there can be some specific circumstances, but typically that is not something that does happen. So, there are specific times - but not for trips to Italy for artwork or going for baseball games."
The latter was a reference to a spending controversy by administrators in the London-area Thames Valley District School Board who attended a three-day retreat at the hotel at the Rogers Centre in the summer, on days the Toronto Blue Jays were playing.
While the four Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board trustees said they will repay the cost of the Italy trip and look for outside help to pay for the statues purchased, Dunlop said she is concerned " accountability was only taken after my ministry and the public expressed clear concerns for the misuse of taxpayer dollars."
Before the trip, trustees at the board had approved new expense rules allowing for first-class travel for long-haul flights, upgraded hotel rooms and, in some circumstances, alcohol for hospitality.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, a former school board trustee, said the travel and artwork purchases by the Brant Haldimand trustees "looks like a particularly unfortunate decision. I think especially right now, when our schools are really struggling, our students are really struggling, not having the resources and supports they need, it's on all of us as elected officials to take that responsibility very seriously."
Meanwhile, Dunlop said the investigator sent in to look at a field trip taken by students at 15 schools in the Toronto District School Board that saw kids marching in a protest will look at "what happened leading up to it, what happened during the trip and afterwards."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Full text: Chow plan needs island airport
Chow wants city to be a business mecca, Oct. 17
Mayor Olivia Chow claims she wants to "create the most competitive business environment in North America." Yet she has all but killed any chance Billy Bishop Airport will continue to operate beyond the next decade or two. If Chow is serious about creating a business mecca of Toronto, one has to ask why she can't see the employment and economic advantage a downtown airport brings. If Chow cares about growing the economy and attracting the next generation of global investment, her 25-year plan must seriously consider a long-term lease and new tri-party agreement for Billy Bishop Airport.
Wayne Fraser, Toronto Island
Inability to plan, budget a disgrace
The real reason government projects take too long, Oct. 20
We should not have to accept or tolerate the fact that government "megaprojects take too long and cost too much" as Anne Marie Aikins wrote. All projects regardless of size/complexity take too long yet we're expected to both understand and accept this fact. A much smaller and simpler "miniproject" like the Glen Road Pedestrian Bridge has gone from a 19-month project to most recently a 34-month project. It is supposed to be finished "end of 2024" but this forecast will undoubtedly not be met. This inability to plan, forecast and budget is a disgrace of which every level of government should be ashamed.
Debby Neill, Toronto
Six-month job to six-year wait
In 2019, the city bought an old medical building at 2299 Dundas Street West, near Bloor Street in Toronto. The plan was to turn it into a modernized shelter for men with a capacity of 80 beds and counselling on-site. Most residents welcomed the idea of the shelter and saw its need. It was supposed to be open by mid-2020. Four years later it is now proposed to be opened by mid-2025. I do not see why a six-month project turns into a six-year job and maybe longer. Homeless people are sleeping in tents, and shelters are full with many people being turned away as there is no space. I think the city auditor should examine why this project is taking such a long time and the cost associated with it.
Ray Van Eenooghe, Toronto
Giving cheques to homeless
Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 16
Premier Doug Ford's blatant bribe for votes is disgusting, shameless and a very typical of his Progressive Conservative government. Our two-person household will be signing over our two cheques to the Ontario Alliance to End Homelessness. We feel the money will be of more use to those homeless folks Ford tells to "get off your a*s and start working like everyone else." One wonders if homeless folks will even get a cheque, since they don't have an address. More than $3 billion spent on bribery, when that money could and should go to health care, education and ending homelessness.
Nancy Van Kessel, Mississauga
Ford wasting
our tax dollars
Four ways to mock Doug Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love, Oct. 21
I could feel Heather Mallick's frustration as I read her article and I agree with everything she said. We taxpayers mustn't overlook where Ford's $200 generosity is coming from. It is not coming out of his personal pocket. It is coming out of our tax dollars. So, in effect, he is giving us back our own money. This is an outrageous, insulting and self-centred waste of our tax dollars.
Patricia Steward, East York
Let's make more products here
What a Trump or Harris win means for trade with Canada, Oct. 19
I have often wondered why Canadians have, mostly, stopped manufacturing in Canada. When I watch "Dragon's Den," and prospective manufacturers are encouraged to have their products manufactured overseas "for more profit," I see red. I enjoy the few products manufactured in Canada (and North America for that matter) and do not mind spending more money for good quality products. I used to buy good clothing made in Canada and the USA that cost more but was definitely worth it. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should promote new Canadian businesses with seed money that doesn't need to be paid back. We don't need a "buy Canadian only" slogan. We just need a "proudly made in Canada" label on Canadian made products and perhaps a tax credit for customers who purchase items made in Canada.
Lillian Shery, Toronto
Israel is wrong to scorch the earth
Canada should be tougher on Israel for defying UN, Oct. 17
Why is the world standing by, watching children starve in the cold with thousands of families who cannot lay their children's heads on a pillow at bed time. Critics like me agree that Israel was right to strike back against Hamas after the Oct. 7 attack. But it was wrong to go to scorched earth. Wrong is wrong. The suffering has gone over the limit. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has inked a dark document in the story of Israel. It is time to draw the line and save what they can of Israel's triumph over evil in the 20th century.
Hugh McKechnie, Sudbury, Ont.
Take action against India
India will pay heavily for deliberately crossing a red line in nations across the globe, Oct. 16
While I hope that Robin V. Sears is correct in stating that the government of India will pay severely for its promotion and practice of state terrorism in Canada. What took so long? India is led by Narendra Modi, a Hindu nationalist leader who is widely noted for his responsibility in the anti-Muslim genocidal massacres in the state of Gujarat as chief minister in 2002. Yet the world ignored his role once he became prime minister in 2015. Since becoming PM, the lynchings and pogroms of Indian Muslims as well as attacks on the country's Indian Christians by his Hindu fanatic base have increased. He has also arrested journalists, expelled Amnesty International and continued India's brutal crackdown in Kashmir. Now, as evidence has come out highlighting how Modi's India has been engaging in state terrorism worldwide by killing dissidents, threatening critics and engaging in criminal activities abroad, maybe it's time to finally impose military, economic and diplomatic sanctions?
Gohar S. Razi, Ottawa
