Document 469 of 500

Protest targets Ford's plan to close safe injection sites: Supporters say facilities save lives and provide pathways to recovery

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protest-targets-fords-plan-close-safe-injection/docview/3119060227/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protest+targets+Ford%27s+plan+to+close+safe+injection+sites%3A+Supporters+say+facilities+save+lives+and+provide+pathways+to+recovery&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Activists staged a die-in at Queen's Park on Monday in protest of the provincial government's plan to shutter 10 safe injection sites across Ontario, which some say will be a "death sentence."
About 200 advocates, front-line workers, health-care providers and families of people lost to the drug poisoning crisis were among those lying down outside the provincial legislature in opposition to the Doug Ford government's plan to shutter the safe injection sites.
Supporters chanted "Harm reduction saves lives" and brandished signs with slogans such as "Save our sites or fill your morgues" as they heard stories from people who have lost loved ones to overdoses or have been traumatized by the health-care system when seeking treatment.
One of those people is Hannah Stahl, a street nurse who lost her brother Dalton to an overdose in 2017 in Hamilton, where there were no safe injection sites at the time. The current site in Hamilton is one of the 10 slated to close.
"He had recently been released from jail and he was really falling through the cracks of the health-care system," Stahl said.
"It's really pertinent when we have a toxic drug supply as dangerous as it is right now that people have a safe place to go," Stahl added. "Because there's no point in offering more detox beds if people are dying."
In August, the Ford government announced new zoning restrictions requiring safe injection sites, also known as Consumption Treatment Service sites, to be 200 metres from schools and daycares, which will result in 10 of the province's sites having to close by March 31, 2025, including five in Toronto.
The sites offer needle exchange, supplies, supervision and drug safety testing to people who use drugs.
Danny Dobric, who has used opioids for more than a decade, said he knows at least 30 people who have died of overdoses when using alone, including two former partners.
"The times that I wasn't there, look what happened," he said. His wife died in 2000, his girlfriend last year.
He said he has tried numerous treatment programs but still depends on safe consumption sites as a lifeline. He is anxious about what might happen if they close.
"I know if I go under, someone's right there, because you only have a few minutes," he said. "I use by myself. So anything can happen."
Supporters of the sites say they not only save lives, but provide a pathway for people to receive treatment services, while critics argue they increase crime and social disorder in the neighbourhoods that have them.
The sites have always been controversial, but they received renewed criticism after Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two, was killed by a stray bullet last summer near Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre.
Ford has said he's personally opposed to the sites, calling them a "failed policy" without providing clear evidence for his reasoning.
He said he's in favour of getting people into treatment, and the government announced plans to spend $378 million on 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs with 375 supportive housing units as well as treatment beds.
In a statement, Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Health, said each site slated to close will have the opportunity to convert into a HART hub.
"Communities, parents and families across Ontario have made it clear that the presence of drug consumption sites near schools and daycares is leading to serious safety problems ... That's why our government is taking action to keep communities safe, while supporting the recovery of those struggling with opioid addiction," she said.
Dr. Nanky Rai, a family doctor and community organizer who spoke at the event, said comparing treatment to consumption sites is a false dichotomy, adding that the injection sites offer wraparound services such as counselling and referrals to other health-care professionals, in addition to lifesaving measures such as overdose reversals and drug testing services.
"The provincial government is using this moment to be like, 'Let's attack these handful of sites that are doing incredibly lifesaving work and create this bogus dichotomy about there needing to be more treatment,' as if treatment doesn't exist through these sites," Rai said.
She believes closing the sites will undoubtedly lead to more fatalities.
"It's a death sentence for clients I work with, for their loved ones, for people in my life personally, and we know that almost every family in Ontario at this point has been affected by the drug poisoning crisis."
CREDIT: Omar Mosleh Toronto Star

Subject: Drug overdose; Fatalities; Poisoning; Capital punishment; Shutdowns; Injections; Demonstrations & protests; Drug prevention; Substance abuse treatment

Document 470 of 500

Liberal MPs ask Trudeau to step down: More than 20 sign call for change, as leadership hopefuls begin to organize

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-mps-ask-trudeau-step-down/docview/3119060219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MPs+ask+Trudeau+to+step+down%3A+More+than+20+sign+call+for+change%2C+as+leadership+hopefuls+begin+to+organize&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Numerous Liberal MPs have signed a private letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down and are prepared to make their case at Wednesday's national caucus meeting as some leadership hopefuls are beginning to organize.
Publicly, however, many are holding their cards close, refusing to divulge either the letter's contents, its signatories or its organizers.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long, who failed in June to elicit caucus support for his call on Trudeau to step down, told the Star "there is obviously a letter. I've signed it." Long, who is not the organizer of the letter, declined to provide much detail about it - even about the number of MPs who had signed it. "Is it over 20? Most likely. And under 40 - yeah," he said.
"It's basically just a letter that's asking for reflection and change," Long said. "I think the letter basically sets the tone for a very frank and open discussion, and I mean, obviously, about leadership."
Those on board with the letter believe Trudeau should step aside as leader, but Long added he isn't advocating for a particular candidate to replace him.
But speculation about the would-be contenders is hot. Economist and former central banker Mark Carney has confirmed he will run for office at some point, although he remains coy about when, or what job he wants.
For now, the top job is Trudeau's.
Several MPs told the Star on Monday they had not been approached to sign the letter and had only heard about it in media reports, downplaying its impact and describing the agitators as a small, if vocal, minority.
Mona Fortier, who is charged with writing the Liberal election platform, summed up the comments of nearly a dozen other Liberal MPs when she said, "Nobody asked me to sign a phantom letter."
The call for Trudeau to step down stems not just from the Atlantic caucus but "a collective from across the country," Long said, pointing to polls in B.C., Ontario and the Atlantic region that "tell us the same thing - that we are not in a good spot."
Among Liberals, he said, "there's just a growing concern about the lack of direction and lack of change. I think the approach that, 'Let's just wait it out and see if Canadians come around,' or 'Let's just kind of run the clock a bit, and maybe Pierre Poilievre will make a mistake' - it's not going to work."
Trudeau still has "a lot of support in caucus" among the Liberals' 153 MPs, Long admitted, but among voters he contends there is a "genuine, personal dislike of the prime minister."
In Long's view, things have only worsened since his failed attempt to call for a national caucus meeting to confront Trudeau with concerns after the byelection loss of what should have been a safe Liberal seat in Toronto-St. Paul's.
"We're four months closer to an election, not seeing any direction, not seeing any sense of urgency," he said. "The stay-calm-and-carry-on approach is, I think, concerning."
That's a common concern even among MPs who say they support Trudeau's decision - which he has repeatedly expressed both publicly and privately - to stay on and lead the party into the next federal election campaign.
Pickering-Uxbridge MP Jennifer O'Connell said Trudeau is "our leader and he should stay. I think he's in the best position to lead us, but I think there does have to be serious conversations happening at caucus for the mood and the reflection of what we're all hearing in our riding."
O'Connell said she does not hear the kind of voter dislike for Trudeau that Long and P.E.I. MP Sean Casey have talked publicly about, but that the party needs to ensure there is not a "disconnect" with voters.
She said it must ensure "we are actually staying focused on things that people in ridings like mine really care about - the economy, overall affordability. The environment is still really important, and how kids or young people are going to afford housing as they grow up. Things like that really matter.
"I do think we have to have some honest conversations about that direction. We can't just be told what that direction is," she said.
If those concerns are heard, " then I think we're in a good position to move forward properly," she said, cautioning that the caucus has been promised things like advertising and communications strategies "for a while now." Asked where those are, she said, "Good question. I think those are the conversations we very fairly need to have."
Several MPs predicted Wednesday's showdown would fizzle.
"I don't think anything's going to happen on Wednesday," said Humber River-Black Creek MP Judy Sgro. "We're here to get ready for the next election. In order to do that we need to all be working together, there needs to be one issue, and that is getting elected and serving Canadians. That's it."
Sydney-Victoria MP Jaime Battiste said he anticipated a "normal caucus meeting."
Although the job of Liberal leader is not open, there is already a sense of movement among his would-be successors, with some making their intentions clearer - albeit not all publicly.
Sources inside the Liberal party, speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations, say François-Philippe Champagne and Mélanie Joly have had conversations with colleagues about organizing future leadership bids, although Champagne denied to the Star on Monday that his calls were about organizing.
Former B.C. premier Christy Clark suggested to Radio-Canada she, too, is interested in the job.
Carney, who was tapped by Trudeau last month to advise the Liberal platform on economic policy, confirmed in a podcast interview with Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith that he is "taking steps" towards putting his name on a ballot at some unspecified point.
Polls have suggested the Liberals would have as hard a time winning the next election under any of the long-rumoured aspirants to Trudeau's job.
Asked if he thinks Carney should be leader, Long said no, adding the party should beware of any coronation, pointing to Michael Ignatieff's troubled tenure after Stéphane Dion cratered as Liberal leader.
"I think we need a wide open race," Long said. "We don't need to coronate anybody. I think that we have a lot of people in the party that would be wonderful leaders ... That's what leadership contests are for. I mean, democracy is messy."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 471 of 500

Premier isn't attempting to make sense, just noise

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-isnt-attempting-make-sense-just-noise/docview/3119060190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+isn%27t+attempting+to+make+sense%2C+just+noise&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Reading Doug Ford's proposed new traffic legislation, the initial relief might be that there's no immediate proposal to rip out existing bike lanes - although listening to the premier, that could well be coming next. But the restrictions on new bike lanes in places where they threaten to slow down traffic will have an immediate impact. On Tuesday, in fact, a Toronto city council committee is set to discuss new bike lanes on Parkside Drive where slowing down traffic is their stated purpose.
See, the proposal on Parkside, running along the east side of High Park between Lake Shore Boulevard and Bloor Street West, is about safety. There have been more than 1,400 collisions there in the past 10 years, three of them fatal. It's a residential and parkland street that serves essentially as a highway access ramp for thousands of motorists. Cars go too fast despite the posted 40 km/h limit - in one month alone last year, 2,279 speeding tickets were issued on that little two-kilometre street by photo radar, tops of any location in the city. Bike lanes are part of a plan to slow them down.
If the province forbids the lanes, I suppose the city could implement planter boxes, or speed bumps, or additional parking lanes. But the goal is to prevent crashes, injuries and deaths, so slowing the speed of traffic - bike lanes or otherwise - is the needed remedy.
In that specific context, do Ford's new bike lane rules make a lick of sense? If you asked him, I suspect his honest answer would be a shrug. Because making a lick of sense is not one of the criteria that guides Ford's policy announcements. Feasibility, practicality, efficacy - these concepts are not in the Ford Policy Shop tool box. His point is to make a grand gesture - swing for the fences! - in a way that shows he's doing things. It's an inclination of the premier's that keeps mucking things up for the people whose job it is to try to make the city work in Toronto.
Remember when he cut the size of city council in half? Or tried to slash the budget of Toronto public health? Or when he took over all transit rail construction? Or when he decided to do something splashy at Ontario Place? Or when he shuttered safe drug consumption sites?
The flagship example - not specific to Toronto - is likely Buck-a-Beer. All these years later, no one sells beer for a buck in Ontario, of course. Beyond headline-grabbing promotions, they were never going to. No one can turn a profit selling it for one dollar. But the actual price does not matter. What matters is that Ford can shout about allowing buck-a-beer sales.
Look at the plan to tunnel under Highway 401, it's buck-a-beer all the way down. Patent nonsense from any practical point of view (never mind the cost: look at the road closures and traffic on Queen Street while we dig a subway under it; now imagine 40 years of traffic on the 401 while they tunnel under it). He's not doing it because it makes sense. He's doing it to demonstrate the absurd lengths he'll go to to move traffic.
Occasionally this compulsion can work in Toronto's favour. One day we may thank him for the Ontario Line subway that showed he was the subway premier. The mayor and council already appreciate the budget room from when he took over the Gardiner to prove he was the highway premier.
But when Toronto's government benefits, it appears to be a happy accident. And when, so often, it doesn't, the practical, street-level concerns raised by the province's grand gestures don't even merit an afterthought. Today, Ford knows some people are very angry about bike lanes and everyone is fed up with traffic. So it's big-time gestures about highways and cycle-path bans.
Funny thing about bike lanes in Toronto: For all the audible rage you hear about them, only two city councillors voted against the hot-spot Bloor West lanes near the Kingsway Ford is now grandstanding on back when they were approved in 2020. The three major mayoral candidates who tried to make opposition to bike lanes an issue in the 2023 election got less than 15 per cent of the vote combined.
And I can tell you residents around Parkside Drive are very concerned with the safety of that street. It may be a place where Ford can get his headline about standing up for the flow of traffic, and I don't doubt it will serve his electoral needs. But it's a place where city council has every impetus to try to slow traffic down, one way or the other. As is so often the case, they're just going to have to work around the premier's grand gestures to accomplish their simple goal.
CAPTION: Parkside Drive may be a place where Doug Ford can get his headline about standing up for the flow of traffic, but it's a place where Toronto city council has every impetus to try to slow traffic down, one way or the other, Edward Keenan writes.RichardLautensToronto Star
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Subject: Speed limits; Traffic flow; Roads & highways; Traffic control

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Title: Premier isn't attempting to make sense, just noise

Document 472 of 500

Philpott to tackle family doctor shortage: Former federal Liberal minister will aim to connect every Ontarian to a physician in five years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/philpott-tackle-family-doctor-shortage/docview/3119060152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Philpott+to+tackle+family+doctor+shortage%3A+Former+federal+Liberal+minister+will+aim+to+connect+every+Ontarian+to+a+physician+in+five+years&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the face of mounting criticism over a worsening shortage of family doctors, Premier Doug Ford's government is turning to Queen's University medical school director Dr. Jane Philpott to solve the problem.
The former federal Liberal health minister will head a "primary care action team" aimed at connecting every Ontarian to a family doctor within five years. Her appointment takes effect Dec. 1.
Philpott's challenge is massive: 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a primary-care physician, a number that is expected to top four million in the next couple of years as the province grows and aging family doctors retire.
The Ontario Medical Association (OMA) estimates the province needs at least 2,500 more primary-care physicians and says as many as one in four Ontarians is without a family doctor.
"I am thrilled that we now have someone who is going to be very focused on ensuring that every Ontario resident who wants one is going to have a family physician ... in five years," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said Monday as the legislature resumed following an unusually long five-month summer break amid speculation about an early election next year.
Meanwhile, a switch to a new supplier last month has left home-care patients under the Ontario Health atHome agency without basics like gauze and bandages - or even painkillers and sedatives - for the past month.
"Patients are waiting days, rather than a few hours for supplies, for important end-of-life medications, for pain medication at home," said OMA president Dr. Dominik Nowak.
That has resulted in "a state of panic," forcing some patients or their families to local emergency rooms for essential supplies, New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles told the legislature's question period.
"Doctors have described the situation as utter chaos after this government suddenly switched suppliers with almost no notice," she said.
Jones called the situation "absolutely unacceptable" and said her officials have been working to fix the problem since it emerged.
"We are not going to allow this to continue. I am on it."
Opposition parties signalled they will pressure the government on health care this fall with Ford seemingly more concerned about bike lanes, building a tunnel under Highway 401 and spending as much as $1 billion to get beer, wine and other alcoholic drinks into convenience stores a year earlier than planned.
Stiles was skeptical about the Philpott appointment.
"I have enormous respect for her," she said, but added "this is not a government that listens to experts, let alone their own experts ... we'll wait to see what they do."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie noted Ford promised in the 2018 election campaign to end "hallway health care" in hospitals, a problem that has grown to about 2,000 patients a day after six years of Progressive Conservative rule.
"Yet again, we see this government appointing an expert or a panel of experts only to write a report which they will ignore," Crombie told reporters, adding she also has respect for Philpott, who was a keynote speaker to the Ontario Liberal party's policy convention in London last month.
To put the doctor shortage into perspective, the Liberals pointed out the 2.5 million Ontarians without a family physician are roughly equivalent to the populations of Ottawa, Mississauga and Brampton combined.
The OMA's Nowak, a family doctor at Women's College Hospital, praised Philpott's appointment as "a step in the right direction" but maintained the province can't wait five years to solve "the crisis in primary care."
"People are using emergency departments as their front door to the health system," she said. "People are getting diagnosed with cancer and other life-changing things in the emergency room these days."
In a statement released by the government, Philpott said "Ontario can build a health system where the guarantee of access to primary care is as automatic as the assurance that every child will be assigned to a public school in their neighbourhood.
"Our goal will be for 100 per cent of Ontarians to be attached to a family doctor or nurse practitioner working in a publicly funded team, where they receive ongoing, comprehensive care and people can access that care in a timely way."
Philpott's school analogy, however, does not account for the fact that in some fast-growing areas of Toronto, school boards have posted signs warning new residents that their children may not be able to attend a neighbourhood school because of overcrowding and may have to go to a school further away.
Philpott, who is also dean of the faculty of health sciences at Queen's and has spent 30 years in family medicine and on global health initiatives, will oversee a Health Ministry team that will look to the "Periwinkle model" she devised with colleagues in the Kingston-area Frontenac, Lennox and Addington Ontario health team to expand access to primary care, including weekends and evenings.
While Jones has invested $110 million in primary health care teams across the province to connect about 328,000 people to family doctors, the doctor shortage persists. In Sault Ste. Marie, for example, a major clinic cut loose several thousand patients earlier this year because it didn't have enough physicians to care for them.
The OMA has complained about a heavy administrative burden on family doctors that is taking time away from patient care, and encouraged the government to streamline paperwork with a centralized referral system for things like specialists and MRIs.
Ontario is opening two new medical schools and has expanded the number of medical school spots available, but it will take time for those students to graduate.
CAPTION: The Ford government on Monday appointed Queen's University medical school director Dr. Jane Philpott, shown in 2019, to oversee an effort to connect 2.5 million Ontarians with a primary-care physician.Chris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 473 of 500

Die-in at Queen's Park targets Doug Ford's plan to close 10 safe injection sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/die-at-queens-park-targets-doug-fords-plan-close/docview/3119258542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Die-in+at+Queen%27s+Park+targets+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+close+10+safe+injection+sites&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Activists staged a die-in at Queen's Park on Monday in protest of the provincial government's plan to shutter 10 safe injection sites across Ontario, which some say will be a "death sentence."
About 200 advocates, front-line workers, health-care providers and families of people lost to the drug poisoning crisis were among those lying down outside the provincial legislature in opposition to the Doug Ford government's plan to shutter the safe injection sites.
Supporters chanted "Harm reduction saves lives" and brandished signs with slogans such as "Save our sites or fill your morgues" as they heard stories from people who have lost loved ones to overdoses or have been traumatized by the health-care system when seeking treatment.
One of those people is Hannah Stahl, a street nurse who lost her brother Dalton to an overdose in 2017 in Hamilton, where there were no safe injection sites at the time. The current site in Hamilton is one of the 10 slated to close.
"He had recently been released from jail and he was really falling through the cracks of the health-care system," Stahl said.
"It's really pertinent when we have a toxic drug supply as dangerous as it is right now that people have a safe place to go," Stahl added. "Because there's no point in offering more detox beds if people are dying."
In August, the Ford government announced new zoning restrictions requiring safe injection sites, also known as Consumption Treatment Service (CTS) sites, to be 200 metres from schools and daycares, which will result in 10 of the province's sites having to close by March 31, 2025, including five in Toronto.
The sites offer needle exchange, supplies, supervision and drug safety testing to people who use drugs.
Danny Dobric, who has used opioids for more than a decade, said he knows at least 30 people who have died of overdoses when using alone, including two former partners.
"The times that I wasn't there, look what happened," he said. His wife died in 2000, his girlfriend last year.
He said he has tried numerous treatment programs but still depends on safe consumption sites as a lifeline. He is anxious about what might happen if they close.
"I know if I go under, someone's right there, because you only have a few minutes," he said. "I use by myself. So anything can happen."
Supporters of the sites say they not only save lives, but provide a pathway for people to receive treatment services, while critics argue they increase crime and social disorder in the neighbourhoods that have them.
The sites have always been controversial, but they received renewed criticism after Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two, was killed by a stray bullet last summer near Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre.
Ford has said he's personally opposed to the sites, calling them a "failed policy" without providing clear evidence for his reasoning.
He said he's in favour of getting people into treatment, and the government announced plans to spend $378 million on 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs with 375 supportive housing units as well as treatment beds.
In a statement, Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Health, said each site slated to close will have the opportunity to convert into a HART hub.
"Communities, parents and families across Ontario have made it clear that the presence of drug consumption sites near schools and daycares is leading to serious safety problems ... That's why our government is taking action to keep communities safe, while supporting the recovery of those struggling with opioid addiction," she said.
Dr. Nanky Rai, a family doctor and community organizer who spoke at the event, said comparing treatment to consumption sites is a false dichotomy, adding that the injection sites offer wraparound services such as counselling and referrals to other health-care professionals, in addition to lifesaving measures such as overdose reversals and drug testing services.
"The provincial government is using this moment to be like, 'Let's attack these handful of sites that are doing incredibly lifesaving work and create this bogus dichotomy about there needing to be more treatment', as if treatment doesn't exist through these sites," Rai said.
She believes closing the sites will undoubtedly lead to more fatalities.
"It's a death sentence for clients I work with, for their loved ones, for people in my life personally, and we know that almost every family in Ontario at this point has been affected by the drug poisoning crisis."
CAPTION: Toronto street nurse Hannah Stahl lost her brother Dalton to an overdose in 2017 in Hamilton, where there were no safe injection sites at the time.A man draws a chalk outline around Toronto street nurse Hannah Stahl during the "die-in."About 200 people staged a "die-in" at Queen's Park in protest of the provincial government's plan to shutter 10 safe injection sites across Ontario.

Document 474 of 500

Doug Ford's proposed bike lane legislation is just another one of the premier's empty grand gestures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-proposed-bike-lane-legislation-is-just/docview/3119258527/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+proposed+bike+lane+legislation+is+just+another+one+of+the+premier%27s+empty+grand+gestures&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Reading Doug Ford's proposed new traffic legislation, the initial relief might be that there's no immediate proposal to rip out existing bike lanes - although listening to the premier, that could well be coming next. But the restrictions on new bike lanes in places where they threaten to slow down traffic will have an immediate impact. On Tuesday, in fact, a Toronto city council committee is set to discuss new bike lanes on Parkside Drive where slowing down traffic is their stated purpose.
See, the proposal on Parkside, running along the east side of High Park between Lake Shore Blvd and Bloor St. W., is about safety. There have been more than 1,400 collisions there in the past 10 years, three of them fatal. It's a residential and parkland street that serves essentially as a highway access ramp for thousands of motorists. Cars go too fast despite the posted 40 km/h limit - in one month alone last year, 2,279 speeding tickets were issued on that little two-kilometre street by photo radar, tops of any location in the city. Bike lanes are part of a plan to slow them down.
If the province forbids the lanes, I suppose the city could implement planter boxes, or speed bumps, or additional parking lanes. But the goal is to prevent crashes, injuries and deaths, so slowing the speed of traffic - bike lanes or otherwise - is the needed remedy.
In that specific context, do Ford's new bike lane rules make a lick of sense? If you asked him, I suspect his honest answer would be a shrug. Because making a lick of sense is not one of the criteria that guides Ford's policy announcements. Feasibility, practicality, efficacy - these concepts are not in the Ford Policy Shop tool box. His point is to make a grand gesture - swing for the fences! - in a way that shows he's doing things. It's an inclination of the premier's that keeps mucking things up for the people whose job it is to try to make the city work in Toronto.
Remember when he cut the size of city council in half? Or tried to slash the budget of Toronto public health? Or when he took over all transit rail construction? Or when he decided to do something splashy at Ontario Place? Or when he shuttered safe drug consumption sites?
The flagship example - not specific to Toronto - is likely Buck-a-Beer. All these years later, no one sells beer for a buck in Ontario, of course. Beyond headline-grabbing promotions, they were never going to. No one can turn a profit selling it for one dollar. But the actual price does not matter. What matters is that Ford can shout about allowing buck-a-beer sales.
Look at the plan to tunnel under the 401, it's buck-a-beer all the way down. Patent nonsense from any practical point of view (never mind the cost: look at the road closures and traffic on Queen while we dig a subway under it, now imagine 40 years of traffic on the 401 while they tunnel under it). He's not doing it because it makes sense. He's doing it to demonstrate the absurd lengths he'll go to to move traffic.
Occasionally this compulsion can work in Toronto's favour. One day we may thank him for the Ontario Line subway that showed he was the subway Premier. The mayor and council already appreciate the budget room from when he took over the Gardiner to prove he was the highway premier.
But when Toronto's government benefits, it appears to be a happy accident. And when, so often, it doesn't, the practical, street-level concerns raised by the province's grand gestures don't even merit an afterthought. Today, Ford knows some people are very angry about bike lanes and everyone is fed up with traffic. So it's big-time gestures about highways and cycle-path bans.
Funny thing about bike lanes in Toronto. For all the audible rage you hear about them, only two city councillors voted against the hot-spot Bloor West lanes near the Kingsway Ford is now grandstanding on back when they were approved in 2020. The three major mayoral candidates who tried to make opposition to bike lanes an issue in the 2023 election got less than 15 per cent of the vote combined.
And I can tell you residents around Parkside Drive are very concerned with the safety of that street. It may be a place where Ford can get his headline about standing up for the flow of traffic, and I don't doubt it will serve his electoral needs. But it's a place where city council has every impetus to try to slow traffic down, one way or the other. As is so often the case, they're just going to have to work around the premier's grand gestures to accomplish their simple goal.
CAPTION: Not everyone wants to see the proposed bike lanes installed on Parkside Drive.

Subject: Speed limits; Councils; Cities; Roads & highways

Title: Doug Ford's proposed bike lane legislation is just another one of the premier's empty grand gestures

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Foreign travel by school trustees should be rare at best, education minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/foreign-travel-school-trustees-should-be-rare-at/docview/3119257840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Foreign+travel+by+school+trustees+should+be+rare+at+best%2C+education+minister+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has clipped the wings of jet-setting school board trustees, saying international travel should happen rarely, if at all.
Slamming a $145,000 summer trip to Italy taken by four elected officials in the Brantford Catholic board - whose governance is now under review - Dunlop said "frankly, I'm very disappointed and I'm hearing the same from taxpayers across the province."'
When asked if trustees should ever take trips out of the country, Dunlop said "there can be some specific circumstances, but typically that is not something that does happen. So, there are specific times - but not for trips to Italy for artwork or going for baseball games."
The latter was a reference to a spending controversy by administrators in the London-area Thames Valley District School Board who attended a three-day retreat at the hotel at the Rogers Centre in the summer, on days the Toronto Blue Jays were playing. Dunlop has sent an auditor in to that board as well to investigate.
While the four high-flying Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board trustees have said they will repay the cost of the Italy trip and look for outside help to pay for the statues purchased overseas, Dunlop has said she is concerned "that accountability was only taken after my ministry and the public expressed clear concerns for the misuse of taxpayer dollars."
Before the trip, trustees at the board had approved new expense rules allowing for first-class travel for long-haul flights, upgraded hotel rooms and, in some circumstances, alcohol for hospitality. The board has yet to release spending details of the trip.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, a former school board trustee, said the travel and artwork purchases by the Brant Haldimand trustees "looks like a particularly unfortunate decision. I think especially right now, when our schools are really struggling, our students are really struggling, not having the resources and supports they need, it's on all of us as elected officials to take that responsibility very seriously."
Meanwhile, Dunlop said the investigator sent in to look at a field trip taken by students at 15 schools in the Toronto District School Board that saw kids as young as eight marching in a protest and joining in chants will look at "what happened leading up to it, what happened during the school trip and afterwards as well."
Patrick Case, a lawyer and human rights expert, "is on the case now," she said, adding "he is ready to hit the ground running" and will interview teachers, principals as well as looking at communications sent home to parents about the field trip.
Dunlop expects a report before the end of the year.
The September outing was at the Grassy Narrows River Run, a community event to support the First Nation and its efforts to address mercury contamination there.
Parents had been told it was an educational event and that students would not take part in the rally - but many teachers and students did end up joining in. Videos have surfaced online showing kids and teachers marching alongside pro-Palestinian protesters and chanting "From Turtle Island to Palestine, occupation is a crime."
CAPTION: Ontario Minister Education Minister Jill Dunlop says she's concerned "that accountability was only taken after my ministry and the public expressed clear concerns for the misuse of taxpayer dollars" at the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic board.A rendering from the website of sculpture studio Giuseppe Stuflesser in South Tyrol, Italy, shows a statue of St. Padre Pio that matches the one the Catholic school board shared with the Brantford Expositor.

Document 476 of 500

Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-bill-limit-new-bike-lanes-will-also/docview/3119063106/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+bill+to+limit+new+bike+lanes+will+also+%27review%27+existing+ones&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a salvo at the city of Toronto, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are hinting at the end of bike lanes on Yonge and Bloor streets and Eglinton and University avenues.
Under sweeping new legislation tabled Monday by Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, the Highway Traffic Act is being amended to say "the Ministry may review existing bicycle lanes" and municipalities must receive provincial approval before installing new lanes.
"We've seen Toronto become one of the most congested cities in the entire world and this is really about a reasonable approach," said Sarkaria, who commutes to Brampton daily and often speaks of being stuck on University Avenue south of Queen's Park.
"We're seeing gridlock in Toronto, whether it be Bloor, University or Yonge Street," said the minister, whose office later confirmed that the province would cover any costs incurred by municipalities to remove bike lanes.
His bill empowers the Ministry of Transportation to take "consideration of traffic flow" before approving any new lanes.
"When considering whether to approve the design for the construction of a bicycle lane, the Ministry may consider whether it would unduly diminish the orderly movement of motor vehicle traffic," the bill states.
Sarkaria stressed "we're not anti-bike lane," but insisted a "reasonable approach" is needed.
"There's absolutely a place for bikes and for bike lanes," he said, adding "major arterial roads" are not the best place for such lanes.
"We don't want lanes of traffic removed at a time where we're seeing a population growth that has been significant for the province of Ontario."
Those changes could impact existing bike lanes on Yonge, Bloor, Eglinton and University, among others.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario criticized the province for "significant overreach" into civic jurisdiction.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow has also pushed back at the usurping of municipal authority. Her office declined to comment on the legislation Monday and referred the Star to her previous statement, which warned that "ripping up our roads (to remove bike lanes) will make congestion worse," and said the province should tackle gridlock by completing the delayed Finch West and Eglinton LRTs.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford is relitigating battles from his tenure at city hall, where he was a councillor when his late brother, Rob Ford, was Toronto's mayor.
"He failed at city council ... and now he's just playing that all out again," said Stiles.
"But this is meddling in important issues that municipalities have a responsibility for. They're micromanaging municipal issues here when he's forgetting the basics of what a provincial government should be paying attention to: health care, housing, the cost of living, our schools," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford clearly "regrets not being elected mayor of the city of Toronto" in 2014 when he lost to John Tory.
"Municipal politicians, they know their residents, they know their roads, let them make those decisions," said Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor.
"The premier needs to stay in his lane. He thinks the solution to our $13-billion congestion gridlock problem is removing bike lanes. We have very serious issues at hand that aren't going to be solved by that. We need investment in public transit," she said.
But the Tories - who are considering an early election in 2025, a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - believe curbing bike lanes would be popular with commuters.
According to Statistics Canada data from May, 71.2 per cent of Toronto-area residents took a car, truck or van to work while 22.7 per cent rode transit and 5.4 per cent used "active transportation," meaning they walked or cycled.
In 2011, the most recent year separate cycling information was available, 1.2 per cent of Torontonians commuted to work by bicycle while 69.9 per cent drove or were passengers in a car, 23.3 per cent took public transit and 4.6 per cent walked.
Sarkaria's omnibus legislation would also expedite construction of Highway 413, the 52-kilometre freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, with an "accelerated environmental assessment process" that has ecologists concerned.
It would also slap hefty fines on protesters of up to $50,000 for a first offence for trying to thwart construction of Highway 413 and $100,000 for a subsequent violation.
"To disagree with the government is one thing, but to block critical infrastructure from being built is absolutely not acceptable. It wouldn't be acceptable on this project or any other project," the minister said.
On Thursday, Ford received applause from many of the 1,200 people at his Empire Club luncheon speech when he said his government was "bringing sanity back to bike lane decisions."
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes. We need to - and will - remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill," the premier said.
The Toronto and Region Board of Trade signalled openness to the province's plans, saying in a statement "decisions limiting road capacity, including adding bike lanes to major roads, must be driven by traffic and cycling data and a clear understanding of the implications for travel and commute times."
Dr. Samantha Green, a family physician and co-founder of the Doctors for Safe Cycling advocacy group, said bike lanes have been shown to reduce deaths and injuries to cyclists, and a "robust network of connected, separated bike lanes" would encourage more people to ride.
"So removing bike lanes is a bad decision for the health and safety of people in the province," she said.
The bill was the first legislation tabled as the house resumed after an extended 139-day summer recess.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford gestures in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Traffic flow; Commuting; Public transportation; Legislation; Roads & highways; Traffic control; Bicycling

Title: Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones

Document 477 of 500

Liberal MPs seek 'reflection and change' with letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-mps-seek-reflection-change-with-letter/docview/3119063090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MPs+seek+%27reflection+and+change%27+with+letter+calling+on+Justin+Trudeau+to+step+down&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Numerous Liberals MPs have signed a private letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down and are prepared to make their case at Wednesday's national caucus meeting as some leadership hopefuls are beginning to organize.
Publicly, however, many are holding their cards close, refusing to divulge either the letter's contents, its signatories or its organizers.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long, who failed in June to elicit caucus support for his call on Trudeau to step down, told the Star "there is obviously a letter. I've signed it." Long, who is not the organizer of the letter, declined to provide much detail about it - even about the number of MPs who had signed it. "Is it over 20, most likely, and under 40 - yeah," he said.
"It's basically just a letter that's asking for reflection and change," Long said. "I think the letter basically sets the tone for a very frank and open discussion, and I mean, obviously, about leadership."
Those on board with the letter believe Trudeau should step aside as leader, but Long, who said he is not an organizer, added he isn't advocating for a particular candidate to replace him.
But speculation about the would-be contenders is hot. Economist and former central banker Mark Carney has confirmed he will run for office at some point, although he remains coy about when, or what job he wants.
For now, the top job is Trudeau's.
Several MPs told the Star on Monday they had not been approached to sign the letter and had only heard about it in media reports, downplaying its impact and describing the agitators as a small, if vocal, minority.
Mona Fortier, who is charged with writing the Liberal election platform, summed up the comments of nearly a dozen other Liberal MPs when she said, "Nobody asked me to sign a phantom letter."
The call for Trudeau to step down stems not just from the Atlantic caucus but "a collective from across the country," Long said, pointing to polls in B.C., Ontario and the Atlantic region that "tell us the same thing - that we are not in a good spot."
Among Liberals, he said, "there's just a growing concern about the lack of direction and lack of change. I think the approach that, 'Let's just wait it out and see if Canadians come around,' or 'Let's just kind of run the clock a bit, and maybe Pierre Poilievre will make a mistake' - it's not going to work."
Trudeau still has "a lot of support in caucus" among the Liberals' 153 MPs, Long admitted, but among voters he contends there is a "genuine, personal dislike of the prime minister."
In Long's view, things have only worsened since his failed attempt to call for a national caucus meeting to confront Trudeau with concerns after the byelection loss of what should have been a safe Liberal seat in Toronto-St. Paul's.
"We're four months closer to an election, not seeing any direction, not seeing any sense of urgency," he said. "The stay-calm-and-carry-on approach is, I think, concerning."
That's a common concern even among MPs who say they support Trudeau's decision - which he has repeatedly expressed both publicly and privately - to stay on to lead the party into the next federal election campaign.
Pickering-Uxbridge MP Jennifer O'Connell said Trudeau is "our leader and he should stay. I think he's in the best position to lead us, but I think there does have to be serious conversations happening at caucus for the mood and the reflection of what we're all hearing in our riding."
O'Connell said she does not hear the kind of voter dislike for Trudeau that Long and P.E.I. MP Sean Casey have talked publicly about, but that the party needs to ensure there is not a "disconnect" with voters.
She said it must ensure "we are actually staying focused on things that people in ridings like mine really care about - the economy, overall affordability. The environment is still really important, and how kids or young people are going to afford housing as they grow up. Things like that really matter.
"I do think we have to have some honest conversations about that direction, we can't just be told what that direction is," she said.
If those concerns "gets listened to, then I think we're in a good position to move forward properly," she said, cautioning that the caucus has been promised things like advertising and communications strategies "for a while now." Asked where those are, she said, "Good question. I think those are the conversations we very fairly need to have."
Several MPs predicted Wednesday's showdown would fizzle.
"I don't think anything's going to happen on Wednesday," said Humber River-Black Creek MP Judy Sgro. "We're here to get ready for the next election. In order to do that we need to all be working together, there needs to be one issue, and that is getting elected and serving Canadians. That's it."
Sydney-Victoria MP Jaime Battiste said he anticipated a "normal caucus meeting."
Although the job of Liberal leader is not open, there is already a sense of movement among his would-be successors, with some making their intentions clearer - albeit not all publicly.
Sources inside the Liberal party say François-Philippe Champagne and Mélanie Joly have had conversations with colleagues about organizing future leadership bids, although Champagne denied to the Star on Monday that his calls were about organizing.
Former B.C. Premier Christy Clark suggested to Radio-Canada she, too, is interested in the job.
Mark Carney, who was tapped by Trudeau last month to advise the Liberal platform on economic policy, confirmed in a podcast interview with Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith that he is "taking steps" towards putting his name on a ballot at some unspecified point.
Polls have suggested the Liberals would have as hard a time winning the next election under any of the long-rumoured aspirants to Trudeau's job.
Asked if he thinks Carney should be leader, Long said no, adding the party should beware of any coronation, pointing to Michael Ignatieff's troubled tenure after Stéphane Dion cratered as Liberal leader.
"I think we need a wide open race," Long said. "We don't need to coronate anybody. I think that we have a lot of people in the party that would be wonderful leaders ... That's what leadership contests are for. I mean, democracy is messy."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is shown in the House of Commons on Sept. 25, 2024.

Parkside Drive bike lanes are a misallocation of public funds

Full text: Traffic on Parkside Drive
Will Doug Ford's bike-lane legislation torpedo a plan to improve safety on this Toronto road? Oct. 17
I have been a proud High Park homeowner for 49 years. The suggestion to remove one lane of traffic and convert that into a bike lane on Parkside Drive would be catastrophic for all concerned. It is already at the tipping point and would become totally congested. An extensive network of much safer bicycle-friendly side streets is already in place, ready to accommodate all bicyclists in this area. This major misallocation of public funds will actually hurt all parties and make the situation very much worse for everyone concerned.
Michael Vaughan, Toronto
Parkside Drive bicycle lane controversy
As a retired city land surveys technologist, I am intimately familiar with Toronto's arterial roads network. If the city is willing to spend millions of dollars for bike lanes, widen the west side of Parkside Drive from the curb to past the guide rails. This is only currently used as a dirt pedestrian footpath and there is a proper concrete sidewalk on the east side. As we know, most bike lanes are underused and Toronto has become the third worst city for traffic congestion.
Gunars Vestfals, Haliburton
We'll donate Ford's cheque to the other parties
Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 17
When we get our cheques from Premier Doug Ford, we will be sure to make donations to the other parties in appreciation of his early and costly election. Our fellow Ontarians are welcome to follow suit.
Sal Amenta, Stouffville
What about the homeless? Will Ford personally hand out their cheques?
So Premier Doug Ford once again wants to bribe me with money to buy my vote. As if. I took the last one, the licence plate rebate and gave it to my local hospital. I will do the same with this "gift." Is part of these billions of free dollars going to the wealthy in our communities as well? What about the thousands of homeless and displaced people in our province who are in desperate need? They will get nothing because they have no permanent residence, and most of them don't even have ID. Will Ford personally hand out their cheques? Well, they can't vote of course, so no. If the Ontario government was serious about helping their citizens, they would properly fund health care, education, the environment and the genuinely poor of our province. This ploy is so transparent, I can see into the back room.
Bonnie Beaudry, Burlington, ON
No facts to support Poilievre's accusationPierre Poilievre has taken politics to a new low. Justin Trudeau is willing to join him there, Oct. 17
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is lying about Conservatives being involved in issues of foreign interference. He makes this accusation without any proof. He could get the proof; however, he refuses to get the necessary security clearance. Instead, he lashes out like a child who knows nothing but still insists that he is right, yet he has no facts to support his position.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
With no meaningful climate change strategy, Ontario risks a repeat of the costly overbuilds of the past
Ontario's track record for projecting electricity demand over the last 50 years is very weak.The forecasted demand has consistently failed to materialize. Overbuilding supply in response to these projections was a major factor in the bankruptcy of the former provincial utility Ontario Hydro. The risks of a repeat of the costly overbuilds of the past are increased in Ontario as climate change alters the forecasted demand for electrification. The province has no meaningful climate change strategy, and no strategies around the electrification of key sectors like transportation or space heating. There are also questions about how the capital investments the government's proposed pathway imply - $400 billion or more over the next 25 years - will actually be financed and paid for.
Mark S. Winfield, Toronto
Netanyahu needs to listen to Linda McQuaig
Canada should be tougher on Israel for defying UN, Oct. 18
Linda McQuaig reveals the disdain with which the present Israeli government treats the United Nations by its attacking of UN aid workers in war-torn Gaza and by its blowing up of UN hospitals and refugee camps. After all, it was the UN that established the right of Jews to make a state out of Palestinian land, one of the first actions taken by the new governing body for world peace. Many Jews in Israel do not condone the militaristic actions of its leader. As Churchill once said, the UN would not give us heaven, but it would save us from hell. Netanyahu has instead created hell for Palestinians, for Lebanese and for Jews themselves.
Janine Murray, Napanee
The UNRWA shows no remorse
I am gobsmacked by this incredibly one-sided article. This war, not wanted by Israel, was started by Hamas. Somehow, the world denigrates Israel, not only for defending itself, but for trying to assure that an attack like this will never happen again. An attack that Iran, Hamas and the other proxies promise will not be the last. And UNRWA, the UN agency, which has admitted that several of its members may have taken part in the atrocities of Oct. 7, shows no remorse, along with the anti-Israel general body of the United Nations.
Dr. Mark Panzer, Toronto
In England they call it council housing
Simpler way to build affordable housing, Oct. 16
I consider the most significant part of this article was the statement "governments don't prioritize nonmarket housing." In many other countries local, provincial and federal governments have entered the housing market. I grew up in England in a house which was owned by the local council. My parents were low average income. We paid rent for the house. I fail to see why councils in cities and towns could not do the same in Canada. In most cities and towns there is plenty of government owned land, including the vacant land from schools that have closed in recent years. The different levels of government could quite easily pay builders to build small homes on that land or modular homes could be placed there. I realize the local people around those lands will object (NIMBYs) but the better good should prevail.
These homes could be rented to lower income people. The solution to the housing crisis is there. Just look outside the box.
Michael Neill, Thornhill
What goes after the bike lanes? Sidewalks?
I heard a rumour that Doug Ford now wants to tear up sidewalks in Toronto to make an extra car lane.
Herb Hyman, Thornbury
CAPTION: Not everyone wants to see the proposed bike lanes installed on Parkside Drive.

Document 479 of 500

Ford government appoints former federal Liberal minister to help tackle family doctor shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-appoints-former-federal-liberal/docview/3118819820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+appoints+former+federal+Liberal+minister+to+help+tackle+family+doctor+shortage&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the face of mounting criticism over a worsening shortage of family doctors, Premier Doug Ford's government is turning to Queen's University medical school director Dr. Jane Philpott to solve the problem.
The former federal Liberal health minister will head a "primary care action team" aimed at connecting every Ontarian to a family doctor within five years. Her appointment takes effect Dec. 1.
Philpott's challenge is massive: 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a primary care physician, a number that is expected to top four million in the next couple of years as the province grows and aging family doctors retire. The Ontario Medical association estimates the province needs at least 2,500 more primary care physicians.
"There's no one I trust more than Dr. Philpott with her considerable experience to keep moving us forward and get across the finish line of connecting everyone in the province to more convenient primary care," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said in a statement Monday as the legislature resumed following an unusually long five-month summer break amid speculation about an early election next year.
Opposition parties signalled they will pressure the government on health care with Ford seemingly more concerned about bike lanes, building a tunnel under Highway 401 and spending as much as $1 billion to get beer, wine and other alcoholic drinks into convenience stores a year earlier than planned.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles was skeptical about the Philpott appointment.
"I have enormous respect for her," she said, but added "this is not a government that listens to experts, let along their own experts ... we'll wait to see what they do."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie noted that Ford promised in the 2018 election to end "hallway health care" in hospitals, a problem that has worsened to about 2,000 patients a day after six years of Progressive Conservative rule.
"Yet again, we see this government appointing an expert or a panel of experts only to write a report which they will ignore," Crombie told reporters, adding she also has respect for Philpott, who was a keynote speaker to the Ontario Liberal party's policy convention in London last month.
To put the doctor shortage into perspective, the Liberals pointed out the 2.5 million Ontarians without a family physician are roughly equivalent to the populations of Ottawa, Mississauga and Brampton combined.
In a statement released by the government, Philpott said "Ontario can build a health system where the guarantee of access to primary care is as automatic as the assurance that every child will be assigned to a public school in their neighbourhood."
"Our goal will be for 100 per cent of Ontarians to be attached to a family doctor or nurse practitioner working in a publicly funded team, where they receive ongoing, comprehensive care and people can access that care in a timely way," she continued.
Philpott's school analogy, however, does not account for the fact that in some fast-growing areas of Toronto school boards have posted signs warning new residents that their children may not be able to attend a neighbourhood school because of overcrowding and may have to go to a school further away.
Philpott, who is also dean of the faculty of health sciences at Queen's and has spent 30 years in family medicine and on global health initiatives, will oversee a Health Ministry team that will look to the "Periwinkle model" she devised with colleagues in the Kingston-area Frontenac, Lennox and Addington Ontario health team to expand access to primary care, including weekends and evenings.
While Jones has invested $110 million in primary health care teams across the province to connect about 328,000 more people to family doctors, the doctor shortage persists. In Sault Ste. Marie, for example, a major clinic cut loose several thousand patients earlier this year because it didn't have enough physicians to care for them.
The Ontario Medical Association has complained about a heavy administrative burden on family doctors that is taking time away from patient care, and encouraged the government to streamline paperwork.
Ontario is opening two new medical schools and has expanded the number of medical school spots available, but it will take time for those students to graduate. The province is also trying to recruit more doctors trained in other countries.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queen's Park as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Monday.

How property flippers transformed this Toronto street + What's going on with B.C.'s election?

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, October 21 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on property flippers remaking Toronto streets, disabled people being pushed into poverty and the results of B.C.'s nail-biting election.
DON'T MISSHow property flippers transformed this Toronto street
Exmoor Drive in Etobicoke was once dotted with nine "strawberry boxes" - tiny single-storey homes that provided one of the most affordable entry points into Toronto's real estate market, Diana Zlomislic writes. But over the past decade, nearly all these homes have been bought up, demolished and replaced with custom houses that sell for at least twice as much as their predecessors. Where are young families supposed to go?
Rising prices: Across Toronto, renovators are buying up more affordable older housing and replacing them with larger, more expensive homes. Here's how the practice is remaking the city.The great migration: Ballooning housing prices and a downturn in Toronto's real estate market have flippers seeking more affordable pastures outside the city. Take a look at where renovators are going and how you'll be affected.Toronto's high cost of living is leaving disabled people with "no hope"
Life in the GTA is more expensive than ever, especially if you're living with a disability. A new report found it can cost up to 39 per cent more for disabled people not just to survive, but to thrive in the city, Reagan McSwain reports. It's partly because of all the extra out-of-pocket expenses disabled people face, from necessary equipment to grocery delivery. Here's what can be done.
Below the line: Nearly one million disabled Canadians live under the poverty line, accounting for almost 40 per cent of all people experiencing poverty, according to the report.Wait, what? Ottawa's new Canada Disability Benefit was intended to drastically reduce poverty rates among disabled Canadians. But it's expected to lift just four per cent of those eligible out of poverty when it rolls out three years from now.What's going on with B.C.'s election?
People in British Columbia are waking up to a reshaped political landscape following a nail-biting weekend election - but they'll have to wait another week for the official results. Neither the Conservatives nor the incumbent NDP secured the 47 seats needed for a majority government, and manual recounts triggered in two key ridings will take days to sort out. Take a look at what's at stake.
By the numbers: Premier David Eby's NDP is leading or elected in 46 ridings, and John Rustad's Conservatives in 45 - a stunning turnout for a party that received less than two per cent of the vote last election.Luke LeBrun's take: Rustad disagrees with the basic science on climate change, regrets being vaccinated and once floated the idea of prosecuting doctors "Nuremberg"-style in COVID-19 trials. His party's success is a sign something's deeply broken in Canadian democracy.WHAT ELSE
MPPs are returning to Queen's Park today. This is their first order of business.
Canada's fight with India shows New Delhi is watching Sikhs around the world.

Document 481 of 500

Four ways to mock Doug Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/four-ways-mock-doug-ford-trying-buy-our-fleeting/docview/3118819750/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+ways+to+mock+Doug+Ford+for+trying+to+buy+our+fleeting+love&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Early next year, Ontario Premier Doug Ford apparently plans to give all 16 million of us about $200, possibly just before an election. My household will rake in a whole $400. Tax-free. Gosh. Supernice!
I take it this is a bribe to win my vote. But no. How did Shakespeare put it: "Roses have thorns, and silver fountains mud; And loathsome canker lies in sweetest bud."
As the Shakespearean TV parody "Upstart Crow" makes clear, the playwright did over-egg his metaphors. Simply put, Shakespeare opposed corruption. He wouldn't have liked Ford's canker at all.
But my vote cannot be bought, especially not for a lousy 400 bucks. In 2000, the revolting Premier Mike Harris did the same. Even then, I donated his gift to charity, probably UNICEF.
In 2025, I suppose I'll do the same, sending it to the UN which feeds the children Israel is starving and maiming in Gaza. But that won't annoy Ford or even register with him. My aim is to make him squirm.
Here's a list of ways to spend money as a statement: (For example, the winner refusing the Scotiabank-assisted Giller Prize would be moving and infinitely stylish too.) 1. Decently to ease my conscience. 2. Publicly as a declaration. 3. Wastefully to show contempt. 4. Locally to help the city Ford despises.
We've done Decency. Let's move to public statements.
There are infinite ways to debase Ford's choice loudly but I could productively plant a sapling at Ontario Place, where they just razed 800 trees to make way for a huge spa. But memorial trees with a little plaque dedicated in my case "To Tommy Tree, the oak that once played happily here" cost $738. Perhaps I could ask my daughters to donate their bribe. I'll get back to you.
Next is wasting the money. Born to a penny-pinching Scottish mother and a free-spending Indian father, I take my dad's side. I'm good at this. I just spent 92 euros on a Svenskt Tenn birch tray celebrating the Swedish fish harvest plus another 55 euros to ship it to Canada so I can nail it to a wall.
But that only adds up to $220. They also sell a lovely placemat honouring the painfully shy Baltic turbot. I could use up my husband's bribe on feeding my passion for art displaying the tragic destruction of the natural world.
Or. I could buy a $400 pair of velvet lounge pants, the kind you can swim in all winter, to replace the sweatpants I wear to work at my home office, six steps down the hall from where we sleep and all pants reside. This life I am living, it is not a good life. Faites un effort, the French say. And I say, look at those fluffy trousers.
We move on to the local spending section of mocking Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love.
There's an economic concept Ford often overlooks. Give spare money to people who desperately need it, because they will spend every cent locally, enriching Toronto businesses, cooking nutritious local food for their children, keeping the local landlord quiescent, buying snowsuits, hockey gear, mittens and crayons and keeping tiny local Canadians distracted and unaware of their poverty.
In other words, raise welfare and disability payments. That money flows through Toronto like water, enriching all of us. It keeps people stable and housed, out of those shocking tent encampments that always make me think the end is near for this city.
This is how I imagine it all began, a blowsy Ontario premier sitting in his office with his feet on the desk like Trump, telling his pals, "Whores will have their trinkets" and blasting $100 bills out of a peashooter at every passing voter.
Will it work?
CAPTION: Heather Mallick's four ways to spend Premier Doug Ford's $200: 1. Decently to ease my conscience. 2. Publicly as a declaration. 3. Wastefully to show contempt. 4. Locally to help the city Ford despises.

Title: Four ways to mock Doug Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 21, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 482 of 500

Mocking Ford for trying to buy love

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mocking-ford-trying-buy-love/docview/3118627139/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mocking+Ford+for+trying+to+buy+love&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Heather Mallick is a Toronto-based columnist covering current affairs for the Star. Follow her on X: @HeatherMallick
