Document 483 of 500

Doug Ford is coming after bike lanes. What will he target next?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-coming-after-bike-lanes-what-will-he/docview/3118627117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+coming+after+bike+lanes.+What+will+he+target+next%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People across Ontario, whether or not they ride a bicycle, have good reason to worry about Premier Doug Ford's scapegoating of cyclists and bike lanes as the cause of road congestion.
The Premier is proposing restrictions on bike lanes as an answer to congestion, no matter that a minuscule number of roads in Ontario - including a mere four per cent in Toronto - actually have bike lanes.
The proposal does have a comforting appeal both by playing to certain prejudices and by distracting us from realities that require mature debate and leadership. It's much easier for Ford to punt the problem of traffic congestion to some future date, instead of admitting that he is out of ideas. The emperor has no clothes.
Ontarians should also be worried about Ford's electioneering tactic because of his obvious interference with democratically achieved municipal decisions. It's no secret that Ford's proposal to restrict bike lanes across the province relates to a Bloor Street West bike lane (near his suburban Etobicoke backyard) that he simply doesn't like.
The bike lane - part of a Complete Streets policy that seeks to balance the needs of all road users - was approved in a 21-1 vote by Toronto City Council after a thorough study, public consultation, and council debate.
The focal point of opposition to the Bloor bike lane is in The Kingsway, a wealthy neighbourhood that has the Premier's ear, much as eager developers apparently did before the Premier's Greenbelt gerrymandering.
The Premier first intervened on Bloor Street in October 2023, absurdly claiming that there was perhaps one cyclist per year. Today, this segment of road is part of a popular bikeway that, after years of advocacy, stretches 21 km across most of the city.
Significantly, in the June 2023 mayoral election in which bicycle-friendly Mayor Olivia Chow prevailed, candidates who railed against bike lanes (including the former police chief who had Ford's endorsement) got a cold shoulder from city voters.
Ontarians, whatever means of transport they use, shouldn't dismiss Ford's intervention as someone else's problem. A Premier who arbitrarily imposes his will on municipal decisions about bike lanes can't be trusted to keep his nose out of other local decisions. Will he next interfere in the siting of a community centre or the extension of a recreational cycling and walking path? In fact, he already has - the question is what will be next beyond Toronto.
In contrast to the Premier, Etobicoke city councillor Amber Morley showed leadership in the Bloor West debate, centring discussion on facts, the need for good urban planning (in a ward with soaring residential development), and the achievement of city policies that prioritize human life, vibrant communities, and climate action.
What most infuriates some of Morley's opponents, whose proximity to power had in the past preserved a car-first status quo that served them very well was that she would not bend obediently to their will.
Doug Ford's meddling in municipal decision-making diminishes his government and our democracy - and will do nothing to solve road congestion.
CAPTION: Critics of bike lanes on Bloor Street West are calling for their removal or modification, saying they slow traffic too much and aren't attracting many cyclists. Cycling advocates strongly disagree.
CREDIT: Albert Koehl Contributor

Subject: Decision making; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Will+Doug+Ford%27s+bike-lane+legislation+torpedo+a+plan+to+improve+safety+on+this+Toronto+road%3F+Oct.+17+Doug+Ford+poised+to+send+out+pre-election+cheques+to+16+million+Ontarians%2C+Oct.+17+Pierre+Poilievre+has+taken+politics+to+a+new+low.+Justin+Trudeau+is+willing+to+join+him+there%2C+Oct.+17+Canada+should+be+tougher+on+Israel+for+defying+UN%2C+Oct.+18+Simpler+way+to+build+affordable+housing%2C+Oct.+16&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Parkside Drive
bike-lane plan
is misguided
Will Doug Ford's bike-lane legislation torpedo a plan to improve safety on this Toronto road? Oct. 17
I have been a proud High Park homeowner for 49 years. The suggestion to remove one lane of traffic and convert that into a bike lane on Parkside Drive would be catastrophic for all concerned. It is already at the tipping point and would become totally congested. An extensive network of much safer bicycle-friendly side streets is already in place, ready to accommodate all bicyclists in this area. This major misallocation of public funds will actually hurt all parties and make the situation very much worse for everyone concerned.
Michael Vaughan, Toronto
As a retired city land surveys technologist, Iam intimately familiar with Toronto's arterial roads network. If the city is willing to spend millions of dollars for bike lanes, widen the west side of Parkside Drive from the curb to past the guide rails. This is only currently used as a dirt pedestrian footpath and there is a proper concrete sidewalk on the east side. As we know, most bike lanes are underused and Toronto has become the third worst city for traffic congestion.
Gunars Vestfals, Haliburton
What goes after the bike lanes? Sidewalks?
I heard a rumour that Doug Ford now wants to tear up sidewalks in Toronto to make an extra car lane.
Herb Hyman, Thornbury
Where are cheques for
the homeless?
Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 17
So Premier Doug Ford once again wants to bribe me with money to buy my vote. What about the thousands of homeless and displaced people in our province who are in desperate need? They will get nothing because they have no permanent residence, and most of them don't even have ID. Will Ford personally hand out their cheques? Well, they can't vote of course, so no. If the Ontario government was serious about helping their citizens, they would properly fund health care, education, the environment and the genuinely poor of our province. This ploy is so transparent, I can see into the back room.
Bonnie Beaudry, Burlington
Poilievre's accusations
short on facts
Pierre Poilievre has taken politics to a new low. Justin Trudeau is willing to join him there, Oct. 17
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is lying about Conservatives being involved in issues of foreign interference. He makes this accusation without any proof. He could get the proof; however, he refuses to get the necessary security clearance. Instead, he lashes out like a child who knows nothing but still insists he is right, yet he has no facts to support his position.
Clyde McDonald,
Bracebridge, Ont.
Ontario risking
a repeat of
costly overbuilds
Ontario's track record for projecting electricity demand over the past 50 years is very weak. The forecasted demand has consistently failed to materialize. Overbuilding supply in response to these projections was a major factor in the bankruptcy of the former provincial utility Ontario Hydro. The risks of a repeat of the costly overbuilds of the past are increased in Ontario as climate change alters the forecasted demand for electrification.
The province has no meaningful climate change strategy, and no strategies around the electrification of key sectors like transportation or space heating. There are also questions about how the capital investments the government's proposed pathway imply - $400 billion or more over the next 25 years - will actually be financed and paid for.
Mark S. Winfield, Toronto
Disdain for UN calls for tough response
Canada should be tougher on Israel for defying UN, Oct. 18
Linda McQuaig reveals the disdain with which the present Israeli government treats the United Nations by its attacking of UN aid workers in war-torn Gaza and by its blowing up of UN hospitals and refugee camps.
After all, it was the UN that established the right of Jews to make a state out of Palestinian land, one of the first actions taken by the new governing body for world peace. Many Jews in Israel do not condone the militaristic actions of its leader. As Churchill once said, the UN would not give us heaven, but it would save us from hell. Netanyahu has instead created hell for Palestinians, for Lebanese and for Jews themselves.
Janine Murray, Napanee, Ont.
UNRWA shows no remorse
I am gobsmacked by this incredibly one-sided article. This war, not wanted by Israel, was started by Hamas. Somehow, the world denigrates Israel, not only for defending itself, but for trying to assure that an attack like this will never happen again. An attack that Iran, Hamas and the other proxies promise will not be the last. And UNRWA, the UN agency, which has admitted that several of its members may have taken part in the atrocities of Oct. 7, shows no remorse, along with the anti-Israel general body of the United Nations.
Dr. Mark Panzer, Toronto
Look to England for housing fix
Simpler way to build affordable housing, Oct. 16
I consider the most significant part of this article was the statement "governments don't prioritize nonmarket housing." In many other countries local, provincial and federal governments have entered the housing market. I grew up in England in a house that was owned by the local council. My parents were low average income. We paid rent for the house.
I fail to see why councils in cities and towns could not do the same in Canada. In most cities and towns there is plenty of government-owned land, including the vacant land from schools that have closed in recent years. The different levels of government could quite easily pay builders to build small homes on that land or modular homes could be placed there.
These homes could be rented to lower-income people.
The solution to the housing crisis is there. Just look outside the box.
Michael Neill, Thornhill

Document 485 of 500

Election leaves B.C. in limbo: Recounts could settle winner after knife-edge result

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/election-leaves-b-c-limbo/docview/3118626976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Election+leaves+B.C.+in+limbo%3A+Recounts+could+settle+winner+after+knife-edge+result&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: British Columbia's political landscape is far from settled following an election that has yet to produce a clear winner, with recounts set for two key ridings that could determine who forms the next government, and the result won't be known for at least another week.
Elections BC said in a statement Sunday - the day after the election - that recounts will take place from Oct. 26 to 28 in the ridings of Juan de Fuca-Malahat and Surrey City Centre, where the NDP and Conservative candidates are separated by fewer than 100 votes.
The New Democrat candidates are leading by very thin margins in both ridings.
The Conservatives would have to win both to secure the narrowest of majorities, and if not, David Eby's incumbent NDP is poised to form a minority government provided it secures the co-operation of two Greens elected on Saturday.
The NDP is leading or elected in 46 ridings, while John Rustad's Conservatives are leading or elected in 45, but neither have reached the 47 seats required to form a majority government in B.C.'s 93-seat legislature after the initial count that wrapped up Sunday.
The initial count does not reflect about 49,000 absentee and mail-in ballots that will be included in the final count starting next Saturday, Elections BC said.
The recounts will be part of the final count taking place between Saturday and Oct. 28, though judicial recounts may occur if the difference between the top two candidates in a riding is less than 1/500th of the total ballots.
An estimated 57.4 per cent of registered voters cast ballots in the election, up from below 54 per cent in 2020.
With nearly 2,037,900 ballots cast, the statement from Elections BC said it's the most votes tallied in a B.C. provincial election.
Regardless of the outcome, it represented a stunning moment for the B.C. Conservatives, who received less than two per cent of the vote in the last election, but are now elected or leading in 45 ridings.
The NDP, who started the campaign last month with 55 seats, was elected or leading in 46, while the Green Party captured two seats.
Green campaign chair Adam Olsen said it was too early to speculate on the legislative makeup, who will form government or even the likelihood of a minority mandate for the eventual winner.
Olsen said he also would not speculate now on the possibility of a minority situation following this election and the shape of the legislature.
"Whatever the outcome at the end of this, when those writs are returned to the legislature, the goal here now for all 93 members of that place is to make it function," he said. "(Green Leader) Sonia Furstenau said multiple times throughout the campaign that neither of these other parties deserve a majority government and it looks like so far the people of B.C. agree with her."
Rustad told supporters on Saturday the B.C. Conservatives will look to topple the NDP as soon as possible if the election results in a minority government.
Eby said in a muted speech that a "clear majority" of voters supported "progressive values."
But he acknowledged that Rustad "spoke to the frustrations of a lot of British Columbians" when it came to costs of living and public safety.
"We've got to do better," Eby told supporters. "That was our commitment to British Columbians. We've got to do better, and we will do better."
Furstenau lost her seat but said her party was nevertheless poised to play a "pivotal role" in the legislature.
David Black, a professor at Greater Victoria's Royal Roads University, said the Greens retaining official party status by winning two seats could give them "some real bargaining power" in what is shaping up to be a very tight legislature.
"The Greens are going to be the kingmakers here whatever happens, if the race is as close as it is right now between two larger parties," he said in an interview on election night.
B.C. Conservatives president Aisha Estey called her party's showing "the ultimate underdog story" and relished what she called a "historic campaign."
Rustad's unlikely rise came after he was thrown out of the Opposition, then known as the BC Liberals, joined the Conservatives, quickly was acclaimed leader, and steered them to a level of popularity that led to the collapse of his old party, now called BC United - all in just two years.
That was brought about in large part by the Shakespearean fall of Official Opposition Leader Kevin Falcon of BC United, who pushed the self-destruct button on his own party less than two months ago. Falcon had thrown Rustad out of the party in 2022 over his online backing for a climate-change skeptic. But it was Falcon who paid the price.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Dirk Meissner and Chuck Chiang The Canadian Press

Subject: Provincial elections; Election results; Legislatures; Voters; Voter behavior; Public opinion; Political analysis

Location: Canada; British Columbia Canada

Company / organization: Name: Elections BC; NAICS: 921190; Name: Conservative Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940; Name: New Democratic Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Title: Election leaves B.C. in limbo:   Recounts could settle winner after knife-edge result

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3118626976

Document 486 of 500

Pierre Poilievre says he wants provinces to overhaul their disability programs - and he could withhold federal money to make it happen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-says-he-wants-provinces-overhaul/docview/3118626902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+says+he+wants+provinces+to+overhaul+their+disability+programs+-+and+he+could+withhold+federal+money+to+make+it+happen&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says he wants provinces to overhaul their disability assistance programs so that disabled Canadians don't see working income clawed back from provincial benefits, and he could put billions in federal transfers at stake to make it happen.
Poilievre made that commitment in a 2023 video recently viewed by the Star, in which he referred to a private member's bill he introduced as the Conservative finance critic in 2018. If passed, the bill would have required those changes as a condition to receiving the Canada Social Transfer, a federal payment to the provinces that helps to cover the cost of social programs.
"For those roughly a million Canadians with disabilities who do work, we need to reform the benefit programs to reward that work. Right now, there are clawbacks if you're a person on disability and you get a job. Often, they take away your income, rental support, even medication," he said in the video, posted online by political commentator Darshan Maharaja and viewed just over 2,000 times.
"I will pass the fairness for workers with disabilities act, which will require provinces, as a condition of getting their federal money, to reform their systems to make sure that every time a person with disabilities earns an extra dollar, they're made better off and that they're not punished for that. Everybody should have the chance to put their talents to work for this great country of ours."
The change would be one step to address what many see as a thorny flaw in the social assistance programs offered across Canada. However, it raises questions about the approach of a Conservative leader who's routinely accused the Trudeau government of overreach and could lead to pushback from provinces antsy about the precedent it would set, said policy experts who spoke to the Star.
"Anything that helps impoverished people with disabilities get to live above the poverty line is a good step," said David Lepofsky, a lawyer and disability advocate in Ontario. "If the Tories are going to take this approach to federal power, there is more to do."
But "imagine trying to put conditions on something that's been unconditional since 1995," said Gillian Petit, an economist at the University of Calgary, pointing to Poilievre's proposal to tie the Canada Social Transfer to the reforms. This year, provinces and territories are expected to receive $16.9 billion from the Canada Social Transfer, with Ontario getting $6.5 billion.
"It would probably be a bit of a political fiasco."
Faced with that question in 2018 - before the bill was voted down by the Liberals - Poilievre pointed to delivery standards set out in the Canada Health Transfer and said this scenario justifies adding conditions.
Whether he is still willing to put billions in federal transfers at stake to implement those changes, however, remains unclear.
In a statement to the Star, Poilievre repeated his criticism of current systems that penalize disabled Canadians that work, but did not say if he would use the threat of withdrawing federal money to force change. And that is no different from what he said in the 2023 video, his office said, though it would not provide details on his proposal.
"As prime minister, I will be working with provinces to ensure workers with disabilities always benefit from working another hour, taking another shift, or accepting a job opportunity," Poilievre said.
Among the unanswered questions is how he would get provinces on board, how much working income he would want exempted from clawbacks, and how this principle would apply to non-cash benefits and towards federal programs.
In the 2018 bill, Poilievre proposed disallowing provinces from clawing back benefits for disabled Canadians on employment income up to $30,000 and any province that did risked losing their portion of the Canada Social Transfer. The bill also said Finance Canada should use available data to calculate clawbacks on benefits and the finance minister should "identify and consider" changes that could be made to federal taxes and benefits in order to reduce those clawbacks.
He said the principle of allowing disabled Canadians to keep their working income should be consistent across all levels of government and argued it would save governments money if people are able to work.
Right now, only a small portion of people who receive disability assistance are able to work, said Alexi White, the director of systems change at Maytree. For example, out of the 367,828 Ontario Disability Support Program cases in 2022/2023, 10 per cent reported working income, according to data from the social policy group.
But Jen Robson, an associate professor of public policy at Carleton University, said the proposal as outlined in the 2018 bill could lead to strong pushback from provinces, including legal challenges. Referencing Poilievre's housing bill, she said that would be another piece of legislation from the Conservative leader that overrides provincial jurisdiction with the "Ottawa-knows best" approach he's accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government of.
"It's well-intentioned but inherently flawed," she said. "It's very heavy handed."
She said it would be best to achieve the results Poilievre wants through negotiations rather than trying to force the provinces, similar to how the Liberals have approached the Canada Disability Benefit (CDB), which is slated to roll out next summer.
The Liberals had argued it would have been unconstitutional to disallow clawbacks in the legislation despite repeated pleas from advocates and some senators. So far, four provinces and two territories have said they won't claw back their own supports to recipients of the CDB after discussions with Disabilities Minister Kamal Khera, while another has increased its own supports.
Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives have not outlined their position on the federal benefit, but all opposition parties have pressed the Liberal government over the historic program that has been slammed as a disappointment by disability advocates. That benefit, which is expected to reach 600,000 Canadians at its peak, will be clawed back from its $200 monthly maximum at $23,000 in annual income, but allows for up to $10,000 in working income exemptions for eligible individuals.
The Liberals and other opposition parties have accused Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives of wanting to cut social programs, focusing their criticism on the federal dental and pharmacare programs.
"If Poilievre truly cared about supporting Canadians with disabilities, instead of putting out slogans, he would support this benefit and our affordable dental care plan which also supports persons with disabilities," a spokesperson for Khera said in a statement to the Star.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on Oct. 8, 2024.

Subject: Provinces; Conservatism; Workers with disabilities; Canadians; Opposition parties; People with disabilities; Disability; Prime ministers

Document 487 of 500

MPPs return after four-month break: Tories vow to fast-track highway projects as parties brace for possible early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpps-return-after-four-month-break/docview/3118626260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPPs+return+after+four-month+break%3A+Tories+vow+to+fast-track+highway+projects+as+parties+brace+for+possible+early+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After an extended 137-day summer break, MPPs return to Queen's Park for what could be the last session of the legislature before an election next year.
With Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives signalling they may choose to face voters more than a year before the scheduled June 2026 election, the fall sitting that begins Monday should be eventful.
As first disclosed by the Star last Wednesday, the Tories will use Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's Oct. 30 fall economic statement to announce 16 million Ontarians will receive cheques of around $200 early in the new year.
That move will cost the treasury more than $3.2 billion.
In a campaign-style speech to the Empire Club last Thursday, Ford vowed to push the economy forward with measures "to build critical infrastructure faster," such as 24-7 construction on Highway 413 and the Bradford Bypass, with Ontario's population expected to grow by two million by 2031.
"Basically everywhere you drive right now ... there's construction. I know it's a pain in the butt, but it's needed," he told a crowd of 1,200 at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.
"Our government remains focused on continuing to rebuild Ontario's economy starting with creating conditions in the environment for businesses to grow, thrive and prosper," the premier added.
Ford made no mention in his 20-minute speech of the doctor shortage, long emergency room wait times or his unfulfilled 2018 election promise to end "hallway health care" in hospitals.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "with the legislature returning after five long months, New Democrats will be taking on Doug Ford's costly failures and holding his government to account."
"We will be fighting for the things that help people build a good life - a home they can afford, a family doctor when they need one, a good public school, and a more affordable life," said Stiles.
"Ontarians are working harder than ever but they're feeling like they just can't get ahead," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford is scrambling to distract from the serious issues facing Ontarians, including the fact that more than 2.3 million people don't have a family doctor.
"They're all gimmicks and they're all distractions from the real issues," said Crombie, pointing to the Tories' intervention on municipal issues like curbing bike lanes and "fantasy" projects like a proposed 60-kilometre tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough.
"You hear the premier talking about bike lanes, you hear him talking about fictitious, nonsensical tunnels, but he's not talking about access to a family doctor. He's not talking about the nursing shortage in the hospitals," she said.
"He's not talking about the teacher shortage, or the fact that we don't have special ed teachers, or the fact that people can't afford housing. He's not talking about any of those things. He's talking about distractions."
Asked whether she expects Ford to call an election next year because he is currently leading in public opinion polls, Crombie said: "The RCMP investigation will play a large role in when this election is."
That's a reference to the Mounties' ongoing probe into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
The Star reported Oct. 9 that RCMP detectives have already interviewed at least eight current and former members of the premier's staff.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Ford is "clearly out of touch," as evidenced by his Highway 401 tunnel "pipe dream and fiscal nightmare" and spending as much as $1 billion to get beer and wine into convenience stores and big box outlets a year early.
"It appears he's in campaign mode and it also appears that he's blowing a lot of smoke to distract people from the real concerns," Schreiner added.
"When is the premier actually going to end 'hallway medicine' (in hospitals), which he promised in 2018 and now it's ballooned to 2,000 people a day?"
Ford ended the spring session a week earlier than scheduled on June 6 and shuffled his cabinet in a major overhaul, moving 14 ministers and expanding the executive council to 36, the largest in Ontario history.
While the summer recess usually ends the Monday after Labour Day, he extended it by six weeks, sparking criticism from opponents.
They charged the Tories were eager to avoid scrutiny on numerous challenges facing the province, such as homeless encampments, overcrowded classrooms, the dearth of doctors and the push to put more medical treatment in private clinics.
In a bid to change the channel on that narrative, the first piece of legislation being introduced Monday is from Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria: the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act."
It will slam the brakes on new bike lanes, requiring Toronto and other municipalities to get provincial approval before taking out lanes of traffic for cars and trucks on main roads.
"While strategically placed bike lanes are a critical part of every city ... what cities should not be doing, however, is taking away lanes of traffic on our most congested roads," Sarkaria told reporters last week, controversially blaming gridlock on bike lanes.
When Ford used Thursday's Empire Club speech to tout his minister for "bringing sanity back to bike lane decisions," hundreds in the audience spontaneously cheered.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes. We need to - and will - remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill," the premier said to more applause.
But the Association of Municipalities of Ontario criticized him for "significant overreach" into their jurisdiction and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow has pushed back at the attack on civic powers, saying that tackling gridlock requires "all types of transportation."
"The province should concentrate on their job of finally getting the Eglinton Crosstown and Finch LRT open, which will have a huge impact on congestion," said Chow.
With no date set for the opening of the Eglinton Crosstown, Schreiner questioned whether Ford is waiting until after an early election to get the transit line running in case there are service snafus that hurt him at the polls.
"If the premier's playing politics with transit, then he needs to be held accountable," the Green leader said.
Provincial transit agency Metrolinx has promised three months' advance notice of an opening date for the Eglinton line, but has not given that yet - meaning it will not open until late January at the earliest.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Hospitals; Scandals; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals

Title: MPPs return after four-month break:   Tories vow to fast-track highway projects as parties brace for possible early election

Document 489 of 500

Premier eyes third term in close campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-eyes-third-term-close-campaign/docview/3118626040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+eyes+third+term+in+close+campaign&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick's election campaign has offered voters a sharp division in viewpoints for the future of the province, especially between the two leading candidates for the premier's office.
Progressive Conservative Leader Blaine Higgs, a unilingual former oil executive running for a third term as premier, presented voters with a two-page platform containing 11 promises, a few of which signal the social conservative bent his party has taken. And he has been noticeable in his absence - on roughly one-third of the days since he called the election on Sept. 19, Higgs has held no public events.
The Liberals, by contrast, have made 100 promises - many of which focus on health care and housing - and bilingual leader Susan Holt, a former consultant and provincial civil servant, has taken full advantage of the campaign's 33 days, taking few off.
Higgs summed up Monday's election as one of the most significant in the province's history.
"There's very stark differences between the parties that are running," he told reporters last week. "I truly believe that the outcome will define the future of this province."
The Tories promised to cut the harmonized sales tax by two percentage points, from 15 per cent to 13 per cent, and to "respect parents." The latter promise refers to a 2023 decision by the Tory government to require teachers to get parental consent before they can use the preferred first names and pronouns of transgender children under 16.
The Liberals have promised to open 30 community health clinics across the province by 2028 and eliminate the provincial sales tax on electricity bills for residential customers.
Jamie Gillies, a political scientist at St. Thomas University, called the Liberal campaign "traditional," one that has put Holt at the centre; the Progressive Conservatives, he said, focused much less on the party leader. He said Holt had a "very clear campaign strategy, which was to focus like a laser on health care."
"I think they probably succeeded in pushing that as the No. 1 issue during the campaign in the minds of voters. Now, whether the election turns on health care, we're going to find out on Monday," Gillies said.
Voters will also discover on Monday whether the Liberals or Tories win a majority; if neither does, the third-placed Green Party could play an outsized role. Leader David Coon has deflected accusations he has a list of demands ready for Holt in exchange for his party's support in the event her party earns a minority of seats but collects more than the Tories.
Polling numbers have favoured the Liberals in the campaign's final stretch. Mainstreet Research, in a poll released Saturday, reported the Liberals had a six-point lead over the Progressive Conservatives among respondents. About 43 per cent of survey participants said they would vote for the Liberals, while 35 per cent said they'd go with the Tories and nine per cent backed the Greens.
The poll, conducted by phone between Thursday and Saturday, had a sample size of 724 and a 3.6 per cent margin of error.
Poll aggregator 338Canada.com projected the Liberals will win 25 seats - just enough for a majority - and the Tories will win 22, but the race is close.
The results of the election will also show whether Higgs was correct that his changes to the gender identity policy in schools will encourage his base to vote. "So we're going to see if voters reward or punish the premier for kind of doubling and then tripling and quadrupling down on that issue," Gillies said.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Hina Alam The Canadian Press

Document 490 of 500

Poilievre wants provinces to overhaul disability programs: Conservative leader says he could withhold funding to make it happen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-wants-provinces-overhaul-disability/docview/3118626033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+wants+provinces+to+overhaul+disability+programs%3A+Conservative+leader+says+he+could+withhold+funding+to+make+it+happen&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says he wants provinces to overhaul their disability assistance programs so that disabled Canadians don't see working income clawed back from provincial benefits, and he could put billions in federal transfers at stake to make it happen.
Poilievre made that commitment in a 2023 video recently viewed by the Star, in which he referred to a private member's bill he introduced as the Conservative finance critic in 2018.
If passed, the bill would have required those changes as a condition to receiving the Canada Social Transfer, a federal payment to the provinces that helps to cover the cost of social programs.
"For those roughly a million Canadians with disabilities who do work, we need to reform the benefit programs to reward that work. Right now, there are clawbacks if you're a person on disability and you get a job. Often, they take away your income, rental support, even medication," he said in the video, posted online by political commentator Darshan Maharaja and viewed just over 2,000 times.
"I will pass the fairness for workers with disabilities act, which will require provinces, as a condition of getting their federal money, to reform their systems to make sure that every time a person with disabilities earns an extra dollar, they're made better off and that they're not punished for that. Everybody should have the chance to put their talents to work for this great country of ours."
The change would be one step to address what many see as a thorny flaw in the social assistance programs offered across Canada. However, it raises questions about the approach of a Conservative leader who's routinely accused the Trudeau government of overreach and could lead to pushback from provinces antsy about the precedent it would set, said policy experts who spoke to the Star.
"Anything that helps impoverished people with disabilities get to live above the poverty line is a good step," said David Lepofsky, a lawyer and disability advocate in Ontario. "If the Tories are going to take this approach to federal power, there is more to do."
But "imagine trying to put conditions on something that's been unconditional since 1995," said Gillian Petit, an economist at the University of Calgary, pointing to Poilievre's proposal to tie the Canada Social Transfer to the reforms. This year, provinces and territories are expected to receive $16.9 billion from the Canada Social Transfer, with Ontario getting $6.5 billion.
"It would probably be a bit of a political fiasco."
Faced with that question in 2018 - before the bill was voted down by the Liberals - Poilievre pointed to delivery standards set out in the Canada Health Transfer and said this scenario justifies adding conditions.
Whether he is still willing to put billions in federal transfers at stake to implement those changes, however, remains unclear.
In a statement to the Star, Poilievre repeated his criticism of current systems that penalize disabled Canadians that work, but did not say if he would use the threat of withdrawing federal money to force change. And that is no different from what he said in the 2023 video, his office said, though it would not provide details on his proposal.
"As prime minister, I will be working with provinces to ensure workers with disabilities always benefit from working another hour, taking another shift, or accepting a job opportunity," Poilievre said.
Among the unanswered questions is how he would get provinces on board, how much working income he would want exempted from clawbacks, and how this principle would apply to non-cash benefits and towards federal programs.
In the 2018 bill, Poilievre proposed disallowing provinces from clawing back benefits for disabled Canadians on employment income up to $30,000 and any province that did risked losing their portion of the Canada Social Transfer. The bill also said Finance Canada should use available data to calculate clawbacks on benefits and the finance minister should "identify and consider" changes that could be made to federal taxes and benefits in order to reduce those clawbacks.
He said the principle of allowing disabled Canadians to keep their working income should be consistent across all levels of government and argued it would save governments money if people are able to work.
Right now, only a small portion of people who receive disability assistance are able to work, said Alexi White, the director of systems change at Maytree. For example, out of the 367,828 Ontario Disability Support Program cases in 2022/23, 10 per cent reported working income, according to data from the social policy group.
But Jen Robson, an associate professor of public policy at Carleton University, said the proposal as outlined in the 2018 bill could lead to strong pushback from provinces, including legal challenges. Referencing Poilievre's housing bill, she said that would be another piece of legislation from the Conservative leader that overrides provincial jurisdiction with the "Ottawa-knows-best" approach he's accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government of.
"It's well-intentioned but inherently flawed," she said. "It's very heavy handed."
She said it would be best to achieve the results Poilievre wants through negotiations rather than trying to force the provinces, similar to how the Liberals have approached the Canada Disability Benefit (CDB), which is slated to roll out next summer.
The Liberals had argued it would have been unconstitutional to disallow clawbacks in the legislation despite repeated pleas from advocates and some senators. So far, four provinces and two territories have said they won't claw back their own supports to recipients of the CDB after discussions with Disabilities Minister Kamal Khera, while another has increased its own supports.
Poilievre's Conservatives have not outlined their position on the federal benefit, but all opposition parties have pressed the Liberal government over the historic program that has been slammed as a disappointment by disability advocates. That benefit, which is expected to reach 600,000 Canadians at its peak, will be clawed back from its $200 monthly maximum at $23,000 in annual income, but allows for up to $10,000 in working income exemptions for eligible individuals.
The Liberals and other opposition parties have accused Poilievre's Conservatives of wanting to cut social programs, focusing their criticism on the federal dental and pharmacare programs.
"If Poilievre truly cared about supporting Canadians with disabilities, instead of putting out slogans, he would support this benefit and our affordable dental care plan which also supports persons with disabilities," a spokesperson for Khera said in a statement to the Star.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, right, introduced a private member's bill as the Conservative finance critic in 2018 that, if passed, would have made changes to the provinces disability assistance programs a condition for receiving the Canada Social Transfer.Spencer ColbyThe Canadian Press File Photo
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 491 of 500

Viva Niagara Falls? Don't bet on it: Instead of trying to recreate Sin City, Ford government should ante up some fresh ideas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/viva-niagara-falls-dont-bet-on/docview/3118447909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Viva+Niagara+Falls%3F+Don%27t+bet+on+it%3A+Instead+of+trying+to+recreate+Sin+City%2C+Ford+government+should+ante+up+some+fresh+ideas&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Las Vegas, Ontario?
As absurd as it sounds, last month Doug Ford indicated he wants to turn Niagara Falls into more of a "Las Vegas of the North" with additional casinos - an effort to revitalize what is already one of Canada's most famous tourist destinations. He'd have better luck trying to add another waterfall to the Niagara River than turning Ontario into a true Las Vegas.
I love Vegas, but even if Ontario collectively decided it wanted to be like it, this old province couldn't pull it off. Vegas is a place with lots of casinos, but more importantly, a debauched sensibility. A vibe, really, one that is baked deeply into its DNA. Ontario has whatever the opposite of Vegas is in our DNA. Just plopping a casino or three down doesn't make Vegas.
It pains me to say this because I wish Ontario could be a bit more Vegas-y. For years I've written columns arguing that both Toronto and Ontario need to loosen up in various ways, whether grudgingly allowing people to drink in parks or providing space for nightclubs in cities. Ontario may not be the place where fun goes to die, but it sure can put a damper on it.
Consider Ottawa recently ticketing people parked in a dark area to see the Northern Lights. The city has a new "night commissioner" (sometimes called a "Night Mayor") to advocate for life after dark, but when contacted about the ticketing, it said this incident wasn't part of his mandate. As embarrassing as that is for Ottawa, it's a deeply Ontario kind of response.
"What happens in Vegas stays in Vegas" is a marketing catch phrase, but Vegas is truly different. There is a deep looseness to how that city works, a tolerance of vice, scandal, noise, architectural chaos and garishness. All things Ontario generally can't stomach much of. At least not overtly.
Ontario's first casino opened in my hometown of Windsor in 1994 with great fanfare and expectation when the city was still hard hit by a recession. My father even became a blackjack dealer soon after opening, staying on for the better part of a decade. He liked it and, like many others, made good money at the same tables where two of my university friends lost their OSAP loans in a matter of weeks that first September.
Casinos give, but they take a lot more. While it became a regular part of Windsor life, the casino didn't revolutionize the city's economy. Windsor's downtown has more challenges now than it did then.
I thought all casinos were like Windsor's until I actually went to Vegas and saw that its were quite different. More fun and easygoing, the line between casino and the city blurred as people carouse from one to another. Ontario casinos, and the experience in and around them, are tame, just a shadow of how Vegas works.
Walking into a casino in Ontario feels a bit guilty from the moment the security guard at the door sizes you up, while Las Vegas is a machine made for having unrepentant fun.
Ford's Vegas push is a window into how he and his government thinks though, shoehorning big ideas and concepts from elsewhere into Ontario. It explains the Therme Spa at Ontario Place. There's nothing inherently wrong with a spa, but Therme usually establishes them in suburban areas surrounded by lots of parking, not on prime, precious and public waterfront land. Ford has also mentioned a casino for Ontario Place too. The same old ideas keep popping up.
When he was city councillor, Ford wanted to put a mall and Ferris wheel in the Port Lands. The Ferris wheel wasn't a terrible idea, but the mall was in an era when they are closing (just Google "dead malls"). More recently, Ford has said if he wasn't premier he would like to open a Cheesecake Factory. Nothing wrong with that restaurant chain either, but where is the imagination and business creativity? Ontario is an endlessly interesting place with unique landscapes, urban and countryside, but you'd never know it based on the kind of projects that keep being proposed.
Sometimes we manage to get it right, though. Niagara-on-the-Lake built on its unique location and history, becoming an attractive theatre destination. Niagara Falls itself recently refurbished and opened the glorious Canadian Niagara Power Generating Station to the public. Few places in the world can boast an attraction like that.
Ford prides himself on being a businessman, but many of the ideas he champions are derivative, tired and boring. Where are the bold, new, unique and entrepreneurial ideas that reflect Ontario itself? We did that, once, with Ontario Place and the Science Centre. Let's build on what Ontario has without trying to copy other unique places.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's push to make Niagara Falls more like Las Vegas is a window into how he and his government are trying to shoehorn concepts from elsewhere into Ontario, Shawn Micallef writes.John LocherThe Associated Press file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Casinos; Gambling; Cultural identity; Economic development; Urban planning; Tourism; Public policy

Document 492 of 500

Why send cheques to millionaires?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-send-cheques-millionaires/docview/3118447851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+send+cheques+to+millionaires%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tony Staffieri, CEO of Rogers, gets $200. RBC boss Dave McKay gets $200. Hockey star Mitch Marner gets $200.
That's the latest imaginative idea from our Ontario government.
According to news reports, the province will soon send a cheque for $200 to every Ontarian. Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will make it official on Oct. 30, the Star's Robert Benzie recently reported.
This may be good politics - free money is free money - but it is terrible policy.
It's also somewhat surprising.
In normal times, Conservatives pride themselves on being careful stewards of public dollars. They don't mind being called cheap, or stingy; they call it being thrifty and prudent.
But these are not normal times, it seems. Premier Doug Ford's September announcement that he will build a traffic tunnel under Highway 401 signalled a new era in Ontario public finance: the era where we have infinite money to spend on things nobody asked for.
The cost of the tunnel is both unknown and, to the premier, irrelevant: "We're getting that tunnel built, I guarantee it," he says. If it is ever dug, the final price tag of this elongated hole in the ground will be too high to count.
In contrast, the cost of sending $200 to every Ontarian is easily estimated: It's about $3.2 billion. But here's a question: Where are they getting the money?
In the short term, it will be borrowed: the province has a budget deficit right now. But in the long term, that $3.2 billion can only come out of Ontarians' pockets - or the services they depend on.
In recent decades, right-wing pundits have been successful at separating public dollars, in the public mind, from the things those dollars pay for. They've created the impression that tax cuts, and cash giveaways, have no consequences. This is not true. It has never been true.
For starters, the latest free-money plan will send public dollars to millions of Ontarians who clearly do not need them. Earlier this month, Statistics Canada reported that the richest 20 per cent of Canadians own two-thirds of all the wealth in the country. Meanwhile, income inequality has hit record levels.
If Queen's Park wants to give away money because "people are hurting" - a frequent Ford comment - then that assistance should go to people who are actually hurting.
With $3.2 billion, the province could triple the Ontario Child Benefit for a year and still have half a billion left over. That would give low- and middle-income families some breathing space.
With $3.2 billion, the province could double payments to Ontario Works recipients, whose incomes have been frozen since 2018. That would support Ontarians mired in deep poverty.
Alternatively, the province could boost critical programs that are currently starved for funds. Spending $3.2 billion could double, for a year, the funding for social services that support children at risk or with special needs. Programs like child protection, children's treatment, and the Complex Special Needs program (to name a few) need all the help they can get.
The list of good places to spend $3.2 billion is a long one. Think hospitals. Home care. Housing. Municipalities. Crumbing schools with ever-larger class sizes.
With that kind of money, we could even get serious about tackling the climate emergency that threatens us all. At the moment, Ontario's "Climate Change and Resiliency" program has a budget of $15 million - less than one two-hundredth of the cost of sending those $200 cheques to every Ontarian. And every dollar spent on climate action now will save money down the road.
The province, of course, says it is already making big investments in public services and infrastructure. But accounting for inflation and population growth, spending on Ontario's public services is, in almost every area, stagnant or falling. And our support for Ontarians in need is going backwards.
That's where Queen's Park should be putting our money.
We don't need to mail cheques to millionaires.
Randy Robinson is Ontario director of the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives.
CREDIT: Randy Robinson Contributor Randy Robinson is Ontario director of the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives.;

Subject: Public services; Budget deficits; Child welfare; Income inequality; Financial aid; Public policy; Low income groups

Business indexing term: Subject: Budget deficits Income inequality

Location: Canada

Document 493 of 500

Ford government must rethink plan to force independent grocery stores to accept empty alcohol containers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-must-rethink-plan-force/docview/3118300135/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+must+rethink+plan+to+force+independent+grocery+stores+to+accept+empty+alcohol+containers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Oct. 31 - Halloween - all grocery stores in Ontario will ostensibly have the right to sell beer, wine and cider products.
But the strings that come along with that right have left grocers in the province wondering if this was the Ontario government's idea of a trick-or-treat program.
Ontario's independent grocers, who represent about 59 per cent of the brick-and-mortar retail grocery stores in the province, initially applauded the announcement in 2023 that the government intended to open up the sale of alcohol products to all grocery stores. This was a welcome change from the system established by the previous Liberal government who, by limiting the number of licensed retailers who could sell beer and wine, created winners and losers on the retail grocery landscape. That should not be the role of government.
However, when ending that system, the current Conservative government is now forcing all grocery stores that opt to sell alcohol to also accept dirty bottles and cans into their stores, creating more winners and losers. It's tantamount to a bad hangover from the previous government's incongruous approach.
Where, exactly, does this government suggest deposit returns should be dropped off in a grocery store? By the meat, produce or bakery sections? Or perhaps by the front entrance so that the smell of stale beer wafting through the store can enhance the shopping experience for Ontario consumers. These dirty cans and bottles will also be coming into the store often broken and accompanied by cigarette butts, discarded needles and insects. As such, the risk of cross-contamination in a food environment, particularly in a smaller store, is more pronounced.
As well, where will an independent grocer operating on margins of two per cent - half that of a retail grocery chain - find the space to sort and store these empties? What products in the store do the bureaucrats in the Ontario government recommend be taken out to make room for these bottles and cans? How will that independent make up for the revenue loss from that forfeited space? How will that small business pay for the costs of storing and sorting these soiled cans and bottles with their margins further reduced? Has the government also looked at this within the context of the existing Blue Box program, which has seen costs more than triple in the last two years?
This past summer, the Ontario government rightly squashed the concept of grocers having to take back dirty, non-alcoholic containers. How then can the same government now foist that concept on grocers when it's a dirty, alcoholic container?
The alcohol changes announced in May contained a stark illustration that the Ontario government had not grasped that the challenges and realities for smaller, family-owned independent grocers are significantly different than those faced by monolithic retail chains. In revising provincial Alcohol and Gaming Commission licensing fees, the Ford government aligned the existing fees for the grocery chains and independents to the same level, reducing fees for the chains, but tripling them for the independents. Independent grocers welcomed Finance Minister Bethlenfalvy reversing that miscue, but it was disconcerting that Main Street and Bay Street grocers had been lumped together in the first place.
Last year, the Competition Bureau of Canada released its long-awaited study into the retail grocery industry. One of the key recommendations coming out of that exhaustive study, was that all governments - federal, provincial and territorial - need to do more to encourage the growth of Canada's independent grocers. How can the Ontario government reconcile its imposition of a costly and impractical deposit return system on its own independent grocers, with the findings and recommendations of that report?
Independent grocers have conveyed to the government our desire to work together to find and enhance solutions to recycling. Looking at community depots like those that exist in other jurisdictions, or returning empties to the LCBO - which does not sell fresh food - are but two options that come to mind.
The decision of the Ontario government to expand where Beer, Wine and Cider can be sold should be a win-win. A win for retailers, for Ontario craft breweries and wineries- and a win for consumers wanting more convenience.
But the low number of independent grocers seeking beer and wine licences - combined with current sellers indicating they will surrender their licences in 2026 when the exemption to operate in proximity to a Beer Store ends - attests to the fact that the province needs to adjust their alcohol modernization plan.
In a refreshing departure from the political norm, Premier Ford has said, to his credit, that when his government recognizes something may not be the right decision, changes are made. Such a change is needed now. It is hard to imagine that this is an issue the Ontario government intended to create.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford should rethink decision to force independent grocery stores to accept empty alcohol containers, writes Gary Sands.
CREDIT: Gary Sands Contributor

Subject: Retail stores; Alcohol; Liquor; Beer; Independent grocery stores; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores Independent grocery stores Breweries

Location: Canada

Title: Ford government must rethink plan to force independent grocery stores to accept empty alcohol containers

Document 494 of 500

Doug Ford talks about creating 'Vegas of the North.' Can't he up the ante with some fresh ideas?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-talks-about-creating-vegas-north-cant/docview/3118300055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+talks+about+creating+%27Vegas+of+the+North.%27+Can%27t+he+up+the+ante+with+some+fresh+ideas%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Las Vegas, Ontario?
As absurd as it sounds, last month Doug Ford indicated he wants to turn Niagara Falls into more of a "Las Vegas of the North" with additional casinos - an effort to revitalize what is already one of Canada's most famous tourist destinations. He'd have better luck trying to add another waterfall to the Niagara River than turning Ontario into a true Las Vegas.
I love Vegas, but even if Ontario collectively decided it wanted to be like it, this old province couldn't pull it off. Vegas is a place with lots of casinos, but more importantly, a debauched sensibility. A vibe, really, one that is baked deeply into its DNA. Ontario has whatever the opposite of Vegas is in our DNA. Just plopping a casino or three down doesn't make Vegas.
It pains me to say this because I wish Ontario could be a bit more Vegas-y. For years I've written columns arguing that both Toronto and Ontario need to loosen up in various ways, whether grudgingly allowing people to drink in parks or providing space for nightclubs in cities. Ontario may not be the place where fun goes to die, but it sure can put a damper on it.
Consider Ottawa recently ticketing people parked in a dark area to see the Northern Lights. The city has a new "night commissioner" (sometimes called a "Night Mayor") to advocate for life after dark, but when contacted about the ticketing, it said this incident wasn't part of his mandate. As embarrassing as that is for Ottawa, it's a deeply Ontario kind of response.
"What happens in Vegas stays in Vegas" is a marketing catch phrase, but Vegas is truly different. There is a deep looseness to how that city works, a tolerance of vice, scandal, noise, architectural chaos and garishness. All things Ontario generally can't stomach much of. At least not overtly.
Ontario's first casino opened in my hometown of Windsor in 1994 with great fanfare and expectation when the city was still hard hit by a recession. My father even became a blackjack dealer soon after opening, staying on for the better part of a decade. He liked it and, like many others, made good money at the same tables where two of my university friends lost their OSAP loans in a matter of weeks that first September.
Casinos give, but they take a lot more. While it became a regular part of Windsor life, the casino didn't revolutionize the city's economy. Windsor's downtown has more challenges now than it did then.
I thought all casinos were like Windsor's until I actually went to Vegas and saw that its were quite different. More fun and easygoing, the line between casino and the city blurred as people carouse from one to another. Ontario casinos, and the experience in and around them, are tame, just a shadow of how Vegas works.
Walking into a casino in Ontario feels a bit guilty from the moment the security guard at the door sizes you up, while Las Vegas is a machine made for having unrepentant fun.
Ford's Vegas push is a window into how he and his government thinks though, shoehorning big ideas and concepts from elsewhere into Ontario. It explains the Therme Spa at Ontario Place. There's nothing inherently wrong with a spa, but Therme usually establishes them in suburban areas surrounded by lots of parking, not on prime, precious and public waterfront land. Ford has also mentioned a casino for Ontario Place too. The same old ideas keep popping up.
When he was city councillor Ford wanted to put a mall and Ferris wheel in the Port Lands. The Ferris wheel wasn't a terrible idea, but the mall was in an era when they are closing (just Google "dead malls"). More recently, Ford has said if he wasn't premier he would like to open a Cheesecake Factory. Nothing wrong with that restaurant chain either, but where is the imagination and business creativity? Ontario is an endlessly interesting place with unique landscapes, urban and countryside, but you'd never know it based on the kind of projects that keep being proposed.
Sometimes we manage to get it right, though. Niagara-on-the-Lake built on its unique location and history, becoming an attractive theatre destination. Niagara Falls itself recently refurbished and opened the glorious Canadian Niagara Power Generating Station to the public. Few places in the world can boast an attraction like that.
Ford prides himself on being a businessman, but many of the ideas he champions are derivative, tired and boring. Where are the bold, new, unique and entrepreneurial ideas that reflect Ontario itself? We did that, once, with Ontario Place and the Science Centre. Let's build on what Ontario has without trying to copy other unique places.
CAPTION: Putting more casinos into Niagara Falls won't turn that Ontario city into Las Vegas, which has vibe opposite to the one in this province.

Subject: Casinos

Business indexing term: Subject: Casinos

Location: United States--US; Niagara Falls; Las Vegas Nevada

Document 495 of 500

'The program had become a bit of a racket': Immigration Minister Marc Miller on immigration politics, international students, and why he isn't the same as Maxime Bernier

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/program-had-become-bit-racket/docview/3118183008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+program+had+become+a+bit+of+a+racket%27%3A+Immigration+Minister+Marc+Miller+on+immigration+politics%2C+international+students%2C+and+why+he+isn%27t+the+same+as+Maxime+Bernier&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In early September, the U.S. magazine Compact ran an article with the headline, "How Canada Turned against Immigration." The headline was wildly overstated. And yet, there has been a change - in policy, rhetoric and public mood - change that has coincided, roughly, with the appointment of Marc Miller, a Montreal MP, as Minster of Immigration, Refugees, and Citizenship in July of 2023.
This year, Miller imposed a two-year cap on international-student visas, reducing the numbers by roughly 35cent. His office has pushed through changes to the temporary foreign worker program, and he has suggested that further changes are in the offing. The federal government has also indicated that it is reviewing its immigration target numbers for 2025 and 2026.
In a wide-ranging interview in early October, edited for space and clarity, Miller denied that the government is capitulating to anti-immigrant sentiment. The changes are motivated, he said, by an enduring belief in the value of immigration. To ensure that Canadians continue to look favourably on the immigration system, Miller argued, reform is necessary - even if it means making hard choices and acknowledging past mistakes.
In asurveyfrom September 2022, just 27 per cent of Canadians agreed with the statement "there's too much immigration in Canada." A year later, 44 per cent agreed. What do you think is behind that change?
The consensus is still there around the value of immigration, but people want a sense that the system is under control. We've had some areas, like international students, where the system's gotten overheated. There's lots of fault to go around. But after identifying who's to blame, it's important to actually do something. That's where I play a role.
You say that people want a sense that the system is under control? What does "under control" mean?
An immigration system that makes sense is one where the volumes are acceptable to the population and they meet a number of needs: labour needs, family reunification objectives, and the kinds of lofty humanitarian goals that Canada has been proud of. But the economic motives have to be clear, and labour markets need to be properly aligned with immigration rates. The criticism is that we have been too slow to adapt. And that's something I take to heart.
It seems that one reason people are getting twitchy about immigration is that we've had a lot of it recently, particularly after COVID. In 2022, the Canadian population grew by more than a million people, and 96 per cent of them were newcomers. Why did the numbers tick up so much?
Our reaction after COVID was the right one: With severe market shortages, we chose to have higher permanent residency levels, to convert more people from temporary to permanent residents, and to take in temporary residents in areas like the low-income stream and international students, to the point where international students occupy half of the temporary resident spaces. That's historically high, and it's something we need to get control over.
What I'm getting from you is that you're always correcting for what recently happened.
We had the possibility of a COVID recession. We had these real labour market shortages, so we had to correct for that. Now we've got a lot of people coming in, and it's putting strain on housing prices, on infrastructure. People are getting anxious, so we're correcting for that. But there are also some long-term trends that are true. We do need to make the labour force younger overall. It's impossible to do that without a higher birth rate or immigrants. The math doesn't work.
Right. There are only two ways to increase the population.
The issue is having the proper intellectual debate and not turning it into value statements about the race, colour, or creed of the people coming in, but also without painting people as racist when they express views you disagree with.
Is it safe to say that immigration numbers are likely to come down in the near future?
I can't speak to that. But there's a conversation about it. There's a world in which it happens.
The immigration targets were originally set for 500,000 people in 2025 and 500,000 in 2026. Those figures are being potentially revised, yes?
Yeah. We have to be more nimble than we've been in the past. I don't think there's value in doubling down on assumptions that were true three or four years ago.
But the numbers aren't going up, right?
I certainly don't see it.
Reuters recently reported that Canada has been scrutinizing international travelers more carefully. Last June, the report said, we deemed more visa holders inadmissible than we have since COVID. Are these changes happening in response to directives from your office?
The short answer is yes. I don't take any pleasure in denying visas, but this is something that had to be done. Going into the summer, I looked at the data I was getting from my department and realized that the system needed an injection of discipline.
Can you give me an example?
I can't, because then people will game the system. We did tighten the screws a bit, though.
Let's talk about international students. In theory, the reason we admit so many is because we want people from around the world to bring their smarts and work ethic to our universities. In practice, many international students are paying inflated prices for a tenth-rate education because they know that when their program of study is complete they can eventually apply for permanent residency.
The program had become a bit of a racket, and I probably should have acted sooner. My biggest mistake was to trust people for too long - to trust provinces and the institutions they regulate. Auditors general have said time and time again that Ontario and B.C. had to get a grip on things. Those are the two provinces where international-student placements had become a runaway train. And they were all promising something - permanent residency or Canadian citizenship - that was not guaranteed.
What should the provinces
have been doing?
They should have regulated their designated learning institutions, put criteria as to the solvency of the potential students, put the infrastructure in place to house the students, and been more rigorous about which colleges get a designated-learning-institution badge to recruit international students.
It does seem to me that the
federal government was in on the hustle too. I watched a video ad, targeted at prospective international students, that Immigration, Refugees, and Citizenship Canada was putting out, which basically said, "Come here to study. There's a good chance you'll get to stay."
We made things attractive. And things should be attractive. But when things get out of hand, you owe it to Canadians to get them under control.
I remember the 2019 election, when Maxime Bernier was running for the first time as leader of the People's Party of Canada. He felt his opponents, including in the Liberal Party, unfairly painted him as a racist. He was not in favour of banning immigration; he just wanted to reduce the overall target numbers. You've said that you too are open to reducing numbers. What's the difference between you and him?
People like him can't turn around and say, "I was right along," because they see something that they feel vindicates their positions. He's misused statistics, and he's headed up a party that's openly xenophobic. There are very few people who have ever accused our party, in its current iteration, of being xenophobic.
I have a non-policy question, one that I personally wrestle with. There's a strain of left-wing utopian thought that says, We should just have open borders. Obviously, no one's doing that.
You will find some folks in the NDP who favour open borders.
OK, maybe you will. And, in their defence, I think that, on a purely ethical level, they're right. Look at me. I get all the privileges of being Canadian just because I was born here. And somebody else gets none of those privileges, just because they were born elsewhere. That just seems wrong, doesn't it?
That argument comes from the heart, and I respect it. I think we can all agree that those of us who were born here were really, really lucky. But it is naïve to think that we can have open borders. Control is important to the integrity of our immigration programs.
There's a critique out there -
I associate it with the journalist David Frum - that too much immigration leads to destabilization, which leads to backlash and far-right nativism. Is that risk on your mind?
I think we do have to look at the impact that high volumes have on people's sense of fairness. I think Canadians are the most decent people in the world. But I am not naïve, and I'm not deaf to some of the things I'm hearing. Showing Canadians that you're reasonable, that you're willing to consider many options, without being cruel or inhumane, is an important thing to do.
CAPTION: Marc Miller says his biggest mistake on international students was "to trust people too long."Blair Gable
CREDIT: Simon Lewsen Contributor;

Subject: Citizenship; Canadians; Foreign students; Passports & visas; Xenophobia; Labor market; Public opinion; Immigration policy

Business indexing term: Subject: Labor market

Location: Canada

People: Miller, Marc
