Document 496 of 500

Doug Ford wants to buy your silence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-wants-buy-your-silence/docview/3117868811/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+buy+your+silence&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some politicians try to buy votes.
Doug Ford wants to buy your silence. And acquiescence.
For a roughly $200 payout, to be handed out to everyone in Ontario, the premier hopes voters will forgive his political sins and forget his legal transgressions.
The planned cash bonanza, to be distributed early next year, is a calculated gamble. The goal is to clear the way for an early election before any bad news - legal and political - erodes the support Ford's Tories now enjoy in public opinion polls.
As my colleague Robert Benzie reported this week, the premier's sudden generosity will be made public later this month. In its annual fall economic statement, the government is dropping any pretence of fiscal probity as it invests heavily and recklessly in its own political survival.
Ford's PCs want an early election, no matter the cost. Never mind the unnecessary $155-million election expense - that's the least of it.
If they settle on the $200 figure for every adult and child in Ontario, that works out to as much as $1,000 for a family of five - and perhaps $3.2 billion out of the treasury in total. That money is badly needed to shore up our schools, our hospitals and our homeless, but the premier believes he needs it more desperately to soften up voters.
There are twin legal challenges facing Ford.
The first is deadly serious: potential violations of Ontario's criminal laws now being probed by a high-stakes RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
The headlines have faded, but few have forgotten the $8.28-billion imbroglio over protected land made available to private developers on the premier's watch (until he reversed course under pressure). That police probe could be released sometime next year, delivering potentially bad news and a political death sentence.
Rather than wait for the police to rain on their re-election parade - scheduled for June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election law - the plan is to move the campaign up by more than a year to early 2025. But to bump up the date, the Tories would have to ignore the election law.
Fortuitously for Ford, that second potential legal peril is more symbolic than real. While the law mandates that any majority government should serve a full four-year term in power before triggering another, there is no legal penalty for flouting it - only a political price if the press and the people cry foul.
Hence the payout. The premier is banking on voters being more interested in money than morality.
It's an elegant, if expensive, election plan: a kickback for voters, gifted by a government accused of kickbacks from developers (despite those opposition allegations in the legislature, no criminality has been proven and the police aren't talking).
Why aim for a potential $200 giveaway? In the past, as a Toronto councillor, Ford famously doled out a wad of $20 bills to impoverished voters at a Toronto Community Housing building in 2013.
He later apologized for the spectacle. And subsequently opted for juicier giveaways that, historically, have hit a sweet spot with voters.
Ahead of the 2022 election, Ford's Tories cut cheques to rebate motorists for licence plate fees that the government cancelled, at a cost of more than $1 billion to the treasury. During the COVID pandemic, parents received as much as $250 per child. And the previous PC government of Mike Harris issued $200 "dividend" cheques.
How to justify such shameless pre-election (early election) vote-buying?
The unspoken reason is to satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale is to support people's purchasing power.
The premier points to post-pandemic inflation raising the price of food and fuel, but it's not so simple. In fact, the inflation rate plunged to a remarkably low 1.6 per cent last month, thanks mostly to falling fuel prices, according to the latest Statistics Canada data.
And then there's the federal carbon levy, one of Ford's favourite targets. It is scheduled to increase again next spring, raising the price of gasoline.
Ford has fulminated over gasoline prices for years - even though the amount taken in by the federal government is fully rebated to the average family. Before the 2022 election, he used the federal carbon levy as a pretext for the province to reduce its own fuel tax in another vote-buying gambit.
For the Tories, the upcoming handout could be the gift that keeps on giving (if not voting). First, by soothing any public disgust over an early election; second, by serving as a counterpoint to the federal carbon levy, allowing Ford to fight an election on his perennial slogan, "Axe the tax."
In a preview of his campaign rhetoric, Ford complained Thursday about the "impact of inflation and high interest rates, alongside the growing cost of the federal carbon tax, which in my opinion is the worst tax you could ever put on the backs of people."
There you have it - an evil levy imposed by Ottawa (albeit rebated), versus a helpful handout from the province, pre-election. Ford has it all figured out.
But does he have voters figured out?
CAPTION: The unspoken reason Premier Doug Ford wants to send cheques to Ontarians isto satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale isto support people's purchasing power, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Chris Young THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Gasoline prices; Election law; Scandals; Voters; Politics; Carbon; Pandemics; Purchasing power

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+s+cheques+seem+like+a+bribe+to+buy+votes+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Doug+Ford+poised+to+send+out+pre-election+cheques+to+16+million+Ontarians%2C+Oct.+16+We+need+more+science+centres%2C+Oct.+15+Metrolinx+shakeup+sees+departure+of+senior+leaders%2C+head+of+light-rail+transit%2C+Oct.+16+We+should+only+be+putting+bike+lanes+in+places+that+make+sense%2C+Oct.+13+Ford+minister+slams+trustees%27+%24145%2C000+trip+to+Italy+to+buy+artwork+for+new+school%2C+Oct.+16&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's cheques seem like a bribe to buy votes
Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 16
As a longtime criminal lawyer, I wonder how such a blatant attempt to buy votes is not bribery. Were Premier Doug Ford to send me, only, a cheque, followed shortly thereafter with an early election call, I'd certainly feel as if he were trying to bribe me. Is the difference to be found in the sheer magnitude of Ford's attempt to influence all Ontario voters? Does that not make it worse? If Ford does this, I hope that people who can afford to will direct the bribe money to some of the causes such as housing, education and poverty reduction, to name just three, that Ford underfunds, because he can. Alternatively, donate it to a group or party to defeat him. Please, don't allow Ford to buy you off for $200.
John Liss, Toronto
I would rather Ford and his pals put all the $200 cheques into one pot and use the funds to provide health care for many of the 16 million Ontarians who have no primary care. However, our premier seems focused on liquor and beer, cutting down trees, bulldozing the Greenbelt, building parking lots, spas and tunnels and cancelling bikes lanes while at the same time increasing speed limits on main highways. Now he is sending out bribes to voters. Why do we keep voting in this man who is squandering taxpayers' money instead of using it for the common good of all people in the province? Maybe we voters are the short-sighted ones.
Sharon Phelps, Lindsay, Ont.
I was told this week that I needed a CT scan and the soonest I can get one is January 2025. Wouldn't it be better to invest in our health system instead of sending us all a cheque just before election time?
Madeleine Nevins, Markham
I am dismayed at the prospect of the Ontario government spending an estimated $3.2 billion of taxpayer money by sending out $200 cheques as part of its bid to win re-election in 2025. At least when Ford gave out $20 bills to constituents as a city councillor in 2013, it was from his own pocket. Now he is reaching into all of ours to try and win our votes. Shame. There are so many ways that this funding could be better spent. How about giving access to the 2.5 million Ontarians without a primary care provider, reducing hallway medicine and the long wait times for medical procedures and access to long-term-care facilities, building more affordable housing, increasing disability support funding, improving the education system, and so on. Imagine what an infusion of an additional $3.2 billion in one year could do to give people access to continuously underfunded government services and programs. Instead, this province will go further into deficit and programs and services will go further into decline. I can't and won't vote for that. What I can and do vote for are governments that actually invest in the health and well-being of this province and the people who call it home.
Isabella Tatar, Toronto
Why Trudeau must stay on
as Liberal leader
Voters hoping to avoid another Liberal-NDP partnership after the next general election should encourage Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to stay on as Liberal leader. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is sufficiently unpopular that he may not win a majority without Trudeau's continued help.
Patrick Cowan, North York
More reasons
to support science centres
We need more science centres, Oct. 15
Here are two more points to add to Joanne O'Meara's excellent article. Science centres provide much-needed support for school teachers in the challenging task of teaching science and related STEM subjects through field trips, curriculum materials, webinars and other resources for teachers. In astronomy, my subject, few teachers have any background in astronomy, or astronomy teaching. Science centres help. They also provide a base for non-profit community organizations, which voluntarily provide public education and outreach. The Ontario Science Centre, until it closed, was the home of the Toronto branch of the Royal Astronomical Society of Canada, which provides dozens of free presentations and star parties across the GTA. And let's not forget that it was a previous Ontario Conservative government whose "common sense revolution" led to the 1995 demise of Toronto's McLaughlin Planetarium, which educated and inspired hundreds of thousands of visitors of all ages!
John Percy, vice-chair (1992-96) of the Board of Trustees of Ontario Science Centre, Etobicoke
Replace entire Metrolinx board for a fresh start
Metrolinx shakeup sees departure of senior leaders, head of light-rail transit, Oct. 16
At last, someone is being held accountable for the many fiascos Metrolinx has inflicted on Ontarians. One hopes it is just a start. After all, the rot starts at the top. When the entire Metrolinx board has been sacked and when the CEO has been shown the door (with cause and without severance), then we'll have the fresh start that is desperately needed.
John Gavin, Toronto
If this is a war
on cars, the cars are winning
We should only be putting bike lanes in places that make sense, Oct. 13
Ontario's transportation minister wants to restrict and remove bike lanes in Toronto. He says we need to consult the data. The data, however, is pretty clear. Looking at Bathurst Street for example, from the lake to Highway 401 there are 14 major, two-way streets. Of these, only three have bike lanes. The data speaks volumes - if this is a war on cars, the cars are winning handily. To reduce these three bike lanes to a mere two would only serve to make cyclists more unsafe and the city less livable.
Bruce Stratton, Toronto
It's time to truly separate church and the state
Ford minister slams trustees' $145,000 trip to Italy to buy artwork for new school, Oct. 16
Brantford Catholic School board trustees swanned off to Italy this summer on an art buying trip. Education Minister Jill Dunlop is enraged. It's time to have only two school boards in Ontario, the English School Board and the French School Board. Religious education can be given by the churches. Then tax dollars will not be going to buy life-size statues of St. Pio. Then four different buses will not be going down the same country road. Then church and state will really be separate.
Sheila Craig, Barrie, Ont.

Document 498 of 500

Municipalities call new bike lane rules 'overreach': Association says premier should focus on housing, leave bike decisions to local politicians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/municipalities-call-new-bike-lane-rules-overreach/docview/3117868122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Municipalities+call+new+bike+lane+rules+%27overreach%27%3A+Association+says+premier+should+focus+on+housing%2C+leave+bike+decisions+to+local+politicians&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Upcoming legislation that would require cities to get provincial approval for some bike lanes is a "significant overreach" of power, Ontario municipalities say, while Premier Doug Ford vowed Thursday to go even further and remove existing lanes.
None of the 444 members of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario were consulted or shown evidence the province is using to justify its proposed veto power over new bike lanes that would remove a lane for cars, the association wrote in a statement.
"Bicycle lanes are an essential element of urban transportation planning and road safety," the association said. "Requiring provincial approval would be a significant overreach into municipal jurisdiction."
Ford's government is set to table the bike lane legislation next week as part of a suite of measures designed to tackle gridlock. Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said this week the legislation is meant for future bike lanes.
On Thursday, Ford contradicted his minister.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes," Ford said in a speech at the Empire Club of Canada. "We need to, and will, remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill."
Ford has previously complained about bike lanes on Bloor Street West creating gridlock on a road that is about a 10-minute drive from his home in Toronto's west end.
"It's an absolute disaster," Ford said last month.
The association said municipalities develop transportation plans based on local knowledge and community input. They are also balancing car traffic flow with active transportation and take into consideration health and environmental concerns.
"It is unclear how the Ministry of Transportation will be in a better position than municipalities to make decisions about local transportation matters," the association said.
"Rather than micromanaging bike lanes, the Ministry of Transportation could focus on accelerating its own approval processes to help support new housing."
Sarkaria said Thursday that cyclist safety is a priority for the government. He said they are "more than happy" to work with cities on the issue.
"As we work with the city to approve those future bike lanes, we'll take into consideration ... elements of safety and how that can be done in a way that supports the future introduction of lanes where they do require a removal of traffic," Sarkaria said.
He also said communities can still implement bike lanes that do not remove a lane of traffic.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Subject: Legislation

Premier touts tax rebate for Ontarians: Ford says plan to send cheques to every adult and child in province will 'stimulate the economy'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-touts-tax-rebate-ontarians/docview/3117868081/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+touts+tax+rebate+for+Ontarians%3A+Ford+says+plan+to+send+cheques+to+every+adult+and+child+in+province+will+%27stimulate+the+economy%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stressing if "you want to stimulate the economy, put money into people's pockets," Premier Doug Ford signalled the cheque will soon be in the mail.
In a speech to 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon at the Metro Toronto Convention, Ford gave the Star a grudging nod for revealing his finance minister would be announcing tax rebate cheques for 16 million Ontarians on Oct. 30.
"I risk stealing Peter Bethlenfalvy's thunder if I get into the fall economic statement," the premier said in a campaign-style address Thursday.
"I'm sure the Toronto Star knows it already," he added, wryly.
His comments came after the Star reported Wednesday that Ford is planning to send cheques to every Ontario adult and child as a possible pre-election goody for voters.
The move will be formally unveiled in Bethlenfalvy's economic update.
"He's tight as skin on a grape," the premier joked about his more fiscally conservative treasurer.
Although the exact amount of the "tax rebate" cheques is still being finalized by government officials, it will likely be at least $200. The payments would be tax-free.
That suggests the scheme could cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the money is distributed in January or February.
Sources - speaking confidentially to discuss a plan that has not yet been shared with all of cabinet, let alone caucus - said the timing would help Ontarians when their pay packets are reduced due to employment insurance deductions and when post-Christmas credit card bills are due.
But The cash injection could also give Ford a political boost as he considers calling an election more than a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"Put money into people's pockets and they'll go out and do something as simple as going out for dinner, maybe buying your kids sneakers or clothes, or maybe putting a little renovation on your house," he told the Empire Club.
"And they might do that because they have money in their pockets. The same with businesses. If you put money into businesses, they'll invest into their people, into technology, into their infrastructure, and they grow. That's what creates economic strength."
In this month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, the Tories were at 44 per cent while Bonnie Crombie's Liberals were at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats were at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens were at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians on Oct. 3-10 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
All three opposition leaders panned Ford's cheque gambit.
"Ford, if you really want to make life better, here's some advice: Build homes, hire doctors, fix schools, and give people a fair shot at planning for the future," said Stiles.
"Ontarians see right through these stunts from a government that's out of touch, out of gas, and clearly out of ideas."
Crombie, who attended Ford's speech, dismissed the plan as "another one-time gimmick from a premier who has promised and failed to cut taxes for more than six years."
"Rather than try to buy your vote in an election year, Ontario's Liberals will cut taxes for all middle-class families - permanently. More to come," she said, hinting at her party's election platform.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner suggested any such payouts should be means-tested.
"Imagine: Galen Weston getting a $200 cheque in the mail," said Schreiner, referring to the grocery tycoon.
"Ontarians need a government that's in their corner - not one that's trying to buy them out," said Schreiner.
"Some ways we could actually make life more affordable: build more homes, protect renters and invest in deeply affordable housing; stamp out oil and gas greed to bring down home energy costs; invest in public health care and education so Ontarians don't have to pay for things that should be covered publicly," he said.
While inflation has dipped to 1.6 per cent and interest rates are dropping, the Tories note that grocery prices remain high in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic and housing and energy costs continue to rise.
Ford's plan is a reprise of what former premier Mike Harris's Tories did in 2000 when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques. Ford pointedly gave a shout-out from the podium to Harris, who was also in attendance Thursday.
That $200 payment a generation ago, which came when Eves announced a budget surplus, would be worth around $335 today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.
Bethlenfalvy's update in a fortnight will not show a balanced budget, but is expected to signal the fiscal situation is much better than it was in his March 26 budget when he projected a surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25.
That was a $10-billion swing from the $200-million surplus the treasurer had forecast last year.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

NDP calls for spa deal probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-calls-spa-deal-probe/docview/3117868034/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+calls+for+spa+deal+probe&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Marit Stiles is asking the province's integrity commissioner to look into the government's spa deal at Ontario Place.
Stiles has filed a complaint "calling for an investigation into the Therme deal," the NDP said. "The complaint includes evidence suggesting that Therme received preferential treatment from the Ford government and seeks an investigation into the minister of infrastructure's involvement."
The NDP says that "over the past two years, we have collected evidence suggesting a deeply troubling pattern of behaviour surrounding the Ford government's deal with Therme. We have reason to believe that Therme received preferential treatment from this government when they were awarded the 95-year-long lease to build a luxury spa at Ontario Place."
Grace Lee, Premier Doug Ford's director of media relations, called the move "nothing more than the NDP reaching for headlines with a frivolous complaint largely based on conspiracy theories. We look forward to making Ontario Place a world-class destination once again."
In its submission to the integrity commissioner, the NDP says the bidding process for Ontario Place development was not a typically competitive process and that, among other irregularities, did not follow the normal bidding rules for larger projects. It also says the deadline for applications was extended.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
